books.title: Disciples of the Inverted Cross
books.author: Ikenna Obiajulu
books.blurb: A forbidden order rises beneath the surface of ordinary faith, and a young reader is drawn into the cost of discernment. Ascendance begins where hidden loyalties, ambition, and sacrifice collide.
books.summary:
Unconsciously, he established the evidence of system he’s a truce breaker.

Although his father, Rev. Joseph Obiajulu had avowed him to God to be a priest, a vow that saved him from death, he absconded from home in pursuit of his dream to become a medical doctor. Then he breached his promise to Ifeanyi, his elder brother, not to join the university’s secret fraternity, a promise that was sealed with the gift of a golden chain and a crucifix pendant.

But when he violated his consecration vow as Hangman of the dreaded Inverted Cross Fraternity, he was forced to give up the hammer, the medallion and the armband.
To escape the penalty of his transgression, he adopted a new name…Jehoshaphat.

He became an apostate and swore to destroy the fraternity he founded… one promise he didn’t fail.

chapters.title: Prologue
content: Perhaps the most perturbing issue for young people of my age is how to make good their dream. However, apart from being anxious about our future aspirations, we are all rattled by the age old mixed feeling of joy and gloom usually associated with the event that culminates our ordeal at Dennis Memorial Grammar School – the rapture of attaining yet another milestone as we strive for academic excellence and the poignancy of the end of a six year-old fraternity.
But unlike my mates, the expectation of my family’s arrival to witness our valedictory service especially saddens my day, a day that ought to be the happiest moment of my life.

When our final results were published last week, I was formally announced as this year’s valedictorian. Although we had waited nearly three weeks to know our overall rating, our continuous assessment clearly shows I had the overall best result. It had been a long, arduous contest for intellectual superiority.

The result for the senior secondary school certificate examinations will not be out until mid-August, two months away. But until then, we all bask in the euphoria of being eligible to join the prestigious Dengram Old Boys Association, whether we pass or flunk the university entrance exams.

I had witnessed, for five consecutive years, graduating students whimpering to one another as they exchange parting greetings and contact addresses after the rendition of the traditional parting hymn adopted for this occasion, God be with you till we meet again, God be with you till we meet again …till we meet again, till we meet again. God be with you till we meet again.

The feeling isn’t any different for about three hundred of us who are spending our last moment together today. Irrespective of the urge to maintain contact with one another, there is a general trepidation that many of us may never cross one another’s path again.

So it’s true after all – there is a time for everything, and a season for every activity under heaven, a time to embrace and a time to refrain. God! Is there no happier way to end this business? Whether it’s the hate of a broken fraternity, or the love of my alma mater, I do not know. But I suddenly feel like spending a little more time in this place I once desired so badly to leave.

My outstanding performance in the lower category of the science subjects, integrated science – a combined elementary study of physics, chemistry and biology – earned me the nickname Rhizopus. But in spite of achieving that feat, I opted for the arts at that time, a decision that baffled Mr. Newton Obiloh, who also happened to be my integrated science teacher. It was my father’s decision that I read the arts, in order that I may study religion in the university, for he wanted me to become a priest, although I had hinted him of my ambition to read medicine.

Mr. Newton Obiloh intervened and urged me to continue with the sciences and especially encouraged me towards the fulfilment of my aspiration to become a medical doctor. He also told me, citing examples, that I could still be a clergyman in the future after my training in the medical profession. And so I continued in the sciences. I’m afraid what my father’s reaction will be if he hears that I’m still bent on pursuing that career.

When I learnt about how he influenced my appointment as the school’s chapel prefect in my penultimate year (when the school’s prefects were selected), I ceased to underestimate his unwavering resolve to have me become a priest. He did that, I believe, in order to induce me towards the supposed future vocation, which he believed God has called me to do. But instead it reinforced my decision not to join him in the clergy.

The ceremony will commence at nine o’clock. Mr. Newton, the moderator, had rejected two previous drafts of my valedictory speech, which I tendered for review. He insisted that I mention my future ambition while delivering my speech as a matter of tradition, which is the reason I’m apprehensive about my father’s visit. I deliberately censored that information in the two previous drafts for obvious reason.

Walking past arriving guests and exchanging pleasantries with students, I leave the hall to meet him and show him a third draft for approval. The hall overlooks the assembly ground, an open field where we usually assemble every Monday to Friday at eight o’clock in the morning for daily devotions and receive routine instructions and announcements after which we disperse to our classes. Not without the recitation of the National Anthem though. Adjacent to the hall, at the northern fringe of the assembly ground is a one-storey building, which in comparison to the main administrative block built in nineteen twenty-five, is referred to as the most modern building in the school. It houses the physics and biology laboratories on the first floor, while the chemistry laboratory and the music room are on the ground floor. The porch in front of the music room, accessed by a flight of stairs, serves as a podium during the morning assembly.

Standing at the porch, I peek through dusty windowpanes into the music room. Mr. Newton is still in a meeting with two new Youth Corps members on primary assignment in the school – Miss Imabong Duke who teaches English language and one of the mathematics teachers, Junior Olajide, whatever his father’s name is, we call him funky Jay. He is left-handed and always has his right hand in his pocket. Mr. Newton himself was also posted to the school for the one-year National Youth Service Corps primary assignment seven years ago, but after his assignment he was employed as a permanent staff.

He is twenty-nine years old and has an MBA, which he hardly shows off. I was of the opinion that Mr. Newton was either so ill-fated he couldn’t land a job in a corporate firm, too lazy to do a meaningful business, or just simply confused about his prospects. But I was wrong. He finds fulfilment in his job as a schoolteacher, and especially as guidance counsellor. Students and fellow teachers alike admire his candour and kindness, and he’s well respected by parents for his judgment and selfless effort to see to the welfare of his wards.

He has distinguished himself in the field to become the most valuable staff in the school and he’s also the most highly paid staff in the school. And his colleagues overtly envy him. Apart from his statutory salary as a secondary school teacher, he earns an extra wage as the special assistant to the commissioner of education. He has the aspiration to acquire a doctorate degree and someday become a university lecturer. Although we do not share like ambitions, Mr. Newton is my role model, and friend.

When he signals me to give him a few more minutes to conclude his meeting with the duo, I turn around and stare blankly at cars turning into the assembly ground, which will serve as parking lot for visitors’ cars. On this porch I once stood and addressed fellow students as a chapel prefect, I recall.
Then I delve into memory.

I’ll never forget the circumstance that brought us together, Mr. Newton and I. We actually started off as enemies. Then the military government passed a decree introducing the self-defeating 6-3-3-4 system of education, the scheme recommends a six-year duration for primary education, three-year junior and senior secondary respectively and four-year tertiary education, consequently extending our secondary school tenure by three hundred and sixty-five days barring a leap year.

The scheme, according to them was adopted to arrest the rapidly declining standard of education. The experiment was based on the assumption that issuing a junior secondary school certificate to technically-trained, less-academic pupils who wish to quit school after three years would encourage them to pursue that level of education and escape illiteracy.

Although the scheme has since been fully implemented nationwide, because of partial execution, its demerits still outweigh the merits. The scheme occasioned the proliferation of secondary schools run by so-called educationists, whose interest is purely capitalist, who churn out tons of underdeveloped graduates. Moreover, the poor facilities in very few tertiary institutions became grossly inadequate to cater for these teeming secondary school graduates. There was no reduction in the duration of tertiary education in compliance with its objective. Lastly, the junior secondary school certificate, if it were ever awarded, would be of no consequence because of inadequate training of students who wish to terminate their career pursuit at that level.

When the scheme was first announced in nineteen eighty-four, I was twelve years old and in class two in the junior secondary school. Although states of the federation had the option to either implement the scheme the next year or adopt it in nineteen-eighty six, Mr. Newton, who had newly been appointed secretary to the Anambra state Commissioner of Education, appeared to be the most disturbed about the trend of decline in secondary school enrolment. Representing the state Commissioner at the Delegates’ Conference to evaluate the scheme, he exuberantly endorsed the prompt implementation of the scheme in the state in nineteen eighty-five ahead of many other states without due consultations with his boss.

His verbal explanation to his hasty decision was that the current trend portends a state of mass illiteracy inimical to our status as a developing nation if immediate steps are not taken to arrest it. He was also quoted as saying that anyone who is against that view is against the progress of the nation. The commissioner considered his remark an affront and threatened to relieve him of his duty as his secretary.

Although Mr. Newton eventually weathered that tide, a major trouble was stirred by his colleagues, who obviously envying his rising profile, petitioned him to the principal demanding his resignation for his rash decision that resulted then in the rumoured exit of nearly half of the students in the third class in the junior secondary cadre, my set. We were the guinea pigs of that experiment. Despite repeated warnings by the school authority to expel any student caught in the transfer deal, many more students were joining the bandwagon to leave the school.

When Ifeanyi, my elder brother heard that the scheme had eventually been implemented, he quickly started making arrangements to have me transferred to a secondary school in what he called a friendly zone – a school located in a state where the scheme had not yet been enforced. I shared the idea with a few friends of mine and they were interested, so I spoke to Ifeanyi about them and he agreed to facilitate their migration as well.

Several days after, Mr. Newton summoned me to his office to inform me that my admission into the school had been terminated. At first I denied being involved in the transfer saga. But I was taken aback when he shoved the original registration papers in my face and asked me to name the other students involved. But I refused. Then he threatened to take the matter up and have me expelled from school, and my brother Ifeanyi arrested for financial scam if I refused to comply.

Contrary to Mr. Newton’s concern, it wasn’t the fact of having a promising career aborted prematurely that bothered me, because at that time, I was leaning towards my father’s will to join the clergy. But, I had sympathy for my brother for the trouble I’d gotten him into. I succumbed and named the other students.

I trembled at my brother’s crookedness when Mr. Newton told me the real story, and how he got to know of my transfer. Ifeanyi saw an opportunity in my request to help my friends secure admission and made fast money out of it. At least fifty students were involved in the deal. According to Mr. Newton, each prospective candidate was asked to pay a fee of one hundred Naira to his agent, a fellow JS III student named Phil, for admission letters supposed to have been endorsed by the school principal. They were asked to report in the school for registration as soon as they are done with the junior secondary examination, when the admission was purportedly expected to take effect.

However, he got unlucky when one of the students discovered that the admission letters were phoney. The victim reported the case to the police and his unsuspecting agent was arrested and detained. The police could not trace Abraham Alfred, the name Ifeanyi had adopted for his shady business. It wasn’t until Mr. Newton investigated the matter privately that he discovered that I had been genuinely offered admission into the school. He also identified the elusive Abraham Alfred as Ifeanyi Obiajulu whose name appears in the document as my guardian.

“Can I have a look at the draft?” Mr Newton’s fruity voice startles me from my thought. When I turn around he is standing behind me. I give him the sheet of paper and watch him peruse the draft. We’ve been through this drill twice in his office yesterday.

As a form of personal protest after the change of school saga, I swore never to attend any of his classes and gradually developed a strong aversion for biology, which consequently began to affect my performance in the subject. Having tutored me in integrated science, he quickly noticed that my weak performance was unnatural, so he invited me to his office for counselling. That was when I bared my heart to Mr. Newton about the problem I was having with my father concerning my career choice. I also told him the reason for my passion for medicine. He strongly encouraged me to pursue my dream. And from that moment we became best of friends.

He had once suggested coming over to my house to talk things over with my father and convince him about my prospects in the field of medicine, but I deterred him. Knowing how fanatical a man Reverend Joseph Obiajulu is, it would only have added fuel to the fire, and possibly led to my withdrawal from school, which would have been a nightmarish scenario.

He looks up from reading the draft with a smile that shows it is approved. Unlike the previous drafts, I mention here that I would have lost the privilege of delivering this speech if it were not for Mr. Newton. A noble, sensitive teacher like him is worth the honour of being mentioned as the one who gave me the privilege to be myself.
“Excellent, very well written,” he grins satisfactorily and hands me the paper. “Let me be the first to say congratulations Dr. Ikenna Obiajulu.” We shake hands.
“Thank you Mr. Newton. I owe it to you.”
We stand there as seconds tick away totally bereft of words turning our faces away from one another. It suddenly dawns on me how much I’ll miss Mr. Newton.
“Is he coming?” he asks me breaking the silence.
“Yes. The entire family will be here,” I reply with a smile that smothers my anxiety. A film of tear blurs my view as I turn towards the hall.
“So, have you resolved the issue with him?”
“Not yet,” I reply turning to him again.
“Not yet?” He bellows and shakes his head, “Do you want me to talk to him, Ikenna?”
“No…I want him to hear it like this.”
“I don’t think that’s a good idea.”

I’m sure he’ll see reasons with me if I tell him the basis of my father’s decision. He’ll also consider it ridiculous that his position bothers on his personal beliefs. “This is the only way I can make him understand.”
He shrugs his shoulders and takes me by my hand. We walk down the stairs to the hall.

chapters.title: Chapter ONE
content: My passion for the medical sciences is sustained by the photograph of Elizabeth, my elder sister. The photograph was taken when she turned five, but she died at the age of seven. I was only three years old and so apart from this photograph I wouldn’t know what she really looked like.

It’s obvious I take much after her. She had slightly protruding cheekbones, rounded chin and prominent fat lips, and a pair of strong, bright eyes staring at me from the photograph. But in contrast to my sturdy neck, hers is long and slender. I think she’d have grown up to be as tall as I am. Lizzy, as she’s fondly called, would have become the most beautiful woman in the world if she had survived the sickness that claimed her life, if my father hadn’t acted so presumptuously. I hope I’ll find it in my heart to forgive him.

This photograph is perhaps the only thing that keeps her memory alive. It’s only a matter of time, when the university entrance exams results are released, then I’ll have the opportunity to pacify myself of our estranged love. I promised Elizabeth, posthumously – I’ll find a cure for the sickness that claimed her life.

My infant memory was too premature to retain the short intimate relationship we had, but my mum told me they were distressed at the strange attachment between Elizabeth and me. She was born two years after Nkeonyere. But she began to exhibit abnormal social traits at year one – she refrained from playing with her peers and was almost completely lost in uncertain thoughts as if she was hallucinating – traits that were unusual of a child of her age. After a while it was compounded by an initial bout of fever that robbed her of her speech, and afterwards she suffered incessant ailments that eventually made her withdrawn.

My birth was the most amazing thing that ever happened to her. Lizzy never showed signs that she could talk fluently until I was born. It was a pleasant surprise for the family. She’d cuddle me in her arms and wouldn’t let anyone else take me from her. When I was weaned, Lizzy would be the one to feed me and that would be the time for her to eat too. She wouldn’t go to sleep unless she lay next to me. My mum said she could spend a whole day talking with me and I would babble in return as if there was a real conversation going on between us.

They thought I brought with me a healing virtue that soothed Elizabeth’s ailment, and like a genie I transcended into her lonely world and released her captive soul from the darkness that enshrouded it. But that excitement was short-lived.

When Lizzy died, I went into hysterics. I became sickly and refused to eat for days because I had become used to having my meals with her. I would call for her to come, but she wouldn’t answer. I would toddle behind the curtains, and behind the doors and search under the bed – the places where we used to play, but I didn’t find Lizzy. She was no more. How could they tell me that I wouldn’t see her again, that she was dead?

I refused to talk or play with anyone else. My parents considered my grief over her death strange, but when at the age of six I fell ill, they became unsettled – it was the same symptoms Elizabeth manifested before she died. My parents thought her spirit had possessed me. My mother confessed she almost suggested to my father the need for me to see a doctor, but she couldn’t for she’d be accursed for denying her faith in the healing power of God. I was left at the mercy of God.

And God indeed had mercy and answered my father’s prayer and healed me. After ten days of excruciating pain in my joints, high fever, diarrhea, etc. he took the petition to the throne of God, and for seven days he locked himself inside the room interceding and praying to God to heal me. He refused to come out until my mum brought the news to him that the sickness had left me.
When he came out of the room, he announced to her that he had made a vow to offer me to God for His service as a priest as soon as I’m eighteen if He will have mercy and heal me.
“Let his will be done!” my mother responded.

Eleven years after, there is confusion about whose responsibility it is to enforce this will, God, or Venerable Joseph Chukwudike Obiajulu. It’s Elizabeth’s voice, which I hear in my spirit, the pain of her suffering, which I feel in my soul and the burden of love for her that inspires my passion to find a cure for the disease that claimed her life, and nobody can stop me, not my father, not even the anxiety of the consequence of breaking his vow will make me change my mind.

Putting back the photograph into the portion of my Bible where I’ve been saving it since I took it from the family album when I was twelve, I rise from bed to go search for what to eat. I’m famished. It’s five minutes to nine by the clock on the bedside drawer. Breakfast ought to be ready now.

Ever since the valedictory ceremony, the mood in the house has not been cordial. With the exception of Ifeanyi, who has been nonchalant about the episode at the valedictory service, everybody criticized me for announcing to the family my ambition to read medicine the way I did. My father was so mad he furiously stomped out of the hall and into his car leaving Ifeanyi and Nkeonyere behind.

My remorse for what happened at my graduation ceremony has petered out, and I’m beginning to resent my father when I think of the fact that he obviously would have employed all means available to make me drop the idea of studying medicine if he knew how seriously I desire it. He probably would have stopped me altogether from sitting for the university entrance exams.
The dining table is bare, there’s no sign of early breakfast today. Nkeonyere is busy sweeping the parlour. I quietly shut the door behind me and stride towards the kitchen.

As I move nearer to the kitchen and hear my mother’s voice humming a tune, it dawns on me why there won’t be an early breakfast today – the Bishop of the diocese is on a routine visit to my father’s parish. He and his wife have been invited to have breakfast with us.

My mother is such a good cook and she’s particularly delighted to have guests come around for dinner. I suspect she feels glad when her guests relish her food and tell her thank you. Typical of her, she is doing the cooking herself while Nkeonyere is cleaning and dusting the house.

Nobody has bothered me with house chores for a while. They seldom talk to me these days. Ifeanyi will not be around, he left the house before sunrise, said he has an important appointment. Strange fellow, he doesn’t like priests. He fled as soon as he got wind of the Bishop’s visit.

She breaks her song and takes an angry glance at me as if I don’t deserve to enjoy her melody. Although I can’t make out what she’s preparing for the feast from the array of vegetables, fish, bread, eggs, boiled potatoes, assorted fruits, the aroma of fried beef sausage informs me to be patient. I resist the temptation of spoiling my appetite and close the fridge.
“Ikenna,” she calls me as I turn around to leave the kitchen.
“Yes mum.”
“Was that exhibition at your graduation ceremony supposed to be a surprise or simply lack of manners?”
I hesitate for a while. I can’t hear Nkeonyere sweeping anymore – she’s broken her chore.
“Mum, you know Daddy will dissuade me if he knew I was serious about my ambition to read medicine.”
“And you think that was the best way to get his consent?”
We both stare at each other. I blush.
“That was very unreasonable of you,” she continues.

My statement at the valedictory service was merely for his information. If he could restrain himself from getting medical aid for Elizabeth, I didn’t expect anything less in this case. My father’s refusal to take her to the hospital for medication contributed to her death although he claims it’s the will of God. He is a strict adherent of the doctrine of divine healing and shuns the use of drugs and would rather die of a disease than go to a clinic. As a matter of fact we were all born at home. Not because he couldn’t afford the maternity bills, but an expression of his faith in God.
She takes another stick of carrot and starts dicing it.
“I asked a question during our devotion this morning. You and Dad ignored me…what is our responsibility as Christians to the unbeliever who is sick?”
My question has obviously taken the wind out of her sails. I can sense her anger and frustration from the unsteady rhythm of the sound from the knife on the cutting board.
She picks another carrot from the bowl and the pitch of her cutting rises.
“I guess I’ll ask the Bishop at the table since none of you have any idea,” I add.
The rhythm of the cutting slows and then she stops and glares at me.

“I was made to believe the right thing to do is to stand aloof and watch people die because they do not agree with my beliefs,” I continue, “I don’t think I can do that. Not now that I know that God has given me a gift to help them live.”
“Will you shut up!” she snarls at me, “How dare you arrogate to yourself the right to give life to another.”
“Mum, I believe that God does heal, but –”
“Your gift is not from God neither do you believe in the Scriptures you ungrateful infidel.” Her eyes blaze with anger.

Nkeonyere enters the kitchen pretending to be alarmed by her outburst, but I know she had been listening all the while. She’s been silent about what happened at the valedictory service. I’m not surprised anyway because I don’t expect her to oppose my father’s decision.

But I’m still eager to make my point clear, so I continue, “Jesus tells us a parable in the Scriptures about a man who was travelling from Jerusalem to Jericho and unfortunately fell into the hands of robbers. They beat him thoroughly and left him half dead. A priest came by and simply passed by the other way, a Levite came and did the same.”
She picks another carrot from the bowl. Nkeonyere squints at me.

I continue, “It was a Samaritan who took care of him. And guess where he took him…to a hospital. Although he was an infidel, Jesus commended him for saving a man’s life. And commands us to go and do likewise to our neighbour.”
“Are you challenging your father’s authority on the Scriptures Ikenna?” she grunts.
“No I’m not. I am challenging the right he has to decide my destiny.”

Nkeonyere perceives the odour of burning food and rushes to the gas cooker at the cracking sound. She turns off the flame and takes the pot away. In the silence, she darts her eyes at me and then at Mum not knowing what to say. Mum looks up at me. Her eyes are glazed with tears.
“You didn’t try to find out why Elizabeth died, did you?” I ask her.
“Don’t tell me you’ve been to a hospital Ikenna,” she trembles.
“Yes I have.”
She startles and gazes at me. “I am in total support of whatever decision your father takes on this matter. And don’t ever ask me to beg him on your behalf.”
I glance at her then at Nkeonyere and leave them both in the kitchen bewildered.

My squatting in Nkeonyere’s room inconveniences her, but she barely accommodates me and hopes that Ifeanyi goes back to Jos soon so she can have her privacy. I moved in to stay with her since Ifeanyi came back home. Usually, I sleep with the lights on, but I had to adjust because she finds it irksome.

Nkeonyere is fast asleep and she’s backing the lamp on the bedside drawer that is between the two beds. The recent trouble in the family has caused me sleepless nights, in addition to nights with the lights out I’ve been tending to insomnia. Lying on my back with my hands behind my head, I gaze blankly at the ceiling.
“What’s eating you up I.K.?” she asks.
I do not respond.
“If you don’t mind please turn the light off?”
“I’m sorry I didn’t know you’re awake.” I turn and switch the light off and I peer at the ceiling in the dark.

After a while she put on the light, rises from the bed and reels to the door drowsily, leaving the room. The toilet door squeaks. The clock on the drawer beside the lamp shows the time is twelve past five. I doubt if I can still catch some sleep before Mum comes banging on the door to call us up for morning devotion at six-o’clock. Nkeonyere comes back into the room and slumps on the bed. I feel her eyes on me. I turn towards the direction of the window backing her.
“What’s the matter with you I.K.?” she sighs.
“Nothing,” I croak.

She has left me in the lurch since this trouble started despite the fact she’s the only one who is capable of making my father see reasons with me. He dotes over Nkeonyere and hardly refuses her a thing. That’s because, of the three of us she’s the one who obeys, in the name of God, the instructions of the man of God, to the letter. To some extent, I am submissive to my father, but Ifeanyi is the worst – he resents the autocratic tendencies of priests and makes my father as miserable as he can by deliberately doing the exact opposite of whatever he tells him.
Her bed squeaks and then she flips the pages of the Book. “You know Daddy may disown you if you are bent on pursuing a medical career.”
“I know,” I reply turning back to face her. “He put it politely – that he won’t have anything to do with me. That’s a way of excusing himself from his vow I guess.”
“How are you going to handle the situation?” she asks me sitting at the edge of her bed with the Bible in her laps.
“I don’t know. But I’ll never be a priest and I don’t mind if he disowns me.” I rise and sit at the edge of the bed facing her, “Nkay, do you really believe his insistence is based on that vow?”
“I don’t know I.K.,” Nkeonyere hesitates and drops the Bible on top of the drawer, “But I think he’s afraid something bad may happen to you in the future if you fail to keep the vow.”
“He made the foolish vow under duress,” I snarl at her raising my voice.
“Ssh!” she hushes me.

The creaky door to Ifeanyi's room is opened. He’s up to it again. Ifeanyi goes to the loo a thousand times every night – the awful consequence of much indulgence in alcohol. The toilet door squeaks and squeaks forever from his usual habit of not locking the door when he’s making use of the toilet. Then comes the sound like rain falling into a half-filled basin. Nearly half of the fluid is spilled on the floor.

Initially, I flatly refused him when he asked me to move in with Nkeonyere because he didn’t like the idea of being denied his privacy. But later on I was forced to leave the room for him when I couldn’t put up with the horrible effects of his excessive drinking. It didn’t take long before the not-too-ventilated room became contaminated with the stale odour of a combination of alcohol and tobacco.
“How can you say a thing like that, it’s his prayer that kept you alive.”
“It’s because of Elizabeth I’m alive, not because of his vow. Besides, I can take consent for my actions. I have the right to excuse him from the vow and he knows that.”
The toilet door squeaks again this time he remembers to close it. But he never flushes the toilet early in the morning. His reason is that he doesn’t want to disturb anyone with his nocturnal ritual.
“You know why he’s particularly against my decision to study medicine?”
“What if I suggest to him that you study another course in the university instead of medicine?” she says looking towards the door.
Incidentally, there is a knock at the door. The time is twenty minutes to six. It’s not yet time for the family devotion. Nkeonyere stands to answer the door. Ifeanyi enters.
“Boy, how are you?” he greets me in his usual style.
“I’m fine. Good morning.”
“There’s nothing good about the morning. But it’s okay, we’ll sort it out uh,” he replies with a grin.

Sort it out! With Nkeonyere? She and Ifeanyi seldom agree on issues because of what she refers to as his weird perception of life. They hardly talk. And whenever they do, he puts her in a strop. His uncanny approach to situations usually appears awkward or impossible at first, but he eventually comes through with it.
“That wasn’t an honourable way of letting your father know your future ambition. I think you should have talked to him man to man,” he says.
“If that’s what you got to say, I’m going to bed. You know he won’t give me the chance,” I reply.
“Boy, listen to me…you’re growing up to be a man. In the real world, men don’t always get what they want all the time no matter how good their ideas may sound.”
I heave.
“Sometimes it becomes necessary to defer our expectations for an appropriate time,” he says smiling, “He who fights and runs away lives to fight another day. I’m sure you’ve heard that?”
“If you’re here to persuade me to follow his decision, I won’t.”
He shuffles to Nkeonyere’s bed, while she moves to accommodate him.
“You put up your cowardice like a lamppost the day you celebrated your victory. That’s the problem with very academic people,” he says with a patronizing smile.
“I’m not a coward!” I snarl at him.
“I didn’t see that,” he replies, “Anyway, that’s not why I’m here.”

Naturally, Ifeanyi is academically deficient and gets petulant about academic discussions that tend to betray his weakness. He’s been insouciant about academics right from our childhood days. After his expulsion from secondary school, he went to stay with Uncle Dike in Jos and turned streetwise. He takes pride in his practical approach to issues of life and denigrates my father’s biblical injunctions.
Nkeonyere peeks at the clock and glances at Ifeanyi.
“By the way, I’m neither a disciple of the Bishop nor am I on his side in this matter. I guess I’m entitled to professional medical services lest I die of a disease.” He glances at Nkeonyere.
She doesn’t like that, I know. She shares my father’s opinion when it comes to seeking medical advice.
“I relish the thought of retaining a trusted hand to take care of my health,” he continues, “I have a feeling I’m gonna live forever when I think of my own flesh and blood as my personal physician.”
Nkeonyere’s face falls.

Ifeanyi enjoys scoffing at the gospel of the kingdom of God, especially the doctrine of eternal life through faith in Jesus. He dismisses the story of His immaculate birth as an illogical myth that is of no consequence, and derides His chastity saying that Jesus either had secret love affair with the ladies that followed him or He was a fag. Ifeanyi writes off the existence of a future place of eternal punishment such as hell fire or the pleasure of paradise.
“Boy we’re in support of your medical career,” he says smiling.
I don’t blame Ifeanyi for his initial indifference about discussing my face off with my father. He’s already fingered to be behind my rebellious feat.

“I called the university to enquire about the requirements for admission into the college of medicine,” Nkeonyere takes up from him, “Your score is two points lower than the cut-off point for medicine,” she says in a tone of disappointment.
I slump on the bed with my hands behind my head. I can’t believe it.
“I suggested to Ifeanyi you take the option of reading a related course like biochemistry. Although I didn’t make enquiries about the cut-off point, it’s usually not as high as that of medicine.”
“What does a biochemist do?”
“In a nutshell, biochemistry is the study of the chemistry of plants and animals,” Ifeanyi answers.
“The chemistry of plants and animals,” I ponder.

“The advantage is, you can switch to medicine after one year if you hit a good GPA. I know you can make it,” she continues, “Besides, I’m sure Daddy will relax if he hears you’re going for biochemistry. Like I said, I can make him do that.”
“That may not be necessary Nkay,” Ifeanyi cuts in, “Ol’man is only putting up a front. He wants the poor guy to be a goddamn sleazy priest like him.”
Nkeonyere clicks her tongue. It exasperates her when Ifeanyi speaks of our father like that.
“As if you don’t know him, he’ll find a reason to decline.”
“Excuse me! Dad is not like that,” she snarls.
Ifeanyi peeks at her and then stares at me. “Boy, we’re leaving tomorrow.”
“Leaving! To where?” I ask.
“Jos,” he replies curtly.
“Wait a minute Ifeanyi,” Nkeonyere demands raising her eyebrows, “We agreed that he won’t stay in Uncle Dike’s house, didn’t we?”
“If there’s an alternative, remember?” he asks curtly. “That’s the only place he can stay for now.”
“Is this what you had in mind?” she replies, “taking I.K. along with you?”

My father and his elder brother, Uncle Dike, haven’t been in good terms for donkey’s years. When Nkeonyere got admission into the Metropolitan University Jos, she was strictly warned by my father never to ask him for anything nor visit his house for any reason. Ifeanyi has been living with Uncle Dike ever since his expulsion from secondary school.
“You know how Daddy will feel if he finds out,” she protests,
“Oh yeah, do you know how I feel when I imagine my brother clad in a sleazy cassock. If you let him know about this, Nkay…”
Needless to say, she won’t. Nkay fears him. No one dares Ifeanyi.
“Boy, get ready. We’re leaving tomorrow morning.” he says with finality and a grin on his face. Then he rises to his feet and leisurely walks to the door leaving the room.
I feel goose pimples all over.
In the uneasy silence Ifeanyi left behind, Nkeonyere and I stare at each other forever.

chapters.title: Chapter TWO
content: Because of my phobia for flying, we opted to travel by land. The motor park is an awkward assemblage of all sorts of vehicles – saloon and estate cars, minivans, minibuses and coaches. It is situated at the foot of a cloverleaf bridge connecting four highways. One of the highways links the Niger Bridge, which spans across the River Niger, the only route in and out of the eastern part of the country westward.
A few inter-city commercialised motorcyclists popularly called okada are up early to take passengers to every nook and cranny of the town.

Arriving the motor park at five-thirty, a beehive of activity is already underway. But in spite of the wooden boards on the rooftop of loading vehicles indicating their destinations, the park is perpetually kept noisy by persistent shouting of numerous touts who call out the designated destinations to prospective commuters. However, their voice is doused by loud music from hawkers of pirated audiotapes.

A few winos are sitting at roadside tables where local gin and adulterated whisky are sold, nodding their heads in relish of the various types of music supplied by the cassette hawkers as they pass by.

A couple of destitutes are bathing near a large drainage ditch at the middle of the highway just in time before daybreak ignoring desperate bread hawkers who are receiving supplies from a bread van parked directly beside them.

The Upper Iweka Park, unorganised as it appears, is the largest motor park in the country. And that is because businessmen and women come from virtually all the states of the federation to do business in the gigantic Onitsha main market. Consequently, vehicles travel from here to nearly all the major towns and cities nationwide.

As we walk to a section of the park to board a vehicle going to Jos, Ifeanyi draws my attention to another table with loaves of bread, sachets of noodles, crates of eggs, plastic cups and assorted beverages – tins of cocoa drink and milk, packets of tea bag. Ifeanyi says they’re called Maishai in Hausa language, which means, tea-seller.

I watch the man with fascination as he dexterously pours tea from one plastic cup into another raising it gradually to full arms span. He does it several times to cool the tea before serving his customer waiting patiently at the table.

Our preferred vehicle is the high-roof, forty-nine-seater Mercedes Benz 0371 bus. After obtaining our tickets, we board the bus. As a matter of principle, there is no scheduled time of departure – the bus will leave when the forty-nine seats are completely taken.

Walking down the long aisle in the middle of the bus that sets off the fours seats in a row in pairs, we look for a pair of seat on same side so we can sit together, but there’s none. The bus is nearly filled with seated passengers. Happily, a kind passenger sitting by the window realizes our predicament and stands up from a seat around the middle area of the bus and beckons us to occupy the two seats. He relocates and sits with a woman occupying the seat beside us. I thank him for being so considerate, he responds with a smile. Ifeanyi chuckles and whispers to me that he’s not a passenger. He’s a tout.

The strategy of using touts who sit in the buses pretending to be travellers in order to deceive unsuspecting, impatient travellers to board their vehicle was adopted to meet the fierce competition for patronage among the transport companies.

When I take a second look at the man, I notice he’s cleaning his mouth with a chewing stick and he’s putting on rubber slippers. He takes the stick out of his mouth and smiles at me I shake my head and turn my face away.

Passengers arrive in trickles, and with each boarding a tout leaves the bus. Not without a curse from the exhausted passengers who arrived earlier. The woman sitting by the window left a while ago. With the entry of another passenger, the man who offered his seat to us stands up for him.
I glare at him. But apparently unperturbed by my feeling, he smiles at me as if to say he fooled me and croaks ije oma, which means safe journey in my dialect.
“Get lost!” I mutter under my breath.

The park is getting busier by the minute as the morning dawns. Stream of hawkers with assorted items and wares for sale scream out to travellers for patronage. A procession of disabled people – the blind, the lame, the deaf and dumb march from bus to bus pestering passengers for alms.

For the screaming touts, the dawn has brought an unusual excitement to the task of calling for passengers. They lawlessly besiege passers-by and struggle among themselves for their bags, pulling them by the arm in a bid to force them to enter their vehicle. In addition to their noisy croak, they have also taken to ringing bells to alert prospective travellers.

Desperate cassette hawkers are not left out of the excitement as they take the volume of their music sets to the max in order to surpass the din of the loading touts mingled with the indiscriminate honking from a swelling traffic bringing the chaos at the park to a crescendo.

Fortunately, we are salvaged from the nuisance two hours, forty minutes after we arrived the jumbled motor park. Our bus is ushered out of the loading pit with loud jubilation from the touts who loaded the bus. They celebrate their feat with loud screaming and punching on the bus. Initially moving at a slow pace because of a traffic jam caused by reckless driving, the traffic decongests as we approach the Niger Bridge. I bid us bon voyage.

I missed my first opportunity to visit the beautiful city of Jos in nineteen eighty-six. My teammates who participated in the nineteen eighty-six version of the Milo annual inter-school basketball competition in Jos never stopped talking about their fascinating experience in the beautiful city set on a plateau. The air trip to Jos was sponsored by the Nestle Food, makers of the branded beverage. My school was the champion in the Eastern region that year, and the Regional Manager was an alumnus of Dennis Memorial Grammar School. But because I’m aero-phobic, I missed the game and because I didn’t want to be scorned by my teammates, I feigned injury. Despite Coach Sebastian’s fervid doubt about my claim, I refused to own up to my fear for airplanes.
Although our school team bungled and was eliminated in the group stages, it didn’t douse the excitement of their trip to the magnificent city.

The feeling is very different now. And the circumstance surrounding the trip is making me have ambivalent feelings that consequent upon my father’s vow, it may not augur well for me. Even though he preaches about it, I doubt if my father will ever forgive me if he hears that I am going to stay with Uncle Dike, especially considering his previous experience with Ifeanyi.

Back in nineteen eighty-four, Ifeanyi sold my father’s landed property to Uncle Dike, without my father’s knowledge. When my father became aware that the title deed was missing, and reported the matter to the police, who investigated the matter and discovered at the lands registry that the certificate of ownership bore the name Dike Obiajulu, my uncle.

Knowing Ifeanyi would have been instrumental in the fraud, my father went to the Saint Andrew’s Seminary where he was schooling to fetch him. But to his amazement, he was told that Ifeanyi had not been in school for nearly eight months. In fact, he had been rusticated from school, with three other students. They were caught smoking Indian hemp in the school premises.

The principal sent letters to the parents of the errant students to notify them of their wards’ misconduct, but that letter never reached my father. Ifeanyi and his friend Paschal Animashaun, also known as Rascal, son of the prominent, charismatic preacher, Pastor Gabriel Animashaun, intercepted the letters written by the principal to their parents. Their escapades reaffirm the age-old debacle of God’s Generals, illustrated by the expression, the bad sons of good men. However, Ifeanyi denied the story when I asked him. He absconded from home when he discovered his deeds had been exposed, and until he came back a few weeks ago, he’d been living with Uncle Dike.

None of us can tell the reason for his coming back home after so many years, but I’m sure it wasn’t to make peace with my father. Surprisingly, whether it was a calculated act or it happened by chance, his persistently unrepentant attitude and incompatible behaviour paved the way for him to finally get my father to consent to ship him abroad. My father was shocked when the Bishop, at the sumptuous feast, hinted him about several petitions to remove my father as presiding priest on the grounds of incompetence. Their petition was on the premise of a Bible passage that says, if a man does not know how to manage his own family, how can he take care of God’s house?

Although the Bishop dismissed their allegation as inconsequential (I’m sure my mother’s savoury meal soothed his soul), he sold the idea of sending Ifeanyi abroad to my father, privately. He was constrained to sacrifice the last piece of land he owned towards the travel expenses in order to redeem his tarnished image as a parish priest.

Since his comeback home, my overbearing father made a motto out of the first epistle of Paul to the Corinthians chapter fifteen, verses thirty-three and thirty-four. He made sure to mention it everyday at our morning devotion. I’ve come to know it by heart.

My mind has been unsettled since Nkeonyere voiced her concern about the likely outcome of excusing myself from the vow my father made to God. Will there really be any consequences? It’s been a long time since he made the vow, eleven years ago precisely. I doubt if God still remembers it. Besides, of what consequence will it be to His kingdom if he loses a weasel like me anyway?
“Boy, you’ve not been listening to me,” Ifeanyi interrupts my thoughts. The intrepid explorer has been doing a commentary on some towns along our route, but I’m too lost in thought to pay attention.
“I uh – I’m sorry,” I mumble.
He raises his hand over my shoulder and pulls me to himself, “It’s okay I.K., everything will be alright you’ll see…trust me.”
I do.

chapters.title: Chapter THREE
content: The orange glow of the setting sun casts a shadow of tall trees planted on each side of the streets and the lush vegetation of fenceless compounds gives the environment an inviting serenity. The government reservation area, or simply called G.R.A., has been, as a matter of tradition, a highbrow residential domain designated to those in authority, statesmen and policymakers, and until recently the well to do in the society.

Typical of such areas, which played host to the British lords before the independence, the streets are dominated by archaic brick houses nearly covered by overgrown ivy plants, relics of the colonial era.

But since the advent of political activities after the liberation of the country, and the exit of its original occupants, the G.R.A. has become synonymous with, not necessarily the class-conscious, but the well heeled who for obvious reasons prefer to pitch their tents here. And for that reason, the newly built-up areas are easily distinguishable from the Victorian architecture of the Britons.

Unlike the section where the Britons lived, the contemporary neighbourhood is virtually bereft of trees, flower gardens and greenery. And in contrast to the short fences that barely separated the vintage houses high fences and iron gates bar unwanted visitors from interfering with the privacy of the occupants of these mansions.

After several right and left turns, the taxi corners off the road into a smaller street with a sign that reads, St. John Close. The close is short and has about fifteen houses. Ifeanyi orders the taxi to stop in front of the house next to the last on the right obscured by an eight-foot-high fence painted white, topped with electric security gauze.

“That’s our bunk,” Ifeanyi tells me as I stare at the impregnable gigantic black gate that conceals the ‘bunk’. Two circular wooden signs are hung on the gate. The number of the building is printed on one, while the other has a hand-painted badly drawn image of a dog, and a warning sign that reads, ‘Beware of dogs’. The gate is firmly locked and the house is not visible from outside.

It’s very cold out here and I’m impatient to get into the house and tuck myself under a blanket. I stand at the gate as Ifeanyi prances to the edge of the fence where the bell is affixed to the wall and presses it exactly twice. It’s been chilly since we alighted from the bus. I think I’m going to have the flu. I wait with arms folded for a response.
A gaunt-looking face instantly shows up from an opening in the gate when Ifeanyi presses the bell switch by the fence. “Who you de fin?” he snarls in pidgin English.
Ifeanyi comes to my rescue and answers the gaunt figure in the Lingua Franca, “Na me Sani abeg open the gate.”

The gaunt face lights up and grins. Nearly all the teeth have gone. The few remaining are stained brown from kola nut addiction, I’m sure. He shut the small hole and pulls several bars noisily to unlock the gate. The dogs tick off at the noisy squeak of iron bars, barking ferociously from a safe distance.
He let us in and welcomes Ifeanyi with a bow, “Saanu Maigida.” A general Hausa greeting that is used to say welcome, how do you do, thank you, and whatever.

The compound is exquisitely landscaped and shielded from the interference of the outside world by the eight-foot high fence. Although the bunk is a bungalow, it is a magnificent architecture flanked by lush floral vegetation. The air is filled with the scent of flowers that adorn the compound. Like the fence the house is finished in white textured weatherproof paint and covered with overlapping brick slates sloping steeply from the centre. The roof is complemented by red casement Gothic windows, and under each casement are window boxes made of baked clay bearing assorted flowers.

The basketball hoop towards the edge of the roof of the house just above the garage put a grin on my face. There is another external garage of a simple fabrication of four sets of two heavy metal pipes creating three parking spaces spread into a triangle upward from its base, all of which are fixed to concrete casts at the base. The metal pipes are finished with red oil coating, and they support the roof made of overlapping brick slates tilted backward from the front. Three vehicles are parked in the garage covered with tarpaulin, one of the vehicles is a four-by-four and the other two are saloon cars.

The lamps at the flank of the recently mown lawn cast its light onto the brick interlocking stones of the front walk. Sani struggles with the heavier bags while I walk behind Ifeanyi to the front door with my duffel bag hung on my shoulder. Ifeanyi rings the bell. We wait for response.

The tiny gateman eventually makes it to the front door. He peeks at Ifeanyi and then glances at me. When our eyes meet, he grins at me as if he just realized that we are brothers and gasps the generic greeting again, “Saanu.”

A dark-skinned figure in short skirt and a tee shirt appears behind the glass door and pulls the curtain. She pops excitedly and turns around and says something to some other creatures in the sitting room before opening the door. The sound of loud reggae music hit my ears.
“Ifeanyi!” she screams and throws her massive figure on my diminutive brother. For a moment I thought they are going to fall. She looks elegantly beautiful in her dark cropped hair.

Two other identically beautiful girls race out of the sitting room with the speed of light shouting, “Ifeanyi! Ifeanyi!!” they yell and pounce on him. This time he is caught off guard and they all go crashing on the marble finished floor of the porch.
Beauty and I watch them in amusement, “It’s okay now ah ah,” she pleads with her sisters who are now tickling Ifeanyi on the floor.
Ifeanyi abandons himself to their tickle and laughs tearfully like a baby. I flush with enthusiasm. Even Sani couldn’t help laughing. I refrain from looking at him.
I glance at Beauty and meet her gaze, “Hi.”
She stops laughing. “Hi. You must be Ikenna?” She stretches her long slender arm for a handshake. I take it. “I’m Julia,” she says with an inviting smile.
“I’m pleased to meet you Julia.”
“Same here,” she replies, “Wow you’re so tall, you play basketball?”
“To keep the height useful, yeah,” I reply.
“Great that makes the two of us,” Julia says excitedly. I can tell from her drenched tee shirt, she just finished training at the makeshift court by the garage.
The puppets are up and giggling, and with some effort, they pull Ifeanyi up. One of them accuses him of having left them for so long. He jokingly apologizes and promises not to ever leave them again.

She threatens him jokingly, “We’ll lock the doors. We’ll drench your wardrobe. We’ll seize your bag and tell Sani to lock the gates and we will not let you leave the house.” Her sister grins mutely at Ifeanyi.
“Alright, alright I won’t go away again I promise,” Ifeanyi replies jokingly dusting his cloth.
“I’m serious,” she warns him.
“Bec- cky,” Ifeanyi raises his eyebrows at her.
Her eyes pop and she covers her mouth, “Sorry,” she says apologetically turning to me. “Good evening.”
“Good evening Becky,” I reply.
“My sisters,” Julia introduces them to me touching their shoulders to point them out, “Rebecca and Anita.”
“Goo- goo- goo,” Anita stutters.
“Good evening Anita how are you?” I bail her out. Jesus Christ! I can’t believe I once wanted to be a stammerer. My mum beat it out of me when she noticed I was mimicking a friend of mine.
“I’m, I’m - fine,” she sputters.

Anita is the cutest of the three sisters. She’s been wearing a constant welcoming smile since we arrived although she doesn’t talk much. She’s fourteen years old but looks rather too big for her age, like Julia she’s dark-skinned. Rebecca is sixteen and fair in complexion, her rich dark hair sharply contrasts her colour and makes her facial features very prominent. These are the only first cousins I have. They look like a cute bunch. I hope my uncle will allow me stay here when Ifeanyi travels.

Julia leads the way into the sitting room. Ifeanyi follows her with Rebecca and Anita on his arms. I help Sani with one of the bags while he brings the other in and drops it by the door. A toothless smile again, then he repeats for the umpteenth time, “Saanu,” and vanishes to the gatehouse.

The sitting room is spacious and tastefully adorned with lush brown Italian leather seats that aesthetically combines with the marble finished floor and a lavishly made floral damask curtains. The window curtains are pulled apart to allow the flowers planted in vases receive the outside light penetrating through the cream colour net curtain with floral designs at its base. An aquarium is built into the cocktail bar, which displays a dazzling array of expensive liquor.

A chest drawer of vintage design is placed in an alcove at the dining section, and an imposing painting of my uncle is hung on the wall directly above the drawer. Although the image is a striking resemblance of my father, unlike him, the gold-framed portrait gives me the impression he’s a hedonist.

Ifeanyi leads the way to the guest room. A large family size bed invitingly made of cream satin bed sheet and covered with a duvet with chequered design greets me. There is a music set and a television on a stand. An armchair is positioned near the wall unit air-conditioner. There are two framed paintings adorning the brilliant white wall. One has the title, Trading Places (an abstract painting of a busy market place in the city, the Terminus market precisely). The other is an untitled painting of a woman coming out from a stream.
Ifeanyi opens the humming fridge between the wardrobe and another door leading to the bathroom and brings out two cans of Becks beer. He hands one to me. “Boy, welcome to Jos.”

He derides my being teetotal and feels convicted and ashamed whenever he is inebriated. Ifeanyi has invited me out to one of his haunts, and at other times offered me a beer, but I usually decline. I take the canned beer from him, but my mind is made up. I’ll not drink it.

Exhausted from the stress of the journey, yet happy to be in this beautiful place, I slump into the chair near the window and kick off my shoes. I pull Ifeanyi’s suit carrier near, unfold it, and then unzip it to empty the contents. There are new shirts and suits covered in Cellophane bags, but they appear too big for his size.
“Boy, what do you think?” he says with a smug smile on his face as if he owns the house.
I look around the room quickly and reply, “It’s a fine place.”
“I’m not talking about the den,” he says chuckling, “How do you see the baffs,” he explains.

Ifeanyi uses a lot of indecipherable slang when conversing that often keeps me in the dark as to what he really means to say. He’d laugh me silly. I don’t have the foggiest clue what the word baff means, and I’m not going to let him break up on me this time.
Still deep in thoughts and watching him with raised eyebrows, he takes another gulp from the can as he waits for my response. What else would he be referring to?
“Ah bros, they’re my cousins,” I reply dramatically, supposing he’s referring to the girls, to Julia precisely.
He bursts into his usual annoying laughter. I goofed again, I know.
“Oh my God!” he exclaims as he gathers himself, “I meant the clothes, they’re for you,” he points at me with the can of beer in his hand.
“These, all of them for –” I’m so excited words fail me. I drop the baffs and hug him. “Thank you bros, thank you.”

“Oh please don’t bother. I don’t know about you boy, I believe the first impression is the only impression people have about you. If you start off looking like a shoe shiner, that’s the way they’ll take you for the rest of your stay in school,” he says and then drains the can.
I stare dumbfounded at him as he turns back to the fridge for another can of beer.
“There are some more shirts and trousers in the other bag,” he says without looking in my direction.

I gaze at the collection of cloth that I took from the bag, which is lying on the bed – two suits, one black, the other navy blue and a few expensive packet-shirts. “Where is your stuff,” I ask not really knowing what to say, “Did you leave home empty handed?”
“Home?” he asks and walks ponderously to me, “This is home.” He taps me on the shoulder and leaves the room.

I scratch my head and return to unloading the bags. There are five pairs of all-wool trousers, four chinos and two pairs of jean trousers in the duffel bag. Reverently, I bring out a pair of beautiful Nike trainers in the bag and observe them with relish.

The wardrobe is inbuilt into the wall, made of polished oak, and runs from the floor to the ceiling. Below the upper compartment with two doors hinged at both ends is a larger section with two sliding doors seven feet high from the floor.

Sliding the door to the right section of the wardrobe, I gawk at a vast collection of trousers and countless number of shirts folded and stacked in the wardrobe. I slide the door back closing it and anxiously move to the other section. Quite a number of suits and jackets are similarly hung in transparent Cellophane bags. There are also some expensive shoes dusty from not being used for a while, and pairs of sandals and two pairs of sneakers carefully arranged on the floor in the wardrobe.
“Jesus Christ!” How on earth did he get all these?

Nkeonyere once hinted that she suspects Ifeanyi’s either an armed bandit or he does illicit trade. Because I’ve spent most of my last six years in the dormitory, I can’t exactly say what he does for a living, but I don’t quite agree with my squeamish sister’s disposition towards Ifeanyi.
“A first impressionist indeed,” I whisper to myself.
“I asked them to prepare chips and plantain,” he announces as he enters the room still having the can of beer. “Is that okay with you?”
“Sure,” I reply curtly, “I hear they grow lots of potatoes here.”

“It’s a staple food here in Jos.” He flips the heater switch on, “I guess you’d like to shower before the meal.” He drops the empty can into the refuse basket by the door, comes to the armchair and takes the can of Becks beer he gave me. “I’ll get them to heat some water in the electric kettle, this could take up to an hour.” He yanks it open as he walks to the door.
“When does Uncle Dike return from work?”
“Well,” he flutters, “He doesn’t live here,” he replies without looking back at me. Then he leaves the room and closes the door gently.

The dining is set on an elevated area in the living room. Julia, Rebecca and Anita are already seated at the table. I stumble to the dining with my eyes dropped under the force of three unfamiliar pairs of eyes staring fixedly at me. The table is a sleek marble work made for a family of eight, three seats on both sides of the table and one each at the shorter ends. Julia is seated at the near end, while Rebecca and Anita sit at the farther side of the table leaving the middle seat between them vacant. I awkwardly squeeze myself into the chair next to Julia, directly facing Rebecca.

Food is served in china bowls with transparent cover – fried plantain and chips served separately, and Chinese vegetable sauce is served in another bowl. Next to the bowl of Chinese sauce is a bottle of Heinz tomato ketchup. I hate ketchup. The plate set before the chair next to me puzzles me, but I smother my surprise affecting a smile at Julia.

She smiles back. Then she glances at her sisters. Their gaze shifts to Ifeanyi as he prances to the dining. Ifeanyi takes the seat at the other end of the table. He turns the plate as soon as he sits down and dishes himself a large chunk of chips. He is idolized, I say to myself.
He passes the bowl to me, but I hand it to Julia.
“You go first,” she says.

Though I’m famished from the journey, I’m shy and self-conscious when it comes to eating in the presence of strangers so I drop a meagre portion of chips and pass the bowl to Julia. I take an equal measure of fried plantain.
“That little?” Julia asks.
Before I can say a word she takes my plate and adds more slices of plantain and then potato chips for me. I smile shyly. She smiles back and comments, “I didn’t see that.”
“What?”
“Don’t tell me you’re shy,” she giggles.
I am, very, very.
“Shouldn’t we say the grace?” I ask Ifeanyi who has already started eating.

He halts and glances at me, then drops his cutlery and shrugs. I close my eyes and bid them to do the same and render a short prayer thanking God for a safe trip to Jos, for providing food for us and for keeping our household including their parents, wherever they are in Jesus name.
They all echo “Amen!”

Rebecca and Anita flinch when I meet their probing eyes. They’ve been staring at me while I was praying, I know. The bowl of sauce almost drops from my hand at another amen from a deep baritone voice behind me. The girls break up.
A hunched diminutive creature sneaks into the table and takes the seat beside me where the funny plate is set. She joins Rebecca and Anita’s laughter. I am really embarrassed.
“Okay it’s enough!” Julia commands them after having her fill of laughter. Rebecca and Anita abruptly cut off their laughter. The eccentric ends hers with an uh huh huh.
“Thanks for the prayer Ikenna,” Rebecca says.
“You’re welcome Becky,” I reply.
“Thanks for the prayer, Ikenna,” the weird one blabs like a four year old.
I turn to her pitifully and smile. My response will be of no consequence so I save my breath.
“Food, food,” she bawls. Then she suddenly starts kicking and banging her fist on the table.

I stare curiously at Ifeanyi for explanation, but he pretends to be too hungry to notice. My eyes meet Rebecca’s stare. There is a mischievous smile on her face. Then I turn to my left again. She kicks the table more fiercely and motions with her fist to be served.
“Food, food,” she continues to demand impatiently.

“Co – co –”Anita bleats shutting her eyes in frustration of the difficulty in expressing herself. She stands on her feet nodding repeatedly as she stutters until she let the words out, “com’on sto – stop it idiot!” she scolds her sharply.

She is broken, starts squalling and eventually stomps out of the table. She’s a little over three feet tall. Ifeanyi hasn’t uttered a word. He meets my puzzled stare, and drops his head to the table picking at his plate. I glare unflinchingly at him. He looks up again.
“Her name is Florence…she’s retarded,” he mumbles.
“How old is she?” I ask.
It is Julia who responds, “She’s fourteen, Anita’s twin sister.”
Anita savours her meal with no trace of remorse for denying her twin sister hers. This must be a regular show at mealtime.
She’s so skinny and pitiful. I suspect she is suffering from a chronological age disorder.
“Excuse me,” I beg the table as I stand up and pick up
my plate.
“Use her plate,” Ifeanyi tells me as I head towards the direction of the room Florence entered, “she won’t eat from yours.”
Florence’s plate is an infant’s cereal plastic plate with a drawing of animal designs inside. It has obviously been around for a while.

“She won’t eat from any other plate,” I mutter to myself. How strange. I transfer the food in my plate into the plastic plate and put a similarly old plastic fork beside it into the plate and leave the table as they all stare at me.

To say that Florence’s room is untidy is an understatement. To describe it in the simplest of words, it is uninhabitable. The window is tightly shut and the air is choked with a nauseating fetid odour possibly from dead rodents and a pungent odour of urine from the bed. The curtains are all torn and the shreds lie on the dust-coated floor. Several broken toys lie around the room and every item in the room is covered with a dense layer of dust that had been gathering over a couple of years perhaps.
She is curled up in bed backing the door sucking on her thumb noisily like a two year old. Irritated by the dust and the nauseating stench, I sneeze.
She’s interrupted. “Food, food,” she demands as soon as she set her eyes on the plate.

I drop the plate on a dusty table beside the bed. She pounces on the plate and eats with a bare unclean hand. I shudder and go over to the window. Applying some effort, I open it to let in some fresh air.

When I return to the dining, Ifeanyi is no longer there. Julia and I take the opportunity to familiarize with one another. She’s affable and she loves basketball, and happily invites me to join her to practise in the morning.

Deep down I know Florence’s condition could improve if she’s first and foremost treated with care. She needs someone to show her love. The way her sisters sound, they care less whatever happens to her. They tactically change the subject whenever I raise the issue of her health, and they evade my queries about their parents and about the family until Rebecca reminds me that I need to relax my nerves after the long journey. Then they bid me goodnight.
He doesn’t twitch when I shut the door. I walk slowly to the bed and slip under the duvet. Ifeanyi is not snoring, so he’s probably not sleeping.
“Ifeanyi, we need to talk,” I say assuming that he’s awake.
“Can’t it wait till tomorrow?” he replies with a sigh.
“I don’t think I can stay here. I’d like to go back tomorrow.”
He turns to face me. I keep looking at the ceiling avoiding his bleary eyes, “Have you made up your mind to become a clergyman?”
I say nothing to him.
“This is only your first night. You’ll get used to them in a matter of days,” he says calmly.
“I’m not sure I can put up with these lunatics,” I mutter.

“Hey! Watch your mouth Mister perfect. These lunatics happen to be your cousins,” he snarls back, “We’ll talk about this in the morning okay. Now go to sleep.” He turns around backing me and flicks the bedside switch. “Good night.”
According to him, there’s nothing good about the night. Yet I respond, “Goodnight.” And blink to unconsciousness.

With the damask curtain pulled apart, the net curtain allows the dull grey of the sky penetrate into the room. The time according to the clock on the wall is barely five-thirty and it is unbelievably already dawn. My elementary knowledge of geography proffers an explanation to the unusually early dawn – the capital city of Plateau state is located on an extensive plateau between one thousand five hundred and one thousand eight hundred meters above sea level. The state derives its name from the terrain. It is the highest inhabitable land mass in Nigeria and it is perennially cold. And because of its proximity to the sky, ice chips sometimes fall from the sky during rainy season, and the sun rises early and sets so late.

The city is still very much asleep, unperturbed by the early light of the day. I’m preoccupied with my habitual early morning contemplation before the busy day. This time it’s not the thought of my sister, Elizabeth, but Florence, Anita’s twin sister. I’m trying to convince myself she could be a good reason for me to stay back after all. Anita virtually said nothing at our discussion at the dining table last night. I’m sure it’s not her speech impediment. There’s a fearful look in her eyes that made me suspect there’s more to her ailment. God! Is there anything I can do to help her?

I recall my days in secondary school, when I was chapel prefect. I remember praying for a fellow student called John Okeke. He used to be a bright student and in fact he came out top ahead of his mates in the junior school certificate examination. But all of a sudden, one day in the dormitory, he began to hallucinate and afterwards talked mostly to himself and became partially mentally retarded. He was promptly withdrawn from school for fear that he may harm other students. No one could help John. His parents were helpless, neither doctors nor psychiatrists could find out what was responsible for his condition. One day in a dream, I saw myself praying for him and as I laid my right hand on him, he became well. It was so real that I actually visited his house the next day.

Because I wasn’t too sure whether what I saw in the dream will come true or not, I took him privately into a room and prayed for him. To the surprise of his parents, John was healed of that condition.

That was a long time ago. I feel like asking God to use me once again to heal Florence, but I feel a weakness in my spirit. And I even fear that I’ll be further separated from Him because, on the face of it, prayer is a strange concept in this seemingly haunted house unlike the humble abode I used to know.

The chime of the wall clock indicates it’s six o’clock, yet the neighbourhood is as quiet as a ghost town. By this time in Onitsha, the morning criers would have succeeded in awakening the entire neighbourhood with loud squawks of unlistened-to sermon blaring from their megaphones demanding of sinners to repent, reminding them of the coming wrath of God and urging everyone to prepare for the coming of Jesus. Not even the cut-throat traders on their way to buy wares, which they will eventually sell at exorbitant prices twitch at their message. Their voices are occasionally doused by taxis and buses honking indiscriminately either to beckon early-risers who chatter noisily on their way to heaven-knows-where, or just for the fun of it.

And by this time, my mother’s banging at the door for the morning devotion would have knocked off the early morning drowsiness. Then we all would cluster around the Bishop, as Ifeanyi calls him, like sheep gathers around their shepherd, for a lengthy rhetoric aimed at keeping us sober in the course of our pilgrimage on earth, after which I return to my room and daydream until I snooze.

The sky is becoming brighter. But there won’t be any morning devotion today, maybe never until I visit Onitsha again. I lost the art of praying on my own, which I once learnt from my sister, Nkeonyere. I can imagine she’d be under fire for being privy to my sudden run-away. But my father will quickly exonerate her involvement in the plan. He hardly finds any fault in her. Ifeanyi, the black sheep will take all the blame as usual.

The fact that barely twelve hours after my arrival to Jos I still don’t have an inkling of the direction I’m headed makes me sick. Ifeanyi’s enigmatic style of doing things makes me sick too. I suddenly cherish the memory of the austere way of life I used to know and I’m homesick. I drift back to sleep.

chapters.title: Chapter FOUR
content: I don’t know whether it is the loud sound of reggae music filtering into the room or the continual tapping of the basketball against the backboard that woke me up at quarter-past-nine. Ifeanyi is no longer in bed, and he’s not in the room either. He is a habitual early-riser.

It is so bright outside the room, but unlike the east, the weather is cool. Julia is playing basketball alone at the makeshift court, the driveway into the garage. She’s kitted in a Chicago Bulls sleeveless jersey and trunks with the number twenty-three boldly printed on it. I imagine playing against her wearing the Houston Rocket number thirty-four.

Julia is nearly as tall as I am, she’s a six-footer. At most two inches less than I am. She’s eighteen, and a few months older than I am, precisely seven months ahead of me. She and Nkeonyere are students at Metropolitan University. Julia is reading English and will be ascending to the second year when school resumes, while Nkeonyere will be going to her third year in Economics.

She straightens up and takes a shot from a spot on the court. Catching the ball on rebound, she chooses another spot and takes another shot. Moving further away slowly, she bounces the ball casually, then hastens her steps and goes for a dunk. I can’t say if she’s shooting the hoops because it’s not visible from here, but she displays fine dribbles and I think she has the feints that’ll upset the best defence. I think she’s good, and disciplined too.

Once I dreamed of playing professional basketball. But I didn’t get the much-needed encouragement so that dream petered out. I still love the game as much as I did way back in nineteen eighty-six when I blew my one and only opportunity that would have made that dream come true. Coincidentally, it was here in Jos. Coach Sebastian never forgave me for evading that game and being responsible for the team’s defeat. That was when I actually started losing interest in basket ball.
Julia told me the Nigeria University Games, NUGA Games, will take place sometime next year and she’s working hard to see that she’s enlisted in the team that’ll represent the school.

My passion is sparked off once again as I watch Julia’s moves. I feel like going out there to do those deft feints, nifty passes and wicked dunks that once thrilled Coach Sebastian and made enthralled spectators scream frenziedly, ‘Go Rhizopus! Go Rhizopus!’
I rush to answer the loud bang on the door. When I open it, Anita is standing and gazing at me like she’s seen a ghost. I think her stuttering embarrasses her.
“Good morning Anita, how are you?” I greet her and wait for the lovely smile of yesterday that accompanied her response.

Her eyes blaze fearfully at me without a speech. She’s instantly reduced to tears. I bend down and look her in the eye. The revelatory quality of Anita’s eyes is striking – they are a perfect complement for her speech impediment. They express what words can’t tell.
I get the impression something terrible has definitely happened. “What’s the matter Anita?” I quiz her.
Tears gather and she starts to weep.
“It’s Florence.”
I look up to the direction of the voice. Rebecca is standing at the passage leading to the sitting room.
“She’s dying,” she trembles.
“Where is she?”
“In her room,” she replies.
Another minute in that infested room! It’s hellish. I fear I’m going to get a bronchial infection.

She’s curled up in the urine-laden bed convulsing. Her face is dry and ashen. Her jaws are ramming together at an alarming frequency. I kneel down beside her at the bed and touch her neck to feel her temperature. She’s cold.
I quickly cover her with a dirty quilt reeking of urine. “Where is Ifeanyi?” I ask Rebecca.
“I don’t know where he’s gone,” Rebecca replies.
Anita comes to the room and stands at the door weeping hysterically.
“Becky please call Julia, we have to take her to a hospital immediately.”
She bolts out of the room.

The windowpane is still open. Although the curtain is not fluttering, I notice that the shreds of curtain fabric that was near the wall are now scattered on the dusty floor of the room. It suddenly dawns on me that I left the window open when I left Florence’s room yesterday night. She’s freezing to death. My knees almost buckle as I walk to the window and shut it.

I remove the tarpaulin covering a blue nineteen eighty-two model Mazda 626 and carry Florence, now wrapped in a neat blanket, into the rear of the car. Julia cuddles her while I take the wheels. Sani opens the gate and off we race, to the hospital.

Mainland hospital is about eight kilometres from St. John Close. There’s a heavy traffic jam caused by a lorry that fell across a section of Murtala Mohammed Way, a major trunk road linking most parts of the city, creating a bottleneck at the point. We are brought to a complete standstill.

Julia excuses herself to try and reach Dr. Nuhu, the family physician, through a payphone. But she returns to the car dejected when the lines refused to connect after several attempts – the perennial poor services of the national telecom company.
Motorists caught in the traffic jam curse whom-it-may-concern for negligence of duty. I learn from an angry motorist that the lorry has been lying there for more than forty-eight hours.

The traffic situation is worsening and the heat from the sunlight is intensifying and I’m perspiring profusely as I try to figure out what to do.
