There must be some other route to the hospital, but I’m a novice in Jos and Julia has no idea either. Okadas carrying passengers meander through stationary cars recklessly honking their high-pitched horns at pedestrians to leave the narrow roadside. It’s not such a good idea, I think, bearing her in one of these to the hospital. If she has pneumonia, which is likely, she’ll be unshielded from the breeze and that will worsen her condition.

“What do we do?” Julia asks for the third time. We’ve been in the traffic jam for ten minutes and it seems like eternity. Her question echoes in my head and my efforts to find an answer to her question makes me sweat the more.

The sound of the rattling of her teeth deafens my ears from the noisy motorists waiting impatiently for another inch movement forward. Afraid of the sight of the convulsing teenager curled up and laying her head on Julia’s laps, I occasionally glance at Julia from the rear-view mirror. Her eyes are bloodshot and her face is red from crying.
What do I do?

I hear shrill sound of a siren coming from behind. When I look up at the mirror, I sight a military convoy approaching and three soldiers carrying submachine guns fiercely tramping towards us ordering vehicles to steer off the road to make way for their convoy to pass. Ironically I squeeze myself into a train of desperate drivers following the military vehicles bumper to bumper. With the help of the military convoy, we are finally able to mingle our way across the bottleneck. Julia directs me to take a left turn. The military convoy speeds away and the wailing siren gradually decrease.

Another right turn finally brings us to Medical Road, a long quiet street that gently slopes downward. The notice at the entrance, No Honking! No Hawking! is strictly adhered to. The Mainland hospital is the last building on the street and the massive complex is visible from here. The rattle of Florence’s teeth has been replaced by an uneasy silence. I look up at the rear-view mirror as the car glides slowly down the slope. Julia’s eyes are shut and she’s shaking her head from side to side. Her face is watery from sweat and tears. She weeps uncontrollably rocking Florence in her arms gently.

I stop the car and turn around to the back seat. Florence is no longer convulsing. Her lips are tightly clenched and her eyes are pallid gazing into space without blinking. She’s grey. My eyes meet Julia’s bloodshot eyes, a film of tears cover my eyes, clots and rolls down my cheeks. I turn on the ignition and steer the car into the hospital premises.
Dr. Nuhu confirms to us that Florence is dead. She died of pneumonia.

After consoling Julia, I put the corpse into the back seat. Julia comes over to the front and we leave the hospital. She suggests we bury her immediately. I object. But she explains that her parents are not around and there’s really no need keeping her in the morgue. Then I consent.
Julia has rallied herself quickly to accept the reality of her sister’s death. She directs me to the suburbs, there we pick a spot and inter the remains of Florence.

Less than twenty-four hours in Jos, I’m already so ill fated. I feel like I’m dragging a ten-ton truck on the wheels, my arms are heavy, my joints are weak and I’m wrecked by the guilt of being responsible for her death. She wouldn’t have died if I hadn’t left the window open yesterday. Maybe it’s a bad idea for me to come to Jos after all. God! But I was only trying to help. Should I confess to Julia? No…maybe later, it’s not just the right time.
The echo of Florence’s deep baritone voice calling, “food, food,” resounds in my head. She’s a casualty of my precarious circumstance. But I was only trying to help. I try to convince myself.

What a strange twist of fate, I have become a victim of the very circumstance that made me abscond from my father – I’m forced to perform a priestly duty, burying my cousin. It’s quite different from the usual funeral service though, there is no choir to sing a dirge at her funeral, and because the burial is impromptu, neither of us thought about a gravestone to mark the spot she’s buried. I doubt, however, if any of her sisters or even Ifeanyi would bother about coming to lay a wreath on her grave. But for the benefit of doubt, I mark the grave with a protruding iron bar.

Florence’s funeral is quite brief. Besides, I’m not conversant with the liturgy of a funeral service. And in this instance, I’m not sure the popular phrase ‘with gratitude to God for a life well spent’ will be appropriate, that would be an obvious pretence. So I say a short prayer, I could barely mutter, God be with you Florence, when I conclude the prayer.
“Amen,” Julia responds limply.

Our journey back to St. John Close is without speech from either of us. My mind is occupied with the thought that this could be the consequence of my rebellion against my father’s will. Julia is seized by the grief of her sister’s demise. She fights back tears taking sharp breaths. Then she finally breaks, “Oh God! Why? Did she deserve to die like this? Why?”

The traffic on Murtala Muhammed Way is still thick. The fallen lorry is still lying across the width of the dual carriageway. For more than forty-eight hours! Such irresponsibility, I wonder. Saint John Close is barely three kilometres from here, but considering the present situation, which fortunately is better than it was on our way to the hospital, I estimate it’d take about forty minutes to get over the bottleneck.
“It’s okay Julia. God has a reason for allowing it to end like this okay,” I say droopily trying to console her.

The traffic situation brings back the memory of Florence. I ponder a portion in the book of Job in my heart. But why should light be given to the weary, and life to those in misery? They long for death and it won’t come. They search for death more eagerly than hidden treasure. Is it not a blessed relief when they finally die? Why is life given to those with no future, to those destined by God to live in distress?
“Pull yourself together Julia you have to be strong for Rebecca and Anita.” I hand her my handkerchief.
“Thank you Ikenna,” she sobs and wipes her face. “I don’t know if I’d have had the courage if you’re not here,” she says as she forces herself to smile.

She doesn’t understand – if I were not here, Florence wouldn’t have died. Things would’ve been different if I had just stuck my butt in my father’s house. I deserve to be punished not thanked for opening the window for death to creep into her sister’s room. This is a bad omen. What do I do? Go home now before it is too late. Go home Ikenna. Go home.

The mood in the house has been grave. Julia’s younger sisters have been weeping. She locked herself up in her room as soon as we returned. I can imagine how devastating it’d be for her to have experienced Florence dying in her very arms. Neither of the girls has eaten any food. I can still feel the touch of her cold pale skin. I can still hear her voice echoing, “food, food”. I have also lost appetite for food.

What a mystery, the value of life, while some are appreciated while they live, others are esteemed in death, and even the stillborn is mourned…what a mystery the value of life. Otherwise, why should the death of a lunatic affect me so much as to take to drinking?

I gulp down the content of another can of Becks beer, stand up from bed, steady myself and lurch my way to the minibar for another chilled can of Becks beer. Then I reel back to the bed and sit up staring bleakly at the television, which has been in the mute mode. The cassette player in the auto reverse mode has been playing, but I’m no longer listening to the music.

I pull the can ring and take a sip and smack at the bittersweet taste of the liquor. I’m having a beer for the first time in my life and I’m already on my fourth. I’m mourning. And I’m trying to douse the guilt conscience I’m having for what happened. I blame the traffic warders for their nonchalant attitude to work. I blame the national telecom company for their ineffective services. “Florence would not have died,” I mutter.

The door opens and Ifeanyi eases into the room. Despite the overlapping blurred multiple images of the wall clock, I’m able to make out the time is approximately ten minutes past eight and it’s already dark outside.

He’s putting on navy blue jacket with a flowery silk shirt overlapping the lapel of the jacket, and a brown corduroy trouser. He obviously left the house in high spirits, but like everyone else, he wears a mournful look. He walks to the fridge for the usual. I try to steady my focus but the multiple images of him keeps going blurry, overlapping each other. I’m also trying to gather my twisted brain to remember the barrage of questions I’ve been rehearsing.
“I want to go home…please.” A wave of nausea sweeps over me and I twitch.

“You said you wanted us to talk,” he says as he picks a can of beer from the fridge. He pulls off the ring and walks ponderously to the bed. When he throws the ring pull into the refuse basket, he stops and scowls at me apparently surprised at the number of empty cans of beer in the basket.
“I feel terrible about what happened to Florence,” I say remorsefully.
“No use crying over spilled milk…we all loved her,” he replies as he comes to the bed.
“Love?” I drawl disdainfully and chuckle, “You couldn’t even give her the milk of human kindness.”

“Hey!” He snaps, “Don’t give me a guilt complex about her death okay. Maybe I didn’t show her as much care as I should have or may be…God! I hate to talk like this,” he says slapping his forehead with his palm shaking his head.
“Maybe what?”
“Maybe it was Sod’s law that I wasn’t around.”

“It wouldn’t have made any difference,” I snap, then I belch and hold myself from throwing up. “Let me tell you what Sod’s law was,” I say drearily, “That I consented to your plan to come to Jos, and let me tell you what the flip side of the story would’ve been. Florence would have been alive if I had listened to Daddy and –”

“Oh shut the hell up, will you,” he cuts in impatiently, “You can go back to your father and become a lousy priest if you like just don’t say anything to insinuate that I’m responsible for what happened to Florence.” He drops the can on the dressing table, removes his jacket, drops it on the bed and sprawls tiredly into the armchair.
“I’m the cause of her death,” I whisper, “That’s what I mean.”

“Because you tried to save her life and you couldn’t? Julia told me everything that happened I.K. The kids also told me about your conversation yesterday…they admire your courage or should I say your compassion.”
“Not until they get to know what really happened to their sister.”
Ifeanyi sips from the can noisily.

I brace myself and explain what happened to Ifeanyi, “Yesterday night when I went to her room to serve her the meal, the stench in the room was unbearable, and it was so stuffy in there so I uh – I opened the window to let in some fresh air,” I pause for a while, “But I forgot to close it before leaving the room…she – she –” I sob.
“It’s okay I.K.”
“That’s how she died Ifeanyi. I killed her, I killed Florence,” I wail quietly.
Ifeanyi leaves the chair and joins me on the bed and consoles me.

“I want to go back home Ify…please.” The can of beer drops from my hand. “I want to leave this place before Uncle Dike finds out I killed his daughter,” I cry. He picks it up and squints fixedly at an invisible object in front of him. How do I convince him I just want to go home? “I want to leave this place before Uncle Dike returns,” I whisper.
“He won’t be around until December.”
My silence is well read.
“He’s serving a five-year jail term. He’ll be due for discharge in three months time.”

My eyes are bleary. My head is spinning and my thoughts are fogged up. But I can understand, he never spoke with Uncle Dike about me. I suddenly don’t feel like sleeping anymore. No need asking what he did, or where he’s jailed. He could go on for hours drivelling about this and I won’t make head or tail from it. This is typical of his style of conversation. Apart from being vague, he never tells me anything until it’s extremely necessary that I must know.
“What did he do?”
Ifeanyi smiles at me as if he was expecting the question, “He’s a lawyer.”
“Well, there are a zillion things that could earn a lawyer a jail term.”
“Like?”
“Like uh – threading on people’s toes you know -” he says evasively.
“What kind of people?”

“Uh – he’s a political lawyer, and unfortunately he’s in the left wing. You know what happens when one group is at loggerheads with another and one of them falls out with somebody who is in authority.”

He’s looking at me eyeball to eyeball and he’s holding the can in his right hand, but I can tell he’s lying. He’s not imprisoned for any political reasons. I suspect illegitimate business and I also suspect Ifeanyi is a racketeer and that he’s involved in his shady business.

Ifeanyi hates to be quizzed, he can’t keep his string of lies under such pressure and he hates much more to be seen as a liar. He’s very conscious of his personality. In spite of his negative moral values, he guards his reputation by all means. He’ll only own up to his lies when his reputation is at stake. The burden of proof is first of all to disorganize his string of lies and half-truths. I leave Uncle Dike’s issue and start with what I already know.
“So you never discussed with him about me?”
“Well…no.”
“And my admission, what are my chances Ifeanyi?”
“I’ve already started making arrangements about that.”
“What kind of plans are you exactly making?”
“Are you now doubting me I.K.?”

It’d be rude to tell him exactly what my opinion is. And going by his commitment towards realizing my goal, he’d take it as an insult. But I have a strong feeling there’s an ulterior motive behind Ifeanyi wanting me to remain in Jos.
“I talked to somebody at the admissions office today. He’s hopeful something positive will happen since they’re still admitting fresh students.”
“Is this why you want me here, to take care of the house while you’re gone?” I ask him changing the subject abruptly.

“Partially,” he says and pauses to think of something intelligent. He obviously didn’t expect the question. “You and Uncle Dike are worlds apart I.K., if you understand what I mean? I thought it’d be easier for you to relate with him if you get to interact with the girls.” He smiles smugly and sips his beer as if he’s finally got his plan in the bag.
“You know I’m not like you.” I’m a jackass nerd, such a clumsy antisocial fellow to compare with Ifeanyi. He has a way of getting in with people. I’m afraid he won’t take me the way he took him.

“Forget it I.K. you’ve barely spent twenty-four hours and you’ve won the heart of the girls. Hey, the girls have a lot of respect for you. They think you’re a hero.” He taps my shoulder and smiles. “Now about Florence, I think he’d be happy it finally happened while he was away.”
“Why do you say so?”
“Do you really think she’d have recovered from that sickness?”
We stare at each other. My faith in the ability of God to correct Florence’s mental age suddenly turns to despair.
“What of Aunty Vera?”

“She left Uncle Dike. A soothsayer told her that he’s a member of a secret cult and fingers him for her mysterious illness. She was also told that he is responsible for her inability to have a male child. She finally left him twelve years ago after surviving a ghastly car accident, which she claimed Uncle Dike set up with mystical powers to offer her soul for ritual sacrifice.”

In contemporary African society, gory stories of ritual murders, activities of secret cult fraternities and voodoo are fast becoming the fancy of idle-minded folks. I’ve heard from many who came to seek counsel or prayers of my father accusing blood relations and neighbours of being responsible for their problems. They allege that they are under witchcraft spells and fetish divinations of such relations.
Unfortunately, many relationships have been ruined by these intangible allegations that pitch family members against one another, and in prolonged cases resulting in series of death.

By my father’s estimation, the intensity of these stories have taken such a heavy toll on the church that many Christians unconsciously overestimate the dark powers behind these diabolic activities, which consequently undermines their faith in God. It has now become the pastime of many base fellows to engage in endless boring and unconstructive conversations about these satanic activities. And the lazy and disillusioned among them have blamed certain members of their family, which they refer to as witches and wizards, for their misery.
I’m not in the mood for such lousy dialogue so I change the subject. “So is this the only thing you’ve been doing in the mean time, managing the house and taking care of his daughters?”
“Well, I also take care of certain of his businesses.”
“But you’re not a lawyer and you’re not a paralegal.”
“Uncle Dike has a lot of other interests apart from the chambers. You’re aware he owns a big pharmacy downtown.”
“What other business is he involved in?”

Ifeanyi explains with emphasis that Uncle Dike is into politics as if academic qualification is required for one to become a politician in this country. Giving our dwindling economic fortunes, politics has now become an occupation for the greedy and the opportunists. Being a politician is synonymous with being a contractor, a consultant and general merchant all in one. At whatever level they’re involved, they devise ways of looting the economy. Sincerely speaking, avarice, not service is the primary motive for going into politics.
I’m not interested in his details and I hate politics. “So what happens to the pharmacy when you leave?” I ask him changing the subject again.

“I have a capable assistant to take care of things until he returns although he doesn’t know about my trip yet. Alternatively, since you are a member of the family, I can introduce you to the staff as the new manager if you don’t mind.”
“Count me out Ifeanyi, I’m not cut out for business, you know that.”
“You’re not liberally minded. Medical doctors still do other things alongside.”
“I know, but I’ve got my life to live.” I’m gradually getting over the muddle in my brain and the questions are turning into probabilities. “Can I ask you something?”
“Anything,” he says with ease.
“Why don’t you want to wait for Uncle Dike to return before travelling?”

Ifeanyi chuckles. “I desperately wanted to travel abroad…that was why I sold that land to Uncle Dike. He promised to help me procure visa and necessary travel documents when I sold the land to him, but since then he’s been giving me one reason or the other why he can’t fulfil his promise and he refused to pay me for the property.” He exhales heavily and continues, “This is the only chance I have to break away from his slavery. I don’t want him to come back here and meet me.”
I squint angrily at him.
“I regret selling the property to him,” he says with a low tone of voice sounding remorseful, “But life must go on,” he says with a cold, cynical tone. “I know what you’re thinking I.K.”
I bet he does.

“I wouldn’t have had any reason to come to your father to ask him to sponsor my trip if not that recently I had some setback financially, but I’m desperate,” he resigns. Ifeanyi always refers to Daddy as ‘your father’ when he’s discussing with Nkeonyere and me. As it is, their differences appear to be irreparable. He resents Dad’s self-opinionated view about issues. I also do. But I can’t get to hate my father as Ifeanyi does. “I don’t want to be around here when Dike comes back, do you understand what I mean?”
I remain silent. I’m not too sure if I’m still with him.

I’ve not met many crooks in my virgin life, but I doubt if I’ll ever come across a more crooked fellow. He is cleverly living out a dual character that has now left me confused about which is his real personality. When he came back home, he gave us the impression he’s a scatterbrain, who probably occasionally forgets his own name when he’s inebriated. But, he’s so organised he could even keep a house and a business running well for five years? How does he do that? Why does he act like one dim-witted dipsomaniac when he’s at home? I ask myself.
“It was Nkeonyere that came up with this plan,” he says shaking the empty can and gazing suspiciously at it as if the content leaked from under.
“Nkeonyere? I don’t believe you.”

“She was worried about your obsession with Elizabeth,” he shrugs and continues, “She was afraid the conflict between you and your father could lead you to do something nasty considering your mental state. She said you needed to get away from the environment…that you needed to get away from Elizabeth.”
She’s the only one who knows about the picture of Elizabeth in my Bible. “So she’s the one who took my Bible out of my bag.”
“No. I did.”
We both stare at each other.
“You must understand I.K. There’s no relationship, no matter how impressive that lasts forever.”
“You have no right to do that,” I snap, “You don’t know what she means to me.”
“She’s our sister too, remember? And we knew her even more than you do.”
“Yeah, just as much as you knew Florence, isn’t it? You don’t have the capacity to feel the pain they lived through…you know nothing about her.”
“Elizabeth was an evil child I.K. she almost took you along with her.”
I have no intention to discuss Elizabeth with him or with anybody else. “Where did you keep my Bible?” I ask him angrily.
“You don’t think I’d bring it all the way to Jos, do you?” he replies flatly.
“I’ll leave tomorrow morning. I no longer want to stay here.”
“I hope you’re not planning to return to Onitsha?” Ifeanyi chuckles and shakes his head, “I spoke with Nkeonyere this afternoon. She told me your father has made good his threat.”

My heart almost jumps out. The haze from four cans of beer suddenly vanishes. No! He can’t do that! He had threatened that if I chose anything other than accept the call to serve the Lord, he’d cut me off. He’s stripped me of all privileges. I’ve been disinherited. I ponder, gazing bleakly into space.

“You must understand some things that are useful may prove to be an obstacle if we hold on to them more than is necessary. The memory of Elizabeth has given you the much-needed sense of direction I.K. but you must start thinking seriously about the future.” Ifeanyi stands up from the bed and brings out a bundle of twenty Naira notes from his pocket. “I’m sorry I brought you to this. If you insist on answering the call, go and apologize to your father, and if it is necessary, I’ll come personally and tell him to lay the blame on me for deceiving you.” He drops the money on the bed and walks away.
I glance at it and then I turn to Ifeanyi. “What if Uncle Dike comes back and is upset about the death of Florence?” I ask him as he turns the door handle.

“There won’t be any problems as long as none of the girls know about the window. You’ve given me another impression about you…I’m happy you’re not as stupid as to tell anyone else about it,” he opens the door, leaves the room and gently closes it.

I trust Ifeanyi to keep this secret between us and I’m relieved that neither the girls nor Uncle Dike will ever know how Florence really died. But I’m disturbed about his lack of conscience…and I am anxious that I am beginning to have that attitude too.
Maybe Ifeanyi is right. It’s Sod’s law I left the window open…I’m not responsible for her death after all.

chapters.title: Chapter FIVE
content: My admission into the Metropolitan University has suffered a kick in the teeth. First, the personnel at the admissions office demanded a huge sum of money as kickback. It is painful that it came down to this, offering bribes to secure admission despite that my score in the joint admissions matriculations exam is well above the cut-off score for admission into biochemistry. However, Ifeanyi was able to come up with the specified amount.
Afterwards, the registry mentioned something about the quota for my state being exhausted and kicked against the inclusion of my name in the supplementary list.

The quota system was introduced by the Ministry of Education with the aim of narrowing the margin between educationally-advantaged and educationally-disadvantaged states in the enrolment of candidates into federal- and state-owned institutions; candidates applying for admission from states with long-standing weak academic performance are given leverage by reserving specified number of slots during admission into such institutions.

Considering the fact that the country is an amalgamation of several tribes, nearly two hundred and fifty at the last count, it was a welcome idea when it was first introduced – the quota system – and promptly enshrined into the codes of conduct of all aspects of governance in order to curb the alleged imbalances levelled by some ethnic groups who felt they were lagging behind in the affairs of national interest.

The major problem with the quota system is the tendency of prejudice. Admission personnel privy to information about available quota often favour candidates from their tribe or state with such slots, unlawfully denying qualified candidates their privilege. On the other hand admission officers have been accused of hoarding slots during the early processing of the admission list. These slots are later offered secretly to desperate admission seekers at various rates depending on the course the prospective candidate is applying for.

This inglorious practice has obviously taken a heavy toll on the standard and quality of education. The entire system has been so pervaded by the corrupt practices of these base fellows that qualified candidates are sometimes forced to buy back opportunities they originally merited. This portends a situation where the ivory tower will be the prerogative of the wealthy rather than the witty in time to come.

When Ifeanyi told me about this unpalatable development, I dissuaded him from offering kickbacks on my behalf insisting that I deserved a place in the school. He convinced me there is practically no way to get around it. The antisocial activity of the admission personnel is an open secret and the attitude of the academia to the abysmal act is informed by a factor that has a contagiously powerful motto, ‘if you can’t beat them, join them’.

We are in the office of the registrar, which is located in the main administrative block of the university, an ancient one-story building also housing the office of the vice chancellor, the deputy vice chancellors and the examinations office. It is not as busy as the admissions office four buildings from here.

A middle-aged man putting on a grey coloured suit holding JAMB admission notification slips and flanked on either sides by two men leaves one of the offices. The notice on the door indicates it’s the office of the deputy vice chancellor admissions. The two men, who refuse to be pacified by the vice chancellor, are audibly protesting against the unacceptable practices of the admission personnel. I gather that the names of their wards were expunged from the list of students offered admission, which was pasted on the notice board, in spite of the fact that their wards both received letters for offer of admission. They all walk hurriedly down the stairs and vanish out of sight and the voices of the enraged men subside as they leave the building.

A student putting on a black sweatshirt that reveals his muscular build, and a black jean trouser leaves the examinations office sobbing and cursing as he walks towards the bay where I’m sitting in a rickety brown sofa provided by the office for visitors. The atmosphere at the bay takes to the strong aromatic scent of the perfume he’s wearing. He sinks in at the other end of the chair, where the springs are slack and the cushion is nearly torn up. I was sitting there before shifting to this more comfortable spot in the chair.

He continues to rain curses on whoever is responsible for his predicament. I am able to make out, from what he’s saying, that he’s a final year student and is angry with the examination officer for refusing to help him sort out a little problem with his result. As it stands, he may not graduate if he’s unable to rectify the anomaly in his records within two weeks. I also gather he’s already named a huge amount of money as kickback, which the officer apparently refused. He curses repeatedly with derogatory words, branding him or her inconsiderate and a wicked religious zealot, and swears to kill him or her if eventually he’s made to spend an extra year in school.

Ifeanyi is out of the registry. He comes to the bay shaking his head disappointedly. He signals for us to leave. I glance resentfully at the boy sitting next to me. He’s wearing greasy tainted curly hairs, gold jewelries on his neck, and conspicuous gold bracelet in his left hand and gold wristwatch on the other, which gives him away as a desperate loafer who unfortunately didn’t take his studies seriously, and apparently chose to while his time away in drunkenness and philandering while his mates were burning the midnight candle. Now he wants to buy the certificate. Bastard!

Ifeanyi storms out of the building shaking his head and cursing who-it-may concern with the F-word. I throw my long legs in order to catch up with his diminutive structure pacing hurriedly in fury to the parking bay. He unlocks the doors of the Mazda 626 and slumps into the driver’s seat.
“Can you imagine what these bastards are demanding?” he rages.
I stare at him without saying a word. I’ve never seen him like this.
“Ten thousand Naira,” he barks, “Where do they want me to get that kind of money?”
This is incredible! Is this how people gain admission into the university? I ask myself again. I could never have imagined I’d have to offer bribes to gain admission into the university.
Ifeanyi starts the car and the tyres screech as we leave the car park.

The security men at the school gate orders us to pull over for not having the car permit given to us at our entry into the campus premises. They threaten to impound the car until Ifeanyi is convincingly identified as the owner of the vehicle. Unfortunately, the vehicle’s particulars don’t bear Uncle Dike’s name or else Ifeanyi’s driver’s license would suffice for identification. They also demand that we proffer a reasonable explanation for losing the tag. Neither of us has any idea how we misplaced the plastic tag.

Irritated by the sound of music playing, he hisses and put it off. Judging by his opinion about the practice of greasing people’s palms to get what he wants, he’s not upset about their unlawful demands, but he’s angry with himself because he doesn’t have the money. He sits at the wheel silently resting his head with his eyes closed thinking, trying to figure out what to do next. He never gives up on anything. He swore that he’d see to it that my admission is tidied up before he leaves for the U.S.
“Boy, you got faith?” he asks with a short smile, which is intended to pacify me rather than him.
If it has come to this, I know he’s now at his wit’s end.
“Yeah,” I reply.
“You know, I think there’s only one way out of this wahala,” he turns away from my curious boyish stare. “I’ll trade my BTA.”
“No. Don’t do that please…I mean you don’t have to.”

“The last supplementary admission list will be out in five days, and there’s no other way I can raise ten thousand Naira in three days. We’ve come too far to throw in the towel.” He turns to me wearing an impassive expression on his face.
“That means you won’t be travelling?”
“Don’t worry about me. I’ll find a way to replace it. We don’t have time and these guys are not willing to listen to anyone who is not forthcoming.”
Ifeanyi is too cynical to make such a sacrifice, but not with me, he thinks I’m his protégé and considers my career a priority.
“I have faith in God. I believe He’ll make a way for us,” I reply.
“Is that the kind of faith you have?” he says chuckling.
I’m used to his mock questions. It’s no use answering him back.

Ifeanyi regards such comments the ranting of a defeatist. When I was the school’s chaplain, he mocked me and called me all sorts of names. Although he’s not a self-confessed atheist, he relates to God with scepticism.
A sleek black Honda Prelude stops at the gate and the driver steps down and goes to open the trunk for the routine check. When Ifeanyi sights him, he honks at him.

He drives across the gate and pulls over when he’s done with the security, and then he comes to meet Ifeanyi. He is dressed in a white brocade caftan that is exquisitely embroidered around the neck. Ifeanyi introduces him as Dickson Shak, a lecturer in the university, but he has the carriage of a tycoon rather than a university staff.

When Ifeanyi narrates our plight to Dickson, he escorts Ifeanyi to the security post at the gate and vouches for him. They eventually allow us to pass. They chat and cackle noisily to Dickson’s Honda. Ifeanyi greets the lady held fast in the car by the seatbelt with a handshake and they chat briefly while Dickson goes to the driver’s seat.

The Honda roars and honks and Dickson is on his way. Ifeanyi waves him goodbye and returns to the car smiling. He looks at his watch and then eyes me. I smile at him lightly. He turns on the ignition and screeches off.

chapters.title: Chapter SIX
content: The period of mourning is over and the house has returned to the usual early morning blast of reggae music courtesy of Rebecca. Julia and I are digging out routine practice on the makeshift court.

The University Games is nearly three months away, in order to have the best possible crop of players, the basketball coaching crew have organized several preliminary mock competitions to hunt for talents. Nearly two hundred and fifty players have indicated interest to compete for the slot of eighteen players that will make up the squad to represent the school at the competition. The number will certainly rise when a few more players among the first-year students arrive.

Before I started training with Julia, I’ve not handled the ball in six clear months. But, I’ve not only recovered much of my form in the game, I’m also helping Julia improve her skills so as to secure a place in the team.
She makes a crossover and jumps up for a dunk, but midway in the air she screams and slumps on the floor with her hand on her stomach. I rush to her at once.
“Are you alright Julia?”
She nods at me wearing a grimace from the pain in her abdomen. I lift her up gently and supporting her on my shoulder, take her to the lawn and make her lie on her back on the grass.
“What’s the matter Julia?” Rebecca asks.

Since after the death of Florence, she and Anita have become claustrophobic and they prefer to stay with us during our practice. Initially, Anita was eager to learn the game and played with us, but she’s too quick-tempered to contain Julia’s persistent rebuke when she makes a mistake, so she quit. Rebecca is not so particular about sport, but she loves to play music. She supplies us the mood music.
“Becky please get her some water,” I instruct her.
Rebecca returns with a bottle of water and cup. She takes it little by little.
“Should I get you a glass of milk?” Rebecca asks.
“You’re a darling Becky,” she replies concealing the pain with a smile.
Rebecca rushes back into the house.
“I have ulcer,” Julia says faintly. “I think I inherited it from my mum. I hate it when it comes. It always reminds me of that bitch.”
It’s hard to tell which is making her angry now – the ulcer, her seemingly poor performance in the game, or most unlikely to me, her mother.
“Are you sure you can cope with this?”
“I’ll try,” she replies offering her right hand in order to be pulled up. I help her up and support her to a garden chair.
Rebecca returns with a glass of milk. Julia takes it from her and bid her to leave us.
“How am I doing?” she asks me.
“I think you’re driving yourself too hard. I suggest you take a break.”
“Are you saying I should forget about the Tournament?”
“No,” I hesitate, “Well, I wish you have a little more time.”
“I understand,” she mumbles staring wickedly at the hoop. “I’m sorry about the way I spoke about my mother.”
That’s the first thing I’m hearing from any of them about their mother. After what Ifeanyi told me about the way she left them, I’ve never felt up to it to ask them about her.
“Where is she?”
“She left my dad a long time ago,” Julia explains.

There’s a trend in the house – there are at least two versions of nearly every event that occurs here. It’s hard to tell which one is real and which is false. Julia is very evasive when it comes to family matters. If I sound too inquisitive, she may become suspicious and remain silent.
“No matter what she’s done she doesn’t deserve to be addressed like that.”
“She was responsible for Florence’s condition,” she whispers.
“How?” I ask.
Julia twitches at spasms of discomfort from the ulcer and gulps down the content of the glass. I take it from her.
“Thanks,” she greets with a gorgeous smile.
“You’re welcome.”

Her gaze drops shyly on the glass in my hand as if she’s trying to remember what we were discussing. “She used to be addicted to cocaine. Daddy tried at the Doctor’s advice to get her to quit when she was pregnant with the twins, but she refused…she left us when they were four years old.” Her eyes squint in anger. “I wish she had died in that accident, selfish bitch.”
Although Julia is kind-hearted she sounds so resentful and doesn’t look like she has the capacity to forgive.

The squint and frown quickly turns into a captivating smile, her fat lips tapers and her cheeks dimple. “I can’t wait for my daddy to come back. How time flies, I can’t believe four years has passed since we got the news of his arrest. Initially I was worried how he’d cope in prison.”
I’d like to know why exactly he was arrested, but I won’t ask. Besides, my guess would be more likely than whatever reason Julia has to offer.

“Except for the restriction on movement, a prisoner abroad could boast of a better environment and welfare than most of our free citizens who are barely etching a living at the poverty line,” I explain.
“You’re already sounding like your brother, Ifeanyi, he’s so pessimistic about Nigeria.”
“So how have you been coping with his absence?”
“So, so…Ifeanyi has been of invaluable help in spite of his deceits. Did Ifeanyi tell you about him, I mean why he was jailed?”
“He said something about, politics,” I purposely drawl politics to flag my reservation.

“Of course that’s not true.” Julia turns sharply grimacing at me. “I’m sorry to say this Iyke, your brother is such a pathological liar.” When she realizes the import of her words she says softly, “But he’s a kind soul. I don’t know how we would have managed all these years without him…I hope you’re as nice as he is?”

My response is a short snigger. I refuse to be deceived by her flattery. Julia and Ifeanyi haven’t been in the best of terms since the death of her sister. They mutually regret not rising to the occasion to save Florence’s life. The initial coldness has become so deep they consciously avoid each other. He has taken to spending extra time with his friends at the fellowship of the spirits.
The feeling of guilt over Florence’s death flushes over me.
“He lied to us for one whole year about Daddy’s whereabouts until his lies became very obvious and we stopped believing him,” she pauses to attend to Sani, the gateman.
“Saanu ku,” he greets us.
“Saanu Sani, yaya aiki?” Julia responds.

“Aiki de godiya,” he responds with a smile. Julia tells me he’s excited about his work and feels honoured when it is appreciated by asking him how it’s going. He informs Julia in pidgin English there’s a visitor looking for Ifeanyi. Julia instructs him to let him in.

“Saanu ku,” he greets us again, and leaves without delay to carry out her instruction. That thin figure carries an enormous amount of strength and tenacity for hard work. Apart from gate keeping he does the house laundry, mows the lawns and shears the plants in the compound. Though in his mid-forties, he has no wife, no child, yet he constantly wears a contented smile.

Dickson Shak is let into the compound. Julia and I rise to meet him as he strides along the front walk. He is dressed in a blue brocade caftan similar to the one he was putting on when I first met him. Julia introduces me to him again. I remind him about the day we met at the campus gate. He’s scheduled a meeting with the head of department of biochemistry regarding my admission. He couldn’t get Ifeanyi on phone so he decided to come over to inform him. Ifeanyi may possibly not be back before the appointed time so I suggest we go look him up at a branch of the store near the school.
“Alright it’s along my way,” Dickson says.
“Please, give me a minute to dress up,” I request.
“It’s okay. Make it snappy,” he replies.
“Please come in and have a seat.” Julia ushers Dickson into the sitting room.

Dickson Shak compliments my tee shirt when I return to the sitting room. It has a picture of Michael Jordan. It’s a gift from Julia. Dickson asks whether I play basketball. I affirm, and brag about being very good at it as a matter of fact. He shakes his head and says nothing.

The interior of the black Honda Prelude is fascinating. Dickson tells me with an air of conceit it’s customised. I sink into the front passenger seat and put on the seatbelt at his instruction. As soon as he turns on the ignition, my ears crash with Fela’s unique Afro beat music emitting from high fidelity speakers all around the car. I compliment his taste. Dickson tells me smugly his wife is an auto craft, referring to the car.

Dipharms Nigeria Limited is a big pharmaceutical store located in the heart of the city. Apart from the head office, there are two smaller branches, one in Bukuru and the other along Bauchi Road, close to the university. The real reason I rejected Ifeanyi’s offer to do the Mickey Mouse job of managing the stores on his behalf until Uncle Dike returns is that I have a gut feeling the pharmacy is a front for hs hard drugs business. And I am of the opinion he was arrested for drug trafficking and that Ifeanyi himself is a racketeer.

We are lucky to find Ifeanyi at the store. Dickson informs him about the date with the head of department for eight o’clock this evening in the H.O.D.’s house and advises that we make ready photocopies of my registration slip. We will proceed from the store at seven forty-five latest.

Dickson Shak is a Harvard-trained accountant and lectures in the department of accountancy. Although he’s in his early thirties, and single, he has an enormous clout in the university, being a member of the royal family, to be precise he’s a nephew to the traditional ruler of the Gunkun kingdom in Jos.

The Harvard graduate was furious when Ifeanyi told him about the awful demands of the personnel at the registry. Dickson pressed him for the names of the perpetrators threatening to ensure that they were dismissed from the university, but Ifeanyi refused to disclose their identity not even with Dickson’s accusation that he’s abetting corrupt practices and aiding criminal acts. He shrugs his shoulders and says it’s a social ill that cannot be eradicated.

At exactly twenty minutes to eight his black Honda Prelude swerves into the shopping complex. He honks to announce his arrival. Ifeanyi and I board the auto craft. I squeeze myself into the cramped backspace of the miniature vehicle spreading my legs so wide that my pelvis cramps. But my anticipation of an auspicious meeting with Dr. Aliu soothes the pain.

The senior staff quarters is a cluster of simple-styled bungalows with spacious compounds, each compound is fenced with a short hedge of smoothly trimmed pine trees. He lives in number seventy-five Aja Nwachukwu Crescent.

My legs are all pins and needles as I stand beside Ifeanyi waiting for the residents to attend to Dickson who is at the front door. Unfortunately, he is not at home, but he left instruction for us to wait for him so the housekeeper let us in.

The sitting room is sparsely decorated with locally manufactured furniture. An overdone archive of framed photographs displayed on sagging curtain box spanning the entire walls of the large sitting room suggests that either or both of them belong to a sizeable extended family, and they are mutually appreciated by the couple, which is not an unusual feeling among the predominantly polygamous Muslim family. There are framed black and white photographs of infants, and of several couples during their Islamic wedding day – nikkai, as well as old photographs of aged men and women most of whom might have kicked the bucket.

The housekeeper comes with a bottle of water and three glasses in a tray, sets it on the centre table and disappears. Dickson turns one of the cups and fills it, Ifeanyi restrains him from turning up another cup, but he hesitates and whispers to him that it’s the custom of most northerners to offer their visitors water as a sign of welcome. Ifeanyi shrugs. Dickson fills the three glasses, passes one to me and gulps down his. Ifeanyi and I sip from the glass because neither of us is thirsty.

Flanked by two children, Doctor Aliu’s wife enters the sitting room, the older of the two is a boy, he’s about three, although he looks too thin for his age. His bulgy eyes are watery and his bony cheeks wet, he’s been crying since we were ushered in. His younger sister slightly less than a year old pulls on her mother’s silk gown to steady her steps making her bulgy stomach conspicuous. She’s chubby and a spitting image of her mother. The oldest of her children races into the sitting room and hugs Dickson.

When we all rise to greet her, Dickson formally introduces her to us. She’s likely under the age of twenty, tender looking and seems unruffled by her pregnant condition and the bustle of raising three children that came in quick succession. She apologises on her husband’s behalf explaining he received an emergency notice for a meeting with the dean of the faculty, but assures us he’ll certainly be back before nine for the nine o’clock news.

Attracted by the picture of air-bound Michael Jordan on my tee shirt, her older son leaves Dickson and comes to me pulling my chinos and asking me to carry him up so he can have a closer look at the picture. I oblige him.

He tells me his name is Kabiru and he’s four years plus, and that his brother’s name is Danladi, his sister’s Nana and all the while curiously caressing the picture on my tee shirt. He refuses to follow his mother and his sibling when they’re leaving telling his mother he wants to stay with Uncle Michael, that’s me.

The two drunkards with me have been murmuring with relish about tonight’s fellowship of the spirits. Because they’d be late by the time the meeting with the don is over, we’ll be going to the haunt straight from here, which means I’ll be going with them. The way they sound, it won’t be a bad idea to check out what happens at the fellowship of the spirits, and meet some of Ifeanyi’s friends too.

It was at such a meeting that Dickson and Ifeanyi first met, and that particular moment, that gathering of seven drunkards became the fellowship of the spirit. Dickson was a habitué of the joint. He was attracted to a group of upcoming guys seated together at a table titrating – their slang word for drinking – and making merry. The outgoing chartered accountant fresh from the Harvard University indicated his interest to be a part of the fun and ordered the waiter to serve them a round of drinks. Dickson was asked to join them at the table, it was Ifeanyi who invited him, and they have since become very good friends.

The housekeeper rushes to the front door at the hooting of an incoming car. Kabiru recognizes it’s his father’s car, leaves me and races towards the door shouting, Daddy! Daddy! His younger brother re-appears and also scurries across to the front door screaming, Daddy! Daddy! His chubby sister babbles after him, Dada! Dada! But frightened by our presence her toddle is brought to a standstill. Then she sprawls on the floor and squalls loudly hysterically until the housekeeper comes to her rescue.

The pot-bellied lecturer comes in and apologizes for keeping us waiting for so long. Without any intention to delay us further, he asks for my result slip. Ifeanyi gives it to him. He’s very impressed with my score and remarks it is well above the cut off-point for biochemistry. He asks me why I’ve not gone to apply at the admissions office. Ifeanyi gives him a censored report of our ordeal at the registry without mentioning the kickback they demanded. He hissed regrettably when Ifeanyi told him I was denied admission on the grounds that the quota allotted to my state has been filled.
As soon as the jingle for the nine o’clock news comes on air he tactfully dismisses us and asks me to see him in his office tomorrow morning. We thank him and he sees us to the door.
I squeeze my massive frame into the backseat not giving the slightest regard for the discomfort I suffered when we came. I thank Dickson umpteen times until he got tired of answering.
“What are friends for?” he replies casually when Ifeanyi expressed his gratitude.
It pays to have friends in high places when money fails. I muse.

Ifeanyi defies Dickson’s reminder to put on his seatbelt. The Afro beat of Fela Anikulapo Kuti comes on as soon as he turns the ignition, then the headlamps flips up. Dickson steers into the lonely crescent and races to Madam Jay Dee’s place, the venue of tonight’s fellowship of the spirits.

Madam Jay Dee’s place is an alfresco joint open for operations mostly at night. The joint is famous for its exclusive irresistible chilli chicken and a six-man musical band that entertains its customers to highlife music. Habitués of the joint relish and look forward to the savoury chicken and constantly chilled drinks.

The fellowship of the spirits meets at seven exclusive joints. The rule in the house is that the meetings are rotated among the joints, and none of these joints is to be visited more than once in a week. The membership of the fellowship has increased to sixteen from seven when it started, and because of the calibre of its members they are recognized and accorded due respect at these haunts. Fourteen of us who are present tonight gather about adjoining tables separate from the other customers. Strangers are not permitted on any account to sit at the table with the fellows.

The basic requirement for entry into the lush club is that the aspiring member must have the capacity to consume at least ten bottles of beer or at least a seven-hundred-mils bottle of any spirits at a sitting. Of course, it is expected that aspiring members earn enough money to keep up with the indulgence.
I don’t particularly have a craving for beer or any form of liquor since the day I first tasted beer, and I don’t earn a living, but for Ifeanyi’s sake I’m permitted to sit with these dipsomaniacs.
Initiation into the lush club requires that I foot the bill for all the drinks at the table tonight. Ifeanyi sponsors my titration – the fellowship parlance for making orders for drinks.

The mood at the table with these drunken fellows is quite irresistible and the laughter at the crack of jokes is loud and contagious. The waiter comes with a fresh order of drinks, which brings a new excitement at the table, and is welcome with a chorus led by Chukwuma, who is referred to as the high priest.
“Ten green bottles standing on the wall, ten green bottles standing on the wall, if one green bottle should happen to fall…” he sings in a husky voice.
“…nine green bottles standing on the wall,” the house chorus after him.

Ifeanyi tells me the fridge is the wall, and at each titration one bottle falls from the wall. The number of bottles mentioned in the chorus will keep dropping until the last green bottle falls, after which any fellow who wants to leave is free to do so. Because it is a fellowship of the spirits, the table forbids any form of soft drinks except when a fellow comes in the company of a lady. According to him, they rarely do.
I shudder at the thought of Ifeanyi being queer. I’ve never seen him with a lady, neither has he mentioned having a girlfriend.

The jokes make rounds and the laughter reaches the heavens, the waiter serves plates upon plates of savoury chilli chicken. Then he comes with another round of drinks and serves each fellow taking away empty bottles from the table. I haven’t poured from the last bottle since it was opened.

“Six green bottles standing on the wall, six green bottles standing on the wall, if one green bottle should happen to fall…” Chukwuma is leading the chorus. His voice sounds like it’s coming from afar.

Another frosty bottle of Star lager beer is placed before me beside the last one I was served. It appears to me as if they are six green bottles. I think Ifeanyi whispered something to the waiter and he lifts three bottles from the table. I try to hold them back and I tip the bottle and the drink spills on the table.
I am feeling dizzy and I’m having a nauseating feeling. I am definitely intoxicated. Ifeanyi stopped the waiter from serving me any more beer. I sit there observing the events from faraway.

At the fall of the last green bottle, Dickson Shak announces the venue of the next fellowship of the spirits to the house, De Roof nightclub, the fellows are unanimously organising a send-forth party in honour of Ifeanyi.

I find myself lying on the bed with my head spinning slowly. The chime of the blurry overlapping image of the wall clock tells me it’s midnight. Ifeanyi flicks the bedside switch, my pupils dilate and I shut my eyes.

chapters.title: Chapter SEVEN
content: Rays of green, red, yellow and blue spotlights flash on the dance floor where a herd of spellbound dancers display exotic dance steps in conformity with the deafening reggae music blasting from giant loudspeakers in the hall. Three bare-bodied men with dreadlocks dressed in tight trousers and three ladies putting on skimpy dresses frenziedly gyrate to the fast drumbeat. Ifeanyi calls me to come to the dance floor, but Julia pulls me back making me sit with her at the table.

My head aches from a terrible hangover as I open my eyes to find Ifeanyi standing by the bed with his towel tied around his waist. I squint my eyes against the brightness of the sun, and then at the clock, it’s seven-thirty. As usual, Rebecca’s habitual early morning reggae music filters into the room.
“Boy, how are you this morning?” he asks grinning.
“Good morning Ify.”
“Morning,” he replies, “I thought you were dead. I’ve been trying to wake you up the past one hour.”
“My skull is splitting,” I mutter as I feel my head.
“You’re having a hangover, don’t worry we’ll knock that off,” he replies facing the wardrobe.
“I need a painkiller.”
He smiles at me dropping his choice of clothes on the bed, “You’ll be good to go after a shower and a chilled glass of Star.”

I’ve heard that before, the antidote for a hangover is a small quantity of the drink one had the previous day. Ifeanyi hums a tune expressing his confidence that my meeting with the H.O.D. will be fruitful.

I rise sluggishly from the bed and go to the window. Julia is not yet on the court. By the time we came back yesterday they had all gone to bed. She’ll be delighted to hear about my meeting with the head of department this morning and if it turns out to be positive, it’d be a prayer answered for her. I’ll miss our routine game this morning.
I reel past Ifeanyi to the bathroom.
“Hurry up, Dr. Aliu looks like a man not-easy-to catch once the sun is up,” he urges me.
“Like you,” I say jokingly.
He turns to me smiling and responds, “Am I?”
“I wasn’t expecting to see you when I woke up this morning.”
“Well, I’m not as unorganised as you think.”
“That’s not what I mean.” I’ve since given up that pretentious impression he gave me about him. Surprisingly, he makes the most of his time.
“I know.” He gives me an I’m-not-hurt smile, “Now will you go and have your bath or we’ll be late. And you’ll be sorry.”
These ten days have changed my impression about him. Ifeanyi and I have never enjoyed such intimacy. I wish I could dissuade him from travelling or at least make him defer his trip…forever.

There’s an amazing sense of brotherhood in the fellowship of the spirits, and amidst their jests yesterday, I was surprised those lushes still found opportunity to discuss politics and business – legitimate business. I’m already benefiting from that brotherhood. Then I turn on the taps and feel the water temperature and begin to whistle the ten green bottle rhymes.

School will resume two weeks from now. However, registration by a stream of newly-admitted students is already in full swing at the twin two-storey building of the faculty of biological sciences. Fellow fresh students are easily recognized by the registration papers they're holding, unlike a handful of older students who are on the go – final year students with drawn faces – who are around to sort out complications with their results. Although they are the most likely to know where the department is situated, they are too impatient or too bitter about their predicament to attend to us. They remind me of the charlatan I met at the registry.

Ifeanyi walks up to two girls chatting on the corridor, while I stand a few metres away. He talks with the older of the two girls. I take to eyeing up her neighbour. The school’s registration file she’s holding tells me she’s also a fresher. She’s tall, lanky and dark-skinned, and looks groovy in her tight-fitted jean trousers and crop top that looks undersized for her busts. She stares back at me as I ogle at her as if we’re waiting to see who would flinch first.
Ifeanyi thanks her friend and faces the direction she pointed out to us. Groovy was the first to smile, she giggles and whispers something to her friend then I smile back flushing with enthusiasm.

His office is in the west wing of the building. The secretary informs us he’s in a meeting and suggests we come back as the meeting will extend into the afternoon. Ifeanyi and I eye each other. I’d rather sit and wait for him.

The secretary shrugs and shows me to a seat, an uncomfortable iron chair with a bare wooden surface without a cushion. Its red paint is worn from old use and it has no backrest. It’s obvious she doesn’t entertain visitors. I’ll certainly have blisters on my buttocks after an hour’s wait but I won’t take a chance on him. I take the seat. Ifeanyi looks at his watch and excuses himself. She’s kind enough to make my stay less boring – entertaining me to a stack of newspapers. The most recent of the pile is four months stale. But I’m grateful.

The gym is divided into two. One of the courts is assigned to female athletes, while male athletes are having their drill on the other pitch. Although this is a preliminary game, there is tremendous exhilaration among the athletes who have come to show their skills. Two teams comprising ten players each are playing on each of the courts under the scrutiny of the coaches.

The assistant coach – Dele Tanko, a final year student of health and physical education – will be assessing the female players, while the head coach is overseeing the match taking place at the court right behind me. Dele is deformed in his right hand, which dangles loosely in the long-sleeved tracksuit he’s putting on.

Julia is not the only one who came to the court with her ‘coach’. Typical of a real basketball match, the girls are given the opportunity to receive instructions from their coach at timeouts for instructions. I notice that Dele is observing our interaction with keen interest as I detail Julia during time outs. I think he’s impressed with Julia’s constantly improving form after each timeout. At the close of the first half, it is obvious Julia is going to make the NUGA basketball squad. She has stolen the show and gotten the crowd on her side.
I wish Ifeanyi were here. He promised to come, but I know he’ll offer an excuse later. He doesn’t like sports, and he particularly doesn’t fancy basketball.

At the end of the game the coach calls the girls together for last minute instructions. Julia and I stare at each other from a distance as her teammates shower her with encomiums and the mammoth crowd chant Jordan! Jordan! Julia is putting on the number twenty-three jersey underneath the yellow apron given to them to distinguish the two teams.
She returns to the sideline where I’m standing screaming, “I made it! I made it!” She hugs me and gives me a kiss.
“You were really stunning out there,” I congratulate her.
“I couldn’t have done it without you. I owe it to you Iyke.” When she kisses me again, the crowd begins to heckle and I curl up.
We break up as the coach coughs seeking attention.
“This is your coach uh?” he asks Julia.
“Yes,” she replies with obvious excitement.”
“My name is Dele, Dele Tanko,” he says offering me his left hand.
“Iyke, Iyke Obiajulu,” I reciprocate, “My pleasure.”
“The pleasure is mine. I suppose you’re in the men’s team?”
“Well, not yet.”
“What the hell are you waiting for?” he replies.
“I’m not a student here,” I hesitate, “I mean I’m still seeking admission,” I whisper.
“Bull shit! You ever played in a competition?”
“Yeah I did.”
“Don’t tell me you’re such a novice.”

I’m not. And I quite understand where Dele is going. Back in our interschool competition days in secondary school, experienced external players are often hired and disguised as students to play with the school team in order to enhance their chances of winning the competition. They are called mercies, coined from the word mercenaries. Mercies are usually recruited by the school’s games master, who eventually persuades the school authority to issue them all the necessary papers to convince the sports council that they are bona fide students of the school that hired them.
“Do you want to play in the NUGA?” Dele asks me pointedly.

The men’s category will be a tough competition, and considering that I’ve not trained seriously for a major competition for a very long time, I doubt if I’d be able to meet up with the required standard.
“I’m not yet in school.”
“Let me repeat my question Iyke, do you want to play this game or not?”
Julia nods to me to say yes.
“Don’t bother about your status,” he says with a reassuring smile.
“I’ve not –”
Julia hisses.
“I need a straight answer. Do you want to play in the NUGA?”
“Yeah, why not.”
“Wait here for me okay. Don’t move an inch.” He leaves us and goes to the other pitch. The men have just finished slugging it out.

I can’t believe Barry is the head coach. His real name is George Adio. His deep baritone voice earned him the nickname Barry. He used to be Coach Sebastian’s assistant until he left in nineteen eighty-seven when he was offered employment by the Plateau state team. When he left, he took the soul of the team with him – the team never won a single major competition thereafter. If Coach Sebastian was the team manager, we knew Barry as the technical director. He knew the game.

He grins cheerfully as we walk up to him and excitedly throws the ball to me and signals me to flaunt my skills. That’s the way we used to greet each other back in my secondary school days. I bounce the ball twice, but I’m not in the mood for it so I throw the ball back to him and we greet each other with a hug.
“You guys knew each other?” Dele asks.
“This,” he says tapping my shoulder, “is my boy. I taught him the game.”
“I’m not surprised,” Dele comments.
“Where did you find him?” Coach Barry asks him.
“On the sideline. God! You need to see what he did with this lady from the sideline.”
After a brief banter, Coach Barry enlists me to take part in the preliminary games. He also concurs with Dele’s proposition to play as a mercenary, if my admission doesn’t sail through.

There is rapturous joy as I break the news about my admission into the university. Anita is so happy the word congratulation comes fluently, though she can’t say more than that, her eyes tells me how happy she is. Rebecca suggests a sumptuous feast to celebrate it, Ifeanyi agrees.

He’s worried sick about breaking the news of his trip to the States. His affection for the girls is really telling on him, coupled with the mutual regret he and Julia share over the death of Florence, he’s afraid they’ll not take the news lightly. He once thought of leaving without notice and calling Julia later to inform her he’s in America. I promptly opposed the idea and persuaded him to come home early so he could take advantage of the excitement at the feast to inform them of his intended trip.

Rebecca and Anita are not unaware of Julia’s indifference to Ifeanyi, and it’s making them very uncomfortable. Because of Ifeanyi’s busy schedule in the day, the girls always looked forward to converging at the dining for dinner, but that has changed since Florence died. It seems her ghost haunts us at the table.

Julia seldom contributes to discussions whenever Ifeanyi is around and if he stays around for too long she’ll quietly walk out on everybody and shut herself up in the room. She once confessed to me that she saw her apparition and heard hallucinatory echo of her babbling for food the night she died. Besides, she’s been suffering from insomnia and has been taking sleeping pills. But she pleaded with me not to let Ifeanyi know. I was pricked in my conscience when she confessed these things to me and thought of telling her what actually happened that night, but I held myself back when I remembered Ifeanyi’s warning.

Julia treats us to a rare three-course meal of breadfruit soup for starters and fruits salad for dessert. For the main dish, there’s an option of sweet and sour sauce to go with spaghetti, potato chips or boiled plantain, or native onugbu soup to go with pounded yam, my favourite meal. I specially requested it for I’ve not had it since I left home.

In spite of the exhilaration that the news of my admission has brought, there’s a deathly calm at the table. Julia is unusually speechless. Although she confessed to me she enjoys bitter leaf soup, she’s apparently lost her taste.

She cuts small portions of food, makes it into small round balls, smears soup sluggishly about it and swallows. Rebecca and Anita deliberately avoid meeting Ifeanyi’s eyes. Without looking up from their plates, they also pick at their food. No one has uttered a word since I said the grace. And all of us seem to have lost our appetite.

His eyes are glazed. He coughs to clear his throat for the third time and brace himself to break the news that he’ll be travelling to Lagos next tomorrow and then to America a few days later. But the words are so hard to come forth.
“I was at the pharmacy today,” Julia says without raising her head from her plate, “I heard you’re leaving for America,” she hesitates, “How could you be making such plans without letting us know?”
Ifeanyi is broken by her words. His lips begin to shake as he fights tears.
“It’s not like that Julia, I can explain,” I respond.

“You too Ikenna,” she says angrily turning to me, “You knew about this all the while and you hid it from us. I knew there was something else behind your coming. He brought you in so he could sneak away from us, isn’t it?”
“Julia please don’t take it like that. I didn’t have the courage to face –”

“This isn’t the first time Ifeanyi. It’s not the first time you’ve travelled without telling us. You disappear and then call from God-knows-where to inform me as if the trip was so impromptu you couldn’t even leave a note. Why do you keep doing this to us?” Julia whines.
Anita drops her cutlery, pushes back her seat and quietly leaves the table.
“You promised, you promised you won’t leave us again bloody liar,” Rebecca snarls and stomps out of the table following Anita into their room.
Julia glares at him and silently leaves the table. Ifeanyi watches her in perplexity.
“You dropped me in it I.K.,” he snarls in a subdued voice.
“I didn’t tell her.”
“Who else did?”
“You heard her, didn’t you? She was at the pharmacy.”
“You’re a bloody liar,” he snaps. His blazing eyes beg for further explanations, but I have none to offer. Then he leaves the table and retires to the room.
He is sprawling on the bed, his legs sticking out at the side of the bed with his shoes on. The telly is not on, and he’s turned off the music.
“Please don’t ever call me a liar,” I whisper supposing he’s listening, “You should’ve informed her before now. I told you.”

His response comes nearly two minute later with his eyes closed, “It’s not as easy as you think.” Then he opens his eyes and turns to me. “Didn’t you see Becky? I feel so awful about this…about leaving them.”
Julia was right. I misjudged him when Florence died. I never knew Ifeanyi could be this emotional.
“May be you should defer the trip say until Uncle Dike comes back.”

“No. I must leave for America,” he replies grimly and sits up. “He exploited my predicament and enslaved me. It took me so long to realize it. He’s a shyster, a liar and a thief,” he takes a pause and squints in anger.
Selfish sloth! He’s trading me to Dike as a ransom to release himself. What does he take me for, a scapegoat or what? I take a deep breath and say nothing.
“I know you’re wondering why I’m doing this in spite of what I know about Dike?”
“You deceived me,” I mumble.
“No I didn’t. We needed each other to be free, I.K. that’s what brothers are for,” he says cuddling, “When I’m settled in America, I’ll make plans for you to come over okay.”
I’m short of words.
“I know you don’t believe these things happen, but there’s something strange about Daddy and Uncle Dike,” he says in a low tone of voice.
I turn and squint to him without saying a word.
“I think he put a spell on us.”
“How can you say that?”
“You won’t believe me, but I know it. You think he’s happy seeing both of us, while he doesn’t have a male –”
“Please Ifeanyi, stop saying this.”
We stare at each other in the hush. He shakes his head and takes a deep breath.
“If what you think is true, then why are you leaving me here?”
“You’re different I.K. There’s something you have which I don’t.”
“And what could that be?” I ask.
“Faith,” he says.

I burst into laughter. I can’t believe this is coming from him. Ifeanyi has no regard for God to start with, and now, the various philosophical laws and ideologies he professes to govern the things that happen to all mortals have undermined whatever remains of my mustard seed kind of faith. Either he’s interpreting a situation with Sod’s Law, or he’s figuring it out using Murphy’s Law or one of those captivating theories. Besides, he’s a disciple of the Machiavellian doctrine.

“Boy, you remember that day at the school gate with those security guys? I was at my wits end,” he pauses and shakes his head smiling, “It’s your faith that brought you this far. Always remember we need each other okay…I’ll go talk things over with Julia.”
“She’ll appreciate it…can she go with us to the club?”
Ifeanyi hesitates and ponders. Besides going to school Julia seldom leaves the house, she’s never been to a party and she doesn’t have a boyfriend.
“I’m not sure she’ll be comfortable going to De Roof night club?” he finally responds.
“Let me tell her about it before you talk to her.”
“I see you guys are getting pretty close. It’s good enough if it doesn’t go beyond this.”
“I don’t have such an intention,” I growl.
“People usually don’t, but when they finally fall into the temptation, they blame it on the devil and claim not to know how it happened…you better be careful with Julia,” he says in a brisk tone.
I’m really appalled he could imagine I’ll have an affair with my cousin, but I do not intend to argue about it.

chapters.title: Chapter EIGHT
content: Coach Barry, the team coach is saddled with the task of raising a formidable squad to represent the school in the competition. In order to actualise that, he sent out scouts to recruit players who have participated in secondary school competitions to compete with the present members of the team for a place. I’ve always known Coach Barry to be high-handed, but his style of coaching obviously isn’t going down well with the older team-mates.
I got the hint that although Dele Tanko, his assistant, is in charge of the girls’ team, he single-handedly recruited nearly all the guys called up for the mock matches.

Following the result of two trial matches, it is certain most of the current players will be replaced with the new players Coach Barry is recruiting. Some of them who are sure they won’t don the jersey have already threatened to lynch him if he dares exclude them from participating in the epic competition.

When the situation in the men’s team selection began to get out of hand, Dele quickly waded into the controversy and asked Coach Barry to let him handle the team selection while he does the job of training the players. In order to save his life, he agreed. But he hinted that he’d resign if the situation doesn’t get any better or he’s not pleased with the selection.

My game at the men’s mock game was beneath form. Had it not been for Coach Barry’s faith in me, I probably may have lost hope of making the team. I was clearly outplayed by the other athletes. Coach Barry told me he’s counting on me and I gave him the assurance Rhizopus will resurrect in the next group stage for team selection.
“Rhizopus!” Dele calls me as I leave the gym.
I halt and wait for him to come up to me.
“Hi,” he greets me.
“How did you come about that?”
“Can a man who stands a head taller than his mates hide himself in the market square? Will he not be acting like the proverbial Ostrich?” He brings out a packet of cigarette and offers me a stick.
“No thanks,” I look askance at him.
“Oh, I forgot you’re going to be playing,” he says with a smile.
“Well, I don’t smoke cigarettes.”

“That’s good for you. You see, we don’t permit such indulgence, but it’s difficult to get players to kick the habit in spite of its negative effect on their performance.” He takes a stick of cigarette between his lips. “I hear your admission is now through. I had to skip my routine to come and see your game.”
“So, what do you think?”

“You’re not as impressive as you were on the sideline,” he takes a pause and let out smoke, “Well, if you’re asking my sincere opinion, I’d say you’re a better coach than a player…I was almost becoming disappointed until Coach Barry gave explanations for your poor performance.”
“What did he tell you?”

“Well, I think he has a lot of confidence in you,” Dele sniggers and puffs a heavy cloud of smoke. “If what he tells me is true…then we have a lot in common, Rhizopus. If you work hard enough…and if you let me guide you, you may be the next champion after Dreck.”
“I heard you were once an MVP.”
“So you went about asking after me?”
“Just wanted to know who the coach is, that’s all.”
“Well, I just came to wish you luck. Remember, if you have the eyes of a champion you’ll see your destiny in the court.”
“I was told there’s a myth about the basketball court. To some it’s the door to the celestial, and to others a cemetery?” I pause.
“That’s right, very correct,” he replies smugly.
“What does that mean?”

“You see this court.” He turns and points it out to me with a wave of his left hand. “So many good players, very good players I mean have had their talents buried here, and only a few have gone from here to high places, you get what I mean?” He takes a long drag of cigarette. “Ask me what distinguishes the one from another, go ahead ask me.”
“What?”

“Will. That’s what separates losers from winners. If you wanna win, you gotta walk by your will, don’t let people influence you…walk with champions. Do what they do, think what they think, and if possible, eat what they eat.”

Dele, I heard, is a troublemaker. Coach Barry told me in confidence he’s the one who stirred up the hornet’s nest, instigating the players against him so he can have his own players in the team. The female team is already under his control. He holds sway there. And Coach Barry is already losing the best players because they are not willing to rise to the occasion to accept his terms for inclusion into the team.
“How’s the gal, your cousin?”
I’m not going to discuss anything about Julia with him.
“Sweet babe isn’t she?” he giggles, “I have a cousin that beautiful too.”
I clench my fist and glance at him in disgust.

“Let’s talk about the game now. There’s something that struck me about her game that day, or maybe about your style…you’re an individual player. You instructed her to concentrate on her own game. The other trainers didn’t see that, but I did. Guess why they didn’t notice?”
I remain silent.

“The general thinking is that without a team spirit a group cannot win. And so, coaches essentially make a team play to win, to win his game. But naturally, every player has his own individual idea, and most of the time a champion’s game sense doesn’t agree with the coach’s idea. So at the end of the game, if they win, the coach rejoices, but the champion, he groans…because he lost the most important game, his own game.”
Is he trying to incite me against Coach Barry?

“That’s how I became MVP. It was obvious we had no chances of winning that game, but I was determined to win my own game and that was exactly what I did, and fortunately it gave the team the lead, and the coach his victory,” he grins widely obviously relishing the reminiscence of that feat. “Coach Barry told me what happened between you and the head coach what’s his name.”
“Coach Sebastian.”

“Sometimes it doesn’t matter if the team wins or not as long as you win your own game,” he says in a low tone. “It’s the same reason some good players never make the team, because they are not willing to play the coach’s game. Sometimes it becomes necessary to make sacrifices to see a dream come true, what do you think?”
I stare impatiently ahead of me knowing what he’s driving at. Bastard! You’ll never have her if that’s what you want.

Julia told me Dele asked her out the next day after the mock match, but she declined. He swore that she’d never make the team despite her impressive performance. I informed Coach Barry about it, although I asked him not to make any fuss about it, it’s possible he discussed it with him. Coach Barry told me there had been several similar complaints by the girls that Dreck told them point-blank that if they’re not willing to rise up to his indecent proposal, they could as well forget about NUGA.
Clenching my fist angrily, I glare at him as he walks away with his right hand dangling in the sleeve of his flannel shirt.

chapters.title: Chapter NINE
content: It’s nearly half past eight and I’m gussied up for the matriculation in my new black Italian suit Ifeanyi bought for me, which I reserved for the occasion. I’m not excited about the event anymore. My enthusiasm about becoming an undergraduate has been burnt off as I reflected on the import of Daddy’s weighty letter – he has formally disinherited me for rejecting God’s will for me, and he put that in writing. He’s bitter about the way I secretly left home to chase the wind, the way he put it. I will never succeed in my chosen career, he said firmly. He ended the letter with a stern warning that I should never return to his house, and that God will judge me and my accomplice for rebelling against His will.

The letter was sent through Nkeonyere when she returned to school at the end of the holiday.
