Two days after the incident that happened in my room on Valentine’s Day, Julia began to manifest signs of slight mental disorder. She suddenly developed a strange attitude towards her sisters, as if they were complete strangers to her and gradually began to lose her memory. After a while, it degenerated into severe introversion. She soliloquised and laughed to herself mostly, and preferred to eat on her own in her room rather than coming to the dining. Rebecca and Anita attributed it to the shock of Florence’s death, but only Bitty and I know the shock of the assault is the cause of her severe disorder.

But when I conferred with him about my plan to take her to a psychiatrist, he advised me against it fearing that she could be made to reveal the truth. Instead he suggested I wait for her father to come back. Bitty obliged to take care of her till we return from Lagos.
“I learnt from Rebecca it was the day after you came to my house that Florence died,” he says fixing his gaze at Julia who is lying on the bed.
My heart misses a beat.
“I was told how you tried to save her life,” he continues.
I exhale deeply.
Then he turns around. His deep-set eyes shrink and become narrower. “Has she seen a doctor?” he asks.

“Yes sir,” I reply. Bitty arranged for their family doctor to come and see Julia here. The doctor did confirm that she must have been through a shocking experience that affected her mental state. When he inquired if we know of such an experience, we denied being aware of what could be the cause. He gave her some medications and assured us that as soon as she gets over it, she’d be okay.
“A head doctor I mean,” he says slightly gesticulating.
“No sir.”
“You’ll take us to a psychiatrist right away okay.”
“Okay sir.”
“Excuse us.”

I close the door gently as soon as I leave the room. Standing still for a while, I breathe in and exhale heavily and shut my eyes. My eyes meet Anita’s when I open my eyes. Without uttering a word, she walks past me and enters Julia’s room. What does she know? I wonder.

Hands clasped behind my head, I lie on the bed unable to sleep. Julia has been admitted into the home of lunatics. I hate to imagine what she’ll be doing with that bunch of crazy folks. Julia doesn’t belong there. She just doesn’t fit into that place. She would have been better off here if I were allowed to take care of her. I would have attended to her for as long as it’d take her to recover. After all, I’m responsible for all this.

The Golgotha has stolen everything from me. First they stole my dream of playing in the NUGA, now my joy, my Julia has been taken from me. A flash of our romance in the room gives way to the memory of her Valentine day visit. I flush with anger. I’ll get back at them. I’ll make Dele regret ever meeting me, I swear under my breath.

There’s a rattle at the door. Uncle Dike eases into the room looking very upset. “I’d like to have a word with you.” His voice is subdued and grave. He darts about the room uneasily as if he’s thinking how to start. He finally sits in the armchair.
“I suppose there must be a good reason for objecting to my decision to have Julia admitted into the institution…do you mind telling me why.”
“I think she has greater chances of recovering if she’s left at home with her sisters,” I explain.

“I’m getting some conflicting picture about Julia’s condition…first, the kids told me she didn’t spend the night here on the night of Valentine’s Day. And when she came back the next day, she refused to talk to any of them,” he pauses, “How come she doesn’t allow anyone else come close to her. What is the mystery behind your closeness with my daughter, Ikenna?”
I stare silently at him.
“There’s no doubt you’re hiding something from me. Here’s an ampoule of a lethal drug I found in her room.” He throws it to me on the bed.
I turn my head slowly and glance at it.

“I don’t know of any reason why any of my children, especially Julia would want to commit suicide. I make sure they have everything they desire in life, everything. Especially, Julia, and that’s because I don’t want her to end up like her mother.” He walks slowly to the armchair and sits down.

“I know I’m not a very good man,” he hesitates. “I’ve been up all night thinking…could the fate of my daughters be a retribution for my sins?” He shakes his head slowly. “No,” he takes a pause and squints at me. “It’s because of you that these things happened, Ikenna. Rebecca told me about your romance with Julia, then the grouse that led to your leaving the house. She told them she was going to see you the night of Valentine to inform you about the trip to Lagos…what exactly happened to Julia?”
I shut my eyes and breathe heavily. When I open my eyes, I meet a fiery gaze and he’s drumming his fingers on the chair’s arm.
“You know it’s a taboo for you to have a sexual relationship with your cousin, don’t you?” He leans forward. “Did you do it?”
“She was raped,” I whisper.
Uncle Dike is stunned. “How?” he gasps.
“I uh – I couldn’t stop them.”
“Are you saying you saw –” words fail him.
“They were armed. There was nothing I could do…I was helpless.”

“No you are hopeless and you are useless. Shit! Listen to yourself, you nincompoop. You saw your cousin being raped and you couldn’t do anything about it, you tell me they’re armed.” He shakes his head. “Ifeanyi wouldn’t have allowed this to happen, never.”
“They’re cult guys.”
“Who the hell are they! Are they not students like you, are they spirits!” he barks.
“They are members of a powerful secret cult, Uncle. I can’t fight against them.”
“You’re a fool,” he drawls angrily. “When you leave this house tomorrow I don’t want to see your face again…you nincompoop.” He stands up and walks slowly to the door.
“I’ll avenge her.”
He holds the door handle still for a while as if he’s contemplating what I said.
“But I’ll need your help, Uncle.”
“Let’s discuss this at dawn before you leave for school. No matter what it’ll cost, I want those bastards punished.”
“There’s something else I want.”
He leaves the door handle and turns around. “What is it?”
“I uh – I want power.”
“You want power,” he chuckles. Then he saunters back looking askance at me. “What makes you think I can give that to you?”
“Let’s not go into that, Uncle. I swear by my life that I’ll avenge Julia’s rape and I’ll do whatever it takes.”
He smiles and sits down in an armchair. “If I’m rightly informed, you were consecrated to be a priest, after your father.”
I stare impassively at him completely short of words.
“If your father’s God were really as powerful as you guys make Him appear, why don’t you go to him?”
“I don’t believe anymore,” I pause,” “Besides … he’s disowned me.”
“Why?” he asks.
“It’s a long story. I want to avenge Julia, but first I need to establish a superior confraternity.”
He smiles and shakes his head. “Are you sure about this?”
“I’ll stop at nothing to get back at those bastards,” I growl.
“To have power is expensive…it’ll cost you.”
“Whatever it’ll cost, Uncle.”
“Time is what I mean.”
“How much time are you asking?”
“Forty days.”

First semester exam will be starting in a few weeks, and the NUGA games less than two months after. If Dele makes good his threat to attack me during the exams, I’d still not be able to take my exams any way.
“How soon can we leave?”
“Next week.”
“It’s a date.”

“When I was in the university, the cult wasn’t very common, but I was privileged to be involved in the formation of the Black Knights. When we return, I’ll put you through on what it takes to start a fraternity. I want you to execute my kind of justice on those bastards.”
“Judgment is without mercy to him that shows no mercy,” I say with a gruff.
He stands on his feet and walks to the door leaving the room. I slip into the duvet.

chapters.title: Chapter FOURTEEN
content: Had the police acted upon our report about the rape charge we brought against Femi and his colleagues, I would never have settled on personally avenging Julia’s rape, neither would I have changed my mind about the fraternity. It would definitely not have crossed my mind to get involved with the cult.

The police dismissed our allegation as lacking in proof and frustrated all our efforts to bring Femi and his friends to book, because of his father’s position in the Government. Bitty felt humiliated at their attitude and talked about getting his cousin Dickson involved, but I disagreed.

Big Brother Dickson was the one, I believe, that suggested to Ifeanyi to sturdily dissuade me from getting entangled in the cult, which he enforced by making me swear an oath. I figured it out when Bitty finally confessed his real reason for not participating in the games – Dickson threatened to personally kill him if he dares go near the basketball court.

He gave me a similar admonition when he learnt of my love for the game, not with a threat though. In addition, Dickson sternly warned me to stay away from the fellowship of the spirits, and gave me an unsolicited lecture about the effect of alcohol and other addictive substances on my tender brain, as if his has some kind of immunity against such terrible defects. I’ve managed to cope without beer since Ifeanyi travelled, but I still miss Madam Jay Dee’s chilli chicken. Nonetheless, I thanked him for his advice.

The two friends, Dickson and Ifeanyi, chose different paths to keep us away from joining the fraternity, but one person is about to change all that. The strength of their admonition will not stand the power of our mutual love for Julia, Bitty and I.

Although he has kept his cousin’s word and kept his distance from the basketball court, and he is studious and takes his academics very seriously, Bitty has a weakness, which I hope to exploit in convincing him to join force with me in my revenge mission against Golgotha fraternity.
I can hear Afro beat playing loudly from his music set, a habit he inherited from his cousin Dickson. When I knock a second time, the door swings open.
“Iyke!” Bitty screams.
“How you dey Bitty?” I greet him in pidgin English.
“Where the hell have you been buddy?”
“Long story. Won’t you let me in?”
“Sorry,” he steps aside.
He offers me a seat and I drop my bag on the floor.
“Wetin happen now?” he asks again.
“It is well,” I reply smugly.

“I’m not sure it is. You missed your entire first semester exams and here you are telling me it’s well,” he says with obvious exasperation at my nonchalance. “Do you know the implication of what you’ve done? You’ll never be able to read medicine again and I seriously doubt the possibility of your completing your studies. What has come over you?”
“Stop acting as if you’re not aware of what’s going on between Dele and I.”
“That’s an empty threat. Was that the reason you abandoned your exams?”
“There’s a saying that he who fights and runs away lives to fight another day,” I reply standing on my feet and pacing to his reading table.
“I told you to let me handle those weasels.”

“You know, Bitty, I used to think that those who take the law into their own hands were irrational. Now I know that sometimes one is left without a choice.” I pick a photograph from Bitty’s collection – the picture Julia, Bitty and I took at the matriculation – and squint at it for a while.
Bitty comes to me and stands beside me.

“See what they’ve done to our Julia, Bitty,” I whisper. “We live in a crazy world, Bitty. Everybody is crazy. What do you call those guys who shoot innocent people down at will uh? What do you call those lunatics who molest and rape girls at will? Why do they do that? You think it’s because they’re the most virile of men?”
Bitty squints at me with pursed lips.
“No Bitty. The world is run by lunatics?” I say ponderously.
“That’s not the way to handle such matters, Iyke calm down,” Bitty replies.
“Calm down? Great! Well, I can’t…I won’t calm down.” I drop the photograph on the table and return to my seat.
Bitty picks up the photograph and looks intently at it.
“The police couldn’t help us…because Femi is untouchable…but I’ll break him.”
“There are consequences,” he warns.
“All is fair in love and war,” I cut in, “I’m ready to bear the consequence.”
“Jeez! Man you talk tough. Where have you been?”
“I went to fortify myself,” I reply standing up.
Bitty’s eyes pop. “Fortify yourself, what do you mean fortify yourself?”
I pace back and take the bag that I dropped near the chair and throw it to Bitty.
“What is this?” he asks as he catches it.
“Have a look.”
Bitty opens it and brings out one of the pistols in the bag. “Jeez! What do you intend to do with this?” he asks me as he put the pistol back into the bag.
“What do you think that is used for?”
“This is madness!” he protests, throws it back at me and stands up.
“No Bitty. It’s not. We’re going to take back what the Golgotha has stolen from us.”
“Count me out, I’m sorry.”
“Don’t assume you’re innocent of her rape Bitty, we wouldn’t have been talking about this if you didn’t give her my address you know that.”
“Don’t try to stir unnecessary sentiments to get me involved in this crazy idea okay, I’m not interested, period!” he shoots back.

“Golgotha has stolen our souls, Bitty. They took our game. Now they’ve taken our girl, and all we have to do is wait for the police.” I take the bag and zip it up slowly. “Is that the honour we could do for love? She’s not gonna be right again Bitty. Julie’s not gonna be right again…don’t you have any feelings for her?”
“I do but – but not as much as to take somebody else’s life. I’m sorry,” Bitty flounders.
“What are you afraid of?”
“You know nothing about the Golgotha, Iyke. They’re not like the rest…they’re diabolic.”
“The time of their reign has ended. I have received authority to reign as the new king, to execute judgment on them.”
“Authority, from who?”
“From the GODs,” I say curtly.

Bitty gives me the look of, what the hell is that. I suspect that if I let him know the true character of the group, the spiritual aspect of the Order, it’ll spoil my chances of getting him to comply with me.
“The GODs is a powerful organization that draws its membership from societal elites, successful lawyers, politicians and some very important people in the society…”
“So what is their interest in this matter?” he snaps.
“My uncle, Dike is a member of the GODs and they are giving me all the support I’d need to establish a fraternity on campus…to avenge his daughter’s rape. Golgotha will submit to our authority.”
“I’ve not consented to your proposal, stop using the pronoun our.”
I pull a chair and sit down. “Sit,” I beckon him to another chair.
Bitty obeys me.

“There comes a time in ones life when we have to release ourselves from the fear within, when we have to stand up against limitations that suppress the revelation of the man within. That is the threshold of self-assertiveness. What’s your definition of a real man Bitty?”
He listens to me silently.

“Haven’t you deceived yourself long enough hiding behind your cousin’s aversion for the cult? Lift your thoughts above the darkness of fear, Bitty,” I pause and brace myself, “I know why you’re so afraid of Golgotha, but I’m afraid if you go on like this you may never grow up to be a man, and you’ll forever be subject to the whims and caprices of scumbags like Dreck and Sazzy.”
He squints inquisitively at me.

Bitty was dating a fellow-fresher that goes by the name Ibironke, a very beautiful girl, classy and of rich parentage too. During the October rush (a period in school when guys exploit the naivety of Jambites to get them to be hooked), she was said to be the most toasted babe on campus. Bitty had the edge over most other guys who fell over themselves to date Ibironke. Bitty and Ibironke had met each other when they both came to do their registration and, naturally, afterwards they started seeing each other. Bitty became as popular as Ibironke and everyone knew he was the stakeholder, as it was referred for such hard-to-gets. But their affair didn’t last long.

Ibironke was spotted by a cult boy nicknamed Sazzy, who was also chasing Ibironke. One day, Bitty went to see Ibironke and bumped into Sazzy in her room. He was asked to leave her room and never to come to see her room again. Ibironke, in defence of Bitty, added fuel to the flames. Sazzy ordered two of his friends with him to carry Bitty and throw him out of the hostel. They did just that. Bitty never went back to that hostel again and he stopped seeing Ibironke.
“How did you know about this?” he asks me with clenched teeth.
“Is that what is important to you now?”
Bitty exhales deeply.
“Let’s do this, Bitty.”
He squints at me and asks, “This group GODs, who are they. Will I get to meet them?”
“Of course you will, when the time comes.”
“Okay, on one condition.”
“Name your price pal.”
“You know, I got to know that Sazzy was also a member of the Golgotha Fraternity,” he quips, “Will you take the court from the Golgotha?”
“You bet,” I say smugly, “I’ll also help you get back to the team and feature at the NUGA. I can persuade Coach Barry to put you in the team.”
“No Iyke you got to understand, Dickson would raise his eyebrows if he hears I’m in the basketball team. It’s too early to start arousing suspicions. So, have you thought of a name for the group?”
“Inverted Cross Fraternity,” I reply.
“Inverted Cross Fraternity,” he repeats grimacing at me. “How on earth did you come about that name?”

I bring out a sheet of paper and hand it over to Bitty. There are twelve names written on it. Bitrus Shak is the first in the list. Bitty looks at me frowning, “How long have you been working on this?”
“Not too long ago,” I reply, “Those are the first disciples of the inverted cross.”

I was right about Bitty. He’s gung-ho about the first mission of the fraternity – to pay Dreck a surprise visit, just the two of us. But when I hint him exactly what I intend to do to him, he trembles and backs out. I expected it.

Bitty thinks it’s extreme, but I maintain my stand – we either bump Dreck off or we forget about the fraternity. When I narrate the experience of the fatal retaliation of his group on the guy who dismembered him, Bitty eventually consents.

There are obviously many ways to kill a man, but choosing among them posed a serious problem, because neither of us had ever been confronted with a decision like this – taking a fellow man’s life. Shooting him in his room when he’s alone would have been a lot easier. But considering that Dreck lives in a densely populated neighbourhood where hoodlums and base fellows call haven, among whom he’s also given kingly honour, it doesn’t sound like a good option. The crack of a pistol would certainly stir up the entire neighbourhood and put us in jeopardy. When I demonstrate to Bitty how everyone will be chasing us down the street shouting, Murderers! Murderers! it put us in a lighter mood.
Bitty and I brainstorm on a couple of options that would attract the least attention, such as, clubbing him to death. Bitty shudders and says it’s too cruel.

Sheer mischief is what inspired my idea on how best to handle Dreck. When I narrated the details to Bitty, he immediately acknowledged there couldn’t be a better option. And so we eventually agreed on a D-day to carry out our plan.
The Seagull

chapters.title: Chapter FIFTEEN
content: After taking care of Dreck, we saddled ourselves, me and Bitty, with the task of organizing the group. We agreed from the onset that initiation into the fraternity was going to be thorough. No sentiments whatsoever would be entertained. And unlike the method employed by most clandestine groups, whereby prospective members are lured, coerced or hoodwinked into it, being a disciple of the Inverted Cross Fraternity will be by the member’s volition. This is to ensure that, in the future, the right calibre of members are recruited.

First, I personally contacted the eleven other disciples one on one and shared the vision with them. Most of them are victims of Dreck’s hard-line attitude to selection of players into the basketball team, so they immediately bought the idea, although at this time Dreck was dead, they were determined to see Golgotha fraternity buried for good.

One disciple whose selection raised Bitty’s eyebrow is Terna Akuma. He is an estranged member of Golgotha fraternity. He and Dreck had severe disagreement over the inclusion of my name in the team list. When I announced my exit from the NUGA squad, he eventually opted out of the basketball team as well. Dreck ordered his immediate excommunication from the Golgotha fraternity.

From that time Bitty harboured a grudge against Terna, which was worsened by our friendship. It was natural for me to be so disposed to him, given the fact that we have mutual enthusiasm to avenge ourselves on Golgotha.

My first official duty after I was inducted into the Order of Dukes as star bearer, and ordained Hangman of the Inverted Cross Fraternity and custodian of the irrevocable testament of the inverted cross was the reading of the family’s constitution to the first twelve disciples. It was a gruesome experience. It was hard for the assembled twelve to take, but they braced themselves and agreed unanimously to abide by the testament, which I explained was essential to the preservation of the family’s influence.

Consequently, I gave up my position as Cardinal to Bitty and Terna became the first High Priest of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. It was a deliberate effort to pacify Bitty that I conceded the position of the Cardinal to him. Terna was, by all estimation, a better candidate for the Cardinalship.

My hair has grown shaggy and my moustache so thick I can barely recognize myself as I stand in front of the mirror. I have neither shorn my hair nor trimmed my moustache in four years. This is the token of my vow of consecration as Hangman of the fraternity. Beside this, I’m also forbidden to have sexual relations with the opposite sex. Although outwardly, there’s an aura of awe about my chastity and separation from girls, inwardly, I burn. I’ve grown weary of this and I can’t suppress this longing any longer. “I can’t wait for an opportunity to enjoy sexual relations with a girl again?” I mutter under my breath.
Not until I shed the last finger of the Hangman. Suddenly the fantasy fades from my mind and my memory goes back to how it all began, the very first day I nurtured the thought of taking a man’s life.

There was a friendly match that evening between our school’s female team and a professional ladies basketball team. I was putting on a black anorak, my head covered in the hood and my eyes shaded behind a pair of sunglasses. Apart from Dreck, no one noticed I was in the court that evening. Not even Coach Barry could pick me out from the crowd that day as I sneaked in at half time and sat down quietly watching Dreck instruct his girls in the court. My presence was immediately felt as he began to flounder with directions and the girls began to perform so badly that Coach Barry had to take over.

By the time I left the court as unnoticed as I had come a few minutes to the end of the game, I was confident the damage had been done, they were going to lose the game and Dreck will be blamed for their dismal performance. For that moment, I was satisfied the patrons of the female team will take seriously the allegation of sexual inducement in team selection, which compromised the selection of the best players, and he will be relieved of his job eventually. But the contemplation of what was going to happen to him later that night made me tremble.

Bitty and I paid Dreck a visit that night. Even though we went along with our pistols, it was merely for self-defence. Besides, it made our mission businesslike, but our real weapon of destruction was a masking tape, a face towel, a rope and a padlock. And of course facemasks to conceal our identity in case any passers-by stumbled on our way.

Unfortunately for Dreck, he was alone in the room playing music and possibly hatching up some evil thoughts. We rattled the Golgotha knock code and he was deceived by the codename Bitty adopted, it was the codename of one of his members. He was terribly shaken when he saw Bitty, and I. We overpowered him and stuffed the face towel into his mouth, gagged him with the masking tape and tied his hands and feet with the tape. I put his head into a loop, raised him up to the ceiling and suspended him there. When the noose tightened on his neck, he struggled and kicked his legs in the air. We locked him in the room with our padlock and vanished out of sight. His body was recovered nearly forty-eight hours later.

That was how I came about the name Hangman for the office that was later ratified by the Grand Order of Dukes. Bitty took my place as Cardinal in the Mount of Horeb. And as it is in the parent cult, in the Order, he and three other disciples make up the inner circle or in our fraternal parlance, the precinct caucus – the High Priest, the Rainmaker and the Navigator. They are in charge of affairs on campus.

My responsibility apart from being the custodian of the irrevocable testament of the Inverted Cross Fraternity is to oversee the activities of the group in the eight universities and five polytechnics where our camp exists presently. The Hangman is the only member of the Inverted Cross Fraternity that fraternizes with the GODs occupying the position of the star bearer in the Order.

After the elimination of the other three culprits that came with Femi that night, the Golgotha began to make frantic efforts for us to reconcile our differences, but nothing would deter me from accomplishing my purpose, not when the chief culprit is still alive. Femi is mystic, and like his mentor, Dreck, he doesn’t hide the fact that he practices voodoo. With the aid of his charms he has staged a vanishing act twice at two crucifixion parades, but his judgment day has finally come and there is no escape for him this time, his charms will not save him.

I hurriedly dress up in a black jean trouser and a black turtleneck long sleeved shirt, and then I put on the crest of the star bearer, a bulky silver necklace with an inverted cross pendant, the symbol of power and authority of the fraternity.

The other items I’ll need for tonight’s hit are hidden in the roof. I quickly take a chair and climb up to reach a false ceiling. Pushing the ceiling board away, I bring down a black duffel bag, which contains a large customised hammer and its custom made leather sheath for wearing it on the body, and a black leather armband that holds five bronze nails, called the fingers of the Hangman. These are the insignia of the office of a Hangman. There are only two nails remaining in the leather quiver. I check to make sure my dagger, black stocking facemask, and a black beanie with the sign of an inverted cross are all in the bag.

After dressing up and lacing up my black sneakers, I return to the cloth hanger to take my automatic pistol, which I tucked into the inner pocket of a red blazer concealed by the clothes on the hanger fixed to the wall. As soon as I push the cloth apart to reach the red jacket hung against the wall, I hear a knock at the door so I stop and push the clothes together to conceal the jacket again, and turn to answer the door.

It wasn’t until after my first meeting with Sephia that I began to have the urge to break my consecration vow. That day, Bitty had asked me to accompany him to visit her in her room in the hostel. I was visibly and completely spellbound by her beauty.
She’s wearing a ravishing smile that betrays her motive for this uninvited visit to my bunk. “Hi,” she greets smiling at me.
“Hello Sephia,” I respond curtly holding myself from gushing.
“Won’t you let me in?”
“Oh I’m sorry please come in,” I shudder making way for her.
“Thank you,” she replies politely and steps in. “Nice place,” she compliments taking a curious look at the painting on the wall, the exotic painting I took from Ifeanyi’s room.

“Thank you,” I reply looking intently at Sephia’s curvaceous body. She’s putting on a mini skirt and fitted blouse. I abandon myself to running imagination. “Please sit down,” I point her to the chair.
She ignores me and sits rather on the bed crossing her legs intentionally allowing her skirt pull up slightly to show her light-skinned thighs. “Looks like you’re about going out?”
“I was, actually.”
“To a party?”
“Well uh – something like that,” I flounder.
“I hope you don’t mind my barging in on you uninvited?”
“It’s okay Sephia, I mean you’re welcome anytime,” I reply. How could I possibly turn back such a beauty?
“Well, I was feeling bored in the hostel, and when I was thinking of some lively place to go to, you suddenly flashed on my mind.”
“A pretty girl like you shouldn’t be roasting on a Valentine Day?” I reply with a jest.
“Let’s say I’m in love with someone who doesn’t feel like spending the Valentine with me,” she says with a tone of humour.
I remain speechless and straight-faced as if I’m really considering her hint. I’m not in the mood for this talk and I haven’t got the time too.
“So, are you going with somebody?”
I look at my watch.
“It looks like you’re late for your date. Maybe I should come and see you some other time?”
“No, no Sephia please stay.”

I’ve kept my consecration vow for four years. Now there’s a conflict between my emotions and my reason. My feelings want her to stay, but my mind tells me to let her go. I fear the consequence of breaking this vow. This night may be my last crucifixion parade. And I have looked forward to it. My fingers will be completely shed tonight, then I’ll call for the Passover and handover the mantle, and then I’ll be released from this vow.
“I’m surprised how you got to know where I stay.”
“Ah ah,” she exclaims with an alluring laughter. “Let’s say an angel brought me,” she continues snickering.
“An angel!” I feign surprise. “So why did you leave her standing outside?”
“It’s a he angel,” she giggles, “I wouldn’t risk having a female as an escort lest she snatches my handsome Valentine from me.”
“You sound like a jealous person.”
“I am very jealous,” she chortles.
“I’m not comfortable with jealous people.”
“Why? There’s nothing wrong with being jealous if the relationship is worth it and the two people cherish one another, is there?”

“I know you’ll definitely try to defend yourself.” I say trying to put an end to any further discussions. I’m already behind schedule for the meeting with my colleagues. I have to make up my mind to leave.
“You’re nervous. I hope I’m not interrupting something?”
I glance at her and remain silent.
“Can I have a look at those?” she says pointing at my picture album. I give it to her.
Sephia silently looks at the pictures, while I think of how best to handle the situation. Maybe I’ll just allow her stay and wait for me. I suppose it’ll be a lot easier for both of us.
“Who’s this pretty babe?” She asks showing me Nkeonyere’s photograph.
“My elder sister, Nkeonyere…she’s now married.”
“Wow she’s so pretty,” she exclaims.

Nkeonyere got married to a rich airline pilot after her youth service in Lagos. I’ve not seen her since she graduated. I pretended to be opposed to her decision to get married to a guy from the west, a Yoruba, and refused to attend her wedding, but the real reason was that I didn’t want to see my father. I swore he won’t set eyes on me until I graduate from college of medicine. I hear she’s now a mother of two. Before Nkeonyere left school, she berated me for making a two point three-six GPA at the end of my first year, which marred my chance of switching over to medical school.

After four years of establishing the cult group my academic career is hanging in the balance. My commitment to family affairs is certainly the nail in my coffin, yet I am bent on fully avenging Julia. I seriously doubt if I’ll ever be able to settle down with my studies until I hand over the mantle of leadership.
“Are you alright Iyke?” Sephia brings me back to the room.
“I’m sorry I was thinking about something.”
“Looks like you’re not comfortable with my visit.” She drops the album and makes to stand up.
“No please Sephia,” I restrain her.
She sighs and shakes her head and takes the album again. “Is this your sister also?” she asks showing me the photograph I took during my matriculation with Bitty and Julia.
“No she’s not,” I reply coldly, “My cousin, Julia.”
“Really! How close?”
“First.”
“I can see a resemblance, you guys have the same shape of nose,” she giggles.
“Do we?”
“Yeah…she’s so pretty. Is she here too?”
“She used to.”
“She’s graduated?”
“No,” I hesitate, “She uh – she quit.”
“I don’t want to talk about her,” I say with a gruff.
“I’m sorry.”
“She’s in the asylum,” I tell her calmly.

She stares at me without responding probably expecting me to tell her more about Julia. The memory of what happened that day flashes on my mind, my vision turns blurry and tears trickle slowly down my cheeks.
“I’m so sorry.”
“It’s okay Sephia,” I whisper forcing a smile.

Sephia closes the album and drops it on the bed. She’s finally ready to go, I suppose. I watch her impatiently. A sudden feeling of rage surges over me, I twitch and look at my watch. I’m already twenty minutes late for the meeting. This could jeopardize our plan. What the hell am I doing here with this brat? I tell myself in annoyance.
“Sephia please I have to go and take care of something urgently. If you don’t mind you can wait for me. I won’t be long.”
“But you said you were –”
“It just came up, I mean I just remembered it. Alternatively…” I hesitate. I can’t tell her to go. “I swear I won’t be long.”
“I have something for you Iyke just hold on,” she says smiling.
“May be later okay, when I return.”
“Just one minute, Iyke,” she coaxes reaching for her handbag. “Close your eyes.”
I hesitate looking askance at her.
“Come on Iyke close your eyes,” she wheedles.
I do as she says.
“Now open your eyes…a gift of love for you,” she whispers holding out a small box wrapped in a shiny paper. “Take it Iyke, it’s for you,” she says as I hold back from accepting it.
“What have I done to you to deserve this?”
“It’s a gift Iyke. We mustn’t necessarily do something to earn a gift. Happy Valentine Iyke…open it,” she whispers.

I gently unfurl the wrapper and open the small black box. An eighteen-karat gold watch, an exquisite craftsmanship of Rolex seats gorgeously in the black box. I look up in astonishment and meet Sephia’s gaze. “This is too much Sephia. I can’t accept this.”
“I didn’t know how to tell you…so I thought it’d be easier to say it this way,” she exhales heavily, “I love you Iyke,” she finally submits.
“It’s beautiful Sephia, but…I can’t accept it please.”
“Come on Iyke,” she enthuses, “Come on let’s see how it fits.”

I submit my left limb. She heartily takes it and removes the nameless watch I’ve been wearing for more than three years, it’s been begging for replacement. She flings it on the ground and fastens the Rolex onto my hand.
“What do you think?”
I admire the watch in my hand.
“Do you like it?” She asks.
You bet! “It’s uh – it’s…” I bleat groping for the right words. “I don’t know what to say Sephia, I really don’t deserve this.”
“Oh! It’s nothing okay,” she says chuckling. “You deserve the best my Valentine.”
It’s nothing!

Sephia is the first daughter of a rich hotelier who owns the prestigious Ocean View Hotels and a shipping company, Ocean Cruiser Nigeria Limited. Bitty told me she’s visited virtually all the major cities around the world. So I think I understand when she says it’s nothing.

At this rate, I seriously consider making her stay and wait for me. But I’m still haunted by the foreboding of the consequence of breaking my vow of consecration. “I don’t want this to happen Sephia,” I tremble, “Please, I can’t accept this.”
“I don’t care what happens, I just want us to be together,” she whispers. Then she rises from the bed, comes to where I’m sitting and gently caresses my hair with her fingers.
The whiff of the scent she’s wearing makes me dotty about her. I twitch and shut my eyes. “You can’t love me Sephia, please,” I whisper holding her hand to make her stop.

“It’s too late Iyke,” she whispers back in a starry-eyed tone. She bends over and gives me a peck on my brow. “I thought I was going crazy after that night I set my eyes on you until Agatha said…” she giggles and cuts off.
“She said what?” I ask her amorously.
“She said I was in love.” She comes over to my front and bends on her knees looking dreamily at me, “Don’t you love me?”

Her question suddenly stirs a mixed feeling of love and hate. Whatever feeling I have for Sephia is cancelled out by my concern about what may likely happen if I decide to break my consecration vow before I fully shed my fingers. I regret swearing that oath. I run my hardened palm slowly on her cheeks. She flushes and closes her eyes.

Two fast knocks, one and three slow knocks – the fraternity’s knock code – is rattled on the door. My heart misses a beat. I startle and turn towards the door. When I face her again, there’s a puzzled gaze on Sephia’s face.
“Are you expecting somebody?” Sephia quizzes.
“I want you to wait for me Sephia, will you?”
Bitty calls my name and knocks at the door again – two fast knocks, one, then three slow knocks.
Sephia looks at the door. “How long are you going to be away?” she whispers.
“Half an hour at most,” I reply.
“That long?” she whines.
“I’ll be back in half an hour, trust me.” I take the black duffel bag and put it on my shoulder.
Sephia looks askance at me. I look around the room and pick up a magazine and hand it to Sephia, “Keep yourself company with this, and make sure you don’t open the door to anybody okay.”
Her eyes are fixed on the inverted cross pendant on my chest. She holds the pendant and examines it. I slowly free the chain from her and tuck it back into my jacket.
She shakes her head disappointedly and whispers, “Don’t tell me you’re a member of the Inverted Cross Fraternity?”
“I won’t be long okay,” I reply ignoring her question. I try to kiss her but she moves her head away from me. Then I turn and walk slowly to the door.
Dewy-eyed she says coldly, “You won’t find me here when you return. And please don’t come looking for me.”

Without turning back to her, or saying a word, I open the door and leave the room. Bitty’s eyes blaze furiously at me against the dark night. He quietly turns and walks away. Terna Akuma gawps at me, and then follows him. I gather myself and tag along.

chapters.title: Chapter SIXTEEN
content: Harlem is the general fraternal parlance for our meeting place. I also adopted the slang fellowship of the spirit as codename for our meetings. The fellowship of the spirit could be convened in a pub, a lecture room or any of the school’s cafeterias depending on the urgency of the meeting, the level of secrecy it demands and how many disciples are expected to attend such a meeting.

For security reasons, especially if a full house is expected, the fellowship of the spirit is convened in Goshen, the family’s meeting ground in a forest. But if for any reason the fellowship is convened elsewhere, it is made to appear as casual as possible so that it doesn’t attract attention of the public, and it must be concluded in the shortest possible time. For the same reason, the fraternity forbids convening the fellowship of the spirit in a disciple’s house or hostel room.

It is also forbidden, for security reasons, with the exception of meetings held in public places, for disciples to address one another by their real names during the fellowship of the spirit. It is an ancient practice among cult groups, especially in the bush, to address one another by false names that had earlier been assigned to them. In the Inverted Cross Fraternity, it is the High Priest’s responsibility to codename each disciple at their induction. A disciple’s false name is his ivy. However, these names are never, under any circumstance, supposed to be used anywhere else among family members except during our secret meetings.
My ivy is the Seagull, Bitty’s name in Harlem is Bloody Sniper, and Terna Akuma, the High Priest’s bush name is Whirlwind.

None of us uttered a word to one another through the fifteen-minute drive on our way to Harlem – a forest located at a short distance from the school premises. Terna is nearly a speechless fellow, he wears a permanent smile on his face that makes him falsely appear timid, but only those of us close to him know it’s only a natural make-up that veils the enormous evil going on in his heart. Bitty is too angry to speak. I know why. We’ll talk it over later.

Bitty parks his car at a restaurant very close to the school gate. We all disembark from the car look around to be sure no one is watching and quietly ease into the road to a spot marked as our entry point into the forest.

“We’re thirty minutes behind schedule already, Hangman,” Whirlwind finally breaks the ice as soon as we get into the bush track that leads to the ‘ground’, “Do you still want the crucifixion parade to proceed?” he asks me.
“He leaves the rabble at ten, doesn’t he?” I ask him. The rabble is the codename for the location where the hit will take place.

“Yes. But the plan didn’t make accommodation for the Valentine’s Day celebration. Sniper is afraid that may altar our plan,” Whirlwind replies and glances at Bitty, but he walks ahead of us, turning a deaf ear to our hushed chatter. “He said he may leave the rabble earlier.”
“Has anyone confirmed that he’s been to the rabble?”
“Of course he is. We dropped Rhesus off on our way to your bucket. Sniper detailed him to keep an eye on him,” he replies.
“I’ve taken care of that. If we don’t get him there, we’ll go to plan B,” I reply.
“Plan B?” Whirlwind chuckles, “There’s no plan B, Hangman.”
“There is,” I say and look at my watch.

“And what’s that supposed to mean?” Bitty snaps angrily and turns around to face Whirlwind and me. “I won’t subscribe to any incoherent plan that would jeopardize my territory. I advise that you terminate the parade and dismiss the fellowship.”
“May I remind you that this is a crucifixion parade, and I, the Hangman am in charge,” I reply smugly.
“Listen Seagull, I’m not interested in your so-called plan B. You have jeopardized the plan from the onset. I suggest you call off the parade,” he shoots back.

“Hey, hey, hey Sniper,” Whirlwind hushes him, “Let’s forget about what happened and hear him out okay. Besides if we leave here in ten minutes time we still have the chance of catching him at the rabble,” Whirlwind explains.

“Chance!” Bitty chuckles, halts and turns to us, “Has it come to taking a chance. This is ridiculous. We’re talking about taking a chance on a crook like Femi. This guy has escaped us twice, which obviously has put a question mark on the claim of our supremacy over the Golgotha. We can’t afford to be taking a chance on him.”
“Our supremacy is not in doubt Sniper,” I snap angrily at him. “If there’s any such doubt it exists only in your mind.”
“Shall we make some progress now?” Whirlwind tells him. They both glare at one another.

Whirlwind and Sniper seldom agree on family issues, especially when it has to do with assaults. Whirlwind is always agitated when there is a sting. He’s the type that unusually delights in hurting people. He once usurped Bitty’s authority as Cardinal and led a group to a sting that was not approved by the precinct caucus.

Apart from his romance with Golgotha fraternity, Bitty was against my appointing him as High Priest because of his addiction to weed. In our fraternity, the High Priest is equivalent of the Bookkeeper in the Mafia. But unlike the Mafia where sensitive information is documented in a book, here it is entirely committed to memory. Bitty cited that he was a drug addict and wasn’t suited for the position, and argued why the secrets of the family should be committed to the care of a junkie.

Whirlwind is addicted to Indian hemp for sure, and he occasionally loses it, but the other three members of the precinct caucus favoured his appointment stating that he possessed the wits required of that sensitive post.

He is flanked on the right by the Navigator, whose real names are Bature Audu and his ivy is Kiwi. Jerome Emenalor, the last of the precinct caucus, the Rainmaker is standing on my left. His inverted name is Liquid fire. With the exception of the crucifixion parade, which is the Hangman’s prerogative, the Rainmaker commands all assault operations, while the Navigator is in charge of logistics and the armoury.

In addition to moderating the initiation and assigning names to new disciples, the High Priest issues decisions taken by the precinct caucus to the underlings called satraps, who are in charge of provinces – hostels and off-campus residential quarters occupied by family members. They in turn pass it on to the respective disciples as directed by the High Priest. In order to maintain the highest level of secrecy required of such organisation, each disciple receives signals directly from the satrap assigned to his province, and oftentimes such information is not communicable to other members of the group.

Because the Hangman is presumed infallible, and too sacrosanct to apologize to subordinates, my address does not proffer an apology for my lateness. It shouldn’t, otherwise I’ll be undermining my authority as the Hangman. “Shezou brothers!” I greet them again.

“Shezou!” they respond impassively. Not only have the thirty-five disciples detailed to take part in the parade apparently lost their patience, they are eager to file out for the sting so my last minute instructions on the attack is brief.

“In addition to the instructions earlier given, the soundtrack is codenamed the gift of the gods. The time of play is five degrees. The disc must disengage at uh, northwest-west.” Time code representing fifteen minutes to ten and the time of play is the estimated duration of the attack. “Recoil code is volcano. Is that taken?”
“Shezou,” they all echo.
“Rolling time is two degrees from now,” I continue, “So is the bee ready to sting?”
“Shezou!”
“Shezou brothers,” Bitty breaks in, “Do I have the permission of the Hangman to address this fellowship?”
“Speak up Sniper you got thirty seconds.”

“I have a problem with your plan B Hangman. This arrangement is strange to the precinct caucus and I believe I am speaking the opinion of the caucus. The plan cocoon lacks proper logistics that is expected of a crucifixion parade.”
Members of the precinct caucus eye each other and remain silent.

Because of what happened in the room, it’s hard for me to tell whether Bitty is acting out of genuine concern or slyly plotting the failure of the hit. He is capable of destabilizing the plan if he succeeds in dragging us into an argument about the timing of the hit. Although he’s evidently breaching authority, I won’t contest.

“Well, I appreciate your concern about the plan Cardinal, but I reckon the silence of the caucus means every one is at home with plan cocoon,” I reply tactically, being wary of his plot to get me into a debate and finally destabilize the group.
“I suggest that this parade be suspended until adequate plans are made.”

“That’s impossible Sniper!” Whirlwind snaps, “We’ve always had an alternative strategy at the planning stages of every hit, if the Hangman says he has made adequate considerations before conceiving the plan we should give him the benefit of the doubt.” His eyes dart between Kiwi and Liquid fire. “The plan cocoon is carried by the precinct caucus,” he submits.
“This is not an option plan. This is a conjecture Whirlwind, and you know it. What is the guarantee that the blot will be found in cocoon?” Bitty giggles.
“Plan cocoon is an option,” Whirlwind replies.
“I’m asking you Hangman,” Bitty snaps grumpily, “What if the plan fails?”
I’ve never seen Bitty like this. “I swear by my throne, Femi’s head will not stand on his neck by midnight.” I turn towards the standing army. “Are the bees ready?”
“Shezou Hangman!” They all pull their facemasks over their faces with gusto and don their berets.

After conducting a routine check on all disciples to make sure that they are properly dressed, in black shirts dark trousers and black shoes, which blends into the dark night, and that their foot wears will not hinder them from moving briskly during the attack, I also inspect every disciple to make sure that everyone is bearing the prescribed weapons, such as, a dagger, an axe, baseball bats, and iron rods, or any other dangerous weapons that is capable of inflicting serious injuries on the victim.

Except a disciple is specifically detailed, the precinct caucus and the eleven underlings – the satraps – are the only ones authorised to bring their pistols to a sting, in fact it is an order for them to come with their flute, as it is called.

Unfortunately Sephia’s unscheduled visit prevented me from bringing my flute with me. My mind races back to my room as I raise my sleeve to check the time by my Rolex. Will I still find her in the room when I return? If she’s still there, what will she be doing all alone in the room – lying on the bed and playing music, curiously fumbling with my effects for some interest, or excitedly trying on my wears for some kind of erotic feeling. What if she finds the pistol in the jacket by the wall? I wonder.
“That’s pretty expensive…a Valentine gift I guess,” Whirlwind says casually and pulls his mask over his face and leaves me.
Bitty glares at me. I draw my facemask and wear the beanie. He looks away from me and joins the waiting army.

We all leave the forest marching in a double file as silently as we had assembled. Kiwi is leading the first row of eighteen disciples to cocoon, Femi’s girlfriend’s hostel, while I lead the second group to the rabble, a pub located in the student’s union building, our original plot for the attack.

Fortunately, there’s no moonlight, the intense darkness affords us a much-needed cover from being seen. One after the other, and timing the passing vehicles, we jump over the fence into the school premises. As soon as we’re in the school compound, we brazen our way out against stunned passers-by who flee on sighting us.
Our calculation is that by the time the astonished public can alert the security to take any actions to stall the attack, we would have disappeared into thin air. And Femi would have been history.

Time catcher bar is the only pub in the student’s union building, and it is the favourite haunt for most frat boys who indulge in liquor. There is a popular saying among frat boys that the joint is the safest place for hard men to drink. Unlike all other joints, legend has it that no one has ever been attacked in the joint before. And that’s because the joint is owned by a retired police officer. That history will be rewritten tonight, and it will not be forgotten in a hurry.

We stop by briefly at a classroom block very close to the joint to tweak our plan. I’ll lead the group through the main entrance as soon as Whirlwind, Rhesus and Planet block the hind door, which leads into the ever-busy refectory to prevent the chances of anybody escaping from the room.

“Silence all man! Be still!” Whirlwind yells as soon as he steps into the room. In spite of the loud sound of rock music playing from a ghetto blaster on the serving table in the bar, I hear the sound of chairs shifting as students scram from the refectory.
In the pandemonium one of the girls in the pub rushes to the door to escape and bumps into me. I hold her and push her back as I lead the rest of the group into the room.
“Shut up all of you. Stand up and raise your hands, now!” Whirlwind orders them.

Planet walks up to another girl squatting on the floor and sobbing. “You got a problem with GS 101 (the undergraduate basic English course titled, Use of English)? Will you shut the hell up and stand up bitch,” he shouts at her. She goes into hysteria and screams out in panic. “Will you shut up and stand on your feet!” Planet shouts at her still.

“It’s okay Planet,” I pull her up by her dress and make her sit on a chair. She stops sobbing and gently stands on her feet placing her hands on her head and peeking at my face as if she’s trying to make out the identity of the face in the mask. I cover her face with my palm and make her drop her gaze to the floor.

Then I pace slowly further into the bar and throw the cassette player down smashing it on the ground. The room is suddenly absolutely quiet. I notice a lollipop stick on the table near an empty bottle of malt on a table. Femi was here.

“I’ll let you continue to have your fun if any of you will tell me where Femi has gone,” I announce as I stare ponderously one after another at the jittery habitués with their hands on their heads. “But if you refuse to tell me, I’ll be forced to do what you all will regret…where is Femi Aguda?” I gamble on the probability that one or two of them may be members of Golgotha fraternity, and if that is the case, he will definitely speak up if threatened.
They remain mute turning away from my concealed face and staring at the floor.
“I don’t believe that none of you has any idea where he has gone.” I continue pacing about the room. “I see. So nobody knows where the penguin has gone…alright?”

I take Whirlwind’s pistol from him and walk to the girl that Planet was talking to and pull her by her right hand to the middle of the room. “You all will have yourselves to blame for the life of this girl if you refuse to tell me where Femi has gone at the count of three.” I cock the pistol and point it to her head placing my finger on the trigger. “One.”
They lift up their heads one after another and anxiously glance at one another speechlessly. “Two.”

At this point I don’t know if I really intend to do this, but I’m desperate to get Femi. Following their glance I notice that one of the boys still has his head dropped to the floor. I suspect he knows something about Femi’s whereabouts.
“You!” I yell at him.
But he maintains his posture.
“Let her go, you murderer!” another boy breaks in angrily, “Why don’t you go find him yourself?”
I take the pistol away and turn slowly facing the boy. Planet comes to me and whispers to me that he’s a Golgite and returns to the position where he was standing.
“Oh! I see, you want to play the brother’s keeper uh. You stupid Golgite…clamp ‘em,” I instruct Whirlwind.
Whirlwind and Planet pounce on him drag him forcefully to the door and hold his hands firmly to the frame.

I take the hammer from its sheath beneath the black trench coat and take one of the two bronze nails from the quiver clasped on my arm and walk slowly towards them. Placing the nail on the back of his palm, I hit it into the boy’s hands pegging him to the doorframe. He screeches and begs me as blood drips from the wound.

Then I put back the hammer into the sheath and take the pistol from Whirlwind again pointing it on his head, “Don’t make me remind you guys how seriously we take our business. Now for the very last time, where is Femi?” I growl.
The boy I suspected to have been with Femi finally speaks up, “Please don’t shoot him. Don’t kill him. He said he was going to see his girlfriend in Victoria Hall.”

I take the pistol away and give it back to Whirlwind. “I wish you all a remarkable Valentine’s Day.” Then I turn to the boy at the doorframe and whisper in his ear, “Pray that I find him there, or else when I return and I’m sure you’ll still be here, I’ll kill you.”

“Compose! The bee will sting at cocoon,” I announce. The faceless heads hidden in black stocking facemasks shift uneasily from one to another, but none of them would dare contest that decision. Despite there are many loopholes in the plan, I maintain a posture of confidence determined to carry it through.

The shortest route that won’t attract the attention of the public leading to the medical centre, our designated rendezvous, is through Liberty Square. The spot was chosen because it overlooks the female hostel and will offer us a proper assessment of the environment, plus, there is minimal security presence at the clinic.

Planet informs me there’s a crusade going on at the square and our presence would definitely stir the crowd and alert the security. We make a detour behind the faculty of education building to join a long but lonely road leading to the medical centre.

Valentine’s Day usually attracts a lot of visitors into the school, especially the female hostels, besieged by lovers visiting from outside with expensive gifts and alluring promises. I glance at my Valentine gift one more time. It’s almost ten thirty.
“What is keeping Kiwi and his men?” I muse.

Whirlwind is the only member of the precinct caucus with me. We put our heads together to fine-tune an ad hoc plan on how to break the dense human traffic at the hostel and secure the area until the sting is completed. But neither of us knows Tinuke’s room number. Somebody in the group should have an idea, but I can’t risk asking them, it will further demoralize them.
“That’s not our immediate problem. I’ll handle that as soon as we break in,” Whirlwind whispers and looks at his watch, “We should be on our way now, Seagull.”
“Shezou sons of darkness!” I turn to address the men with me.
Apparently disoriented by the absence of Kiwi’s men, they grunt, “Shezou.” And stare stonily at me.
“The sting will proceed according to plan. We’re rolling immediately, compose for sting.”
Planet comes to me and whispers, “What of Kiwi and –”
“They’ll join us at cocoon,” Whirlwind snaps, “We don’t have time.”
“I think we should send somebody to find out why they’re not here,” Planet suggests.
“Planet, we’ll see to that later okay,” I reply, “Are all disciples ready to roll?”

They give one another a curious glance and reluctantly put on their masks. Planet pulls his facemask over his face and joins the line. Whirlwind braces the group for assault forming two rows. We stamp our feet and hum as we march to Victoria hall. Frightened passers-by take to their heels screaming at the intimidating sight of a procession of faceless men dressed in black overall.

Traffic outside the two-story building thins out and the hostel is thrown into chaos. There is a stampede at the door as we approach the building. The sound of music abruptly stops and lights in the rooms begin to go off one after another. When we enter the hostel, there is not a single soul left in the foyer.

The porters’ office is securely locked. I instruct Planet to cut the telephone lines to the office and order Rhesus, Naked Wire and Migraine to secure the gate, while the others disperse briskly to both wings of the ground floor. Whirlwind leads the rest to the upper floors of the hostel. Eight disciples are manning the ground floor, four in both wings, and two of them are armed with automatic pistols, while three of them are bearing local pistols.

Escapee goes to a room in the right wing of the ground floor to make inquiry about Tinuke’s room number. His real name is Isokari Ese and he’s about five foot ten inches tall, but very muscular and weighs an incredible hundred and five kilograms. Locked wooden doors are no problem for the macho man. “Room 307,” he announces on his return.

We both race up the stairs to the second floor. Whirlwind joins us to the room. He turns the door handle but it is locked, he pushes the door with his shoulder but it won’t open. When he steps back to kick the door, Escapee stops him and holding the door handle he nudges it with his shoulder. The door flings open.
One of the inmates is kneeling down beside her bed muttering a prayer when I flick the light switch on. I tap her gently on the shoulder and whisper, “Where is Tinuke?”
She twitches and turns around without responding. Whirlwind lifts his hand to slap her.
“Don’t touch the ringo,” I warn him in a hushed tone holding his hand to restrain him.

I sight my gold chain with its cross pendant that Femi took from me four years ago visibly lying on the reading table. I pick it up and put it in my pocket. I notice a man’s leg sticking out under the bed. He is coiled up behind an empty carton. When I push the carton away and drag him out to see his face, I discover disappointingly he’s not Femi. He’s apparently a pleasure-seeker attracted by the Valentine’s Day celebration. He looks forty, clean-shaven and dressed in a grey suit. He begs me not to hurt him.

When I stand up, I notice pieces of broken glass on the bed near the window, and some louvers are missing from the windowpane. Putting my head out of the window, I sight Femi slowly walking on his side on the extended slap of the floor.
“He’s out there,” I whisper to Whirlwind as I turn to him.
“What are we going to do?” he asks.
“I’m going out there to get him. I think he’s going to jump down…get all disciples ready to leave the building.”
“He’s not going to jump from this height Hangman. I don’t think this is such a good idea,” Escapee adds.
“Do what I told you Whirlwind. Evacuate the bees.”
Rhesus breaks into the room. “We got a problem Hangman,” he announces. “Golgotha has regrouped against us.”

The disciples at the ground floor are clustered together at the foyer and Planet is whispering something to them. I can hear Whirlwind’s voice as he barks instructions to the disciples at the first floor. They hastily join the troop from the second floor rushing down the stairs. They sluggishly break the cluster as I approach them.
“Shezou Hangman. Golgotha has regrouped against us,” Planet trembles.
I hesitate. Whirlwind glances at me. I turn to Planet. “What is their strength?” I ask him as I pace to the door.
“We’re outnumbered. They’ve surrounded the entire building,” he replies.
“Any signal from Kiwi’s men?” I ask slowing my pace.
“Not even a breath,” he replies, “What do we do Hangman?”

They all watch me in absolute silence. I know they’re all asking the same question. I open the door a little and sight Cyclone and his men at the car park directly facing the hostel door. He takes a long drag from the cigarette in his hand and throws the stub on the ground. He rubs his hands together and edgily paces about while gazing at the door. There are about fifty boys with him at the car park. Many of them are clutching half-broken beer bottles in their hands, some are having long sticks and iron rods, and two are armed with cutlasses, while one of them is holding a rifle.

By my calculation, they are taken unawares by the hit and if I engage them in a fracas, we’ll overcome them since we have more firepower. But that will put us in a vulnerable position, security-wise. Moreover, it was not part of our plan.
I turn around and glance at their stunned, but masked faces. “Evacuate all man at the signal open sesame,” I command Whirlwind, “I’m going out to meet them.”
“You can’t do that Hangman,” he objects blocking me, “That’s too dangerous.”
“I suppose you have a better idea?” I reply.
“We can break through them. They can’t stop us,” he replies.
“That’s exactly what they want, Whirlwind. But that would be fatal, we won’t recover from it if we walk into this trap,” I explain.
He shrugs and steps aside.
“I’ll go with you Hangman,” Escapee says cocking his pistol.
“Stay,” I tap him on his shoulder and turn to Whirlwind. “Do as I say okay.”
“Shezou Hangman,” Whirlwind finally submits.
I motion the two guys at the gate to open it. They reluctantly obey.
“Have this Hangman,” he says giving his pistol to me.
“Keep it Whirlwind. You’ll need it.” I step out of the hostel and motion them to close the door.

Cyclone is the Capone, the head of the Golgotha fraternity. His real name is Eugene Adebisi, and he’s a part three student of French. Cyclone had met me three times in confidence to intercede for Femi and reconcile our differences. At our second meeting I was constrained to make an empty promise that I will pardon Femi on the condition that he follows me to the institution where Julia is and apologize to her. They suspected I had an ulterior motive – to lynch him – and so they got Femi to put his apology in writing. I tore it in his face and warned Cyclone never to discuss the matter with me again.

He brings out a pistol from his jacket and holds it by his side. I walk with steady steps spreading my hands to indicate I’m not holding a weapon. Half way between the door and where they’re gathered, I stop. He signals his group to hold fire and walks up to meet me.
“You’re very stubborn Natural Mystic,” he says standing in front of me.
“Ask your men to move away,” I bark at him.

He turns and glances back to see if there’s anyone with him then turns back to me, “Why are you bent on doing this in spite of our discussion? You’ve had three of them killed already, isn’t that adequate for an atonement?”
We stare at each other speechlessly for a while.
“Can we talk this over tomorrow, please?” Cyclone pleads. “You must put an end to this bloodshed.”
Two of his men are bearing Femi on their shoulders as they lead him to join the group. “He’s evil…he’s evil,” he mutters glaring at me.
He jumped from the second floor! He should have died.
“An eye for an eye, Cyclone. He must pay for his deed,” I growl. “Judgment is without mercy to him that showed no mercy.”
“If you’re still bent on doing this in spite of our discussion I will take it that you have declared war on the Golgotha, and war you’ll get,” Cyclone threatens me.
I am unperturbed by his threat. Golgotha is no match for the superior power of the Inverted Cross.
“This is personal Cyclone. It has nothing to do with the kparakpo.”
“Damn it! Why didn’t you come for him by yourself if it’s personal? You also nailed one of my men on a doorframe. If anything happens to Femi I swear you’ll pay with your life,” Cyclone warns me.
“You don’t threaten me, Cyclone.” I reply with a giggle gazing steadily at him behind the mask. “Now ask your men to move away.”
Speechless, Cyclone glares at me.
“Where do you think you’re going?” one of Cyclone’s men barks.
Cyclone turns to their direction. It’s Bitty. His men are blocking him from passing. Kiwi has finally come, should I say right on time.
“Let him pass!” Cyclone orders them.
Cyclone squints at the mask trying to identify the person behind it as Bitty walks past them to meet me.
“What is the situation Bloody Sniper?” I ask Bitty briskly.
“The troop Mogadishu is standing by for back up,” he replies curtly.

“Aye,” I respond. There’s no troop Mogadishu, but I quite understand what he’s saying. When I turn around to signal my men, I notice that inmates in the hostel are peeking at us from nearly all the windows. They hurriedly hide away their faces as soon as I turned. “Open Sesame!” I shout with a loud voice. The two leaves of the door creak open. Whirlwind leads a lean, but well-armed band of fifteen men out of the hostel in two rows to join us.

Then I turn around to Cyclone and notice his men have started moving closer to him. But they’re taken unawares by the foot stamping and humming of the advancing troop led by Kiwi. They halt and slowly move back to allow them pass.
“Now ask your men to retreat or this place will become Aceldama,” I tell Cyclone firmly.
He smiles and takes a few steps backwards, turns around and walks towards his men. I angrily watch them disperse taking Femi with them. I failed again.
“That was a smart move,” Whirlwind says.
“Psychological warfare,” I whisper, “That’s what it’s called. Signal for recoil Whirlwind.”
“Volcano!” he thunders.
Bitty and I gaze at each other without saying a word as we all assemble quickly to march to the forest from where everyone will disperse.
He kindly offers to drive me back home from the forest. He has been silent since we left the forest. In the hush, I ponder over the future of my enviable position as Hangman.
“I should have listened to you,” I whisper.
Bitty exhales heavily without saying a word.
“Do you mind telling me what happened, why didn’t you guys show up at the rendezvous?”
“Are you insinuating that’s the reason the hit was botched?” he replies coldly.
Neither of us says a word to each other again until we reach Bauchi road.

I take the silver chain off my neck and undo the sheath bearing the hammer from my body. After loosening the leather armband having one nail in it, I place the symbol of my office as Hangman between us, near the handbrake. Bitty watches me mutely.
“From now on, you are the Hangman, Sniper.”
“No you can’t do that, Seagull.”

I take the gold chain I recovered from Tinuke’s room from the black trench coat and fold it slowly into the duffel bag. “I was taught to keep a vow when I make one,” I say with a sigh. “You made me swear in front of the disciples.”
“What about Femi?”
“I think I’ll let the fire die down for a while.”
“He’s leaving school next month,” Bitty says.
“I’ll let the fire die down for a while,” I whisper, “That doesn’t mean I won’t still do it.”
Bitty glances at me. “Take the hammer then, and the nail, you deserve the honour of fully shedding your fingers.”
“No Bitty.” I turn slowly to him. “It belongs to the Hangman, the new Hangman.”
“Goodnight Bitty.”
“Shezou Hangman,” he greets me with glassy eyes.

“Shezou Hangman,” I reply extending my hand for a handshake. Bitty gives me the fraternity handshake. I alight from the car and watch him drive off. Pacing slowly along the path leading to the apartment, I put the gold chain on my neck and feel the cross pendant with my fingers, it is still as new as it was when I received it from Ifeanyi.

When I raise my head to the block, I notice there’s a crowd gathered outside my room, but I’m too muddled up to figure out what could be happening. I hear footsteps behind me. Intermittently, bright light flashes from my room coming on and off, again and again – it’s a camera flash. As I move closer to the block I notice there’s something that looks like a human body covered with a white cloth lying on the ground. And I can hear voices of people weeping. The sound of the footsteps behind me is now more audible.

I halt and suddenly turn around to face two men right behind me. One of them grabs the waistline of my trouser while the other brings out an identity card from his breast pocket and flashes it to my face.
“Police officers,” he announces, “You are Ikenna Obiajulu, aren’t you?” he asks.
“Yes I am.”
“You are under arrest for the murder of Sephia Rwang,” he says in clipped military tone. His colleague, the one that showed me his ID immediately brings out a handcuff and fastens it on my wrists.

chapters.title: Chapter SEVENTEEN
content: There is no distinction between those in custody awaiting trial and those serving their sentence at the Katampe Maximum Prison. As far as the warder is concerned, we are all guilty and condemned to death until the judge, if we are lucky to get a trial at all, decides otherwise.

Katampe Maximum Prison is located in the desert area of southern Kaduna, the prison is popularly referred to as the last bus stop, and there’s a myth that only a handful of detainees have been privileged to leave the gaol alive. They say it takes a miracle to get out of here alive. Inmates are seldom arraigned in court, and when they get the chance, it’s a mere formality or in the words of the warder, ‘to fulfil all righteousness’.
Because of the slow judicial process, most inmates who have been lucky enough to get a trial do not stay long enough to hear the Judge’s verdict.

Everybody here is potentially a corpse and we look sickly from a meal per day, usually a banquet of beans soaked in brine. I can swear that if it hadn’t been for a few inmates who occasionally receive provisions from visiting relatives and friends many of us would have died of malnutrition. I do not have the privilege of such luxury, I doubt if the news of my detention will ever reach any member of my family, and I’m not expecting any of my fraternity brothers to visit me for obvious reasons.

The law forbids anyone from the fraternity visiting an arrested member in prison custody or detained by the police. I personally wrote that law. The fraternal code also clearly stipulates that in the extreme case, especially if there is fear that the detainee may let on the family secrets to the police during interrogations, the precinct caucus will take the drastic measure of eliminating him to forestall the possibility of the disciple divulging the family’s secret. In other words, as it stands now, I’ve been excommunicated from the family. My link with the family stands broken until I am released from custody.

The wailing siren awakens me from sleep. Inmates are agitated at the number of prisoners disgorged by the Black Maria. They jeer and yell at the prison warder to send them away because the rooms are already filled beyond capacity. But the prison warder pays no attention. He barks at new detainees, urging them to leave the prison limo. Many of them are going to die here, I say to myself.

The rooms are about twenty feet by twenty in dimension, cramped and poorly ventilated. My room is called the Kings quarters. It is probably the best around here and houses sixty-two inmates. Sometimes we get the chance to stretch our legs to sleep, but with the inclusion of nine new inmates, temporarily, according to the warder. He explains that he hopes to relocate them when there’s a vacancy in any of the other rooms – as a result of the death of inmates, which occurs every now and then. Most of us, except the ward officers who sleep on mattresses flattened from long use and infested with the evil bloodsucker (bedbugs) sleep on used paper cartons provided by the warder.
“One day…I’ll find a way out of here,” I mutter.
“There’s a way,” Steve Idoko breaks in whispering. He’s lying on a mattress supporting his diminutive body with his right hand. “But it’ll cost you a fortune.”

Steve is nicknamed Mirage and he’s the oldest surviving inmate in Katampe prison. He seldom joins the jeering crowd or complains about anything. He was arrested eighteen years ago for assassinating a military officer and has never been arraigned in court. Steve is fifty-one years old, has no family, no kids and I can swear he has no future.

“I know I’d get out of this place one day, some how,” I say in a low tone and turn my face back to the window and sadly watch the Black Maria drive away from the prison premises blaring its siren. “One day I’ll be able to live my normal life again,” I say droopily.
“I can help you.”
I turn back to him not knowing whether to believe him or not.
“Like I said, it’ll cost you some money.”
“How much are we looking at?”
“Five grand,” he says as he sits up.
I slump on the floor near the window. “That’s too much.”
“Is it?” he replies with sarcasm.
An inmate peeps in from the corridor and announces, “Harry is here.”
Steve rises from his mattress. “If you’re interested, we’ll talk about it later okay.”
“Are you sure about this?”

He chuckles and leaves the room. I doubt Steve’s seriousness about his supposed chance of escaping from here. If there is, why hasn’t he escaped? Why hasn’t he sold the idea to any one else? Steve is known to be irrational at times, this purported plan maybe one of his illusory idea conjectured from his faith in God. I hope he’s not trying to tell me that God will break open the prison door and lead me out. Although he reads the Bible Harry gave to him conscientiously, I still doubt the genuineness of his conversion.

Harry Onaguruwa is an evangelist working with the Gideons International. He and Steve met five years ago when the group came to preach the gospel and share Bibles to inmates. He has been coming since then to minister to the inmates, but somehow he took particular interest in Steve, and regularly brings supplies of provisions for him. I bet he also prays for him every blessed day.

Harry’s fellowship, as we refer to it, is the only lively activity in the prison. His session of praise songs is usually a great relief for us, and virtually every one of us looks forward to it. We usually break our routine and spare the time to hear him preach, notwithstanding that we hardly take whatever he says with a pinch of salt. Apart from the soothing effect of the songs, everybody knows about Harry’s generosity. Many of us neither believes his message nor trust in his God, but because of the provisions usually distributed after the session, we put up with him.

When Steve introduced me to Harry, he took me exactly the way he took Steve. He was particularly impressed when I requested for him to help me with journals, which was a break from the usual request among the folks for edibles and toiletries. He gladly accepted to keep me updated anytime he comes around and he’s not failed his word since then.

I join a host of inmates who flock around the pragmatic preacher so I can get a bunch of old and recent magazines. Harry is a kind soul and goes the extra mile to meet everybody’s needs, as much as he’s able.

It is understandable why the accused, the condemned and new inmates green with the expectation of a trial look forward to the fellowship with Harry, many of them say his message lifts them from the depths of despair, which as far as I am concerned is an illusion – they’d promptly turn down an invitation to a church service were they free citizens. For me, it’s the occasional supply of magazines, which soothes away the ennui and doldrums of prison life, that takes me to his fellowship. I wait patiently, half-listening and mostly hoping that I’ll make it out of here some day.

On each visit, Harry has a new and different message for us and at the end he invites us to commit our lives to Christ, he would also request that we confide in him and tell him our needs after which he’d pray for us and with comforting words tell us he’d continue to pray for us. I’ve never answered his call to accept the lordship of Jesus, neither have I ever asked him to pray for me.

Today, Harry is preaching a heart-searching sermon about the two thieves who were crucified with Jesus to an enthralled audience sitting in a cramped room nicknamed the Gavel. Leaning on the wall where I usually stand with hands folded, I listen with obvious disaffection as Harry renders a matter-of-fact account of the incident as if he were on Calvary hill at that precise moment. A strong feeling of guilt sweeps over me as he begins to narrate the confession of one of the thieves at the crucifixion of Jesus. At the end of his message, Steve and a few others walk up to Harry, in tears, in response to his call to come to Christ and receive the gift of salvation.

This emotional response occurs nearly every time Harry preaches, and Steve always leads the procession. But today, there’s something unique about their response to Harry’s, or the Lord’s call in his words – they’re all weeping as they march to Harry and either kneel down or sprawl on the dirty floor at Harry’s feet whimpering like little children begging God to forgive their sins. Harry prays to God to pardon their sins in the name of Jesus. Unable to bear the guilt feeling from watching this bunch of criminals, I quietly leave the room.

When I return to the Gavel after the session of prayers to receive journals from Harry, I find they’re all sitting down and beaming with smiles at the assurance that they now have a part in God’s kingdom and their names written in the book of life. Great! What an illusion?

Harry disappointingly informs me that he was unable to bring any journals for me. In his habitual good nature, he apologizes and promises to make it up on his next visit. But there’s something else I want to ask Harry.
“Can you do me a favour uh – Brother Harry?”
He smiles widely. I know, Brother! I could call him anything now – evangelist, apostle, prophet, bishop or whatever – anything that’ll make him help me deliver this letter to Uncle Dike.
“I’m sorry I can’t do it, please,” he whispers.
“Why? It’s not a difficult place to locate –”
