I know that when Felix comes up for cross-examination he’ll not fail to lay it bare to him that I’m not only in the fraternity, but that I’m a very dangerous person. He’ll be so shocked to hear that and Felix will stop at nothing to damage my reputation.
“Professor Aliu, among other things you referred to the accused as being a focused young man,” Felix says wearing a mischievous smile.
“Yes,” Prof. Aliu reaffirms.
“Are you aware that the accused person at the time of his arrest was already doing an extra year in school?”
“Yes, I am aware,” Professor Aliu replies.
“Are you also aware that the accused person is standing trial today for the rape and murder of a fellow student called Sephia Rwang?”
“Yes I am.”
“Are you aware that sequel to the murder of Sephia Rwang the accused person was expelled from the school for involvement in cult activities?” Felix continues.
“It is both regrettable and speculative.”
“How exactly do you mean, Professor?” Felix quizzes.
“His expulsion is regrettable, the claim of his cult involvement speculative,” he explains.

“Uh – Professor, apart from spending an extra year in school, failure to pass exams as at when due and the inability for a young man to graduate with his peer,” Felix pauses, “What other of these deficiencies are your parameter for judging a student as focused?” he asks with derision.
“What nonsense are you talking about?” Prof. Aliu responds irritably.

“I’m talking about a young man that was expelled for involvement in secret cult activities after spending an extra year in school, unable to pass his exams and in that frustration murdered an innocent girl, whom you so assertively refer to as your son,” Felix says in clipped tone.
Frank holds Segun’s hand preventing him from standing up to object Felix’s line of questioning.
“What nonsense. I cannot take this insult I am a don,” Prof. Aliu flares at him.
“Mr. Felix, please be courteous,” Justice Kachalla calls Felix to order.
Felix takes a newspaper and flips the pages quickly while the court remains silent. “Professor, would you please acknowledge the authorship of this article.”
The clerk comes to him and takes the newspaper to Professor Aliu. He glances through it and gives it back to the clerk.
“I wrote the article,” he responds.
“My lord, I would like to admit the Daily Times newspaper dated nineteenth February nineteen eighty-two as evidence,” Felix says.
“Objection my lord! In view of the date of publication of such an article, we wish to challenge its relevance as evidence in this case,” Segun blast back.

There is slight hesitation from the Judge. “The court admits Daily Times newspaper dated nineteenth February nineteen eighty-two in evidence, it is marked exhibit B. But the weight of the evidence will be determined upon hearing the content of the said article. Please, read out the relevant portion of the article.”
“Thank you, my lord,” Felix greets.
Segun shrugs his shoulders and takes his seat.
“Do you have any problem with my decision Mr. Segun?” Judge Kachallah noticed his shrug.
“Not at all my lord… my lord I apologize,” Segun says bowing gently to the Judge.
“Professor, you certainly have an unusual interest in cult related issues,” Felix says as he leisurely unfolds the newspaper in his hand.

“Every patriotic citizen of this nation should be concerned about the spate of cult activities in our institutions of learning and its ominous effect on the potentials of our children,” Prof. Aliu replies coldly.

“You made some points in your article that suggest you’re either a protagonist of the cult system or a renegade cultist…I will read a portion to refresh your memory.” Felix takes his eyeglasses from the case.
Professor Aliu glares at him.

“You said here and I quote,” Felix peers into the newspaper, “The war against cultism, secret societies and the likes in our universities, polytechnics and institutions of higher learning will remain the nation’s longest internal battle, yet all the organizations that oppose its activities have been greeted with reprisal sophistication from these groups who appear to be winning the battle. The irony of this war is that like prisoners of war, these cultists are themselves taken captive of their own internal battles...bla bla bla,” Felix looks up facing the witness box, “Professor, how did you come to know about the internal battles within these groups if you were not at one time a part of this system?”
“It amazes me that the very elites cannot read between the lines,” Prof. Aliu responds.
“Prof Aliu, please answer my question we are in court and not in your lecture room,” Felix snarls at him.

“I was referring to the captivity of the mind, the centre of all life’s battles, to the innate strife of the juvenile mind particularly the crisis of identity at adolescence, the internal conflict between the perception of right and wrong values, which is the cradle of cultism, of crimes and the promiscuous lifestyle adopted by these young people. That’s what my article reads and not the strife among the ranks of the cult systems as the learned colleague erroneously reads,” he explains and leans back on the chair.

Segun and Frank are engaged in a head-to-head deliberation, Frank is pointing out something from a notepad on the table before them while Segun listens attentively ignoring the prosecutor’s incoherent line of questioning. He admitted leaving off the selection of witnesses and arrangement of the order of their appearance to Frank. I think they’re trying to agree on who the next witness should be.
The don’s response has obviously put the Ruthless General in a dither. He asks a few more questions and finally resigns.

Dr. Reginald Idanusa is firmly gripping an old brown attaché case wearing a thick lens fixed on old plastic frames. His suit is old fashioned and grey, made of wool. No tie. He briskly overtakes the orderly who is escorting him to the witness box and braces himself to recite the oath.

After the routine oath swearing, he takes a glance at me, and then at his watch – an indication he has a limited time to spend in the box. It is clear the only thing this near sixty-year-old man cares about is his job.
“Dr. Idanusa can you recollect on the fifteenth of February last year the body of a young girl named Sephia Rwang referred to your laboratory for post mortem?”
“Yes,” He replies coldly.
“Can you enlighten the court on your findings?”

“It was the body of a young lady of about eighteen years of age. She died of excessive haemorrhage caused by a bullet wound possibly fired at close range. Giving the degree of haemorrhage and further post-mortem carried out on her, I estimate she died less than a minute after the gunshot. It must have been shot at very close range.” Dr. Idanusa takes a pause and bends to carry his briefcase. He brings out a brown file jacket “I have with me a copy of the X-ray of the deceased.”
Without any objection from Felix, Judge Kachallah accepts the exhibit and names it exhibit C.
“Dr. Idanusa can you please throw more light on this result,” Segun tells him.

“One interesting thing the X-ray of her skull reveals is the path of the bullet. From my deduction, the bullet was fired into the upper right side of her head, which suggests that the killer is definitely left-handed,” Dr. Idanusa explains.
“Objection my lord! Felix barks, “The witness is only a medical doctor and is not qualified to tender forensic evidence on the incident.”
“Objection sustained,” Judge Kachallah responds.
Frank hands Segun a sheet of legal paper that he took from a file.

“Dr Idanusa, I have a copy of the forensic report by the police, which you appended your signature as the medical practitioner who conducted the autopsy on the deceased, would you like to have a look at it?” Segun asks handing the paper to the clerk. Felix refuses to take it from the clerk for verification.

Judge Kachallah also takes a look at it and says it will not be necessary to admit it as evidence. “The exhibit should have come as an exhibit of the investigator. I wonder why the prosecution did not invite the police as a witness. Is it an oversight or was it deliberate?”
Felix remains silent.
“You may proceed, Mr. Segun.”
“The forensic report states that the murder of the deceased was motivated by rape. Was this idea based on your autopsy?” Segun inquires.
“Can you explain how you arrived at this inference?”

“Actually the deceased had severe bruises on her arms and thighs. She also had burns on her body from cigarette. These clearly suggest that the killer’s original intention was to rape her and she apparently tried to resist him,” Dr Idanusa replies.
“Do you think the killer eventually succeeded?” Segun quizzes.
“Yes,” Dr Idanusa grunts bowing his head, he heaves, then looks up again and drawls, “She was deflowered in the process.”

A feeling of gall sweeps over me at the words of Dr. Idanusa. My head drops to the floor, I fight back tears in my eyes and the memory of her lifeless body covered in my bedspread the night she died. “If I get out of prison…if I ever get out of prison, I’ll find the person who did this…and kill him,” I mutter under my breath.
Dr. Idanusa has been looking at his watch nearly every thirty seconds. Realising he’s eager to go, Segun in his usual style concludes this poignant, but brief session in less than ten minutes.
“Thank you very much Dr. Idanusa for sparing the time to come and witness.” Turning to the Judge he submits, “I have no further questions for the witness my lord.”

Quite unlike Felix, he spares Dr. Idanusa the opportunity to go and take care of whatever has kept him looking at his watch every now and then. He makes Dr. Idanusa answer two short questions and then concludes. Even Judge Kachallah couldn’t help raising his eyebrows and wondering, has Ruthless Gee has suddenly lost steam.

Judge Kachallah rattles the gavel to signal another adjournment and quickly leaves his seat and out of sight. Felix and his male colleague, Mike Okonta, slowly pack their bags, while Miss Bimbo Eleru goggles at me.

If she’s thinking what I’m thinking, she’s probably contemplating why the law affords criminals like me the chance to escape being punished on the guise of proving a crime beyond reasonable doubt. Besides, I’m pondering, who could have done such a gruesome thing to such an innocent girl?

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-THREE
content: We are told that the Ministry of Health has finally waded into the situation in the prison and has promised to send medical aid to arrest the outbreak of cholera that has claimed fourteen lives and threatening to wipe out the entire prisoners.
I don’t believe them.

They fight everything, corruption, poverty, HIV/AIDS, malaria and polio, everything. Just name the disease, government will tell you they’re fighting it. But nothing works! It’s always the same old story. Those in charge of the sector pretend to be so concerned about an epidemic situation and blow it out of proportions in order to arouse the sympathy of the international community so that grants may be disbursed, not because they have compassion for the infirmed. As soon as they lay their hands on the money, they share it among themselves and afterwards they start telling stories.

Some of the monies are lodged into their foreign accounts and the rest they spend on buying up properties, expensive cars and a few privatized government investments. Some of them that have political ambition in the future make themselves self-acclaimed philanthropists and make sycophants out of those who partake of their loot.

The only consolation for folks like us is that one day, they’ll give account, not to any man or earthly institution, but the Most High of how they spent, not only the money that was entrusted into their care, but their life. God will surely judge them! That is the lamentation of my father, the Bishop.

Although I know he must be in tremendous pain at this point, Steve is smothering it. Instead, he’s wearing a weird smile on his face as if he’s actually mocking at the idea of dying. After uttering hard-to-believe words about his hope in Harry’s paradise, Steve finally gives up the ghost, right in my arms. This brings the death toll from the deadly epidemic to fifteen, yet the prison authority is still dilly-dallying about eradicating the disease.

Steve’s demise makes me very apprehensive that if the trial is unduly delayed, I may eventually catch the bug and the whole process will be a wasted effort. The last words he said to me are also highlighted in his Bible, which he willed to me, “When this happens, when our perishable earthly bodies have been transformed into heavenly bodies that will never die, then at last the Scripture will come true, ‘Death is swallowed up in victory.’” Steve made me pledge to read the Bible I inherited from him everyday of my life and then gave up the ghost.

I don’t know exactly why I should, but I figured I have a string of broken vows in the past – the vow of consecration unto God, which my father made to Him, the promise I made posthumously to Elizabeth, to discover the cure for her disease, the vow I made to Ifeanyi not to join the cult, and the vow I made to the GODs, and by extension, all disciples of the inverted cross, to be their Hangman.

But after deeply mulling over his conversation with Harry, I thought I should honour the promise I made to my friend, Mirage when I received his Bible from him. Surprisingly, my hope is gradually restored and reflecting on the words of God has helped me unwind from the cult and given me the right frame of mind for my testimony, which will probably come up today if Segun does not succeed in his search for the last defence witness, Ekanem Udofia, the guy who took photographs of the scene the night Sephia died.
The gallery is filled beyond its capacity, the seats are fully occupied and more people are still quietly entering the poky courtroom squeezing themselves into the limited standing area at the back.

Much unlike Judge Kachallah, it’s nearly half past nine and the bench is still empty. Both counsels are also not left out of the uneasy feeling. Segun’s eyes are practically fixed on his wristwatch. Frank, his think tank is patiently going through his legal pad with his right hand on his chin. Felix and his crew are weighing up their arsenal and plotting their last assault to send me back to the prison, permanently.

At one moment I’m impatient to hear the Judge’s decision, at another moment I wish the hearing should just go on for eternity, but I’m afraid of the cholera outbreak. We lost two other inmates last night. I can’t contain the thought of returning to that infested place. If the Judge finds me guilty and I’m sentenced I’ll break through the security and try to escape. I think I prefer to die in that attempt than go back to Katampe.
“Co- ou- rt!”
I get butterflies in my stomach as the clerk announces the entry of the Judge.
The learned one settles down in his chair and apologizes for being late. He has a fever, but managed to come. He quickly calls up the counsels and sets the ball rolling.
“My lord the defence wishes to call its next witness, Mr. Ekanem Udofia,” Segun says flagging off the day’s proceedings.
He strides into the courtroom file in hand dressed in the Nigerian Youth Service Corps uniform.
“Mr. Ekanem please tell the court what was your discipline and the duration of your study in the Metropolitan University.”
“I was a student of the Metropolitan University from nineteen ninety-three until May this year, and I read mass communication,” Ekanem replies.
“Do you know the accused person?” Segun asks him.
“Very well, we lived in the same neighbourhood, and we’re also friends,” Ekanem beams and glances at me.
“Where were you between nine and ten p.m. on fourteenth of February last year?”
“Right in my room,” Ekanem answers.
“Can you recollect what happened that night as it relates to this case?” Segun tells him.

“Yeah sure,” he clears his throat, “At about ten p.m. that night my landlord knocked at my door and informed me that there was a gunshot and the sound came from Iyke’s room so I took my camera and followed him to his room to see what had happened.”
“When you got to the scene, did you meet anyone else around the vicinity or in his room?”

“No. We were the first to get there,” Ekanem replies. “When I turned the door handle, I found that the door wasn’t locked so we opened it and entered…then we saw a girl lying on the bed in her own pool of blood.”
“Was she still alive at the time you got to the room?”
“No she was dead.”
“Was there any particular reason why you took your camera along with you when you were going to his room?”
“Yes. I was the photo editor of the campus journal at that time. I covered all the photo stories published by the journal.”
“And I suppose this incident was published?”
“Yes it was,” Ekanem replies.
“Now, Mr. Ekanem, if I’m correct, part of your training as a photo editor is to make reasonable deductions of events from photographs.”
“That’s correct.”
“Can you describe what you observed when you got to his room that night?”
Ekanem brings out some photographs from a picture paper wallet in his file. The clerk takes it from him and hands the photographs to Judge Kachallah.

There is silence in the courtroom as the Judge looks at the photographs. The furrow on his brow deepens as he goes from one photograph to another. Then he raises his head and glances at me with disgust. He hands the photographs over to the clerk who takes them to the prosecution bench. They all shake their heads in unison in revulsion in the stillness of the courtroom. Miss Bimbo Eleru gapes resentfully at me. Felix hands the photographs over to the Clerk. He passes the photographs on to Segun who obviously had seen them before.
I try to dispel the thoughts prompted by the images in the photographs they are looking at.
“My lord, I would like to admit these picture photographs as evidence.”
“Mr. Felix what is your reaction?” Judge Kachallah responds.
“No objections my lord,” Felix replies.
“Photographs of the scene of incident of the fourteenth of February nineteen ninety eight is hereby admitted in evidence and marked exhibit D...the defence may proceed.”
“Thank you my lord,” Segun responds.

Unable to smother his feeling about the blood-curdling image he saw in the print, Judge Kachallah gives me another quick agonizing glance gnashing his teeth. When our eyes meet, I blush and drop my eyes to the floor. I wish I could cry, to make him understand how sorry I am that this happened.
“If I’m right you were the one who filed in the report about the incident for publication.”
“Objection my lord!” Felix blasts.
“Sustained. Mr. Segun please rephrase the question,” Justice Kachallah responds.
“Do you have a copy of that edition of the campus magazine where your photo coverage was published?” asks Segun.
“Unfortunately, I don’t. As you can see, I’m coming from my place of assignment. But there should be a copy for the archives back in school.”
“Was that report based on your personal estimation or the deductions you gathered from the scene which appears in picture?”
“The report was based on my direct observations from the scene, although it was scrutinized by the board of editors before going to press, in other words, it was not based on my personal opinion.”
“Mr. Ekanem can you give us a brief rundown of the published article as it relates to who the unknown killer might be?”

“The summary of the article is that the killer’s intention was obviously to rape the girl, which is corroborated by the cigarette burns she sustained on her body in the course of the attack. There was a packet of cigarette on the ground and a cigarette butt on the bed, both of which clearly appear in the photographs.” Ekanem adjusts himself and takes a pause. “Expectedly, the published article stirred so much sentiments and irrational reactions among the readers. Some of them even clamoured that Ikenna be executed. When I aired my opinion, that Iyke was not the one that murdered Sephia Rwang, of course I was almost crucified for that view…but my opinion hasn’t changed since then.”
“What makes you so sure about that?”
“Objection my lord –”
“Objection overruled!” Justice Kachallah snaps.
“So, Mr. Ekanem based on your findings, what makes you so sure that the accused person is not the one responsible for the rape and death of Sephia Rwang?”

“Permit me to say that I am a smoker, and anyone else who does so in this room will attest to the fact that it is impossible to light a stick of cigarette except you suck through it,” he hesitates, “Everyone in our neighbourhood knows about Iyke’s aversion for smoking. Ikenna Obiajulu that we knew never smoked a cigarette, and I strongly believe he wasn’t in the room that night.”
“Did you report this in your…”
“My lord, I pray the court to strike this out of record,” Felix stands up and demands firmly, “Is this supposed to be a forensic report of the event or what?”
Judge Kachallah acknowledges and cautions Segun.

“Mr. Ekanem, you said in your statement that you are also a member of the students’ union government. What was the reaction of students’ union government to the death of Sephia, especially after considering the information about the accused?”
“We wrote several letters to the security demanding a thorough investigation of the incident,” Ekanem replies.
“And what was the response of the security, was there any attempt to investigate it fully and find out who the real culprit is?”
“The security didn’t even flinch. They told us there was no need for investigation since the culprit has been arrested.”
“Did they say culprit or suspect?”
“Culprit, that’s the exact words the chief security officer used to describe him.”
There is murmuring in the gallery. Segun consults briefly with Frank and then submits, “My lord that will be all for the witness.”
Felix springs to his feet at the Judge’s request to cross-examine Ekanem. “Mr. Udofia, what is the name of this campus journal?”
“Campus spice,”
“And how regularly is it published?”
“Monthly.”
“Monthly, and when is the monthly copy usually circulated?”
“At the beginning of the month, precisely the first week of that month,” he replies.
“So this event was actually published in the March edition of the monthly journal.”

“In that case, that edition obviously failed to fulfil its primary aim as far as this incident is concerned, which is to inform the public since most of the readers would have heard all about the incident and that the culprit had been arrested,” he says with a mischievous smile etched on his face. “Those who bought that edition probably never read the article you laboriously put together to enlighten them about your deductions from the photographs you took, don’t you think so?”
“Well –”
“Now Mr. Ekanem,” Felix snaps, “can you remember the major stories that featured in three editions of that journal before that March edition?”
“I can’t remember,” Ekanem replies.
“Well, I bothered to look them up the archives and I discovered that the December, January and February editions captioned several rape assaults, true or false?”
“True.”
“On the average, how many of such cases were reported per edition?”
“Three, I suppose.”
“Now Mr. Ekanem, on the whole, that is putting the three editions together, you reported five of such incidents, true or false?”
“May be.”
“And there was a major suspect that featured in the December and January editions of your publication,” Felix presses on.
“Not to my knowledge.”
“Of course there was – the College Club. It was alleged that its members were responsible for the frequent cases of rape at that time.”

Ekanem remains silent staring thoughtfully at the prosecutor with wrinkles on his forehead. He’s probably wondering, as I am, how much information this sniffing dog has gathered to dislodge his testimony.
“Why didn’t the students’ union government make attempts to get the security to arrest any of the members of the College Club?”
“It was a speculation.”
“Whose speculation Mr. Udofia, the editorial board’s or the students’, who depend on you, the media for information?”
Ekanem gawps at Felix without answering.

“Are you aware that even when the subsequent editions of the Campus spice stopped featuring the College Club as prime suspect, there was still a strong opinion among the students that they were responsible for rape incidents and yet they went scot-free?”
Ekanem stares at him. I think he’s now being cautious of the prosecutor’s highly informative questions.
“I put it to you, Mr. Ekanem that the Campus spice was serving the interest of some people rather than informing the public.”
Judge Kachallah takes notes as Felix takes a pause.
“Mr. Udofia, are you a member of the College Club?”
“Of course I’m not,” he replies.
“Mr. Udofia about this editorial board or whatever you call it, you said the final analysis of your report was reached by a consensus, am I correct?” Felix asks him.
“So you are telling this honourable court that the individual members of the editorial board are all aware of the fact that the defendant doesn’t smoke cigarettes?”
“That’s not what I mean.”

“But you denied that the report was based on personal estimation. I put it to you that by informing them of his non-smoking habit, you biased the editorial board by your sentiments for the primary suspect in the rape and murder of an innocent victim,” Felix says raising his voice.
“Objection my lord,” Segun reacts.
“Overruled!”

“Lastly, Mr. Udofia, did your prejudiced editorial board ever consider the possibility that there could be more than one person in the room that night and one of them might have been the one who burnt the victim with the lit cigarette and dropped the cigarette pack in the accused person’s room that night?”
Ekanem smiles, shakes his head and replies, “The opinion of the editorial board of the campus spice is very competent, the school has always relied on its viewpoint.”
Felix looks at him disdainfully.

“May I also emphasise that although I am invited to witness in the capacity of an amateur journalist, my present assignment in the homicide department of the state CID makes me more convinced that these deductions are flawless and should not be treated with levity,” Ekanem concludes.

“I appreciate the fact of your knowledge that you are testifying as a student who merely witnessed a murder incident and not as a police officer. One more thing Mr. Ekanem, since you are so blessed with an aptitude for homicide investigations; did you make any effort to conduct any interview on Sephia’s friends, her roommates, classmates to know the background of her visit to Iyke’s room?”
“No,” he gasps.
“Your report was based essentially on your subjective opinion about his non-smoking habit rather than thorough investigation of facts,” Felix says wearing a smug smile.
Ekanem frowns at him.
I shudder at the mention of my name. Although Segun has spent the last one-week preparing me for this, my knees wobble as I walk to the witness box.
“Are you a Christian or a Muslim?” the clerk inquires.
“I am a Christian.” I suppose.
Felix smiles wickedly at me with his hands crossed. Is he actually expecting that I’d refuse to swear the oath?
“Place your right hand on this Holy Bible and repeat after me,” he says holding out a Bible to me.
I brace myself for the oath taking placing my right hand on the Bible and raising my left hand up with my palm open.
“I, mention your name,” the clerk directs me.
“I, Ikenna Obiajulu,” I respond.
“Do solemnly swear.”
“Do solemnly swear,” I repeat.
“That the evidence I shall give in this court.”
“That the evidence I shall give in this court.”
“Shall be the truth.”
“Shall be the truth.”
“The whole truth.”
“The whole truth.”
“And nothing but the truth.”
“And nothing but the truth.”
“So help me God.”
“So help me God,” I conclude.
“My lord the witness has been sworn in with the Holy Bible in English language,” the clerk announces facing the Judge.
I stare bleakly at the congested gallery.

Segun’s examination is a mere formality, in the words of the prison warder, to fulfil all righteousness. We’ve rehearsed the questions a dozen times, but for the court’s benefit he establishes the fact that I’m not left-handed by making me write the same words on separate sheets of paper with my right hand and then with my left hand. Afterwards the Judge admits the sheets of paper as evidence.

Despite the fact Segun is aware that I wasn’t at a party that night, in his examination, he makes me consent to the statement I signed at the police the night I was arrested. So I adduce the alibi that I was away at a party when the incident took place even though Felix would have heard of the hit that night at Victoria hostel.

I also affirm that I wasn’t expecting her that night, in fact we never had any prior engagement, and that when I asked her to accompany me to the party and she refused, I told her I had to put in an appearance and return as soon as possible because of the promise I made to my friend Bitty, who was purportedly the host of the party.

I feel sorry about what happened to Sephia, and that I have to tell these lies, especially under oath, but the truth is…I didn’t kill her. I couldn’t have. As a matter of fact, because of my empathy for the weaker vessel I instituted the injunction in the fraternity that on no account should any disciple hurt a ringo.
I’m surprised there is no objection from Felix throughout Segun’s examination.
Judge Kachallah takes notes.
“The Carpenter, Monk, Bad influence,” Felix recites, “These are a few of your numerous nicknames Mr. Obiajulu.”
I do not respond.
“Are these your nicknames Mr. Obiajulu?” He reiterates.
“Apparently your skills in woodwork earned you the nickname Carpenter?”
Segun had warned me earlier to be careful about Felix’s leading questions. Although it’s not proper practice, he does it deliberately to affect his witnesses’ thoughts.
“Probably,” I reply coldly.
“Mr. Obiajulu do you have any other girlfriend beside Sephia?”
“No I did not. Sephia was an acquaintance.”
“In the past, what do you usually do on Valentine Days?”
“It is personal.”
“I hope so,” he says, “Going by your nickname Monk, and your own statement that you weren’t expecting any visitor that night, I presume you had planned to spend the Valentine alone.”
I do not respond.
“Is that correct?”
“I said my friend was having a party that night.”

“Oh yes, I remember. And you said that Sephia denied your invitation to attend the party with you, which from the account of her roommate seems very unlikely. I therefore put it that you intentionally didn’t want her to go anywhere with you in the first instance,” Felix takes a pause. “Why did you not ask Sephia to go back to her room when she refused to go to the party with you?”
“Objection my lord!” Segun barks.
“Overruled!” Judge Kachallah snaps.
“Answer my question Mr. Obiajulu,” Felix says in a cold unfriendly tone.

“When I told her I only wanted to put in an appearance at the party, she said she preferred to wait for me until I returned since I wouldn’t be staying long.” I heave and turn my eyes away from his glare.
“How did the nickname Monk come about?”
“I don’t know.”
“Your friends apparently call you a monk because of your abnormal aversion for girls…the same way you loathe cigarettes, wasn’t it?”
“I said I don’t know.”

“I hope young people will learn that good virtue from you,” he says with a light giggle, “I suppose your religious inclination could have influenced your abstinence from sexual relationships and cigarette smoking.”
I remain silent.

“Mr. Obiajulu, from the accounts of your escapades in school, one of your distinguishing features is your ability to adopt a dual or multiple personality depending on the circumstances you find yourself for which some of your friends also call you the Magician and Natural mystic…to what extent are you involved in occultism?”
“My lord, this line of questioning is out of context,” Segun barks standing on his feet.
“Objection overruled!” The Judge snaps. He turns towards the witness box. “Please answer the question Mr. Obiajulu.”
“As much as stargazing,” I reply.
The gallery breaks up. Segun and Frank look at each other, smile and shake their heads.
I know where this question is coming from. Son of a bitch! I was right about his relationship with that sleazy quack shrink. I’m sure he’s prepared a barrage of questions from my confession to him.
“Order!” the clerk barks.
“I understand you used to be a student of biochemistry, where did you learn carpentry work?”
“My father thought me.”
“Really! What else does your father do apart from carpentry work?”
“He’s a priest.”
“And when last did you see your father, Mr. Obiajulu?”
Segun objects, but Justice Kachalla angrily overrules him again.
Could he have actually gone to my house to tell my father about my trial? I wonder.
“When was the last time you saw your father?” Felix reiterates.
“Seven years ago,” I coo and shut my eyes.
“Seven whole years!” Felix exclaims. “And I’m sure you haven’t informed them you’re in prison, they probably do not know you murdered a fellow student.”
“Objection my lord!” Segun flares.
“Sustained,” The Judge responds, “Mr. Felix,” he warns him.
“You are a member of the group called College Club, right?” Felix frowns at me.
“And it is a secret cult?” Felix adds.
“I don’t know what you mean by that, the College Club is duly registered as a social fraternity with the student affairs department,” I reply.

“The College Club is a name merely adopted to cover up the activities of the cult group,” Felix reaffirms strongly, “And most of its members are either affirmed or suspected members of a university secret society.”
“You’re making that up,” I reply angrily.
“College Club is a secret cult group!” Felix flares.
“That is not true.”
“You are a secret cult member Mr. Obiajulu?” Felix shouts desperately.
“No I am not!” I reply.
I look towards Segun for him to bail me out, but he’s facing the Judge with his arms folded smiling lightly at the drama.
“Yes you are,” Felix barks hitting his fist on the table, “And may I remind you Mr. Obiajulu that you are under oath to speak the truth... are you a member of a secret cult or not?”
“I am not!”

Segun finally stands up ponderously. “Objection my lord! May I remind this honourable court that the defendant is not on trial for membership in a secret cult, but the murder of Sephia Rwang. If the state has no further questions, I beg to excuse him from this examination,” he says boldly facing the Judge.
There is silence in the courtroom.
“Mr. Felix do you have any further questions for the witness?” Judge Kachallah responds.
“No my lord,” he gasps.
“Are you re-examining the witness Mr. Segun?”
“No my lord.”
The clerk escorts me out of the dock.

Judge Kachallah looks at his watch and announces, “The court will go for an adjournment. We will resume hearing on the twelfth. At that time, the court will hear the summations of the trial form both counsels.” He rattles the gavel twice signalling the conclusion of the proceeding for the day.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-FOUR
content: The gallery is bursting with a herd of audience listening with rapt attention to Felix’s speech as he is called upon by the Judge to render his summation first.

“My lord the prosecution’s case is that on the fourteenth of February nineteen ninety-three, the accused person brutally murdered his fellow student, Miss Sephia Rwang in his room. In the course of carrying out this dastardly act, he went the extra mile by forcefully deflowering the lady of virtue, thereby bringing an otherwise promising future to an abrupt end and consequently throwing her family into mourning.

“First and foremost, I wish to state that the absence of the investigating team, namely, the police force is not an omission, neither is it deliberate. The Police was duly invited as a witness by the prosecution, namely, Inspector James Kala and Sergeant Saeed Ombo who carried out the arrest of the accused that night. But regrettably, for reasons best known to them, they turned down our invitation. We were forced to carry on the course of pursuing justice. We are aware that this has created a loophole for the defence.

“Notwithstanding, to establish our case against the accused, the state in his evidence called four witnesses. Prosecution witness one was the accused person’s neighbour and schoolmate who testified to being a witness to the events of that fateful night. He heard the last screams of the poor victim as she battles for her life in the hands of a man who was supposed to be her friend.

“Prosecution witness two established that a relationship actually existed between the deceased and the accused person, and that the accused had a prior knowledge of her visit, consequent upon that, he had enough time to conceive his dastardly act.

“Prosecution witness three gave an insight into the mental state of the accused and his general destructive behaviour while in custody, which view was given professional significance by the evidence of prosecution witness four, who established that the accused was neurotic, compulsive, a loose cannon waiting to explode and whose explosion that fateful night led to the death of the deceased.

“The accused in his own words admitted the various titles borne by him,” Felix emphasises, “while he was a student in a school where his extra-curricula activities included membership in the College Club, a group acclaimed to be a secret cult. And whose members, as corroborated by the testimony of one of the defence witnesses, are prime suspects in the series of rape incidents that occurred at that time.

“The university authorities identified him for what he is and brought his self-extended academic programme to an end,” he takes a pause and scowls at me, “I urge this honourable court to do likewise, identify the accused for what he is and allow the law to take its due course, to avoid a repeat of this awful incident. The penalty of this offence according to the provisions of the Penal code is death, and I strongly urge the court to order same.”

Felix takes a long pause and turns his face towards the Judge dropping the sheet of paper in his hand, which as a matter of fact he never looked at. “My lord we rest our case,” he concludes and sits down.
The gallery is thrown into a rumble.
“Thank you Mr. Felix,” Judge Kachallah responds and faces Segun. “Mr. Segun, is the defence ready to summarize?”

“Yes my lord,” he beams as he stands up. “My lord we are in agreement with the prosecution …we agree that on the fourteenth of February nineteen ninety-three Sephia Rwang was brutally raped and murdered in the accused person’s room. We agree that, as at that time the accused person was a student of that institution. We agree that for that crime, the accused is currently standing trial before this honourable court.
“From this point however, the prosecution has made it difficult for us to agree any further, mainly because they have left the rail tracks and are heading in the opposite direction.

“The charge before the court is murder, but the prosecution has laboured to establish the accused as a member of a secret cult and a neurotic. In so doing, they have failed to prove the necessary ingredients to establish and sustain a conviction for murder.

“None of the prosecution’s witnesses established that the accused was anywhere near his room when the murder took place. He was not sighted by any of them. He was not seen with the murder weapon, and evidence was not adduced by any of them to sustain a conviction based on circumstantial evidence. It is trite law that where an accused person offers an alibi, it is the duty of the police to verify the authenticity or otherwise of the alibi as failure to do so will be fatal to the prosecution’s case. There are a plethora of authorities supporting this principle.

“To assist the court and the prosecution, the defence called a witness, the coroner who testified that the deceased assailant was left-handed. The prosecution did not refute this bit of incontrovertible evidence. We also wonder why the prosecution did not deem it fit to call this witness to establish the cause of death. We can, however, afford to leave that by the way.

“To prove our case, we called three other witnesses including defence witness one, a professor in the accused former institution who testified to the accused person’s good character. Finally, the court was able to hear the story from the accused himself, who felt strongly compelled to let the world hear his side of the story.

“My lord, our criminal justice system places the burden of proof on the prosecution and in discharging that burden, they must prove their case beyond reasonable doubt. I submit that the prosecution has failed to fulfil that requirement in this instance.

“It is also trite law my lord, that whatever doubt is created in the prosecution’s case should be to the benefit of the accused. I submit that the defence has created sufficient doubt in the prosecution’s case to sustain a discharge and acquittal of the accused.

“The death of Miss Sephia is tragic and with sustained effort, the police will bring the true culprit or culprits to book. It will however be greater tragedy if an innocent man is convicted for a crime he did not commit. The court has an opportunity to right the wrong done to the accused by giving him what is actually due to him in the circumstance…freedom.”
I’m having a strong premonition about Judge Kachallah’s verdict as I watch him sitting calmly with his chin on his fists impassively listening to the lawyers winding up.
“We rest my lord,” Segun concludes with a fixed stare at the Judge.

Grave silence comes upon the court as Judge Kachallah bends down and writes quietly. Except for Felix who is darting an uneasy look and smiling irritably at God-knows-what, the entire court is lost in thought. Most of the people in the gallery are crossing their arms in anticipation of the Judge’s decision. Segun and Frank now wear a pensive expression. My heart plunges away.
Judge Kachallah looks up from writing, without any expression he announces, “The court will go on recess for two hours. We will resume at twelve o’clock.”
“As the court pleases,” we all chorus.
He rattles the gavel twice and disappears from the courtroom.

His announcement throws the anxious gallery into a roar. None of them is eager to leave the room. Pairing themselves up, they immediately constitute an unsolicited jury and debate in hushed tone with vigorous gesticulations, what the Judge’s decision ought to be.

The weary-looking policemen who had been escorting me to and fro the court since the trial whisk me away to a separate room. I stand there looking out of the window protected from burglars by a criss-cross of heavy metal bars also preventing me from escaping, while they wait at the door for the final session to commence.

One of them scornfully told me they’re giving me the last opportunity to say my prayers before the Judge finally approves my condemnation forever so they can be relieved from the boring routine I have subjected them to.

But I’m too downhearted and dispirited to utter a prayer. I take a look at the chain in my hand and the shackles on my feet and muse, “This is beyond hope.” I try to erase the attempt of escaping from my mind. The policemen are armed to the teeth. They won’t hesitate to shoot me to death if I should try to break away from them. I’m unable to grasp the fleeting thought of home that comes to my mind. I try to imagine what will be on Segun and Frank’s minds. They’ve really been tremendous in court. I doubt if I could have had a better defence.
Suddenly the feeling of guilt bulks up my weight and my knees bend, “God, please help me,” I mutter.
“What do they say, have a coke and a smile?” I turn around to see who’s talking. It’s Harry.
I didn’t hear the door open, must have been deep in thoughts. I just needed someone to talk to, and of course, a drink. I’m so thirsty. He hands me a bottle of coke.
“Thanks.” I rise up and take the drink from him.
“You’re welcome,” he responds with his usual friendly smile.
“Have you been coming for my trial?”
“First time.”
“You know, I’ve been thinking about your conversation with Steve, about being acquitted. Do you still believe it’s going to be possible?”
“Jesus said to the people who listened to him, you can say unto this mountain move, and it will obey you if you have faith as a mustard seed. But they doubted His words.”
“Sometimes it’s hard to believe,” I say droopily.

“I know. But that’s because many people still misread what Jesus was saying…He was pointing them to what He was sent to accomplish for them, for me…and for you, Iyke. If you acknowledge the love that God demonstrated in Jesus, by His crucifixion, and that He died on your behalf for the remission of your sins, and that He did rise from the dead, and that you have eternal life through His resurrection, you shall be justified before God. Then you shall have the assurance that your sins have been forgiven and that He hears your prayer…if you ask it in the name of Jesus.”

I’ve always had the impression that Harry is of no consequence. I got that impression from the vagueness of his message about the kingdom of God, but now I’m puzzled at his incomprehensible words. “Do you think my prayer can affect the Judge’s decision?”

“Well, prayer does affect things, but it depends on who exactly is praying, it depends on whether the person is standing on praying ground…whether you are justified by the blood. The Bible says that Jesus ascended and passed through the heavens after His resurrection and is sitting at the right hand of God’s power making intercession for the saints…are you a saint?”
I remain silent.

“Let me ask you a question Ikenna. Even if Judge Kachallah acquits you, will you really be free from guilt?” he hesitates, “Your hands are stained with blood, and the judgment of our conscience is imposed on us without mercy if we ourselves have not shown mercy to our neighbour.”
I gaze at him without saying a word.
“The general impression about court proceedings is that the Judge’s decision is absolute. But I tell you the truth, there is a superior advocate to whom you can appeal,” Harry enthuses.
“I know,” I whisper.
“Then put your trust in Him.” He turns away from my stare and looks out the window. “For if the Son of Man shall set you free, you will be free indeed,” he whispers.
The door bursts open and a policeman barges into the room. “You said you wanted to give him a drink and I allowed you one minute to do that. Now get out!” he barks and forces him to leave the room.
“Thanks Harry.” I hand him the empty bottle. “Will you pray for me?”
“I have been praying for you,” he beams cheerfully and leaves the room under the glare of the policemen.

My brief conversation with Harry has really cheered me up. I enter the courtroom wearing a smile that makes Felix particularly worried. I can’t explain it, but subconsciously I feel optimistic the Judge’s decision will go in my favour.
At exactly twelve o’clock, the clerk yells, “Co- ou- rt!” announcing the Judge’s arrival.
Then we all rise to usher in the Judge as he walks briskly to his chair, ahead of a police orderly carrying his briefcase. He sits down and arranges his table. Then he put on his glasses.

Everyone else takes his or her seats but me. I browse the gallery in search of Harry, not focusing on the many pairs of eyes gazing down at me. They’re all eager, after many days of trial, to know what the Judge’s verdict will be after having reached their personal conclusions about his ruling. I spot him sitting in the last row of seats. His head is bowed to the ground with his hand covering his face. He’s praying.
Similarly, I bow my head in the dock and mutter, “God, please have mercy on me…for Harry’s sake and…and…for the sake of Jesus, help me…” my lips tremble and I break down.
My heart thumps faster and faster as the Judge begins his address.

“I have listened to the evidence of the prosecution and the defence as well as the submissions of both learned counsels in their address to the court. And on weighing the evidence adduced by both parties, it is my considered judgment, in agreement with the defence, that the prosecution has not adduced sufficient evidence to prove their case beyond reasonable doubt.

“In addition, the failure of the police to verify the alibi of the accused is fatal to the prosecution’s case, as well as their inability to controvert the evidence of defence witness two. All these lapses weigh in favour of the accused.

“The state must be reminded that this is a court of justice and justice will not be served by allowing the conviction of a man who has innocence smeared all over him…consequently, I see no reason to further detain the accused a minute longer. He is hereby discharged and acquitted and I order his immediate release from prison.”
“As the court pleases,” the gallery responds in a chorus.
“As the court pleases,” I mutter under my breath sobbing with joy.

Justice Kachallah hurries out of the courtroom as soon as he delivered the verdict. I bow my head and shed tears uncontrollably. The police orderly ushers me out of the dock. Segun comes to me and hugs me.
“Thank you very much Segun,” I sob ecstatically.

Miss Bimbo Eleru sits still in the chair bowled over and gazing into space, while Ruthless General impatiently gathers his materials into his briefcase and hurriedly leaves the courtroom. Mike Okonta strolls to the bench and shakes hands with Frank Odikpo. They chat briefly and Mike heads to the door. Frank returns to gently stowing away their files into Segun’s briefcase after chatting with his colleague.
“There’s something I said I will tell you if we ever make it to this point, Mr. Obiajulu,” Segun says as he leaves my embrace.
“Call me Iyke.”
“Okay, Iyke,” he beams, “I said if this case goes in our favour and the judge acquits you, I would seriously advise you to go back to your father. Will you do that for me?”
I’m still thinking of how to respond to Segun’s request when Harry breaks in, “Hello Mr. Babade.”
“Hello,” Segun replies with a grimace of unfamiliarity.
“Segun meet Harry. Harry is a friend of mine,” I introduce them.
They shake hands.
“Harry is uh…”
“A lawyer,” he snaps.
“Oh that’s interesting. Where’s your chamber?” Segun inquires.
“Well, I quit the profession a few years ago,” Harry replies.
“For what? What in this life is better than being a lawyer?” Segun enthuses.
“Well, let me say, I have a higher calling.”
A higher calling indeed! Harry never hinted he was a lawyer.
“There’s no higher calling than the privilege of defending the rights of the oppressed,” Segun says beaming and hugging me. “What do you say, Iyke?”
“Yeah,” I mutter hazily.

My mind goes back to Nkeonyere’s warning about my turning away from the vow of consecration of my father. But how can I forgive him for placing such a curse on me? How can I return back to him scorned, broken and empty?

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY- FIVE
content: At the moment, I’m putting up with a guy I recently made acquaintance with, Osas Ishaya. I met him while I was hunting for a job. Osas gladly introduced me to his manager when I indicated interest in a job, any kind of job. Because it’s the kind that hardly requires intellectual skills – my physical fitness was of paramount concern to my employer – once I was considered fit enough for it, I was offered the job right away.

But the meagre stipend I’m paid for a day’s job can hardly buy me a decent three-square meal let alone renting an accommodation, so Osas took me in to stay with him. We are illegally occupying a three-bedroom bungalow in a government-developed residential estate. The initiative was introduced to alleviate the problem of housing for the middle and low-income earners, but that purpose was defeated. Most of the houses have not been rented because the cost is beyond their reach. Consequently, most of the houses are illegally occupied by tramps like us.

At first, I was worried about the arrangement, but Osas assured me there was no need to be scared, he himself has lived here for three and a half years without any government or security agents ever bothering him.

I took this menial job of detailing cars to sustain myself. I had also hoped I could save enough money to enrol for the university entrance exams and get back to school to fulfil my dream of reading medicine, but three months into it, I’m still not able to save a farthing from the stipend I’m paid.

Osas suggested that I join him in the early morning runs in order to make extra money. Some taxi drivers who convey workers early in the morning to the metropolis come to the garage as early as five o’clock to wash their cars for the day’s business. Whatever we make from then till eight in the morning when the company resumes business officially is off the company record, we add it to the stipend we are paid.

It’s a great incentive for Osas, but for me it’s now a nuisance and I’m beginning to find the early morning routine of waking up as early as four o’clock in the morning rather agonizing. Besides, I’m not a habitual early-riser.
“Patime, which one you chide for bed you no wan go hassle today?” Osas tells me.

Osas mostly speaks a unique kind of Pidgin English common in the estate. He is a secondary school drop out, although, whenever he feels intimidated by certain literate customers, he brags, ‘Patime, you think say na only you go school, me sef I put university way as you dey see me so. No dey pasmager for me o because say you be graduate’.
“I no sure say I go fit go work today,” I reply.
“So how you wan take make grech today?”
Osas is right. If I don’t go to work today, I’ll have to go begging for food. The pay is so meagre it doesn’t last beyond the night.
“God dey,”I reply.
“I hate bad. No just hassle me for wad, Patime. Even if you want make you crimose I no go even pity you,” he warns me.
If I survived Katampe, I know I can’t die of hunger. “If say you know where I dey come from, you go know say I no fit cromze per say I no chow for one day.
“Where?” he asks.
“Fashi Osas, ma’me me with you no begin argue this early morning.”
“Patime, I don discover you.”
“Wetin you discover?”
“I don discover say you put jaguda way.”
“Which one be jaguda way?”
“Patime, e be like you been don chide for prison tey tey. Wetin come carry you enter that way now?”
I shudder. But when Osas realises the import of his word and how gravely I’m taking it, he quickly dresses up and dashes out to work.

Staying with Osas has been recuperative, but after three months of detailing cars, and now that he’s beginning to find out my secrets and my true identity, I think it’s time to pick up fragments of my future again and move on to something more concrete, a more lucrative job, a better home – a more decent life generally. But that dream seems far-fetched because I’m a bloody dropout.

Osas had suggested helping me obtain a forged certificate to facilitate my job hunt, but I refused. He had a similar phoney certificate from a state university, which he once used to get a job as a clerk in a supermarket when he was in Birnin-Kebbi. But it nearly landed him in jail. The manager of the supermarket found out it was not genuine and contacted the police. He was lucky that one of the staff leaked the information to him and he took to his heels. He discarded it as soon as he left town.

Osas has a couple of rich clients who retain him to detail their luxury cars on home service. I’ve had to cover for him twice when he took a casual leave. Initially, I thought of asking him to link me up to them for a job, but on a second thought, I dismissed the idea because of his tendency to fray at the slightest disagreement. Besides, he’s too sly and fractious to be trusted.

Omega Car Leasing Services is one of our regular corporate clients. At the close of every day’s business, their fleet of cars are herded into our garage for thorough cleaning, always under the supervision of blowhard drivers who chatter and jest among themselves while chewing on sumptuous pastries and sipping from large plastic bottle of soft drinks. It has become a habit Osas and I envy.

The company is reputed to offer the best airport shuttle services. I learnt from their noisy prattle that the company is expecting some new cars and may likely recruit drivers to man the vehicles. I didn’t bother to ask how much they are paid, judging by the drivers’ extravagant habit, my monthly stipend and that of Osas put together is nothing to compare with each driver’s weekly earnings. Besides, it’s a more honourable job than detailing cars and like my present job I don’t need a university degree to be employed as a chauffeur. I could save up the money they spend on pastries and drinks for school.

I am known in the company as Harry Roberts, the same name I told Osas although he didn’t hide his opinion that it wasn’t my real name, he didn’t object to calling me Harry anyway. According to him, there’s no big deal haggling over whether it is my real name or not, even dogs have one and they answer when their owners mention it to their hearing. My second reason for adopting an English name is because of my premonition that it may not augur well for me in a company since the staff is predominantly Yoruba. My experience during my admission into the university and the ill treatment I received from the prison warder made me very sensitive to ethnic bias.

The day’s business in Omega begins with morning devotion, which according to the operations manager is compulsory for its entire staff. Although it wasn’t spelt out in the contract agreement at employment, my herd instinct induces me against contesting the decision. At the end of the devotions, each chauffeur is inspected to be sure that our uniform – grey shirt on black trousers and black cap is complete and satisfactorily clean.

I made no attempt to hide my frustrations from the general manager about being the only staff from the east and who doesn’t understand the generally spoken Yoruba language. In response to my grouse, he prohibited the use of vernacular during office hours. Although he said it jokingly, the staff sympathized with me and took him up on it.

However, after two weeks, my view about my colleagues changed completely, we began to relate as a family. I think I let my ill feelings at the garage carry over into the office. Sincerely, I’ve never come across a more friendly lot. I think I’ve learnt a lesson from that – to take people as they come, regardless of ethnic differences.

Our relationship became so cordial I asked my colleagues to teach me Yoruba language. They were so excited when I declared my interest in the language they not only obliged to do so, but because they were eager to continue to flow in their mother tongue, it was a good reason for them to flaunt the manager's order on communicating in vernacular in the office. He ignored it since I didn’t complain anymore.

Business in Omega is heaven. The company operates mostly charter services and our official working hours is from eight in the morning to seven in the evening after the last flight touches down at the airport. I feel like I’m being paid for doing nothing. It’s a general feeling among the chauffeurs, especially those of us who take driving as a hobby rather than an occupation.

However, I’m now able to save some money to pursue my academic career. But in spite of the conveniences, a feeling of emptiness still lingers within – I feel like I’m in the wrong place, like I don’t belong here. I feel like being in a family again – I’m homesick.

But I had said to myself on the day of my matriculation into the Metropolitan University, after reading the letter from my father, that he won’t see my face again until I obtain my first degree in medicine. How humiliating it’d be for me to go back home now – not after his ill wish that I’ll never succeed to become a medical doctor has seemingly come true. Sometimes I’ve had to ponder over his words, is it just a mere utterance or is it really the bane of my life? What the hell is happening to me?
“Hey! Hello!” a young man of about twenty-three years beckons my attention rattling the car window and interrupting my thought. “Are you free at the moment?” he asks.
“Yes but –”
“Can you take me to Crown estates?”

I’ve dropped a passenger once in Crown estates. The estate is less than twenty minutes from the airport. Because residents of the beautiful highbrow residential quarter are mostly diplomats and top government functionaries, visitors are not permitted into the estate except there’s a confirmation from the resident they’re visiting on appointment. Painted regular taxis are not allowed into the estate for any reason.

By my estimation, he certainly doesn’t have the features of a resident of Crown estate, and who would welcome such a fellow into the estate, I wonder. “Are you going to Crown estate or somewhere around Crown estate?” I ask him after checking the time by my watch – I have nearly one hour before my next booking, when the next flight arrives.
“I am going to Crown estate.”
I weigh him up again. He’s obviously not a resident and if he’s visiting a resident, why would he take a charter from the airport, and a costly one for that matter.
“Six hundred Naira,” I say with contempt with the intention of driving him away.
He stands away from the window and walks round the car from the rear to the front passenger door and lifts the handle. “Let’s go,” he says as he sits down.
Because I still don’t trust him, I ask him to pay me upfront. He tells me his boss is waiting at the left section of the car park assigned to private vehicles.

The real client is a couple who had just come back from an overseas trip. He is mad to hear that the car he came with to pick him is bad, and that none of his numerous cars were functional to come and pick him up at the airport, according to his driver, the guy with whom I negotiated.

He is furious and curses Ugochukwu, who apparently is their only son, who is in secondary school and who is responsible for his predicament, for wrecking his cars. He thinks he’s too young to drive, but he does. She fondly calls him Ugo, and tries to abate his temper and advises him not to lay blames on him until they ascertain what actually happened. He blames her for having indulged him so much. She talks back angrily and that fuels the quarrel.

I couldn’t help eavesdropping. I gather from his complaints that his little boy, Ugochukwu, has a bizarre behaviour that is becoming intolerable and he’s planning to ship him off to a remand centre. In addition to being unintelligent and unserious with his studies, Ugochukwu is a kleptomaniac, which his father considers incongruous. In comparison with this couple, I consider my father a very lucky man.

It baffles me, however, that my father could be so impatient and small-minded to disown his two sons for misdemeanour if Chief Amanze could endure and pardon such embarrassment from this enfant terrible, yet he claims to be so close to God. How could he be so hard-hearted? I wonder if Chief Amanze were to be a parishioner in my father’s church and sought his advice on what to do about this Ugochukwu, what would he counsel him? Disown him! Disinherit him! Send him away!

The couple’s duel about family matters gradually comes down as we approach their destination. While waiting for the gate to be opened, he mentions having an appointment with his school principal to discuss an important issue concerning his son, which the principal would not disclose to him on the phone, when he called. But he’s made up his mind not to keep the appointment. She says the future of the boy is at stake. Why don’t you go, he barks at her.
When I honk the second time the metal gate gives way to an imposing masterpiece of architecture. I gape at the magnificent marble house set in rich lush landscape of assorted flowers.
With all pleasure I render a helping hand to the steward who has come to pick up their luggage just so I can get an opportunity to enter the palatial edifice.

I recognize Ugochukwu from a life-size family portrait in the lush living room. He takes after his mother. He’s cute, handsome, fair-skinned and chubby. Considering the parent’s conversation, I put his age to be around sixteen, but in the picture he looks very much bigger than that. I’m not surprised. Before I could savour the beauty of his older sister and the Amanze’s only daughter in the portrait, the steward calls my attention, hands me crisp hundred Naira bills and ushers me to the door.

When I count the notes in the car, there are ten pieces. “One thousand Naira,” I almost scream. What generosity. Four hundred Naira is enough for me to purchase the University’s entrance form and I’ll still have some change.

The memory of the Amanzes’ house keeps coming to me. I’ve not stopped thinking about the enchanting beauty I saw in the photograph. The more I think about the family, the more I’m convinced I can help the Amanzes’ son. I also find myself dwelling on the thought of the possibility of gaining access into the palatial edifice, and the fantasy about getting married to his beautiful sister is beginning to crystallize.

Counting steps, as it is called in my days in the fraternity requires skills to ensure the victim does not notice he is being followed. My groundwork on the lad is yielding results – he lives a triangular life, chauffeur driven to school at seven-thirty in the morning, he hangs out with some friends in a regular haunt after school, but he makes sure to return home before his father.

Although his daily routine appears uninteresting and simple on the surface, the hardness of his fiery tiny eyes tells me there’s something sinister about this baby face, and I’m strangely getting more and more attached to him.
Fortunately, my application for a three-day casual leave at the Omega Leasing is granted. I need to wrap up my surveillance on him.

The haunt is a small nameless bar in the filthy suburbs of Kaduna. Not the kind of place one would expect to find the likes of Ugochukwu. The gang of five usually meet here every day after school by three, definitely not in their school uniforms. Four of them are already here, they are paired up in two long seats facing each other and quietly whiling away the time playing cards and drinking beer.

Wearing a baseball cap to conceal my identity, I sit quietly at a table behind them, close enough to hear the faintest whisper. I take timely nips of stout, which I particularly don’t like, while awaiting his arrival.

Ugochukwu eventually eases silently into the bar twenty minutes later. He has a good carriage of a muscular five-foot eleven physique. He’s putting on a blue jean and a black fitted shirt that reveals his macho physique and a pair of dark designer sunshades. He takes a quick glance at the habitués sitting at the tables as he strides with a confident gait to the waiting group.
“Hi Hugo Boss whad up?” hails one of the boys that goes by the name Paul.
The dark shades come off and he sits down speechless, eyeing them one after the other.
“Wetin happen?” Ganza asks in Pidgin English.
“They’re charging us for larceny,” he says coldly.
“Wetin that one come mean?”
“E be like one of us be sabo,” he whispers in reply.
They all look at one another in bewilderment.
“They’ve found the toy and the blacks have contacted the school authority,” he reports, “Our names have been given to the police.”
“It’s not possible,” Paul snaps.
“They can go to hell!” Ganza mumbles with clenched teeth. He’s been doing most of the talking since they came into the bar.
“Ganza, you don’t understand. The accusing finger is on us, five of us,” he whispers, “One of us here is singing?”
“This is unbelievable,” Reuben whispers.

“Believe it or not, one of us leaked the secret. I’ll get to the root of this and God save the asshole that let this secret out,” he fumes with his eyes fastened on one of the boys who hasn’t uttered a word since they came in.
“So wetin we go come do now?” he finally gasps. He goes by the name Timo.
“You dey mad abi? Wetin com warrant that kin’ question as if say na me carry you enter this runs,” Ugo rebukes him in a hush.
“Why you dey talk to Timo like that now?” Ganza blasts him.
“Five fingers share a palm Ugo, remember?” Paul tells him gesticulating by hitting his left fingers on his right palm.
“Says who?” Ugo replies bluntly.

Ganza bungs the cards on the table and rises angrily. “Na craze dey worry you, wetin you think say you be sef, wetin dey puff your head?” He drops the cigarette stub on the floor, glares at Ugo and criticizes him, “No be you cause this wahala? You dey craze you hear me, you dey craze.” He gesticulates by punching the side of his head with his index finger.

“Ganza me with una no get anything to yan, you understand?” Ugochukwu replies. He stands up and quietly strides out the way he came. Ganza furiously stares at him while his apologetic friends shake their heads in disbelief.

This robbery gang is called palm fronds. Similar to the university secret cult groups, they call themselves by nicknames, and converse in indecipherable secret code. Chief Amanze, I’d say, has not heard the last about his son. I wonder what he’ll do if he discovers that his son’s kleptomania has degenerated into heading an organized robbery gang.

I’ve tried without any clue to understand how he developed such a negative trait, which from his father’s complaint he didn’t inherit from either of them. Is it sheer adventure that led him into robbery or is it merely a progression of negative behaviour induced by his peers? Whatever it is, I can’t explain my growing interest in this lad.

He has a potentially large ego, which he’s yet unaware of and if only I can make him realize it, I may be able to help him kick the habit of stealing. Even though he still leaves home every morning dressed in his school uniform and as usual chauffeur-driven to school, Ugo has stopped attending classes since their operation went sour. He usually dons on a grey wool jacket over his school uniform – a white shirt and grey trousers – as soon as he alights from the car, and heads the opposite direction to the school gate, to a joint where he pensively whiles away the study period alone reading a James Hardley Chase.

The conversation between the principal and the visitor in his office is audible from the waiting room where I am sitting with three other people who have come to see him for a similar matter – to plead with him to commute the decision to expel the students charged with larceny. He explains to his visitor that as a matter of policy, the school cannot afford to harbour such criminals and that the board of trustees are very resolute about their decision to have the students expelled. Not even the visitor’s argument, that the moral upbringing of the children ought to be the primary responsibility of the school, could sway the principal.

“I work very hard to see that I’m able to pay your throat-cutting exorbitant fees. It is your responsibility to correct him when he misbehaves, and that’s what I expect and not to expel him,” he explains to the principal, “You can not do this,” he blasts.

“Cut-throat school fees? I hope you’re not referring to our tuition?” the principal responds in an angry tone. “The point is, you parents abandon your primary responsibility to school teachers in pursuit of filthy lucre supposing erroneously that you are saving for the child’s future, and when the child misbehaves you turn the blame on us forgetting that we can only build on the foundation you parents lay in the child’s early development.”

The woman sitting next to me has been busy reading her Bible inattentive to the conversation going on in the principal’s office. I peek at the page she’s reading, following the portion she’s highlighting with her pen, which reads, ‘Elisha said, as surely as the Lord lives whom I serve, if I did not have respect for the presence of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, I would not look at you or even notice you’. It’s the popular story about king Jehoshaphat of Judah and Ahab, king of Israel.

After a while, there is a brief silence in the principal’s office, and then the door opens. The man steps out of the office looking disappointed he gesticulates and muses to the exit followed by his ward or son, or whatever he is to the man. It’s Ganza. He follows the man sheepishly, hanging his head in shame.
The principal’s secretary beckons me to go in and see him.
“Good morning Mr. Danladi.” I got his name from the plastic nameplate on the door.
“Hello! Please have a seat,” he responds without raising his head from the table. “Can I help you sir?” he asks while concentrating on what he’s writing.
“I uh – I’m here to see you in respect of my cousin.”
“So what about your cousin?”
“Maybe I should start by introducing myself,” I say with a bluff, “My name is Jehoshaphat Obiajulu.”
He looks up slowly, drops his pen and asks, “Did you say Jehoshaphat?”
“Yes Mr. Danladi.”

I’m certainly not dressed like a king, but I’ve played this trick of impersonation before using Bitty’s name. I hope this works. In this case, I noticed at the shingle the initial JP after his names, the title given to Christians who have been on pilgrimage to Jerusalem. If he’s as religious as that suggests, he should know who king Jehoshaphat is.
“Is anything the matter Mr. Danladi?”
“No, no not at all…I’ve never actually come across anybody apart from a king in the Bible who bears that name,” he comments.
“Well, what a coincidence, I am also a king,” I reply grinning at him.
His face lights up. “So what can I do for you sir?”
“My cousin’s name is Ugochukwu Amanze.”
The principal exhales and leans on his chair. However, he gives me a royal audience.

Intentionally, my discussion with the principal is very brief and straight to the point – reconsider the decision to expel the boys. I also offer to use my royal office to mediate in the matter to ensure that the parents are liable for any future misdemeanour by their wards. When I stand on my feet to leave, he shakes me with two hands and bows to me.
“I shall not fail to show appreciation…in the most generous way if you will reconsider the decision to expel him.”
“I’ll try to make the board understand,” he replies.
“I’m sure you can convince them to rescind their stand, Mr. Danladi. I’ll appreciate it if you will get my word to them on this,” I say curtly.

The term ‘appreciate’ is a diplomatic way of pledging to offer someone, especially public servants an incentive to do something dishonest. I learnt it from the maestro himself, Ifeanyi. I had to emphasize it so that Mr. Danladi doesn’t interpret it for its natural meaning.
“I’ll try,” he quavers.
We shake hands again and I’m off to work.

The Principal addresses me as your Excellency His Royal Highness at my second visit, one week after, on appointment. He informs me how difficult it was for the board to accept my application. But he confesses that were it not for my involvement, the decision to expel them would not have been reversed. He reluctantly receives the brown envelope from me pretending not to be too interested in its content, but I know that’s his main incentive for doing me this favour.

As soon as I leave the school, I go straight to purchase the form for the university admissions examination. Bearing in mind that I have a criminal record at Metropolitan University, for which I was rusticated, and that I am sentenced to death according to the book of the law of disciples of the Inverted Cross Fraternity, I’ll choose any of the universities in the south, far away from my past. And knowing that by now the fraternity would have spread to nearly all the major universities, I’ve made up my mind to change my identity, to apply for admission using a different name so as to escape the penalty of my transgression in the fraternity.

Because of the magical effect of the name I adopted at the principal’s office, I fill in Jehoshaphat Robert Obiajulu on the dotted line dropping the name Harry that I had been going by, which I inherited from the evangelist. Robert is my real middle name, but I’m hardly known by it.

The thought of Harry’s words about going back to my father and answering the call gives me grave concerns about my next plan. I’ve been nursing deep fears since this idea came to my mind, but I don’t have a choice. I just can’t go back home now. I’ve lost my heart to Ugo’s pretty sister, and if this plan works, I’m sure I can make his parents take me as their own son.

My heart trembles as I watch Ugo park his car in front of the uncompleted building. The house is unfenced and the number boldly written in black coal marking. He looks at the piece of paper in his hand, then at the building to confirm he’s in the right place and steps out of the car.

Having mastered Ugo’s schedule, I went to the house when I was sure he wouldn’t be at home and left a message with the gateman for him to meet me here at five-thirty this evening. I didn’t leave any clue about my identity or why I wanted to see him. I simply introduced myself as Mistory in the note. It takes a curious person to honour such a queer invitation. I was right about his curiosity.
“Hello! Where are you Mistory?” Ugo’s voice echoes as he steps into the building.
“I’m over here come on in,” I respond from one of the rooms.

He examines me thoroughly as if he’s trying to remember where he had seen me before as I lean by a window opening purposely to the backlight cast by the setting sun so he doesn’t see my face as he steps into the room.
“What do you want?” he asks sternly.
“What do I want?” I repeat and pace to where he’s standing, “Don’t be afraid Ugo, I’m a friend and I’m here to help you.”
“I’m not afraid, I don’t trust strangers and of course I don’t need your help,” he says coldly.
“I’m sure you’d like to know how the police found out about the bus.”
“I don’t know what you are talking about,” he says with a grimace, “And please don’t ever come to my house again or I’ll get you arrested.”
“My name is Jehoshaphat…sure it rings a bell, doesn’t it?” I offer him a handshake.
“So you’re the one,” he replies, but refuses to shake hands with me.
I take back my hand and turn around backing him. When I turn around to him again, the puzzled expression is still etched on his boyish face.
“I can teach you winning ways Hugo Boss.” I raise my hands showing Ugo my open palms. “See...nothing,” I close my palms for a few seconds and then unfurl my right palm.
He gapes at the coin in my hand.
“Head or tail?”
He swallows hard, not knowing what to expect.
“You see…you can make things happen the way you choose.” I place the coin on my thumb and flip it to him. “Catch!”
He grabs the coin in the air and stares askance at me.
“You can be the head at all times…take a look,” I say pointing at his palm.

He drops his head to the coin in his palm. The face of the coin shows a head. He looks up and smiles at me. I smile back smugly. He takes a gaze at his palm again. When he looks up a second time, his face is in a grimace. The face of the coin in his palm has changed to a tail.
“Who are you?” he gasps.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-SIX
content: After brief inquiry about my personal life, Chief Amanze employed me to coach Ugo in his studies. Ugo suggested that I tell his father I am a youth corper. He said his father has always preferred them as his private tutors. So I lied to him about being a youth corper, and also about how distant my house is from here. Consequently, he offered that I stay in the boys’ quarters behind the main building.
