I seldom come to the living room, but whenever I do, and if no one is present, I feast my imagination on the ravishing beauty of Chief Amanze’s only daughter, which is so conspicuous in the life-size family portrait. Lately, my devotion for her has become my major inspiration to go back to school. Considering how seriously Chief Amanze takes education, I doubt if he’d give his daughter off in marriage to a drop out. I need to work very hard towards actualizing my dream.

She’s fondly called Uzii, she’s eighteen, and she’s a second year student of sociology. Maybe in the future we’d pool our talent together and set up a hospital…will she accept to marry me…will her parents consent to give their daughter to a pauper like me…? I ponder.
“Her name is Ozioma.”
I twitch and turn sharply at the sound of Mrs. Amanze’s voice.

“She’s coming back this evening from Calabar. That’s why I sent for you,” she says as she ambles down the stairs entering the sitting room, “I want you to help me pick her up at the airport…if you are not busy.”
“Yes ma’am,” I reply. Actually, I’m very delighted to do this.

“I don’t know why my driver is not here today,” she adds as she saunters to a chair and slumps into it. Then she stares fixedly at me. I blush and turn my eyes away from her. “I didn’t recognize you at first until I started thinking of who I could send to the airport in his absence, then I remembered where I first saw you. I couldn’t miss the height,” she says.
“I’m sorry I lied to you madam,” I offer a shamefaced apology.
“So what’s your mission here…why did you have to lie to us?”
I do not respond.
“I don’t trust liars,” she says gazing at me for a response, “You took advantage of our conversation the day you brought us home, didn’t you?”
“It’s not like that madam.”
“Tell me, how is it like, Mr. Jehoshaphat?”
“I uh – I had sympathy for you after listening to your plight about Ugo and I felt our meeting wasn’t merely a coincidence.”
“It is definitely not,” she snaps, “because you planned it all from the onset.”
“No madam I didn’t…your conversation stirred a deep burden within me. I had a deep conviction within me that I could help him…that’s why I sought this opportunity.”

“So Mr. Jehoshaphat, now that you’ve succeeded in breaking into my house, what do you have up your sleeve?” she snarls. Then she heaves a sigh of relief and says in a low tone, “Be honest Jehoshaphat, what is your motive?”

“I needed a place to stay …a place I could call home,” I let my face drop to the Persian rug upon which I’m standing. When I look up again, I meet her staring at me with a grimace, “I do not mean any harm madam.”
“Anyway, I think I like you, I mean your positive influence on Ugo. He’s our only son as you can see. Have a sit,” she beckons me to a chair and lights a cigarette.
I sit at the edge of the lush leather arm chair.
“There was another after him, but he died.”
“I’m sorry ma’am,” I whisper.

“It’s alright. My husband wanted two more kids,” she giggles obviously concealing her emotions, “But I just couldn’t have anymore…Kelechi’s death was traumatic…he was a beautiful child,” she heaves and puffs a thick smoke from her tiny nostrils, “Ugo killed him.”
What! I feel like screaming, but I’m stunned silent.
She smiles as if it was a joke letting out the remaining smoke in her lungs from her red-painted lips. “What’s that look on your face, don’t you smoke?”
“I uh – I don’t,” I respond gathering myself. It’s not the smoke that’s making me fussy. “But – but why did he do it? I mean how?”
“I knew you’d ask,” Mrs. Amanze hesitates either to let out smoke from her lungs or to think of a smart answer to evade my question. “Do you have a family?” she replies.
“Yes I do.”

“And you have family secrets, don’t you? I mean for instance, a black sheep who always does things that gets your daddy always complaining and cursing and contemplating, how in the world did I make this,” she pauses and smiles widely, “Every family has one or two of such. Such characters that their siblings are even ashamed to talk about in public.” She stops and takes in a lungful of smoke. Then she continues as she let smoke out of her mouth, “You know what, sometimes it’s difficult to explain why we mothers still stand up for them and protect them in spite of their excesses. I always wonder, whether it’s because of our love for them or our faith in them that one day they’d change their ways.”
I sit still pondering over her words.
“If what you’re saying is true, then we have something in common. I know my son will change. You believe that too, don’t you?”
“Yes I do.”
“But you have to love him the way I do, can you do that?” she asks rhetorically and sucks her cigarette again.
“I uh – I…”

“He has a lot of respect for you Jehoshaphat,” she snaps, “I’ve never known anybody he adores that much. I know you can help him come over those habits. You will be well rewarded I promise you. But, I also want you to know that no matter what you hear or notice about the Amanzes, it remains here, do you understand?”
“Yes ma’am.”
She sucks the cigarette again and crosses her legs. “So which of the other vices do you indulge in…drinking, babes?” she asks with a friendly smile.
I smile and shake my head at her humour. “None of them.”
“Eh e, now you’re lying again. I caught you looking at the picture,” she says pointing at the gigantic frame. “You like my daughter, don’t you?”
Are you kidding! I’m crazy about your daughter.

She taps her cigarette into a marble ashtray with a mischievous smile and takes a bunch of keys on the table. Then she holds out the bunch of keys to me. I rise and pace to where she’s sitting and take the car keys. “You will help my boy, won’t you?” she says with deep emotions.
“I’ll try,” I reply edgily.

Ugo and I obviously have our personal interests in our friendship. It was the craving for filial affection that initially prompted me to seek to be a part of the Amanze family, to live in the mansion. It’s difficult to say what Ugo’s real interest is – to pass his exams and gain admission into the university, which from all indications he’s doing to please his father, or his enthusiasm for the black art, which is the very thing I used to entice him and make him do my bidding. Although I know the likely consequence of breaking our magic session, I’ve made up my mind to get him to take his studies seriously.

The flight from Calabar is scheduled to arrive at six-thirty, so I’m one hour before schedule. Rather than sit alone in the car, I take a walk to my former office, the Omega Car Leasing to while away the time with my former colleagues. I thought that since I’m going to meet one or two of them at the arrival lounge, it’d be wise to greet them at the airport office to avoid any undue familiarization between us that could embarrass Uzii, and possibly spoil my chances.

They are surprised to see me again since I left the company. They’re also excited to hear that I stay in Crown estates, with my uncle. However, I intentionally refuse to disclose his identity. I break off at the announcement that the flight from Calabar has just arrived.

I couldn’t have mistaken her even though I’m meeting her for the first time. She walks into the arrival lounge dressed offhandedly in a jean pinafore over a tight red blouse and a snicker. She is accompanied by a boy who apparently made her acquaintance in flight and has obviously been amusing her. They stand by the conveyor belt waiting to take their luggage. She takes his diary writes something and gesticulates. He’s succeeded in getting her address.

Being mindful of my personality, so I don’t leave the impression that I am a chauffeur, I intentionally delay showing up until her luggage is brought outside the building. He also has a chauffeur who has come to pick him up. He persuades her to join him.
“Hi Uzii,” I greet her.
She turns and grimaces at me in surprise without responding.
“Mummy sent me to come and pick you up. Her driver is unavailable,” I tell her impassively.
She politely declines his offer to join him in the black Alfa Romeo that has come for him and disentangles herself with a promise to keep in touch. I take her luggage and lead her to the car.

“The family portrait in the living room didn’t reveal something about you,” I say with a broad smile deliberately allowing our eyes to meet as I glance at her from the rear-view mirror after nattering briefly about school.
“Really, what is that?”
“You have the most extraordinary dents.” I didn’t mean to flatter her.
She smiles and glances at me blushing.
“I was wondering if anybody upset you when the photograph was taken?”
“Why?” she asks politely.
“You refused to let them show,” I say with a humorous tone, “And there’s something else I observed from the portrait. But, please don’t take this personal.”
Uzii’s shy and introverted. Initially, she’s undecided whether she wants to hear my observation or not. After a while she asks, “So what else did you notice?”
“You’re not photogenic at all.”
She smiles coolly and shakes her head.
“It’s actually a compliment.”
“You sure have a way of expressing compliments.” Her lips thin into a smile and she turns away.
“You have a beautiful name too.”
“Wow, I don’t know men to be so generous with compliments. Thanks all the same, it’s actually a pet name…the real name is Ozioma.”
“I know. That’s the one I’m referring to.”
“You’re not my mummy’s chauffeur, are you?”
“Nope.”
“So,” she pauses, “Who are you? I mean what else do you do apart from helping her out with chores like this?” she asks with a friendly tone.

“Well, my name is Jehoshaphat Obiajulu. But, since you guys prefer a nickname, your brother calls me Jshap. I’m a youth corper, and I’m also Ugo’s private tutor. He told me you’re studying sociology…that’s an interesting course.”
“Well, I’ve just gotten through with the basics. Can you stop the car please?” she tells me.

I pull over at her command. Uzii asks my permission to let her drive a few kilometres. I couldn’t refuse her. She takes over the wheels and attempts a fluky two-kilometre nearly running the car into a parked lorry. I return to the steering and she goes over to the front passenger seat.
“You’ll teach me how to drive, won’t you?” Uzii pleads.
“On one condition.”
“What? I’ll do anything you ask me.”
“Are you sure?”
“Yes, tell me.”
“Uh – that you’ll put on the seatbelt,” I flounder.
She breaks up. “Okay,” she enthuses and pulls the belt. Then she turns to face me and beams, “Raymond promised me he’ll come tomorrow to coach me on driving.”
“Who’s Raymond?”
“The guy I met in the plane.”

“Oh that,” I say coldly. “First you have to be sure he’s good enough, otherwise it could be a fatal attempt. Have you heard of the tragedy of the man who took it upon himself to save a drowning friend when he himself didn’t know…”
Uzii breaks in and I let her complete the sentence, “…how to swim and so in the end two people were lost instead of one.”
We glance at each other in the hush.
“Was that really what you wanted to say, Jshap…I mean the condition you mentioned for teaching me how to drive?” she asks.
When I turn again, I meet her gaze. She blushes.
“I feel so embarrassed when I’m with my friends and they gist about how they drive around town to nice places.”
“It’s okay Uzii. I’ll make you so perfect on the wheels you could pass for a formula one driver by the time the hols is over,” I say jokingly.
“My birthday is seventeenth,” she says coldly, “I wish I could drive perfectly before that day so I can show off to them.”
“If you have courage, Uzii, you can even fly an aeroplane.”

Mrs. Amanze is sitting outside in the garden with a visitor. Uzii races off to greet her as soon as she sights her, while I drive into the garage. There’s a queer note in Mrs. Amanze’s smile when I greet her. Uzii and I smile to one another without saying a word as I walk pass them on my way to my room.

The house is full of excitement since Uzii came back. Mrs. Amanze has often persuaded me to join them at the table for dinner and generally spend time with the family, but I decline. I’m afraid of getting too close to them in order not to betray my identity. She pries about my family and asks whether I communicate with them. I tell her I speak with them on the phone every other day.

By my nature, I don’t usually like getting involved with other peoples’ affairs. But Mrs. Amanze’s warning about whatever I hear concerning the family gives me the impression that there’s more to the Amanzes than meets the eye. What can I possibly hear that would be a more terrible secret than what she said about Ugo – that he killed his brother? Except maybe, the details of how it really happened, which Mrs. Amanze wasn’t too generous to share with me.
There’s a knock at the door. It’s half past nine in the night.
“Door’s open.”
Uzii steps in. “Hi Jshap, hope I’m not intruding?”
“Not at all, Uzii, please come in.” Her voice reminds me of Sephia and gives me goose pimples.

She’s putting on a pyjamas obviously set to go to bed. I’ve been thinking a lot about Uzii since we returned from the airport. My soul has been occupied with the blissful fantasy of marrying this rich beautiful babe. I can tell Uzii also feels the same way about me, perhaps, and if I play my cards well, she may want this feeling to last longer. I’ve lived through this moment in my imagination. Now she’s here.
“I don’t feel like going to bed so early, I thought I should come over and talk with you.”
“That makes the two of us,” I reply.
The Amanzes are early sleepers. Dinner is usually served at about eight o’clock and almost immediately after the entire house goes to bed leaving Ugo who stays up to watch late-night movies.

“My brother likes you very much,” she giggles, “Wow! He’s never talked about anybody the way he talks about you. You must be a genius to put up with his temper, he’s had several private tutors but none of them lasted two weeks. By the way, how on earth did you make him stop taking people’s things without permission?” Uzii asks chuckling, “That’s the way he refers to his sticky fingering.”
“Maybe he’s gradually outgrowing the bad attitude,” I reply casually.
“I don’t think so. And if you ask me I’d say we’re yet to see the worst of him.”
“Well, I used to be like him,” I say jokingly.
“You?” she chuckles, “It’s a lie you can’t be anything like him, you’re too straight. You guys don’t have anything in common.”
“Let’s just give him time. I think he’ll change.”
“I hope you’ll be staying with us for a while?” she pauses and darts around the room, “Will you still teach me how to drive?”
“Sure, as soon as you’re ready.”
“Daddy has agreed for me to have a birthday party,” she enthuses, “You’ll help me organise things for the party, won’t you?”
“No wahala.”
“You know what I was thinking when we were coming from the airport?”
“What?”
“Maybe I’ll go by road when I’m returning to school after the hols.”
“Why? That’s quite some distance.”
“Can’t you drive that far?”
“Sure, I can.”
“It’d be quite an experience wouldn’t it, just the two of us,” she rhapsodises, “I’ll tell mummy about it, we can use her car. I know she won’t say no…do you mind?”
“Anything you say Uzii, your wish is my command,” I reply calmly smothering my excitement at her proposal. She blushes and gives me the most beautiful toothy smile.
Our romantic moment is brought to a rapid end by a harsh knock at the door. Ugo steps in smiling mischievously.

Although I tried not to allow what his mother told me affect my relationship with Ugo, reflexively my attitude towards him has changed. Apart from the routine tutorials, we barely talk on friendly terms when I discontinued teaching him the art of magic.
Uzii stands up as soon as Ugo sits down on the bed with me, she gloomily bid me goodnight and takes her leave.

He happily announces that he saw a magician perform a trick with an egg and wants me to teach him how to do it. I censure him instead about being taken up with the art and advise him to focus on his studies. He rains abuses on me, calls me an opportunist and a gold digger and threatens to tell his sister I’m a common taxi driver, then stomps out of the room angrily.

I regret initiating him into the mysterious art of magic. Although I’m eulogized for helping him overcome his kleptomania, his parents will have greater worries when they get to know of his new obsession, and they’d curse me when they realize I’m responsible for it. I know it’s only a matter of time. Maybe I should leave before his new mania gets out of control…but where can I go? But for Uzii, I’m not sure I’d like to continue staying with the Amanzes.

After today’s session of driving lesson, Uzii opts to take me out to lunch – to her favourite joint, the Rubies. The Rubies is an exquisite restaurant owned by two Italian brothers. I first heard of the place when I was with Omega Car Leasing, some of the extravagant and outgoing chauffeurs had complimented their exquisite Italian dish. Apart from the fact that I couldn’t afford such luxury at that time, I’m not so keen about overseas dishes, especially Italian.

She reads out the restaurant’s slogan written in Italian language in the menu book, “Affamare non vengo Rubies, solo l’mangi per vivere.” Then she tells me it means the famished don’t come to the Rubies, but those who eat to live. In other words, she explains, apart from the pastries, meals are prepared fresh at the Rubies as soon as customers place their orders.
I let her make the order and we sit patiently drivelling and nibbling at the starters – bread and mushroom soup. After approximately eight minutes, the waiter comes with the main dish.
“What’s the matter Jshap, are you alright?”
I was…until she asked me if I was the one who taught Ugo a magic trick he showed her with an egg.
“Did I say something wrong?” she asks.
“Oh no please, I’m alright.”
“You can request for something else if you don’t like my taste,” she snaps, “Let me call the waiter.”
“No Uzii it’s okay.”
“Are you sure?”
“Yes I am.”

But I still can’t get myself to eat. Uzii’s been eyeing me secretly. When she couldn’t hold it anymore, she folds her napkin and drops it on the table. “You’ve been picking at the food, Jshap. What’s the matter? Is it about your family…talk to me Jshap,” she says impatiently.
“I lost my appetite.”
“That’s not true. You said you were famished before we came here …what’s the matter?”
I have to confess to Uzii, but how do I start? Where do I start?
“It’s either you don’t like the food…or there’s something bothering you.”
“Uzii, there’s something I’ve been wanting to tell you.” I drop the cutlery slowly in the plate. “Please don’t get mad at me.”
She stares hard at me swallowing either masticated food in her mouth or saliva in anticipation of what I’m about to say.
I’ve thought out an easier path to this confession. “I’m not actually a youth corper,’ I hesitate, “and I don’t teach in Ugo’s school either.”
Uzii freezes. She slowly takes the napkin from the table and gently dabs her mouth and sips water from her glass.
“I made Ugo tell your dad so that he’d let me stay in the house while I coach him.”
“You taught him to do those tricks, didn’t you?” she says in a low angry tone. “So who are you and what do you do?”
“Please Uzii.”
“How long have you known my brother?” Uzii frowns at me.
“Five months,” I reply.

“You guys are all the same, liars, cheats and opportunists,” Uzii says with exasperation, “I knew there was more to that sweet tongue of yours. What’s your real name? Your name is not Jehoshaphat, what’s your real name?”
I stare blankly at Uzii.
“Who are you?” she growls. “Hey! What’s the matter with you…I’m asking you, who the hell are you?”
“I’m a fugitive,” I say ponderously.

“You’re good enough for a vagabond as well,” she snarls rudely. “If that name was taken from the Bible, Cain should have been a more appropriate choice to Je-ho-shaphat. Now let me warn you, if anything happens to my brother, I swear, I won’t let you go free.”
She beckons to the waiter to come. Her eyes are bloodshot and the tone of her voice deep and harsh. I shudder at the gruff temperament beneath her exterior beauty and gentle appearance.
“Go back to wherever you came from okay.”
“Don’t bother I’ll pay the bill,” I mumble.
“Keep your bloody money, I don’t need anything from you, scumbag,” Uzii snarls.
“Uzii please just hear me out,” I plead.
“I don’t have time to listen to your bloody lies.” She stands up and walks out on me facing the door.
“Aren’t you forgetting something Uzii?” I ask her shaking the car key.

She sighs, walks back to the table. Knowing how daring Uzii can be, I withhold the key and plead with her to at least let me drive her home, but she threatens to report me to her parents if I refuse to give her the car key. She angrily snatches the key from me and leaves the restaurant. I pray she doesn’t run herself into another vehicle or run over passers-by.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-SEVEN
content: The Amanzes are class-conscious crowd-pleasers. I was detailed by Mrs. Amanze to see that everything is well arranged for the evening. The bottom line of the preparation is to make sure the guests are not disappointed, that the party goes on without a hitch, and of course that everyone regardless of their age or gender is well attended to.

So far, they are pleased to find that the occasion is going well. The palatial home is besieged by a herd of guests – older family friends and Ugo and Uzii’s friends – who have come to celebrate with Uzii. Mrs. Amanze welcomes them and renders a short speech extolling her eighteen year-old daughter, and then leaves the stage for the youngsters who are eager to get on with the shindig. She’s such an orator and a bag of humour in contrast with her hard-to-please, arrogant husband who seldom smiles, and whose few words are rebukes and sharp criticisms. He won’t show up, I know.

As soon as Mrs. Amanze leaves the stage, I also disappear from the party to my room. I have decided to keep my distance and stay indoors until the party is over to avoid any possible embarrassment in front of her friends.

Lying on the bed and facing the ceiling with my hands clasped behind my head absent from the environment, absent from the sound of music filtering into the room, I’m rattled by a feeling of déjà vu as my mind flashes back to the night I took the decision to leave Uncle Dike’s house. I don’t feel welcome here anymore.

Ugo has been acting churlishly towards me ever since I refused to gratify his new obsession. I’ve not been in talking terms with Uzii since my confession. My grouse with her brings back the memories of Julia. How is she faring? Will I ever set my eyes on her again? Suddenly, the thought of not being able to avenge her rape makes me flush with anger.

“Once again my hopes are dashed, my dream is aborted, and the bliss has died out,” I whisper to myself as I ponder over my decision to return home. I’ve finally made up my mind to leave the Amanzes for good although I informed Ugo’s parents that I’d be travelling for a while, to see my parents and dash back. Will my father receive me back again after almost eight years? If he’s heard what happened, how will he take me?
Suddenly, the rattle at the door stirs me from my thoughts. Uzii is at the door, accompanied by one of her friends, who is carrying a tray. She looks brassy in her red dinner gown.
“Hi Jshap.”
“Hi Uzii,” I reply and glance at her friend who is grinning at me. The smile on her face tells me she’s probably heard an earful about me.
“My friend, Gertrude, she came all the way from Calabar.”
I remember Uzii telling me about her. She’s her best friend.
“Hi Gee tee,” I greet her.
“How did you…okay,” she breaks off and eyes Uzii.
“Jehoshaphat.” I stick out my right hand unconscious of the fact her hands are not free.
“Sorry,” she replies shrugging, “Uzii told me you’re a good dancer. I came to ask you for a dance, if she’d permit us.”
I chuckle and remain silent. Uzii eyes her.

“Here, I brought you some food. I can’t imagine you missing the whole fun,” Uzii says taking the tray from Gertrude. There’s a packet of fruit drink, two glasses, a plate of food, a large chunk of cake and a cup of ice cream in the tray.
“Thanks Uzii but I’m not hungry.”
“Won’t you at least have a drink on me?”
“Please Jshap…today is my birthday,” Uzii pleads.
“Wow! That’s my jam,” Gertrude exclaims in response to a track playing in the sitting room and breaks in, “Excuse me.” She scurries away with a gaiety dance step.
“But this is too much,” I say as I take the tray from her.
“It’s for both of us…you don’t mind if I come and stay with you for a while, do you?”
“Your guests are going to come looking for you.”
“Oh, what do I care?” she replies with a cavalier manner, “They’re already damned too occupied with the fun to miss me.”
The tray nearly falls from my hand as I step away from the door to let her in.
“Are you alright Jshap?” she asks after helping me steady my steps.
“Yeah…I’m alright.” It’s the feeling of déjà vu – the thought of Sephia’s Valentine Day visit.
Uzii locks the door and sits down on the bed with me. “Let me serve you,” she whispers romantically taking the tray from me.
“I thought my presence would upset you so I decided to stay away lest I ruin your day,” I explain.

“That was very thoughtful of you…but unfortunately, I forgot to tell you I had wanted to open the dance floor with you,” she says as she opens the packet of fruit juice and pours it slowly into the glasses.
“Me,” I chuckle, “How about Raymond?”
“Raymond is not my boyfriend,” she replies coldly as she gives me a glass of juice and a hearty smile. “I didn’t invite him.”
“Thanks,” I say taking the glass from her.
“You’re welcome. I’m sorry I overreacted at the restaurant the other day.”

“No Uzii please,” I pause, “I think it was a good thing you didn’t hide your feeling. You don’t know what you’ve done to me. I’ve been able to take a sober assessment of my life…to really examine my life. I’ve been running away from my past. I’ve done too many things wrongly. I wish I could get a second chance to make up for them,” I say grimly.
“Let’s not talk about that now Jshap okay.”
We both sip from our glasses. Uzii gives me a slice of cake and insists on feeding me. I let her.
“Ugo told me you’re planning to leave,” she hesitates waiting for me to respond.
I occupy myself with masticating the piece of cake in my mouth, while I think of how not to lie to her about my intention not to come back.
“You suddenly became homesick?” Uzii asks and when I do not respond, she continues, “Don’t go Jshap please. I want you to stay…forever.”
“I have to go.”
Her face falls.
“But I promise I’ll come back after sometime.”
“Don’t lie to me,” she says, “Ugo told me you’re leaving us for good. Is it true, Jshap?”
“Well, I don’t know where he got that. I never discussed that with him,” I reply.
“He asked me to apologize to you on his behalf for dissing you…he doesn’t want you to go,” she says in a hush.
I exhale heavily without saying a word.
“He adores you Jshap. Please forgive him.”
“Well, if you insist,” I sigh, “But I really don’t think there’s any need for that. He’s done nothing wrong to me.”
“Must you go now?”

“Yes Uzii.” For the first time, I realize I truly love Uzii and I sincerely feel like opening up, not just to let Uzii know who I really am, but to free myself of the burden my life of falsehood and deceit has placed on me. “I’ve been away from my family for nearly a decade.”
“Oh my God!” she exclaims, “What happened?”
“It’s a long story.”
“No I want to know. How could you be so separated from your parents for so long? Don’t you have any siblings?”
“I do. A brother and a sister, they’re both older than me.”

Uzii sits up to show that she’s eager to hear the whole story, a story I’ve refused to share with anyone else. I am lumbered by her affection to tell her the story of my life, but I have to be cautious, or else it could end up being counterproductive if she misreads it and reacts the way she did at the restaurant. I recite a selective account of my past to her.
“But why did you run away from home?” she coaxes me.

“My father wanted me to join him in the priesthood, but I decided otherwise to read medicine,” I pause to take a bite as she holds up a piece of cake to me, “The circumstances that led to my father’s vow to God is complicated. After I left home, I was admitted into the Metropolitan University to study biochemistry hoping that after one year I’d switch over to medicine, but I didn’t make the minimum GPA…it was because of the curse my father placed on me.”
“He cursed you?”
“More than that…he disowned me.”
“Oh my God! I’m sorry,” she darts her eyes about the room apparently thinking of what to say, “May be he didn’t really mean it,” she says with a short smile.
I turn to her slowly. “He meant every word of it, Uzii.”
She swallows saliva.
“It’s okay Uzii. You guys have been very wonderful to me, especially your mum, she has such a large heart,” I hesitate and grin widely.
“What’s amusing you?”
“Did she tell you she caught me in one of my string of lies?”
“No,” she answers, “You don’t know my mum, she can keep secrets. I wish I could be like her. So what was it you told her?”

Uzii breaks up when I tell her about my experience at Omega Car Leasing, how I fell head over heels in love with her when I saw her in the portrait in the living room and how I broke into the family in search of love.
“Oh my God, this is incredible,” she rolls with laughter, “You are impossible Jshap. What gave you the impetus to do that?” she asks with fascination.
“You won’t understand Uzii…but I don’t want to go on living like that anymore,” I reply soberly, “I’ve taken the decision to go and make peace with my father.”
“What if he still asks you to join the ministry?” she asks worriedly.
I take a deep breath and reply, “Do you think I should do it?”
“I don’t know. It’s your life.”
“Will you still marry me if I decide to become a priest?”
“It hasn’t come to that,” she giggles.
“It has, Uzii. You’re the reason I came here. Will you marry me?”
“You’re making me nervous Jshap. Let’s not talk about this yet,” she quavers. Then she drops the tray and leans on me placing her head on my chest. “I love you Jshap…I don’t want you to go.”
“I love you too Uzii,” I reply stroking her hair gently. “I can’t get you out of my mind since the first day I met you.”
“I’m afraid,” she whispers caressing my hand, “I’m afraid I won’t see you again if you go now.”
“I’ll come back Uzii, I promise…I’ll come back for you. Trust me.” I cuddle her tiny frame in my arms and kiss her hair.
“You know what Jshap, I wanted to sing a song for you at the party tonight.”
“Really?”
“I want to share a bit of my heart with you as you go. I want you to think about me and know that there’s someone who cares for you.”
I grin awkwardly at her.

“Some times in our lives, we all have pain, we all have so – or – row. But if we are wise, we’ll know that there’s, always tomorrow. Lean on me, when you’re not strong, I’ll be your friend, I’ll help you ca – ar – ry on. For it won’t be long till I’m gonna need somebody to lean on…”

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY- EIGHT
content: My stay with the Amanzes prepared me for reintegration into family life and reunion with my family after nearly one decade. For nearly a decade I’ve put up a great deal of ego and inner will contending with the notion that my father is responsible for my misery and misfortune. It was a difficult decision for me to come back home at this time. But for Uzii, I would have changed my mind again after we squared off.

She asked me to swallow my pride and persuaded me to go home immediately and apologize to my father. Uzii is not very religious though, but she cited the story of the Prodigal son, a very popular story in the Bible, which I know so well. She also assured me that my father will accept me again no matter how severely strained our relationship may have been.

I learn from our neighbours that my parents had gone to the mission for fellowship. They are elated to see me again after such a long time, and willingly accepted my request to stay in their house and wait for my parents return since our house is locked and I can’t gain access.

Shortly after my arrival, the news of my comeback reached the entire neighbourhood. They stream in one after another to catch a glimpse of me. I feel like a total stranger watching these folks screaming out for joy at my return. Although none of them has asked me where I had been all these years, I can tell they’re very anxious to know. Gossip is the favourite past time in this neighbourhood, and it doesn’t look to me like they’ve given up this obsession.

When I knock on the door, I hear my father’s voice responding from the sitting room, and when I mention it’s me, he calls out to my mother to come and answer the door. When she asks him who the visitor is, he hisses and doesn’t respond.
“Honey, it’s Ikenna,” she trembles and gawks at me. She’s so shocked she can’t answer my greeting.
“So? What about Ikenna?” he grumbles.

We hug each other and I break down on her shoulder. My father sits still in the sofa with a puckered brow and his right fist supporting his chin. This is going to be hard, I tell myself. When my mother let go of me, I amble to him. He scowls at me. I go on my knees weeping and asking him to forgive me, to forget all that happened in the past and take me back as his son. He hisses loudly. Then his hand comes off his chin, he clasps his fingers together and wags his legs.

My mother comes over and sits down beside him in the sofa. “Honey, please forgive him I beg you in the name of God,” she pleads, “Nkem, biko…gbaghara zie nu ya o. Ejim aha Jesus n’ario gi. Biko gbaghara.”
Without saying a word he stands up and gently pulls me up and we hug each other.

My mother and I have been catching up on family affairs the whole evening. Nkeonyere now has two kids, a boy and a girl from her marriage to a Yoruba guy. Ifeanyi should be doing well in America, she says, and he’s married to an African American. He and my father had misunderstandings about his decision when he called on the phone to inform them. He was adamant and refused to take my father’s advice, since then he’s not called.
After, my mother helps me clean up Nkeonyere’s room, where I choose to stay.

At exactly six o’clock in the morning, my mother knocks at the door to call me up for the family devotion, one ritual I know we’d never outgrow as long as we’re here. I dust my old Bible, which had been lying in the cockroach-infested wardrobe, and join them in the sitting room.

Absent-minded from the Bishop’s tailored sermon, I casually flip through the pages to the book of Song of Songs, looking for Elizabeth’s photograph. Somebody must have taken it away. I go through the pages again much slowly, but I still can’t find the picture. Instead I notice there’s a portion in the book highlighted with green fluorescent ink. Somebody had definitely been using the Bible.

I silently read the portion covered by the ink in my heart, “All his days the wicked man suffers torment, the ruthless through all the years stored up for him. Terrifying sounds fill his ears when all seems well marauders attack him. He despairs of escaping the darkness. He is marked for the sword. He wanders about – food for vultures. He knows the day of darkness is at hand. Distress and anguish fill him with terror, they overwhelm him, like a king poised to attack, because he shakes his fist at God and vaunts himself against the Almighty, defiantly charging against him with a thick, strong shield.” I raise my head from the Book and catch Mummy’s stare. She drops her eyes on her Bible immediately.

Uzii’s first letter arrives five days after I left the Amanze’s. I feel like reading it for eternity and each time I do, I find that it’s as if I’m reading it for the very first time. She wants to know how things are going with my father and me, and how things are with my family generally, and when I’m coming back to Kaduna.

Her words revive the reminiscence of my days with the Amanzes – my first visit to that magnificent house, the impact her picture had on me that day, and the impression of our last moments together. The memory hasn’t waned a bit. She wishes me well and reminds me of my promise. Her rapturous laughter that night I opened up to her echoes in my head.
“I.K., I’ve been calling you,” my mother says.
“I’m sorry Mum I didn’t hear you,” I reply and fold the letter.
“Breakfast is ready,” she announces.

Although we usually had our meals together, except for some reason any member of the family is not at home at mealtime, I’ve always come up with an excuse to decline her invitation to join them at the table since I came back home. Knowing how persuasive my mother can be, she won’t give up.
“Alright Mum, I’m coming,” I reply.
“We are waiting for you,”
“Just go ahead I’ll join you okay.”

Apart from the morning devotion my father and I seldom communicate. He still has a chip on his shoulder about my resolve not to answer the call. He adopted an indirect way of persuading me – by deliberately choosing Bible texts and stories that warns about rebelling against God’s word during our morning devotions. That hasn’t helped matters at all, rather my antagonism towards the call is continually reinforced.
I dilly-dally in the room before making up my mind to join them. Unintentionally, I overhear their conversation at the table as I reach the door.
“Are we going to wait for eternity? What’s he doing in the room anyway?” my father asks.
“I think he’s trying to get acquainted with home again,” she replies.
“What does that mean?” he barks.
“He’s going through a stack of old photographs and a letter.”
“That boy is making me sick.”

“Honey, Ikenna has been away for so many years, we don’t even know where he’s been, who he’s been with or what he’s been through all this while,” she explains, “If you ask me I think you pushed him too hard.”
“How dare you say that, May,” he snarls, “He was deceived by that evil son of yours who manipulated him and led him astray, now look where it has landed him.”
“So Ifeanyi is no longer our son. He’s now my evil son. Anyway, there would have been a better way to communicate it to him rather than imposing your will on him.”

“Point of correction, it is not my will, it is God’s will and nobody challenges Him. Wisdom they say is proved right by her actions. Is it not obvious, as you can see from his failure that I was right all the while and he is wrong? I just hope he has learnt his lessons.”
“All I’m saying is, we’re loosing our two sons. Don’t you think…maybe the way we’re communicating God’s will to them is wrong. Honey, we can’t continue to blame Ikenna.”
“I have the Spirit of God, Nkem. Whatever decisions I’ve taken for our children are in accordance with God’s will. They just choose not to listen, am I supposed to be blamed for that?”
“But the Bible says we should not provoke our children to anger.”
“So what are you implying?”
“I think we’ve been too extreme in our corrective measures, especially in Ikenna’s case…let’s be patient with him. Maybe we should pray and ask God to reveal His will to him,” she says.
“Please say the grace,” he growls.

The tone of my mother’s voice reminds me of the words of Mrs. Amanze the day I went to the airport to pick up Uzii. A feeling of remorse flushes over me, she doesn’t deserve the mental torture I’m inflicting on her just because of my malice with my father.

Mum is in the kitchen humming a tune as she does the chores. She excused herself from the table fifteen minutes ago pretending to be busy. I know she deliberately left both of us at the table so we can have the opportunity to talk and possibly sort out our differences. But unfortunately, as soon as she left, neither of us has uttered a word to the other. He picked up a newspaper and pretends to be so engrossed with it, partially covering his face with it.

I chomp bread and intentionally sip noisily from my cup of tea in order to irritate him and get him to say something, anything, but he ignores me. He drops the newspaper every now and then to sip tea from his cup and steal a glance at me. I peek at him from the corner of my eye. It’s obvious we mutually feel uneasy with each another. Frustrated by the stillness, he folds the paper gulps the last of the beverage and goes to his armchair.
“Thanks Dad,” I greet him after I’ve had my meal.
“Thank God,” he replies.
I clear the table and take the plates and cups to the kitchen. “Thanks Mum,” I greet pecking her on her cheek.

She’s plucking off vegetables from its stalk for lunch. I put the plates in the sink and turn the tap on to wash them up. We’ve never had a housekeeper. My father says he can’t afford one. My mother usually does the house chores with Nkeonyere, during the holidays those days.
“I.K. Hapu ya o.” She tries to stop me from doing the dishes.
“Mummy hapu wa ka m saa ya,” I insist.
“Guess what, I’m preparing your favourite dish for lunch, I hope you’ll come back early for lunch,” she says grinning.
“Thanks Mum, I’ll come back early. Lest I forget Mum, I’m expecting a mail, it’s addressed toJehoshaphat Obiajulu, in case it arrives when I’m absent, please receive it on my behalf.”
“Onye bu kwanu Jehoshaphat?” she inquires.
“Just keep the letter for me okay, please.”
She grimaces at me.
“Alright, we’ll talk about it later okay…see you later Mum.”
“God bless you my dear. And take care of yourself,” she responds.

My father is still in the sitting room, but he’s no longer reading the newspaper, which is now on the side stool. He is resting his right hand on the arm of the chair, drumming his nose with his index and middle fingers.
“Bye Dad,” I greet as I walk past him to the door.

He’s too absorbed to respond to my greeting. Either he’s horror-struck by some news in the paper or, if he was eavesdropping on my conversation with Mum. I think the contemplation of who Jehoshaphat is liable for his pensive posture.
I shut the door gently with the knowledge that the subject of their discussion for the day will be the analysis of how the name Jehoshaphat evolved.

Originally, I made up my mind to stay indoors as much as I can, to avoid letting any of my childhood friends know that I’m around. I intended to while the time away until my admission into the university comes through, but the ennui of staying at home reading novels and watching the telly prompted my occasional visits to the library.

Mum is dressing the dining table and singing her favourite chorus, the chorus she usually sings whenever she’s preparing a feast in anticipation of important guests. She’s been busy all day cleaning up the house and preparing an assortment of dishes. From the look of things there’s going to be a host of them. The last thing I want now is to be grilled about my academics, which is usually the major concern of Daddy’s friends.
I cough lightly to get her attention. She turns around and stops humming.
“Where are you going?” she asks.
“To the library,” I reply curtly and sit in a chair to do my shoelace.
“Why would you be going to the library on Saturday?”

“Mum,” I drawl, “I know you may not like to hear this. I’m optimistic about making it into the college of medicine this time. I want to use this period to catch up on recent developments in the field of medicine.”
“Well, since you’ve decided to be so unreasonable, I’ve given up on challenging your determination to pursue a medical career, but if you don’t mind can you spare going to the library today?”
“Mum, I’ve made plans to –”
“I know, but I’m asking you.”
“Mum, I…I can’t.”
“You can’t?” she replies taking a deep breath. “Why are you so self-centred I.K.? I once said I won’t beg your father to reverse whatever decision he takes concerning you, didn’t I?”
“Of course you did.”
“Do you know why I changed my opinion the day you came back home?”
“Perhaps you’ve come to know the truth,” I reply coldly.
“What is the truth I.K.?”
I remain silent.

“I vividly remember the parable you cited to defend your apostasy, yet you have rejected the pattern of life Jesus demonstrated in that very parable,” she says, “Why are you so self-centred, Ikenna? Don’t you ever consider other people in your plans,” she barks angrily raising her voice.

Initially, I restrain myself from answering back and then I rise up slowly from the chair. “Let’s not open up old wounds Mum, you and Dad have refused to consider that I have my own dreams, my own life to live.” I glance at the table she has beautifully set for dinner, “Having a feast or a holiday is the least of my concerns for now, Mum…” I cut off my speech when I hear the creak of the door of my father’s room.
“This is not about your future career anymore, I.K.,” she says fretting, “You’re so consumed with your selfish ambition that it has burnt off the sensitivity required of that profession.”
“What is the matter?” he demands frowning.
“Honey I don’t know, I don’t know what’s wrong with Ikenna,” she replies with resignation.
“What is the matter Ikenna?” He asks.
“Nothing,” I reply and take my file from the chair.
“And where are you going?” he asks.
“To the library,” I reply abruptly.
He squints at me in bewilderment.
“Nkeonyere and her family are coming over today. We intended it to be a pleasant surprise for you I.K.,” she quavers.
He glances at my mother, turns around and slowly walks back to his room.

My sister and I used to be very fond of each other, but I’m ashamed of seeing her the way I am. I know Nkay, this surprise family reunion is her handiwork, but I can’t stand her censuring me in front of everybody and letting everyone know the dark side of me.

My poor performance in my first year, which ruined my opportunity to read medicine, confirmed her suspicion about my involvement in the cult, even though I never owned up to it. She kept referring to the dream I shared with her and quizzed me about my association with Uncle Dike when after the murder of Femi’s friends, she got the hint that the College Club was responsible for the serial killings, but when she confronted me, I denied it, and reproved her for associating me with the incident. But that didn’t change her opinion. I fear that Nkeonyere may bring up these issues. I can’t stand it.
I turn and walk towards the door. The shrill sound of the doorbell makes me halt. I stare at the door as my mother walks past me, reaches the handle and opens the door. I’m extremely bowled over.

“It’s a lie, Ikenna!” Nkeonyere greets me with excitement and opens her arms wide to embrace me. I let my file drop. We hug and hold each other for a long time. Then she leaves hugging me and puckers her face as if she’s examining whether it’s really me or whether she’s seeing a ghost.

She looks gorgeous in her native Buba and Iro made of Aso Oke material and adorned with two layers of glittering pearls around her neck. Because of her frequent deliberate abstinence from food when we were in school, she used to be a bag of bones and her six-foot height made her unusually lanky. But now she’s grown into a very beautiful woman, although I think she’s a little overweight.
“You kept us all in the dark I.K. Where have you been all these years?” she whimpers.
I hold back tears.
“I missed you so much,” she whimpers.
“I missed you too Nkay,” I quiver with emotion. When we hug each other again, Nkeonyere begins to weep bringing me near to tears.

I excuse myself with the pretence that I’m going to drop my file in the room. I shut the door behind me and break down sobbing. When she knocks at the door and calls my name, I brace myself and open the door for her.

She slowly paces about the room darting her eyes here and there reminiscing her youthful days here. Except for the ageing paint on the walls, the room hasn’t changed much from the way she left it. She sits on the bed and feels the bedspread with her right hand.
“Please forgive me for not replying your letters…and for being absent at your wedding. I shouldn’t have been so stubborn.”
“I thought you probably didn’t get my letters when I learnt that you changed your name,” she replies.
“It was only recently I adopted the name,” I explain.
She motions me to come to the bed. Sitting beside her reminds me of the night before my departure to Jos with Ifeanyi.
“So this reunion party or whatever, is it a portrait of the proverbial prodigal son or what?”

“No, to be precise, it is a portrait of the proverbial lost sheep. You completely abandoned the faith that is the centre of your existence. Did you really believe you could do without God, I.K.?” she replies with a gruff. “I’m not angry with you I.K…I’m worried. And you know why I’m worried.”
I remain silent.
“I heard about Sephia,” she hesitates and shakes her head, “How could you have degenerated so much to such enormity?”
I shut my eyes and take a deep breath. “Why doesn’t anybody believe me? I don’t know how it happened, I didn’t kill her,” I heave.
“God is not mocked, Ikenna. Whatever a man sows, he must surely reap. Her death was surely retributive of your past atrocities.”
I remain silent.
“I understand how your disagreement with Daddy may have affected your faith in God, but how could you be so vengeful about what happened to Julia as to take the life of another, how?” she quizzes.
“Daddy is the cause of all these. If he had…”

“Oh come on shut up, will you? You’re acting out pure human nature – always looking for an excuse to cover up sins or someone to blame for a wrongdoing. God asked Adam a question, and he goes, God it’s this woman that You put in the garden with me…human nature,” she sighs and takes a pause. “Daddy means well for you, Ikenna and you know that for sure.”

She shakes her head again “I know you didn’t kill Sephia…you led your bloodthirsty group to Victoria hall that night, I heard.” She squints angrily at me and says with a low tone, “Poor girl. God used her as an instrument to judge you.”
“How do you mean?”
“You loved her, Ikenna, didn’t you?” she asks.
I contemplate her question silently.
“When God tested Abraham’s faithfulness on Mount Moriah, He asked him to bring Him his only son, whom he loved as a sacrifice in Moriah hills, I’m sure you remember that story?”

The burning sensation in my heart reminds me of Harry’s last words to me before the judge’s verdict. Was it a coincidence that he mentioned the exact words I adopted as the fraternity’s motto? Is it also a coincidence now, Nkeonyere is talking about Abraham’s sacrifice on Mount Moriah?

Suddenly, I hear a hallucinatory voice in my subconscious, “The devil’s alternative…the devil’s alternative.” My mind flashes back to Morashe hill where I received the testament. I feel like I’m losing my breath. “I hate God for that,” I mutter.
“I know, but it wasn’t for that, it was because of Daddy. When Sephia died, God wasn’t testing your faithfulness to Him, He wanted to show you how far you had gone from Him, Ikenna.”
“Did He have to take an innocent girl to do that? Couldn’t He have spoken to me by some other means?”
“He had been speaking to you ever since, I.K., but because of your lust and wickedness you hardened your heart to His word so He did what He promised every sinner He’d do.”
“What?”
“Let their sins find them out, Numbers thirty-three twenty-three. There’s nowhere to hide from the Almighty, All-knowing and Ever-present God.”
There’s a knock at the door. My mother peeps into the room and cheerfully announces that her husband and the children have arrived.
“Ifeanyi told me it was you who initiated the idea of following him to Jos,” I growl.

“Oh come on carnal man, don’t go fishing for people to blame I.K., there’s only one way to deal with sin,” she hesitates, “Confess it, repent and turn away from your evil way. But if you think you can cover up your atrocities by adopting a new name then you’re kidding. Second Corinthians five seventeen says, if any man be in Christ, he becomes a new creation, old things are passed away and behold all things are made new…surrender your life to Jesus and He’ll give you a brand new life, Ikenna.

“You may have remorse over your past life, but there is no remission for sins without the shedding of blood. Jesus has shed His blood for your sins, I.K. and until you believe it and open your heart to Jesus, you will not be saved. Only in His name is there remission of sins,” she concludes and leaves me to join the family at the sitting room.

Once I had a tinge of discontentment about our activities in the fraternity, a desire to quit – when my future was vaporizing before my very eyes. But I was constrained by the fear of breaking the vow that we sealed with our blood, I was bound by our sworn oath of allegiance and so I smothered the feeling. That’s what the cult is about – only death can annul the vow.

In the light of all that Nkeonyere and my mother has said, it seems I’ve really been up against a conspiracy of circumstances initiated by God because of my rebellion. First I rejected…His will – the higher calling, according to Harry, then I broke the promise I made to Ifeanyi not to join the cult.

One constant truth that baffles me is that I never really wanted to get involved in the cult. To think of the fact that these circumstances swayed me into creating the worst of all fraternities and the grave consequences of these offences makes me tremble. Will God really pardon me? How did it all happen? How did I come to be filled with so much hatred for God that I turned against him? How could I have sold my soul to the devil…I practically crucified Jesus again.
“Uncle Dike,” I mutter to myself, “You deceived me.”

My mind flashes back to my forty-day isolation on that mountain, when I was inducted into the Grand Order of Dukes – the ritual, the imposing altar and the skeletons. I shudder and hurriedly leave the room.

Nkeonyere’s husband is the first to notice when I quietly ease into the sitting room. Mum is playing with her grandchild, Nkeonyere’s first son. Dad and Nkeonyere are sitting at the dining table discussing in a low tone. What else could they be talking about? The mood in the house makes me feel so ill at ease.

I awkwardly totter to him and we shake hands and exchange pleasantries. Nkeonyere breaks her conversation with Daddy and comes to meet us. “Iyke meet my husband, Olusola Kayode. Honey, my darling brother Ikenna,” she says putting her right hand around my shoulder.
“I’m delighted to meet you, Mr. Olusola,” I greet him.
“The pleasure is mine Ikenna. Your sister has told me so much about you already and we’ve all been looking forward to meeting you,” he replies.

The older of her kids who was playing with my mother is going to four and he’s bowlegged. He leaves her and waddles over to us, and taps his mother’s leg. “Mummy, mummy is this Uncle Ikenna?” he cries pointing at me.
“Yes Ayo, he is your uncle, Ikenna,” Nkeonyere tells him.
“But you said he looks like me…he’s so tall. Mummy carry me up let me see his face,” he blabs.

I stoop down slowly and kneel on the rug to let him see my face and open my arms to him. He walks into my arms and I hug him intimately. The younger toddler, who has barely turned one attempts to walk to us, but quickly gives it up and crawls speedily, excitedly to her brother. I raise her up and cuddle both of them in my arms.
“These are my children, Dubem and Ayo,” Nkay introduces them to me.
“They are beautiful, sister Nkay…your children are so cute,” I comment as I caress them gently.
“They’ve been longing to see you, especially Ayo, he’s been very eager to see you after he was told he took after your name at baptism,” Olusola explains.
“When Mummy visited us after his birth, she said he has identical features you had at infancy so we named him after you,” Nkeonyere adds.
Nkeonyere is right. I used to be bowlegged when I was his age.
“Grandma calls me I.K. Junior,” the babe says with the serene smile.

I couldn’t help admiring the purity and the innocence in his voice. We do have identical features, me and Ayo. But what inner traits do I have now that I’d like him to inherit? I ask myself. Tears blur my vision of his charming face. “May you never grow up to be like me Junior,” I whimper.
“No Uncle, I want to be like you,” he tells me in a childish tone.
“I’ve sinned so much…please don’t let him grow up to be like me,” I sob aloud.
“Don’t cry uncle, mummy loves you.”

“I know Junior, I know and I love you all too.” I embrace the kids. Then I give in to my emotions in the deathly hush as all eyes are on the kids and me. “I have done so much evil…my hands are stained with blood. I beg you all to forgive me…please forgive me,” I sob my heart out shedding tears.

Mummy is already in tears. My father cuddles her to stop her from weeping. Nkeonyere breaks down and starts weeping too. Olusola goes to her and pats her shoulder. Nkeonyere’s younger daughter starts wailing.

Daddy leaves her as soon as the bell rings to answer the door. Unable to contain the surge of anger at the sight of Uncle Dike, I leave the kids and rise to my feet frowning at him as he exchanges pleasantries with my father.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY- NINE
content: I learnt from my mother it was Nkeonyere’s idea to use this occasion to reconcile my father’s differences with him. The two brothers haven’t seen each other in over two decades. His resentment for my father has to do with his inclination to the Christian faith and his vocal condemnation of Dike’s devilish acts. I know the man of God will not cease to speak against him even if they eventually make up.

Typical of Dike’s affectations, he has succeeded in deceiving everybody into believing he has come to make peace with his only brother. But considering his choice of a companion, I’m sure he’s here, not to make up with my father, but he wants to see me. He is escorted by a member of his evil occult society, the cross bearer of the GODs and Shepherd of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. His real name is Nosa Fregene.

As soon as the get-together is over and the Bishop says the closing prayer and blesses the family, Uncle Dike asks my father’s permission to have me follow them to show him a good hotel where they’ll pass the night. My father is ill at ease with the idea, but in order not to appear suspicious or unfeeling by telling his brother to go groping around town for a place to lay his head, he grants Uncle Dike’s wish. I’m suspicious of his request because I know he is seeking opportunity to talk privately with me.

Nosa is driving the car while Dike and I are sitting at the backseat. He tries to cheer me up with an update on Becky and Anita, and informs me in a fraught low tone that Julia’s condition has been deteriorating. Surprisingly, I’m not moved. I fix my stare out of the window paying the least attention to him.
He suddenly changes the subject. “I believe you and Nosa have met,” he says.

It was Nosa who led me up the mountain during that forty-day isolation. He adjusts the rear-view mirror so he can see my face. I take a quick glance at him and turn my face away. My uncle calls him the fixer. He does practically everything for him. When he tells me that he works with the Drug Law Enforcement Agency, I suppose he must have influenced his employment into the Agency so he can take care of his dirty drug deals. He’s such a scavenger.
“He scouted for the lawyer that handled your case,” he continues.
I meet Nosa’s gaze in the mirror. He grins at me smugly.
“Why did it take you so long to come?” I ask him in a low tone.

“It doesn’t matter how long it takes to do something, the most important thing is that you eventually get it done.” He smiles and turns to face his front. “But if you’d like to know, we would have encountered a major problem if we went to court too early.”
“What problem?” I ask.

“With our rivals, what are they called, Golgotha,” he hesitates, “Femi was making frantic efforts to contact the prosecutor so he could be given the opportunity to testify in court…we had to wait until he was silenced…permanently.”
He killed him! I squint at Nosa. There’s a grotesque smile etched on his face making his lips slant.
“Your friend abdicated the post of Hangman and returned the staff you gave him,” he says with an angry tone, “That was an insult to the Order.”
I remain silent.
“I expected you to come back home immediately after your discharge,” Dike grunts floundering for some subject that’ll interest me and open me up for discussion.
“I am home,” I whisper.
“I see the prison has changed you so much,” he chuckles.

“I would have preferred that I died in prison. What use is my life now? I’ve lost everything – my dream, my destiny, and now I’m no longer in school. I lost everything to you Uncle, everything,” I complain.

“I understand you have been through so much lately. Adversities are inevitable in life. I was also in prison for five years. That experience has taught me that if we make the best out of the trials we face we’d discover we’ve lost nothing. That’s what destiny is all about…what you make out of life,” he replies, “Feeble-minded fellows are like amateur sailors caught in the tide, they see adversities as a deterrent to their destiny. But we, we see an opportunity in every opposing storm, we turn the tide in the direction we want it to go,” he pauses and crosses his hand over my shoulder. “It is the same instinct that inspired you to change your name from Ikenna to Jehoshaphat? And the tide has brought you to where you are, who do you think did it? Ask me boy, it’s you my son, you turned the tide, can’t you see it? Open your eyes and you’d see that your life has just begun to blossom.”
I purse my lips and remain silent.
“I know you’re angry the crucifixion parade failed that night.”
I don’t want to talk about it. And no matter how he tries to wheedle me into it, I won’t talk about the fraternity.
“You lost your power to the love of a woman. You breached your consecration vow.”
“I didn’t –”
“Yes you did, I.K,” he snaps, “in your heart. That’s where the spirit resides, the mediator of the covenant. He left you when you lusted after her.”
I turn and scowl at him.
“Anyway, her death was necessary for the sake of the brotherhood…she was the devil’s alternative.”
“I don’t understand.”

“The family would have been disintegrated on account of your infatuation,” he pauses and smiles lightly, “Jealousy is cruel as the grave the coals thereof are coals of fire. There are some secret that saves lives and are better to remain secret than told. That’s why I put you away in Katampe so you could die to live again. You did a wise thing to change your name, that way Ikenna Obiajulu is truly dead.”
“So it was you who circulated the rumour?” I gasp.
“It was Nosa’s idea actually. Aren’t you surprised our tentacles are spreading so rapidly? I’m glad to know that you’re determined to take another chance towards your academic pursuit.”
“How did you know about my plans to go back to school?”

He brings out a brown envelope and passes it to me. “I spoke to the vice chancellor about you, he’s a fellow in the Order. He has been informed about your visit. That’s twenty thousand Naira for your transport and minor expenses. Call me as soon as your meeting with him is done okay.”
Nosa is engaged on the wheels trying to get over a crowded street.
I stare blankly into space in perplexity. “So what’s the price this time?” I ask coldly.
“What do you mean?”
“You know exactly what I’m talking about, what price do I have to pay for this gesture?”
“You have become so suspicious,” he chuckles. “I am restoring your authority as the Hangman. I want you to continue to make disciples for me,” he falters.

“You are offering me the opportunity to go back to school so I can go on hurting my fellow students without provocation, shedding the blood of innocent souls and crucifying people upon the presumption that they are my enemies.” I throw the envelope back to him. “How come you derive so much pleasure in hurting other people? What do you gain?”
“You don’t seem to realize your situation, you have only four fingers and a sentence of death. I am the only chance you have to live, Jehoshaphat.”
“I don’t need your protection,” I bark at him.
“I am your father,” he states firmly.
“You are not my father…you’re a beast!”
“How dare you call me that? Withdraw that statement immediately.”

Hallucinatory echoes of his words resound in my head and a dizzy feeling sweeps over me as if I’m losing consciousness. Ifeanyi’s voice echoes in my subconscious, ‘there’s something you have, which I don’t…faith, faith, faith,’ he echoes. “You’re not my father,” I mutter. As soon as I open my eyes I notice from the rear-view mirror that Nosa’s eyes are closed and he’s muttering something silently at the wheel. “Stop this car now!”
Nosa opens his eyes, but ignores me.
“I said stop this car.”
“Pull over,” Dike tells him gently.
Nosa obeys him.
“I swore an oath of allegiance and sold myself as a slave to you, but now I’m renouncing that oath.”
“It is not in your power to do that,” he replies, “This is the path you have chosen and it is immutable. You know the oath is irrevocable,” he chuckles wickedly.
“I can’t believe you came from the same womb with my father. I will find a way to get my life back together again, certainly not with your help.”
“It’s too late to quit Ikenna, I gave you the seed you planted, but I water it and now the tree has grown to become the desire of every young man.”
“Even if it’ll cost me my life to destroy that testament, I swear I’ll do it.”

“It’ll certainly cost you your life, yet there will always be disciples of the inverted cross. And don’t forget, by the shedding of blood you enter and by the shedding of the blood only can you leave.”
“You’re an evil man Uncle Dike.” I step out of the car, bang the door angrily and stand away at the kerb. “You are infinitely evil.”
Nosa screeches off and the car vanish from sight.

The roaring sound of thunder startles me. The sky is black and void of stars. When the first drop of rain hit my nose, I suddenly realize how far away from home I am. And I’m not prepared for the showers, but I’m not deterred. I trudge my way back home, in the rain.
Daddy is sitting outside the house in his rocking chair when I arrive home at about quarter past ten, drenched by the rain. He is visibly worried.
“Good evening Dad.”
“You’re welcome Ikenna,” he replies. “Why didn’t your uncle bring you back home?”
“I didn’t want to bother him. But unfortunately, half way into my journey back home it started raining. I decided to carry on instead of going back to the hotel,” I lie to him.
Nkeonyere and Mum comes to the veranda looking drowsy from sleep and worried that I’m drenched. They must have been awakened by the sound of the gate.
“Nna nno o,” my mother welcomes me.
Nkeonyere looks askance at me. None of them except Nkeonyere has the slightest idea what has been going on between me and Uncle Dike. But it’s all over now. I’ve overcome him at last.

Everybody has gone to bed, except Nkeonyere. I can hear her voice in the sitting room praying in a subdued voice. Burdened with a strong feeling of remorse I walk clumsily to the sitting room and interrupt her prayer. She gives me audience as I confess to her all the atrocities that I committed in school. She asks me if I would like to accept Jesus as my Lord and personal saviour. I affirm. She prays to God to pardon my sins and commends me to Christ by making me repeat a short prayer.
“You have become born again,” she beams, “From this moment on, you have become a disciple of Jesus Christ and all your sins have been washed away. Don’t ever forget that.”
“I won’t,” I hesitate, “Thank you Nkay.”

I feel an unusual peace within me, and a ray of hope that brings exceeding joy to my heart. For the first time in several years I am able to sleep soundly through the night, without any fear, without any nightmares. I almost find it hard to believe this could happen in the twinkling of an eye, just because of my confession.

I’ve been indoors reading my Bible since Nkeonyere and her family left for Lome this morning. I promised her I’d pay her a visit soon. I told mummy not to bother serving my breakfast. When at lunchtime she returns and I tell her I’m not interested, she smiles and gently closes the door. Amazingly, I can’t get myself unplugged from the Book. This sudden understanding of the Scriptures is giving me an unusual satisfaction that leaves no appetite for food.
The door swings open the third time. This time it’s my father. “Jehoshaphat!” He calls with a tone of exhilaration.
“Yes Dad.”
“You have a mail,” he says cheerfully extending an official envelope to me.
I take it from him. The envelope has been opened already. I bring out the letter inside. The content of the letter leaves me speechless. I look up to him beaming with joy.
“Congratulations!” he says.
It’s my admission letter into the college of medicine. Mummy comes screaming from her room and hugs me.

“Sit down Ikenna,” Daddy beckons me and joins me on the bed, while my mother sits on the reading chair by the table. “Your sister told us about the murder case and your incarceration,” he pauses shaking his head. “Please forgive us. We didn’t hear about all these…nobody informed us.”

“It’s all right Dad, I also want to thank you for your patience, I uh – I thought you’d send me away when I came back home. And thanks for your prayers too. I would have been lost forever if it weren’t for your prayers,” I say with deep emotions. They couldn’t contain their feelings too.
“We’ve all learnt our lessons from all that has happened recently. I do hope you have also learnt some lessons?” he says.
“Enough lessons Daddy, enough,” I reply amidst tears of joy.

“God expects us to love one another the same way he loves us, unconditionally. But we fail because we always want to have things our way. I want you to know that we love you and we will always stand by you no matter what happens.”
“Thanks Dad I’ll never forget that. I promise I’ll never let you down again.”
“Well I don’t know how you came about it, but you chose a beautiful name for yourself,” Mum replies.

“I’ve been humbled by this experience to accept the fact that the choices we parents make out of good intentions for our children may not necessarily appeal to them. Because most of the times we fail to accept the fact that God has created them with a unique purpose in mind. I failed to realize you’ve come of age and even attained the wisdom to decide who you want to be, to be responsible for your decisions…even old enough to choose the name you want to be called,” he says.

“Dad you’ve always been right about your choice for me. I stubbornly refused what God has predestined for me and like Jonah I’ve suffered the terrible consequence of my rebellion all these years, but through my meditation in the Scriptures, and constant communion in prayer, the Lord has revealed that purpose to me and I’ve personally accepted the responsibility of fulfilling my calling…to honour the vow that you made to God, my Heavenly Father.”

They glance at each other in surprise. Daddy takes a glance at the Bible in my hand and asks me to give it to him. He takes a pen from his breast pocket and writes on the fly page, ‘This Book will keep you from sin or sin will keep you from this Book’.
Albatross

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY
content: Right now I’m trying to fit into the classroom regime. I hate schedules, and I can’t stand the lousy class timetable. Rules, order and the paraphernalia of systematic way of doing things make me nervy. And as a matter of habit, I hardly keep an appointment at agreed times – it makes me feel vulnerable. Mum describes that character in extreme terms, as unpredictable. Miriam, my ex-girlfriend tells me I’m the most unorganised man under the sun. But I like to be that way, to exude an air of mystery.

Miriam sneers at my jingoistic style of dressing and taunts at my constant use of Pidgin English. It was when she began mocking me in the presence of my friends, whom she refers to as people of the ghetto, that I asked her to stop seeing me.

It wasn’t long after we parted that I discovered who she really is, a hopeless faker and an opportunist. She had been two-timing me with a pauper who lives on her street in downtown Kay Dee. He goes by the name Tim. The only edge he has over me is that he’s in the university, and I suspect he’s in the cult because he’s always in the company of rugged guys who talked hard and always chatted about the brotherhood. I felt humiliated that I’ve been carrying a torch for Miriam, and I felt used when I discovered Tim was milking a fortune of me through her.

Initially, I was angry about the whole affair, but when it settled down, after he ditched Miriam, Tim and I became friends and he introduced me to another side of life, the real street life. There’s an uncanny fun about life on the streets that made me begin to find the mores of opulence boring and dreary. I hate to be regarded as one born with a silver spoon. And I think I’m normal.

Although on campus, I belong to those referred to as the crème de la crème, who are easily distinguished from the crowd by certain status symbol. I kind of enjoy being classless and easily blending with all sorts, especially everyday people. But there is a group I find irritably intolerable – paupers who pretend to belong to the rich class. For a camouflage they dress in cheap second-hand designer’s clothes that give people a false impression about them. They usually tell funny stories in funny accents about places they may never visit in their tenth life to cover up their inferiority complex. They piss me off when they revile my original Naija style.

Gambling is an essential part of street life, especially playing cards. Nearly everybody has an idea how to play at least three varieties of card game, for leisure. Back in my secondary school days, I used to enjoy playing cards with Paul, Ganza, Timo and Reuben, but I really took to it when Jsahp taught me how to do card tricks.
One day, during a card game in the hostel, I did a trick that amazed the crowd and somebody hinted that my talent could fetch some fortune if I took it to Oloko house.
“Come on board!” Lanre barks at me grinning widely because he’s been sweeping the stakes.

When we started the game, the bet was twenty Naira. But when the group saw the bundle of crisp notes I was carrying, he suggested we raise the stakes up to fifty. The house concurred. And he’s been sweeping the stakes ever since.

Oloko house is not a place for amateur gamblers, I was told. It has a reputation of overlooking any kind of tricks as long as you’re not caught in the act. There are usually no rules at such gambling joint except that fighting, which is usually unavoidable, is prohibited. As a matter of tradition, old boys usually rip off first-timers at such joints. And when that happens, it is advisable for the greenhorn not to argue despite apparent conspiracy by the house to defraud them of all they came with.

I’ve learnt from experience that after a few rounds of play on a rough surface as this, most of the cards usually receive tiny marks that are only visible to a sharp-eyed player. It’s a trick known to very few gamblers. The secret is to try to recognize a few key cards that’ll aid in guesstimating the predominant suits in the hands of the players, and predict how they’d respond to the call suit. I’m yet to master the cards, and when I do, I won’t stop winning until I suck every one of them dry.

“Flush cards,” I instruct Olumide, while I drop a fifty Naira note on the wooden table, which brings the total stake for each game to three hundred Naira. He’s next to me by my left. He is a second year student of chemistry, same department with me.

He flushes a brutal combination of an ace of hearts, followed by a six of spade, then a seven of hearts, a three of diamonds, an eight of spade, a ten of club, and lastly a five of spade on the table and then deals cards to each player. I observed from a tiny mark on the back of the last card he dealt to himself that he has the ace of diamond.

My combination is a five of club, a three of heart and a four of diamond. I drop the four of diamond as a call card. Olumide sighs bitterly. Lanre, whose turn it is to play, exhales heavily and drops a ten of diamond, and glares at me. I ignore him. Olumide is the last to cast his card. I was right – it’s an ace of diamond.

He’d be the first to pick from the seven cards on the table. He reluctantly picks up the first card on the table. I take the next two cards after him – the six of spade and the seven of heart bringing the total sum of cards in my hand to twenty-one. When we all tender our cards, no one tied with my twenty-one score. The nearest score is nineteen.
Olumide scored eighteen. I pick up five fifty Naira notes leaving one of the notes on the table as my stake and announce to the house, “I’m on board.”
They take turns to place their bets. I shuffle the cards and deal to everyone observing the back of the cards as I dole them out.

I have noticed that there are two categories of players here and they react to the set of cards they receive in two obvious ways after the cards are dealt. Those favoured by the call suit ignore the cards they’re holding and study the other players’ reaction to the cards on their set, the other group squint hopelessly at the cards and sigh or shake their heads as they drop the suit that agrees with the call card when the cards that are on the floor do not favour the set of cards they’re holding.
The seven flushed cards are a three of club, followed by a five of spade, then an ace of club, a six of diamond, a nine of club, a four of diamond and an eight of heart.

Frederick drops a ten of diamond as the call card. Olumide is the first to react. He sighs. He sighs virtually at all the games as a way of deceiving us. But I’ve been able to tell when he’s faking from when he’s really in a fix.
I lift up my cards and glance at them. I’m having a nine of spade, a six of same suit and a four of heart. I study the cards on the floor.

Fredrick usually plays a straight but deceptive game, his style is to drop a suit of a high value that is least represented on the floor with the intention to get the house to respond to the call suit. Naturally, the high value of the call suit suggests that he’ll definitively pick one or two of the cards on the floor in exchange. But considering the very low value of the first two cards, he leaves the house guessing whether he’ll go for the two cards or just one. It’s a tricky game that could be won by a score as low as fifteen I think that’s exactly what Fredrick is trying to do.

My set does not favour me for a premium score either. But from my estimation of the probable reoccurring cards with respect to the flushed cards, I presume that it’d be difficult for anyone of us to score a premium. Evidently none of us have the ace of the call suit and luckily none of my cards are of identical suit with the call card, which gives me some room to decide on the better of two options – to drop the five of spade or nine of spade.

Fredrick will definitely pick a card. If he picks the first two cards, it is likely that Lanre, who is next to Fredrick, will go for the ace of club because he didn’t respond to the call suit, but he dropped a heart of same value, which clearly shows that he’s aiming to exchange it for the ace of club. On the flip side, if Fredrick takes the first card only, Lanre will be out of the game because he’s not likely to pick the five of spade and ace of club, which sums up to seventeen since the sum of the two cards he’s holding can’t be lower than six. If I drop one of the cards of less value, I may end up with a score of fifteen, which will put me at a disadvantage.
