Alternatively, I drop the nine of spade to settle for a score of sixteen, or nineteen on the far end of the floor.
“I’m home,” says Fredrick. In other words, he is not picking any card from the floor.
“Shit!” shouts Lanre. I was right – he was aiming for the ace of club. After some hesitation he takes the first two cards. Fredrick laughs at him. Fredrick’s trick paid off.
Niyi who is next to Olumide takes the ace and Olumide heaves and picks the six of diamond.
The nine of club makes me two points less, and eventually ties me with Fredrick at the top. Niyi scored seventeen and Olumide scored sixteen. The other two tie at fifteen points each.

The fact that I’m a first timer in the house and I’m a fresher on campus got them infuriated. Lanre and Fredrick resort to collaborating with one another, but despite the shamefaced schemes of the frustrated tricksters to sway the game again, I continue to win most of the game until Niyi and Titus are depleted and angrily resign.

Olumide is taken off next. He’s broke. He turns down Fredrick’s offer to raise him and backs out. I suspect he’s in a fog. His exit gives me full sway of the game. Lanre throws in the towel after a few more games. Fredrick is feared stiff of playing alone with me, and so he equally backs out, although he still has some money on him.
I’m three thousand and fifty Naira richer than I was when I arrived the joint. I drop the card and walk slowly to the door as the fraudsters furiously glare at me.
“Hey!” he calls and comes close to meet me. “Hi. My name is Olumide,” he says sticking out his right hand.
“I know.” I fold the bundle of money and tuck it into the back pocket of my pants deliberately denying him a handshake anticipatory of a confrontation.
“I really like your style of play. It’s never happened in the house, that a first timer deflated all of us.”
“That’s why I’m a first timer. There’s always a first time for everything,” I reply sarcastically, “Let’s say I’m favoured today by mother luck. Who knows tomorrow may be your lucky day.”
“I’m not a novice pal. This aint no luck,” he replies with a smile. “What’s your secret?”
“Why don’t you go straight to the point, what do you want?”
He darts to his left and to his right. “Will you buy me a meal?” he says smiling sheepishly.
Lanre is leaning on the trunk of my car and Titus is sitting on a drainage culvert holding a long metal bar. I turn back to Olumide.
He comes to me and taps me on the shoulder. “They won’t hurt you as long as I’m with you.”
When we get to the car, he leaves me and whispers something to Lanre after which he glares at me and finally leaves the trunk. Titus stands up and follows him.
“So what is it you wanted us to talk?” I ask him as soon as we leave the area.
“Let’s get to the restaurant first…where do you know?” he replies.
“You’re the one who suggested I should buy you a meal.”
“Alright, let me show you where it’s at, the most exclusive joint in town. That’s where I love to go on my lucky day when I hit the jackpot.”
“Are your friends going to be there?”
“I die! None of them knows this place…I told you you’re safe as long as you’re with me.”
“I’m not afraid,” I retort and glance at him. “So where are we going?”
“Lattice,” he replies.
I grimace at him.
“Never heard of it right? Guys like you go to five star hotels to burn your inherited riches, but…”
“Excuse me! What are you implying?”
“Don’t give me that tough guy looks,” he chuckles and hit my head.
“I’m not an Aje butter okay.”
“Alright, alright.”

The real name of the restaurant is Lettuce. It occupies an entire story building. From the front view, it appears quiet and serene because the parking lot, big enough to contain up to twenty cars, is behind the house and awkwardly, the entrance into the eating hall is also at the rear.

The medium-sized signboard on the wall that simply reads, Lettuce, boldly printed in red on white background gives a false impression it’s an interior décor outfit rather than a restaurant. There are eight dining sections in the restaurant four on both floors, and each of the sections is exquisitely furnished. A wooden lattice separates each section and their furniture conspicuously differs from one another. Although the tables in the ground floor are not fully occupied, Olumide suggests we sit in the upper floor, which according to him offers a lot more privacy.
“Nice place,” I whisper as I dart about the room.
“Wait until you taste their food,” Olumide replies.

Surprisingly, the Lettuce is not a continental restaurant as I presumed from the name. It is purely African. The menu lists several African dishes with their origin italicised beside the names of the dishes, next to their prices. The prices listed in the menu makes me wonder if Olumide is really hungry or is he intending to make me burn up my jackpot as a way of getting back at me.
“There is a riddle about the place. Can you tell why it’s called the Lattice?”
I figure it’s the partition between the sections that gave it the popular name among its habitués. The craftsmanship of the wooden lattice cannot escape an observant customer’s notice.
“You are sharp-eyed really,” Olumide comments, “I failed it the first time.”
But my eyes are fixed on a group of girls sitting at a table behind Olumide. He waved at them as we were walking in.
“Besides what is listed in the menu, the Lattice also offers things that are pleasing to the eye,” he says when he notices where my mind is at the moment.
“Who are they?” I ask nodding in their direction.
“Red labels,” he whispers.
“Red labels! What does that mean?”
“Something to do with fire, which suggests that they are unapproachable,” he answers.
“Interesting!”
“That’s the fraternity of chicks dating the guys that head the cult groups on campus.”
“How interesting.”
“I advise you to keep your eyes away from those girls if you will live to complete your study.”
The waiter returns with Olumide’s food. “Sir, are you ready to place your order now?” he asks me.
“No…I lost my appetite,” I reply vaguely.
“Stay away from those girls,” Olumide warns me.
“What’s the name of the one putting on a black dress sitting by your left?”

Olumide turns and takes a quick glance at their table. “Lizzy,” he answers, “They say she’s the most beautiful girl on campus. I disagree…if there’s anything like a female pimp, you’re looking at one.”
“Really! And who’s she dating?”
“Ebube…he’s popularly known as Steel. He’s the most dreaded guy on campus.”
“I hope you’re not trying to get me intimidated,” I reply and beckon the waiter to come.
“I personally believe there are so many other girls more beautiful than Lizzy. I can link you up with the sweetest babes on campus.”
“You can pick from those, Olu. I’m not interested.”
“Please could you serve those babes whatever they want, drinks, food, whatever and let me have their previous bill as well,” I instruct him pointing at their direction.
“So, how long have you been gambling?” Olumide asks me.
The waiter goes straight to their table.
“How rich is Steel?”
“If that’s the way you intend to woo her affection, forget it. Lizzy won’t fall for such cheap moves. Steel literally lives off her.”
This fellow is either underestimating me or he’s trying to intimidate me. “So…her old man is wadded?”
“I told you before, she sucks,” Olumide says bitterly.
I grimace at him.
“She’s a contractor…a pimp. And please don’t make me lose my appetite.”
“You’re jealous.”
They rise from the table chattering on towards the stairs. My eyes meet Lizzy’s quick glance, she turns away immediately. One of the girls comes to meet us at our table.
“Hi guys. I’m Emem,” she greets us casting her eyes on me. “I suppose you’re the one who paid our bill. So which of us are you interested in?”
I’m thrown off balance.
She smiles. “Anyway, thanks for the gesture.”
I rally and reply smugly, “You could say thanks for nothing.”
“One of us is having a party on matric night, would you like to come?”
“I’d be delighted. Uh you guys are all freshers?”
“Nah,” she replies, “One moment I’ll get you the IV, excuse me.” She races after her friends.
Lizzy and the two other girls are waiting by the roadside for a taxi a few metres away from the restaurant’s gate.
“Lizzy, please can I have some cards,” Emem requests as she gets to where Lizzy is standing.
“How many?” Lizzy asks.
“One,” I barge in, “For a distinguished guest.”
They both turn around at my voice.
“Hi. Hugo Boss.” I put out my hand.
At first she hesitates. Then she obliges me. “Lizzy.”
“Nice meeting you Lisa,” I respond.
“My name’s not Lisa, my name is Lizzy.”
“Lisa’s nicer, Lizzy’s too common. What do you say Emem?”
“Yeah, I think you’re right,” Emem answers smiling at Lizzy, “Lisa…it sounds nice.”
“Sounds romantic,” one of the girls adds.
“So where are you guys headed? I can give you a ride to wherever.”

The other two girls drop off at the hostel. Lizzy and Emem stay together, off campus. My motive for taking them home is not hidden from them. Most of the girls I’ve met in the past make a big deal out of telling their addresses. I’m not expecting these to be less hesitant so I didn’t even bother to ask.
“You know you threw me off balance when you asked me which of you babes I was interested in.”
“Really?” Emem replies.
“Yeah, because I was still trying to make up my mind…you guys are so cute I was confused making the right choice.”
“You’re not serious,” she chuckles.
“I hope you’re not insinuating that we’re going out just because I accepted your offer for a ride,” Lizzy snarls.
“What do you take me for, a puppet or what? Why should I insinuate my interest? I’ll ask you out straightforward if I want to date you.”
“So, which among us are you asking out, lover boy?” Emem asks.
“I’ll let you know who at Lisa’s party,” I say gazing at her from the rear-view mirror. She’s looking out of the window.
“What are you going to do, give her a gift?”
“No. Well, since you’ve pre-empted me, I’ll let the cat out of the bag. My plan was to ask for her hand in marriage,” I reply.
Emem breaks up. But Lizzy’s not taking it lightly.
I gaze at Lizzy’s fallen face from the rear-view mirror. I’ve beaten her to it. “I’m serious Lisa, won’t you marry me?”
“It depends,” Emem replies coldly.
“On what? I know how to treat a woman, nicely.”
“Is that why you paid our bills? I can get any guy to pay my bills if I want to,” Lizzy replies.
“Any guy,” I repeat, “You know I’m surprised you could talk about men generally like that. It doesn’t sound like you,” I say impassively. “I’m not any guy, Lisa.”
She glances at the mirror and our eyes meet.

“I’ll show you why babes get stuck on me.” I open the glove compartment and bring out my international passport. I stick my finger between the pages that has a stamped visa. “How about joining me for a vacation to the States this holiday?”

Emem gapes at me stunningly. She takes the passport from me and then turns to Lizzy and shows her the passport. “This guy is crazy. It’s real!” she tells Lizzy. I take it from her and put it back in the compartment.
“You shouldn’t be keeping such a valuable document in a pigeonhole,” Emem advises me.

“Valuable? That’s a bloody collation of sheets of paper,” I reply in a cavalier manner. Yet I’m dead serious about it. It took me three previous unsuccessful visits to the embassy before I was finally issued the visa. I’ll be damned if anything should happen to the passport.
“That bloody collation of sheets of paper has a U.S. visa on it that’s what makes it different,” Emem reminds me, “Somebody could steal it from you.”

“Forget it Emem,” I giggle, “Nobody steals from me…I mean nobody.” But I’ll take the passport away and hide it somewhere safe as soon as I drop them off. This chick, Emem is sounding desperate, she cannot be trusted to resist the temptation to steal a passport with a valid U.S. visa. They’re capable of tipping the wink to somebody and make a fortune from selling the passport to a desperate visa seeker.
“You talk too big. What’s making your head swell?” Lizzy butts in.
“Lizzy,” Emem drawls, “Don’t talk to him like that.”
“It’s alright… so what do you say baby, you got a passport?” I ask Lizzy peeking at her from the mirror.

Emem turns around to face Lizzy. She draws close and they whisper to one another. Emem turns again facing her front. She clears her throat and smiles mischievously at me, “You know what Mr. big stuff. Lizzy’s stuck on this guy that has threatened to kill her if she ever thinks of leaving him.”
“Steel?” I respond.
“Who’s this guy?” Lizzy asks.
“Well, you’ll have your Lisa all to yourself if you’re strong enough to wrestle her from his grip,” Emem says.
“I’ll break him in two,” I brag.
“So about your trip, are you travelling alone?” Emem asks.
I know she’s speaking on Lizzy’s behalf now.
“Until now, yes I planned to go alone.” I peek at Lizzy again. “So do you have a passport?”
“Of course I do, why do you ask?” she snaps pretending to be angry.
“I hope you don’t think I’m saying all this to impress you…I’m crazy about you Lisa.”
“So Mr. Casanova, have you been to a vacation with a babe before?” Emem asks.
My response is a short laughter.
“I want to know if you’re planning to stay in a hotel or some persons’ house,” Emem continues.
“We have a family house in Florida, Tampa, precisely.”
Emem shakes her head. Lizzy turns away from my stare to the window pretending not to be excited, but her thin smile is a dead giveaway. Emem directs me into a compound.
“Uh oh!” she exclaims as I swerve into the compound. “Li-zz-y,” she drawls.

A group of five boys are sitting on a fallen log of wood by the building. A wee figure among them putting on a tan jacket over a white tee shirt and a baggy jean trouser stands up, another boy follows him towards the car. The girls are so frightened that in the silence I can almost hear their heart thumping.
He peeps into the rear window and thunders, “I told you I’ll be here at two. You kept me waiting.”
Lizzy apologizes and alights from the car.
Steel is barely five feet tall and probably weighs about sixty kilos, a small figure of a man. Going by Olumide’s description of him, he is far from the impression I have about him.

He lowers his sunshade showing off his bloodshot eyes. “Hey Grizmo! Listen to me and listen well. Any other day I sight you around my babe, or find her in this jalopy of yours, I’ll burn you alive inside this box, do you understand?”
“What a rude way of saying thanks,” I reply boorishly.
“Oh yeah, how about this?” He pulls a pistol out from his waistline and presses it hard against my nose.
“Will you stop piercing my nose with this empty barrel?”
“If you know what’s good for you stay away from her,” he threatens.
“Why don’t you tie her to your balls in that case?” I engage the gear and screech off.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY -ONE
content: It’s hard for me to tell whether Lizzy really loves me, or whether she’s only interested in the relationship for what she can get. If the latter is the case, she’s playing her cards well, but then she’s also playing with fire because I’ll make her pay dearly for it. I hope I’m not infatuated with her. I’m serious about taking her along on vacation, if her visa application goes through.

Although Lizzy’s giving me the impression she cares less about affluence, her friend’s comportment tells otherwise. Most girls are despairing and will fall for any guy that’s serious about taking them abroad, even if they have no iota of affection for him. They’ll give anything for that, anything – body and soul – for that opportunity. She’ll be taken aback if she comes to realize how serious I am about my promise.

I can’t believe my first date with her is turning out to be a flop. I’ve been sitting here for more than one hour and now I’m not sure if she’ll still show up. I bring out her international passport from the envelope and open the page that has her passport photograph. I’ve been gazing at her pretty face every now and then since I collected the renewed booklet from the immigration office. It will be a very pleasant surprise to her. And if she knows what’s good for her she’d better start thinking seriously about ditching that scumbag she calls her boyfriend.
“Hi!” It’s Emem’s voice.
I quickly tuck the passport into the envelope and raise my head.
“Have you been waiting for long?” she asks.
“Well not exactly. Where’s Lizzy?”
“Lisa, have you forgotten?” she replies jokingly.
She is dressed in a transparent white linen blouse with flowery patterns and like the day we first met, she’s awkwardly wearing heavy make-ups. I notice a portion of her face is discoloured.
“Won’t you at least offer me a seat?”
“I’m sorry, please.”
She stares at me with a wicked smile and offers me a cigarette from her pack.
“Not my brand.” I decline her offer. I could smoke anything that puffs, but I’m wary about getting too familiar with Emem.
“Really! So what do you smoke?”
“Nothing.”
“Nothing? I don’t believe you.”
“Where’s Lizzy?”

She draws in smoke for quite a while, and holding her breath she gasps, “Lisa you mean.” Then she smiles as she exhales a thick smoke. “She’s at home.” The smile on her face fades and she squints at me. She drags her cigarette apparently waiting for me to respond. “I wanted to talk to you.”
“About what?” I grumble.
“About Lisa of course,” she says confidently.
“Is she not coming?”
“Nope.”
“She doesn’t know about this meeting, does she?”
“Nope.”
It was Emem who actually informed me about the date and told me that Lizzy would meet me here. What’s she’s up to.
“I’m not interested in you Emem, do you understand that?”
“Yes I do. I don’t need a soothsayer to know that,” she replies obviously trying to be humorous. “Is this the first time you’re falling in love?”
“What sort of question is that?”
“I wasn’t expecting you to own up anyway…I know.” She taps her cigarette on the floor. “They say that when people fall in love, they lose their senses. But you…you’re about to lose your life.”
“What is your business with what I do with my life? Steel is a man and I am a man too…”
“It’s not about Steel,” she snaps, “Lizzy may not live long enough to make your proposed trip,” she whispers.
“What are you talking about? Is she sick?”
“Perhaps,” she hesitates, “But please don’t let her know I told you this.”
“About what? Look Emem I hope you didn’t come here to play hide and seek with me?”

I try to recollect what happened at the restaurant the first day we met. Lizzy was unusually calm and withdrawn at the table. Emem was the one who talked mostly that day. Even when we got into the car, she was still very moody and stared out of the window most of the time. I took it for her fear of Steel, but from what Emem is saying, there’s more to her calm demeanour than meets the eye.
“I’m listening.”
Emem takes a deep breath. “Forget it,” she says and stands up to leave.
“Wait a minute Emem.” I hold her hand stopping her from leaving. “Is he trying to hurt her?”
“No. He doesn’t even know about this,” Emem replies.
“About what? Is she infected with a disease or something?” I query her impatiently groping for some clue to what could be the problem.
She smiles lightly and remains speechless.
“I can help if she’s in any trouble, believe me…no matter what it takes I’ll do it.”
“It’s beyond you Ugo,” she says and blinks uncontrollably. “I want to help you Ugo. There are a lot of babes on campus you can toast. I could link you up with girls twice as beautiful as she is.”
“Now I get it,” I giggle, “He hired you to come and talk me out of dating her, right?”
“Leave Steel out of this Ugo,” she insists.
“Then talk to me. I can’t believe you kept me waiting here for nearly an hour only to give me bullshit,” I take a pause to let off steam, “Where’s she?” I growl.
“Forget about Lizzy Ugo,” she hesitates, “She belongs to someone else.”
“Where is she,” I roar.
“At home. But please don’t let her know about this meeting.”
I glare at her and leave her without saying a word.

The first advice Emem gave me the day after our first meeting was to be very careful with Steel. She said he could kill for Lizzy. Because of that I’m taking the anticipatory measure of parking my car far off from sight, to avoid a coincidental meeting between Steel and me should he or any of his friends decide to pay her an unexpected visit.

I hate the idea of sneaking around, because it gives me the impression that Lizzy has no feelings for me, that she’s leading me on until she gets the chance to travel abroad. But I still think I can make our date work, if I can make her feel secure and unafraid of him and assuring her that I care for her. Otherwise, I may resort to getting Steel out of the way, by all means.

Apart from parking my car a safe distance from the street, Lizzy introduced me to Murphy so he could check on her to be sure Steel or any of his friends are not around before coming to her apartment to see her. Murphy is barely ten years old, and helps his mother vend food items and household wares in a popular neighbourhood kiosk at the end of her Close.

Murphy’s real name is Edwin. I got to know when her mother called him by the name. He told me he doesn’t like the name, especially because it was his father who chose the name when he was born. He abandoned him and his mother when he was four years old. Murphy is too sneaky for his age and enjoys being a proficient accomplice in my secret romance with Lizzy, and he looks forward to my visit, because of the tip he gets for his errands.

As usual he’s thrilled to see me and quickly informs me that Steel left about half an hour ago. No need asking him to go tell Lizzy I’m around. He’s the only one attending to customers in the shop, besides, if Emem is right about her mood, she may not want to see me.

My initial attraction to Lizzy was purely sensual, but what Emem told me has sparked off a deep feeling of love within me, a genuine concern for her happiness, and I’m having the feeling her problem is more psychological than medical.
“Why didn’t you send him?” she asks wearily frowning at me as I ease into her apartment.
I notice a fresh wound on her face and slight bruises on her lips. “Are you alright Lisa?”
“Why is everybody asking me if I’m alright?” she chuckles, “I’m fine.”
“Well, I heard Steel left about half an hour ago so I thought there was no need sending him.”
She looks askance at me.
“Is it true you’re calling off the party,” I say as I pace to a seat next to her.
“I don’t want to talk about the party okay,” she replies.
“If it’s about money…”
“It’s not about money,” she snaps, “Why don’t you just leave me alone.”
“Because I want to be sure you’re happy, Lisa,” I reply.
“Can I ask you something Ugo?”
“Call me Hugo Boss.”
“Okay Hugo Boss, whatever. Why are you being so nice to me?”
“Why am I being so nice to you?” I reiterate and chuckle, thinking of what to say. “Well, that’s a rather difficult question …I don’t know why I’m being so nice to you.”
“I don’t want to break your heart. I think I’ve led you on too far and I’m beginning to feel…” Then she breaks up. “It’s not that I don’t like you…I don’t want to hurt you.”
My mind goes back to what Emem told me and a million thoughts flash through my mind – maybe she’s engaged to someone and she’s struggling with the decision now, or maybe she’s caught the Virus…
“If it’s about Steel, I’ve told you not to worry about him. If he tries to touch me I’ll put him exactly where he belongs.”
“It’s not him…I’m afraid something terrible is going to happen to me,” she says lifelessly.

“Lisa,” I say turning to her, “Nothing is going to happen to you okay, trust me. You know what, we can extend our vacation and stay in America as long as you want, get a good job and a cosy apartment and start raising our own kids, think about it Lisa. I’ve already got a good hand on your visa. As soon as…”
“I’m going to die,” she breaks me off suddenly. Then she leaves me and goes to the bed sitting up and leaning her back on the wall, she stares forlornly at me.

I swallow my words and gape at her. Then I stand up and pace restlessly about the room, trying to think of what to say at this precise moment to console her. “No, you won’t die Lisa. I won’t let you. You know what? I’ll talk to my mother about this okay.”
“Is she a native doctor?”

“Native…” I almost chuckle. “We have specialist doctors as our personal physicians. I can convince my mother to arrange for you to see one of our family doctors. I can explain our relationship to my parents and even get them to foot the bill for your medical treatment abroad if it’s necessary.”

“It won’t be necessary,” she sighs, “My problem is beyond physical,” she says in a despairing tone. She stretches and takes a book on top of the bedside drawer and brings out a crumpled sheet of paper. I quickly recognize it and bow my head as she slowly unfurls it.
“I can explain that.”
“You don’t deserve that privilege,” she replies bluntly.

From the way the paper is crumpled I suspect she denied writing the note, and that he came down very hard on her, and even lifted his hand on her. “Why did you lie to me about the mark on your face and your lips?” I ask her angrily.
“I could hardly convince myself I didn’t write this note,” she guffaws, “How on earth did you learn to copy people’s handwriting?”
“I’ve not owned up to that, you’re accusing me falsely.”
“Who else would have done it, you smart crook,” she replies humorously. “I’m guilty of underestimating you from the onset. Now I’m beginning to fear you interestingly.”

She won’t believe that this idea of leaving a note for Steel drafted in her handwriting and in her name came from the ten-year-old errand boy she refers to as, the small boy that sells in the kiosk. We stare at each other for a moment. I bet she doesn’t know what to say next, and I don’t either. But I’m concerned about the far-away look in her eyes. I take a long shot at bringing her back.
“You don’t know how beautiful you are Lisa.”
Initially she stares blankly as if she didn’t hear me. Then she responds with a grin.
“I want to ask you a favour Lisa,” I continue.
She hesitates.
“How long has this uh – ailment lasted?”
“I don’t want to talk about it okay,” she says droopily and rises from the bed. I watch her walk slowly to the door. “Please leave me alone,” she says holding the door ajar.

I stare at her for a moment trying to figure out a way to make her talk. Also trying to make up my mind whether to ask her directly about what Emem told me or not. I know I’d be betraying her confidence, but it seems it’s the only way I can get her to talk. I’ve got to find a way to help her. I stand on my feet and walk up to her. “Are you engaged to someone else?” I ask her in a low tone.
She frowns at me and sharply turns her face away from my stare.
“Emem tried to persuade me to leave you. At first I thought she was playing games with me, until she told me you belong to someone else.”
“What gives you the audacity to go asking about me from my friend?” I let her ease the tension. “Get out of my house, now!”

I shake my head in pity. Then I gently take her hand away from the door. “You’d be making a big mistake denying me the opportunity to help you, Lisa. I…I love you. I can’t leave you, Lisa, and I won’t let anyone or anything come between us.”
“How do I make you understand?” she sniggers and goes back to the bed.
“You don’t know how much you mean to me.” I bring out the pistol in the bum bag about my waist and drop it on the table. She trembles and eyes the pistol speechlessly.

Once bitten twice shy, they say. I don’t want to be caught off guard again. I don’t want Steel and his friends to treat me the way Tim and his fraternity friends did when we bumped into each other. I bought the pistol from a desperate drug addict who is a cultist. He was under the influence when we made the deal and mentioned that it often failed, so I bargained for half the actual cost and gave him one-fourth of the amount with a promise to give him the remaining when I’ve fixed it and tested it to be sure it’s in good condition.
“When you guys asked me if I’m ready to wrestle you out of Steel’s hand, I said yes…now I want you to know that I’m ready to take you by all means from anybody to whom you are affianced.”
“You’re crazy,” she whispers.
“No I’m beyond crazy…for you Lisa.”
“Please put this thing away.”
I put the pistol back into the bum bag and zip it up knowing that I’ve achieved my aim. “Promise me, for the sake of my love, that you’ll let me help you.”
“You won’t understand Ugo,” she says resignedly shaking her head. “There’s really nothing you can do.”
“You’re underestimating me again. Well, let’s start with the party, are you going to change your mind?”
“Are you insisting on it?” she sighs.
“List all the things you’ll need for the party, everything. I’ll come back tomorrow for the list.”
She shut her eyes and smiles. “Why are you doing this?”
“I’ll let you know at the party.” I peck her on her cheek. “Goodnight Lisa.”
“It’s so nice talking with you uh – Hugo Boss, thanks.”
“My regards to Emem. And please don’t let her know I told you about my conversation with her okay.”
“I can keep secrets too.”
“I can imagine. Sweet dreams.”
“Goodnight.”

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-TWO
content: When I called my mother to ask if they’ll be coming for my matriculation, she sounded grave. When she asked me, ‘who for God’s sake is Lizzy,’ I knew the travel agent had betrayed me in spite of my plea to keep our transaction confidential. She seized the passport and informed me that she and Dad have decided on withdrawing me from the university and sending me abroad to further my studies. When I told her Lizzy is my fiancée, the line went dead.

My mother can be cynical when it comes to my relationship with girls. She contributed to breaking my relationship with Miriam when she made her stop coming to the house, which eventually prompted my going to visit her. That’s how I discovered she was cheating on me. She accused Miriam of being the one instigating me into pilfering and shoplifting. Although I thought she was unreasonably overprotective, she actually bailed me out of the shackles of that crook. How would I have known of her relationship with Tim?

She called back later to inform me she was on her way to Lagos and asked me to come to her hotel room and see her in the evening. If she’s still in a state about our previous discussion on the phone it is to severely censure me. I’ve made up my mind not to see her.

Most of the students have left for the morning lectures leaving the hostel tranquil. The endless noisy chattering and loud music will commence as soon as they return from classes. I’m too woozy to put up with any intellectual stuff now, besides, I anticipate that my mother will come looking for me at the lecture hall since she didn’t see me yesterday evening as we agreed. So, instead I’m using this quiet period to think things over.
I pretend not to hear the knock at the door. But the visitor is persistent.
“Who is it?” I bark.
“Ugo…it’s me,” comes a male voice. Although the voice is familiar, I wonder why some people cannot answer a simple question. I reluctantly open the door.
“Jesus Christ!”
“Surprised? I intentionally refused to announce my name,” he responds.
“What in this world are you doing here?” I enthuse.
“What are you doing here?” he drawls in reply.
The folder in his hand and a textbook on clinical anatomy tells me he’s studying here. I remember he once told me about his ambition of becoming a medical doctor.
“How long have you been here?" I inquire.
“Two years. I’m going to my third year.”
“How come we never crossed each other’s path all this while?”
“The school is not as small as you think, besides the college of medicine is somewhat out-of-the-way from other faculties,” he explains.
“No o,” I drawl, “Don’t tell me that Jshap. Don’t you visit the female hostel, or the joints? There are so many places we could possibly have met.”
“Anyway we’ll talk about that later now that I know your room. Actually, it’s your mum that made me know you are here.”
“My mum!”

“Yeah, she’s at the carport. I coincidentally ran into her on my way to the registrar’s office. She said she checked you in class, but you were not there so I inquired about your room number from one of your classmates.”
“Well, I don’t want to see her,” I reply.
“Nothing, I don’t want to see her, period,” I reply and return to the bed.
“What is the matter Ugo?” he asks raising questioning eyebrows, “Do you realise the distance she covered all the way from Kaduna to come and see you. How could you say that?”
“Did I invite her to come?”
“Will you stop this nonsense,” Iyke says with a tone of annoyance.
I remain silent.
“Tell me, what’s the matter?” He coaxes me into telling him the problem and persuades me to go with him to see my mother.

Notwithstanding she hardly trusts people, she took to Jehoshaphat right from the day I brought him home and introduced him to my family. While he was staying with us, my father got the hint that he was dating Uzii, he was mad about it and wanted to evict him from the boys’ quarters, but my mother intervened and convinced him that it wasn’t true because Uzii threatened to elope with him if he forces Jshap to leave. As a matter of fact, Jshap is naturally congenial and likeable.

He enjoyed playing the Big Brother, and after he left, when my admission into the university came through, my mother kept wishing I’d meet someone like him who would affect me the way Jshap did. Her wish has come true. I never thought I’d meet Jshap again.

He censures me before my mother, diplomatically requests to take the matter off her hands, and persuades her to leave everything to him. Consequently, his Big Brother role earns us a two-bedroom apartment in order that both of us can stay together, so he can properly keep an eye on me, declares my mother.

I have deep respect for Jshap. And his height and muscular build sent shivers down my spine the first time I met him. But I hope he doesn’t make the mistake of taking me for the little boy of yesterday or try coming between me and Lizzy. Otherwise, he’ll be in for a big surprise.

Chief, as my father wishes to be called, entertains everybody with olden-days myths when we return to our new apartment from the matriculation ceremony. He’s not as old as most of his tales, but he usually enjoys making an impression that he’s aged. I guess that was how he bagged a chieftaincy title at an early age of twenty-six.

He is by nature a quiet fellow, but it takes two small bottles of Guinness stout, the only brand of beer he takes, to get him into this mood. My mother usually doesn’t allow him exceed that limit. He’s not a habitual drinker anyway, except when he’s in the company of Uncle Meniru, my mother’s younger brother. When I first heard his nickname, I attributed it to his lanky stature, but it’s not. Uncle Meniru is addicted to liquor. My mother calls him Tilapia. And says his only usefulness to mankind is that he’s a natural cure for hypertension, in other words, a comic relief. And my mother is right – there’s never a dull moment with Uncle Meniru.

David, Uncle Meniru’s only son is also matriculating today and we both agreed to host the two families jointly in my new apartment. There are nearly fifty people in attendance, and apart from Jshap, everyone in the room is of the Amanze and Jideofor stock.

Were it not for Jshap’s alertness, this celebration would have turned out a humiliation. I was so completely preoccupied with Lizzy that I forgot to make necessary preparations for the reception. When I hinted him about my predicament, when the formal event was about to wind down, he simply asked for some money and dashed out to get things ready. I’m amazed that he’s able to make this level of preparation in less than an hour. I’m even more surprised that he took the pains to get particular things my parents enjoy.

In addition to the delicious meal of fried rice and chicken he went the extra mile to make available well-prepared pounded yam and bitter leaf soup, my father’s favourite, which really surprised, but pleased him. He made sure that everyone has enough of his or her choice of drinks and food. Even the eight children that came along with their folks are not left out of the fun, Jshap bought them ice creams and also arranged for games and toys to keep them lively.

As soon as my father ends his tales, Uncle Meniru takes over. My father is his first victim. He tells a joke to emphasize a point they had argued about on our way from school, about parents who will not show their children any example, yet are impatient with their ward’s performance in school.

He tells everyone that I once came to his house complaining about my father’s constant rebuke over my poor results in school and he, Uncle Meniru, consoled me and told me that my father didn’t do any better in his days. One day, my father asked me to go and clean up his room for him. While I was about the chore, I came across his final year report sheet when he was in secondary school – he took the last position in class. I was excited at the discovery and showed it to those who had witnessed the way I was being lashed for performing poorly. When my father was called to explain the rationale behind his brute action since, obviously, I am doing a lot better than he did, my father who claims to be a guru in the sciences explained that the report sheet that I showed them was the negative copy of the original report sheet, claiming that he actually took the first position in class – in the original copy with the school.

We all break up, and this one really gets to my father. He can’t stand Uncle Meniru’s ribbing. My mother is always the one to rescue him. This is the part Uzii’s missing. My mother says she couldn’t make it because she’s down with malaria. We usually enjoy Uncle Meniru’s visit to the house and we’re never bored watching them rib one another, especially about their childhood days – their pranks and misdemeanours. My father would taunt my mother later with the information he gathered from his jokes.

My mother asks me to show her around the house to see if everything is in order. First, I take her to the kitchen, then to the toilet and bathroom. When we enter my bedroom, she sighs about the scattered state of the room and teases me about needing a girlfriend to help me organise things, then she asks me why I didn’t invite my fiancée to the reception.

Because I am an only son, my parents had always hinted about my getting married at an early age, especially my mother. It is usually the case in the East for two reasons. The first is the anticipation of a grandchild. And secondly, which I call the death wish, is my father’s grave concern – the fear of any eventuality that may cause discontinuity of the lineage and family name. Although he hasn’t asked me about my relationship with Lizzy, from the way he reacted when she came to greet us during the matriculation ceremony, I am convinced that my mother had mentioned our relationship to him. And from the tone of her speech, I think she likes her.
“What’s her background like?”
“Uh – you mean –”
“Will you stop bleating like a goat and answer my question,” she snaps, “What kind of family is she from?”
“Well Mummy, honestly I don’t know,” I reply, “But you like her Mummy, don’t you?”
“Not until I’m sure about her stock.”
“I mean do you like her as a person, you know as an individual.”

“I know what you mean,” she chuckles, “Yes, she doesn’t look bad. But you must understand that marriage involves a lot more than just dating a girl you pick on the street. It’s a lifetime investment.”
“Investment! You make it sound dead serious.”
“As a matter of fact, it’s a serious investment. And that’s why I’m worried about your relationship…you’re too young to understand what marriage is.”
“Mum,” I drawl, “I thought we’ve been through this line before,” I chuckle, “I’ll be twenty in November, you married Dad at the age of nineteen.”
“We have different perceptions about marriage, men and women. By the way how old is she?”
“She’s nineteen,” I beam, “I’m a year older than her. But she’s quick-witted and she encourages me to read if you’re bothered about my studies.”
“Your father is worried about her…but I’m more concerned about you,” she says gravely.
“Mum everything is gonna be fine trust me.”
I hear the laughter in the sitting room. Uncle Meniru is still telling jokes.

“The standard genetic rule states that every parent contributes a part of themselves to the personality of their children, their aptitudes, temperament and intelligence,” she hesitates, “But there are exceptions in every law.”
“What exactly are you saying Mum?”

“There’s one thing you never really took from your father and me…the capacity to love. Don’t mistake my asking about her family background to be about the affluence of her parents. I wouldn’t be fair if I insist on that, after all, we weren’t any better when your dad and I met. She can’t love you if she doesn’t have it, if she’s never inherited it in the first place.” She darts about the room and fumbles with my packet of playing cards. Then she drops it again on the reading table. “I don’t think this girl, what’s her name again?”
“Elizabeth.”
“Yes Elizabeth…I don’t think she has the capacity to love?”
“Of course she loves me.”

“We’ve not come to that part yet Ugo. It takes more than words to express love.” She sits down on the chair by the reading table and motions me to sit on the bed. “So tell me what you know about her family?”
“Well, to be honest we’ve not talked much about our families.”

“That’s the new order in relationships, I know. Let’s keep momsy and popsy out of the relationship. That’s why divorce is on the increase. Civilization has influenced you children to build marriage relationships on misplaced priorities and inordinate selfish expectations.”
“Mum it’s not like that,” I chuckle.
“So have you defined your relationship?” she asks in a brisk tone.
“You mean proposing to her? No I’ve not. It’s just an intention.” I blush and drop my eyes to the floor away from her questioning squint. “Well, I only said it to her as a joke.”

“But you promised to take her on a vacation to the States. If she knows you can afford it, then she’s no longer taking it for a joke. If you ask me, I think you’re going to change your mind about her with time,” she says with a smug smile.
“No I won’t.” But I’m worried about the smile on her face. “But why do you think so Mum?”
“Because you’re going to be so mad with her when you realize that she never loved you, that it was your trip to the States that lured her into the relationship.”
“I’ve known you to be too suspicious. Why don’t you give her the benefit of the doubt,” I reply impatiently.
“Okay, okay,” she says raising her hands in resignation.
“So what do you say about the vacation, are you giving me the nod?” I ask her with exaggerated enthusiasm.
“No!” she answers with a tone of finality.
“Mum, this is –”

“Save your breath mister,” she snaps, “My mind tells me she’s evading discussing sensitive issues with you and deliberately leaving you to make up for those essential details with assumptions for fear you might change your mind. A long lasting relationship such as marriage doesn’t permit such assumptions.”
“What kind of details are you referring to?”
“Have you asked about her past life? She doesn’t look like a virgin to me.”
“Don’t mum me,” she snaps, “And I hope you don’t think I’m prejudiced...but she has the traits of a two-timer.”
“I know where that’s coming from,” I quip, “Mum, she’s not like Miriam.”
She chuckles and remains silent.
“We talk a lot Mum, she tells me everything,” I reply.
“And you don’t know anything about her family?” she sighs as she rises from the chair.
“I didn’t say I don’t know anything. I said we’ve not talked much about our families.”
“Anyway, I’d like to study her closely, why don’t you bring her over to the house at break up this semester. I suggest you guys take a train.”
“My goodness! A train!”
“Yes. It’d give both of you the time and right environment to talk deeply. That’s the only way you can prove her, by what she says. And I want you to insist that she answers your questions.”
“You’re making me nervous.”
“Better to be nervous now than have a nervous breakdown later. You’ll be more confident of your decision by the time the ride is over.”

There’s a knock at the door. Jshap brings two huge slices of cake and two cups of ice cream for desserts for me and my mother. My mother doesn’t like cakes that much because of her weight, but she can hardly resist a gateau. How Jshap got to know about that? I ponder.
“Thank you very much, what’s that name you usually call him?” she asks me humorously.
“Jshap,” I answer.
“Yes Jshap. And thanks for everything. Ugo has told me how nice you’ve been to him.”
I’ve not told her anything. But she likes him a lot. However, I owe Jshap for putting this together. How could I have coped without him?
“I guess that’s because we have a mutual desire,” Jshap replies.
“And what could that be?” she asks.
“The need for a brother. The Bible says that there’s a friend that sticks closer than a brother.”
“You didn’t ask about your sweetheart, or has someone else stolen your heart from her?” she teases him.
“Nobody is strong enough to do that,” Jshap replies with ease. But I’m sure Jshap is not comfortable talking about Uzii, about any woman generally, especially with my mother.
“Are you sure? But you’ve not been communicating with her since you left us, have you?”
“Actually uh – I wrote her some time ago.”
“Not since you entered school, I’m sure.”
“No,” he hesitates, “Excuse me ma’am.” And he leaves us.

We both ease back into the sitting room to join Uncle Meniru’s jokes. He’s still talking, and he’s definitely still on the bottle. But nearly everyone wearing a wristwatch is now peeking at it. And I’m late for my appointment with Lizzy as regards concluding arrangement for the party tonight.

Jshap has tirelessly been attending to the guests single-handedly. I tried to help him when we came back from school, but he refused and asked me to go be with my folks and have fun. She stares at him absent-mindedly as he paces to the children to attend to a wailing child.
“He’s such a humble fellow. I wonder why he sounds very indifferent towards Uzii,” she says almost in a whisper.
I can tell when my mother is upset about a thing.
“I never knew he didn’t have a brother,” she quips to conceal her emotions. “Have you ever met his parents?”
“Strange fellow, but he’s exceptionally nice…does he talk about Uzii?”
“Not anymore.”
She turns to me. “Who’s he dating?”
“Don’t shout. He doesn’t have a girlfriend.”
She gapes at me.

At first I didn’t believe it myself. Although he’s not rich and he’s not sophisticated, Jshap has an amiable aura that attracts both sexes to him. In spite of his charisma, he eschews damsels and he’s turned down invitations from various groups to join the cult. Perhaps the only thing I know about his family is that they didn’t attend his matriculation. He told me when I was apprehensive about hosting my guests and asked him how he coped during his matriculation. Although he didn’t explain why, I get the impression they may not be as rich as to gather the entire family for the ceremony. But the fact that he is willingly doing this for me despite being denied the privilege is completely supernatural. I guess it’s this Jesus thing about him.
“He’s born again.”
“And what’s that supposed to mean?” she quizzes.
“That means he’s a disciple of Jesus.”
She’s short of words.

My father is the first to express his satisfaction when the banquet is over. He particularly enjoyed the pounded yam and that surprised my mother who knows how choosey he can be with food. Even the kids can’t hold back their feelings – some of them break down and start whimpering that they want to stay with Uncle Jehoshaphat when they’re told it’s time to go home.
And for the third time he tells him to look after me and gives him a gift of ten thousand Naira. He deserves it.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-THREE
content: As soon as my parents and the guests left, I race to her apartment. I know she’d be worried-sick by now. I’m already one hour behind our scheduled meeting and the party is expected to be underway three hours from now. Yet there are still too many things to be done, and the time is so short. How on earth am I going to coordinate all these?

Jshap has proven on two occasions to be very good with organising things, which happens to be my greatest weakness. If it were possible, I would have asked him to help me put things in order for Lizzy’s party. But considering his strong inclination to the faith, it’s needless even thinking about it.

When I apologize to Lizzy for my lateness, she accepts it with ease and tells me she anticipated it. She says she was able to get Steel to help her out in my absence. She commends my parents for taking the time to come all the way from Kaduna. I am tempted to ask after her family – why they didn’t come? But I refrain. It’s not the right time.
She has no fuss about following me to my bunk for me to dress up for the party after patiently waiting for her to tog up. I’ve always wanted to show off my crib to her.
Jshap is not in the sitting room, but I notice he’s busy cleaning up the house after the feast. He doesn’t mind doing the chores.
“Please make yourself at home okay.” I beckon her to a seat.
“Nice place,” she comments darting her eyes about the ostentatiously furnished living room.
I stop the song that’s playing, slot a compact disc and turn up the volume. Jshap speaks from his room and tells me he’s coming.
“I’ll be with you in a minute okay,” I tell Lizzy.
“Take your time please,” she replies.
“Do you care for a drink?”
“Nah. Can I see your picture collection?”
“Sure, let me get it.” I saunter to my room leaving her in the sitting room. Jshap’s door squeaks, he joins Lizzy in the sitting room.
“Hi,” Lizzy greets him.
“Hello,” he responds, “You must be Elizabeth?”
“Yeah I am. How did you know my name?”
“How else? Anyway I’m pleased to meet you. You may call me Jshap.”
“Sorry,” Lizzy responds, I believe with raised eyebrows.
“That is short for Jehoshaphat,” he explains.
I return to the sitting room with my photo album. Jshap goes back to his chore at the dining area.
“One moment, I’ll be with you okay.”
“It’s all right. I just need something to keep me occupied till you get dressed,” she replies taking the album from me. “You are this domesticated?” Lizzy tells Jshap.
“If you’ve heard of the man who goes by the name meticulous, that’s him,” I chip in as I head to my room, “He won’t sleep until he’s laid the tiniest speck of dust outside.”
“Aren’t you coming for my party?” Lizzy asks him.
“I’m just hearing about it. Are you a Jambite, or you’re celebrating something else?” he replies.
“Well, I’m a Jambito,” she replies humorously and they both laugh. “Didn’t he tell you about the party?”
“Maybe he mentioned it, I can’t remember.”
“Anyway, I’m inviting you.”
“Just like that?” Jshap asks her sounding pretentious.
“Let’s say you’re a distinguished guest,” she replies jokingly.
“You’re flattering me. Anyway I don’t go to a party I’m not invited to.”
“Properly invited you mean?”

I smile to myself and shake my head as I eavesdrop on their conversation and kit myself up in a jean trouser and a denim jacket. I’m amused at Lizzy’s futile effort to get Jshap to come for her party. He knows about the party all right, but I didn’t bother inviting him, I know he won’t come. Poor Lizzy! If only she knew that Jshap is only pulling her legs, she’d just forget him and watch the album. He won’t even put in an appearance lest he goes to hell, for revelry. I lace up my sneakers and join them.

“Is someone trying to lead someone into temptation?” I break in rhetorically as I ease back into the sitting room, “Forget about Jshap Lisa, he won’t go even if you dragged him to the party. Hasn’t he told you he’s a son of God and doesn’t fellowship with sinners like us?” I say derisively.
He plays it cool and answers back jesting, “If you make me change my mind and follow you guys to the party, I’ll outdo everyone on the dance floor.”
“I die.”
“Now I see the connection with the name. Anyway I’m pleased to meet you,” she replies with amusement.
“Same here. I’ve accepted your invitation,” he says humorously.
“I won’t dare you,” Lizzy beams, “I kinda like that name, Jehoshaphat.”
“Let’s go,” I growl.
Lizzy keeps the album on the chair and stands up. But apparently feeling giddy, she sways and falls back in the sofa before I could reach her.
“Is she alright?” Jshap asks.
“Of course she’s fine,” I reply impatiently.

She comes around and offers me her hand to help her up. Then she smiles widely at Jshap and offers him a handshake. “Can I come and see you some time later,” she hesitates, “If you don’t mind I’d like you to pray for me.”
“Anytime Lizzy,” Jshap replies with a smirk on his face.
She dawdles with disengaging from their handshake while staring at him.
“Can we go now?” I snarl at her.

Lizzy reluctantly follows me to the door. But her eyes are still on him. Even when we enter the car, Lizzy absently gapes at the door as if it is transparent and she could see him through it. She’s obviously entranced, I reason. “I wish I had met him before now,” she mutters as I turn on the ignition.

I stop and gaze at her. “And what’s that supposed to mean?” I sneer at her overtaken with envy. “Before you start dreaming of making a Romeo out of him, he’s next to a eunuch,” I tell her coldly as I engage the gear.
But she still doesn’t respond.
“Lisa, Lisa!”
She is still, her mouth is wide open and her eyes are fixed at the door.
“Lizzy! Oh my God!” I leave the car and race back into the house. “Jshap, Jshap! Please come quickly, I don’t know what is wrong with Lizzy.”
He breaks his chore and follows me hurriedly to the car. He places his index and middle fingers on her neck to check her pulse.
“Is she alright?” I ask anxiously.

Jshap is silent. He carries her out of the car and takes her into the sitting room. He lays her on the floor and asks me to fetch him his apparatus and stethoscope. He removes her jacket, unbuttons her shirt and places the stethoscope on her heart area, and then he moves it towards her right breast and after to the left. The expression on Jshap’s face tells me something is wrong.
“Is she alright?” I repeat.
“It is well,” he replies calmly. He keeps the stethoscope down and kneels beside her. Placing his right hand on her brow he begins to pray.
“Should I go and fetch a doctor?”
He ignores me and continues praying.

Pacing about the room nervously, I watch for a sign of her recovery peeking at my watch every now and then. Nearly fifteen minutes after, Jshap is still praying, and the situation hasn’t improved at all – she’s still lying lifeless on the floor.
“This is no use,” I mutter under my breath. I better go and fetch Emem she’d probably be able to contact Lizzy’s doctor or somebody to help her.

The Astoria Hotel is already teeming with invited guests, while a herd of uninvited guests waiting for the slightest opportunity to gatecrash into the party hall are at the gate. Three hefty bouncers posted by Steel to check gatecrashers halt me at the gate and demand for the invitation card. I don’t have my card and I don’t have the time to start explaining the situation to these people either. I doubt if they’d have the patience to listen to whatever I have to say anyway.

Steel is leaning on the wall some distance away from the gate watching and smiling at my frustration. After a while, he whispers something to one of the boys with him pointing his finger at me. Then he turns and walks into the party hall. In desperation I put my hand behind my back to bring out my pistol, which is tucked into the waistline of my pant, but a strong grip on my hand from behind restrains me.

“Don’t!” he whispers in my ear. It’s a very familiar voice. I turn around. It’s Paul, my close friend in secondary school. He leads me away from the crowd to the car park. “What are you doing here Hugo Boss?”
“We’ll talk later Paul. I’ve got a situation right now. I’ve got to fetch somebody in there,” I gasp.
“Take it easy, calm down,” he says calmly.
“This is my party. How can those bastards not let me in? I have to see Emem and if those idiots try to stop me I’ll blast my way through.”
“You don’t have to…here use my IV.” He hands me an invitation card. “But make sure you’re out of the place in three minutes a bomb is about to detonate, now hurry.”
“A bomb?”
“Yes a bomb. Hurry it’s already ticking.”

Paul and I haven’t been in touch since we left secondary school and I don’t have any idea what he now does for a living or what he means by ‘a bomb detonating’. I do know in those days we communicated with such secret codes. But what do I care, I won’t stay long.

When I present the card at the gate, they let me pass. Two other bouncers stop me at the entrance into the hall and demand that they search my body, but because of the pistol in my trouser waistline, I refuse to yield. They insist on having me searched before I can enter the hall and order me to go back if I’m not willing to submit to them.

I turn around and walk slowly to the gate in frustration so as to put away the pistol in my car and come back. But unconsciously I can’t feel the metal on my waist and when I touch my waistline to be sure, I notice the pistol is not there. I hear somebody whistling at the fence when I turn, Paul waves my pistol at me and beckons me to leave the compound. I turn back to the entrance to the hall instead and yield myself to the bouncers. After fumbling my pocket and frisking all over my body and finding nothing, they let me in.

In spite of Lizzy’s absence, the party is well underway. Emem sights me and freezes at the expression on my face. Steel blocks me off from reaching her and asks about Lizzy's whereabouts. I clench my fist and land him a hard blow on his right cheek. He falls like a pack of cards and the lights go off instantaneously.

Bang! Bang! I duck at the sound of the gunshots covering my head with my arms. In reprisal, a submachine gun cracks, and then the pistol. Then the shooting is followed by a momentary silence, then a shriek of pain. Suddenly, someone pulls me up and leads me hurriedly out of the dark room.

I am deafened by the sound of the gunshots. And I’m dizzy. His face is covered with a black facemask and he’s demanding for something impatiently, but I can’t hear him. He finally put his hand into my trouser pocket, takes my car key and throws it to another fellow standing some distance away clutching an AK- 47. One of them takes me by the hand and guides me through a small gate leading out of the hotel premises. After walking a short distance, he stops and pulls the mask off tucking his pistol into his waistline. My car stops abruptly in front of us, one of them sitting at the back opens the door and the one who led me out of the hall beckons me to enter. Everything is happening so rapidly I think I’m losing it. Sandwiched between two of them at the backseat, I mutter under my breath, “This must be a dream.”

A black Toyota Land Cruiser SUV overtakes us and the one driving my car follows him into a deserted road. He drives past it and stops. They all step out of the truck and stand beside the door waiting for a dark figure walking towards the car against the blinding beam from the headlamps.

He comes straight to me where I’m sitting in the back seat of my car and announces, “You’ll find details in the glove compartment,” he says, “Oh, I almost forgot. He asked me to give this back to you.”
I take my pistol from him.
The others match off behind him and into the four-by-four and they screech off.

Sitting in the backseat silently, I contemplate what to do next after they drove off. Going back to the hotel to fetch Emem will not be such a good idea – by now the police would have surrounded the area and anybody found loitering around the area may likely be arrested, branded a suspect and detained. The thought of Lizzy comes back to my mind. “When is this nightmare going to end,” I mutter to myself.

I’m too muddled up to figure out what to do about Lizzy as I drive back home. “So this is what she had been afraid of…but how could she die like that?” I ponder. I park the car and muster up some courage. Then I step out of the car and trudge on to the front door. I can hear Jshap’s voice faintly. Is he still praying? I move closer to the house. His voice is more audible with every step I take closer to the door. Then I realize he isn’t praying. I think he’s talking to somebody. Maybe he was able to get some help eventually, I wonder. He’s talking to a doctor perhaps. But what about Lizzy, is she well?
I knock at the door and turn the handle. I am amazed to find two of them sitting down with the Bible in their hands. Jshap gives me an impassive glance. Lizzy smiles at me. Words fail me.
She ignores me and smirks at Jshap.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-FOUR
content: Campus strong man hamstrung is the lead caption of the campus journal, Enclave. Bad news travels so fast. The newspaper stand is teeming with students who are captivated by the story of what happened yesterday night. Depending on the perspective from which each person is judging the incident – causative or otherwise – everyone here is affected differently towards the victims of the shootout. And everyone is eager to air his opinion.

The gist is that some are suggesting that the celebrant, who was conspicuously absent at her own party, connived with the group that carried out the attack. She is prime suspect and should be arrested and interrogated, and if possible detained. Others argue that the authority is too lenient over the cult menace, and suggest that stricter measures be taken to clamp down on their activities, such as rusticating any student suspected to be a cultist and sentencing confirmed members of the fraternity to death by execution or electrocution. I pay for a copy of the newspaper and silently leave the scene.

Disappointingly the report is a far cry from the truth, a hurriedly fabricated version, which evidently is a mere deduction from a generally acclaimed liable motivating factor that sparks off such hostilities – woman palaver.

Apart from Steel, who the paper says was hit by a bullet in his leg, there were three other casualties. Two of them are believed to be members of Steel’s cult group, they are already in the University Teaching Hospital and Emem Sikibo, Lizzy’s friend, she was hit by a bullet in the spine – she’s bought it. The paper also has it that security reports say that an unknown secret confraternity is responsible for the bloody attack. But urges the security to arrest Elizabeth Abebe, the celebrant, to explain why she was absent from her own party insinuating she had a prior knowledge of the attack.

Maybe I should go to the security and tell them the reason Lizzy was absent at that time. But, who would believe my story – that at the time the shooting was going on Lizzy was lying in my living room, dead. At least I have a witness. And if the security believes the report, Jshap will become an instant hit and accorded the respect of great magicians.

I close the newspaper and reflect on what happened yesterday. How did Jshap manage to bring her back to life? Jshap lied to me about quitting black magic. I’ll go and see Lizzy instead, to find out exactly how Jshap brought her back to life.

Murphy says he hasn’t seen her since morning and doubts if she slept in her house last night. He also tells me that Sammy, Steel’s closest friend has been to her house three times already this morning. Murphy pries me to get more facts about the hit last night. His mother looks fiercely at me. I quickly drop a line for Lizzy and instruct him to let her have it as soon as she comes back and zoom off.

Although Paul and I have done heinous things in the past when we were in the gang, I am really thrilled by the spontaneity and swiftness of the attack yesterday, and to crown it all, the secrecy of their operations. Who are they? I feel conceited that I’m the only one who has a hint about the mysterious group that stormed the party yesterday. And I may just want to explore this group, not that I’m going to report them, but…the guns. Who are these guys? I ponder over this as I sit in the couch.
“Hey what’s up?” Jshap greets as he comes in from his room.
I pretend not to hear him.
“Hey, what’s this I hear about the shootout at the party?” he says as he goes to the fridge.
“What else do you want to know, haven’t you heard it all?” I reply.
“Well, I was wondering why you didn’t tell me anything like that happened.”
“Maybe that’s because you never showed any interest in the party from the onset,” I snarl at him.
He chuckles and pours water into a glass. “I read about it in the campus journal.”
“Yeah…a concocted version of the incident you mean.”
“You definitely should know better. So tell me, what really happened?”
I exhale heavily and rest my head on the chair saying nothing.
“How’s Lizzy?” Jshap asks in a low caring tone, “I can imagine she’s devastated at what happened to her friend.”
“Shouldn’t I be asking you,” I reply grumpily.
“What’s that supposed to mean?”
“You said you’re no longer into mystics, didn’t you?”
He drinks from the glass ignoring my question.
“How did you bring her back to life?” I pause, waiting for him to respond. But he remains silent. “By what power did you raise her to life if she actually died?”
“What you witnessed is called a miracle and not an art of magic. There is no magic spell that can bring the dead back to life, but Jesus, because He is the resurrection and the life. ”
“You’re a bloody liar! You put a spell on her now she’s avoiding me.”
“Hey! Don’t ever call me a liar okay,” he replies obviously trying to control his temper.
“Why don’t you go back to Uzii and leave her alone.”

Jshap comes over and sits down beside me in the sofa biting his lips. I can sense he is livid. Then he smothers his anger with a smile. “Let me make it very clear to you Ugo, I’m neither interested in dating Elizabeth nor your sister okay. And please will you stop nursing such defamatory opinion about me.”
From the queer smile on his face I suspect he knows something about Lizzy’s whereabouts.
“I’ve not seen her today…I think she’s avoiding me,” I say droopily.
“Is it true she’s dating Steel?” he asks.
“I know what you’re thinking.”

“No you don’t. There’s another version of the story that the Enclave didn’t tell, which I think you should be concerned about now rather than fantasizing about the girl,” he hesitates, “Did you fight with Steel at the party?”
“How did you know that?”
“Answer me first, did you and Steel fight?” he quizzes.
“It wasn’t meant to happen okay.”
He sips water again eyeing me curiously.

“He tried to prevent me from entering the hall yesterday when I went back to the hotel to intimate Emem of what happened to Lizzy, but when I eventually got in he stood in my way demanding to know her whereabouts…I got angry and hit him,” I explain.

He slowly keeps the glass on the table shaking his head. “The news is that you belong to the rival group that attacked Steel and his men and if you ask me Ugo, you have a double trouble. Not only is the security on your tail, your life will be in constant danger until Steel and his group retaliate.”
“That’s ridiculous,” I snigger, “You don’t believe that do you?”
“Well, I don’t, but that’s of no consequence now.” He drums his fingers on the table anxiously.

“It was only a coincidence that the lights went off as soon as I hit him. And before he could get to his feet, the bullets started coming,” I pause and breathe out heavily, “It’s incredible…how they made their target even though it was dark.”
Jshap mutters something subconsciously.
“What?”
“Nothing,” he shudders, “I was just thinking of something.”
“You said blind target. I heard it.”

“Oh, that – uh – I was just pondering over that sort of attack,” he flounders. He takes the glass unconsciously again to drink not realising it is empty. Then he gazes blankly at it for a while and keeps it back on the table. “You don’t have any idea the group that carried out the hit, do you?”

The memory of the attack flashes on my mind again. I recall what Paul said before I entered the hotel, ‘a bomb is about to detonate’. If I tell him about Paul he will likely persuade me to report the matter to the police, and that’ll start a chain of events that may land me in real trouble with these faceless guys. Paul is too dangerous. The police won’t get him and he’ll come after me.
“I don’t know anything else beside what I’ve just told you.”
“You know what my fear is Ugo…you may end up joining a cult group unless you do one thing,” he says in a flat tone.
I do not respond.
“Are you thinking of that?” he continues.
“No I’m not,” I reply curtly.
“You ever heard of the word, devil’s alternative?”
“Yeah, and I know what it means.”

“Your choices are bad, Ugo. They are definitely going to attack you,” he pauses and then continues, “If you ask me I’d say you forget about the girl and let this whole thing die out like a smouldering stick taken from fire.”
I laugh at the idea of leaving Lizzy. It’ll never happen.
“I see. Well, just before your infatuation leads you to do something stupid, none of you will have Elizabeth anyway. Neither you nor Steel, she’s in love with someone else.”

A flush of jealousy sweeps over me. So she finally opened up to Jshap, or is he insinuating that Lisa belongs to him or what? I glare at him. “Well, thanks for your information, but that was before she met me.”
“No. She met him yesterday,” he says smugly.
“So what are you feeling like?”
“It’s not me, she’s in love with Jesus, God’s Son. And neither you nor Steel can snatch her from His love.”
I burst into laughter. “Jesus died a long time ago. What do you think she’d be doing with a ghost anyway?”
“No, He’s alive Ugo…He was the one that raised Elizabeth,” he replies, “The same power that raised Jesus from the dead raised her back to life.”
“You don’t know her, Jshap. God won’t waste His time or energy on a sinner like Lizzy. She’s not that type okay, she’s just deceiving you.”
“No, you’re the one blinded by your lust from the truth.”

“Let me tell you this story about two men who owed a certain moneylender. One owed him five hundred Naira and another fifty thousand. Neither of them had the money to pay him back so he cancelled the debts of both. Which of them do you think will love him more, Ugo?”
“I suppose the one who had the bigger debt cancelled,” I answer.
He smiles patronizingly at me.
“People don’t change in the twinkling of an eye preacher man. Besides, Lisa is in love with me and nothing on earth can change that, okay.”
“You may not know this, but nine out of ten cult aggros are induced by a tussle for babes.
“What do you know about the cult, Jshap?”
He squints at me and shakes his head. “You can avert this impending danger and save your life, Ugo.”
“I won’t let that ass hole have Lisa okay! Forget it.”

“Circumstances beyond our control sometimes bring us to crossroads, a point in our life when God expects us to take a crucial decision that’ll determine the course of the rest of our life…and affect our future.”
“I’ve heard enough of your religious jargon. God does not tempt us with evil, I thought I heard you say that once?”
“Certainly not,” he replies.
“So what are you implying?”

“What I’m saying is, sometimes God allows things happen to us…things that He alone can solve. Then He stands aloof waiting for us to take the step of faith – the risk of entrusting our life to His care,” he says chuckling, “Temptations come knocking at the door of our heart when the basic instincts that led to the fall of our first father, Adam, induce our desire negatively. And when we become enticed and begin to dwell on such thoughts…there’s zero resistance to sin.”
“What instincts are you talking about?”

“Our inordinate crave for power, the obsession to dominate. Man’s natural insatiable sensual lusts, and lastly, the passion for recognition, in other words, the pride of life,” he pauses and takes a deep breath, “It was the impulse that led Cain to kill his only brother, Abel even though he was warned by God
I squint at him completely overwhelmed with guilt.

“A Jewish psychologist who survived the holocaust described the nature of evil as the lack of capacity to empathize with our neighbour. We all are born that way…I too was once such a man, Ugo. But for God’s mercy, I would have been dead. The wages of sin is death, but God in His infinite mercy sent His Son, Jesus as propitiation for my sin. And by faith in Him, by believing in Him, I am cleared from the guilt of my past sins.”
I shake my head disagreeably and remain silent.
“Jesus says, he who finds his life shall lose it, but he who loses his life for His sake shall find it. Do you know what that means?”
“That’s obviously a paradox.”

“The message of the cross is a mystery, to those who are perishing it is foolishness, but to them that believe, it is the power of God unto salvation. Anyone who accepts that Jesus Christ died for his sins and that He rose again on the third day is bound by that oath to have figuratively crucified himself with Christ. He will live no longer for his pleasure, but the Spirit of Christ enables him to live the life of God.”

I know exactly what he means by losing my life. Judging by Jshap’s way of life and his incessant criticism about my drinking habit, he’ll definitely expect me to give up drinking as well. I wonder what will happen if he gets to realize that I smoke gem. He’ll go off the roof. I can’t imagine life without Lisa – I’ll die.

“I know what you’re thinking Ugo. You see anyone who wants to be a disciple of Jesus must be willing to bear the cost of following him. There’s a price to pay in order to walk with Him, but there is great reward for those who are willing to pay that price. Those who despise their life in this world will keep it for eternal life when He comes in His Father’s glory. But those who love their life in this world have already lost it.”
The feeling of deep conviction from his words makes my heart thump as we stare at each other in the silence.
“I think you are at the crossroads of destiny Ugo…are you ready to surrender your life to Jesus, to accept Him as your Lord and personal Saviour?”

This is not the first time Jshap and I are discussing about this subject of the kingdom of God, but he’s never been so profound on the subject as he is today. And I’ve never been so impacted by his words as I am now. Besides, unlike previous times he has never sought my opinion about accepting Jesus as my Lord.
I gather myself, let out a deep breath and mutter, “I’ll think about it.”

“I am convinced that God allowed you to come to this point in order for you to take this crucial decision. Today as you hear His voice, do not harden your heart, Ugo,” he says in a low tone, takes the empty glass and stands up. “I hope you think about it.”
Jshap leaves me sitting on the couch, bemused.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-FIVE
content: He’s never told me the details of his past life or how he came to the decision to serve God, with such intensity, but I’m getting the picture that he practically dined with the devil before he repented. But for Lizzy, he almost succeeded in getting me converted…how can I leave Lizzy now…she’s so luscious a babe to let go of just like that…no…this is definitely not the right time for this.
I’m particularly disturbed about Jshap’s premonition that Steel will attack me. What if he’s right…what am I going to do? I ponder.

When Jshap was speaking about Cain and Abel, it reawakened the guilt feeling of Kelechi’s death I thought I had overcome. Does Jshap know about Kelechi…did Uzii tell him about it…but why would she tell a stranger about our family…if she did, I’ll deal with her…I’ll…God! I wish it never happened.
The harsh knock on the door startles me from my contemplation. I rally and go to answer it.
“Surprise, surprise,” Paul enthuses with a toothy smile.
“Holy Moses! How did you get my address?” I gasp.
“Small world, isn’t it?” he replies. He’s casually dressed in a thick black cotton short sleeve tee shirt, a light brown chinos trousers and a baseball cap.
“You look good man,” I compliment him. Then I fix my gaze on a dazzling metallic 5-series BMW parked on the street.
“Oh, I just bought that,” he says.
“You just – hey, what have you been up to?” I ask curiously.
“You busy? Come on follow me, there’s somebody I’d like you to meet.”
“You’ll buy me a drink uh, to wash the car.”
“Small.”
“Give me a minute okay.” Jshap is still in his room. I knock at his door and inform him that I’m leaving the house.

Paul used to be the oldest member of our gang, and also the most malevolent. He and I began to have problems, after the bus was recovered, when he and Ganza came up with the idea of robbing a bank. I opposed them and swayed the other members against their plan. They accused me of being inconsiderate and selfish, citing that my refusal was due to the fact that I came from a wealthy home and I was settled.

It was at that point that the Palm frond fell apart, we all parted ways in annoyance. Paul and Ganza vowed to become millionaires before their twentieth birthday. Neither of them is entitled to an inheritance of such value, nor does any possess the skills for those fast returns-yielding occupations such as acting, singing or even sports. And both are of very poor parents.
“So you made good your word at last,” I ask.
“You still remember that?
“Of course I do.
“Well, actually I failed by two years.”
“So what do you do?”
“Me. Well, uh, I do all kinds of runs.”
“Once a thief, always a thief, just as they say about the leopard, never sheds its spots,” I say jokingly.

“Are you speaking of yourself?” he says with laughter, “You know up till now, I’ve not been able to understand why you chose to throw oranges on the street with poor lads like us in spite of the fact you come from a wealthy home. But somehow I came to the conclusion that for some it’s beyond what is said about the leopard’s skin, it’s in the gene.”
“Hey, my father is a decent man.”
“I know…bad son of a good man,” he chuckles.
“Anyway I don’t do that anymore.”
“I die! Has school changed you that much?”
“So what else do you do apart from shooting people in the dark?”
He chuckles. “Well, I’m pretending to be reading history in the polytechnic and this is my second year at it. How about you?”
“I’m in chemistry, and I’m not pretending about it.” We laugh it off.
“You know, I never thought you’d still make it into the university.”
“What are you implying, that I’m a dullard?”
“Not that. I thought you’d join your father in business. So why haven’t you blended?”
“Blended,” I reply, “How are you so sure I’ve not?”
“Hard men are known by their fruit, my friend,” he says.
“If shedding blood is all you people do in the fraternity, count me out Paul,” I reply sarcastically.
“If you’re referring to what happened yesterday, we didn’t intend that anybody should die. It was only meant to be a message,” he replies.
“A message?”
“Yeah, a message. Sorry, you won’t understand these things if you’re not in the system.”
“Well, that was obviously a costly assumption, only I wonder why it had to be the chick and not Steel.”
“We don’t leave the blind target to chances. Apart from the girl’s death, the hit was successful…unless you’re judging it by your affair with the chick.”
“I wasn’t dating Emem.”
He glances at me. “You obviously have a lot of questions you want to ask. Meanwhile, how about your rival, the cripple, have you seen him lately?”
When our eyes meet, he winks his right eye at me. I shake my head and turn away.
“I think for now you should be concerned about him,” he says.
