The report sent to the school’s security department is that one Linus Kono, a student, was arrested and detained, and that he has admitted being responsible for the death of Obi Shina. And that further investigations are underway to track his accomplices in the crime, that’s Legion and me. There’s no Linus Kono, and nothing will be heard of him or his accomplices again, I know. We laugh it over.
“Sammy asked after you after the fellowship,” Legion tells me, “But I lied to him that I’ve not set my eyes on you,” he stops and stares at me.
“What?”
“Is it true what I hear about you being a spy, Hugo Boss?”
“What makes you think I’ll own up if I really am?” I ask chuckling.
“I think Sammy wants to see you. He told me in a hush he’s been to your room severally. I think he said something about slipping a note for you under your door.”
I nod my head without uttering a word. Apart from Sammy, no one else knows how that security report came about, not even Legion.
“I think it’s a set up Hugo Boss,” he says.
“It’s okay Legion. Sammy’s my friend.”

Sammy was in my room all right, and he did slip a note under the door the same way I did in his room when I informed him in advance of the outcome of the police case. It’s an invitation for me to meet him at Golden egg restaurant at seven p.m.

It’s twenty minutes past seven. And it is not in Sammy’s character to keep late appointments. I am tempted to lean towards Legion’s fear that he is setting me up. But I know Sammy won’t do that to me, not after shaking the police off his tail. I’ve been mulling over my discussion with Paul, and over my flagging affair with Lizzy.
“Hey what’s up?” Sammy greets me.
“Hi Sammy.”
“Sorry I kept you waiting,” he apologizes as he sits down.

“I had to branch off to the security department to clear a few things with them.” He shakes his head in disbelief of what happened. “How did you manage to get your parents involved in this, I mean how on earth did you convince them to meddle in a murder case?”
“Forget about it Sammy,” I smile smugly at him, “The important thing is that I kept my promise.”
“You’ve done more than that my friend,” he replies grinning, “Haven’t you ordered anything yet?”
“I didn’t feel like it,” I say coldly.
“Oh come on please. This calls for a celebration,” he beams and darts his eyes about for the waiter. “I know you like fiesta. Please get us two fiestas and uh – you care for a beer?”
“No, a soft drink will be okay.”
“And a packet of juice,” he instructs the waiter. I squint at him as he pays the waiter. “What’s the matter Hugo Boss?” he asks looking at me with a worried expression.
I wait for the waiter to leave us before replying, “I want you to get Steel to drop this stupid allegation.”
Sammy hesitates.
“You know it’s not true,” I continue
“I can’t Hugo Boss,” he replies droopily.
We break our conversation when the waiter returns with our order.
Did anybody report seeing me with Paul? Have they discovered Paul’s group? How on earth did Steel know about my mission? I ponder.

“Are you spying on us Hugo Boss?” he asks in a whisper as soon as the waiter leaves. He opens the packet of fruit juice on the table and pours it into the glasses. Then he raises his glass and toasts silently.
“Are you going to betray me with a meal like you did to Demola?”
Sammy freezes and lowers his glass.
“You know what they say about a dog. When you want to eliminate it, you give it a bad name and hang it. Wasn’t that the same reason he was poisoned…he was branded an infiltrator,” I add.
He gently keeps his glass on the table. “Who told you this?”

“I’m not stupid Sammy. You don’t think I’d receive that poison and carry out that task without wanting to know what he did, do you?” I lean forward to Sammy and tell him in a subdued tone, “I killed Demola because I believed in the inverted cross. If Steel thinks I’m a spy, let him come up with facts and let the disciplinary panel give me the opportunity to defend myself.”

Sammy cocks his head to the right and eyes me with askance speechlessly. My gaze drops to the glass of juice on the table. I sip from the glass and take a bite of the snack. “You have been my guardian angel Lethal weapon, can’t you counter his allegation on the basis of that relationship?”

“I’ve had reasons to suspect Steel’s decisions recently,” he takes a pause, “One of such decisions is your invitation to the inversion. The incident at Lizzy’s party is enough to disqualify you from the inversion, and I championed that opinion, but surprisingly Steel thought otherwise, that it’d be a good idea having you with us in spite of his suspicion that you are privy to the attack. For that reason, I personally requested to be your guardian angel.”
“Are you telling me you’re the one who suggested to the family that I’m a spy?”
“How did you know it was his watch that gave away his position?” Sammy quizzes.

I laugh at him. “Well, in that case you’ll be more than disappointed in your friend when you hear what I’m about to say. But,” I pause and take a sip of juice, “Let me ask you first, Sammy. Are you for me or are you for them?”
“Them?”
“Of course, you don’t know who really is controlling the affairs of the family and who Steel is really working for.”
“Don’t be ridiculous Hugo Boss,” he snickers, “Are you now turning around to accuse him of treason?”
“No I’m not. I’ll give you the privilege of discovering the truth yourself, Lethal weapon. Ask Vince about who Steel intends to ordain as the next Cardinal and why he’s postponing the handover?”
“This is ridiculous.”
“Mistletoe,” I whisper, “He’s the one taking over from Steel. Not you.”

Sammy squints at me apparently contemplating what I just said. Although Sammy is the most eligible disciple for the post, and everyone is expecting him to take over the office of the Cardinal from Steel, I was privy to his conversation with Mistletoe over the issue.
“Steel is rotten Lethal Weapon and he’s not your friend. His allegiance is to the Provost not us, Sammy. He doesn’t care about you or anyone else in the family.”
“I can’t believe you could be this petty,” he replies.

“Don’t think I’m trying to get back at Steel because of Lizzy. Vince is your friend, why don’t you go ahead and ask him. Have you bothered to find out why, in spite of being the oldest guy in the family, he’s never been the Cardinal?”
“He’s not qualified to head the group,” he says flatly.

“You’re not any more qualified than he is, and I’ll tell you why, because you’re not in their clique. And may I inform you, Lethal Weapon, he’s already got a good reason to disqualify you from heading the group.”

Steel took advantage of the recent attack that he led to discredit him and succeeded in poisoning the minds of the caucus against Sammy. I got to know all about Steel’s plan because I bugged his room when he was discussing with the other members of the precinct caucus. Vince was livid when I told him about the conversation between Steel and Mistletoe. He and three other satraps waylaid Mistletoe and interrogated him brutally until he admitted it. He was warned sternly not to tell Steel that he had revealed the plan to them, and if he did they threatened to kill him.

However, they didn’t know the reason why Steel is going against the fraternity’s code to ordain a disciple who is not a satrap as Cardinal, until I revealed to them Steel’s loyalty to the Provost. Vince and the other satraps then decided to topple Steel’s regime if only Sammy would collaborate with them, for it is said in the family that the fear of Lethal Weapon is the beginning of wisdom. They reckoned that Sammy is capable of foiling the coup. I assured them I’ll get Sammy to partner with us.
He wrinkles his nose in disgust and mutters, “I need some time to check out these facts okay.”
“And if they’re true?”
“Then I’ll figure out what to do,” he replies.

“You better start doing it in a hurry because the handover will take place in three days. I don’t bear a grudge because of Lizzy, but I’ve vowed to unseat Steel. If you check out the facts that I’ve laid before you, I pledge my support to help you with plans towards your installation as the next Cardinal.”
“You’re a pupa, Hugo Boss. What can you do about this?” he says derisively.
“Don’t underestimate me Lethal Weapon,” I reply firmly.

New precinct caucus are usually chosen secretly by the incumbent caucus and later presented to the house at the next fellowship of the spirit after that caucus meeting. They in turn choose the Satraps from among hard working disciples.

Although Sammy appears on the outside to be Steel’s loyalist, judging by his complaints about his letting out the family’s secrets to Lizzy, I know he has reservations about him. Steel has bruised his ego, and I know Sammy won’t take this lightly with him.

chapters.title: Chapter FORTY-TWO
content: Because in strict terms, going by the code of conducts, disciples of the inverted cross, particularly members of the precinct caucus are forbidden by the code from having intimate affairs with girls, it is taboo for two disciples to be in conflict over a ringo. But because nearly everyone is guilty of the offence of having a girlfriend, that transgression is treated with levity. And the case of disciples contesting for a ringo is unheard of.

Because the members of the caucus know about my fight with Steel over Lizzy at the party, Vince suggests I present that as the motive behind his allegation of my being a spy. However, he warns that if it boomerangs, the consequences could be severe.

The panel decries levelling such grave allegation against me on account of jealousy and the caucus, following the code, unanimously reach the decision to suspend him. But Sammy intercedes on his behalf. Being my guardian angel, he feigns to have misinformed Steel on the matter. After considering his explanation, it is struck off.

It’s all a set up. It was Vincent’s idea, for us to depose the caucus by force, to let Steel fight for it. That way the Provost will then know that a new regime has begun. Apart from drawing them into a battle, we figured the decision to suspend him would hamper the drug deal, which is almost materializing. As soon as the deal is closed, Sammy will take over the position of the Cardinal. He vowed to secede the Inverted Cross Fraternity from the GODs.

Vincent will be the Rainmaker. His long awaited desire to be a member of the precinct caucus will soon become a reality. I declined Sammy’s offer to step into his shoes as the Arrowhead. I have another agenda, which I have not shared with Sammy. I do not know yet how things are going to play out and how the plan will be hatched, but I’m glad that the pieces are falling in the right places.

Sammy told Steel about my efforts in the progress of the deal and formally got me included in the deal. Steel was contacted by the Shepherd of the fraternity and cross bearer of the GODs, a narcotics agent, to source for someone who would buy five kilograms of cocaine, part of a consignment seized by the drug law enforcement agents. He’s the Provost’s eye in the family and so I understand the reason for his closeness with Steel. But Sammy took charge of the negotiations after Steel was incapacitated.

According to him, Mr. Nosa, the Shepherd is representing some top-notch officers in the DEA to sell the drugs illegally. The act is called chop and clean mouth, a tradition in public service where officers make use of money or goods at their disposal, illegally and afterwards declare it missing.

“The talc is finally out of the store, and Nosa says he doesn’t have much time, he needs the cash on Thursday when he comes,” Sammy says, “Steel has contacted a certain friend of his who promised to finance the deal…the problem is, he’s asking for seventy percent of the stakes.”
“That’s ridiculous!” Vincent blasts.
“Why doesn’t Steel want us to negotiate with them?” I ask. “I don’t think they’re asking that much. I think he’s systematically putting clogs in the wheels to discourage us and divert the deal.”
Both of them eye each other.
“I think you’re right Hugo Boss…but what do we do?” Vincent asks.
“Well, at this rate we’re left with no other options if we must close this deal,” Sammy adds.
The option Sammy’s talking about is to go throwing oranges on the street, in other words, going for an armed robbery operation. Sammy has been hinting me about it, but I’m not up to it.

Besides, I made a vow to Jshap after he spoke to me in Kaduna that I won’t steal anymore. But there’s so much profit in this deal to let it slip by. Nosa is giving away the drugs for five hundred grand but from our studies, the street value is close to two point five million. And that’s worth the trouble of taking the option that has been coming to me since we started thinking of how to raise this money.
“I’ll make the dough available on Thursday,” I tell them.
Sammy and Vincent turn to me.
“You got to be kidding,” Vincent chuckles.
“How the hell are you planning to raise five hundred grand in four days?” Sammy adds.
“Let me bother about that,” I hesitate, “But, I’ll get five percent extra off you guys.”
“No. That is unacceptable,” Sammy barks.
“Hey Sammy come on don’t start being greedy. The guy says he’ll get the money, let him do it,” Vincent says, “I think it’s a better option.”
“We have only four days Hugo Boss, four days. You know how much time I’ve put into this, I don’t need –”
“Save your breath Lethal Weapon…the money will be ready next tomorrow, on Tuesday at five percent extra on all of you,” I reiterate.
“It’s a deal,” Vincent answers.

Sammy’s sceptical about my ability to raise the money, more so because I do not bother to tell any of them how I intend to go about it. And he’s not happy about the five percent I’m taking off his cut.

When everyone else has gone to bed, Uzii and I sit in the living room catching up on each other’s affairs. She came back home at the weekend to refuel, she ran out of money. I lie to her that I came back for the same reason also.

My mother has told Uzii about Lizzy already. She questions my intention to get married at such a tender age. There’s no need telling her about my problem with Lizzy. When she starts talking about Jshap, I suddenly feel like going to sleep and excuse myself leaving her in a sad mood.

Lying on the bed, I brood over our planned visit to the bank later today. I imagine that Legion is doing the same in the cheap hotel room where I lodged him adjacent the Havana Bank. It’s his first visit to Kaduna. Legion hasn’t shown any sign that he’s fretting about going to unlawfully withdraw six hundred thousand Naira without the consent of the account holder. He simply said, at the worst they’d refuse to pay him. But he could go to jail if the operation goes awry.

My father had given the bank manager a standing order not to pay cash withdrawals exceeding two hundred thousand Naira without his notification and consent on phone. But I remember once when a staff returned with a cheque of one million Naira due to the fact the bank manager was unable to reach my father to confirm the cheque, because the phone lines were not connecting. However, the cheque was eventually honoured when he returned to the bank with a letter of authorization written in my father’s private letterhead and signed by him proffering explanation that his office phone lines were faulty.

As soon as we arrived Kaduna, I went to the office and got a copy of the company’s identity card. I stole the accountant’s wallet from him, which bears his ID so as to make a similar copy for Legion. My father didn’t leave his office for a moment until we both left for the house so I couldn’t lay my hands on his chequebook and personal letterhead. But I’m sure I’ll get the opportunity this morning.

Uzii gives me a letter for Jshap as she’s leaving the house for the airport to catch the first flight going to Calabar. I promise her he’ll get it as soon as I get back to school. But as soon as she leaves my room, I shred it and put it in the trashcan.
When my father and I arrive the office, Chiadi Uba, one of my father’s young friends is already waiting at the reception. He waves him to come in as he strides into his office.

Chiadi is a stockbroker and a hardheaded one for that matter. Without wasting time, he presents his wares to my father with his usual expertise, always beginning with a nifty lecture on how important it is to invest in shares as if my father is a newcomer in the field of investments. Somehow, he always succeeds in making him buy more shares than he actually wanted to.

As soon as the deal is concluded, the conversation takes on politics, and economic matters – two subjects he particularly likes to discuss with Chiadi. Although he can’t afford to buy all journals every morning, he’s well informed on what the papers say, Chiadi. Giving his disposition as a graduate of political science and now in the financial sector, he has a hard-edged viewpoint about the country’s state of affairs.

After a long enthralling discussion, he suddenly remembers he has another appointment. He breaks the conversation and gathers his papers together to leave. My father is apparently unable to unplug himself from Chiadi’s analysis of the coming elections, and so he follows him out of the room to see him off.

Havana Bank is the principal banker of Amanze & Amanze group. I tear a leaf from their cheque booklet and take one sheet of my father’s personal letterhead, and put the cheque booklet back into the drawer. Then I append the company stamp on the cheque leaf and his personal stamp on the personal letterhead. After returning the stamps to the stamp rack, I tuck the cheque leaf and letterhead into the file I came with and sit still, waiting for my father.

Legion’s identity card as a staff of Amanze & Amanze group of companies bears the designation of an assistant accountant, and from the date of appointment that appears in the card, he has spent six months in the company.
First I sign the cheque and then the letter. “That’s exactly the way my father writes,” I say smugly. Then I fill in the amount to be paid on the line.
“What! six hun – what are you going to do with such an amount of money. That’s too much man.”
“It doesn’t matter whether it’s six thousand or six Naira we’re withdrawing. The same risk is involved so why don’t we just go for something big.”
“But this could make them want to alert your father,” Legion complains.
“This letter will take care of that,” I say waving the letterhead to him.

The letter is an instruction for the bank manager to pay the bearer who is a new staff in the accounts department and who has never been to the bank before. Because of an urgent need, he is to withdraw the cash in place of the accountant who is indisposed at the moment. The letter also intimates the manager that the office lines has been dysfunctional for some hours now, in case he tries to call and cannot reach him he is authorizing the prompt payment of the said amount to the bearer, Emeka Ifeajusi, the assistant accountant.
“Are you nervous?”

“Me, of course not,” he pauses, “Some risks are worth taking. Meanwhile, I’ve been watching the building since yesterday. Two sickly-looking policemen resumed duty this morning at seven. They are stationed at the gate and decorated with obsolete rifles, which I seriously doubt if they could harm a dog.”
We both burst into laughter.
“It’ll take a battalion of policemen to catch me if the alarm ticks off and I tear from the bank don’t worry about me,” Legion assures me.

Sitting at a pub opposite the bank, I watch Legion enter the bank’s building. After two minutes, I gulp down my drink and walk across the street into the building. None of the bank staff knows me as an Amanze anyway. The waiting chair is fully occupied so I join a few customers who are standing at a corner in the banking hall and cast my gaze on the cashier.

Legion goes to a counter and presents the cheque to a lady sitting inside one of the cubicles. She takes the letter and his ID card and bids him to wait. Leaving the cubicle with the cheque leaf and letter, she goes to a man at a desk in the open office area behind the cubicles. Legion turns around and signals me to keep an eye on the cashier. I move from the position where I’m standing to a point where I can peek at her movement.

She registers the cheque in a ledger at the table and goes with it to the manager’s office, I suppose. They’re going to try to reach my father on phone in spite of the cover letter, I know. But they won’t succeed.

Rather than outright disconnection of the phone lines from the mains, I switched off the ring volume of the central connecting telephone at the reception and my father’s direct line. Neither of them will be aware that calls are coming in, not until a caller comes over to the office probably, and complains why his or her call is not being answered. And that could take the whole day.

After about three minutes the cashier comes with a tall fair man wearing thick gold-framed lenses, who is now holding the cheque leaf and the letter. He points out something to Legion in the letter and after a brief dialogue he signs on the cheque leaf and hands it over to the cashier. Legion is asked to append his signature at the back of the cheque leaf.

He winks at me as he walks to a corner at the counter for the routine security photograph. Then the cashier packs bundles of Naira notes into a black cellophane bag and hands it over to Legion. I saunter to the door as he bends down to sign the cheque a second time.
Legion joins me in the taxi. He hands over the bag to me and we shake hands. I instruct the taxi driver to take us to the motor park.

chapters.title: Chapter FORTY-THREE
content: The rendezvous is the Palm Beach hotel. When I break the news that the money is ready, Sammy greets me with renewed respect but Steel’s dread for me is escalated. He gives me a cold handshake. Then we all sit down to discuss how to dispose the stuff and how to share the proceeds.

I remind them of my extra cut in the deal, and because I don’t trust Steel, I also add that the stuff will be in my custody pending when we make enough sales to pay off the money I invested. They have no qualms with my keeping the stuff until I get my money back. But Steel insists on arranging for another buyer and also suggests that I take twenty percent of the profit and leave Sammy and him to share the remaining eighty.
I disagree.

After several minutes of heated argument in the air-conditioned room and threats by Steel to call off the deal, Sammy finally brokers a truce between us – he will hold the drugs for a while until we come to terms on who will eventually buy it, after which he’ll hand it over to me pending when I receive full payment of the money I invested. This is to forestall the deal going awry after having come this far.
The family knock code sounds at the door, we brace ourselves and Steel opens the door to let Mr. Nosa Fregene in.
Nosa is in his mid-thirties and has the look of a regular cop, clean-shaven, and he’s also wearing a cold expression. He frowns at Steel. I know why, it’s because of my presence here.
“Never mind he’s one of us,” Steel explains.
I stand up and stretch my right hand looking sternly at him, “Shezou, eminent one.”
“Shezou,” he replies suspiciously and turns to Steel, “I didn’t know that’s what you meant. Sorry, Mr.?” he asks turning back to me.
“Hugo Boss.”
“Hugo Boss?”
“His name is Ugochukwu Amanze,” Steel chips in. “I believe you’ve met Sammy.”
“You guys don’t have an ivy code. In this instance I prefer your bush names to addressing you by your real names or nicknames,” Nosa explains, “For security reasons.”
“I’m Gehenna, he’s Black face,” Steel says pointing at me.
“Please don’t introduce me if you don’t know my inverted name,” I retort.
“What was that? Is that how you address your Cardinal?” Nosa berates me. “Will you apologize to him right away?”
“I said apologize to him, what’s this boy’s ivy?”
“Albatross,” Sammy answers.
“I’m sorry,” I say to Steel brazenly.
“Bloody hell,” Nosa exclaims and looks at his watch. “I don’t have the whole day so where’s the dough?”
“Where’s the stuff?” I ask curtly.

Nosa is obviously exasperated at my forwardness. Steel glares at me, stands up and whispers something in Nosa’s ears. He nods his head like a male lizard and brings out a finger-size transparent bottle containing white powder from his jacket.
“Here.” he croaks holding it up for me to see. Then he gives the container to Steel.
Steel opens it and takes a little quantity of the white powder. He put it in his mouth, nods and exclaims, “Hmm this is good.” Then he gives the bottle back to Nosa.
“Can I see the money now?” he says as he put back the bottle in his jacket.
“That’s not five kilos,” I say without hiding my suspicion.
He opens the bag he’s carrying and shows me a mass of white powder wrapped with transparent Cellophane. “So?”
“I need to taste it too,” I reply.
“Are you crazy?” Sammy whispers sharply.
“Shut up!” I bark at him.
Steel glares at me.
“I am a law enforcement agent, Albatross. I will not allow you snort this substance in my presence,” Nosa says with a gruff.
“Then I suggest you go look for somebody else to pay for the stuff. I don’t pay for what I’ve not proved,” I insist.
“Cocaine is very addictive, Albatross,” he continues in a hushed voice, “If you take a pinch of this stuff you will certainly end up a drug addict.”
“What am I paying for, a drug enlightenment lecture or what? Don’t waste your saliva Shepherd man, either you let me taste of it or you look for another buyer period.”
“Will you stop this madness, Albatross,” Sammy begs me.
“Stay out of this!” I warn him.

Nosa beckons on Steel. He whispers in his ears and Steel nods his head in agreement and returns to his seat. Then Nosa put his hand into his jacket, brings out the small bottle and hands it over to Steel. He eyes me and drops it on the table.

I take the bottle, pour some of its content in my palm and lick it. “It aint bad,” I quip, “Why is there so much fuss about cocaine?” Then I hand the bottle over to Nosa, but he refuses to take it from me so I keep it on the table. Steel takes it and hands it over to him.
They all conspire to remain silent.
“Now where’s the money?” Nosa asks impatiently.
I pretend not to hear him.
“Albatross!” Steel beckons me.
“I heard him,” I say in a low tone. I slowly lean forward towards Nosa smiling lightly in anticipation of their reaction at what I’m about to say. “There’s one more thing eminent one.”
Steel and Sammy cast anticipatory anxious gazes at me. Nosa frowns at me with obvious disinterest in whatever it is I have to say.
“I have six hundred grand in the trunk of my car. I’ll pay five hundred grand for the stuff and another hundred for the hammer and the inverted cross brooch.”
My words hit them like a bolt from the blue. Nosa scowls at me. Sammy turns and glances at Steel in bewilderment. Steel squints angrily at me biting his lips.
“Nonsense!” Nosa exclaims hitting his hand on the table. He turns to Steel. “Hey Gehenna is this supposed to be a joke or what?”
“No it’s not Shepherd man. I’ll tell you what it is, payback time. This is for a friend, Demola, you remember him don’t you?” I hesitate, “Of course you do. You assholes used me to eliminate him.”

Demola used to be a drug courier for the Provost until he was arrested by the police. In order to conceal the deal going on between the Provost and Steel, they plotted his elimination and told the family he was an infiltrator. Even Sammy doesn’t know the real reason why Demola had to die.
Nosa’s lip drops, he turns to Steel, his eyes drops to the floor. I light a cigarette and puff smoke noisily from my mouth.
“Listen Albatross,” Steel mutters.
“Shut the hell up! And stay out of this okay!” I yell at him. “Are you playing ball or not?”
“I won’t do it,” Nosa says flatly.

“Don’t dare me Shepherd man. You don’t know what I’m capable of,” I say with a brassy attitude, “I’ll make it easy for you. You nominate me as the next Hangman and the Order doesn’t get to hear the truth about Demola. But if you deny me…I’ll start with confessing openly about our little transaction here and I’m sure you’re aware of government’s recent efforts to eradicate corruption and corrupt officers like you from public service.”
“Are you threatening me?” Nosa quavers.

“Of course not. I’m giving you a picture of the future. This room is bugged. You’re looking at a very, very long jail term Shepherd man, and by the time you’re through, if you survive, you’ll be a worthless old man…think about it Shepherd man. I’m making you an honest offer. Are you accepting the hundred grand or not?”
“Well, nobody will believe you.” He picks up his bag and stands up to leave.

“You were lousy to have disclosed your rendezvous two days earlier. Somebody is already listening and recording this conversation on tape and I intend to pass it on, first to Barrister Ilaje, then to your office and then to the police.

Nosa freezes and gapes at Steel. He drops his head to the floor. Sammy taps Steel on his shoulder and whispers something to him. He knows I’m capable of bugging the room following the dust I raised about the handover issue, which has not yet settled. But it’s only a threat.
Steel calls Nosa’s attention and whispers to him. Sammy shakes his head in bewilderment.
“What you are asking for is impossible,” Nosa says calmly.
“Everything is possible Shepherd man, to them that believe,” I say with a short smile.
“I am highly disappointed in you,” Nosa tells Steel in annoyance and turns to me. “I’ll let you know when the Passover will take place.”
Steel and Sammy stare at each other in dismay.

I have waited long for today, the day of the Passover. Today, I’ll be decorated with the prestigious inverted cross brooch and receive the hammer, to become the Hangman. What an awesome privilege it is to step into the shoes of the Seagull, the mystic man.

I fold the tip of a crisp twenty Naira note to make a triangle and roll it into a pipe leaving both ends open. Then I remove the silver foil from a cigarette pack on the table and fold it in the middle. With my thumb and index finger I take a pinch of white powder and sprinkle it into the straight line in the middle of the foil and hold the pipe between my lips. Then I pass the foil over a burning candle and suck the oil coming from the burning powder with the pipe. I lean back into the sofa grinning and laughing to myself.

Awakened by heavy banging on the door at fifteen minutes past five o’clock in the evening with the realization that the effect of the drug had knocked me off to unconsciousness, I clear the table and reel to the door to see who is knocking.
“Hello Ugo.”
“Hi Jshap,” I reply.
“May I come in?”
“Yeah sure.” I let him pass.
He looks around the living room. “Nice place you have here.”
“Thanks, sit down. Do you care for a drink?”
“No please don’t bother.”
I sit next to him on the sofa. We stare at each other silently for a while. I can’t figure out what’s on Jshap’s mind, but he’s down in the dumps. “So what’s about this surprise visit?”
“Your mother came looking for you.”
“When?”
“She left about an hour ago. But she’s still in town. I told her to give me some time to find out your new address, she’ll be coming back this evening.”
“That’s very thoughtful of you. But I won’t be able to see her.”
“Why?” he quizzes.
“Well, let’s say I’m already occupied this evening. Tell her to come tomorrow okay.”
“Are you crazy Ugo? This is the second time you’re doing this. The first time it was because of your grouse over a girl, now what’s it this time, have you lost your senses?”
“Okay, okay. I know where she’s putting up. I’ll go and see her first thing tomorrow morning, how about that?”
“May God help you out of this,” he says as he stands up to leave.
“Amen.”

The temple of the GODs is a storey building painted white. There is a symbol of an inverted cross in a star and the letters G.O.D on the gigantic gate that leads into the building. The gate is locked and two security men are manning it. One of them attends to me at the gate. He double-checks my name in a sheet of paper he’s holding to be sure it’s on the list, then he let me in. The other leads me to the entrance door into the house, which also has similar symbols I saw on the gate. He rattles on the door, and it is opened.

Surprisingly, there are over fifty persons already quietly seated in a curved raised area near the walls facing a dais that looks like a judge’s seat in a conventional court. Three chairs are positioned on the stage behind a marble lectern. Those sitting towards my right are older and more socialized, they chatter among themselves, but fewer and younger faces are directly facing them on the other side – these eighteen Cardinals squint at me as I stride to take my seat without an exchange of greetings. When I get to the chair reserved for me next to Sammy and sit down, I drop my face to the floor, away from the probing eyes of the older men facing us. They must have heard about me.

Barrister Ilaje and two others, all dressed in red gowns, march in to occupy their seats at the dais. We all rise to usher them in and bow our heads as they sit down. One of the two men with him is the Provost. I distinguish him by the staff he’s bearing – a long slender rod that looks like that of a Bishop. But the cross on its tip is inverted.

Nosa stands up and strides to the lectern. “On behalf of the Provost, the chief convener of this convocation, the pallbearer and the macebearer,” Nosa’s voice blares out of the loudspeakers, “I welcome all eminent dukes here present and our future leaders as we call them, the Cardinals, to the first convocation of Passover of the Grand Order of Dukes. I hereby call on the eighteen Cardinals present at this occasion to introduce themselves one after the other starting from my left.”

The Cardinals are acknowledged with applause as they announce their names, their ivy, their discipline and year of study and the mountain they represent. Then the older members also introduce themselves stating their names, inverted name, their discipline and year of graduation and the mountain where they worshipped, which means the schools from which they graduated.

Although only Cardinals qualify automatically as members of the GODs, some other dukes who were not Cardinals during their time in school are chosen on the basis of their dedication to the fraternity while they were students. But the Hangman has a permanent seat among the GODs as the star bearer. Nosa is the cross bearer and moderator at every meeting of the order, I gather.

“The Inverted Cross Fraternity is undisputedly the most powerful cult group in the eighteen campuses where we have our mountains,” he continues, “In view of the challenges that face the existence of cult groups and the need for us to extend our operations, the review of section seven of our constitution as per the ordination of a Hangman became imperative, hence our gathering tonight for this first convocation of Passover,” he takes a pause, “In accordance with the proceedings of the convocation contained in article five section seven of our constitution, I hereby present to the house the Provost of the Grand Order of Dukes…the Provost.”

He is applauded with a standing ovation as he walks to the lectern wearing a charming smile. He waves everybody to silence. “As you already know I am Dike Obiajulu esquire, I am the Provost of the Grand Order of Dukes, and I am also known as Amphibious. You are all welcome to this sacred convocation, the feast of Passover,” he pauses for the applause and clapping of hands to stop. “Thank you very much, or should I say Shezou,” he beams.
“Shezou! Shezou!!” the Cardinals chorus cheerfully and clap their hands.
“That is to formally welcome and recognize our Cardinals who are doing a great job out there in the universities and polytechnics. Once again I say shezou Cardinals,” he continues.
“Shezou Provost!” we all chorus.

“Nearly a decade ago, I saw the need to preserve the fundamental ideals of the Order by instilling our values in a certain class of young people, for posterity’s sake. I shared the vision of establishing a fraternity of distinguished undergraduates with two eminent dukes in our midst today. The vision of that fraternity was to provide these young people with everything they need to realize their dream in life and attain lofty heights in every sphere of life,” he pauses, “Gentlemen please join me in welcoming these eminent dukes who have been with me right from the inception, Duke Wilfred Enedo, to my right and Duke Babatunde Ilaje.”
The house applauds them as they stand up for recognition.
“Duke Enedo is the police commissioner of Bauchi state. He has come all the way from Bauchi state to join us tonight. His inverted name is Hurricane, and he is the macebearer of the order.”
The house applauds him again.

“Next to the macebearer is the pallbearer, and his inverted name is Octopus. He is a Senior Advocate of Nigeria, gentlemen please join me in welcoming a friend and learned colleague, Duke Babatunde Ilaje.”
Chief Ilaje stands up for recognition and waves to the house.

“Permit me to say…especially to the Cardinals that the men you see here today are not the entire members of this Order since this convocation was only summoned for the ordination of a new Hangman. Speaking about the post of the Hangman, or the star bearer as he is known in the Order, we saw the need, if the vision of the Order is to be properly imbibed in the fraternity, to create an office among us for an undergraduate member that will, under the supervision of the Order, oversee the affairs of the fraternity. That office became the star bearer or what is known among our student members as the Hangman.

“At that time we had only one mountain, the mount Horeb, the Metropolitan University. But we foresaw that in the future, which is now, that young men would embrace our ideology and become enlightened to the freedom and infinite opportunities our fraternity offers as they look to the future. Today, our first Hangman is no more and the office of the star bearer has remained vacant for nearly half a decade. The vacuum he left has no doubt affected not only our growth, but also resulted in a watering down of the fundamental beliefs of the order as it is transmitted from one generation of disciples to the next.

“We, the precinct caucus after duly evaluating the situation resolved to amend our constitution with respect to the ordination of Hangman to effect a Passover. Upon the advice of the Shepherd we resolved to ordain a Hangman to preserve our belief, not just a Hangman, but one that will be imbued with the doctrine of the Order, grow with it, and fearlessly infuse that doctrine in every disciple of the Inverted Cross Fraternity.”

My heart skips at the strength of the Provost’s words. I peer at Barrister Ilaje. When our eyes meet, he gives me a short smile. I drop my face to the ground. On the strength of what happened between Nosa and I (although I didn’t tell Barrister Ilaje what transpired), I convinced him that this was the right time to appoint a new Hangman, and urged him to compel the Provost to call for this ordination. Hence, Nosa was asked, being the Shepherd of the fraternity to scout for a suitable replacement for the former Hangman.
The Provost concludes his opening remarks and he’s given a standing ovation as he returns to his seat. Nosa returns to the lectern and eulogizes him.

“This brings us to the highpoint of the occasion tonight, the first Passover which is thus interpreted, the ordination of the second Hangman of the Inverted Cross Fraternity and induction of the star bearer of the Grand Order of Dukes. I crave the permission of the Provost to proceed with the ordination, your Excellency sir,” Nosa says turning to the Provost.
The Provost is startled from his chat with Barrister Ilaje, “Please proceed,” he responds.
“With the permission of the Provost, I wish to introduce to the house the chosen one, the next Hangman of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. Please join me as I welcome Mr. Ugochukwu Amanze.”

Sammy folds his arms as the Cardinals applaud me with clapping, shouting and a standing ovation. He reluctantly joins them clapping his hands slowly. I stand on my feet and walk up to Nosa with self-assured gait. He tells me to introduce myself to the house stating my real names, my inverted name, discipline and year of study, in addition my aims as the new Hangman.

After, he leads me to a table having the insignia of the office of the Hangman. Nosa wears the hammer’s sheath on me. He brings a long black trench coat and holding it behind me, he guides me as I put my hands into the sleeves. Then he put a black beanie with the sign of an inverted cross on my head – called the turban. “The unshed finger of the Sea gull,” he says aloud as he fastens a black leather armband with a bronze nail around my left arm. He turns to where the Cardinals are sitting and announces, “Who is the Cardinal representing Mount Horeb?”

Sammy responds and comes to Nosa. He opens a black box with the inscription, the seal of the Hangman – order of the star bearer. There are two brooches, a star brooch and the inverted cross brooch set in a wooden case and an empty rectangular space in between the brooches that accommodates the black book, I suppose.
“Decorate him with the seal of the Hangman, the inverted cross brooch,” he instructs Sammy.
“Excuse me,” Barrister Ilaje barks from the dais, “Are we not forgetting something?”
“I beg your pardon, Duke Ilaje,” Nosa replies.
“Don’t beg my pardon,” Ilaje responds, “The Hangman ought to be sworn in by the testament before being decorated. Is the Provost aware of this oversight?”
“It’s not an oversight Duke Ilaje. The amended section of the constitution accommodates the changes in the procedure for ordination of the Hangman –”

“Let’s not get into an argument about interpretation of laws and amendment,” he snaps angrily, “The testament of the Order clearly states that in the event of transgression, which may lead to the inability of the incumbent Hangman to completely shed his fingers, he shall by obligation take the devil’s alternative in fulfilment of the law of the Passover and where these are not obtainable, his office shall remain vacant until the confirmation of his death. Now, the amended article,” he continues waving a sheet of paper, “clearly states that this condition has been suspended, or in other words rendered null and void by the precinct caucus for obvious reasons. That is understandable. But the decoration of a Hangman without proper swearing in or not being sworn in at all is a serious transgression of the council’s constitution.”
Bamboozled by Chief Ilaje’s words, Nosa gapes at the Provost to rescue him.
“It’s okay cross bearer,” the Provost breaks in, “Can we talk this over privately, Octopus?”

The proceeding halts and silence beset the room as Chief Babatunde and the Provost excuse themselves and leave the room. Nosa shakes his head and keeps the black box back on the table. When our eyes meet, he blushes with guilt. Sammy returns to his seat.
When Chief Ilaje and the Provost return to their seats, they beckon Nosa to come to them. After whispering to him, he comes back and apologizes on behalf of the precinct caucus.

“Taking cognisance of the unavailability of the irrevocable testament of the Inverted Cross Fraternity, Mr. Ugochukwu Amanze inverted name, Albatross, shall be accorded provisional rights by the council as the second Hangman of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. In accordance with the Passover statutes, article ten amended section three, he shall therefore be decorated with the inverted cross brooch being the seal of the Hangman while the council withholds the constitutional rights of the star bearer pending the complete shedding of the fingers of his predecessor.”
Nosa calls Sammy back to affix the brooch on me. I receive applause from the house as Sammy put the brooch on the trench coat.
“Please come with me Hangman.” Nosa leads me to another table as Sammy returns to his seat.

He asks me to sit down at the table that has a big book on it with the inscription, the Grand Order of Dukes. Nosa opens the book to a page with the title, the order of the star bearer. He gives me a pen and points at the space under a name previously written in the page.
“Your name and signature Hangman,” he whispers.
I take the pen from Nosa and lean forward to write, but the revelatory awareness of the identity of the name on the page shocks me. I hesitate.
“What’s the matter?” Nosa whispers.

As soon as I look up at the dais, I meet Barrister Ilaje squinting at me. Dike looks distantly ahead of him, either he’s lost in thought or he’s absent to what is happening. I lift up my head to Nosa again.
“Write your name and sign,” Nosa repeats.
I shut my eyes to the page and whisper, “I can’t…please.”
“You must,” Nosa tells me with a tone of finality.
I raise my head to the dais again. Chief Ilaje is wearing a wicked smile on his face nodding his head lightly.
“It’s too late you can’t back out now,” Nosa tells me.

My heart misses a beat as I turn back to the page and stare at the name Ikenna Obiajulu again. Casting back my mind to the things Jehoshaphat has in common with him. First, he’s also a mystic, then I recall my conversation with Jshap, when he mentioned the blind target. Finally, he was also at Metropolitan University. Couldn’t this be the same person I know as Jehoshaphat Obiajulu?

I lean forward to the table and write my name and append my signature a space beneath his. Nosa then pierces my thumb with a pin. He asks me to smear the blood to cover my thumb and print it in a box below Jehoshaphat’s thumbprint.

chapters.title: Chapter FORTY-FOUR
content: The pomp and circumstance of the feast of the Passover is over, so is the euphoria of attaining the supreme and enviable position that has eluded many disciples, that has fizzled out to the thought of the enormous responsibility I’m saddled with – to find the Hangman in diaspora and make him fully shed his fingers or else I cannot receive the full honour of a Hangman, and as stated on my ordination, I will never be star bearer until my predecessor fully sheds his fingers.

To fully shed his fingers, I will have to cut off the thumb of the Hangman, if I find him and thumbprint it in his own blood next to the print in the annals of the star bearer. The thumbprint must match that of Ikenna Obiajulu, the Seagull, which appears in the book. Alternatively, where I cannot find him, I must adduce to the Order a proof that the Hangman is dead before I will be accorded the rights of the star bearer. In other words, the responsibility has fallen on me to solve a decade of mystery surrounding the disappearance of the Hangman. For that matter, I no longer find pleasure in being Hangman or star bearer, or associating with this group anymore.
“The oracle didn’t lie after all,” Barrister Ilaje says excitedly. “After our first meeting, I went back to inquire about you, Albatross. Guess what the Oracle told me?” he asks rhetorically.
I remain silent.
“He mentioned your name as the one who would lead me to the book.”
“He’s a liar,” I reply.

“Well, if a prophet didn’t lie in the first instance, it is not reasonable to doubt his next prediction. I do not think that is sensible. I almost doubted him when he said the testament was here with us, but I now know better…you know who the Hangman is, don’t you?”
“I beg your pardon chief. I don’t know what you’re talking about.”
“Don’t beg my pardon young man, and don’t play games with me. You’ve met him, haven’t you?” he coaxes me.
“Met who?”
“Why did you hesitate to sign the book?”
“The deal we had is to get you the book, isn’t it?” I hesitate, “Honestly, I don’t wish to continue with this.”

“Well, maybe you didn’t realise it my friend, I’m not the only one that knows you have the hint about the Hangman’s whereabouts. The Provost was watching you also and you know what he’s going to do?” he pauses and stares at me, “I’ll leave that to your imagination.”
I hold myself from replying and stand up to leave.
“You’re not going to survive this Ugo,” he says shaking his head. “Work with us for God’s sake, and let me protect you.”
“Then what happens to him?”
He remains silent.
“Between us Chief, what chance does he have to survive if he agrees to hand over the book?”

“The real issue is, whether you have the chance of survival if you fail to fulfil the requirement of the law.” He shakes his head. “The Provost has hidden him from us all these years. You’ll be risking your life getting in his way. He will eliminate you, if necessary to make sure he continues to protect him.”
“Let me bother about my life…I’ll trade the book for his life.”

“And your life for his…then we’ll lose everything. Whatever you choose Ugo, a life must be lost, and whatever you choose the Provost or I will lose something…that’s the situation. Are you willing to make that sacrifice in order to hide him? Don’t you realise that it is better for us that one man dies than that the future of the Order be jeopardized?”
“You want me to betray him to you…I won’t…I can’t do it, he’s my friend…my teacher.”
“Then get somebody to do it. I can pay you upfront,” Ilaje coaxes.
I mull over his proposal for a while. “How much are you willing to pay?”
“I’ll send a hundred thousand to you through Paul.”
“Three hundred. I’ll come for it tomorrow morning.”
“Deal,” he beams.
We shake hands in agreement, and then I leave.

Today is Lizzy’s birthday. Although she’s not expecting me, I’ve made up my mind to pay her a surprise visit, and I’ve bought her an unexpected gift – a beautiful dinner dress and pair of shoes and handbag to match. Plus a set of jewelleries and a gold wrist watch, and if that is not enough, I’ll buy her whatever she wishes for a birthday present.
Murphy tells me she left with a stranger, and that Steel and two other boys have been here to check on her.

She once hinted being in love with someone else. I’ve not seen her with anyone else, and I’ve not mustered the courage or patience to ask her who this person is, but I am aware her relationship with Steel packed up immediately after the hit. And it is not likely she’s dating Jshap. No. He’ll bore her ass out. I don’t give a damn who the hell she’s in love with. I know she loves dresses and trinkets. She won’t refuse me now. I cleaned up big time with the deal, and it’s earned me a new reputation in school.

Although I’ve been playing around with a lot of girls who flirt around me because of my new level, I have given my heart to Lizzy and I’ve promised myself that if Lizzy agrees to go out with me tonight, I’ll ditch all those bitches and stick to her. If everything had gone according to plan, she would have been celebrating her birthday on our vacation in the States.

Murphy waves me to halt as I swerve into her Close when I return to check on her again. He nervously tells me Lizzy’s back with the fellow she went out with, and that there’s a fracas between the man and Steel over Lizzy.

As I walk towards Lizzy’s apartment I hear her voice shouting at them to stop beating him, but none of them would listen. They push him down, punch and kick him ruthlessly in spite of her pleading. She tries to hold Legion from hitting him with a hockey stick, but he pushes her away forcefully and charges at him. Lizzy falls down and turns her face away from watching, and when he starts pounding him with the stick, she breaks down weeping aloud and calling for someone to help.

The voice I hear groaning in their midst sounds familiar. When I come closer, I realize it’s Jehoshaphat. Filled with rage at their brutality, I charge at them and order the beating to stop. First, I take on Steel. I pull him away and land him a blow on his cheek. He staggers and falls to the ground. Legion and the other two disciples stop beating him. They stand beside him panting and glaring at me.

“Will you throw that thing away?” I bark angrily at Legion. He’s still holding the stick in his hand. “What is the meaning of this?” I say turning to Steel. Steel gathers himself and rises from the ground frowning at me. Legion and the other two disciples follow him to a Volkswagen Beetle parked near the building and the car rattles away.

Then I bend down to attend to Jshap. His lips are torn and saliva mingled with blood drools at his mouth. His eyes are almost closing from bruises and his face is black-and-blue. “I’m sorry about this Jshap,” I tremble.
He let his hand drop and he stretches his hand reaching for his gold chain with a cross pendant. I take it and give it to him.
“Thank you,” he mutters.
“I’m sorry Jshap,” I sob.
“You should be more sorry for yourself, you murderer! As if you don’t know about this you bloody liar!” Lizzy says as she fights back tears.
“It’s okay Lizzy,” he says in a low tone of voice.

Following the direction his eyes are focused, I realise he’s gazing at my chest – the silver chain with an inverted cross pendant. He raises his hand slowly and touches it. From a slit in his bulging eyes he peers at me.
“Dear Lord, do not hold this sin against them,” he mutters as he looks up to the sky. Then he turns to me and whispers, “I’m sorry…I made you.”
I slowly let his head drop to the ground. Lizzy comes to him weeping and cursing me. I stand up and slowly turn around and toddle to the car.

My passion for the mysterious book has now been replaced by deep fear, the fear of realizing that the one who stands between me and my object of desire is the very one that once was the custodian of the testament…the one who in his own words made me what I am, I contemplate. I’m also startled by the tone of liberty in his voice despite being fully aware of what awaits him if I decide to fulfil the requirement of the law. Is he confiding in me to protect him?
“Murderer,” she growls at me as I walk to the car.

Suddenly a feeling of jealousy sweeps over me. “Bloody hypocrite,” I curse him under my breath, “He’s definitely cast a spell on her…I won’t let you have her,” I mutter, “I’ll make him surrender the book. I’ll take everything.”

He once hinted about a certain mysterious book when I first met him, but when he started being worried about my obsession, he confessed that it possessed immensely the power to destroy a man’s soul. Considering his disposition to his professed faith, he may choose rather to lay down his life than reveal where he hid the book let alone giving it to me. The words he spoke the very first day we met comes to my mind. “You can make things happen the way you choose…and be the head at all times,” I whisper.

Legion is having dinner and invites me to join him, but I decline and tease him about eating dinner so early in the evening. I brief him of the purpose of my visit as he eats and also tell him that I had been to Sammy’s house, but he’s not there. Legion says he’ll likely be in church and obliges to escort me.

The church is a stone throw from Sammy’s house. We waddle to the cathedral as soon as we alight from the car. Legion and I halt at the door. He makes the sign of the crucifix, although I do not feel like it, I imitate him. He whispers to me to wait outside while he goes to fetch him and ambles into the massive auditorium.

Today is Holy Thursday, the commemoration service of the Last Supper Jesus had with his disciples before His crucifixion. It takes me back to the yesterdays when my parents literally drag me and Uzii to church. It’s one of the very few days when my father comes home early from work and he usually doesn’t resume work until after Easter. Uzii usually comes home from school to spend the Easter break. I know she’ll be at home now.

Legion strolls down the aisle looking to his left and to his right in search of Sammy, unnoticed by worshippers in St. Peter’s cathedral soberly listening to the priest standing in a box-like pulpit, dressed in the traditional white cassock with a red sash hung on his neck. His fruity voice blares from the loudspeakers, “…My son if sinners entice you, do not give in to them. If they say to you ‘come along with us let us lie in wait for someone’s blood, let us waylay some harmless soul. Let us swallow them alive, like grave and whole like those who go down to the pit. We will get all sorts of valuable things and fill our houses with plunder. Throw in your lot with us, and we will share a common purse’. My son do not go along with them, do not set foot on their path for their feet rush to sin, they are swift to shed blood…”
Legion eventually sights Sammy, beckons him to come outside and then turns around towards the exit. Sammy stands up and follows him.

“…surely in vain the net is spread in the sight of any bird. And they lay wait for their own blood, they lurk secretly for their own lives. So is the way of anyone that is greedy of gain, which takes away the life of its owner…” the preacher’s voice fades as we leave the cathedral.

Wearied from staying awake most of the night thinking of this responsibility thrust on me, I ask Sammy to drive to my house so we can talk. We drop Legion off on the way, and then Sammy and I head home.
“You’ve not been yourself lately. What’s the matter with you?” he says taking a short glance at me.
I remain silent.
“I’m still your guardian angel,” he chuckles, “Come on tell me what’s bothering you?”
“I found the Hangman in diaspora,” I say frostily.
“You what!”
“You heard me right Sam…he’s one of us.”
“Of course I know he’s one of us. Did you actually meet him? Did you talk to him?” Sammy asks with agitation.
“What I mean is, he’s a student…he’s among us.”
“Wait a minute…are you saying that this guy you suppose to be the Hangman is right here on campus?”
“Yes. And he’s in his third year.”
“How come no one has identified him all this while?”

“He was admitted into the university with a different name…he changed his identity. Besides, the Provost has been protecting him from being punished by the GODs,” I take a pause, “He was succeeding until that moment when I was called to sign the star bearers’ annals. I betrayed myself by my expression.”
“But, you said he changed his name, how are you sure this guy is the same person?” Sammy quizzes.
“It’s a long story Lethal Weapon. He’s a crook, and a very smart one,” I whisper, “I want to protect him from shedding his finger…I want to protect him from Barrister Ilaje.”
“Barrister Ilaje is a desperate man, Hugo Boss. I won’t advise you to do that,” Sammy replies.
“I know what Barrister Ilaje wants…the testament. He’d turn his eyes away from him as soon as it is handed over to him,” I explain.
“And you think he’d be willing to give it to you?”
“That’s the problem Sammy. He’s now a disciple of Jesus Christ…I need your help Sammy. I want you and Legion to escort me to his house…to do the blind target?”
“That’s impossible. No one has ever tried the blind target after him,” he argues.
“I know how,” I mutter.
“You!” he gasps, “I don’t believe you.”
“There’s only one way to prove it.”
Sammy considers the proposal for a while. “I sure would like to see you do that.”
“So what are you saying?”
“How soon are you planning the visit?”
“Tomorrow night nine p.m.”

Because of the reverence accorded to the Hangman in spite of his absence, there is a general impression among members of the family that there’s a jinx on the throne of the Hangman. This idea is imbibed into every disciple right from the point of entry, a notion handed down from one generation of the family to the next.

No one really stays long enough in the system to see how such traditions and legends shape the cult system and how the myths we received from our predecessors affect us. But I’ve come to realize that in addition to the irrevocable testament, which although none of us had seen, but we strictly adhere to, the mystery that enshrouds the throne of the Hangman is what makes the Inverted Cross Fraternity exclusive among other confraternities, because mystery begets curiosity and what makes people ponder is a wonder. I got that from Jehoshaphat…Iyke Obiajulu.

This legend, I was told, was established by the one who succeeded the Seagull, Bloody Sniper. He abdicated the post and returned the Hangman’s staff, but not the testament. Why hasn’t anyone mentioned anything about him or wondered why he didn’t return the testament to the GODs? What if he’s actually the one who has the book?

A loud sound of the blare of horn jerks me from my thoughts. Jolting towards the wheel, I swerve the car away from an oncoming truck and veer off into the bush. We rattle and joggle through shrubs until Sammy slams on the brakes.
“What the hell was that?” I bark at him.
“I lost my sight, Hugo Boss,” he replies.
“I can imagine,” I retort.
“I’ve gone blind,” he mutters.
“What the hell do you mean you’re blind?”
Sammy stares distantly ahead of him, without saying a word without blinking. I wave my hand across his face, but he doesn’t blink.

books.title: Merchants of the Ivory Towers
books.author: Bloody Sniper
books.blurb: Sammy’s exile exposes a hidden network where politicians, patrons, and power brokers harvest young dreams.
books.summary:
He laid the Cross upright on the grave of the man who founded the fraternity, and by that single act, Sammy Briggs became both a truce breaker and a marked man.

Once ordained as Lethal Weapon, the feared Cardinal of the Inverted Cross Fraternity, he had lived by violence, loyalty, and the illusion of power. But when he rejected the political machine of the GODs and the fraternity’s blood economy, and chose the Crucified One over the gods of the Order, the family that once protected him became the darkness hunting him.

To escape the penalty of his defection, he fled into exile. Stripped of rank, money, protection, and identity, Sammy discovered that the gifts he had once used to mentor boys into darkness could be redeemed to guide wounded young people toward purpose.

And though the GODs came for him again, Sammy Briggs had found the one thing stronger than fear — purpose.

chapters.title: Chapter ONE
content: Being the heir apparent to my father’s throne and still single at the age of twenty-nine has given my father grave concern about upholding the family legacy.

When I visited Nigeria the last time, my father arranged for me to consult a medium, in order to find out the cause of my delayed marriage. The first mystic said the gods of our ancestors revealed to him in a vision that my wife would come from afar, across the great seas. We all understood he spoke figuratively that my wife would either be a foreigner or at least a native who lived in a foreign land. The second option is more likely because, customarily, it is a taboo for anyone who would ascend the throne as Guna Gunu to marry from outside my tribe.

But my father wasn’t satisfied and so he sought the counsel of yet another diviner. Of course his prediction was totally different – that I’m spellbound by my stepmother, Jumai not to actualise my marriage plans so as to prevent me from succeeding my father to become the ninth Guna Gunu of Gunkun kingdom. He also advised my father to bring her to take an oath after which she’d be driven away. My father believed him.

Foreseeing that his prediction would break the family apart and having an inkling of what could be responsible for my delayed marriage, although I couldn’t explain it to my father or anyone else, I dismissed the clairvoyant’s prediction and warned my father against acting on it. And since none of these diviners came near to the true cause of my problem, I couldn’t rely on them to help me solve it either. My father consented to my advice not to send my stepmother, Jumai away because of me, on the condition that I would bring a bride home on my next visit to Nigeria, at the Easter holiday.

But after six failed attempts to establish a lasting relationship, I’m sure I’ve finally made the right decision. Although she fits the soothsayer’s ‘one from afar and across the sea’ description, she fails on one point – she’s not of my tribe.

Lillian Wakamma is from Rivers state, according to the medium, that makes her an alien, and an unsuitable choice. She’s going to twenty. She’s beautiful and homely – my parent’s idea of a woman fit for the Guna Gunu.

However, considering my father’s desperation to see that I’m settled, I’m hoping that he’ll be constrained to accept her even though I’m brazenly going against the ancient legacy of the throne of the ancient Gunkun kingdom. But if he refuses, I’ll forfeit the throne and follow my heart. Lillian and I love each other and we’ve invested much into the relationship. We met in the University of Duncan during the spring semester, the school hosted exchange students of international relations from universities in various commonwealth states.

Considering Lillian’s quiet disposition, I was reluctant to ask her out when I made her acquaintance through a friend who introduced me to her. Even though I was totally taken by her right from the first day I set my eyes on her, she was quick to notice I was unnecessarily self-conscious whenever she’s around and apparently mindful of saying something wrong or doing anything that I think she may consider inappropriate. When she asked me pointedly if I had been in a failed relationship, I didn’t deny I was recovering from one. I was surprised to discover we were in the same boat.

Initially, a mutual feeling of suspicion existed between us, heartbreak of our previous relationships carried over into our friendship. We both secretly struggled to come over it. Eventually the golden moment came when Gerald, a mutual friend, unwittingly played the umpire when he started a discussion about his recent break up with a girl he had dated for eight years. He blamed her for what happened. Lillian equally narrated open-mindedly about her own failed relationship and baffled us by sincerely accepting to have contributed to its failure. It was her first date and she was nervous, jealous and unreasonably demanding.

In spite of their query about my love life, I was hesitant about speaking of my past relationships and how many times I’ve been heartbroken, or rather broken the heart of my dates. I was afraid it would escalate her fears and spoil our newly growing affection at that time. Lillian is my seventh. Not the seventh woman in my life, but this is the seventh time I’m trying to work out a lifelong relationship.

However, I took it up from that point and after our discussion that day I began to admire Lillian deeply, until I fell in love with her. But it was the night Lillian was leaving London after the exchange program that lasted two months, at the Heathrow airport that I proposed to her. I couldn’t steady my wobbly lips as I let out those scary words. I was expecting her to give me the usual, I’ll think about it reply, but she laughed off my anxiety and said, yes. We hugged each other and kissed for the first time. I still savour that moment with much relish.

After about eight hours in the air, the plane finally touches down at the Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport, Abuja at exactly ten p.m. We all disembark from the aircraft and stream into the arrival lounge. Since I didn’t make any arrangements for anyone to pick me up from the airport, I negotiate with a car hire to take me to the Nicon Noga Hilton Hotel. After booking into a room, I book a call to Lillian’s number with the receptionist in thirty minutes.

When I intimated my father about the reason for my visit and my relationship with Lillian shortly before I left London, the first thing he asked about was where she’s from, and when I told him, he opposed it saying he won’t be the one that would break the tradition of our forefathers and bring a curse on the throne his ancestors bestowed on him. Although he didn’t mention it, I know his decision was the advice of one of the mediums who always loitered around the palace.

I don’t know how Lillian will feel when she gets to know I changed my flight schedule to land in Abuja so I can dash to Jos to iron things out with my father and also make known to him my stand before proceeding to Lagos to formally seek Lillian’s parents’ consent.
The telephone rings and I pick up the receiver and answer, “Hello.”
“Is that room 657?” the voice at the other end asks.
“Please hold on for your call.”
“Hello…hello, Lillian are you there?”
“Bitty,” she responds coldly.
“Yeah. What’s up baby?”
“Bitty, are you in town?”
“I’m in Nigeria. I arrived a few minutes ago. You sound so cold are you okay?”
“I am. There’s something I want us to discuss. Are you coming over to school?”
“Not tonight.”
“I’m in Abuja right now…something came up. I had to reschedule my flight in the nick of time before leaving London. Please help me explain to your parents that I’ll be in Lagos tomorrow.”
“They won’t miss you,” she says.
“What’s that supposed to mean?”
“It’s not something I can tell you on the phone…maybe we should talk about this when you come okay.”

“Lilly, Listen to me,” I pause to take a deep breath. “I know we’ve been away from one another for a long time Lilly, but I want you to trust me. I’m doing everything I can to make our dream work okay…. I’ve been longing for this moment ever since that night at the airport.” I wait to see if she’d respond, but she remains silent, “Everything will work out fine, trust me.”
“Bitty for Christ sake this is marriage. You’re not just asking me out. How could you keep such vital information from me?”
“I beg your pardon,” I quaver, “What are you talking about?”

“A family friend who happens to have come from your town broke the news to my parents that you’re a Crown prince. Can you imagine how embarrassed I felt when I laughed him off saying that he was probably talking about someone else,” she takes a pause, “How on earth couldn’t you have told me that?”
“Please don’t take it personal Lilly. I uh – I felt it wasn’t the best time to talk about it.”
“You felt?” she sighs, “Are you this selfish, Bitty. Don’t you care about my feelings?”
“It’s not like that Lilly, please I can explain,” I bleat.
“When do you suppose is the best time…alright let me guess. On this fateful day the king and queen and a bunch of his cabinet members or whatever they’re called…”
“Lillian,” I try to interrupt her.

But she continues, “So they all storm London in search of the Crown prince, first they go to Mayfair expecting to find him in a lush apartment, but they’re taken aback when they finally discover that their son, the Crown prince himself is living in squalor, and bang! I suddenly discover that my groom…”
“Lillian!”

“…who has been pretending to be a pauper actually has his portrait on currency notes and I agreed to marry him for whom he is, without the slightest hint he’s a prince,” she continues, “How about coming to London for a caption?”
“Lillian, will you stop this.”
“Wake up Bitty, we’re living in a real world.”
“You’re insulting me Lillian.”

“Am I? Now you’re sounding like a prince. How did I miss that tone,” she says with a gruff. “Do you want to know how I’d feel if one morning I wake up in a pool of flowers and a herd of maids waiting on me to take my clothes off and have me bathed like a baby…awful, terrible and sick.”
“Will you stop saying this Lillian? Stop this!”
“Any way let me give you a piece of advice. I heard that aliens are not supposed to be married to an heir apparent so please don’t bother coming. I’m not cut out for that kind of life to be sincere.”
“You’ve obviously been fed with wrong information…but please hear me out for the sake of love.”
“Bitty, what is love?”
I remain silent.
“Love does not demand its own way,” she drawls.
“You once told me Lilly, that love does not keep any record of when it has been wronged…forgive me Lilly. I know I’ve wronged you…I’ve struggled to keep this from you for obvious reasons.”
“Why Bitty, why?” she whines.

“I was afraid of losing you Lilly. Please forgive me,” I take a pause, "You’re right, I wanted to make it happen my own way, but now I know better. I love you Lilly and I know you love me. No one has ever made me feel the way you make me feel.”
“I do love you Bitty…but I don’t think this is going to work.”

“What do you think will hinder us Lilly? I have made up my mind to give up the throne if that will cost my love for you. Please give me this one chance to prove my love for you. I’m on my knees…the Crown prince himself is on his knees, please,” I ask her with a jest.
“I don’t know what to say,” she replies fighting tears.
“My mind tells me one thing should I listen to my heart? Should I say yes, should I say no – I wish I knew you better…” I say with a rhythm.
“You’re crazy,” she chuckles.
“For you Lillian. I’m crazy for you. Just tell me you love me and hang up, let me have a good rest.”
“On one condition.”
“Anything.”
“Take the first flight tomorrow back to Lagos.”
“No buts, just do it,” she giggles, “Love does not demand its own way, remember?”
I take a deep breath and finally reply, “Okay, okay. Anything you say. But –”
“I said no buts! I love you. Call me as soon as you arrive okay.”
“I will.”
“See you tomorrow. Sweet dreams.”
“Are you breaking like that won’t you at least give me a hug?”
“Over the phone?” she giggles.
“Just close your eyes and feel me …can you feel me now?”
“You’ve been watching too many movies recently, haven’t you?”
“Yeah, don’t you believe the movie magic?”
“You’re really crazy,” she says laughing, “Let me leave you to rest, you had a long journey. We’ll talk tomorrow okay.” She blows me a kiss and hangs up.

Because of her chastity I take much precaution when I converse with Lillian. She doesn’t condone the use of coarse or derogatory terms and she told me from the onset of our relationship, “It would be platonic until our wedding day”. I’ve been down this road before. I made such a pledge to Henrietta, one of my exes, but I broke it one day when she came to visit me alone. After the incident, we eventually went our separate ways.
I don’t trust myself to keep that promise, but I’ll do everything within my capability to make this relationship work.

chapters.title: Chapter TWO
content: Because of the severely hazy weather, the morning flights are rescheduled to leave in the evening, hopefully, if the weather situation improves airline operators say. I also gather that it has been like this for some days.

The local wing of the airport is scanty. A few intending passengers who have a word in their ear from a travel agent are patiently sitting at the lounge reading the dailies and drinking hot tea or coffee with their legs crossed. They were also promised that flights would resume as soon as the weather condition improves.

After waiting for about thirty minutes, one of the intending passengers who had gone to make an enquiry returns to the waiting chair shaking his head disappointingly and sighing. He says, according to the meteorologist, ‘it is unlikely any aircraft will be leaving the airport today’. And that he has an urgent meeting to attend in Lagos tomorrow morning so he has decided to travel by road. He takes his briefcase and walking stick and saunters away from the lounge.

Travelling by road is the last thing I would have wished for. Apart from the inconveniences of traffic jam and the risk of an auto crash due to reckless driving, the deplorable state of the roads and the hazard of armed bandits attack on the highway not only makes travelling such a distance by road uninviting, but a high-risk option.

When I called the hall porter’s lodge from a pay phone in the airport to inform Lillian about the weather situation and that I’m coming by road, she could not be reached. I had to leave a message with an irascible lady that claimed to be her roommate who answered the phone on her behalf, who also promised to give her my message whenever she sees her.

Because of the fear I’m nursing about Lillian’s likely reaction to my arriving late I can only afford to glance through the newspaper I bought at the motor park to keep me occupied through the gruesome nine-hour journey – she’s hardly convinced about my excuses. I know she’ll think that in spite of her plea to take the first flight, I ignored her and dashed to Jos to see my father. The tone of her voice on the phone yesterday portends an end to our romance.

My mind goes back to what the seer said about my stepmother, Jumai. I try not to believe that she has a hand in this…not until I’m able to deal with this problem. But if eventually I break up with Lillian, I’ll take it that the gods have conspired against me according to the decree of the priest. I’ll forfeit the throne and forget about marriage and go back to London for good. And probably remain single the rest of my life.

Certainly, my previous dates had not all failed for the same reasons, but I have a foreboding this could be a bad omen for my relationship with Lillian. It always starts on a very good note – we fantasize about all those things that lifts up one’s spirits about marriage, so much that the air of expectancy of that future bliss suppresses our differences and conflicting interests. But after a while, like a shifting cloud, the euphoria begins to peter out and suddenly the unexpected happens. Then it all goes down the tubes.

Because of the ocean view, I usually prefer to lodge in the Quintessence Hotel whenever I’m in Lagos. Schools are on vacation for the Easter, but Lillian says she and a handful of students are around to complete their exams. Because I’m not conversant with the school I make enquiry for the direction to Mary Slessor hall.

Lillian stays in room 142, on the ground floor, I know. The hostel is streaming with pairs of a male and female walking hand in hand, which brings back memories of the good old days in the Metro, as it is popularly called.

After locking the doors, I proceed to the hostel taking along the gift I bought for Lillian from London. I suddenly sight my old friend leaving the hostel with a girl in his arm. I freeze and stand still studying him for a while from a distance. I can’t miss his height, his gait. “This is incredible,” I whisper to myself.

When they get to the road, he flags down a bike, waves her goodbye and vanishes from sight. She turns and saunters back to the hostel. I quietly follow her into the hostel walking briskly after her as she climbs the stairs to the second floor.
“Excuse me please,” I say politely when I finally reach her as she turns the handle to open the door.
She turns around looking askance at me.
“Good evening.”
“Good evening,” she replies.
“My name is Bitrus Shak.”
“Elizabeth.”

“I’m delighted to meet you Elizabeth,” I gasp, “I am a chemical engineer with the Deapsea Petroleum Development Company in the U.K.” And hand her my compliment card. “I uh – I came to see my fiancée Lillian, room 142.”
That put a frosty and uninviting expression on her face. “Room 142 is downstairs,” she replies bluntly.
“I know. Actually, I want to find out from you about the guy you just escorted from the hostel. He looks like a friend of mine called Ikenna?”
She grimaces at me.
“Iyke as a matter of fact.”
“Not any of those,” she replies, “His name is Jehoshaphat.”
“Jehoshaphat! Are you sure?”
“Of course I am,” she giggles and turns the door handle, “Excuse me.”
“Wha – what about his last name, what’s his surname?”
“His name is Jehoshaphat Obiajulu.”
“Yes that’s him, Obiajulu, Iyke Obiajulu,” I snap clicking my fingers. “Please Elizabeth, how can I see him? Do you know where he stays?”
“I do, but I actually came to attend to a sick friend of mine. I can’t leave her now.”
“Please Elizabeth. I have a car, I’ll bring you back as soon as possible.”
