Knowing Lillian will not take it lightly if I dash into her room, spend a few minutes and tell her I’ll be back in a jiffy, I decide to go find out about Iyke first and see her later when I return to drop Elizabeth. I’ll be so sorry if Lillian gets the hint that I went out with another lady – that’ll be the end of story. She can be jealous.

Iyke is such an intriguing character, and his life has been enshrouded in mystery following his visit to Morashe hills. One thing that still amazes me is how he managed to keep that vow for nearly five years? Shortly after our degree exams, there was a rumour that he had died in the prison and no one ever heard about him again. Could that be his ghost or am I seeing an apparition?

Until this moment, I’ve not been able to fathom how he got involved with Sephia, and going by what he told me about his consecration vow, I attribute the failure of the hit that night to his secret affair with Sephia. He told me himself he was forbidden, as a star bearer, to see the nakedness of a ringo.

That’s one of the reasons I abdicated the office of the Hangman, apart from the fact that the Hangman is forbidden to cut his hair (how could I have explained that to Dickson or to my parents anyway). I didn’t think turning myself into a eunuch for the prestige of the office was worth it. Dickson in particular would have discovered from my bushy hair that something was terribly wrong with me and possibly suspected I had joined the fraternity. Besides, going by what happened to Iyke, that would have cost me my career, which was my primary concern at that time.

My mind goes back to my last moment with Iyke, the Valentine night of nineteen ninety-three. I recall our last conversation in the car on our way from the botched crucifixion parade he led against the Golgotha, against Femi precisely. I couldn’t pay him a visit in prison during his detention for obvious reasons. I’m sure he would have been utterly disappointed that I abandoned him when he needed me most, but I just couldn’t stand looking him in the face after what happened that night. What if he asks me why I refused to come and see him in prison? I ponder.

If he’s the one and he’s adopted a new name, it’d be for this singular reason – to beat the GODs. I’m sure the position of the Hangman will still be vacant and they’d still be after him to get him to completely shed his finger. I recall Dickson’s advice when he confronted me about my membership in the fraternity, he said, “If the devil invites you to dine with him, make sure you go with a very long spoon.” He said there are dire consequences for cult activities and assured me that time will prove it.
“You mind if I call you Lizzy?”
“It’s okay. That’s what my friends call me,” she replies with a charming smile.
“So, Lizzy, did you say this guy’s name is Jehosh –?”
“Jehoshaphat,” she bails me out, “It’s a Hebrew name.”
“I see…you guys are very close.”
“Yes of course,” she responds.
“I mean are you dating?”
“No,” she replies blushing, “Must I date a guy before he can qualify as being close to me?”

“You ever watched him play basketball? That’s his best part. Back in those days in Metro, girls found him irresistible…he was a sex symbol. Some girls are so forward they boldly ask him out after watching him play.”
“I beg your pardon,” she cuts in impatiently facing me, “Can we talk about something else?” She shakes her head and turns back to the window.
I chuckle and avoid innuendos about their having an affair.
“Are you sure this guy is actually the person you’re looking for?” she asks.
“I’m trying to convince myself about that too,” I reply.

Iyke lives in a small bungalow surrounded by a fence made of a block work that is not rendered and the battered gate is not locked, in fact, in my opinion it is never locked. The compound is not so spacious.

Lizzy directs me where to park and asks me to wait in the car while she goes to intimate him about me. My imagination is stimulated as I watch Lizzy walk to the house with a sweeping gait. I recall that Iyke has always been lucky with beautiful creatures like this. But from her reaction towards our discussion about their affair, I doubt whether they’re in a serious relationship. It won’t be a bad idea getting it on with her.

The thought of Julia, Iyke’s cousin flashes in my mind. In spite of slurring him in order to sway her, she still clung onto him just as Sephia left me for him. It’s so sad what happened to Sephia, but I still blame Iyke for her death. If he had stayed faithful to his vow, she would still be alive today. I shut my eyes trying to get rid of the haunting memory of her death.
“We can’t see him now,” she announces as she enters the car.
“I was watching Lizzy, you didn’t even knock.”
“He’s praying.” She looks at her watch.
I do the same unconsciously. The time is fifteen past nine.
“It’ll take a while. I suggest you drop me in school and come back yourself. I’m sure you’ll be able to find your way here on your own.”
“Yeah sure,” I agree. I take a pause staring directly into her eyes thinking of a decent way to tell her what’s on my mind. But I can’t get myself to do it.

She exudes an unfamiliar charisma and sense of contentment, which I have not found in the ladies I’ve come across in the past and from the tone of her voice, she’s not going to be an easy lay. But she’s definitely worth a try, after all, she’s a woman. I could promise her anything right now to have her spend the night with me.
I notice a film of tears is covering her eyes as she turns her face away shyly.
“Is anything wrong Elizabeth?”
“Nothing,” she says fighting back tears. “Can you please drop me off in my house?”
“Aren’t you going back to school?”
“No. Please just drop me at home,” she gives in to her emotion.
“Is he in trouble?”
“Lizzy!” The voice is coming from behind.
She freezes. We both stare at each other.
“If he wasn’t expecting you, how did he know you’re here?”
She opens the door without answering me.
His squeaky footsteps approach the car. “Bitty,” he calls.
“Iyke,” I mutter and step out of the car.
“I’m sorry we interrupted you,” she explains to him, “He met me on my way back to Naomi’s room after seeing you off and pleaded with me to bring him to you.”
“It’s all right Lizzy,” Iyke replies coolly, “Bitty is an old friend.”

“Actually, before I could recover from the shock of seeing you again you had vanished, so I coaxed her to bring me here,” I re-emphasise what she said in order to excuse her. “What a pleasant surprise,” I beam.
“Yes indeed,” he replies distantly while gazing at the gate. “Shall we go in?”

“Wait a minute!” I dash to the car to get the bottle of non-alcoholic wine that I intended to present to Lillian’s father. She told me they are devout Christians and that her father would prefer that.
“So, when are you getting married to Lillian?” Iyke asks me as soon as we’re seated at the dining table.
“Excuse me,” I reply with astonishment.
“Are you surprised? Is her name not written all over the bottle?” he beams.
Then I recall he conjures.
“Anyway, let’s bless the wine and drink to the fulfilment,” he suggests.

A strong feeling of déjà vu and a hardy feeling of guilt sweep over me as I join them in bowing my head. I remember my first visit to Julia’s house after our matriculation in Metropolitan. I remember the sharp disagreement between Julia and Rebecca that spoiled the meal that evening because of Rebecca’s request that he offers prayers before the meal.

“Almighty God, it is written in your Word that Your works are known to You right from the foundation of the world. It has pleased You to reunite us together this day in your unfailing love. May You by the communion of the blood of our Lord Jesus Christ, which this wine symbolizes and His flesh keep us all together in your love until Your everlasting kingdom comes, and may You also grant that my friend Bitrus by whom You have provided this wine also receive the grace and salvation in the blood of Jesus Christ, and that his name be written in the Lamb’s book of life who loved us and died for us, while we were yet sinners. By Your grace in Jesus Christ grant that we all may sit in this manner at the Marriage feast of the Lamb in the regeneration. Amen.”
“Amen,” Lizzy responds.

Me too, but with quavering lips because I don’t quite understand or agree with his faith and I’m not particularly a religious person. Giving my present predicament, however, I’d welcome any form of prayer that could salvage my situation. And if as he claims God answers his prayers, maybe I should share my burden with him. After all we’re covenant brothers.
“Man! I never thought I’d see you again,” I say opening the bottle of wine at his request, “We were told you died in prison.”
“So I heard,” he replies.
“You heard it?” I repeat.
“Yeah, my lawyer told me.”
“Wait a minute, did you say prison?” Lizzy cuts in.
“I’m sorry for bringing this up before –” I apologize to Iyke feebly.
“You’re an ex-convict?” she asks Iyke pointedly.

“It’s okay Bitty,” he snaps, “Lizzy has a right to know everything about me,” he takes a pause, “Some times it’s difficult to share some painful experience with those you love, not because you intend to hide it from them. I hope Bitty will tell you about it sometime.”
“He’s told me how close you guys used to be, but of course he didn’t mention that you were ever jailed,” Lizzy replies.
“Tell me my friend how did you metamorphose into this? She told me you’re a preacher.”
“It’s a long story Bitty,” Iyke says.
I remember the promise I made to Lizzy to drop her off at the hostel. “Let me know when you feel like going okay,” I say turning to her.
“I think I’m interested in hearing this testimony too,” she replies.
“Good.” I turn back to Iyke, “We’re all ears, and we’ve got all the time.”
“Where do I start, where do I start?” Iyke whispers.
“Start from your experience in the prison. How did you leave the prison?” I kick him off with the question.
“Maybe the rumour was true after all. Figuratively, I died in Katampe prison, in that dungeon,” he says as he stares into space.
“Katampe!” I say with astonishment, “I thought you were jailed in Jos?”

“For a few weeks yes. That’s what substantiated the rumour about my death. But I’m thankful that God took me there, because it offered me the opportunity of receiving the forgiveness of my sins through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.
“So what happened in the prison?”

“It’ll take a book to fully describe. But the summary of it all is that Jesus sent me His word by a prophet called Harry. He used to come to share the gospel of Jesus Christ with inmates in the prison. Although I was brought up in this Way, I considered Harry’s message absurd and the mystery too weird to believe – that a man died and rose again from the dead after three days and that believing in His name my sins will be forgiven and I’d be saved.”
“If that’s coming from you Iyke, I can understand how profound it is,” I chip in.

“But I was wrong Bitty,” he pauses and takes his Bible turns to a page and reads, “For the message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but for those who are being saved it is the power of God that brings about the salvation of those that believe.” Then he looks up. First at Lizzy, then he casts his gaze on me. “When Harry foretold my acquittal and discharge from prison. I didn’t take him seriously because I didn’t deserve it…I was condemned by God’s commandment and by my conscience to die. I was not worthy of life.” Then he says droopily, “But the Bible rightly says that the law was given through Moses, but grace and truth came through Jesus.”
When I turn to Lizzy, she’s overwhelmed with pity shaking her head slowly. “What did you do?” Lizzy asks him with a sympathetic tone of voice.

“That’s not so important now, Lizzy,” I snap to prevent him from answering, from telling her of the gory tales of our past lives. “So what happened after you left jail, did you eventually go back home?”

“No I didn’t. Because I was ignorant of the fact that the only way to appreciate God for His salvation was to offer up my life as a living sacrifice, by living a life that would not only please Him, but also glorify Him. I continued in my rebellion to gratify my sensual lusts,” he takes a pause, glances at Lizzy and mutters, “That was when I met Ugo.”
“Who is Ugo?” I ask.
“That’s not so important now Bitty okay,” Lizzy snaps angrily, “Continue Jshap.”
I chuckle.

“Elizabeth,” Iyke calls her in low tone, “A quarrelsome person starts fights as easily as hot embers light charcoal or fire lights wood. Never pay back evil for evil to anyone, or it will get the best of you, but rather overcome evil with good.”
“I’m sorry.” Then she turns to me and says remorsefully, “I’m sorry Bitty.”
I’m flabbergasted at the influence he exerts on her.
“You’ll get to meet him Bitty,” Iyke hesitates, “You’ll get to meet him very soon,” he says in a droopy tone looking at the door.

Then he takes a sip of wine from his glass and then continues his narrative. “I spent about six months with the Amanzes before returning home to my parents. I became his teacher and mentor…and I impinged on him,” he quavers and takes a pause. “While I was with the Amanzes, I found myself in the midst of a tug of love, between Ugo’s mother and a greater love that was unconsciously drawing me back to my parents who, as at then, I hadn’t seen in about eight years.”
“Jesus!” Lizzy whispers.
“I’m sure you were homesick?” I respond. “So what happened when you eventually got home? How did your parents receive you?”

“It wasn’t the desire to see my parents, but the unconscious longing of my weary soul to return to its real home that tugged at my heart. The love of my Father in heaven, the love He demonstrated by sending His beloved Son into the world as an atonement sacrifice for our sins that we who believe in Him might be justified and live by faith in Him.

“Shortly after my return, I accepted Jesus as my Lord and personal Saviour and laid down my life to serve Him. In the words of Apostle Paul, I died to the law which was at work in my life crucifying myself with Him no longer to live my earthly life as I please, but by faith in the Son of God, that His life may be evident in my body for He loved me and gave Himself for me. The following day after my sister led me to Christ, God restored to me the dream I thought I had lost forever. I received my admission letter into the college of medicine,” he pauses, “But with it He gave my life a new meaning.”
“This is really incredible,” I chuckle as I try to contain the emotions evoked by his story.

While he leaves us digesting the incredible things he has said, he silently takes the Bible again and flips the pages. I take my glass of wine and steal a glance at Lizzy our eyes meet and we sip from our glasses in unison.

“This is my testimony,” he grins, “I am thankful to Christ Jesus our Lord for considering me trustworthy and appointing me to serve Him, even though I used to scoff at the name of Christ, I hunted down His people harming them in everyway I could. But God had mercy on me because I did it in ignorance and unbelief. Oh, how kind and gracious the Lord was! He filled me completely with the faith and love of Christ Jesus,” he reads out loud, “This is a true saying, and everyone should believe it, Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am the worst of them all.” He closes the Bible and raises his head staring fixedly at me and saying as if he’s addressing me personally, “But that is why God had mercy on me, so that Christ Jesus could use me as a prime example of His great patience with even the worst sinner. Then others will realize that they, too, can believe in Him and receive eternal life. Glory and Honour to God forever and ever, He is the eternal King, the unseen One who never dies, He alone is God. Amen.”
“Amen,” Lizzy mutters and gives in to her emotions. I flush with guilt as the thoughts of our days in the Metro flashes on my mind.

Then he takes his glass of wine, and in the momentary silence, he takes a sip and smacks silently. Lizzy looks at her watch the third time, but Iyke is not paying attention. He gulps down the remaining drink in the glass.
“You never cared to visit me in prison Bitty,” he says coldly, “Tell me the truth Bitty, it’s not the fear of transgressing the law that kept you from coming to see me in prison.”

I am conscience-stricken by his question as it brings back the memory of our days in school. I take the bottle and pour another round of drinks in the glasses intentionally attempting to evade the discussion.
“Did you get to know what actually happened to Sephia…who killed her?”
Dumbfounded at the question, I drop the bottle gently on the table and exhale heavily then reply, “I thought you should have forgotten that by now…anyway, she took her own life.”
“That’s not possible Bitty, she was raped,” he blasts.
“Then she shot herself that’s what happened,” I pause and glance quickly at Lizzy, “The same way Julia tried to take her life too.”
Lizzy squints at me. She’s probably disgusted with this talk about rapes and death.
“Let’s forget this talk about Sephia okay. What has happened has happened. Sephia is history.”

“That’s what it seems to you, Bitty. Do you know how it feels to be in prison for nearly two years for a crime you didn’t commit?” he says coldly with his eyes squinting. “How can I forget? Can anything erase these scars on my wrist,” he says showing us his hands. Then he raises his right hand and wipes tears trickling down his cheeks. “They put me in chains and tortured me…I was bruised…all for the sake of love…how can I forget it, Bitty?” he stops and stares at Lizzy.
She blushes and drops her eyes. “Can you drop me off now, please?”
“Sure.” I reply curtly standing up with the eagerness to free myself from this agonizing moment.
“Will you come back and spend the night here, Bitty? I have a spare room,” he says expressively.
“That’s very considerate of you Iyke, but I uh – I have to see Lillian first to decide my movement.”

I am taken aback by the knock on the door, the Inverted Cross Fraternity’s secret knock code – two fast, one knock then three slow knocks. I stare at the door in perplexity, then at Iyke and meet his dispassionate gaze.

He stands up and walks to the door at the second knock and opens it. The visitor is putting on a black trench coat over a black jean. His face is covered with a mask and he’s wearing the beanie with the symbol of an invented cross. In his right hand is the hammer and the leather armband with a bronze nail clasped on his left arm.
“Jesus Christ!” Lizzy whispers.

chapters.title: Chapter THREE
content: He wobbles into the sitting room with a confident gait, while Iyke moves backwards making way for him. Three other boys step in behind him, all wearing facemasks as well and black berets. And two of them are having pistols in their hand.
“What do you want?” Iyke demands speaking to the one in trench coat.

The content of the law comes back to my mind. Although this fellow has received the staff of the office of the Hangman, the GODs will not recognize him as the star bearer of the Order until he forces Iyke to shed his remaining finger or he adduces proof that Iyke is dead forty days after he received the inverted cross brooch. He’s here to fulfil the rite of Passover.

He ignores Iyke and ambles to where Lizzy is standing by the dining table, and gazes at her for a while. Then he turns around to Iyke and suddenly swings his left hand backwards catching Lizzy unawares. She falls on the impact of his backhand slap and hit her head on the table.
“You son of a bitch what has she done to you?” I shout at him charging forward.
“Bitty!” Iyke calls me with an unruffled tone, “Calm down.”

He hasn’t spoken a word since he and his colleagues barged in. He turns and walks up to me, standing face to face with me and gazing at me from the mask. Then he paces to Iyke and beckons him to bend down. Iyke does. He whispers something in his ears and steps backwards throwing the hammer and the black wallet on the floor at his legs.
“I won’t do it,” Iyke responds.
“Then you will surely die,” he replies with an obvious attempt to disguise his voice.
“Leave her out of this,” Iyke tells him.
I glance at Lizzy where she’s squatting on the floor and our eyes meet.
“The book for her life,” he says as he mooches about the room.
Iyke gives me a quick glance.
“If you give me the book…I’ll also let you walk away free,” he urges him.
“You are the one that needs freedom,” Iyke replies.
He strolls back to Iyke and bends down picking up the hammer and wallet. “Let me refresh your memory…Virus!” he calls one of the boys with him who is having a pistol.
“Shezou Hangman,” he answers.
Iyke and I glance at each other again.
“Clamp her,” he orders him.
“If you are the Hangman, take what you want and leave the ringo out of this,” Iyke replies.
He turns and walks towards Lizzy.
“Leave her alone! You son of a bitch,” I yell and rush towards him.
He drops the hammer, pulls a pistol from his waistline immediately and points it on my forehead. “One more step and you‘re a dead man.”
I freeze.

Still pointing the pistol on me he orders Iyke, “At the count of five if you refuse to tell me where the testament is, I’ll burrow his skull… one… two… three… four.” I shut my eyes expecting the click of the hammer.
“Let him go,” Iyke succumbs.
“Where?” he shouts at him.
“Take the pistol away,” Iyke replies calmly.
“Don’t tell him anything. Let him do it,” I reply. “Come on go ahead and shoot me you son of a bitch!” I tell him with a gruff.
He hit me very hard with the butt of his pistol. I fall on my knees bowing my head drooling blood at my mouth.
“Stop this Ugo, stop it!” Lizzy shouts at him.
He freezes at the mention of his name. “Virus, Gecko, Clamp her!” he yells angrily breaking his false accent.
“Leave the ringo out of this Albatross, you’re breaking the rules of engagement,” the other boy who is also holding a pistol whispers.
“Shut up Troubled Waters!” he barks at him.
“Is that why we’re here, to crucify a ringo? I will have no part in this transgression Albatross,” he continues.

“I said shut up, shut the hell up,” he barks at him pointing the pistol at him. “And don’t call me Albatross, I am the Hangman and I am the custodian of the testament. Either you address me accordingly, or you keep your dirty mouth shut. Do you read me…do you feel me Troubled Waters?”
“Yes Hangman,” he drawls, “But I won’t take orders from you if you have brought us here to crucify a ringo.”
“Good. Then keep quiet and when the time comes, you do what is assigned to you, okay,” he whispers and turns his face towards Lizzy.
“Shezou brothers!” Iyke’s voice cracks with emotion.
Ugo halts. The boys holding Lizzy stare at each other in surprise and turn to Ugo.
“What the hell is going on Albatross?” Troubled Waters asks him.
“Ignore him,” Ugo tells them.
“Identify yourself son of darkness,” Troubled Waters orders Iyke.
“I said ignore him are you deaf?”
“I won’t,” he barks, “Identify yourself!”
“Angel nest Mount Horeb, name at inversion the Seagull…XYZ Hangman in diaspora.” Iyke says.
“God damn it!” Troubled Waters whispers.
The boys holding Lizzy leave her instantly.

Ugo cracks up mischievously, clapping his hands and slowly walking towards Iyke. “The mystic man has finally revealed himself,” he says as he stands face to face with Iyke. “I had it in mind, by the authority given to me, to set you free forever from the iniquity of the law. But I can see you have made up your mind to die,” he whispers.
“I belong to a kingdom that is not of this world,” Iyke replies, “You would have no power over me except it is given to you from above. But now is your hour… the power of darkness.”
The bulbs in the room blink once. Then the lights go off again, the boys holding Lizzy scuttle away from her and fall on the floor and then the light comes on.
“Abort this mission now Albatross,” Troubled Waters charges him.
“No Troubled Waters…wait,” Ugo replies in a hushed tone stepping away from Iyke as he gazes at the two bulbs in the room one after the other with fascination.
Standing behind Ugo, Troubled Waters raises his pistol, points it at Ugo and cocks it. “Abort this mission now!” he demands.
Ugo turns around and sights him pointing his pistol at him “Are you crazy Legion?” he asks him rhetorically.
“You’re the one who’s crazy, you junkie,” he replies.
“He’s the one,” Ugo tells him pointing at Iyke.

The light blinks again. In a flash I spot Ugo raise his pistol pointing it towards Iyke. As soon as the light goes off again, I rush towards Iyke in the dark and push him away from his position with the intention of taking him off Ugo’s aim. His pistol rattles as soon as I reach him. Almost immediately, I hear the sound of another pistol rattle.

When the light comes on again, I notice that Troubled Waters is holding his pistol towards the direction where Ugo was standing. Ugo is lying by my side bleeding in the head. He was shot in his forehead. Iyke is also lying on the floor bleeding.

Troubled Waters lowers his hand, tucks the pistol into his trouser waistline and ambles to where Ugo is lying. The other two boys come over and help him lift Ugo from the ground. One of the boys put him on his shoulder and they all leave the room.
Lizzy is wearing a faraway expression as she gazes at Iyke.

chapters.title: Chapter FOUR
content: It appears I’m disaster-prone as far as women are concerned. The doctor at the Fountain of Life hospital diagnosed Lizzy with traumatic anaemia. She has not uttered a word since the incident. When she’s awake, she stares bleakly into space and she’s refused to eat anything. Although he assured me she’ll come around pretty soon, I am concerned that things may never be the same with her again.

After Julia was assaulted, she never recovered from that traumatic experience. Sephia too, she took her own life. I blame myself for letting Lizzy stay long enough to witness this horror? I should have dropped her off earlier.

Speaking of doom, my affair with Lillian hit the buffers this morning on account of the rumour bandied about campus of what happened in Iyke’s room. The gist is that two cult groups clashed in the preacher’s apartment and both sides recorded a casualty each. Although the names of the groups were not mentioned, those who are aware of the cult system say it’s the Inverted Cross Fraternity versus an unknown cult group. What a scandal! That the famous preacher Iyke, no Jehoshaphat Obiajulu, as he’s popularly known here is a member of an unknown secret cult group.

Of course as soon as I mentioned being at Iyke’s place as the reason for my not being able to see her yesterday, she tagged me a secret cult member, shut her ears to my alibi, shouted abuses on me and walked out on me without even giving me the opportunity of clearing up the incident.

Apart from the time I went to see Lillian in school when the morning staff resumed, I’ve not taken my eyes off Lizzy. She dozed off about half an hour ago giving me the rare opportunity to freely ogle at her. I abandon myself to the fetish thought until I’m reminded subconsciously that it was one act of such indecency that brought us to this point. “What’s the matter with me?” I mutter under my breath and quickly leave the room.

The hospital reception overlooks a long corridor leading to the theatre, which is at the very end of the passage. Although the workers on morning duty resumed at seven, those assisting the surgeon are still in the operating room. The time by the clock on the wall is ten minutes past nine. The operation has lasted three hours since he was resuscitated.
From the look of things, he has very slim chances of survival. Nevertheless, I’m willing to pay whatever it’ll cost to save his life.

Dr. Imoke initially refused to accept the responsibility of treating him because, by law, a police report is usually required before patients with bullet wounds can receive medical treatment. Although the factors that informed such a decision is quite understandable, I argued against its expediency. In the part of the world where I live, the health insurance scheme, which is obviously not available here, deals with the risks that the police report purports to be covering. But when he saw that he was in a critical point and that he may die if immediate treatment is not administered, he eventually agreed and asked me to sign necessary papers authorizing the surgery.

Two nurses wheel a woman screaming and kicking in a stretcher into the hospital and straight to the female ward. A young lady who came with her is denied access into the ward. She stamps her feet apprehensively, screaming, “Jesus! Jesus!” as if he were one of the doctors. The lady receptionist tries in vain to appease her, and refusing to sit down she continues screaming aloud, “Jesus! Jesus!” staring fixedly at a framed portrait hanging on the wall of a man supposed to be Him with his hands stretched out. A French word is written at the bottom of the frame, which is interpreted in English, ‘I am the resurrection and the life’.
“If you are the Son of God… save my friend from dying,” I whisper to myself, “Since he trusts in you.”

When I turn towards the theatre, the team of doctors are filing out of the theatre pulling off their surgical masks and removing their gloves. Their faces are inexpressive of any particular mood. I gaze anxiously at the passage until one of the attendants wheels Iyke in a stretcher along the corridor into another room.
“Let’s see in my office Mr. Shak,” Dr. Imoke says as he briskly walks past me heading to his office.
He eventually breaks the news that Iyke didn’t make it. He is dead. I sit still gazing at Dr. Imoke sunk in thoughts.

“He said I should hand this over to you before he passed on.” Dr. Imoke gives me Iyke’s gold chain, the very chain Ifeanyi his older brother had given to him, which has a cross pendant and the image of Jesus hanging on the cross. “One more thing Mr. Shak, he said I should tell you to take care of Lizzy and let her know how much he loves her… and when he was drawing his last breath, he whispered that you should ensure that he is buried in a cemetery.”
I shut my eyes and fight back tears, but I just can’t. I break down weeping aloud.
“That’s obviously a weird request for a young man of his age…doesn’t he have a family?”
“He does,” I sob.
“Well, I’d be willing to attest to his uh – will in case there’s a controversy about that okay.”
“Thank you Dr. Imoke.”
“How is the girl doing?”
“I don’t know, she’s sleeping. Do you think she’ll be alright doctor?”
“She will. If she’s sleeping it’s a good sign she’s responding to the drugs already. I’ll come over myself to check on her in an hour or so. I’m sorry about your friend, Mr. Shak.”

I don’t have a problem with the doctor as far as Iyke’s death is concerned. How could he be so unreasoning as to believe that God, or whoever he referred to as the Father, was going to send people to help him from above? Where is the survival instincts for which disciples of the inverted cross are known? How could he have practically given himself wilfully to die like this?

I glance scornfully at the portrait on the wall on my way to the private ward to check up on Lizzy, and then the image on the pendant. How could he have believed that this man who died on the cross could have delivered him from the Hangman’s crucifixion parade?

She’s resting her back on the pillow sitting up on the bed when I open the door, and she’s awake. She follows me with an intent look as I totter to the side of the bed and sit in the chair. I know she’s anxiously trying to figure out what has happened to Iyke from the expression on my face.
“He’s dead, isn’t he?” she asks me as I sit down on the chair next to her bed.
I do not respond. She stares blankly into space.
“You knew that this was going to happen, didn’t you?”
Tears trickle down her cheeks.
“How did you know about it?” I quiz her further.
“When we were coming from the hostel after praying for my friend, he began to talk funny,” she sobs, “But I didn’t realize what it meant until now.”
“What did he say, Lizzy?”
“Unless a grain of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed, but if it dies, it produces many seeds,” she says and gathers herself.
“A grain of wheat falls to the ground, it remains a single seed, but…” I pause, “You think he was speaking about his death?”

“Jesus used that parable to illustrate how he was going to die. And show us the pattern of life his disciples should lead. A life of sacrifice, even to the point of death,” she hesitates, “But I think he meant something else when he spoke those words to me.”
“You think he was foretelling his death?”

“Yes. He also asked me to take care of his things when he’s not around. I never thought what that could mean until I took you to see him yesterday…he was groaning in prayer asking God to help him fulfil His will.”
I lean back on the chair and exhale deeply. “If you had told me about this yesterday, we could have averted this. I would have alerted the police about it and –”
“I’m not sure we would have been able to stop this from happening, Bitty,” she snaps, “Jshap has a unique relationship with God. If He willed to save his life, He wouldn’t need our help anyway.”
“I can imagine how powerful He is,” I chuckle derisively, “If I didn’t know Iyke so well, Lizzy, I would have concluded that you guys have lost it.”
“You’ll be surprised you know so little about this person you call your friend.”
There is a knock at the door. Lizzy and I turn to the door at the same time as it swings open.
“Ah there you are. You’re talking so loudly already,” Dr. Imoke says with a cheerful tone as he steps in. “So how are you feeling?”
“I’m fine Dr. Imoke, good morning,” Lizzy beams back at him.
“It’s a good morning indeed, and I hope Mr. Shak has told you we’d cherish your company for at least one week?” he says jokingly.

“No, no, no I know you want to tell me you’re alright,” he cuts in on Lizzy, “You see the hospital is such a lonely place, so whenever we have such nice people like you around, we don’t want to discharge them in a hurry.” We all share his joke with laughter as he does the routine check. Then he asks me to excuse them for a while.

Dr. Imoke is trained in the Lincoln University. Although he’s in his late twenties, from the framed photograph in his office, he has a family of five already. His wife is also a medical doctor and they jointly run the hospital. He happily told me they met each other while in college.

Although we’ve called it quits, Lillian and I talked about having three kids as well. But considering my position as an heir apparent, that was tentative, the number could rise to a dozen or more depending on whether we’re able to have a male child because I definitely have to raise an heir to the throne after me. But that is history now.

My father has eighteen other children from three wives, which is beneath the record of his own father, whom he succeeded as king, who reared twenty-seven from seven wives. In my thinking, it is not for the love of the women that they married so many wives. Perhaps it’s a way of life for a king to have multiple wives and plenty of children. It was one of the things that Lillian complained about when I sampled her opinion about getting married to a prince. I can’t say much about loving a woman, but my relationship with Lillian caused me to really think over the idea of polygamy.

chapters.title: Chapter FIVE
content: The precinct caucus of the Grand Order of Dukes ought to have come to bury their own and pay their last respect to Iyke since he attained the status of an eminent duke having served as star bearer, but because he died an apostate of the Inverted Cross Fraternity, following his profession of faith in Jesus, the only member of the Order that accompanied Dike here is Nosa, the cross bearer.

Apart from Iyke’s uncle, Dike, I am probably the only one in this cemetery who has an inkling of the reason behind Iyke’s choice of having his body buried here in a cemetery. And apart from Ifeanyi, the entire family is present at his funeral. Nkeonyere is here with her husband and their three children. There is also a sizeable host of well wishers and Iyke’s friends from school present here, especially folks from his church.

When I informed Iyke’s father, the Right Reverend Joseph Obiajulu about his will to be buried in the cemetery instead of his family compound as it is the custom of the Igbos, he didn’t object to it but wondered what could have been his reason for choosing to be buried in a cemetery rather than customarily, in his compound. ‘Well, Jehoshaphat has gone to be with the Lord, should we then begin to agonize about where his body will be buried,’ he said with resignation.

Wailing voices of the mammoth crowd standing beside his grave rises as the officiating priest emphasises the fact of Jehoshaphat Ikenna Obiajulu’s demise reading out loudly a text in the book of Isaiah chapter fifty-seven, “The righteous perish and no one ponders it in his heart. Devout men are taken away and no one understands that the righteous are taken away to be spared from evil. Those who walk uprightly enter into peace. They find rest as they lie in death.”

Dike is standing next to Iyke’s father. I can tell he’s absentminded from the funeral from his fastened scowl on me. I stare back at him from the sunglasses I’m putting on. Lizzy notices he’s eyeing me and asks me who he is, I whisper back to her.

Iyke’s parents are devastated by his death. His mother weeps uncontrollably as his corpse is interred. Nkeonyere tries to control her with the assurance that he has gone to paradise. But the old woman weeps the more loudly. Lizzy, the only one who had inkling of his death is so shocked she hasn’t uttered a word since his interment. Her unsteady, blinking eyes tells me she’s probably wondering if she did the right thing not acting on time to save his life. She’s obviously overwhelmed with the regret of her conspiracy of silence that led to his death, I’m sure. Dike smiles lightly and walks away from the graveside as soon as the undertakers start covering his grave with sand.
No matter where they think he’s gone, heaven or hell, Iyke doesn’t deserve to die like this. Where is the honour he had as the hero of the disciples of the Inverted Cross Fraternity?

When the mood is less tense, Nkeonyere introduces me to her husband and tells him how close Iyke and I had been. He commends my efforts towards saving his life and for undertaking the funeral. And says I’ve proved to be his friend truly. I also introduce Lizzy to Nkeonyere, as Iyke’s friend. Dike shows up again and excuses me for a private chat. I leave Lizzy in their company.

I never really fancied Iyke’s uncle, Dike, and until I returned the items that I received from Iyke, I never crossed his path. I started avoiding him like a plague when Iyke revealed the secret behind the power of the testament to me.
“I can see you’re enjoying a lot of accolade around here,” Dike tells me as we both amble towards the gate of the cemetery, “Doesn’t it remind you of something?”
“What?” I reply.
“Do I need to take you back?”
“I don’t understand what you’re saying.”

“The applause I gave you guys back in the Metro when you tamed those assholes who had the erection problem, have you forgotten?” he takes a pause bringing out a packet of cigarette from his breast pocket, “You’re stealing my show again Bitty.”
“Am I? Well, I guess your evil powers couldn’t save him after all,” I say in a gruff tone.
Wearing a mischievous smile, he takes a stick of cigarette and shows me the pack. “You care for a cigarette?”
“No thanks.”
“You quit smoking?”
“I’m not in the mood.”

“That was what earned him his freedom from jail, did he tell you? I worked out a perfect alibi for him and got a smart lawyer to prove that he never smoked a cigarette.” His lips thin into a smug smile. “He’s very fortunate not to have picked up that habit from you otherwise he would have been languishing in prison by now.”
I take a glance at him without saying a word.

“There are two things that rule this world Bloody Sniper,” he continues, “Money…and power. Those who work hard make money, but authority is given to a privileged few that are courageous and willing to take advantage of its rare opportunities. That’s where both of you differ. You’re a coward.”
“I don’t regret abdicating my position as Hangman, if that’s what you’re referring to.”
“Of course not,” he replies with sarcasm, “Women are the one thing that destroy men of authority. I warned him about it…but you got him ensnared into it.”
“What are you talking about?” I reply angrily.
“Were it not for your incontinence, he would have been alive today.”
“Excuse me Mr. Dike.”

“I sent someone to privately investigate the circumstances of Sephia’s death immediately I heard about it. Guess what he discovered?” he asks rhetorically, “The girl was burnt with cigarette butt, the brand was Camel. And a packet of Camel cigarette was also lying on the floor. There’s only one person known to habitually smoke Camel cigarette, who was also close to Iyke and who also knew Sephia,” he says with a wicked smile on his face.
I scowl at him without saying anything.

“I’ll keep this between us, if you are willing to return the book to me…otherwise…I’ll put you exactly where you put him, but I can assure you that for the same reason he was acquitted, you’ll be convicted,” he takes a pause and drags his cigarette, “What do you say, Bloody Sniper?”
“Are you trying to blackmail me or what?”

“What an insult! Anyway, I didn’t call you out to trade words with you. You rejected the position that naturally fell on you and returned the mace. But you lied to me about the book that should be in your custody, which is the property of the GODs.”
“Why didn’t you ask your nephew about the book? He was the one you gave the book, wasn’t he?”
“He said he gave the testament to you,” he replies.
“You’re a bloody liar. Iyke couldn’t have told you so. And may I use this opportunity to let you know that I am no longer a part of your diabolical group and I have nothing that belongs to you.”

“You know that’s impossible…blood in blood out, you remember?” he asks rhetorically with a wicked smile etched on his face, “I’m going to employ every resource within my power to make sure you comply.”
“I promise you that if I ever find it, I’ll destroy it before you or any other member of the Order lay hands on it,” I say with a strong tone of voice.
Dike chuckles, and then frowns and says, “I can see you have decided to tread the path of your friend. You’ll soon end up like him, Bloody Sniper.”
“That’s not my name,” I retort.

“I give you forty-eight hours to return the testament or I’ll hand you over to the police. Don’t think you’ve escaped judgment for the death of Sephia Rwang. Imagine what a scandal it would be that the Crown prince is a murderer…your people will be so ashamed, they’ll disown you as their king.”
“Over my dead body,” I growl.
“Then you shall surely die,” he says with finality and walks away. Nosa hastens his steps and joins him. They both walk to the gate leaving the cemetery.

I find a note slipped into my room from underneath the door when I open the door. I expect it’s from Dike or one of his boys, but it is not, it’s Lizzy. She was here in my absence. She complains about my abandoning her after Iyke’s burial, and asks if I’d be free to escort her to the cemetery to lay a wreath on his grave tomorrow.

After the funeral, Lizzy was compunction-ridden over Iyke’s death and kept whining that it was Ugo’s mounting envy over her relationship with Iyke that prompted him to kill him when he could no longer contain it, and avers that if she hadn’t been in the room that night, Ugo wouldn’t have had the compulsion to shoot him. I quite agreed with Lizzy’s analysis that Ugo’s action was motivated by jealousy. And she’s at the centre of it all. There’s an aphorism among frat boys that nine out of ten cult aggros are instigated by a tussle for babes.
However, I stopped visiting her when she almost constrained me to tell her the real reason he chose to be buried in the cemetery.

I had intended to travel to Jos tomorrow to see my parents briefly and then go back to London since my affair with Lillian has packed up. I’ve also booked a flight to London in three days. I want to let my father know that I have decided to suspend the issue of marriage indefinitely and that if an heir is so urgently needed, someone else can take my place.

I’ve seriously considered the motive of my relationships and I’m beginning to feel that it’s cynical for me to marry a woman just because I want to inherit a throne. More than once I’ve been infatuated, only to discover later that I never really loved those ladies. And even though I can’t assert whether I’m in love or whether a woman loves me, it hasn’t changed my opinion about its importance in a marriage relationship. Maybe one day, I’ll find a woman who truly loves me, a woman who will make me understand what love is.
She sounds affable from the tone of her note, and I think she’s gotten over her grief. I’ll postpone my proposed trip till some other time and escort her to the cemetery.
“Good morning Bitty.”
“Morning Lizzy, how are you today?”
“I’m fine. I’m glad you could come,” she beams, “I hope I’m not bothering you with this?”
“Oh, small thing,” I reply, “I’d do more than this for Iyke’s sake.”
“Thanks.”
“Well, uh – I’m sorry I abandoned you since our friend was buried.”
“I didn’t mean that, Bitty,” she giggles. “I was only kidding.”
“So are we going right away?”
“Yes, only I have to collect flowers I booked yesterday. The florist is on our way to the cemetery.”
“No wahala,” I reply casually and turn on the ignition.

The feeling, as I expect, is quite different from what it was the first time we met. But beyond my expectation, Lizzy is quite knowledgeable and vast in fields you wouldn’t expect such level of awareness of a woman. She talks freely about herself – where she’s strong and her weakness, her hobbies and her ambition. But she still refrains from talking about her relationship with Iyke.
“I’m surprised to hear that you’ve not been to his grave since he was buried,” I tell her as we walk to his grave.
“I was afraid of coming here alone,” she mutters.
“What are you afraid of? You think he’s going to jump out from the grave or something?”
“I saw him in my dream yesterday, Bitty. He – he rebuked me for abandoning him. That’s why I came looking for you.”

“So that’s where you got that word?” I say with a smile to suggest that I don’t mean to denigrate her. “You don’t have to be afraid, I’m right here with you. I’ll push him back into the grave if he tries to pop his head out.”

Lizzy obviously didn’t find that amusing. In spite of my chatter to allay her fear as we walk through the tombstones, she walks straight-faced to the grave with the least attention to my stories. When we’re a few metres from the grave, she suddenly slows her pace and then halts, staring frightfully and fixedly ahead of her.
“What is it now Lizzy?” Then I follow her gaze to Iyke’s tomb. There is an imposing bronze cross lying on the grave glittering in the sunlight. “Jeez! What the –”
“What is that?” she asks.
“We got to get out of here!” I turn to leave.
“I’m not leaving until I lay my wreath,” she protests.
I take the bouquet from Lizzy, and rush to his grave and drop it next to the bronze cross observing it for a while. She comes close to see the cross.
“Now let’s get out of here before somebody sees us.”
“Excuse me!” she drawls.
I take hold of her hand firmly and drag her along to the car. “Are you satisfied now?” I ask her as I speed off.
“You hurt my arm, Bitty.”
“I’m sorry. I don’t want you to come back here again okay.”
“What the hell is going on, who placed the cross on his grave?”
I remain silent.
“I want to go back to the cemetery and pray at his graveside.”
I take a glance at her. “Will you stop this bullshit religion, Iyke is dead.”
“I’m going back there,” she demands with her eyes probing for an explanation.
I ignore her.
“Stop the car!”
I overlook her protest and fire on. She pulls the door handle and pushes the door open.
“What!” I bark, “What the hell is wrong with you?”
“Stop the car!” she shouts back.
I slow down and pull over.
She steps down and starts walking back the way we came.
“Lizzy stop,” I say pulling her hand.
“Don’t touch me.” She continues walking.
“Okay, let me explain.”
“Who placed that cross on his grave?”
“The cross was laid by the GODs.”
She halts and turns to me. “Excuse me. Did I hear you correctly?”
“Yes, the GODs, gee o dee ess, GODs.”
“I know the spelling of gods. Iyke has only one God, no other deity or gods has the right to lay claim of his body, nobody.”
“The Grand Order of Dukes. It’s a cult to which Iyke belongs…or belonged.”
Lizzy squints at me in shock.

“The bronze cross is a wreath of honour laid on the tomb of the principal officers of the Order. Traditionally, the wreath is laid at midnight by the next inferior officer three days after he is buried, in this case, the Cardinal or head of the Inverted Cross Fraternity in the university where he’s buried.”

Lizzy turns around walks slowly back to the car and shut the door, and asks me to take her back home. “So, this is what he wants to show me in the dream…the cross,” Lizzy mutters, “I know who placed the cross on his grave.”
“You do?”
“Yes. If it is the Cardinal’s responsibility to lay the wreath, I think I know who it might be.”
“Well, whoever it is, he has just bought himself a ticket to join him.”
“Why do you say so?”
“The cross is pointing the wrong way. The cross ought to be laid in an inverted position from the tomb head.”
“It might have been a mistake considering it was placed at midnight?”

“People don’t make such mistakes with the GODs, Lizzy,” I say chuckling, “He ought to have known the dire consequence of placing the wreath in that direction. It amounts to recanting the oath of allegiance to the fraternity…I think he did it deliberately,” I say in a low tone.
“Inverted cross,” Lizzy mutters and remains mute until we reach her house.
“Are you sure you know this guy…the Cardinal?”
“Yes I do.” She pulls the door handle. “Goodbye Bitty…I’m sorry to have bothered you.”
“I did bother you once, and I’ve not stopped regretting doing that.”
“It’s okay Bitty. You know, I’ve come to really believe that it was good for us to be there that night. Don’t you think so?”
“Well, maybe you’re right.”
“And don’t run away again,” she says jokingly.
“I won’t.”
“Promise?”
“I promise.”
“Bye,” she whispers.

On my way back to the hotel, I ponder over the cross wreath. And the mystery called Iyke. My mind goes back to the first day we talked about the Inverted Cross Fraternity, and the night we visited Dreck and…I get goose pimples and twitch.

chapters.title: Chapter SIX
content: There are many ways to tell a woman that you love her – either you say it verbally in a genteel tone, or you tell her silently in printed words by a greeting card. But I consider those too direct an approach for a lady I had hardly hinted I have such affection for.

The gift of a bouquet will be an appropriate way to express my feelings for Lizzy, although it’s not a traditional way of showing affection in this part of the world where most people are unaware that flowers are nurtured for such purposes. The closest many of us come to relating with plants is either using them for decorations or nutrition, grown in the garden as edible vegetables. In this part of the world, flowers are seldom appreciated as gift items.
Like those who hear and interpret the language of the African talking drum, very few still have the eye to recognize them and interpret the language of flowers.
Lizzy flaunted an exceptional knowledge of flowers that surprised the florist when she selected by herself the flowers for the bouquet we took to the cemetery. I was taken aback myself.

In order not to embarrass myself or send the wrong message, I take the pains to describe my relationship with Lizzy and my intention and ask the florist to select a bouquet that is most appropriate to convey my feeling for her.

From the rear-view mirror I notice a red Toyota Camry has been on my tail since I left the hotel. I spotted the car as I crossed the street to the florist’s and also noticed there are three men in the car. Switching lanes instantly, I speed up and abruptly turn into a busy one-way street deliberately going against the direction of traffic in order to shake them off my tail. When I burst into the major road connecting Lizzy’s Close and look up at the mirror again I sight the red Camry behind a few cars caught up in a four-way traffic light with its pointer indicating it’s going right. I slow my speed and watch carefully as it takes the right turn, then I continue to Lizzy’s place.

Apprehensive that they’d spot the metallic grey Peugeot 406, I park the car inside a fenced compound next to Lizzy’s. Unaware that Lizzy’s peeping at me from the window, I watch my back as I walk briskly to her apartment. The door swings open before I can get to knock.
“Hi Lizzy.”
“Hi,” she beams, “Wow! These are beautiful.”
“I uh – I thought of something to shake off your grief for the loss of our friend…so I uh…”
“How nice of you,” she enthuses, takes it from me and ushers me into the room. “Thank you so much Bitty.”
“You’re welcome.”

Lizzy stays in a one room self-contained apartment, a little too lavishly furnished for a student. The aspect of the room tells a lot about her disposition. The room is neatly arranged and it appears virtually everything is in a perfect, well-situated position.
“Please have a seat…oh this is beautiful.” She smells the flowers again.
I’m sure she understands exactly what I have in mind.
“You were watching your back as you were coming, were you suspecting someone to be following you?”
“Some chaps were on my tail on my way here, but I lost them.”

“I hope so,” she says casually, “If they’re of Ugo’s gang, they probably already know you are coming here. The young boy who sells at the kiosk at the end of our close told me they gave him your description and asked him to watch out for you.”
I sit down quietly.
“Don’t worry, he gave me his word, he won’t tell them anything,” she says as she put the bouquet in a vase, “But what exactly do they want, Bitty?”
“Well uh – I don’t know,” I reply evasively.
“It has to do with that cross, doesn’t it?”
More than that, but I’m not going to feed her curiosity. “Maybe,” I reply.
“So what do I offer you, Bitty, what would you like to drink?” she asks me as she admires the flower in the vase.
“A brandy will be alright.”
“I’m sorry I don’t have a brandy.”
“Then I wouldn’t mind a bottle of Star in that case.”
“I uh – actually Bitty I can’t offer you any alcoholic beverage. I’m sorry,” she replies politely.

Now I get it – it’s because of her faith. I remember my days in the university when some fanatic Christian students came to preach to us about Jesus. We would argue for hours about whether it was good or bad to drink alcoholic beverages, and whose responsibility it was to decide – the priest, the medical doctor or the indulger. We also used to argue about the sufficient quantity of the substance one should take. As often as we bickered about it, we gained the edge. I also gained the knowledge that there are contradictions in the Book as to whether it is God’s command to absolutely abstain from alcoholic beverages or not.

Although I later stopped arguing about it when I realised that truly my argument was provoked by self-condemnation rather than logic, because deep down, I really hoped I could kick the habit, for health reasons. Certainly, I can do without brandy or even a beer, for her sake. I wouldn’t dare argue with Lizzy about it, nor do anything that’ll spoil our nascent relationship. “Then I can manage a Coca cola,” I resign.
Although it’s only a few minutes past seven, she’s obviously dressed for the night. She’s wearing a pink satin nightdress.
“You stay here alone?” I ask as she serves me the drink.
“Yes,” she hesitates, “Actually I was living with a friend, but she died few months ago.”
“Oh, I’m sorry. What happened, was she sick?”
“No…she died.” The tone of her voice tells me she doesn’t want to discuss her late friend.
“I can imagine what you’ve been through losing two people very close to you within a short period…I was thinking maybe you would like to go out. I feel like spoiling you a little.”
“I appreciate your offer Bitty, but I’m not a very outgoing person,” she replies with short laughter, “Besides I doubt whether we share a common place of interest.”
“Well, there’s only one way to find out.”
“Don’t say. I may consider your invitation during the day, but not this night.”
“Okay, okay. But don’t give me the impression you’re an introvert,” I beam, “That’s the part you didn’t tell me about you.”

“About me?” she replies pointing at her chest, “There’s nothing about me that’ll interest you, Bitty. Let’s talk about Iyke instead,” she says emphasizing his name to indicate she wants to know more about the old Jehoshaphat.

“Do you know what amazes me about you guys? The tremendous influence he has over you. Why don’t you tell me about you and Iyke, I mean, how did you guys meet?” I’m not particularly interested in their romance, but I’m hoping that I’ll be able to decipher what makes ladies so attracted to Iyke.
She first let out a deep breath and replies, “It was Ugo that actually brought us together.”
“Wait a minute, Ugo, Ugo,” I whisper clicking my fingers as I try to remember. “That’s the guy that attacked him that night?”

“Exactly,” she replies and leans back at the chair, “I was having a party that night. Ugo obliged to help me organize things. He asked me to accompany him to his house to freshen up and change for the party so I followed him. He was living with Jehoshaphat then…that was how we first met. But something happened that night,” she hesitates, “While I was leaving the house with Ugo to return to the hotel, I passed out in his car.”
“You fainted?”
“No…I died,” she says.
“What do you mean you died?” I chuckle.

“A strange thought of death had been hunting me at that time. I had been to the psychiatrist and visited some spiritualists, but none of them could help me.” Lizzy squint her eyes fixedly at a book on the table. “I persuaded him to follow us to the party, but he declined my invitation.” Then she smiles as if she just remembered something amusing. “It was when Ugo despitefully called him a son of God, and teased me about trying to tempt him to sin that I felt strongly about telling him about my fears so I asked him if I could come and see him some other time for prayer…that was the last thing I remembered.

“When I came around, I noticed I was lying down on the floor in the sitting room…and Jehoshaphat was praying for me,” she pauses and swallows hard, “I was afraid to look into his eyes, they were fiery and crimson red in colour and his muscular body was sticking to the light shirt he was wearing from sweat …he called me back to life. Then he asked me right away if I would like to surrender my life to Jesus so He could be my Lord and Saviour. At that point, Bitty, I was willing to do anything to live. But I was filled with loathe because of what I’d subjected myself to in search of help,” she takes a pause, shut her eyes and purse her lips in disgust, “A native doctor said I was an Abiku, and that I was going to die at eighteen.

“He told me with confidence that Jesus had paid the ransom for my life, and if I would confess Him as my Lord, I would be saved. He spoke with such assurance that my unbelief cowered before him. He asked me to confess my sins to God in order to receive forgiveness in the name of Jesus. Three times, native doctors, who promised to help me reverse the curse, had abused me but yet the thought still lingered. He offered me this hope, and demanded nothing from me…not even a dime, and certainly, unlike those fraudsters, he didn’t take advantage of me in any way.” Lizzy shut her eyes and breathes heavily.

“This is hard to believe, Lizzy.” My mind goes back to the portrait that I saw in the hospital, the writing that I am the resurrection and the life. “If God indeed answered his prayer and raised you back to life, why –”

“Couldn’t God prevent him from dying?” she cuts in beaming at me as if she has the answer to my question, “You made me ask God the same question at the hospital…why, why did God allow it?” she shrugs her shoulder as if she’s resigning from the thought. Then she speaks in a low tone of voice, “I didn’t get it until we went to the grave the other day.”
“What about the grave?”
“First there’s something I’d like to ask you, Bitty. And please give me an honest answer.”
“I will, provided it has nothing to do with the fraternity.”
“Did he know somebody, not necessarily Ugo was going to come for him someday?”
I stare at her for a while to convince myself about responding to her question.
“Tell me the truth, Bitty.”
I know what her next question will be and I promise myself not to answer it. “Yes.”
“Thank you.”
I’m completely taken aback. “Is that all you wanted to know?”
“But you’ve not answered my question. What has what happened at the grave got to do with why God allowed him to die?”
“Didn’t you say he personally requested to be buried in the cemetery?” she asks.
“Yes that’s what Doctor Imoke said,” I reply.
“It’s because of the wreath he chose to be buried in the cemetery, wasn’t it?”
“I don’t know.”

“Although it sounds blasphemous for me to say that God allowed me to live the life I led in the past, but I really think he foresaw this and included me in the inverted cross equation,” she says casting her probing eyes on me. “I know some things about the Inverted Cross Fraternity…perhaps too many things about the fraternity.”
If she intends to draw me into a conversation about the fraternity, I’m definitely not up to it.
“You were with Jshap right from the start Bitty. Was he the one that wrote the testament?”
“You don’t want to know that?”
“Of course I do,” she insists.
“Then I won’t tell you.”
“If only you know what I was offered to get this information from you?”
I freeze and stare at her. “What? Who offered you?”
“Dike, Jehoshaphat’s uncle. He is willing to pay me as much as three million Naira to make me get information where the testament is.”
“Did you – did you accept the offer?”
“Of course I didn’t, or else I won’t be telling you,” she chuckles, “Steel brought him to me.”
“And who’s Steel?”
“That’s the Cardinal’s name. If I were offered one fifth of that before I met Jehoshaphat, I wouldn’t have turned them back. And I would have succeeded in making you tell me.”
I squint at her.
“I’ll never ask you if you have it or where you kept it,” she whispers, “But you have to be careful. He’s desperate about it and I think I know why.”
My silence and my squint are well-read.
“I think his death shook the power of the GODs. That’s why God allowed his death, to destroy the power of the testament. And that’s why he willingly accepted to die.”
“How did you come to that conclusion?”
“It’s not a conclusion yet. There are still too many questions to be answered.”
We stare at each other in silence.
“Do you know the meaning of the name Jehoshaphat?” she continues.
“How on earth will I know that, am I a Jew?”

“The Lord our judge,” she answers, “If he was sentenced to death by the law written in the testament, the cross laid on his grave demonstrates that the Almighty has judged the fraternity and broken the covenant of death witnessed by the testament.”

Lizzy’s calmness obscures her voracious appetite for facts. Her analysis is not only logical, but also amazingly profound. I have a feeling she wants to dig into the circumstances surrounding Iyke’s death, and more facts about the fraternity to consolidate her analysis, but she won’t get it from me.

I made up my mind the very day I left school not to have anything to do with the fraternity, relate with anybody who is a member or even discuss its affairs ever again. And I’m definitely not going to be insinuated into chasing after uncovering the mystery behind Iyke’s death.

chapters.title: Chapter SEVEN
content: Apart from her beauty and brain, the most striking quality I’ve come to discover lately about Lizzy is the oddity of her knowledge of clandestine activities. As I reflect over her well-thought-out analysis of the incident, while driving back to the hotel, I admire her even more. Being conscious of her warning to be careful with Dike, I glide through the car park to check whether the red Camry that trailed me when I was going to her house is here. After making sure I’m not followed, I hide my rented car at a corner in the car park and stride into the hotel.

When I was leaving to see Lizzy, the front office staff that booked me in recognized that I’m a member of the royal family when he heard my surname and accorded me the appropriate respect. He thought I’d be interested in seeing a musical group from Jos that’ll be performing a live concert in-house and asked if I’d like him to make reservation for me. I consented and asked him to book two seats. I was hoping to come in the company of Lizzy. However, I warned him not to reveal my identity to anyone.

My experience with Lillian has made me conscious of exposing my identity, especially to a musical group, because of their practice of praise singing. Besides I’m not so particular about folk songs anyway, and since my intention of bringing Lizzy along didn’t come through, I’m not keen about the show anymore.

Unlike folk songs in developed countries that extol heroes who stood for some laudable cause such as the independence of the people, their liberty, justice and so on, praise singing has become the order of the day for most materialistic local musicians who pay tribute to people they judge as very important personalities in the society, mostly politicians and well-to-do businessmen and women, identified by nicknames and chieftaincy or social titles.

And because these folk singers make celebrities of these moneybags who in most cases have never done any humanitarian or noble deed, it is fast becoming a trend for those conscious of fame, and a benchmark for determining how rich and popular one is. Much of our traditional rhythm has been sacrificed as folk songwriters now take to making money from these status-minded VIPs at the expense of making music.

They’d wax lyrics for any charlatan, who could afford their services, whether he or she has looted the treasury, liquidated a bank, or whether he does dope or runs guns, they’ll sing his praise so far he has the wad. Some of them are contracted to pay tribute to social clubs and popular fraternities to whom they are affiliated.
If these folks get the hint that the Guna Gunu of Gunkun is in the house, they’ll conjure up a song for me on the spot and look forward to receiving a handsome reward at the end of their performance.
“Welcome your excellency!” he greets me with a grin giving me the key to my room. “Your excellency, you have visitors.”
“Visitors?” I ask surprisingly.
“Yes two men came looking for you about twenty minutes after you left. They said they had an appointment with you. They said they’d be in the concert and asked me to book them next to your table.”

I take my tickets from him and proceed to the Capital hall, the venue of the concert. The receptionist is bemused to find that my table is still empty quite alright, but married couples occupy the tables around it and I’m not acquainted with any of them neither is any of them looking for me. And they’re certainly not the people he sold the tickets to.
“They probably sold their tickets to the couple,” I suggest.
He nods in agreement.
“You can give these away too,” I say giving my tickets back to him.
“But, your Excellency –”
“Please, stop calling me your Excellency. My name is Bitrus, okay, Bitrus, not your Excellency, do you understand?”
“Yes your Excellency.”
I shake my head and leave him.

As soon as I insert my key into the hole to open the door, I notice that the door has been forced open and the key damaged. My room has been ransacked, the mattress has been shifted from the bed frame and the bedspread rumpled, the wardrobe is open and my cloths are lying on the floor. My briefcase is open. My passport and travel documents are missing.
“Oh my God,” I mutter.

The hotel management apologizes for what happened and promises to invite the police to investigate the theft in order to track down the culprits. But I lost faith in the police after Julia’s incident, besides, I’m not eager to get the police involved so I tell them to forget it. I’ll file for a new passport.

She turns the light off and peeps through the window facing the road. The visitor knocks on the door again and Lizzy answers. He introduces himself as a policeman and hands her an envelope. She opens it and peruses the content, and then she gives it to me. It’s an invitation by the police for her to come to the station and collect some of Iyke’s valuables, mostly books, which was removed from the house after the fatal attack.
“What do you think?” Lizzy asks.
“The tone of the letter is suspect,” I reply.

The police sealed Iyke’s former apartment on the presumption that the incident is being investigated. But after reading the brief from the police, I wonder why they had to remove his books during the investigation in the first place.

I suspect the purported investigation was masterminded by Dike – he may have lobbied the police to seal the house so his men can search the house thoroughly for the book. But, I know my friend Iyke; he won’t be so stupid as to keep such a material in his house.
“Can I ask you a favour, Bitty? Will you escort me to the police station tomorrow?” she asks.
“I’m occupied,” I reply flatly. I know Dike is behind this. He wants to use her as bait to track me.

She sits quietly next to me staring curiously at me. She’s getting more interested in the affairs of the fraternity and it’s not only making me nervous, but I regret bringing her into this. The way she’s going about this she may get hurt or even killed.
“Lizzy, promise me that after your visit to the police station you’ll stay out of this.”
She remains silent.
“Things are not the way they appear on the surface Lizzy…the cult is too complicated for you to fully understand. This whole thing can get really nasty.”
“I can’t make that promise,” she replies bluntly, “It can’t get any nastier.”

“What do you want…to die? Listen to me Lizzy, there’s no doubt you probably know more about the fraternity than any other babe, but that doesn’t make you a cult member. What do you think, that we’re shooting a movie scene or something?” I say chuckling, “Things can get out of hand, and if that happens, you could get yourself hurt or even lose your life as Iyke did.”
“Have you considered what will happen if Dike by chance stumbles on the book?”
“You’re using that as an excuse, you know they’ll never find it,” I reply.
“You can never tell, Bitty. That’s why we need to work ahead of them.”
“We,” I giggle.

“I’m surprised at your attitude, Bitty. You act like you don’t care what happens to a multitude whose lives and future will be jeopardized because of your atrocious fraternity. Well, I’ve decided not to allow the purpose of his death be in vain and if I perish, I perish.”

Persuaded by Lizzy’s words, I eventually changed my mind about coming to the police station, although she’s not aware that I’m here. Without her knowledge, I replaced the Peugeot 406 with an old model Volkswagen Golf that would attract the least attention since Dike’s men have marked my former car. I also tinted the glasses to avoid being noticed. I watch the station from where I’m parked a few metres away from the police station across the road. I arrived at exactly one o’clock, one hour ahead of the time Lizzy was asked to report and positioned myself under the shade of a tree that gives me a perfect view of the entrance into the police station.

The red Camry drives past me and stops in front of the station. There are two people in the car. The one sitting in the passenger’s side opens the door and alights, he looks to his left and to his right. He’s searching for the 406, I guess (they’re probably expecting I’d be coming here with Lizzy). Then he walks hurriedly into the police station, while the driver moves the car to a close distance to park. He also steps out of the car and enters the station.

Apart from the noise from the police station, the street is quiet and deserted. We sit quietly in the cars waiting for the bait. I check the time – it’s ten minutes to two. After about five minutes, they reappear from the station walking towards the car. The first guy that entered the station is carrying a box. I believe that’s what the police invited Lizzy to come and receive.

Lizzy alights from a taxi at exactly two o’clock oblivious of my presence. They watch her from the Camry as she walks into the building. After a short while, she comes out of the station wearing a worried expression, but reluctant to leave. She looks at her watch, raises her head and slowly sweeps her eyes over the cars parked on the street. She stops at the red Camry and stares anxiously at it. Then she gathers herself and walks at a gentle pace back to the waiting taxi that brought her.

Few seconds after the taxi left, the Camry flashes its headlamps, honks only once and steers into the street following the taxi. Then, a black Cherokee that parked behind me when I changed my parking position roars and glides past me following the Camry. I would have fallen into their trap if I didn’t pay attention to decipher the signal.
I wait for another three minutes and then turn on the ignition and sneak out of the street as secretly as I came.

chapters.title: Chapter EIGHT
content: Lizzy calls me on the intercom at five minutes to nine and informs me that she’s waiting in the restaurant.

She is sitting gracefully at the table and tastefully dressed in an emerald-green skirt suit that gives her fair delicate skin a golden look. She’s also wearing a chequered cotton scarf on her neck that blends with her handbag.
“Hi Lizzy.”
“Good morning Bitty,” she replies looking up from the magazine she’s reading.
“I’m sorry I kept you waiting,” I apologize and sit down.
“It was a good decision for you to leave the hotel after what happened.”
After losing my travel documents in Quintessence Hotel, I relocated to Sheraton Hotels and Towers in Ikeja to keep away from Dike until the immigration issues me another passport.
“I also changed my car. Do you know you were followed from the police station?”
She squints her eyes at me.
“Yeah, I was at the police station,” I say responding to her suspicion. I take the hotel menu on the table and glance through it.
“You said you weren’t coming.”
“I changed my mind.” I close the menu and look straight into her eyes. “You know who they are, don’t you?”
“The smaller one is the guy I told you about, Steel, the Cardinal and the other Raphael…it’s you they actually want Bitty. I think they’re using me as bait.”
“I know.”
She opens the magazine, brings out a notebook and tosses it to me. “I found this among the things they returned.”
The title of the notebook is, my prison memoir. There are signs that the notebook had been used frequently, the edge of the pages are dog-eared.
“I suppose you’ve read the memoir. Is there something you want to show me?”
We silently wait for the waiter who has come to serve our meal to finish and leave us.
“Well, there is, but I don’t know if this place is appropriate to discuss it,” she replies.
“Shall we,” I ask her pointing at our meal on the table.

She bows her head slightly with her eyes closed. I watch her as she whispers a prayer to God blessing the food we’re about to eat. My thought goes back to the first time I sat at the table with Iyke in Julia’s house.
“Bitty!” she whispers.
I abandoned myself to the memory of Julia so deeply in the moment I didn’t realize she’s done praying.
“Are you alright?”
“Yeah,” I gasp gathering myself. “So, when are you going to stop this expedition and focus on something reasonable?”
“Reasonable?” she retorts, “You think I’m acting irrationally?”
“Iyke’s life is a long, complicated story that won’t do you any good, Lizzy,”
“You’re hiding something from me, I know. But I’m not going to ask you.”
“You think so.”
“I know so. And I’m going to figure it out.”
“Well, I’m beginning to fear your detective instincts. Consider it that I’m doing you a favour if I’m hiding anything from you.”
“No. I am doing you a favour.”
“If I tell you all you want to know about Iyke, you’d resent him.”
“You’re forgetting something Bitty. It was his prayer that brought me back to life. What do you think I’ll hear about him that’ll make me feel differently about him?”
“You think so?”
“I know so,” she says with a short laughter, “Agreed you and Iyke were very close…but, you know nothing about Jehoshaphat.”
“So it’s in the name, right?”
“More than that,” she quips.
“I wish you’d get to see things from my point of view.”
“I have a problem with your perception of things,” she replies.
The waiter returns with our main course. We wait quietly for him as he sets the table.
“This used to be Iyke’s favourite breakfast, broiled fish, bread and fresh milk. I’ve not had this again since the morning of the last valentine we spent together.”
“That’s the day Sephia died?”
My cutlery drops from my hands on the table. I cast a suspicious squint at her.
“He mentioned it in his memoir,” she says.
I take my glass of milk and sip from it.
“Do you have any idea who did it?”
Her question startles me and makes me spill the fluid on my shirt but she didn’t notice. “Of course not,” I reply curtly.
Lizzy takes her cutlery and cuts some fish and chews. “Hmm, tastes nice.”
“Try eating it together with bread, it’s tastier,” I say affecting a smile.
She cut bread and more fish and responds, “You’re right.” Then she sips from her glass of milk.
Although I’ve lost my appetite because of her question about who killed Sephia, I force myself to eat in order not to arouse her curiosity.
“Did you enjoy the meal?”
“Yes I did, thank you,” she replies with obvious relish.

We stare at each other without the slightest idea what to say to each other. Our eyes drop at the same time to the notebook lying on the table. I wish she doesn’t say anything about it, especially about Sephia.
“Can I ask you something personal, Bitty,” she pauses, “Did you ever have any remorse about the atrocities you guys committed while in school?”
“There is no one without a vice, Lizzy,” I giggle.

She stares fixedly at me almost squinting with anger. I don’t know the extent of our nocturnal clandestine activities Iyke revealed in his so-called memoir, but I’m not going to give in to the slightest allegation she may want to level against me, especially anything to do with the death of Sephia Rwang.
“Which of them are you particularly referring to?”

“It doesn’t matter. All sins are of equal weight in the sight of God regardless of how you refer to it, vice, weakness or shortcoming. I just want to know whether it ever occurred to you to ask God to forgive you for your atrocious acts.”

“If you are asking whether I had remorse about some of the things we did in school, I do,” I hesitate and drink water from my glass. Apparently unsatisfied with my answer, she scowls at me. “As a matter of fact, maybe I would have been a better person if I hadn’t met Iyke.”
“You make him sound like he was the devil,” she pauses, “Anyway, you can’t excuse yourself from God’s judgment.”
“Can I ask you a personal question, Lizzy?”
