She gazes impassively at me.
“Were you in love with him?”
“Of course I –”
“No, no, not that way Lizzy. You know what I mean…did you have a romantic feeling for him?”
Lizzy drops her eyes on the notebook on the table. “Why are you asking me this question?” she asks nervously.
“I’ll tell you if you answer me.”
“I can’t answer that question.”
“You have already done so,” I chuckle.
“Nothing will ever appear pure to you if your mind is impure,” she says angrily.
“Well, there’s something else he told Dr. Imoke before he passed away that I’ve kept it away from you…Iyke said to tell you that he loves you and he’ll always be with you.”
Her lips tighten and then shake uncontrollably as she tries to fight tears.
I take the notebook and flip through the pages. I’m not particularly interested in whatever is written in the book, but I just want to allow her gather herself.
“I wish he were still alive,” she emotes. “Will you take time to read the memoir?”

A mixed feeling of jealousy and resentment flushes over me. I thought she was driven by her desire to assert his love for her and that she’d be content to hear that he loves her and stop this meaningless escapade. But I guess I’m either misjudging the circumstances of their affair, or I’m underestimating the power of his love. Now she’s urging me to read his memoir.
I push the notebook back to her. “Can we go up to my suite and talk about this in detail.”
She looks at her watch.
“Don’t worry I’ll ask a chauffeur to take you back to school.”
She breathes heavily and says nothing. I think she’s reluctant.
I beckon on the waiter to come. “Please credit room 225.”
“That won’t be necessary sir, your food has already been paid for,” the waiter replies.
“That’s not possible. You must be mistaken.”

“The man who ordered me to serve you paid the bill. He was sitting over there.” He points at the next table to ours. Whoever sat there, although I didn’t notice anyone sitting next to us, also ate bread and fish and left fragments of both in the plate, and an empty glass of milk.
When I turn to Lizzy, my suspicious squint meets her eyes. “You weren’t the one who ordered the meal?”
“No, I thought you did,” she replies.
I glance at the plate one more time as the waiter clears the table.

I lead the way to my suite. Because I don’t have the patience to wait for the long haul of elevators from the ground to the topmost floors and back again to the ground floor, I seldom use them, which is the reason I usually prefer to lodge in suites in the lower floor easily accessible by the stairs. Lizzy agrees that we use the stairs.

Lizzy has tickled my curiosity by her skills at cracking the indecipherable. Now she’s about to saddle me with the responsibility of decoding the mysterious writings of the grandmaster himself, the originator of a decade of mystery codenamed Inverted Cross Fraternity. But I’m also thrilled to discover, from her reaction when I told her how much Iyke loves her, that in spite of her seemingly prissy attitude, she also has the capacity to love, and like any other ringo, she’s bound with the same lust.
“So what is it you really want us to discuss about Iyke?” I say as I offer her a seat in the lounge section of the suite and turn on the television.
“Not only Iyke. This has to do with you, Bitty. It has to do with the future of the fraternity.”
“How many times will I tell you, Lizzy? I don’t have anything to do with that group.”
“It’s so easy to say. You are bound by a blood oath and you can only leave by the shedding of the blood. Does that ring a bell?”
“That’s bullshit!”
“Do you guys wear a tattoo?”
“No we don’t,” I reply.
“What of Jehoshaphat, did he have a tattoo?”
“You should have known if he had one on his body,” I reply smiling mischievously.
“For the avoidance of doubt, we didn’t get that close,” she says with exasperation glaring at me. “Jshap was like a brother to me.”
I shrug my shoulders.

“I’ve been trying to figure out what the symbols in this diagram represent,” she says as she flicks through the pages. I move closer to her sitting on the arm of the chair. She points at a diagram in the book. “Your inverted name is Bloody Sniper, isn’t it?”
I dither over her question.
“Even though he didn’t call people by their real names, I couldn’t have missed you in his writing. He wrote much about you, Bloody Sniper,” she says.
“I’ve not said I am.”

Lizzy shows me a hieroglyphic drawing of a triangle that has a bird, a bow and arrow and a key in it, and reads out a portion that indicates that the symbols represent certain members of the family who will play crucial roles in the affairs of the fraternity. Lizzy believes I can decode the message if I read it more carefully. She closes the notebook and hands it over to me, to decode the diagram and tell her what I make of it, tomorrow.

“Wait a minute Lizzy, there’s something I have for you.” I excuse myself and dash to the room to bring the necklace that Dr. Imoke gave me the day Iyke died. “I want you to have this…it belongs to Iyke. He asked Dr. Imoke to give it to me before he died, but I think you should have it.”
“No Bitty I can’t take that.”
I briefly tell her the mystery behind the chain and how much Iyke cherished it. “Take it Lizzy. I’m giving it to you as a gift of love on behalf of Jehoshaphat.”
Lizzy takes it from me and caresses the crucifix pendant on the necklace. “Thanks Bitty.”
“You’re welcome.”
She holds the pendant with her fist closed and the chain dropping on her lap.

“Let me help you put it on.” I take it from her and make her stand up and turn around. I slowly unlock it and put it around her slender neck as she holds up her hair. But a surge of erotic feeling sweeps over me. I kiss her neck and turn her around swiftly and try to kiss her lips, but she slaps me on my cheek. I hold her hands and draw her towards the bed.
“No Bitty stop it!” she says as she struggles to free herself from my grip.
I can’t.
She stands up to me and slaps me again and threatens to shout. No one would come to rescue her. The windows are closed and the doors are soundproof.
“Why are you doing this to me?” she cries and begs me to leave her alone.
I turn deaf ears to her plea, and drag her forcefully to the bed and struggle with her dress until I overpower her. Then she starts weeping aloud.
But I can’t get it up. Disappointed that I wasn't able to have it, I lie down beside her completely mortified, burying my head in my palms.
“How could you have nursed this thought in your heart,” she sobs, “I never thought you could be so…so vile…so low.”
“I’m sorry Lizzy I – I don’t know what came over me.”
“Oh God! How could I have trusted you so much?”
“Don’t say that please – I’m sorry really I am.”
“I don’t ever want to see you again,” she says with a gruff, “And don’t ever come to my house again!” She walks to the door and leaves in annoyance.
“Why does this keep happening to me?” I say to myself as I lie on the bed pondering over the nasty incident.

The guilty conscience of Sephia’s death induced my resolution not to ever hurt a woman, more so nursing the thought of rape, but in spite of this, I’m too weak to resist the temptation whenever it comes and the compunction is renewed every time it happens.
“Why is this happening to me,” I mumble.

chapters.title: Chapter NINE
content: The only reason she accepted my apology is because of the good news that I have a clue about what the diagram represents. When I explain it to Lizzy, her ego trip takes on a new direction – to unravel the true identity of a mysterious disciple codenamed the Key. According to her, he’s rightly named the Key – the destiny of the fraternity is hinged on him.
I was forced to read the memoir that night when I couldn’t sleep as a result of the remorse for behaving indecently towards her.

The most striking record in his memoir is the dream he had on the twenty-first of June nineteen ninety-four – his trip to Endor to visit the Oracle, with Uncle Dike. I tried to connect it to Ugo, who became the Hangman, and then his inverted name.

My curiosity was aroused when I considered the second meaning of the word Albatross. Then it dawned on me the diagram represents pictorially the future of the fraternity, as the Oracle mentioned – three dispensations of Hangman. Iyke, the Seagull is represented by the bird, while the bow and arrow stands for me, Bloody Sniper, even though I never really considered myself a Hangman. But going by the first meaning of the word Albatross, Ugo is also represented by the bird.

However, according to its second meaning, Ugo was destined to prevent something from happening. I came to the conclusion, considering all that has happened, that he prevented the GODs from getting the book. In my opinion, Ugo, the third Hangman, scuttled the timetable and closed the dispensation of Hangman in the fraternity by killing Iyke. In other words, Albatross also thwarted the reign of the Key.

In spite of my warning about the hazards of prying into the affairs of the cult, Lizzy, unrelenting in her efforts, brings in the most bizarre hypothetical conclusions. She agrees with my view from the second meaning of the word, that Ugo was destined to prevent something from happening, but she argues that the GODs will still swear the fourth Hangman into office when the time comes, and takes the Oracle’s prediction that he shall rule with the authority of a foreign God to mean that he will betray the Order. And finally, she strongly suggests we find the disciple who bears the inverted name, the Key.

My relationship with Lizzy has become so regimented that I’ve lost the morale and patience to continue seeing her. Besides, she’s becoming increasingly rigid and formal with every meeting. For security reasons, and especially for fear that I may assault her again, we rendezvous in obscure cafes and snacks bars far away from the school and unpopular among students.

She usually, calls me to tell me where she has arranged for our meeting and the time, and never fails to remind me to take a cab and to watch my back to be sure that I’m not followed. Sometimes it takes up to half an hour to locate the venue of the meeting that usually doesn’t last beyond five minutes, if an argument doesn’t ensue.

The last time we met, she tried to convince me that his death was meant to put an end to the cult. She believes that Iyke buried the book somewhere in Morashe hill and tried to coax me into taking her to Morashe hill to search for the spot where he buried it. I fervidly refused. Although I made it very clear that I wouldn’t go beyond explaining strange terms if she ran into hitches on that, I have finally made up my mind to put an end to this wild goose chase.
I’m arriving nearly forty minutes later than the time scheduled for our fifth session and I have no qualms about it. She has no problem accepting my apology too.
“The way you’re going about this hide-and-seek rendezvous, I wonder if you still attend to your studies. You have no business in this matter, Lizzy.”
“I’m doing it for you,” she snaps.
“For me…you’re protecting me, is that what you’re saying? I should be more concerned about this not you. After all Iyke and I started it all.”

“That’s what you’ve not bothered to explain, why you’re so detached from seeing that the purpose of his death is actualized,” she whines. “Anyway let’s forget about who should be doing what and go straight to business. I was able to get some information from Steel. He denied laying the wreath on his grave.”
“If he’s the Cardinal, then he’s lying!” I reply.

“He’s no longer the Cardinal. Another disciple, Sammy, toppled him. He said he and Ugo conspired against him and executed the coup five days before his death. What surprised me Bitty is, he had no idea the cross was placed upright. He visibly trembled when I told him about it,” she hesitates, and then asks me in a hush, “What’s going to happen to Sammy…is he going to die?”
I remain silent.

No one would expect that a Cardinal would one day lay a wreath in the reverse direction, and so I doubt if a specified penalty was stipulated in the family’s secret code for such an offence, but turning the cross up side down amounts to recanting one’s faith in the inverted cross, or renouncing the oath of allegiance and membership of the fraternity. And the penalty for that is a death sentence.
My curiosity is once again aroused by Lizzy’s discovery – what could be his reason for him laying the cross upright…did he do it deliberately?
“Why did he have to depose Steel,” she says almost to herself, “He would have ended up being the Cardinal anyway, toppling him was totally uncalled-for.”
“Do you know him?”

“Yeah, he used to be Steel’s best friend,” she pauses and squints fixedly into space, “Ugo,” she ponders, “He must have masterminded the coup. He used to be very persuasive and he and Steel were never the best of pals.”
“This whole thing is pointing at you Lizzy.”
“How do you mean?”
“Can’t you see the web? First you and Steel, then Ugo and then Jehoshaphat,” I whisper, “Jealousy is at the root of all cult aggro, yet it is an inevitable feeling among brethren.”
“I guess he lived up to his name, Albatross,” she says ignoring my analysis. Then she looks at her wristwatch again, which signals that our meeting has come to an end.

Lizzy’s swelling ego about her perception of the cult lifestyle is beginning to get on my nerves, and I’m beginning to sense a tone in her persuasive logic that suggests I have become too senile to figure out what is going on in the fraternity.
“Lizzy, there’s something I have to tell you,” I pause, “I’ve been issued a new passport.”
“Oh praise God!”
I stare coldly at her.
“I’m happy for you Bitty. You’re not planning to travel so soon, are you?” she asks coldly.
“I’m afraid I have to,” I reply.
Her face falls. She drops her handbag on the table and darts her eyes edgily, “You can’t do this Bitty,” she laments.
“Would you rather have me risk my life sneaking in and out of joints attending secret meetings for what is not my business?”
She goes pale and dumb.
“Those days we used to call this kind of place Harlem, that’s what this romantic escapade reminds me of, and it’s making me sick.”

“Is that what you call it, a romantic escapade,” she snarls, “I’m not crazy, Bitty. You should be sorry for taking part in the formation of this monstrous organisation. And you should, like Jshap, be eager to correct that wrong rather than excuse yourself.”

“The Inverted Cross Fraternity is the brainchild of your saintly lover, and if you want my candid opinion, there’s no price Iyke or Jshap or whatever you call him pays that is too much. He started it all and deceived us all into it, he and his devilish and wicked uncle. And I suppose his death is enough atonement for their evil imaginations.”
“You’re more wicked than both of them to say such a thing about the person you call your best friend,” she blasts back.
“Because I refused to act along with your sentiments? Why don’t you forget about these guys and go face your studies okay.”
Lizzy takes her handbag stands up and leaves.

chapters.title: Chapter TEN
content: The elders have a saying, “You don’t know the value of what you have until you lose it.” I wonder why it took so long for me to realise I’ve come to have deep feelings for Lizzy. With a heavy heart I unload my luggage, which I had earlier packed up in preparation for my trip and reflect on all that has happened since I came from the U.K.

The primary purpose of my trip has been aborted. I made my first acquaintance with Lizzy the very night I went to see my bride, the same night I lost my best friend. And for that reason too Lillian ditched me. Although I’ve convinced myself after a few failed relationships that I can’t love a woman, I think I’m probably falling in love with her anyhow.

My curiosity about Sammy placing the cross in the reverse position is making me lean towards helping her even though I can’t fathom her motive for doing this, and I don’t understand the philosophy that guides her interpretation of these events. Perhaps, if she knew a little more about the fraternity, she probably wouldn’t need my help.

For the third time I’m going to Lizzy’s. This time, not to try to make her quit her mission, but to let her know that I’ve made up my mind to give her all the support she desires of me. I have also resolved deep down to make this relationship work, even though I’ve not declared my intention plainly.
“I’m glad you finally understood,” she says with every sense of formality. “You came at the right time. I’m actually trying to gather the courage to go to the police station.”
“What’s happening at the police station?”
“Sammy is detained,” she replies.
“What did he do?”
“I don’t know. That’s what I want to go and find out. Can you come with me Bitty, please?”
“It’s alright I’ll go with you.”
“Thanks,” she enthuses.
“Save your breath Lizzy…I’ve been involved from day one.”
She smiles heartily.

It was Steel who told Lizzy about him. And according to him, Sammy has been in detention for two nights – he was abducted by secret policemen believed to be working for Dike and dumped in cell. The police will find a charge against him, I know, but his offence according to Steel is that he refused to comply with Dike’s instruction to turn the wreath to its proper position. If after three days he’s still not willing to go and reverse the cross, Dike may be forced to take drastic measures.
“I spotted the red Camry at the immigration office the day I went to receive my passport.”
“Did they see you?”
“Of course not.”
“It’s high time you reported the matter to the police,” Lizzy suggests.
“That’s exactly what Dike wants, but I won’t play into his trap. He’d turn the case against me with the intention of using it to persuade me to tell him where the book is.”
Lizzy hesitates a while then asks, “Do you really know where it is?”
“I spent some time reading through Iyke’s memoir over again. From what you’re saying, I have a feeling Sammy’s arrest has more to do with their interpretation of that drawing than the wreath.”
“You think he’s the Key?”
“It’s likely.”
“I doubt if they would have returned the memoir if they attached that much importance to the symbol.”
“Dike doesn’t need his memoir to decipher the truth. He probably never set his eyes on the diagram.”
“Are you sure they wouldn’t be looking out for you at the station?”
“They’re not expecting us.” I take a U-turn into the other side of Herbert McCauley Way, which is against our destination, the divisional police station.
“Where are you going?” Lizzy quizzes.
“To get something for Sammy to eat. You don’t expect him to answer any question if he’s hungry, do you?”

The police station is situated along the Herbert McCauley Way. Like every other police station, the compound is crammed with confiscated vehicles and also crashed vehicles towed here by the police, which have been abandoned by their owners. The only parking space for its visitors nearest to the station is on the dual carriageway, but I don’t think it’s right for vehicles to park along such a busy road. However, after searching for a convenient place to park and finding none, I am tempted to follow other visitors to the station who park their cars on the road side and naturally stride into the station with a wave of hand to a dark-skinned, mean-looking cop sitting under a mango tree by the fence, who responds with a sign that means, “we’ll see later”.

Part of his duty is to dissuade visitors from breaking the law – who obstruct traffic by parking their vehicles along the road – but rather, he’s blatantly encouraging the visitors to do the opposite. He turns a blind eye at prospective defaulters, who will settle him later on or else be severely penalized, their vehicle confiscated and they even stand the risk of being detained in the cell if they refuse to play ball and bail themselves with an outrageous fine.

Lizzy, being conversant with the system, discourages me from following the defaulters and suggests rather that we take the pain of walking nearly a quarter kilometre back to the police station from where we could find a convenient spot to park.
The slogan on the signboard reads ‘the police is your friend’, but the crease-browed cop with wicked tribal marks on his chin casts a hateful eye on us when we approach him to make an inquiry.

The fact that an average citizen is prone, not necessarily of his own making, to break the law every five minutes makes most police stations as busy as the stock market, in some cases busier. And because this is a divisional police station, it is busier than usual. Whether a man is caught urinating by a fence or a child is arrested for hawking on the streets, whether a woman is accused of husband snatching or a harlot is arrested and charged for illicit business, whether one is caught picking pockets at the motor park or another is arrested for high-level advance fee fraud, however severe or trivial the case may be, these guys here, not the judiciary interpret the law and decide who is right or wrong.

An argument is going on at the counter (a raised block work that serves the dual purpose of a partition barring visitors from the inner section of the station and also serves as a general office table). Due to the high crime rate most of the rooms have been converted to cells. A lawyer dressed in a black suit and a striped trouser has come to make a case for his client who happens, as he claims, to be his younger brother. He was arrested and detained yesterday at about eight in the night for wandering, when, according to the lawyer, he was actually coming back home from an errand. His traumatized parents only got to discover early this morning from a neighbour that he was arrested by the police. When the police demanded the sum of one thousand Naira as bail charge, which his parents do not have, they went to fetch the lawyer.

The police station is in a state of chaos, visitors to the station are at loggerheads with the police over the arrest of the minor, even those who have come for other matters are arguing in support of the lawyer who furiously criticizes them for unlawful detention and infringement of the boy’s rights. In a desperate attempt to establish a case against the boy, the police suddenly slam an allegation of theft on the lad. Meanwhile, there’s no proof of a reported case of theft at that time, and the poor boy is less than sixteen.

When they suspect that the lawyer is going to make do his threat to sue them for violation of human rights, one of them secretly sneaks out to fetch the Divisional Police Officer who invites the lawyer and the boy’s parents into his office. Afterwards, the boy is released.

Apathetic to the agony they had put the family through, one of them says in self-defence, “Oga make you no dey send your pikin to buy something for night o. We go arrest am if we see am again o.” And apparently exasperated at his comment, the counsel turns and spits on his face. The reaction from his colleagues throws the station into another round of hysteria, and in the confusion the policeman pulls a rifle angrily, cocks it and points it at him. Were it not for the DPO, the lawyer would have been a dead man.

The embittered policemen were kind enough to allow us see our ward. We are led into a small room that appears to be the only office in the block apart from the DPO’s and DCO’s offices, which, from every indication is a multi-utility office, and for lack of space, it also serves as visitor’s room.

Sammy is escorted chained in his legs and hands by a policeman into the room and made to sit down on a bench facing us. His light-skinned face has been marred by days of torture and although he is famished and starved, he hasn’t lost much of his muscular form. But his face is badly bruised and he’s practically losing his right eye.
His battered, grazed lips form a faint smile at the sight of Lizzy, which quickly vanishes when he makes contact with my squint.

Lizzy silently fights back tears as Sammy and I gaze at each other. My gaze drops at his wrist. I make out a tiny healed scar just above the metal rings holding his hands, the small mark of inverted cross given to him at inversion.

If he’s the Cardinal, the one who placed the wreath on Iyke’s tomb, and if Lizzy is right in her calculation that this guy has chosen to suffer this brutal torture rather than conform to Dike and turn the cross, then I doubt if there’s anything we’d say to him that’ll make him reveal his inverted name to us.
“You got something for me to eat?” he gasps. Two of his teeth are off and the wound in the gap is still fresh and his bloody lower lip is severely cut.
“Yes,” Lizzy replies with a sob.

We silently watch him as he rapidly munches the snacks and chomps fried rice. In spite of his blistered and sour mouth he cracks the chicken bones as though they were sticks of carrot. When he’s had his fill, he let out a deep belch and mutters, “I’ll take this to my inmates.”

Being conversant with life in detention, I bought enough snacks for his inmates to have some fragments and two packs of rice, or else he’ll have it thorough with them if he returns to the cell empty-handed.
“So who’s your friend Liz?” he asks.
“His name is Bitrus Shak,” Lizzy replies.
“My friends call me Bitty,” I chip in sticking out my right hand for a handshake.
“Sammy,” he replies ignoring my handshake, “And thanks for the meal.”
“You’re welcome,” I reply, “I’m a jolly friend of the Seagull.” Introducing Iyke as a jolly friend flags my identity as a son of darkness, and Sammy understands.
“Are you for the Provost or for the Pallbearer?” he asks with a gruff as he peers at me.
“I am neither for the Provost nor the Pallbearer,” I reply curtly, “We are here to help you.”
“How did you find me?”
“Steel told me this morning,” Lizzy chips in. “I actually asked him about you.”
“You asked him about me?” he giggles, “For what?”

“A friend of mine was shot by Ugo a few days ago…the former Hangman to be precise. Bitty and I went to the cemetery to lay a wreath after his burial. We were puzzled when we saw that somebody had placed a bronze cross on the grave,” Lizzy drawls her speech watching his reaction closely, “When Bitty informed me that it fell on the Cardinal to perform that rite to the Hangman, I went immediately to Steel,” she pauses, “Then I found out that you deposed Steel from the post a few days before the Hangman was attacked.”
“So?” Sammy responds rudely, “Have you come to persuade me to go back to the cemetery?”
“We’re on your side Sammy. We’re here to help you,” she replies.

“Yeah I know. The way your boyfriend did. Look what he did to me.” He points at his bruised right eye. “He gave me up to Dike. But I’ll deal with him when I come out of here. I’ll kill him,” he hisses.
“You’re in a difficult situation, Sammy…if you understand the implication of what you did at the cemetery,” I try to explain.
“I did what my heart led me to do and no one can make me reverse that cross,” Sammy snaps.
Lizzy and I glance at each other. The fact is established – he did it intentionally.
“Sammy, your life is in great danger,” Lizzy whispers.
“My life has been in danger for a very long time. We all have our crosses to bear in life okay, so let me bear this cross,” Sammy retorts.

“Yeah I know, but the truth is you’re not only bearing your cross, you are bearing the cross of the entire family, and that is a very heavy cross for you to bear, and it’s definitely going to drag you into your own grave.”
“I’m not afraid to die!” he growls.

“Sammy, I witnessed Iyke Obiajulu’s death. Both of us were in his parlour when Ugo stormed the house with his group.” Lizzy pauses and quickly changes the topic as an officer enters and walks towards our direction.

He opens an office cabinet with four drawers one after the other in search of something. Lizzy continues with her diversionary story, Sammy understands and plays along. The policeman apparently realizing that we’re drivelling aimlessly because of his being around says in frustration, “Make una do quick o una time don expire.” And then he leaves.

“The Hangman knew the time had come for him to die, in fulfilment of the law, which condemned him to death. But there’s something else we discovered that made us believe that there’s something peculiar about his death that has to do with you,” Lizzy concludes.
Sammy remains speechless, his bruised left eye darts between Lizzy and me.

If the conspiracy to topple Steel was initiated by Ugo, according to Lizzy, the only way Ugo could have swayed Sammy and who else took part in the coup to join him was to lie about the victim, because no one would have consented to Ugo’s conspiracy to attack a Hangman. Naturally because the cult is enshrouded in lies and deceit, it’s common in the cult, especially when a group of disciples intend to undertake an unofficial hit, to come up with something to justify an assault, evidence that the victim had offended somehow.

My deduction from the scary look on his face is that he must have been privy to the plot to attack Iyke, and like the guys that escorted Ugo to Iyke’s house that night, he probably didn’t know at that time the plot was to kill the Hangman.

Lizzy is already warming up to introduce her abstract philosophy of faith in Jesus, which is grossly out of reach as far as this circumstance is concerned. If that is the only solution, I doubt if Sammy will give any thought to it. Besides, from what Lizzy told me about Sammy, he’s not that gullible to accept such claptrap, neither will he open up or reveal such secret as his codename in the presence of a ringo.
“Lizzy, please excuse us,” I tell her.
She is taken aback at my request. She gives me the look of, ‘I hope you know what you’re doing’. But without any objection, she stands on her feet and walks out of the room.
“Shezou!” I greet him in a low tone of voice.
He turns to his left then to his right without responding. He’s afraid.
“IV code, Bloody Sniper, angel nest Mount Horeb.”
He’s pokerfaced and silent. My trick has failed.

“Now listen Sammy, after I left school nearly seven years ago, I told myself I wasn’t going to have anything to do with this fraternity, and anyone who is involved with it. I guess you may want to know why I’m wading through this muddy water again.”
He still maintains a straight face.

“We found a strange entry in the Hangman’s diary, the account of his life while he was in prison, which aroused my curiosity. If you are conversant with the family history, you’d have learnt that I took over the mantle of leadership from him.”
Sammy’s left eye dims in astonishment.
“However, Lizzy came up with the opinion that he foretold something about the one who placed the wreath on his grave…about you, Sammy.”
“Lizzy’s full of shit!” he snaps.
“I know, but unfortunately, she’s not the one in trouble, you are Sammy. You’re the devil’s alternative.”
“What the hell does that mean?” he asks me.
“I’ll tell you Sammy, if you tell me what your IV code is,” I reply.
“Then we have nothing to talk about…she knows I won’t talk to her that’s why she brought you in. Tell that whore I’m not like her puppet boyfriend who can’t keep his mouth shut.”
“Don’t ever refer to her like that!”
“I see you’ve also fallen for her spell huh. If you love your life, stay away from her. She’s bad news.”
I doubt if Lizzy knows that Sammy has a chip on his shoulder about her, or is he just naturally a woman-hater?

“What are you doing here mister?” A police officer passing along the dark corridor barks at me. Then he turns into the room and frowns at Sammy. The two star icons on his shoulders tell me he’s an ASP. “Hey,” he calls a sergeant, “Get this man back into the cell, and let this be the last time you’ll allow any visitors see him. If they have anything for him let them drop it at the counter,” he barks.
“Please you can leave now.” The sergeant glares at me as I stand up and leave the room.
When I turn back, two busybody policemen hit him with baton on his way to the cell.
“How did it go?” Lizzy asks as soon as I return to the car, “Did he tell you.”
“No,” I gasp from trudging from the station, “We were interrupted.”
“So what have you guys been discussing?” she asks impatiently.
“It doesn’t matter who he is now, we have to find a way to get him out of there.”
“You shouldn’t have asked me to leave, Bitty.”

“I know what you’re thinking Lizzy. Your sermon wouldn’t have made any difference. He doesn’t look like he has the patience for that crap. Besides, he has chosen to die, so it wouldn’t have made any difference.”
“So what else did he tell you?”

I take a glance at Lizzy. Looking for a trace of the derogatory term by which Sammy described her, but I can’t find it. And the fact that supposedly, their amity is what made Lizzy eager to visit him in the first place makes me wonder at his utterance. I can’t imagine what could have given Sammy that impression about her, but I know Lizzy still harbours affection for him.

“I’ve been thinking about what you said about Dike’s motive for his arrest. Contrary to your opinion, there’s only one reason for Dike’s desperation to have him reverse the cross. I think the way the cross is facing has affected the evil power of the GODs,” Lizzy says.
“How did you come about that?”

“The Bible says concerning the resurrection of Jesus, that he cancelled the written code with its regulations and destroyed it by nailing it on the cross of Christ, having disarmed the evil rulers and authorities, he shamed them publicly by His victory over them on the cross at Calvary. That’s what the cross signifies, victory over satan.”
“Lizzy, if I am correct, that was written nearly two thousand years ago, what relationship does what is written in your Bible have to do with Sammy’s destiny?”

“The Bible tells us about God’s creation, and it also tells us about our individual lives. I’ve been trying to connect the circumstances surrounding his death. What do you make of the breakfast at the hotel?”

“Well I don’t believe in spirits, neither do I believe in the resurrection, nor do I accept the existence of angels. And I’m certainly not a Jew. Now if you don’t mind, can we go back to the main issue that took us to the police station, which is, how do we save your friend from the wrath of the GODs?”
Ignoring me, Lizzy whispers, “The Lord our Judge…the Judge of Israel has vindicated him and condemned the GODs and their power by the sign of the cross.”
“Lizzy, can we approach this problem more realistically?”
“Since you want to be so realistic why don’t you think of a way,” she replies with sarcasm.
I heave a sigh of resignation.

“The only realistic way to walk with God is by faith in His Son Jesus Christ,” she whispers wearing a short smile on her face, “I believe that Jesus paid the penalty of the sins of Jshap, of Sammy and any other disciple of the inverted cross who would not be ashamed of the cross, and in believing they will receive the gift of God in His name.”
I shake my head in resignation, turn the ignition on and quietly steer into the road.
“Did he tell you the reason he placed the cross that way?” she asks in a low tone.
“Conscience,” I giggle, “I wonder where he got that. As a matter of fact, the interpretation of the secret code is not subject to individual judgment.”
“What are you implying?”

“What I’m saying is that everyone is bound by the oath to submit to the authority of the fraternal laws and conform without question to whatever it views as right or wrong. Disciples of the inverted cross sacrifice their volition the very day they swear that oath.”
“If it’s a matter of conscience, he’ll not change his mind.”
“In that case, he has signed his death warrant,” I respond.
“He signed his death warrant the very day he swore the oath to join the fraternity.”
I take a deep breath.

“Just as you did, Bitty,” Lizzy adds and glances at me, “But his faith in Christ will break that covenant of death and raise him up from the dead. You were just lucky to have escaped this kind of predicament when you resigned your position as Hangman.”
“It wasn’t a coincidence. I made my own choices.”

“And you suppose that joining the cult in the first place wasn’t as bad a choice as abdicating the position of the Hangman, right? I’ve seen smart guys in worse situations, and some who never really got to sort out their problems. They either quit school or something terrible happens along the line.”
“Well, I guess they weren’t smart enough.”
“Don’t pride about being smart, Bitty,” she says droopily and yawns, “If that law has that much authority over its subjects, then your time of reckoning probably hasn’t just come yet.”
When I glance at her, she closes her eyes and tilts her body sideways facing the window. She’s so weary. I doubt if she’s been having enough sleep since Iyke passed on.
I drive her home in a hush not disturbing her nap.

chapters.title: Chapter ELEVEN
content: “Lizzy! Lizzy! We are home.” I tap her shoulder lightly to wake her from sleep.
“Oh thanks Bitty,” she yawns.
“Take a break, Lizzy. Get out of town …”
“Not yet,” she snaps.
“Do you mind my asking? Why are you doing this?”
She chuckles.
“I admire your courage, Lizzy. I’ve not seen such determination in a woman…but what really is your motivation?”
“Love.”
“Love,” I ask her derisively.
“I have compassion for him, Bitty. That’s how God found me.”
“I can understand your case, Lizzy. But Sammy…he’s practically attempting a suicide by refusing Dike’s order.”
“If this is a matter of conscience, it lies with God. He will vindicate him.”
“There you go again, God. Must you always say that? Can’t we figure out something? I mean, must the bulk always end with God?”
She shakes her head and remains silent.
“What does God have to do with this?” I giggle.

“Conscience is the law of God written in our hearts. It precedes any written code. When we act in submission to its authority, even though we flout man-made law, we are appealing to the Sovereign Lord, the Judge and lawgiver, and he definitely responds and vindicates whoever acts in obedience to his conscience. Regardless of the fact that Sammy’s bound by that code, the authority of conscience is superior to it.”
“The guy doesn’t even believe in Him, in that sense.”
“Why do you think we have guilt feeling when we do something wrong in spite of our varying religious belief?”
I remain silent.

“Being created in the image of God, we possess the consciousness of God, and by nature acquire the knowledge of good and evil irrespective of any religious laws. No one is excused from that judgment, whether we believe in the existence of God or not, whether we accept the Lordship of Jesus or not. Not even our rejection of His Word excuses us from its conviction. Or else we would be no different from a beast, if we are void of God-consciousness, and that is what disciples of the inverted cross make of themselves because they claim not to have conscience,” she explains. Then she smiles as if something just occurred to her. “If in response to his conscience Sammy stands firm on his will, in the end he’ll save his life because the cross is a symbol of faith in Jesus Christ, the Son of God.”

I ponder over the ambiguity of her statement – Sammy has had his chips and Lizzy has accepted the fact that the GODs will kill him, whether or not he eventually agrees with Dike to reverse the cross. But, how can she now say that he will save his life?
“You’re wondering what I mean by Sammy saving his life if he refuses to obey the will of the GODs,” she asks.
I am. But admitting it will spark off a long discourse about the kingdom of God again so I remain silent.
“What does the devil’s alternative mean?” she asks.
I shudder at her question.
“I read about it in his memoir…that’s what this is all about, isn’t it?”
“I don’t know.”
“You do.”
“Hey Lizzy, why don’t you go to sleep now okay.”

The devil’s alternative is the most secretive term in the Inverted Cross Fraternity. I doubt if there’s any Cardinal ordained after me that will be able to decipher the real meaning of the term if at all they’ve heard about it, only Iyke and I knew about it and when he left the scene, because of the horror of even imagining it, I refused to share it with anyone else.
“It would have been more severe for him if he violated his conscience in obedience to the statutes of the irrevocable testament,” she argues.
“You’d have a different opinion if you know what the devil’s alternative really means.”

“I’m not afraid of men who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather I fear the One who is able to destroy both soul and body in hell. Anyway Sammy has made an appeal to the Almighty, the covenant of death shall be broken,” she whispers. Then she gathers herself to leave the car.
“Lizzy –”

“Save your breath Bitty,” she snaps, “I believe in miracles, and I believe his death is a fulfilment of the requirement of that testament, so that disciples like Sammy, whose conscience is undermined by the written code may be freed from this mesh.” She alights from the car, shuts the door quietly and walks home.

I sit still in the car and watch her unlock her door. She turns and waves me goodbye. I couldn’t have imagined that we’d come to this – that the consequences of our exuberance would be so grave. Iyke is dead, and I, the only living witness to the testament, am saddled with the responsibility of saving this folk who’s not even willing to live, for conscience sake. If only she knew what the devil’s alternative means, she’ll consider my point of view. Sammy’s case is beyond remedy.

As long as the fraternity remains, no one really gets to know what he’s up against until he belongs. And when one comes to realize what the cult really is, he marvels at his gullibility, and sadly discovers that he has sold his soul to the devil and enslaved himself to wickedness, and acts that inwardly he constantly regrets. But in order not to be overwhelmed by such vulnerability, the disciple of the inverted cross is expected to be hard-faced to contend with other hard men and hard-headed adversaries to ward off the uneasy fear within – the fear of judgment for his nefarious activities.
In Lizzy’s words, the wages of sin, this is death.
“If Sammy has appealed to God, let God save him,” I mutter and slowly drive back to the hotel.

chapters.title: Chapter TWELVE
content: The knock at the door wakes me up from sleep. With a pistol in my hand, I listen again to be sure it’s the family knock code or else I wouldn’t respond. The time is past one in the morning and because of the tension down campus – the Inverted Cross Fraternity has been fighting a running battle with the Golgotha – I have become wary of strangers. The fight has become so severe in the past three weeks and the number of casualties from both sides has risen to eighteen. My heart beats loudly as I gaze at the door wondering who could be visiting me at such ungodly hour.
Two knocks, one, and three slow knocks again. Then I rise from the bed and go to answer the door with my pistol in hand.
“Down or above?” I whisper our identity code standing close to the door.
“Down below.” His voice sounds very familiar.

A sudden flapping of the wings of a black crow as I open the door for the visitor frightens me. The creature rises and disappears into the dark sky. I look around but find there’s nobody outside. “Where could the visitor be,” I mutter and pace silently towards the end of the building.
“Bitrus!” His voice is like the sound of a multitude. I feel strong heat from a ray of light behind me where he is standing. I lift up my eyes to the sky, but it is dark.
“Bitrus,” he thunders again, “I am here.”

I turn around to see who is calling. He is standing at the door. Dressed in dazzling white flowing gown, it’s so bright I can’t look steadily at him. I block the rays from his dress with my hand and peer at him. He is wearing a beanie on his head, the type worn by the Hangman, but the colour is white and there is a sign on it, a cross, an upright cross.
“Do not be afraid Bitrus, I have overcome.” The voice sounds like Iyke’s voice, but the face is shining so bright I can’t make out whether he is actually the one.
My knees buckle and I lie prostrate before him.
“I have come in response to the prayers of your friend to explain to you the symbol that speaks of the time of the end of the Inverted Cross Fraternity, for the appointed time of the end has come.”
“What do you want me to do?”

“You will return to Elizabeth and help her in the good work she is doing for Me. Samuel has been ransomed by a priceless sacrifice and I have come to deliver him because he has done what is right in My sight. You and Elizabeth will save him from the hands of his enemies for indeed he shall be taken to the hills of Morashe, where the testament is hidden – he is the devil’s alternative. But I will surely deliver him from the expectation of the GODs. And I will show you where the testament is buried, you shall take it and destroy it on the abominable alter on the hill according to the determined counsel that I revealed to My servant, Jehoshaphat for the outcry against their wickedness has come to my ears and I have come down to judge them.

“As for you Bitrus, if you will obey my voice, I will pardon your sins and give you your life as a ransom. Although they have fashioned their weapon against you, it shall not prosper for I, the Sovereign Lord will surely deliver you. And afterwards I will turn their swords into ploughshares and their spears into pruning hooks. Therefore gird up your loins and go to Elizabeth now.”
The sound of telephone ringing wakes me from sleep. To my surprise, I’m lying prostrate on the floor in my hotel room instead of on the bed. I raise my head and notice there’s no one at the door.
“It was a dream,” I mutter.

Rising from the floor, I pick up my watch and check the time – it’s five minutes after one o’ clock in the morning. Trembling from the tone of the words I heard, I pick up the telephone receiver with wobbly hands. “Hello,” I quaver.
“Operator, please hold on for your caller,” comes the reply.
“Hello Bitty.” It’s Lizzy on the line. “I’m sorry to bother you please can you come over to my house now.” She sounds urgent, but there’s no fuss in her tone.
“Lizzy, can’t this –”
“Bitty please come over now!” she demands and hangs up.

Lizzy demanded with the same exigency as of the forceful voice of the man in the dream. Did she have the same revelation as I did? ‘Gird up your loins and go to Elizabeth now!’ his voice resounds in my head.
“What is happening to me?” I mutter to myself. My head drops to the floor from where I woke up. “How did I get down here?”

The time by my watch reminds me of the dream and the seeming reality of the dream terrifies me. If I had my way I wouldn’t want to interfere in this, because, if Dike were determined to offer Sammy as the devil’s alternative for his apostasy, I’d be risking my life trying to get in his way.

I get a nightmare when the lurid details of Iyke’s last crucifixion parade comes to my mind – unlike the three other members of the Golgotha fraternity who were involved in Julia’s assault, Dike demanded that Femi be apprehended and brought to him alive – he would have been the guinea pig of his devilish ingenuity codenamed the devil’s alternative. It was on the same hill that Iyke received the irrevocable testament of the inverted cross.

I never thought of it from this point of view, but in the light of the message I heard in the vision, that parade was foiled either by the handiwork of Ighmiha, the god of the fraternity or the Being that appeared to me.

Apart from howling neighbourhood dogs that have taken over the streets in the area, there is no sign of life, not even the usual din of children living in Alhaji Gidado Street – they’ve all gone to sleep. When they notice my car approaching, the dogs moved away from the road as I swerve into Douglas Close, but soon cluster together at my door barking furiously at me as I park at the usual spot where I hide the car when I visit Lizzy. I wouldn’t dare these ferocious creatures so I remain in the car signalling Lizzy with a slight hoot of the horn that I have arrived.
She comes to my rescue chasing them away. We exchange pleasantries and she leads me into the house.
Still looking battered and bruised from the torture of the cops, Sammy is lying down on the couch fast asleep.
“Who is this?” I ask in a whisper not believing my eyes.
“Come on don’t tell me you’ve forgotten Sammy so soon,” she replies.
“But – what’s he doing here? How did he get out of the cell?” I gasp.
“It’s a long story. You better hear it from the horse’s mouth.” She taps him on his hand waking him up.
“Good morning Bitrus,” he greets me drowsily and sits up.
“How do you do Sammy?” I reply and steal a glance at Lizzy.
She shrugs and sits down next to me. “He wants to know how you got out of the cell,” Lizzy tells him.

Sammy gives me a cautious look from his bulgy left eye as if he’s undecided about telling it. Then he clears his throat and begins timidly, “At about midnight, I was fast asleep on the floor, suddenly I noticed a strong beam of light flashing into the room filling the room with smoke. A man putting on a police uniform was standing at the gate, and the gate was open. While I wondered at him he commanded in a voice that sounded like thunder ‘Get up Sammy.’ I stood up. And he said, ‘Follow me.’

“He took me to the counter where I deposited my things with the cops and also my cloth. I was surprised to find that all the policemen on duty were sleeping. He commanded me to dress up and put on my shoes. I did. So I left the cell following the man. But all the while I thought it was a dream. I didn’t realize it was really happening. When we came to the iron gate leading to the street, it opened by itself and we passed through. As we started walking down the street, he said to me, ‘Hurry up Sammy I have come to get you out of here. Go to Elizabeth, she will instruct you on what you must do to be saved, for I have chosen you to witness of My name everywhere,’” Sammy pauses for a while and continues, “I thought it was a dream. But when I realized it was real, I said to myself, the Lord has sent His angel and saved me from the Provost and from what the GODs were hoping to do to me.”

Sammy ends his story with a reaffirmation that he’d rather die than invert the cross. And a contrite admission that he is not worthy to bear the name of Jesus considering the extent he had wandered from Him. “I have sinned greatly against the Lord,” he says regrettably.

“It’s alright Sammy, anyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved,” Lizzy responds, “When Bitty told me the reason for your refusal to comply with Dike’s order to have the cross turned, I knew the Lord would vindicate you.” she continues as she turns to me, “I was right after all about the authority of our conscience.”
Sammy listens to Lizzy with creased eyebrows.

“When Gentiles, who do not have God’s written law, instinctively follow what the law says, they show that in their hearts they acknowledge what is right. But when we know the truth, it gives us the confidence that we have been made right with God, and we have liberty to come to Him as sons,” Lizzy takes a pause darting her eyes back and forth from me to Sammy as we both digest her words.
Still overwhelmed by the dream I had early this morning and seeing Sammy here, I stare mutely at her.
“What is the truth?” Sammy asks.
“Sammy, your instincts have brought you so far. But the only one who can save you from the wrath of the GODs is Jesus. Except you surrender your life to Him, you will surely die.”
A childish grin is etched on Sammy’s face. He remains silent.
“If you are willing to surrender your life to Jesus, I’d be happy to pray for you,” she urges him gently in a low tone.

The idea of having Lizzy pray for him, I’m sure, amuses Sammy. He silently turns away from Lizzy’s probing stare facing me as if he’s asking my permission. He turns back to Lizzy and replies, “I’ll think about it okay.”
Lizzy’s face falls. “I wish you had time to think about it,” she replies, “Can he pass the night with you Bitty?”
“Sure,” I reply, “Can we go now? I have a busy day, gotta catch some sleep.”
“Thanks for everything Lizzy.” Sammy rises and greets her. “Your words are really thought-provoking. Can I come tomorrow alone so we can talk more about this?”
“It’s already morning,” I chip in.
“You mean later today?” she says.
“Oh yeah, later today,” Sammy admits.
“I’d be through with my lectures at two, can we meet then?”
“You know I can’t come to school. They’ll pick me up if they sight me,” Sammy explains.
“Well then let’s meet here,” Lizzy replies.

I expect her to suggest one of those rendezvous where we used to have our meetings when we were searching for him. I’m struck with guilt again at the sense of security behind her inviting Sammy to meet her here alone. I leave both of them and go to the car.
“Before you came she’s taken me through some drill on the Bible. Wow! In my wildest imagination I wouldn’t think that could’ve come from Lizzy,” Sammy comments as we leave the Close.
I remain silent.
“You should’ve seen her before now…she used to be a nympho,” he stops and gazes at me to be sure I’m not offended.
I still do not respond.
“You know, I’ve been a church boy all my life,” he brags, “But I’ve never heard the gospel explained the way she did this morning.”

“You probably have not been paying attention all your life,” I reply, “Do you really believe in that – that merely believing in the name of some prophet who was crucified on the cross can actually take the punishment of all your sins and throw it into the deep blue sea?”
“I don’t have a problem with that.”

“I’m not surprised after all. We all are imbued with particular religious teachings from the cradle and we grow up to follow it sheepishly. We are told not to question the doctrines, because they are from God!” I grunt, “They give us a feeling of satisfaction about life, a hope of life that is based on assumptions. In the end, you find it’s all a hopeless futility. Everyone dies Sammy – the rich man as well as the poor, the mighty and the feeble, everyone dies, even the priest and the pimp they all end up in the grave…there’s no paradise except here.”
“If you knew Lizzy before now, you probably would take what she has said with a little more seriousness,” he concludes.
“Do you believe her then?” I ask him.
“Not on her account,” he whispers, “She told me you were his best friend, the former Hangman. And you also took care of his burial.”
“Yeah she’s right.”
“I learnt he was a holy man.”
“That’s what he claims. But I knew Iyke better than anyone else,” I hesitate. Sammy gazes anxiously at me with his bloodshot left eye, “He was a devil.”
“I believe he is a holy man,” Sammy snaps, “Because of what happened to me at the cemetery when I laid the wreath of honour.”
I glance at him and meet his bulgy eyes.

“After we ousted the former Cardinal, Gehenna, and I took over, Ugo constrained me to do the blind target. Then I didn’t know anything about the former Hangman, but something strange happened that day. As soon as I consented to his plan, I went completely blind. I didn’t understand why it happened. But three days after he was buried, when I went to lay the traditional wreath of honour…after I placed the wreath my sight was restored.”

“When I heard what happened to Ugo, that he was dead and how the hit went, I was pricked in my conscience for consenting to Ugo’s plan after being warned by God through the preaching I heard in church that Thursday.”
“You go to church?”
“Yes,” he replies with childish giggle.
“You must be a queer disciple in that case.”

“People knew Jehoshaphat Obiajulu as a prophet of God…of course the lot fell on me to lay the wreath on his grave. My instinct told me the only way to pacify my conscience would be to pay him the respect of a prophet, to lay the cross in accordance with his faith,” Sammy takes a pause and exhales heavily, “As soon as I dropped the cross, my sight was restored. Then I saw a fluffy white dove perching on top of the tombstone. I scurried back in fear when it flapped its wings and ascended into the dark sky and out of sight.”

Sammy’s story takes me back to the dream I had early this morning. I reacted exactly the same way as he did when the black crow flapped its wings. What I don’t understand is how the two experiences with a common course produced contrasting feelings in both of us. Up till this moment the fear of the message brought to me about Sammy hasn’t left me – that he’ll be taken up to Morashe hills – but on the contrary, his encounter at the cemetery has made him so bold that he is even willing to die. And I’m supposed to help him.
“What does that mean?”
“I think God sent the white dove to bear witness to the very power that raised Jesus, the Holy Spirit,” Sammy explains.
“Let me ask you, do you believe in the resurrection?”
He remains silent.
“Now I know why Dike is after you.” Our eyes meet when I turn to glance at him. “What’s your bush name?”
“The Key. But my friends call me Lethal Weapon.”

Needless to bother Sammy with the details of the information in Iyke’s memoir about him, he wouldn’t know anything about it. But it may be useful to know some facts about Sammy’s antecedents for the sake of interpreting the diagram. “How did you join the cult, I mean what led to your decision to join the cult?”
“It’s a long story Bitty.”
“We obviously don’t have much to talk about, do we?”

“I told you earlier I used to be a church boy,” he says grinning, “Sometimes I’m confused about who I really am. I notice an astonishing look when brothers see me coming out of the church with the holy Book in my hand, but on the other side, my childhood friends keep me at arms length because they know I’m a hard man. I was raised an alter boy by my foster parents.”
“You lost your parents?”
“No. I never knew them,” he says in a low tone.
I thought it’d be absurd to ask him how he found himself there.

“I spent the first few years of my life in an orphanage before I was adopted by the family that oversees the primary school owned by the church that took care of us. They were Irish. My foster father was a priest, and his wife was a teacher. They were a young and adorable couple…but she had a problem with child bearing. That’s why they adopted me as their own son and showered me with all the love I could ever ask for, more than I could imagine my mother would have given me. They gave me the best of education and taught me the way of the Lord…to walk in the fear of the Lord…I loved them too.” Tears run down Sammy’s cheeks as he narrates the story of his life.

“But our sweet relationship turned awry when I entered the university. After my first year, during the second semester holidays, my father travelled to America for a missionary assignment. Because his wife was critically ill, she couldn’t go with him.”
“She died?” I snap abruptly.
“No,” Sammy hesitates, “She had a niece that was staying with us at that time…she kept persuading me to go to bed with her.” He turns and glances at me. “But I refused.”
When I turn and glance back at Sammy, he looks away from me staring through the window.

“When I couldn’t stand her frequent persuasion, I fled from the house,” he takes a pause, “When he returned from the trip, and probably asked after me, I guess he was told another version of the story.”
“That you assaulted her?”

“Probably. When I heard that he had returned and came back to the house, to my surprise, he called me early in the morning and hauled me over the coal for taking advantage of her in spite of all they had done for me. I tried to explain what happened, but he wouldn’t let me…he laid a curse on me, disowned me, and banished me from his house telling me never to call him my father,” he says biting his lips.
“How could he do that?”

Sammy remains silent for a while. “I was so bitter with God for allowing such a thing to happen.” Sammy stops and shakes his head regrettably. “That incident left a scar in me…a deep resentment for women and a negative notion about preachers. Then I stopped going to church…for a while. That was when I met Steel and later on he introduced the fraternity to me. They became the only family I had,” he pauses then begins to laugh, “But strangely, I began to feel empty and insecure and lost. I knew I had lost the love I used to know. That’s when I started going to church again…in search of the love I once knew.”

He turns to me breathing heavily. “But that night at his grave, I knew when I placed the wreath I was taking the risk of losing the only family I’ve had since then. But the voice of my consciousness also told me that God, Who in His infinite wisdom didn’t allow me to carry out that hit lest I be guilty of his blood, had found me,” he pauses, “It was the story of Joseph that gave me the hope that one day, God was going to release me from the prison of my conscience just as He vindicated him after thirteen years of imprisonment for a similar allegation.”
“That’s one of the fairy tales you get from the Bible, I guess.”
“It’s not a fairy tale,” he snaps angrily.
“Anyway, your case is quite different because of what you’re up against. I will advise you to blanket for a while until the heat dies down.”
“But I have a date with Lizzy today.”
“Don’t be ridiculous Sammy! Are you going to forfeit your life because of a woman’s love? Dike has set spies on her house and you know what he’s going to do to you if he finds you again?”
“What? Kill me, what else?” he retorts, “I’m not afraid of men who can only kill me, but I fear Him who is able to destroy body and soul in hell.”
“Can you think of a more painful way to die than to be grilled slowly while you are still alive?”
He looks askance at me.

If Lizzy is right about the power of the GODs being shaken as a result of what Sammy has done, and considering that the irrevocable testament of the inverted cross is missing, the only possible way to rekindle the powers of the GODs is through an offering, a human sacrifice perhaps.
And from all indications, Sammy, the Key is the devil’s alternative.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTEEN
content: “Sammy will not be able to keep your appointment,” I announce to Lizzy as we saunter from her lecture hall to the car park.
“You dissuaded him from coming, I know,” Lizzy replies coldly.
“Why do you always jump into conclusions like that?”
“Don’t stand in the way of the Lord, Bitty. I don’t have a problem if you don’t accept what I’m saying, but I won’t have you poisoning his mind with your unbelief.”
“Maybe you’re the real obstacle. He’s told me an ear full about your past life,” I reply.
She smiles and shakes her head.
“Sammy is the Key.”
“He told you?”
“Yeah, this morning as soon as we left your house.”
“I have to see him immediately. Where is he?”
“I left him in the room.”
“Can we go now?”
“Of course.”

I locked Sammy inside the room when I was leaving and dropped the key at the desk. But surprisingly no one seems to know where the key is. The hotel manager apologizes on behalf of his staff for their laxity and gives me a duplicate key to my room.
“Bitty, how is a Hangman selected in the fraternity?” she asks me as we walk up the stairs.
“Any one of the Cardinals is eligible to become a Hangman, but usually the Provost, Dike has the right of veto.”
“What happened in your case when Jshap handed over the mantle to you?”

“It was illegal. No one is allowed by law to take the place of a Hangman unless the fingers of the current Hangman is fully shed, otherwise the position will be vacant until he is confirmed dead…that’s the significance of the wreath, it is a confirmation of the demise of the Hangman.”
Lizzy squints distantly into space while I insert the key to unlock the door.
“Lizzy!”
She startles. “I’m sorry.”
“What are you thinking?”

“I’m trying to connect some facts in my brain. I think Jshap had a foreknowledge of this, and that could be the reason he gave the instruction for his body to be buried in the cemetery…to lead us to the Key.”
I shrug my shoulders and open the door. The volume of the television set is turned up very loudly. I hurriedly switch off the set. “Sammy!”
There’s no response. He’s not in the room and he’s not in the bathroom.
“He’s gone,” I report to Lizzy.
“Where?”
“I don’t know.”
“Maybe he went down to get some air,” Lizzy suggests.
“No.” I bend down to pick the left leg of Sammy’s shoe lying on the floor and pointing towards the door. “They were here,” I whisper.
“Who was here?”
“Dike…Sammy’s been abducted. He left this message to let me know,” I say holding the shoe out to Lizzy.

In the fraternity, when a disciple deliberately leaves one of his shoes pointing towards the door, it is an alert that there’s danger, or that the disciple who left the shoe is in danger. It’s also a warning for any other disciple who sees the shoe to leave the place.
“Come and have a look at this Bitty,” Lizzy whispers holding Iyke’s memoir in her hand. She gives the notebook to me.

There’s another diagram I saw in his memoir – it’s a matchstick figure of somebody tied to a table and another is standing at the end of the table over the head of the one tied to the table holding a book in his hand. Above the figures he drew a circle, which I suspect represents the moon, in which case the ritual will take place tonight.
“He was right, he’s the devil’s alternative,” I whisper.
“What…whom are you referring to?”

At first I didn’t want to tell Lizzy about the vision I had this morning, I was afraid she’d coax me into escorting her to Morashe hills. But I never knew it’d come to this – that Sammy would be taken to the evil mountain, to be offered as a burnt sacrifice on the altar. Now it’s our responsibility, me and Lizzy, to save him.
“Where’s the place?”

“I don’t know the exact location, but it is somewhere in Ogun state. I’m concerned about the certainty of his words and I’m afraid that if Sammy is taken to the hills, I doubt whether we can stop the GODs from their plot.”
“If the Lord has sent His angel to declare this to you, then He will do it. He hasn’t said a thing and not done it…what is this devil’s alternative?”
“I don’t exactly know how Iyke or Dike came about it, but the term is used to describe the person that is chosen by the goddess for sacrifice,” I explain.
“My goodness! Don’t tell me you guys offer human sacrifices?” Lizzy grimaces with disgust.
There’s no need offering the excuse that such ritual had never been observed, and there’s no need trying to exonerate myself – I’m as guilty as Iyke is in the crucifixion parade against Femi.
“Did you give Sammy a hint about this?” she asks.
“I mentioned it, but I can swear he doesn’t have the foggiest idea what it means.”

Lizzy drops her gaze on the table, to the Bible placed in the hotel rooms by the Gideon. It is left open at a page. She probably has seen something that interests her and picks up the book ponderously. “I think he knows,” she whispers, “Look at this Bitty,” she says handing the Bible to me. “He’s comparing that figure in his memoir with the story of Abraham and Isaac.”
“What’s the meaning of this?”

“That’s the origin of the term the devil’s alternative. Once God tested Abraham’s loyalty by asking him to offer his son to Him as a sacrifice, but later God provided an alternative, a ram, in place of Abraham’s son, Isaac when Abraham proved his love and faithfulness to his God. In the reverse, Sammy is the sacrifice chosen by the GODs, a contrast of the ram, which Abraham, the servant of God offered to his God. Sammy becomes the devil’s alternative…just as God’s alternative for Isaac was a ram,” Lizzy explains. “Now I know why the fraternity is called inverted cross,” she whispers, “It’s a volte-face of what the cross of Jesus, the symbol of the Christian faith stands for.”
“Lizzy, if they have taken him to the Morashe hills –”
“Then the God of Abraham shall deliver him,” she snaps, “Because he has given his Seed as a ransom for those who by faith are the sons of Abraham.”
“But you said God stopped him from offering his son and provided a ram instead,” I argue.

“Yes He did. But figuratively Abraham offered him to God, and by his obedience he proved that God was able to bring him back to life again because God had promised him that He was going to build a nation through the descendants of Isaac. So in that sense Abraham received his son back from the dead. God was through Abraham signifying what His Son, Jesus would accomplish for us. The Bible says for God so loved the world that He gave His begotten Son that whoever believes in Him shall not perish but have everlasting life. As Isaac came by promise, so was Jesus given to us by promise, and as Isaac was figuratively raised from the dead, so did Jesus resurrect from the dead…and so will all who believe in Him rise from the dead on the last day.”
I stare at Lizzy dumbfounded. She takes the Bible from me and drops it on the table and asks me, “You know the way to the Morashe hills, don’t you Bitty?”
I hesitate.
“Don’t be afraid Bitty, God has given us the victory over the powers of darkness,” she beams.
“How can you be too sure?”
Lizzy draws out the gold necklace belonging to Iyke and shows me the cross pendant. “This is the sign of victory. He is the resurrection and the life…let’s go and take Sammy from them.”

As I reflect on the words that the angel spoke to me in the dream, a surge of power comes upon me and I tremble. For the first time I see the possibility of Sammy living. Without saying a word, Lizzy and I go down to the car park, board the Rabbit and head to Morashe hills.

When she called me early in the morning sounding urgent, I took my pistol along to her house in anticipation that something dangerous had happened. It is still hidden under my seat, unknown to her. I’m not taking any chances. I won’t underestimate Dike in the name of faith.

We set out at exactly ten forty-five p.m. for Morashe hill, which is approximately sixty kilometres from Lagos. By my understanding of the activities of the GODs, the ritual should take place at midnight. Perhaps if we get to the top of the hill in the next hour, we’ll be there early enough to intercept the ritual.

Iyke had once taken me to the Morashe hills just before the crucifixion parade against Femi. We had gone there to place a dummy of him on the altar and invoke his name in order to subdue the evil power by which he escaped previous attempts to gun him down. Under the full moonlight I’m sure I’ll be able to find my way. The narrow path leading up to the hill top is marked by two large trees that appear black in the night. They are dry, and have neither a single leaf nor fruits. Iyke told me they were the guardian angels of the Morashe hills, when we descended from the hill that day. He also said that legend has it that any stranger who passes them into the territory vanishes.

As soon as I beamed my headlight and sight the giant trees at a distance a hunched old woman suddenly appears on the road carrying branches of a tree tied in a bunch on her shoulder and walking slowly across the road. I swerve off to avoid running into her and the car races into the bush, collide with the stem of a tree and stops abruptly.
“Lizzy! Lizzy! Oh my God!” I remove her seat belt and raise her up. Blood drips down her brow from a cut in her head.
“I’m all right Bitty,” she mutters.
The car refuses to start. We both step out and wade through the bush towards the main road. There’s a beam of light from an oncoming vehicle on the lonely road so we hide behind a tree.
“We arrived too early,” I whisper.
“I’m not sure,” Lizzy replies.

Instantly, we hear the sound of screeching tyres from stamping of brakes, then the sound of the car skidding off the road, and then the clatter from its violent collision. Lizzy and I stare at each other in amazement.
“Let’s go,” she whispers.

We hit the road and match silently to the giant trees. When we reach the foot of the hill, between the trees, Lizzy halts. “I’ll wait for you here,” she says, “The Lord is with you Bitty don’t be afraid.”
“If I do not return Lizzy,” I coo, “I want you to know that –”

“I know,” Lizzy replies smiling. Then she draws out the gold necklace and whispers, “This is the sign of victory over satan. He is the resurrection and the life.” She removes it and put it on me. “Go get Sammy. Time is running out,” she beams.

I lift up my head to the top of the tree to a crow perching on its top, which had been crying harshly as we approached the hill. It suddenly dawns on me I’ve eventually forgotten to take the pistol from the car after the crash.
“I’ll be praying for you,” she assures me.

Gradually, I have begun to draw confidence from Lizzy’s emphatic words and her God even though her doctrine is weird and out of the ordinary. Initially it sounded abstract, until her elucidation of the devil’s alternative and its link with the death and resurrection of Jesus.
“Could God have actually raised Isaac to life if Abraham had killed him?” I mutter as I trudge my way up the hill.

It’s a tedious walk up the Morashe hill – the peak of the steep-sided, dreadful hill stands at almost nine hundred feet above sea level. The journey is made difficult by the thick forest, which people dare to traverse. When I near the top, I take my bearing from Sammy’s voice as he loudly recites, I suppose, a portion of the Bible.

“…the people who walk in darkness will see a great light – a light that will shine on all who live in the land where death casts its shadow. Israel will again be great, and its people will rejoice as the people rejoice at harvest time. They will shout with joy like warriors dividing the plunder. For God will break the chains that bind his people and the whip that scourges them…”

“Will you shut up?” Dike yells at him in a loud voice. “Even if you call on Him from now till tomorrow, he won’t answer you because you belong to the devil. Besides, if He wasn’t able to save the former Hangman he won’t be able to deliver you.”

“…for a child is born to us, a son is given to us. And the government will rest on His shoulders,” Sammy continues, “These will be His royal titles – Wonderful, Counsellor, Mighty God, Everlasting father, Prince of peace. His ever-expanding, peaceful government will never end. He will rule forever with fairness and justice…”

Sammy is blindfolded and firmly tied to the imposing altar while Dike bends down under the metal gauze on the altar to arrange wood in preparation for the sacrifice as Sammy’s voice blares in the dark. I pick up a thick dry tree branch and tiptoe to where he is squatting. My move is too swift for him. As soon as he catches sight of me he fumbles in his pocket, for a pistol, I guess. But it’s too late. I stop him with a swift, hard strike in his right hand. He attempts to take it again, I hack his head with the stick and he falls on his face. I take the pistol in his pocket, cock it and point it at him to finish him off.
Then I stagger at the crack of a rifle. Blood runs down my chest. I buckle on my knees and fall headlong beside Dike’s still body.

chapters.title: Chapter FOURTEEN
content: Iyke leads me into the courtyard of a glorious building with twelve pillars. There are four pillars on each side of the building. In the spirit he shows me a door standing open in heaven. A voice sounding like a mighty trumpet says, “Come up here and I will show you what must happen after these things.”

I turn around to see the voice that is speaking to me, but there are seven golden lamp stands and a man dressed in a robe reaching down to his feet with a gold sash across his chest standing in the middle of the lamp stands. The hairs on His head are white like wool, as white as snow, His eyes are as bright as flames of fire, and His feet are as bright as bronze refined in furnace. And His voice thunder like mighty ocean waves. He holds seven stars in His right hand, and His face is bright and brilliant as the sun in radiance.
I fall prostrate at His feet as if I’m dead.
He lays His hand on me and says, “Don’t be afraid Bitrus! I am the First and the Last. I am the Living One who died. Look, I am alive forever and ever. And I hold the keys of death and the grave.”
When I raise my head, Iyke is standing before me.
“Rise up Bitty,” he tells me grinning. He is putting on a fine linen dress, bright and clean and without a wrinkle.

There is a magnificent throne at the middle of the courtyard, which is encircled with a rainbow resembling an emerald and surrounded by another twenty-four thrones with twenty-four elders sitting on each of them. They are all clothed in white and have gold crowns on their heads. There is also a shiny sea of glass sparkling like crystal after the seven lamp stands in front of the throne. Flashes of lightening and rumble of thunder comes from the throne.
“What are these lamp stands?” I ask.
“They are the seven spirits of God,” he replies.
Four living beings are around the throne and they keep saying, “Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty – the One who always was, who is, and who is still to come.”

One of the creatures looks like a lion, the second has the form of an ox, and the third has a human face, while the fourth resembles an eagle with wings spread out as though in flight. The four creatures are covered with eyes front and back and their wings are covered with eyes.
“Why do they keep saying that?”
“That is what they were created to do. The same way every one of us is created to worship God, and for God’s pleasure,” Iyke replies.

I notice that whenever the living creatures give glory and honour and thanks to the One sitting on the throne, the One who lives forever and ever, the twenty-four elders fall down and worship the One who lives forever and ever, laying down their crowns before the throne they say with a loud voice, “You are worthy, O Lord our God, to receive glory and honour and power. For you created everything, and it is for your pleasure that they exist and were created.”
The One sitting on the throne takes a scroll in His right hand with writings inside and outside and sealed with seven seals.
An angel appears and shouts with a loud voice, “Who is worthy to break the seals on this scroll and unroll it?”
And there is silence.
“Is there no one worthy to open the scroll?” I whisper to Iyke.
Iyke drops his gaze without responding. I suddenly begin to weep.
“Why are you weeping Bitty?” he asks me.
“Because no one could be found in heaven and on earth to read the scroll,” I reply.

But one of the twenty-four elders says to me, “Stop weeping! Look, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the heir of David’s throne, has conquered. He is worthy to open the scroll and break its seven seals.”

When I look in the direction he’s pointing, I notice the lamb that had been killed is now standing between the throne and the twenty-four elders. He steps forward and takes the scroll from the One sitting on the throne. The twenty-four elders immediately fall down before the lamb. Each of the twenty-four elders has a harp and they are also holding golden bowls filled with incense.
“Do you know what the incense in the bowl is?” Iyke asks.
“No,” I reply.
“They are the prayers of God’s people,” he explains.

Then they begin to sing to the Lamb, “You are worthy to take the scroll and break its seal and open it, for you were killed, and your blood has ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation, and you have caused them to become God’s kingdom and His priests. And they will reign on the earth.”
