After, they join a host of thousands and millions of angels around the throne, together with the living beings, singing a mighty chorus, “The Lamb is worthy, the Lamb who was killed, He is worthy to receive power and riches and wisdom and strength and honour and glory and blessing.”

Then a voice like the sound of a multitude as if it is the voice of every creature in heaven, and on the earth, and under the earth and in the sea sing aloud, “Blessing and honour and glory and power belong to the One sitting on the throne and to the Lamb forever and ever.”
And the four living creatures respond, “Amen!”
The twenty-four elders fall down and worship God and the Lamb, for the Lamb and the One sitting on the throne are one.

I hear a loud voice from the throne saying, “Now the dwelling of God is with His people! And He will live with them, and they will be His people. God Himself will be with them and be their God, and He will remove all their sorrows, and there will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain. For the old world and its evils are gone forever.”

“Look, I am making all things new,” says the One sitting on the throne, “It is finished!” he continues, “I am the Alpha and the Omega – the Beginning and the End. To all who are thirsty I will freely give the water of life.”

As soon as he ends speaking an angel comes to us and says, “Come, I will show you the bride, the Lamb’s wife.” Then he carries me away in the spirit to a great, high mountain and shows me a city descending out of heaven from God filled with the glory of God and sparkling like a precious gem and clear like crystal. The city is surrounded by great, high walls with twelve gates and twelve angels on the twelve gates, and each gate has inscriptions on them.
“What are those words written on the gates?” I inquire.
“Those are the names of the twelve tribes of Israel, the son of Isaac, the son of Abraham,” the angel replies.
“And what are those written on the foundation of the city?” For the twelve foundations of the walls of the city bears twelve inscriptions written on them.
“Those are the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb.”

The city is laid out like a square and its appearance is like pure gold, and its main street is also of pure gold, as clear as glass. The walls are made of Jasper built on foundations decorated with precious stones. And the mansions are an indescribable architectural masterpiece, glorious in splendour, each different from the other.
“That is the holy city, Jerusalem,” the angel announces. The city is a desirable city, a home to be sought after.
“But I see no temple in the city?” I comment.
“The old order has passed away Bitty,” Iyke replies.

“The Lord God Almighty and the Lamb is its temple. The city needs neither the sun nor the moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light and the Lamb is its lamp and there will be no night in the city so its gates shall never be shut, but only those whose names are written in the Lamb’s book of life shall enter the city,” he beams.

“Nothing impure shall pass through its gates,” adds the angel, “Such as the cowardly who renounce the name of the Lamb for fear of being persecuted, the unbelieving, the corrupt and the murderers, the sexually immoral, those who practice magic arts and witchcrafts, and all liars and all who bow down to images and worship idols, they have no place in the holy city neither shall they enter it. But God has prepared a place for them – their doom is in the fiery lake of burning sulphur.”
“But how about him,” I ask the angel pointing at Iyke.
“Jehoshaphat?” he asks, “He has washed his clothing in the blood of the Lamb that was slain before the world was made,” he replies.
“As for you, hurry and make yourself ready Bitrus for the time of the marriage feast of the Lamb has come,” the angel says to me as he flaps his wings flying away from me.

Suddenly, I perceive a nauseating odour coming from the filthy clothing I’m wearing. I also notice that Iyke is no longer with me, there’s now a deep gorge separating us. When I turn back again, I observe that the glorious city has vanished, in its place a fierce ugly-looking beast is standing behind me. He has a book in his hand from which he continues to recite its contents – all the deeds that I had done in violation of my conscience and in disobedience of God’s word. I try not to hear him, but I can’t, he speaks furiously against me, accusing me with a loud voice.

Suddenly, I hear voices of people weeping and wailing with a loud voice, and there is thick darkness everywhere. I feel myself lying down on a bed, and I can feel my body is moving. I try to move my hands, but I can’t. I am trapped and my body is damp with sweat and it is airless.
As I struggle, gasping for breath, the movement stops abruptly and I hear Lizzy’s voice screaming, “He’s alive! He’s alive!”
Rays of the light fall on my eyes and I block my eyes as the Cellophane casing is unzipped and opened. I slowly rise from the stretcher and sit up staring at the bamboozled hospital crew.
Lizzy’s cheeks are wet with tears, “Thank you Jesus,” she mutters smouldering with exhilaration and shaking her head from side to side, “Praise God! Thank you, Jesus.”
The bewildered expression of the doctor and two nurses with him tells me the whole story.

I’ve been sitting up on my bed from the early hours of the morning in my hospital room anxiously waiting for Lizzy to come. My mind has been pondering over the revelation of heaven, and over what happened on the mountain – is Sammy still alive?
Then there’s a knock on the door. “Come in,” I croak.
The door opens and Sammy walks in smiling. His wounds are healing and his face is radiant with joy.
“Hi Bitty,” he greets me sitting on the chair beside the bed.
“Hello Sammy,” I reply, “I was just thinking about you. How are you?”
“I’m all right,” he beams, “Thanks. Lizzy told me how you risked your life for me.”
“Don’t thank me Sammy. Lizzy deserves all the praise.” I twitch at a sharp pain in my chest as I move my body.
“Sorry.”
“How did you get out…and how did I come here?”
“I guess you miscalculated. There were two of them that took me to the mountain, the Provost and the Shepherd.”
“Nosa.”

“Yes. He went to gather wood for the offering when you attacked and struck the Provost. Perhaps he heard the rumble and came to his rescue. He was the one who shot you. I guess the sound of his rifle attracted the police and they came to our rescue. They untied me from the altar, and chained the Provost and the Shepherd. When they noticed you were still breathing, they hurriedly made plans to bring you to the hospital…that’s how we got here,” he explains.
“The police?” I mutter, “But we didn’t inform the police.”
“They followed Lizzy to the mountain,” Sammy beams, “They would have burnt us both alive if it were not for their swift intervention.”
“Where is she?”
“She should be on her way. She’s preparing breakfast for you,” he replies.

There’s a rattle at the door, it opens almost immediately and Dr. Daniel comes in. He is accompanied by a police officer dressed up in complete uniform. “How are you this morning Mr. Shak,” Dr. Daniel inquires.
“I’m fine Doctor,” I reply coldly casting an askance stare at the cop who is wearing a smug smile on his face.
“Mr. Shak, DSP Ekanem Udofia would like to have a word with you,” he says.
I dread cops. “But Doctor –”
“It’s okay Mr. Shak,” he replies cutting me off. “Please make it brief and no harsh words okay,” Dr. Daniel tells him. “May I ask you to excuse them, young man?” he says turning to Sammy.
“No, let him stay,” the officer replies stopping Sammy who is already standing, “Sit.”
Dr. Daniel leaves the room.
“You are very lucky to still be alive, Mr. Shak. The story would have been different if we hadn’t intercepted Mr. Obiajulu and his accomplice early enough,” he begins.
“Thanks uh – DSP Ekanem,” I reply.
“Well, you really owe Elizabeth the gratitude,” he says.
I squint at him wondering, did she invite the police to follow us?

“Well, sometimes some bad habits can turn out to be a blessing in disguise,” he continues smiling wickedly. Then he saunters to the window, opens the curtain and closes it again. “Mr. Obiajulu has been in the bad books of the criminal investigation department for sometime, but he has managed to escape because of his connection with the powers that be. But he got unlucky this time…he will be arraigned in court and I want to invite you to come and witness against him.”
“I beg your pardon…I don’t know who he is. I have nothing to say on his behalf and I have nothing to say against him,” I reply angrily.

“Yes you do Mr. Shak,” he says calmly beaming smugly at me, “You co-headed the Inverted Cross Fraternity with his nephew, Ikenna Obiajulu who later changed his name to Jehoshaphat,” he pauses and stares hard at me, “I also graduated from the Metropolitan University, and it was my testimony that freed him from the accusation of the murder of Sephia Rwang.”

Naturally, I have a weakness with acquaintances – matching faces with names is not such an easy task for me. Notwithstanding Ekanem obviously would have been a bloody civilian when he was in school, and so it is likely he knew me from a distance.
“I have to call my lawyer. I won’t say anything else to you in the absence of my lawyer,” I reply.

“That may not be very necessary Mr. Shak. In order not to open up old wound, I will not bother about charging you for the murder of Sephia Rwang. I’ll leave that to your conscience. I’m here to negotiate.”
“You have no right to accuse me of her death.”
“I said I’ll leave that to your conscience, Mr. Shak.”
“That’s what you’re insinuating officer and I can hold that against you.”

“Well, I learnt a term in psychology when I reviewed the brief of your best friend’s trial, which gave me the clue to Sephia’s murderer, the term evidence of system. You don’t know what that means, do you Mr. Shak?”
“I’m not testifying for or against anybody. Now if that’s the only thing you have to say, can you excuse me now, I’d like to have some rest.”

“I expected you’d decline my offer. But there’s one more thing Mr. Shak, you have a charge to answer and so you will have to suspend your intended trip back to London. Two weeks ago, on the seventh of July precisely, a case was brought to our office against you for assault and attempted rape. I became interested when I heard that the culprit’s name was Bitrus Shak. I couldn’t miss that. But for the plea of the complainant, I would have ordered you to be arrested. So I detailed my men to keep an eye on you,” he takes a pause.

“However, our attention shifted when we discovered that someone else was trailing you. We followed the track, and eventually it led us to Mr. Obiajulu…I may have no choice but to press charges against you for the death of Sephia Rwang if you refuse to comply with me Mr. Shak. I’m sure you wouldn’t want to be publicly disgraced over such an accusation.”
“You have no proof of your claim,” I retort.

“Yes I do. One of your most prominent features in school then is your brand of cigarette – I could get someone to testify to the fact that you are perhaps the only student who regularly smokes Camel as a matter of style. The photograph of the scene clearly shows a cigarette packet that most likely fell off from you during your brutal act.”

Now I remember this son of a bitch. He was a mass communication student and also writes for the campus journal, and particularly enjoys doing stories on cultism. But it was Sephia’s case that really brought him into the limelight.
When my eyes meet Sammy’s, he pleads with his stare for me to consent.
“So why didn’t you turn the evidence to the security?” I ask the cop.

“Let’s say, there is always a right time to do the right thing,” he says smiling smugly at me, “Besides, it was my personal effort in fully investigating that case that convinced me I had a future in the police. In order words, you helped me discover my prospects in the police force. And so, I’d like to pay back that gesture, if you’re willing to accept it.”
“What happens to the evidence afterwards?”

“Oh the photographs?” he gasps, “I’ll hand them over to you as soon as the trial is over.” He brings out a tape recorder from his pocket and presses a button stopping it. “Sammy has already agreed to help us. He has furnished us with privileged information about the recent activities of the Inverted Cross Fraternity. I want to assure you of the safety of your life during and after the court proceedings until you return to London,” he concludes.

Sammy drops his eyes on the floor as I turn to him. It is unheard-of for a disciple of the inverted cross to let out information to the cops let alone agree to divulge the secret activities of the fraternity.
DSP Ekanem comes to the bed and offers me a handshake. “I wish you a speedy recovery. The trial will commence in ten days, I’ll keep you informed.” He turns around and walks briskly out of the room.
“I want to be left alone,” I tell Sammy coldly.

Sammy quietly stands up and walks towards the door. He halts, turns back to me and whines, “Destroying the photographs won’t guarantee freedom from the wrath of God’s judgment, Bitty. There’s another record of charges against you that is weightier, and only the blood of Jesus can annul it. If you let him, He’ll cancel it by nailing it to the cross and free your conscience of guilt.”
“Get away from me you murderer!” I bark at him.
“There is no longer any condemnation against me Bitty. My sins have been forgiven…I am born again.”
“I’m not surprised you could be so gullible. She’s deceived you by that vain philosophy of hers you lily-livered traitor.”

“The real coward is the man who is afraid to take responsibility for his actions,” Sammy replies, “Swallow your pride and ask God for forgiveness. You know deep down that Lizzy’s message is true. If you repent and confess your sins to God, He is faithful and just to forgive, and He will purify you with the blood of Jesus for there’s no other name by which a man may be saved.”

“I don’t blame you. Would you be here talking this nonsense if I hadn’t risked my life to rescue you from the mountain? Were you not reciting the holy book, why didn’t Jesus come down from heaven to save you?”

“May God have mercy on your soul for your arrogance. What happened on the mountain was a fulfilment of the prophecy written by Isaiah, that in the last days, the mountain of the Lord’s temple will be established as the highest of the mountains. It will be raised above the hills and people from all over the world will go there to worship. Many nations will come and say, “Come and let us go to mountain of the Lord, to the temple of the God of Israel that He may teach us His ways so that we may walk in His paths. For in those days, the Lord’s teaching and His word will go out from Jerusalem. The Lord will settle disputes for many peoples. They will beat their swords into ploughshares and their spears into pruning hooks.”
“Get out!” I blast at him.
Sammy leaves the room and gently closes the door.

I have resisted the persuasion to accept this strange doctrine. Not because of what Lizzy and Sammy are saying about Jesus, about the resurrection and about the kingdom of God. But because of the strange dreams I’ve had recently. At first they seemed weird and implausible, but up till now none of those words has failed. And none of Lizzy’s predictions since the death of Iyke has failed too.

The most convincing of her predictions is Sammy’s rescue from the hill, which is a confirmation of what the angel man said, which I could have sworn to be an impossible mission. Sammy’s right about my fear of taking responsibility for the consequences of my actions. I find it hard to believe that my sins will be forgiven just by merely telling Him about it and believing that Jesus is Lord despite that it was precisely declared by the voice that spoke to me.

Although I’ve hidden this from Lizzy all this while, I am convinced, from what the police officer said she has been told about it at the police station. Now I know why the subject of repentance from sin has featured in most of our conversation after my attempt to rape her.
“Hi Bitty! How are you feeling?” she says as she storms into the room without knocking.
I remain silent.

“I had to rush home to get you something to eat,” she continues as she drops the picnic basket on the table removing the stuff inside on the table, “Guess what I brought you – bread, fish and milk, your favourite.”
“I don’t want your poisoned food,” I growl.
She stops and turns to me. “What’s the matter, Bitty?”
“Does the name DSP Ekanem ring a bell?”
She refrains from answering and continues unpacking the items in the basket.
“I promised you I won’t do it again, Lizzy, why did you have to take the issue to the police?”
“Bitty, please don’t take it like that. I didn’t mean to –”
“How else do you want to see it?”

“I don’t mean to insult you Bitty, but from what I’ve experienced, I didn’t have any reason to believe that it won’t happen again. My fear was justified when Ekanem informed me about Sephia’s murder,” she takes a pause. “He sort of took a personal interest in the case when I mentioned your name.”

“That sounds like the meaning of the term evidence of system, is it? Now let me guess what happened later, you made a deal with the police not to have me arrested in order to use me as a guide to get to Dike.”
“That’s not true,” she replies, “I did request that they stay action, but that decision had nothing to do with Dike,” she explains.
“You guys, you and your police friend took advantage of me to achieve your selfish goals. Shit! I’ve been such a stooge.”
“Will you stop being ridiculous, Bitty? I didn’t even discuss about our plan to save Sammy with him.”
“Your plan! Yeah it has always been your plan, not ours. Then how did they get to know about our trip to Morashe hill?”
“I don’t know Bitty, please will you cut this out?”
“You betrayed me to the cops. I should have known you were merely putting up an appearance with your hypocrisy all these while.”

“You know what your problem is, you act like the proverbial fool who criticizes someone else for having a pin in his eye, while ignoring the log of wood in his own eye. Why don’t you deal with the conviction of your heart once and for all?”
We stare at each other in the hush. Then she shakes her head and comes to the side of the bed sitting on the chair.

“I’m not condemning you, Bitty,” she says in a low tone, “And you know the good news, God is not condemning you either. He didn’t send His Son to condemn the world, but to save it. But if your heart condemns you, surely God is greater than your heart and He knows everything. Are you so blind to see, and so ungrateful to acknowledge God’s mighty deliverance in spite of your sin. If He hadn’t sent the police to the mountain you probably would have been burnt on the altar together with Sammy.”
My conscience pricks me at her thought-provoking words. I remain silent, staring sheepishly at her not knowing what to say.
“You revile Jehoshaphat for his wrongs, but…” she chuckles, “God has forgiven Him and given him a place and inheritance among the saints in Christ.”

Then I recall the vision I saw when I was on the brink of death – the glory of heaven, the words of the angel and the worship songs of the creatures and the elders, and all that was said about the Lamb. I bite my lips tensely as I recall the flight of the angel and the gorge that appeared and separated me from Iyke and the recitation of the beast against me, the recitation of all the evil deeds I have done, even the ones I did in secret.

“I won’t forget the night I guided you to his house – he introduced you to me as his best friend, which tells me how much he trusted you Bitty. But you betrayed him, you made him go to prison and suffer for a crime he didn’t commit. He was led as a lamb to the slaughter when he stood trial for what he didn’t do. He was scourged, wounded and crushed because of your sin, for which he died a painful death afterwards.”
“Enough! I scream.

“You turned the other way and refused to visit him in prison,” she continues, “Because you thought in error that he was punished by God for his sins…no Bitty. He took upon himself the punishment of the sins of the disciples of the inverted cross, that through his death God may set them free from the bondage of the irrevocable testament. Even though this has been made clear to you Bitty, you still deny and reject him…yet you call him your friend.”

When Whirlwind and I came to check on Iyke and I heard Sephia’s voice in the room with Iyke, I was overwhelmed with jealousy. When I couldn’t contain it anymore, the thought of going back to the room to rape her came to my mind so I left the group without anybody’s notice as we marched to the hostel to hunt Femi.

Immediately after I left the room after assaulting her, I discovered that my cigarette packet had fallen off my pocket. Knowing it would give off my identity, I turned back to go and fetch it, but I had to beat a hasty retreat when I heard a loud blast – Sephia had shot herself! I recognized the sound of the pistol– it was the sound of Iyke’s automatic pistol. Sephia made good her threat to kill herself if I forced her. Knowing that the gunshot would attract people to his room and because I was afraid of being noticed, I turned back and proceeded to the female hostel. Iyke didn’t realize I had been away from the group. I resolved within me to absolutely deny being to the room if Iyke discovers the cigarette packet.

But he never did…he was arrested on his way to his room after the botched hit. And because I saved the situation at the hostel that night, he didn’t suspect me. Even though I didn’t kill Sephia, I can’t exonerate myself from being responsible for her death.
“I regret what happened okay. I regret forcing her, but I didn’t shoot her. God knows I didn’t.”
“That’s not the attitude Bitty,” she says lowering the tone of her voice.

“What do you want me to do? How else do you want me to show that I’m sorry about what I did,” I reply with frustration, “I don’t know what comes over me, I don’t know why I keep doing it,” I say gasping, “I can’t help myself, Lizzy…I can’t help myself,” I snivel.

“Bitty…God will help you if you will turn to him right now, if you will acknowledge that you are a sinner and confess your sins to Him instead of excusing yourself. If you believe that Jesus shed His blood for you and if you receive Him into your heart as your Lord, you shall be forgiven and He will purge your heart of the evil conscience, from the condemnation of your sins.”
“Please pray for me, please pray for me,” I weep loudly.
“Are you willing to accept Jesus as your Lord and Saviour?”
“Yes Lizzy, I am willing,” I cry shedding tears.
“Say this prayer after me…Heavenly Father.”
“Heavenly Father,” I repeat.

“Your word testifies against me that I am a sinner,” I sob following Lizzy’s words, “Even though I have denied your word and rejected Your Son Jesus, my conscience accuses me everyday that I have grossly sinned against You. I repent today of my sins and ask You to forgive me and cleanse me with the blood of Jesus. I renounce satan’s dominion and influence over my life and accept the lordship of Jesus by my confession today. I also ask that by the power in the blood of Jesus Christ that the covenant of slavery and death to satan be abolished. I enter into a new covenant relationship with You by the precious blood of Jesus and promise to live by Your word. Please accept me as Your own son and send me Your Holy Spirit, as I take You to be my Father through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. Amen.”

After reciting the prayers, Lizzy prays to God to accept me and manifest Himself to me, and that He may heal my weaknesses. When I open my eyes after her prayers, everything around me seems bright and beautiful, and suddenly there is a release from the state of insecurity and fear that has plagued my soul. And for the first time in my life I am experiencing peace, an indescribable joy fills my heart.

Lizzy comes back later in the afternoon with Sammy. She presents me with a new Bible and expounds the gospel to me, and patiently answers my endless questions. It is amazing getting to know more about Jesus not just as a master who compels you to obey a religious code, but also especially as a loving friend who is acquainted with my weaknesses, my sufferings and is willing to help me when I am tempted.

By the power of His love and His grace upon my life I have shared the message of God’s love that I once opposed, the gospel of Jesus Christ to many people explaining from the Holy Scriptures that Jesus Christ is the Son of God and that believing in His name we have eternal life.

chapters.title: Chapter FIFTEEN
content: Trolleys squeak over polished tiles. Children cry because they do not understand why one hand must leave another. Porters drag luggage through impatient legs. Families gather in small circles of laughter and sorrow, speaking quickly as if words could delay boarding time. Above us, the public address system swallows names and destinations in a metallic voice, calling people away from those who still need them.
I stand beside Bitty and Lizzy with my hands pushed into my pockets, watching strangers cling to their luggage as if the bags contain their whole lives.
Perhaps they do. Mine is not even in a bag. Mine is scattered between a grave, a Cross, and a fraternity that now watches me as a traitor.

Ever since I laid the Cross upright on the Hangman’s grave, my life has ceased to belong to me. I feel it in the way disciples look at me now. I hear it in the silence that follows my name. I see it in the strange dreams that trouble my sleep and in the fear that sits quietly at the back of my mind, waiting for nightfall.
The GODs do not forgive rebellion.
And what I did at that grave was more than rebellion. It was a declaration. Only I am anxious of how what I declared will change the course of my life.
“Hello Sammy, are you here?” Bitty asks, touching my arm lightly.
I return to myself.
“I am sorry,” I reply. “I was carried away.”
“You look as if you did not sleep.”
“I am thinking of how I am going to carry on,” I confess. “With you leaving, I do not have anyone. The only family I know are members of the fraternity.”
Bitty’s face falls.

For a moment, the prince in him disappears and the fugitive in him stands before me. I have always sensed that Bitty carries more than regret. Regret has a way of bending a man. But what I see in him is not merely the weight of what he has done. It is also the burden of what he knows and has refused to say.

“Stay true to the Cross, Sammy,” he cautions. “You will have pushback. You already know that. But you have God’s backing, and you have our support too. Do not go back because hunger or fear starts preaching to you.”
I nod, but his words do not settle me.
Support! It sounds good when people are standing beside you. But it sounds distant when one of them is boarding a flight to another continent.

There is something I have wanted to ask him since the day I walked away from the Hangman’s grave with the world around me altered. Something about the memoir. Something about the image. Something about the Program the GODs spoke of before I laid the wreath. The more I try to forget it, the more it presses against my thoughts.
The Seagull. The Archer. The Key. He had not drawn those images for decoration. Prophecy never wastes ink.
“There is something I have been meaning to ask you, Bitty.
“The matchstick image in the memoir,” I continue, lowering my voice. “Is there more to the connection between the three of us—me, you, and the former Hangman?”
Bitty’s lips tighten slightly.
I glance at Lizzy.
She reads that the conversation is for men who were once bound by a brotherhood covenant. She rises before Bitty could say a word.
“Let me use the restroom, I’ll be back soon,” she says. She walks away with that calm dignity that always makes her seem taller than her body.
When she is gone, I lean towards Bitty.
His eyes flicker past my shoulder to Lizzy. It is not fear. Not exactly. It is the look of a man who hears a door opening in a house he has spent years trying not to enter.
Bitty lifts his drink and takes a sip, but he does not swallow immediately. Unease passes across his face.

I press on before he can shut the door again. “You have been evading my questions since the night I was rescued. Every time I bring up the fraternity, you tell me to build my new faith and forget the past. But I cannot forget what keeps following me.”
His eyes move briefly towards the departure gate. Then back to me.
“I am not asking because I am curious,” I say. “I am asking because I need to understand what is happening to me.”
He says nothing.
So I tell him. I tell him about the Program. Not everything. Not loudly. Not carelessly. But enough.

I tell him about what the GODs expected of me before I laid the wreath on the Hangman’s grave. I tell him about the pressure I feel from the Order, as if some invisible hand still insists that I must complete what was written for me. I tell him about the suspicion that Ikenna knew more than he revealed, and that the image in his memoir may have been a map rather than a memory.
Then I ask the question I have been afraid to ask. “Were you chosen too?”

For a while, he only stares at the drink in his hand. Then Bitty lowers his cup. The noise of the airport grows around us. A child laughs somewhere behind me. A woman argues with an airline attendant. Another boarding announcement rises and dissolves into static. Life continues with offensive indifference.
Finally, Bitty speaks. “Ikenna mentioned a Program to me.”
The words enter me like cold water.
“But we never discussed it further,” he adds quickly. “I was not interested in the family’s affairs anymore, and he knew that.”

I study his face. I believe him. Partly because I want to. Partly because I know men like Bitty exist in the fraternity. Men who joined for ambition, fear, friendship, lust, or youthful stupidity, then quickly discovered that initiation is not the same thing as belonging.

Some regret their decision after one week. Some after one month. Some after the first order they cannot obey and the first secret they cannot unknow. But they stay because there is no way out. They become present but absent. Members but not believers. Useful but not trusted.

We recognize them. We coerce them. We mock their weakness. And if they persist in their unwillingness, they are excluded from serious conversations about family affairs. They are given errands, not secrets. They hear rumours, not instructions. They see shadows, not the throne.
But Bitty is different.
He was not an ordinary disciple. He stood beside the Hangman at the beginning. He was close enough to see the foundation when it was being laid.
And perhaps that is why his ignorance troubles me.
“Considering who you were to him,” I say quietly, “how could the Hangman had mentioned something that important and leave it there?”
Bitty’s jaw tightens.
“Because he knew I had stepped back.”
“From him?”
“From all of it.”
There is pain in his voice now. Pain and defensiveness.
“I was tired, Sammy. You think everyone inside the Order is hungry for secrets. Some of us were choking on the ones we already had.”
I look away. The answer should comfort me. It does not. “Did he tell you what the Program was meant to do?”
“Did he tell you why the three symbols were drawn?”
“Did he tell you why I would be called the Key?”
Bitty’s eyes return to mine.
This time, he does not answer quickly. And that hesitation frightens me more than a lie.
“What did he say?” I press.
Bitty exhales slowly. “He said some doors are not opened by strength.”
I feel my chest tighten.
“What does that mean?”
“I do not know.”
“You do.”
“I don’t, Sammy.”
His voice hardens, then softens almost immediately. “I know you want answers. I know you think answers will make the fear easier to bear. They will not.”
“That is not your decision to make.”
He looks at me then, and for a moment I see the old Bitty, the co-founder, the almost-Hangman, the man who once stood near darkness and understood its grammar.
“There are things the Order hides even from those who think they are near the throne,” he says. “I did not bother wanting to know things they did not want me to know.”
“Or things you were afraid to know.”
The words leave my mouth before I can restrain them.
Bitty flinches. For a moment, guilt opens in his face like an old wound.
Then he looks away. Perhaps I have gone too far. Perhaps not far enough.

“Ikenna was not like us,” he says finally. “Even when he was lost, he was searching for something. The rest of us wanted power, escape, women, protection, influence, revenge. He wanted meaning. That made him dangerous to the Order long before he opposed them.”
“And me?”
Bitty turns to me.
“What about me?”
“Why am I in his memoir?”
His silence becomes an answer.

“Why did the GODs speak to me about a Program before I even understood what was happening? Why did my sight return after I laid the wreath? Why did the Cross stand upright on his grave if this was only about leaving the fraternity?”
My voice has risen slightly. I lower it. “What did Jehoshaphat expect me to do?”
Bitty’s expression shifts. There it is again. A guarded fear.
“I don’t know everything,” he says. “But I know this. Whatever Ikenna saw concerning you, the Order saw something too. That is why they will not simply kill you.”
A cold sensation moves through me.
“They need me alive.”
“For now.”
The words are spoken softly. Too softly.
The boarding announcement for his flight blares through the hall.
Lizzy returns before I can ask another question. She looks from Bitty to me, and I know she senses the tension between us.
Bitty gathers his passport and ticket. I help him with his hand luggage to the barrier.
For a few seconds we stand there, surrounded by passengers moving towards the departure gate, each person obeying a call to somewhere else.
Lizzy embraces him. “Have a safe trip.”
“Take care of yourself, okay?”
“I will,” she says.
Bitty takes his bag from me. He hesitates before turning towards the boarding gate.
Then he leans close enough for only me to hear. “Do not let them use your loneliness to call you back,” he whispers. “Sometimes the devil does not come with horns. Sometimes he comes as family.”
He grips my shoulder. “And Sammy…”
I look at him.
“If the Program comes looking for you again, do not answer it from fear. Wait until God tells you what the Key is for.”
Before I can respond, he hugs me quickly and steps away.
I watch him disappear into the line. And I know that another door has closed behind me.

Sometimes I think of Jehoshaphat and wonder if he knew the cost of the seed he was planting. Perhaps he did. Perhaps that is why his life troubles me more in death than it ever would if I had met him when he walked among us.

He has become a witness I cannot argue down. A memory I cannot shrug off. A voice I cannot silence. I do not know where the road is taking me. I do not know whether Bitty has told me all he knows or only enough to leave with a quieter conscience.
But I know this. The Cross on Ikenna’s grave did not end anything. It opened something. And whatever had been opened was now waiting for me.

chapters.title: Chapter SIXTEEN
content: The lecture hall smells of chalk dust, damp wood, and bodies that have sat too long under ceiling fans that only stir the heat around. The class has just ended, but the lecturer’s voice still seems trapped in the room, hovering above scattered notebooks, half-erased formulas, and the restless scraping of benches.

Students file out noisily, relieved to be free from the suffocation of the hall. Some argue over assignments. Others laugh too loudly. A few linger around the front row, copying what remains on the blackboard before the next class wipes it away.
I leave with a course mate who has been trying to explain the topic we just finished.
His voice is urgent, his fingers moving in the air as if invisible numbers are still hanging between us. “So, once you get the variance, you square the –” He stops. Not gradually. Instantly.
His eyes shift past my shoulder to the foot of the stairs. The corridor ahead of us has changed. I know it before I turn. Some silences do not fall by accident. They arrive with authority.
Steel is standing near the staircase with Legion and two other disciples.

They are dressed in black tops and black berets, as if mourning the man I buried on the day I laid the Cross upright on his grave. Their dark clothes absorb the afternoon light spilling through the corridor windows. Steel’s sunglasses sit firmly on his face even though he is indoors, giving him the detached arrogance of a man who wants the world to see only what he permits.
My course mate takes one step back. Then another. “I’ll see you later,” he mutters. He does not wait for my reply. I do not blame him.
In the university, wisdom sometimes looks like cowardice.

Students slow their steps as they pass. Nobody stops. Nobody wants to be a witness. Nobody wants to become part of a matter that does not concern them. That is how fear survives on campus. Not because everyone agrees with evil, but because everyone learns to leave evil alone.
Steel removes his sunglasses slowly and folds them with ceremony. The smell of alcohol and tobacco reaches me before his words do. “Lethal Weapon.”
I do not answer.
The name no longer sits comfortably on me. Once, it was a title. A warning. A badge of relevance. Now it sounds like a curse someone keeps trying to return to my hands.

Steel steps forward. The other two disciples remain behind him, their faces hard and watchful. Legion stands slightly to the side, his expression carrying something more complicated than hostility. Admiration. Confusion. Pity. Perhaps all three.
“The GODs are giving you a final notice,” Steel declares. His voice is calm enough to sound official. “Reverse the Cross.”
The words settle in the corridor.
A few students pretend not to hear, but their footsteps slow. Someone closes a classroom door nearby. Another student suddenly remembers something in the opposite direction.
I look at Steel. “What Cross?”
His jaw tightens. He does not like games unless he is the one playing them. “You know the Cross I am talking about.”
“I know many crosses.”
His eyes narrow. “The one you placed on the Hangman’s grave.”
At the mention of it, something moves inside me. A grief. A fear. A strange courage I did not own before that day.
Steel watches my face, perhaps expecting guilt, or panic, or apology.
I give him none.
“That Cross is not mine to reverse,” I reply firmly.
Steel laughs once. A dry, humourless sound. “You are beginning to talk like those church people.”
“I have placed my allegiance on the One who was crucified on the Cross.”
For a moment, even the air seems to pause. Legion shifts his weight. One of the disciples behind Steel scoffs.
Steel studies me as if trying to decide whether I have become brave or merely stupid. “You are playing with fire.”
“No,” I reply quietly. “I am walking out of it.”

His face hardens. Then he looks me over from head to toe, as though poverty is a stain on my shirt and hunger a weakness he can smell. “Dey no dey tell person say rain dey fall,” he taunts in pidgin. “Hunger go give you sense. You never hungry reach.”
The words sting because there is truth inside them.
I have no father to call. No mother to hide behind. No home that is truly mine. No money. No protection. The only structure that ever claimed me now stands before me demanding my soul as rent.
Steel knows this. That is why he smiles.
Legion steps forward slightly. “You see dat demon-possessed chick wey you dey follow go church,” he says, “she go ruin your life.”
Lizzy.
Something in me rises. I turn to him slowly. “You, Legion, call somebody demon possessed?”
His smile fades.
“Do you even know the meaning of your nickname?”
His eyes flash.
“Yes, I am Legion,” he boasts. “And I am indomitable. I am proud of who I am.” He says it loudly, but something in his voice lacks conviction.
He looks younger than I remember. Or perhaps I am seeing him clearly for the first time.
“I used to look up to you, Lethal Weapon,” he continues. “Many of us still regard you as a role model. What happened to you? Why are you like this?”

The question almost disarms me. Not because it is profound. Because it is honest. For a moment, I see the boy beneath the nickname. The boy who joined the fraternity looking for power and found identity in violence. The boy who still believes that being feared is the same as being secure.
Perhaps I am looking at my former self.
“Let’s say I woke up from the slumber you guys are still enjoying,” I reply.
Steel cuts in. “You know you cannot do this.” His voice drops. “It is blood in, blood out.”
The words carry the weight of an old law. A law I once repeated to frighten others. A law I once believed.
I look at him and feel a strange calm settle inside me. “Then let him who owns the blood contend for the covenant.”
Steel’s eyes darken. “You dare the great one?”
“I belong to something greater.”

He steps closer. For a moment, I think he will strike me. The corridor becomes completely still. Even the students who had pretended to walk away now hover at a distance, caught between fear and curiosity.
Steel’s face is inches from mine. His breath smells of gin and cigarette smoke. “Do not deceive yourself,” he whispers. “I know that cross.”
I say nothing.
“Your faith will not save you on the day.”
The day. He does not need to explain. In the fraternity, some words carry entire rituals inside them. Some days are not dates. They are appointments with darkness.
Steel puts his sunglasses back on. The gesture ends the meeting. Or postpones the sentence. He turns to leave, and the two disciples follow immediately.

Legion remains. Only for a breath. His eyes meet mine. For a second, the boastfulness is gone. The indomitable mask slips. What remains is not anger. It is pity. Or fear. Perhaps he is pitying me. Perhaps he is afraid that I may be right. Then he looks away and follows Steel down the corridor.
I remain where I am. The world gradually resumes. Students begin moving again. Voices return. Feet slap against the stairs. Somebody laughs too loudly to prove he was never afraid.
But the day has already changed.
I look toward the open windows where sunlight falls across the corridor floor in broken rectangles.
Steel is wrong.

My faith may not save me from pain. It may not save me from hunger. It may not save me from loneliness. It may not even save me from death. But it has already saved me from going back. And for now, that is enough.

chapters.title: Chapter SEVENTEEN
content: The road from the faculty block to the hostel area bends behind the old Pharmacy laboratory building before opening toward the student union building. At this hour it is usually alive with noise. Students returning from lectures. Photocopy vendors calling for customers. Okada riders leaning on their motorcycles beneath the almond trees.
Girls in pairs walking slowly as if the afternoon belongs to them. Boys arguing football, politics, and exams with the seriousness of men debating national destiny.

But this afternoon, the road feels strangely thinned out. Not empty. Just withdrawn. As though the campus has taken one careful step backwards to see what will happen to me. I walk alone with three notebooks pressed beneath my arm.
The sun bears down on the cracked asphalt. Heat rises from the road in trembling waves. Somewhere behind the laboratory, I hear the sound of a car. The engine approaches too fast for a campus road.

A dark saloon car tears past me, close enough for the wind from its movement to slap my shirt against my body. The boys inside turn their heads as they pass. All of them. As if they have been looking for my face in a crowd.
I keep walking.
Do not run. That is the first rule when danger recognizes you. Do not give it the satisfaction of seeing your fear arrive before your body.
The car screeches ahead, brakes hard, then reverses. Students nearby slow down.
A girl crossing the road stops midway and retreats towards a kiosk.
The car blocks the path.
I step to the left.
The driver jerks forward and blocks me again.
My grip tightens around my notebooks.
Four doors open. Four boys step out. I know the fraternity before I know their names. Golgotha. Old debts travel faster than forgiveness.

The tallest of them smiles and stretches his neck from side to side. Another boy pulls a short metal rod from inside the car and taps it against his palm. The sound is light, almost playful, but every tap carries a promise.
“Lethal Weapon,” the tall one calls. “Where your angels dey?” Laughter follows. Not loud. Confident. The kind of laughter men use when they are sure nobody will stop them.
I glance around.
A few students are watching from a safe distance. Nobody moves closer. Nobody calls security. Nobody wants to become part of my punishment.
I do not blame them. I know this theatre. I once acted in it.
The tall one walks towards me. “You no dey greet again?”
“I do not want trouble.” The words taste strange in my mouth. Once, I would have moved first. The old Sammy rises inside me like smoke from a buried fire.

I know how to step into the tall one’s blind side. I know how to break the rhythm of the boy holding the rod. I know how to turn surprise into advantage. I know that the driver will be the slowest because drivers always think the vehicle makes them important. I know how to make the watchers scatter before they understand what has started.
My body remembers violence faster than my spirit remembers grace. But I also know something else. If I answer violence with violence, I may win the road and lose my soul.
The first blow comes from the side. Not with the rod. A fist.
It catches my jaw and turns the world white for half a second. My notebooks fall.
Before I recover, another kick lands against my thigh. I stumble backwards. The tall one shoves me, and I hit the ground with my elbow first. Pain shoots up my arm.
“Stand up now,” one of them taunts. “Abi your Jesus no give you strength?”
I push myself halfway up. A foot drives into my side. Air leaves me. The sound from my mouth is not a cry. Not yet. More like the body protesting against humiliation.
The notebooks scatter across the road. Loose sheets fly toward the gutter. Somebody laughs and kicks one of the books beneath the car.
“See am,” the boy with the rod says. “The hard man don turn church rat.”
I taste blood on my lip. I spit it aside.
For one dangerous second my eyes catch an iron implement lying near the gutter. A broken piece of something. Useful. Heavy enough. Close enough. My fingers twitch.
The boy with the rod notices. He follows my gaze, walks over, and kicks the object farther away.
“Ah ah,” he says, grinning. “You still dey calculate?”
The laughter comes again. This time it enters me deeper than the pain.
The old Sammy wants to rise. He does not beg. He does not explain. He does not take shame in public. He answers it.
I close my eyes.
Not to pray. To hold myself down. “I do not want to fight,” I mutter.
The tall one bends toward me. “What did you say?”
“I do not want to fight.”
“Because you are afraid?”
I open my eyes. “No.”
He frowns.
“I just do not want to fight. If there is anything I have done to you, forgive me.”
For a moment, silence falls. Then they burst into laughter.
“Did you hear that?” the boy with the rod shouts toward the watching students. “The hard man is begging for forgiveness.”
The tall one kicks my books into the dust.
“Forgiveness no dey settle old account.” He bends closer, his voice dropping. “Today na message. Next time, na sentence.”
He stands and snaps his fingers. The others move back toward the car.
The whole attack has lasted less than two minutes. Yet by the time they enter the vehicle, it feels as though the afternoon has been dragged across my body.
The car reverses, swerves around me, and speeds away. Dust rises behind it. For several seconds nobody comes. Then danger turns the corner and courage returns to the spectators.

Two students approach first. Then a third. A girl retrieves one of my notebooks from the gutter. Another student picks up loose sheets, dusting them with his palm as if carefulness can restore dignity.
“Bro, can you stand?”
I try.
My knees refuse. Two boys lift me by the arms. I lean against shoulders that do not know my name. For the first time, I understand how heavy helplessness is.

Mainland Hospital smells of antiseptic and drugs. The waiting area is crowded with patients who have learned to suffer quietly. A woman fans a feverish child with an old hospital card. A man with a bandaged wrist argues softly with a nurse. Somewhere beyond the corridor, a trolley squeaks over cracked tiles.
Lizzy sits beside me long after the nurse finishes dressing my bruises. She says very little. But her silence is not empty. It is the kind of silence that sits with pain instead of preaching over it.

Every now and then her eyes move to the swelling near my cheek, then to the blood-stained gauze in the metal tray, then back to me. Each time, she looks as if she is restraining herself from asking the same question again.
Why did you not fight back?
I am grateful she does not ask. Because I am not sure I can answer without sounding weak.
“I’ll be fine,” I say.
She looks at me. “You keep saying that.”
“Because it is true.”
“No,” she says softly. “Because you want it to be true.”
The words find the wound beneath the bruises.
I look away.
Through the half-open door, I can see the corridor. Patients move past slowly. Nurses call names. Evening settles over the hospital, turning the fluorescent lights harsher than they should be.
Lizzy rises after a while.
“I’ll get you something from outside.”
“I don’t need anything.”
“I did not ask.”
There is a firmness in her voice that makes arguing useless. She adjusts the scarf around her neck and walks toward the door. I watch her leave.
Then I hear Steel. “Hi Lizzy.” His voice comes from the corridor.
Too close. Too familiar. Lizzy stops.
“Hello.” Her reply is flat.
I shift slightly on the hospital bed, pain pulling at my side.
“Can I have a word with you?” Steel asks.
“I am sorry. I do not think there is anything we have to talk about.”

There is a pause. Long enough for me to imagine the corridor. Steel standing near the wall with his dark glasses hooked into the collar of his shirt. Lizzy trying to move past him. The fluorescent light falling between them like a warning line.
“Lizzy, wait.”
“I said I have nothing to discuss with you.”
His voice drops. “Why are you treating me like this?”
No answer.
“You cannot just erase everything.”
“Steel, please move.”
“You think I don’t see what is happening?”
“What is happening?”
“You and Sammy.”
The accusation enters the room before either of them does. My body tenses.
Lizzy’s voice remains controlled. “This is not the place.”
“Of course it is not the place,” Steel says. “Because everywhere is suddenly not the place. Every time I try to talk to you, you act like I am a stranger.”
“You are not a stranger.”
“Then what am I?”
Silence. I can almost feel Lizzy measuring the cost of truth.
When she speaks, her voice is lower. “You are someone I have moved on from.”
The corridor goes quiet.
Then Steel laughs. A short, wounded laugh. “Moved on.”
“Please let me pass.”
“You moved on to Ugo. Then to Jehoshaphat. Now Sammy?”
“Do not do this.”
“Answer me.”
“I owe you no explanation.”
A sudden shuffle. My fingers grip the bedsheet.
“Let me go, Steel.”
Her voice is sharp now. Not frightened. Angry.
“You’re hurting me.”
The bed creaks as I try to rise. Pain flashes through my ribs and forces me back.
Outside, Steel’s voice changes. Softens. Breaks at the edges. “I am sorry.”
Another pause.
“I did not mean to hold you like that.”
Lizzy says nothing.
“Lizzy…”
Her footsteps hurry away down the corridor. For several seconds, only the hospital remains. Nurses. Footsteps. Murmurs. My own breathing. Then Steel appears at the door.

He enters my room as if the corridor has not exposed him. As if I have not heard the desperation in his voice. As if Lizzy’s name is not still hanging between us. He closes the door gently behind him.
His eyes rest on my bandaged arm, my bruised face, the tray beside the bed. A smile threatens the corner of his mouth, but it does not fully form.
“So this is what they did to the Cardinal.”
“I am no longer Cardinal.”
“That is not how it works.” He drags a chair closer and sits without asking. The smell of tobacco follows him into the room. “Otunba asked after you.”
I close my eyes.
The name feels like another injury.
“He wants you to lead the operation for his election,” Steel continues. “He is banking on you. You cannot let him down, bro.”
“I am not interested.”
“You are injured, not dead.”
“I said I am not interested.”
He leans forward.
“Do not start this your church thing with me.”
I open my eyes.
“Is that why you came?”
“No. I came because boys from Golgotha raised their hands on you in broad daylight.” His jaw tightens. “That insult is not yours alone.”
“I have forgiven them.”
Steel stares at me. Then he laughs. Not because it is funny. Because he cannot understand it. “Forgiven them?”
“Forgiveness is for people who have strength to punish and choose not to.”
“That is exactly what I am doing.”
His face hardens.
“No. You are confusing weakness for holiness.”
I turn my head toward the window. Outside, the last light of evening is fading.

Violence once offered protection. Cultism once offered family. Power once offered meaning. But now the things I trusted are peeling away like paint from a damp wall. I am unprotected and exposed. I have no one. I am an orphan again. And I am powerless.
Steel stands.
“I will find them.”
“Do not start a fight for me.”
“It is not for you.”
“Steel.”
He looks at me. His eyes are bright with anger, but beneath the anger there is something else. Loss.
He has lost Lizzy. He has lost position. He is watching me walk away from the one brotherhood he still believes in. And because he does not know how to mourn, he reaches for revenge.
“It is all for one, and one for all,” he says. “That is how we buried many boys.”
His expression flickers. For a moment, he looks almost human. Then the mask returns. “Otunba will not wait forever.”
“Let him not wait.”
“You are asking for trouble.”
“No,” I say quietly. “Trouble is asking for me.”
Steel walks to the door.
Before he leaves, he turns. “You think that girl will save you?”
I do not answer.
“She is the reason you are becoming soft.”
Still I say nothing.
His voice drops. “Or maybe you were never as strong as we thought.”
He leaves before I can respond.

The door closes. Revenge tries to return to me. Not as rage. As memory. Faces of boys I once hunted. Names of rival cultists I once marked. Nights we moved through campus believing violence made us gods.
I shut my eyes against them.
My ribs ache. My lip throbs. My pride burns worse than both. But beneath the shame, something steadier remains.

I did not fight today. I did not win either. But perhaps not every victory looks like standing over another man. Perhaps sometimes victory is lying bruised on a hospital bed while the old self begs to rise… and refusing to let him.

chapters.title: Chapter EIGHTEEN
content: I remember the details of those haunting thoughts as they build up inside my head, one after another. At first they come as fragments. A voice. A grave. A knock on the door. The sound of boys laughing after they beat me on the road. The Cross standing upright on the Jehoshaphat’s grave.

Then the fragments begin to gather shape. They become faces. Footsteps. Dark corridors. Hands reaching from behind. Doors opening into rooms I do not remember entering. And always, somewhere inside the dream, something waits for me. Not someone. Something.

I wake most nights with my shirt soaked in sweat, my heart racing as if I have run across the whole campus barefoot. Sometimes I sit up immediately, listening for sounds outside my room. Sometimes I remain still, afraid that if I move, whatever followed me from sleep will discover I am awake.
It is not only fear that troubles me. It is shame. Fear I understand. Fear is simple. Fear tells a man to run. Shame tells him he has nowhere to run to.

I am beginning to understand that a plot is not merely what men plan in secret. A plot is also what the heart agrees to when it is too tired to resist. And these days, my heart is tired. Tired of looking over my shoulder. Tired of wondering whether the next friendly face is a messenger from the Order. Tired of pretending that the old life no longer calls me by name.

The cafeteria is half full when I arrive. It is the kind of afternoon when students pretend to be busy because exams are near. Books lie open on tables. Plates of rice grow cold beside notebooks. The ceiling fans creak above us, pushing the smell of food, sweat, fried plantain, and cheap perfume around the room.

Once, I would have entered this cafeteria and heads would turn for a different reason. Fear. Respect. Curiosity. Now the students who know me glance quickly and look away before our eyes meet. That is worse.
People can fear a dangerous man. But they avoid a fallen one.

I sit near the wall, away from the entrance, where I can see everyone who comes in. It is an old habit, one I have not yet learned how to surrender. My hands rest on the table, but they do not feel steady. I keep rubbing my thumb against the edge of my notebook, as though I can wear down the anxiety by touch.
I requested to see Lizzy. She arrives a few minutes later.

Lizzy enters the cafeteria with the quiet certainty of someone who does not need the room to notice her, yet the room notices all the same. The room seems to change when she enters, not because anyone else notices, but because I do. She carries herself with that composed dignity that makes her appear untouched by the disorder around her.

Her hair is neatly packed, each braid drawn back from her face with an effortless order that makes her features appear softer and sharper at once. A few loose curls frame her cheeks, moving gently as she turns her head. She wears no loud colour, no careless ornament, nothing that pleads for attention. Still, there is a grace about her that makes the noise of the cafeteria seem to fall back a little.

Her Bible is pressed against her chest, held not like an accessory, but like something alive and trusted. Her fingers rest over the cover with familiar tenderness. Her eyes move through the cafeteria, searching from table to table, and when they find me, something in my chest hesitates.
When she sits, she does not immediately speak. That is one of the things I have come to appreciate about her.
Lizzy does not rush into people’s pain with ready-made answers. She sits first. She looks. She listens with her face before her mouth begins to speak.
“Thank you for coming,” I say.
“You sounded troubled.”
“I am always troubled these days.”
She studies me. “Have you eaten?”
I shake my head.
“What would you like?”
“Nothing.”
“You cannot keep living on fear and stubbornness.”
A faint smile tries to come to my face but fails halfway. “I am not hungry.”
“That is not an answer.”
“It is the only one I have.”
For a moment she says nothing. Then her expression softens. “What is happening to you, Sammy?”
The question should be simple. It is not.

I look at the students around us, at their laughter, their whispered gossip, their open books, their ordinary worries. I envy them. Their fears have names: exams, school fees, relationships, home trouble. Mine feels older than language.
“I am constantly having terrible dreams,” I confess. “Nightmares.”
Lizzy becomes still.
“As if I am haunted by strange beings. I know it has to do with my past and all the terrible things I have done. Not like I have personally shed innocent blood, but…” My voice weakens.
The words resist coming out. “But I have carried blood in other ways.”
Lizzy’s eyes do not leave my face.
“I have given orders. I have led boys to places they should not have gone. I have stood by while people were punished. I have called it loyalty. I have called it family. I have called it strength.”
My throat tightens.
“And now something in my dreams keeps telling me I belong to it.”
Her fingers tighten slightly around her Bible. “It?”
“A terrible being.”
The cafeteria noise seems to recede.
“It tells me I belong to… him. That I was marked before I knew what I was carrying. That the Cross on Jehoshaphat’s grave has only delayed what must happen.”
I lean closer.
“And then I see the image in the Book.”
Lizzy whispers, “The Key.”
I nod. “The Key.”
For a while she is silent.
Not because she does not know what to say. Because she understands that some confessions must be allowed to breathe before they are answered.
Then she opens her Bible.
Her movement is calm. Certain. She turns the pages carefully, as though she is not merely searching for a verse but reaching for a weapon she knows how to use.
“Sammy,” she says, “God has forgiven your sins.”
I look away. “That is easy for you to say.”
“No. It is not easy.” Her voice remains gentle. “But it is true.”

She reads from a passage in the book of Romans, her voice low enough not to attract attention, but firm enough to steady the trembling inside me. “There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit. For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus hath made me free from the law of sin and death.”
The words fall on the table between us. I want to believe them. I do. But guilt is stubborn. It does not leave because one verse has entered the room.
Lizzy closes the Bible but keeps her hand on it.

“Your mind is being purged,” she explains. “Like a memory that has stored too many corrupt files. Some things have to be deleted. Some thoughts have to be overwritten. Some voices have to lose authority.”
I listen, but I do not speak.
“It is a process,” she continues. “Sanctification. You have to lock in and allow the Holy Spirit complete His work in you.”
I nod because I do not have strength to argue.
She accepts my silence as agreement. Then she asks the question I knew would come. “Are you still attending meetings of the fraternity?”
Her eyes sharpen slightly.
“I have been avoiding them.”
“Are they threatening you?”
I hesitate.
If I tell her everything, fear will enter her eyes again. And I have seen enough fear lately to last me a lifetime.
“They are watching me,” I say.
“That is not what I asked.”
“I know.”
“Sammy.”
I sigh.
“They need me alive.”
Her face changes. “To reverse the Cross?”
I nod.
“That is my insurance.”
“Insurance?”
Her voice tightens. “That is not insurance. That is a delay.”
Before I can answer, I see Legion enter the cafeteria.
He walks in with his usual swagger, but there is caution behind it. He scans the room quickly, sees me, and begins walking towards our table.
My body reacts before my mind permits it. The muscles in my shoulders tense. My fingers curl. A strange heat rises in my chest.
Lizzy notices. She turns slightly and sees him.
Legion stops beside our table. “Lethal Weapon.”
I do not respond.
His eyes shift briefly to Lizzy. Then back to me. “Can I have a word with you?”
“Say whatever you want to say here.”
He smiles.
“Not in front of your prophetess.”
Lizzy’s eyes harden.
I stand. Not because he deserves privacy. Because I do not want his poison near her. “I’ll be back,” I tell Lizzy.
She holds my gaze for a second longer than necessary. A silent warning. Do not become what you left.
I follow Legion to another table near the corner.
He sits first, relaxed, one arm draped over the back of the chair. I remain standing for a moment, then sit opposite him.
“What do you want?”
He leans closer. “Otunba still wants you.”
“I am not available.”
“He is willing to pay.”
“I said I am not available.”

Legion smiles as though he expected the answer. Then he brings out a brown envelope and places it on the table. The sight of it does something to me. Not because I want the money. Because I know what it represents. The old road. The old hunger. The old bargain.
The old lie that a man can sell one more piece of himself and still remain whole.
“He said I should tell you this is just mobilization,” Legion says. “Five hundred thousand.”
The number lands heavily. Five hundred thousand. There was a time that amount would have sounded like proof of relevance. Now it sounds like bait.

The Order had sent notices for me to meet with Otunba to perfect the plan for his election. I ignored them all. I know Otunba would have provided much more than this if I appeared interested. He is not a man who spends small money when power is at stake.
Legion pushes the envelope closer. “Take it.”
I stare at the brown paper. My heart begins to beat faster. Not from temptation. From anger. The envelope is not money. It is an insult. It assumes I am hungry enough. Afraid enough. Lonely enough.
It assumes that beneath my new confession, Lethal Weapon is still waiting for the right price to resurrect.
“Will you take this thing out of my face?”
Legion’s smile widens. “Bro, no vex. Na business.”
Business. That word. I do not know when I rise. The chair scrapes loudly against the floor. A few students turn.
Legion starts to stand, still smiling, but I am faster. I grab him by the shirt. The old Sammy returns in one violent flash.
His eyes widen. For the first time since he entered the cafeteria, Legion looks afraid.
I snatch the envelope from the table and slam it against his chest. “Go to hell,” I bark, “you and Otunba with his dirty money.”

The cafeteria freezes. Someone drops a spoon. A chair shifts sharply. Students begin to rise from nearby tables, not calmly, but with the practiced speed of people who know violence can spread faster than fire.
Legion does not speak. His face has changed. Shock. Humiliation. Maybe fear.
I release him.
He steps back, adjusts his shirt, and stares at me as if trying to reconcile the preacher with the predator he just saw. Then he leaves. No threat. No insult. Just silence.
That silence frightens me more than his words would have.
Lizzy reaches me before I sit down.
“Sammy.” Her voice is low.
I cannot look at her. My hands are shaking. Not visibly to everyone. But I can feel it. The old anger has retreated, leaving shame in its place. I sit. Slowly. As if my body belongs to another man.
Students continue leaving the cafeteria in hurried clusters. They do not know what has happened, but they know enough to escape before the answer comes with bottles, blades, or gunshots.
Lizzy sits beside me. She says nothing at first. That makes it worse.
“I am sorry,” I say finally. “I lost my cool.”
She gives me a small, reassuring smile. “I know.”
I look at her.
“You are trying your best,” she says.

The kindness almost breaks me. Because I am not sure my best is enough. I look at the table where the envelope had been. Empty now. But in my mind it is still there. Brown. Thick. Waiting. Calling me back.
“Everything will be alright,” Lizzy says.

I want to believe her. I want to believe the nightmares will stop. That the fraternity will forget me. That the Cross will remain on Jehoshaphat’s grave without demanding anything more from my life. That the old Sammy will one day become so dead he will no longer rise whenever insult, fear, or money calls his name.
But as I sit in that cafeteria, with students fleeing quietly and Lizzy’s hand resting near mine, I know the truth. I am not free yet. I am only learning how to refuse my chains.

chapters.title: Chapter NINETEEN
content: I talk less now. The old Sammy would have entered Legion’s room with his shoulders squared, his voice raised, and threats already waiting on his tongue. He would have filled the room before anyone else had the courage to speak. But this new man is learning to listen.
Not because he is weak. Because he is beginning to understand that words can either pull a soul out of darkness or push it deeper in.

I still feel anger. I still feel the old fire moving beneath my skin whenever I remember the envelope Legion pushed at me in the cafeteria. But I no longer trust anger as a guide. Anger is a poor prophet. It sees fire and calls it light.

That is why I am visiting Legion. Not to fight. Not to prove anything. I come because I see what Legion does not see. Otunba’s money is not an opportunity. It is a rope. And if Legion keeps holding it, it will tighten around his neck.

His hostel corridor is dim when I arrive. The fluorescent bulb above the stairwell blinks with tired persistence, throwing brief shadows across the walls. Someone is playing music from a room at the far end. The smell of cigarette smoke hangs in the passage.

I stand before Legion’s door and knock. Not the fraternity knock. A simple knock. Three ordinary taps from a man trying desperately to live an ordinary life. There is movement inside. A pause. Then his voice.
“Who be that?”
“Sammy.”
Silence.

I hear something fall. A chair maybe. Or his courage. The door opens slowly. Legion stands in the doorway, bare-chested, wearing a pair of jeans and a startled expression he quickly tries to bury under swagger.
His eyes move past me into the corridor as though expecting others. He is afraid.
That surprises me. And saddens me.
“I come in peace, brother,” I say.
He recovers with a crooked smile. “Sure. Come in, Bro.”
I step inside.

His room is small, crowded, and restless like the mind of its owner. Clothes hang from nails on the wall. A black beret lies on a plastic chair. Posters of musicians and footballers share space with crude fraternity symbols sketched near the wardrobe. Empty bottles sit beneath the reading table. A pair of boots, still stained with mud, rests beside the bed.
This is not merely a room. It is a shrine to becoming someone feared.
Legion shuts the door.
For a few seconds we stand there awkwardly, as though the cafeteria incident is sitting between us on the floor.
“I’m sorry about what happened earlier,” I say.
He tilts his head.
“Sure… my guardian angel. I knew you’d come to your senses.” The smugness returns.

He stretches out his hand for the fraternity handshake. I look at it. Once, my body would have responded automatically. Fingers locking. Knuckles shifting. Silent codes passing between blood-bound men. But now the gesture feels like a gate I cannot step through.
I do not take his hand.
His smile thins. “What now? You no dey shake brothers again?”
“I am not here as Lethal Weapon.”
“Then who are you here as?”
I meet his eyes.
“Someone who does not want to watch you die.”
For the first time, his expression shifts. Only slightly.
Then he laughs. “You came to preach.”
“I came to warn you.”
“Same thing.”
I move towards the only free chair, pick up the beret, and place it on the bed before sitting. “Preaching is for your soul. Warning is for what is about to happen to your body.”
That gets his attention. He leans against the wardrobe and folds his arms. “So talk.”
I look around the room again. Young man. Small room. Big ambition. Dangerous friends. Dirty money. I see too much of myself in him. That is the problem.
“Return Otunba’s money,” I say.
His eyes narrow. So there it is. The real reason.
He looks away and chuckles. “I should have known.”
“I am serious.”
“So am I.”
“Legion.”
He pushes away from the wardrobe and walks to the table.
“Otunba has raised the offer.”
I say nothing.
“He is willing to pay two million.”

The number does not excite me. It frightens me. Because men like Otunba do not give two million naira to boys because they are generous. They invest in obedience. They purchase silence. They buy blood before it spills.

“If you don’t want to be involved,” Legion continues, “just give me the contacts. Tell me who to meet. Show me how to set the structure. I’ll handle the rest.” He smiles. “After all, you are my guardian angel.”
I look at him with pity.
“It is not about contacts or people.”
“Then what is it?”
His impatience sharpens. “Teach me how to do it if you won’t lead the campaign.”
I bow my head.
“You don’t know what he is asking for.”
“I know exactly what he is asking for.”

“No. You know the money. You know the respect. You know the boys will hail you if you pull it off. You know Steel will stop treating you like errand meat. But you do not know what Otunba is really buying.”
His face darkens.
“What is he buying?”
I remain silent. Because this is where the danger begins.

The GODs operate by strict protocols that must be followed to the letter. The election operation is not merely about ballot boxes, thugs, polling units, and violence. Those are the outer garments. The thing beneath my connection with Otunba is darker… they call it The Program, to keep everyone else from knowing about it.

The confidential project encoded in the irrevocable testament of the Inverted Cross by the former Hangman. It was not activated before Jehoshaphat recanted, and then died. Now Otunba wants it opened through his election.
That is the real reason they keep circling me without striking. That is the real reason they need me alive. And perhaps I am the only one conscripted into it. Unless Bitty is.
Legion mistakes my silence for contempt.
“You see?” he says. “This is the problem with you now. You talk in parables like those church people.”
I lift my eyes. “You think this is a joke?”
“I think you are pretending.”
“Pretending?”
“How long do you think you can continue this thing?” he asks. “You know membership in this fraternity is blood in, blood out. You can only leave by the shedding of blood.”
“I have a covenant backed by a superior blood.”
He laughs. A sharp, mocking laugh. “God. Jesus. Blood. Covenant. You, Lethal Weapon…?” He looks scornfully at me, “No.”
“I was in the Way before,” I say quietly. “Before I fell away.”
His laughter fades.
For a moment, he studies me differently.
“You are not meant for this life, Bro,” I continue. “Can’t you see what it is doing to you?”
He scoffs. “Look who is talking.”
“I know what I became.”
“Then don’t judge me for becoming the same thing.”
“That is why I am here.”
The room settles.
His eyes flicker.
I lean forward. “Do you know I was meant to go for the blind target with Hugo Boss?”
Legion’s face changes. “What?”
“I was meant to go with Albatross that night.”
He stares. “So what happened?”
“I went blind while driving.”
His expression hardens with disbelief. “Blind?”
“Temporarily.”
“You expect me to believe that?”
“God used it to stop me from shedding the blood of an innocent man.”
“God?” He says the word as if it offends him. “I can’t believe you are saying this. God? You, Lethal Weapon?”
He starts laughing again, but this time there is unease beneath the mockery.
“So you don join religious people?”

“I know how you feel about religious conversation,” I say. “People think moral truth is too subjective to discuss. So we avoid it. We prefer to talk about government, society, poverty, bad leadership, bad economy. We say the state will fix the problems of the masses.”
I stand and walk slowly towards the small window. Outside, students move along the hostel walkway, unaware that a soul is being weighed in this room.

“But you are working for the likes of Otunba,” I continue, “not because you believe he will serve your best interest or the good of society. You are working for him because of money. Because of status. Because you want to rise.”
Legion’s jaw tightens.
“This same clique inflicts pain on the masses and then funds religious organizations to keep the people compliant while the shepherds are fed fat from the state’s purse.”
“I hope you didn’t come all the way here just to preach at me.”
“You know why people resist moral truth?” I ask.
He says nothing.
“Because it exposes weakness. It challenges the idols we have made of ourselves. It takes away the excuse we use to gratify our desires.”
I turn to him. “Deep down, you know this is true.”
His face clouds. “I’m not listening to this rubbish.”
“You are listening.”
“No, I’m not.”
“You are angry because you are listening.”
He looks away. For a moment, I think I have reached him. Then pride comes back to his face like a curtain. He picks up a bottle from the table, realizes it is empty, and drops it again.
“You people always think you are better once you start carrying Bible.”
“I am not better than you.”
“Then stop talking like it.”
“I am trying to stop you from making the mistake that may kill you.”
“Everybody dies.”
“Not everybody sells himself first.”
The words strike him. His eyes flare.
But before anger fully forms, I change the direction. “Do you know what Hugo Boss was seeking for?”
Legion stills. He did not expect that. “What?”
“You heard me.”
He hesitates.
“But you were sent to eliminate him.”
His silence confirms more than his mouth would have.
“Why were you in the room that night?”
He looks at me sharply. “You’re no longer with us. Why do you want to know?”
“I want you to understand what you are stepping into.”
“Or are you longing to come back?”
I look at him with pity. That question no longer has power over me.
“People who seek the knowledge of mysteries by all means end up deluded,” I say. “Or they sacrifice their lives for things they were not given to carry.”
He grimaces.

“You see, the revelation of mysteries is a call. It is not received by ambition. It is not bought by money. It is not forced open by sacrifice. Many are destroyed because they seek what they are not given to understand.
“I learnt this from Scripture,” I continue. “The Parable of the Sower. Jesus said there are those to whom it is given to understand the mysteries of the Kingdom.”
Legion laughs under his breath. “That may be for people who believe the Bible. In our kingdom, things work differently. Once you pay the price, the books are opened for you.”
“The books.” I chuckle sadly.
He does not know what he is saying.
Or perhaps he does. That is what frightens me. I consider telling him more about the Program. Just enough to scare him. Just enough to convince him to return the money.
But what use will such knowledge be to a bloodthirsty and deluded mind? Give a man obsessed with darkness one more secret, and he will call it illumination.
“I know you are up to something,” Legion says suddenly. His voice lowers. “And Steel knows it too.”
I say nothing.
“But your days are numbered.”
The room becomes very quiet.
I turn to him. “Have they instructed you to kill me?”
The question shocks him.
He turns sharply.
“What kind of question is that?”
“A simple one.”
“You think I would tell you?”
“I think you just did.”
His eyes harden.
For a few seconds we stare at each other.
The boy I came to save is still there somewhere.
But he is buried beneath hunger, pride, fear, and the intoxicating promise that Otunba’s money will make him more than an errand boy.
I stand.
“Be careful, Legion.”
He scoffs.
“The fraternity is not what they make you believe it is.”
“Please take your religion away with you.”
I walk to the door. My hand rests on the handle. Then I turn back. “If you have Otunba’s money, go and give it back to him.”
His face twitches. There. A flicker. Not guilt. Fear. So he has it. Or part of it.
“Return it before it becomes blood money,” I say.
He does not answer.
I open the door.
Behind me, Legion mutters, “You are really gone.”
I pause.
“No,” I say quietly. “For the first time, I think I am being found.” Then I step into the corridor and close the door behind me.
The music from one of the rooms is still playing. Students are still moving. The world has not changed. But as I walk away from Legion’s room, I know something with a heaviness I cannot shake.
