I came to pull him back. I came to warn him. I came to stop Otunba’s money from becoming a curse in his hands. But some men do not hear warning until it becomes prophecy. And I fear Legion is already listening to death in a language he mistakes for opportunity.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY
content: Night settles over the campus with an unusual weight. From my room, I hear motorcycles tearing through the street, their engines rising and falling like angry insects in the dark.

Election day comes like a storm everybody saw gathering and still pretended would pass over. Somewhere beyond the hostel blocks, voices break into slogans, then dissolve into shouting. A bottle shatters. A group of boys laugh too loudly. Then another silence follows, the kind of silence that does not mean peace, but waiting.
I know the sound. I have heard it before. I have created it before.

There is a particular noise that comes before election violence. It is not one sound, but many sounds working together: motorcycles without number plates, boys gathering at odd corners, beer bottles clinking in nylon bags, sudden arguments at newspaper stands, party songs playing from moving vehicles, whispers becoming instructions, instructions becoming threats.

To the ordinary citizen, election day begins in the morning when polling units open. To men like Otunba, it begins long before that. It begins when the first youth leader is given transport money. When the first student union officer is invited for a private meeting. When the first local strongman is promised a contract. When the first hungry boy is told that loyalty today will become opportunity tomorrow.

By the time ballot papers arrive at polling units, the invisible election has already been running for months. What the public sees is voting. What operators see is territory. Every ward has a temperament. Every polling unit has a history. Every neighbourhood has people who can influence it, frighten it, calm it, or turn it into chaos.

That is the part most citizens do not understand. Violence in elections is rarely random. It is often organized, funded, encouraged, excused, or ignored by people who will never appear where stones are thrown and blood is spilled. The boys on the streets become the faces of the violence, but they are seldom the architects of it.
They are instruments. I was once one of those instruments. Worse. I was trusted to coordinate others.

The GODs had a political machine, though we never called it that openly. We had softer words for everything. Mobilization. Protection. Logistics. Field coordination. Crowd control. Monitoring. Stakeholder engagement. Every dark act sounds cleaner when language is washed properly.

I learned early that politics often borrows the energy of the young and spends it without remorse. Young men have strength. They have anger. They have hunger. They have pride. They have the dangerous desire to be recognized.
A politician does not always need to convince such boys with ideology. Sometimes all he needs is to give them a role, a T-shirt, a bottle, a bundle of cash, and the feeling that they matter.
That feeling is more intoxicating than alcohol.

Many students who become foot soldiers in election violence do not begin as criminals. Some begin as boys looking for school fees. Some are seeking protection. Some want access to powerful men. Some are angry at society and glad to find someone who will give their anger a uniform. Some simply follow friends and discover too late that politics has no refund policy for damaged lives.
I know this because I have seen it. I have seen boys who could barely defend their own academic results suddenly speak of defending democracy with machetes hidden beneath their shirts.
I have seen those who never attended one serious political debate become willing to die for candidates whose manifestos they had never read.
I have seen brilliant students become thugs for leaders who would never allow their own children near such danger.
That is the tragedy.
The children of the powerful are often abroad or safely indoors while the children of the poor are mobilized to defend political interests with their bodies.

The language changes depending on the audience. Before the public, politicians speak of service, unity, development, and the future. Before sponsors, they speak of strategy, numbers, alliances, and returns. Before thugs, they speak of loyalty, courage, betrayal, and reward. Every group hears the version of politics designed to move it.

Religion is useful in that game. Tribe is useful too. Not because faith and ethnicity are evil in themselves. They are not. A people should be free to love their faith, honour their ancestry, speak their language, and preserve their culture. But when desperate politicians discover that citizens can be moved more easily by fear than by reason, they turn sacred and cultural identities into weapons.

They ask people to vote not because a candidate is competent, but because he is “one of us.” They warn people not to trust the other side, not because policies are bad, but because the other side worships differently, speaks differently, comes from another region, or bears another name.

Slowly, the election stops being a civic decision. It becomes a tribal contest. A religious suspicion. A survival panic. And once citizens begin to vote from fear, they stop asking the questions that matter.

Who is competent? Who is accountable? Who understands education? Who can strengthen institutions? Who can manage public funds? Who respects the rule of law? Who has character? Who has a record that can be examined beyond noise and posters?

These questions are dangerous to bad politicians because they cannot be answered with slogans alone. So the people are distracted. They are divided into camps. They are made to suspect one another. They are taught to defend identities while those who manipulate those identities negotiate power quietly among themselves.

The same men who provoke religious division in public attend private dinners together. The same men who inflame ethnic suspicion share contracts across tribal lines. The same men who tell poor people to hate one another often marry across the same boundaries they condemn when votes are needed.
Power is rarely as divided as the people are made to believe. The masses quarrel at polling units. The elites reconcile in boardrooms.

I did not understand this when I was serving the machine. Or perhaps I understood enough and chose not to care. Back then, I thought politics was simply another theatre of power. The strong organized. The weak complained. The bold took what they wanted. The fearful stood aside.
That was how I justified it. But now, as I sit alone in my room listening to the campus tremble under the coming election, I hear the sounds differently.

Every motorcycle sounds like a warning. Every slogan sounds like a boy being led somewhere he may not return from. Every shout reminds me that democracy becomes fragile when citizens are too hungry to think freely, too divided to unite wisely, and too afraid to demand better.

Yet I do not blame the citizens alone. A man who has not eaten can be tempted by a small note. A graduate without work can be bought with a promise. A community neglected for years can be deceived by a borehole commissioned three days before an election. Poverty does not excuse everything, but it explains why manipulation succeeds.

Bad politics feeds on desperation. And desperation is abundant. That is why elections in a wounded society are never only about ballots. They are about hunger, fear, memory, identity, resentment, ambition, and survival. The ballot box becomes the visible object around which invisible wounds gather.
From outside my room, the shouting grows louder. Then suddenly fades.
I sit still.

My Bible lies open on the table, but my eyes are not reading. My mind is moving from one polling unit to another, remembering how operations are arranged. Who mans the entrance. Who watches the presiding officer. Who intimidates voters. Who creates confusion if results go the wrong way. Who snatches materials when all else fails. Who escorts the money. Who disappears when police arrive.
I know too much.
That is part of my burden. The fraternity did not merely teach me violence. It taught me systems. And now I see systems everywhere.

That is why Otunba wants me back. Not because he loves me. Not because I am irreplaceable. But because I understand the invisible wiring of the machine. I know how fear travels. I know how boys are grouped. I know how rumours are planted. I know how violence is timed. I know how chaos can be manufactured and blamed on passion.
But I also know something Otunba may not know. Every machine eventually demands payment from the hands that operate it.
The knock comes suddenly. Three firm taps. A pause. Then two. The code of the fraternity.
My body stiffens. I do not answer.
The knock comes again.
“Lethal Weapon,” a voice calls from outside. “Please answer. It is an emergency.”
I remain seated.
Emergency is one of those words the fraternity uses when it wants obedience before explanation.
“Lethal Weapon!” The voice trembles now.
I rise slowly and walk to the door.
When I open it, Hillary is standing there. His face is pale. His breathing is broken. Something terrible has already happened.
“What is it?”
He swallows. “Legion asked for you.”
My chest tightens before he says the rest.
“He is in the hospital.”
A coldness moves through me.
“He was shot.”
For a moment, the room disappears.
The slogans. The motorcycles. The warnings. The money. Otunba! The conversation in Legion’s room. My plea that he returns the dough before it became blood money.
Everything gathers into one terrible understanding. Election day has collected its first debt. And Legion is the receipt.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-ONE
content: The hospital corridor is crowded with wounded bodies and frightened relatives. Election day has followed us here.

It is no longer a political event. It has become blood on shirts, swollen eyes, bandaged heads, trembling mothers, angry fathers, anxious friends, nurses running from bed to bed, and doctors trying to decide which emergency deserves the next available hand.

The corridor smells of antiseptic, sweat, blood, stale air, and fear. People sit on benches, on windowsills, on the bare floor. Some clutch hospital cards. Some hold laboratory forms they do not understand. Some argue with nurses. Some pray aloud. Some stare silently ahead, as if silence is the only dignity they have left.
This is where the speeches end. This is where slogans become groans. This is where democracy, when corrupted by violence, removes its fine clothes and reveals the body underneath.

A boy lies on a trolley near the entrance with a torn campaign shirt pressed against his side. Another sits against the wall, his face bruised, his eyes fixed on nothing. Two men argue over who will pay for drugs that should have been available in the hospital pharmacy. A woman pleads with a nurse to attend to her son. The nurse, already exhausted, tells her to wait.
Wait!

That is the unofficial anthem of suffering in this country. Wait for the doctor. Wait for test results. Wait for blood. Wait for oxygen. Wait for money. Wait for a bed. Wait for government. Wait for a miracle. And sometimes, while people wait, death arrives first.

I follow Hillary through the corridor, and with every step I feel the consequence of the night before gathering around me. The motorcycles. The shouting. Otunba’s money. Legion’s ambition. My warning that he should return the dough before it became blood money.
Now the debt has matured. We reach the ward.
Legion lies on a narrow bed near the wall. For a moment I do not recognize him. Not because his face has changed, but because his pride is gone.
The boy who once called himself indomitable now struggles beneath the weight of his own breath. His beret is missing. His shirt has been cut open. His eyes move restlessly until they find me.
“Lethal Weapon,” he whispers.
The name breaks something inside me.
I move closer. “Don’t talk.”
He tries to smile. It fails.

Around us, medical personnel move with weary urgency. A doctor in a rumpled coat speaks quickly to a nurse. Another nurse checks a chart and hurries away. Someone calls for gauze. Someone else asks whether the patient’s relatives have bought prescribed drugs. A ward attendant wheels an empty oxygen cylinder past us as if emptiness itself has become routine.
I look around and feel a familiar anger rise in me.
Not the old violent anger. A different one. The anger of realizing how cheaply human life is treated when institutions decay.

Election violence may have brought Legion here, but the hospital is fighting him with broken hands. Poor equipment. Overworked staff. Expensive drugs. Delayed attention. Fake medicines in the open market. Wrong diagnoses made in poorly equipped clinics. Quacks operating where trained professionals should be.

Patients moving from prayer house to chemist, from chemist to private clinic, from private clinic to government hospital, losing time at every stop. And at the centre of it all, ordinary people – poor, frightened, desperate – trying to survive a system that often asks them to pay before it tries to save them.

This is the collateral damage of bad politics. It does not end at the polling unit. It enters hospitals. It enters classrooms. It enters police stations. It enters courts. It enters homes. It enters graves.
A nation does not collapse only when buildings fall. Sometimes it collapses quietly, when citizens begin to expect failure from the very institutions built to protect them.
Legion coughs lightly and reaches for my hand.
I take it.
His fingers are cold.
“Please,” he mutters.
I bend closer.
“Please pray for me.”
The words enter me with unbearable force.
Legion. The same boy who mocked faith. The same boy who boasted of his name. The same boy who said books could be opened once a man paid the price.
Now, at the edge of his own life, he is no longer asking for power. He is asking for prayer.

Religion has become the last refuge for many in this country. Not always because people understand God, but because every other door has failed them. When the hospital has no equipment, when the doctor has no answer, when the money is not enough, when science is delayed by poverty and governance, people turn upward.
Sometimes in faith. Sometimes in fear. Sometimes because they have nowhere else to turn. What an irony. A country can neglect its hospitals and still expect heaven to run emergency services.
My vision blurs.
I hold Legion’s hand with both of mine.
“Will you receive Jesus as your Lord and Saviour?” I ask. “He can forgive you. He can keep you in His mercy.”
His eyes tremble.
“Yes,” he whispers. “Please pray for me.”
I begin to pray. The words shake inside me. Not because I do not believe them. Because I do. And because I know I am praying for someone I failed to save from the road that brought him here.
I pray for mercy. For forgiveness. For peace. For God to receive a boy who wanted to be feared but is now asking to be forgiven.
Legion’s fingers tighten around mine. For one brief moment, strength returns to his grip. Then it leaves. His hand slackens. I stop praying.
“Legion?”
No answer.

A nurse rushes in. She checks him quickly. Her face tells me before her mouth does. It is not shock I see in her eyes. It is exhaustion. The tired grief of someone who has seen too much dying in one day and still has other patients waiting.
I do not know when I begin to weep. Not quietly. Not with dignity. Something breaks loose from a place I did not know grief had reached.
Disciples of the Inverted Cross present in the room gather around me. Someone holds my shoulders. Someone says my name. Someone tries to pull me away from the bed. But I do not feel their hands.
All I feel is the silence of a boy who thought violence would make him powerful and died holding my hand like a frightened child.

In my few days on earth, I have seen more death than a young man should see. I have seen boys fall in the name of brotherhood. I have seen enemies hunted. I have seen fear manufactured and sold as courage.

But Legion’s death does something different to me. It does not make me angry first. It makes me afraid. Because for the first time, I understand that the system does not only destroy those who oppose it. It also consumes those who serve it.

And if Legion could die this way, with ambition still warm in his pocket and Otunba’s shadow still hanging over him, then none of us were ever soldiers. We were offerings. Young bodies laid on the altar of older men’s ambitions.
The ward continues moving around me. Patients groan. Nurses call for supplies. Relatives whisper prayers.
A doctor walks away to attend to another emergency. Life continues because suffering has become too common to stop everything.

I look at Legion’s still face and remember the way he laughed when I warned him. Return the money. Leave the fraternity. Do not mistake opportunity for destiny. He did not hear me. Or perhaps he heard me too late.

Outside the hospital, the election will continue. Votes will be counted. Results will be announced. Winners will celebrate. Losers will contest. Commentators will analyze. Politicians will speak of democracy. But here, in this ward, a boy lies dead.
And nobody outside this room will remember that his blood also formed part of the result.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-TWO
content: The day dawns over my crib with the uneasy patience of a place that has seen too many secrets and learned to keep quiet.

I wake before the first noise of the compound. For several minutes I remain still on the bed, staring at the ceiling, listening to the quiet. It is no longer the peaceful quiet of morning. It is the tense quiet of a room waiting to be searched. Every wall seems to have ears. Every familiar face feels newly suspicious. Every footstep outside my door makes my chest tighten.

Since I laid the cross upright on the Hangman’s grave, nothing in my life stands where I left it. The old world has not merely turned its back on me. It is bearing down on me, demanding that I account for my defection.

To the Order and the fraternity I once called family, I have become expendable. This does not arrive as an announcement. It arrives as pressure. A tightening of the road ahead. A question sitting in the chest.
How long can a man survive after the family that gave him a name decides it wants his soul back?
The knock at the door breaks my thought. Not gently. Not politely. A command disguised as a knock.
“Lethal Weapon!” Steel’s voice.

I had asked him to stop calling me by that name. But my conversion means nothing to him, or to any other disciple. As far as they are concerned, I am under Lizzy’s spell, trapped in what they call hypocritical religious madness, waiting only for hunger, fear, or loneliness to restore me to reason.
They believe I will come back. They believe every prodigal eventually misses the smell of the pigsty if he has nowhere else to eat.

But the one thing that keeps my faith alive is the miracle at the cemetery. Steel dismisses my blindness as pretence, an excuse I invented to avoid following Ugo on the night he went after the former Hangman. But I know what happened to me. I know that darkness took my sight. I know that mercy restored it.

And I know what I meant when I placed that cross upright. It was not an accident. It was not confusion. It was not Lizzy’s influence. It was my confession. An agreement with the Man who was crucified on the cross as the redemption offering for my sins.
Nothing will make me go back to turn that cross upside down.
When I open the door, Steel eases into my room with three other disciples behind him.
They do not greet. They do not sit.

They enter like men who have come to collect something already judged to belong to them. Steel’s face is stern. But his sternness is too arranged, too theatrical. He is not here merely as my brother in the Order. He is here as a messenger, and messengers must perform the authority of the man who sent them.
“Otunba has sent us,” he says, “to ask for the refund of his two million naira.”
The name enters the room before the man himself.
Otunba. Even absent, he has weight.

That is the first lesson about certain politicians: they do not need to be present to occupy a space. Their influence arrives ahead of them, sits down before them, and waits for others to adjust their voices.

I have seen Otunba walk into rooms filled with men who claim to fear nobody, and watched their shoulders soften as if an invisible hand pressed them down. He is not physically intimidating in the crude way cultists admire. His power is not in muscle. It is in composure.

He is tall, broad in the shoulders, smooth in movement, always dressed as though every gathering might become a campaign photograph. His agbadas are never merely clothes. They are statements. Cream today to suggest peace. Deep blue tomorrow to imply stability. White when he wants to appear pure. Wine or royal purple when the room requires authority.
He wears tradition like strategy. His smile is one of his most dangerous possessions. Warm enough to comfort a widow. Bright enough to flatter a crowd. Measured enough to conceal calculation.

Otunba can greet a market woman with both hands clasped around hers and make her believe he remembers her late husband. He can call a student leader “my son” with such tenderness that the boy forgets he is being recruited. He can sit with pastors, imams, chiefs, union leaders, lecturers, transport chairmen, and fraternity boys, and each group will leave believing he understands them best.
That is charisma. Not noise. Not beauty. Not eloquence alone. Charisma is the ability to make people feel seen while carefully deciding how they will be used.
Otunba has that gift.

He listens with his eyes. He laughs at the right place. He lowers his voice when speaking of sacrifice. He raises it when speaking of destiny. He knows when to quote Scripture, when to invoke ancestors, when to speak of youth empowerment, when to lament unemployment, when to blame the past, and when to promise a future nobody has budgeted for.
Men like him do not simply campaign. They interpret hunger. They study grievance.

They know that a tired people do not always ask for justice first. Sometimes they ask for relief. Sometimes rice. Sometimes transport fare. Sometimes school fees. Sometimes a connection. Sometimes protection. And where institutions fail, politicians become personal institutions. That is how dependence is created.
A functioning society asks citizens to trust systems. A broken society teaches them to beg powerful men. Otunba understands this better than most.

To the public, he is a philanthropist. A bridge-builder. A man of the people. To the clergy, he is a patron of God’s work. To traditional rulers, a respectful son of the soil. To students, a believer in youth participation. To the poor, a generous benefactor. To the Order, he is an investor. And investors expect returns.
That is why the two million naira now matters. Not because Otunba cannot afford to lose it.
Men like him lose more than that on hotel bills, campaign convoys, late-night consultations, and envelopes that never appear in audited accounts. No.
The money matters because it represents control. A politician may forgive failure if it still leaves him in command. But he rarely forgives embarrassment.
And Otunba lost the gubernatorial election. That loss has changed everything.

A failed candidate is often more dangerous than a powerful one. Power makes men arrogant, but defeat makes them paranoid. After defeat, every ally becomes a suspect. Every unpaid debt becomes betrayal. Every missing naira becomes evidence of conspiracy.
Steel steps farther into the room.
“You and your witch girlfriend have brought a curse to the fraternity and the Order,” he says. “I’m sure she deceived you into placing the wreath upside down.”
“The cross is upright,” I reply calmly. “And it stands in agreement with what Jehoshaphat believed.”
The three disciples behind him shift angrily. One steps forward.
Steel raises his hand. “Stand back.”
The command is sharp. The boy stops.

That confirms what I already suspect. They are not here to kill me. Not today. Perhaps not yet. Steel has been instructed to apply pressure, not to let the visit go awry. They still need me alive. They still need me to reverse the cross. They still need me for something they do not fully understand.
Steel steps closer until only a narrow space separates us.
“You know,” he says quietly, “I don’t know why the GODs haven’t cut off your head by now. But I think you have stepped on the wrong toe.”
“I do not have Otunba’s money.”
“You will explain that to him.”
“I didn’t accept the offer Legion brought.”
Steel smiles. Not happily. Knowingly. “Well, guys,” he says, turning to the others, “I think our assignment is done.”
The boys straighten up with the exaggerated dignity of small men carrying borrowed power.
But I see what they want hidden. Relief. Too quick. Too convenient.

Legion did not return Otunba’s dough, because he thought he could run the election process himself. He thought the money would become his ladder. But I am also certain he did not hold the money alone. These greedy fellows must have taken their share, directly or indirectly, and now that Legion is dead, the dead boy cannot testify.
So, they have brought the debt to my door. I have become the scapegoat.
Steel rests one foot on my chair and leans forward casually, as if this room still belongs to the hierarchy he represents.
“You will be hearing from Otunba himself,” he says. “When he requests the meeting, I will bring him here.”
The statement is not information. It is a sentence awaiting a date. He lowers his foot and turns towards the door.
The others follow him out.

Before leaving, Steel gives me a smile that betrays him. There is satisfaction in it, but also fear. He too knows Otunba’s generosity has teeth. He too knows campaign money does not disappear without consequence.
When the door closes, the room feels smaller.
I sit on the edge of the bed and stare at the place where they stood.
The love of money is the root of all evil.

I have heard the Scripture many times, but it begins to feel newly alive. Not as a church quotation. As a diagnosis. Money has entered the room though no currency is visible. Mammon has taken a seat beside me. It has turned dead Legion into a missing account. It has turned Steel into a collector. It has turned me into a debtor for money I never touched.
I sense it clearly now. Things are about to become ugly. Legion is not here to speak. The boys who shared his ambition will not confess.
Otunba will not accept ignorance.
And I am no longer protected by brotherhood. A politician’s money is never just money. It is a hook. A leash. A record. A future accusation. A rope waiting patiently for a neck.
By the time I rise from the bed, I know what I must do. I cannot remain here and wait for Otunba’s shadow to fall fully across my door.
I must leave this environment. Not forever. But for now.
Sometimes wisdom is not standing firm. Sometimes wisdom is knowing when to flee before men who trade in power make merchandise of your life.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-THREE
content: In the estate of the GODs, fear rules. Not faith. Not loyalty. Not brotherhood. Fear.
We never called it fear, of course. We gave it finer names. Discipline. Respect. Order. Code. Covenant. But beneath those words, fear sat like a king in darkness, receiving tribute from all of us.
Fear was the tool of manipulation. Fear was the instrument of suppression. Fear was the veil that made deception look like mystery.

Every disciple of the Inverted Cross was made to believe that the fraternity was so powerful it made him invincible. The first lesson was never spoken plainly, but it entered us all the same: ordinary men fear death; sons of darkness do not.
That was the purpose of the inversion. The initiation. The breaking. The remaking.

A tadpole was exposed to pain, humiliation, secrecy, violence, and darkness until something tender inside him retreated. We called that courage. We called it transformation. We called it becoming. But now I know better.
It was not courage. It was injury wearing a mask.

The inversion did not remove fear. It buried it deep and taught us to call the burial strength. Then, as Guardian Angels to younger disciples, we were assigned to continue the lie. We drilled them. We intimidated them. We trained their faces not to tremble. We taught their hands not to shake when given an order.
We told them fearlessness was the mark of a son of darkness. But the violence we glorified was not born from courage. It was fear acting out before anyone could expose it.

A truly courageous man does not need to oppress the weak to prove his strength. He does not need blood to confirm his relevance. He does not need a nickname, a beret, a secret knock, or a hidden knife to persuade himself that he exists.
Courage defends the oppressed. Violence silences them. I did not know this before. Or perhaps I knew it and buried it beneath the convenience of belonging.

Now, stepping away from the grip of the Order, I see things more clearly. The same virtues we claimed to defend — justice, brotherhood, loyalty, protection of the vulnerable — were only slogans hanging over a marketplace of fear. We recited noble words while selling ourselves to darker appetites.
It is strange how light can become painful when a man has lived too long in darkness. Everything it reveals looks like accusation.
Legion’s face returns to me again.

Not the face he wore when he boasted. Not the smug smile he carried when he pushed Otunba’s envelope toward me. Not the restless eyes of the boy intoxicated by the promise of power. I remember his face on the hospital bed. Breathless. Afraid. Young. Too young.

The nickname had fallen off him by then. Legion was no longer indomitable. He was a boy with cold fingers clinging to my hand, asking for prayer because the gods he boasted in could not follow him to the edge of death.
That memory unsettles me more than the threats. Because I warned him.
I told him to return Otunba’s money. I told him the dough would become blood money. He laughed. Now he is dead. And the money is alive.

That is the cruel thing about certain debts. The man who collected them may die, but the debt continues breathing. It moves from hand to hand, from mouth to mouth, from accusation to accusation, until it finds a neck.
Now it has found mine. Two million naira. The amount sits in my mind like a verdict. It is not merely money. Not anymore. It is motive. It is accusation. It is evidence without proof.
It is a rope woven from greed, suspicion, and political embarrassment.
Otunba lost the election.
That makes everything worse.

If he had won, perhaps the money would have disappeared into celebration, swallowed by victory, forgotten among contracts, appointments, and new schemes. Winners have the luxury of ignoring some losses. But losers count everything.
Losers remember every promise. Every contribution. Every envelope. Every boy who failed to deliver. Every naira that did not return with power attached to it.
Otunba will count.
And when he counts, he will find that two million naira left his hand and did not return as votes, victory, violence, or refund.
Legion is dead. He cannot explain. Steel will not confess. The boys around him will protect themselves.

And I am convenient. Too convenient. I was the original choice. The point man. The one conscripted into the Program. The one who refused the assignment. The one who placed the Cross upright on the Hangman’s grave.
The traitor. The apostate.
The orphan without a family to ask questions if he disappears. A man can survive many accusations when he has people around him. I have none.
The thought makes my chest tighten.
I sit on the edge of my bed and look around my room. Everything suddenly appears temporary.
The books stacked on the table. The shirts hanging from nails on the wall. The plastic bucket near the bathroom door. The worn sandals beside the bed. The Bible Lizzy gave me.

The room has never been beautiful, but until tonight it was shelter. Now it feels like a place already entered by enemies. Every object looks as though it has been waiting for me to understand that I must leave it behind.
Outside, the compound is quiet. Too quiet.
A generator hums in the distance. Somewhere, a dog barks once and stops. Footsteps pass along the corridor, then fade. Each sound makes me pause.
Are they coming? Is that Steel? Is that a disciple sent to watch me? Is Otunba already in town?
Fear begins to move through me, but this time I do not mistake it for weakness. Fear is information. It tells me that the danger has changed shape.
Before now, the Order needed me alive. Now money has entered the matter. And money makes men impatient.

A spiritual threat may wait for a ritual day. A political debt does not wait. Money turns brothers into witnesses. Money turns allies into accusers. Money makes cowards courageous and violent men practical.

Otunba is not someone any mortal should play with. He is not one of those politicians who shouts before he strikes. Men like him smile first. They invite you. They call you son. They ask questions gently. They give you a chance to explain. Then, if your explanation does not satisfy the arithmetic of their loss, they allow other men to translate their disappointment into pain.
I have seen it happen. I have helped make it happen. That is why I know I cannot stay.
The decision does not arrive dramatically. No thunder. No voice from heaven. No vision. Just a quiet certainty. Leave.
I rise.
My legs feel strangely weak, but my hands move quickly.

I pull my old travel bag from beneath the bed and place it on the mattress. The zipper coughs open. I begin packing without order at first – shirts, trousers, underwear, a towel, notebooks. Then I stop and force myself to slow down.
Panic makes noise. Noise invites attention. I fold the clothes properly. Not because neatness matters, but because routine steadies my hands. The Bible goes in last.
No. I take it out again. I hold it for a moment.
Lizzy’s handwriting is on the inside cover. A short note. A prayer. A reminder that I am not what I used to be.
My throat tightens.
I place the Bible in the side pocket where I can reach it easily. Then I sit again and listen.
Nothing. The corridor remains quiet.
I move to the small table and gather what little money I have. It is not much. Not enough to begin life anywhere, but enough to move. Sometimes survival begins with transport fare.
I look at the room one more time. A strange grief rises in me.
This school was supposed to be many things. A place of learning. A place of becoming. A place where young men dream beyond the boundaries of their fathers’ failures.
For me, it became initiation ground, battlefield, court, prison, altar, and now exile. I think of Legion again. His laugh. His arrogance. His hand going slack inside mine.
A pain moves through my chest. “I tried,” I whisper.
But the room gives no answer.
I switch off the light. Darkness covers everything immediately.
For several seconds I remain still, allowing my eyes to adjust. Then I lift the bag and move towards the door.
I do not use the main path. I know too many eyes may be there.
I slip through the back of the compound, past a broken fence near the abandoned generator house, and enter the narrow path that runs behind the old lecture buildings.
The night air is cool against my face.
Somewhere in the distance, students are laughing. Music drifts faintly from a hostel. Life continues, careless and loud, unaware that I am walking away from it.
Every few steps I look back. Not because I expect to see someone. Because fear keeps turning my neck.
The campus at night is a different country. Buildings become shadows. Trees become watchers. Empty classrooms look like open mouths. The road ahead appears longer than it should.
I walk fast, but not fast enough to attract attention. The bag pulls against my shoulder. My ribs still ache from the beating. My body complains. I keep moving.

At the edge of campus, I stop. For a moment, I look back at the university town. The place that made me. The place that broke me. The place that called me Lethal Weapon and then watched me become prey.
I feel no victory. Only sorrow. And fear. And a strange fragile hope that perhaps exile is not always punishment. Sometimes it is mercy dragging a man away before the knife arrives.
I walk nearly two kilometres before I find a taxi waiting near a closed provision shop. The driver is half-asleep behind the wheel.
“Where you dey go?” he asks, rubbing his face.
I hesitate.
The question is simple. The answer is not. Away.
That is the first destination.
“Motor park,” I say.
He names his price. I do not bargain.
I climb into the back seat and place my bag beside me. As the taxi pulls away, I sink into the seat and close my eyes.
The university disappears behind us, street by street, shadow by shadow.
I do not know where I am going. I only know what I am leaving behind. The Order. Otunba. The debt. The grave. The nickname. The boy I used to be.
But fear follows quietly.
So does guilt. So does the cross.
And somewhere in the darkness ahead, I sense that my flight is not the end of my story. It is the beginning of the wilderness.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-FOUR
content: By the time the bus enters Ogbomosho, evening has began to loosen its gold across the sky.

I had not slept properly throughout the journey. Each time my eyes closed, I saw Steel standing in my room with Otunba’s name in his mouth. I saw Legion gasping on the hospital bed. I saw the brown envelope on the cafeteria table. I saw the cross standing upright on Ikenna’s grave like an accusation against everything I once belonged to. So I kept my eyes open. Watching roads. Watching passengers. Watching every vehicle that seemed to follow us for too long.
Fear had made me suspicious of distance. I had left in the night without telling Lizzy. That was the hardest part.

Not because I wanted to disappear from her, but because I feared the Order would expect me to run to the only person who still cared whether I lived or died. If they were watching me, they would watch her. If they could not find me, they would ask after her. If they suspected I had fled, they would listen for my name around everyone who loved me.
Love had become dangerous. So I left without goodbye. Without explanation. Without blessing.
I tell myself it was wisdom. But all through the journey, it felt like betrayal.
Ogbomosho did not welcome me loudly. It simply receives me. That is the first thing I notice.

Unlike Lagos, it doesn’t rush at me with noise from every direction. Unlike the university town I fled, it doesn’t look at me with the memory of what I had done. It moves at a calmer pace, as though the town has learned long ago that life would not become easier merely because men hurried through it.
Ogbomosho has a rustic dignity. It is old without being dead. Quiet without being empty. Simple without being poor in spirit.

Beyond the bus windows, I see the town’s agricultural soul. The outskirts open into stretches of wooded savanna, farmlands, and dusty paths leading toward places where men still measured prosperity by harvest, not invoices. There is a groundedness to the environment, a connection to soil and season, to yam, cashew, mango, and sweat. Even the air seemed to carry the memory of farms beyond the visible roads.

It is my first time there. I had heard many things about Ogbomosho. Some dismissive. Some exaggerated. Some spoken by people who considered every town outside Lagos a smaller version of inconvenience. But as the bus moves deeper into the town, I discover a place far more layered than hearsay.

The city balances its past and present with an honesty I had not expected. In some areas, modern buildings rose beside older compounds as though time itself had refused to choose sides. Painted bungalows stood near weathered mud-walled houses with corrugated iron roofs browned by years of rain and harmattan dust. Some compounds carried the architecture of families that had lived together for generations: wide courtyards, low walls, carved doors, shaded verandas.

The streets held traces of commerce and farmland at once. Yam sellers lean against wooden stalls. Cashew nuts filled basins in front of women who call out prices in Yoruba. Mangoes set in yellow-green pyramids beside baskets of pepper. Young men push wheelbarrows loaded with produce. Traders haggle with the patience of people who understood that bargaining itself was part of relationship, not merely transaction.

The bus groans past low buildings, market stalls, repair shops, churches, mosques, open-fronted stores, and narrow streets where motorcycles move with the confidence of people who knew every bend of the town. Roadside traders are sitting beneath umbrellas faded by sun and dust. Women arrange tomatoes, pepper, onions, yam tubers, and smoked fish in careful heaps. Men in loose shirts gather outside provision shops, discussing politics with the familiar seriousness of people who had long stopped expecting politicians to surprise them.
The driver who brought us from Lagos help me find a cheap guest house near a busy junction. I told him I had come to visit a relative, but the relative was out of town.
The lie came easily. That frightened me.
A man who has lived too long among violent people learns to lie even when the truth would have cost him nothing.

The guest house is a tired one-storey building painted a fading shade of blue. The reception smells of insecticide, dust, and old furniture. A small television mounted in the corner shows a Yoruba movie with the volume turned too high. The receptionist barely looks at me after I paid. That comforts me. I do not want curiosity. I want a bed, a door, and a night in which nobody would knock with the code of the fraternity.

My room has a narrow bed, a plastic chair, a small table, and curtains that had surrendered long ago to age. The fan turns with a tired clicking sound. The bathroom tap coughed before releasing water the colour of weak tea. A gecko watches me from the wall as if it had seen many fugitives before me and was unimpressed by my arrival.
I drop my bag on the bed and sit beside it.

For the first time since leaving school, the full meaning of my flight enters me. I am alone. Not temporarily. Not dramatically. Truly alone. No father. No mother. No siblings. No fraternity. No campus status. No money worth counting. No plan strong enough to call a future. Only a Bible in the side pocket of my bag and a name I was trying to stop answering to. Lethal Weapon.
The room seems to shrink around me.

Cult life had toughened me. Or so I tell myself. I had survived initiation, rivalry, rituals, violence, hunger, betrayal, and fear. I had been trained to adapt, to watch exits, to read faces, to make danger hesitate. I believed I was built for survival.
But survival is different when there is no enemy in front of you. It is easier to face a man with a weapon than to sit alone with your own life and admit you do not know what to do with it.
It’s my first morning in Ogbomosho. I step into the town with little money in my pocket and the intention to become invisible. Ogbomosho has awakened before I understood its rhythm.

Motorcycles buzz through the roads. Traders raise shutters. Women sweep the fronts of shops. Schoolchildren in uniforms move in clusters, their bags bouncing against their backs. Elderly men sitting outside compounds converse in Yoruba, in slow, weighty tones. Somewhere nearby, a mosque releases the last echo of morning prayer. Not far away, a church loudspeaker answers with worship music.
Faith lived openly here. So did tradition. So did trade.

The town carries its Yoruba heritage without apology. Language flowed around me in greetings, negotiations, jokes, warnings, and blessings. I understand enough Yoruba to survive simple exchanges, but not enough to belong. That suits me. Belonging had nearly destroyed me.

As I walk around, I notice the relaxed pace of the town. People still hustle, but their hustle did not have the violence of Lagos. It did not trample everything in its path. Here, life seems to move with deliberate steps. Men stop to greet one another properly. Women spoke across the road without fear of wasting time. Traders argue prices and then laugh as if disagreement was not enmity.
There is community here. That unsettles me. Because community reminded me of what I did not have.

I pass a cluster of artisans working under open sheds: welders, carpenters, tailors, mechanics, and fabric sellers. A man beats metal into shape with steady rhythm. Another measures wood with a seriousness that gives dignity to the simplest task. Somewhere, a radio plays Fuji music. Somewhere else, a woman’s voice rises sharply in complaint before dissolving into laughter.

I watch people work. Ordinary work. Honest work. Work that did not require secrecy, blood, or allegiance to invisible gods. I wonder if my hands still knew how to do such things. I need a job. Anything. Farm labour. Restaurant attendant. Motor conductor. Site labourer. Security guard. Messenger.
Anything that would earn enough to eat and keep a roof over my head. Nobody knows me here. That is both comfort and punishment.

In school, I had been feared. Known. Named. Avoided. Summoned. Respected. In Ogbomosho, I am simply another young man walking with a travel bag and uncertainty in his eyes. The town does not care who I had been. Perhaps that is mercy. Perhaps that is humiliation.

By noon, the sun has hardened over the roads. Dust cling to my sandals from my long aimless trek. Hunger begins to move inside me with quiet insistence. I buy bread and a bottle of soft drink from a small shop and sit beneath the shade of a mango tree near an old compound.

Children play nearby, chasing a worn football across a patch of dry earth. One of them fall. The others laugh. He stands, dusts his knees, and run back into the game. I watch him longer than I intended. A strange anger rises inside me. Not against the children. Against life. Against society. Against God.

Where was God when a boy became an orphan inside a living world? Where was society when young men like me were being collected by fraternities, politicians, criminals, and false fathers? Where were the institutions that should have protected us before we became weapons? People condemn the violent boy after he has become dangerous. Few ask who abandoned him when he was still soft enough to be guided.
The thought makes me bitter. I had been exploited. By the fraternity. By politicians. By older men who understood our wounds and used them as doorways.
Even the society that feared us had helped produce us through neglect, hypocrisy, and silence. Yet the bitterness did not save me.
By evening, I return to the guest house more tired than hopeful.

I had asked around for work. Some people told me to come back. Some looked at me suspiciously. Some asked who I knew. One man laughed and said jobs were not lying around waiting for strangers. He was right.
Nothing was waiting for me. Not work. Not family. Not forgiveness. Not certainty.
But, as I walked through the streets of Ogbomosho under the dimming sky, I felt something I could not name.
The town had not healed me. It had not welcomed me with answers. It had not made my fear disappear. But it had given me distance. Sometimes distance is the first kind of mercy.

At night, from the window of my small guest-house room, I watch the town settle into quiet. Motorcycle sounds thin. Shop lights go off one after another. Voices fade behind compound walls. Somewhere in the distance, drums begin faintly, perhaps from a gathering, perhaps from a festival rehearsal, perhaps from life continuing without asking permission from my sorrow.
I lay on the bed and stared at the ceiling. This is my new home. For now.
A town of farms, markets, old compounds, patient roads, Yoruba voices, and strangers who did not know what kind of darkness had followed me here.

I do not know what to do with my fractured life. But I know what I have to do first. Survive today. Then tomorrow. Then the next day. One day at a time. One breath at a time. One unknown road at a time.
And if God had not forgotten me as I fear, then perhaps He has brought me to this quiet town not to hide me from my enemies alone… but to hide me long enough to begin again.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-FIVE
content: By the time harmattan begins to dry my skin and my pocket at the same pace, darkness starts speaking to me in a language I understand too well.
I do not give the thoughts names. Some thoughts are snares, and to name them is to give them furniture in the room. It is enough to say that I am tired.

And tiredness can become a cruel counsellor when a man has no roof he can call his own, no family expecting his return, no school to return to, no fraternity to protect him, and no clear tomorrow waiting with mercy.

Ogbomosho is colder than I expect. Not the cold of Jos, which descends with mountain confidence, but the dry, dusty chill of harmattan evenings when the wind touches the skin and leaves behind a fine ash of loneliness. By day, the town moves with patient energy. Traders sell. Farmers arrive with produce. Motorcycles buzz through narrow roads. Children laugh in compounds. Women call one another across fences.
But by evening, when the shops begin to close and cooking smoke rises from behind old houses, the town reminds me that I am a stranger.
No one is looking for me here. No one is waiting for me. No one knows what I am fleeing from.

I sit behind an unfinished classroom block, my back against a rough wall, watching the last light drain from the sky. The building belongs to a school still under construction. Concrete pillars rise like tired bones. Iron rods point upward from the walls. Blocks are stacked in uneven piles. A torn cement bag flutters near a heap of sand.
Everything around me is unfinished. The building. The day. My life.

A small boy runs past, chasing a rubber tyre with a stick in his hand. He cannot be more than seven. His slippers slap the earth as he runs, and each time the tyre sways away from him, he laughs as though the world has never betrayed anybody.
The tyre rolls towards me and falls against my foot.
He stops in front of me, breathing hard.
“Uncle, throw am.”
I look at him.
His shirt is oversized. One slipper is beginning to split near the toe. Dust covers his knees. But his eyes are bright with the careless authority of childhood.
“What is your name?” I ask.
“Sunday,” he says proudly. “But my mummy calls me Miracle.”
The answer touches something old inside me.
Miracle. A name given by someone who believes a child is an answer. A child desired. A child received. A child named with gratitude.
I push the tyre back to him.
He grins.
And for one brief moment, I see another boy in him. A boy I used to be before bitterness found me.
“Why you dey sit alone?” Sunday asks.
“I am resting.”
He nods with the authority of a doctor. “When you rest finish, you go stand up.”
Then he runs off, chasing his tyre into the dusty evening.
I sit there long after he disappears.
Sometimes God does not send an angel with wings. Sometimes He sends a child with a broken slipper to tell a man to stand up.
A combi van stops a few metres from where I sit, its body painted in fading colours, its windows dusty from the road. The conductor leans out and calls to intending passengers.
“Camp ground! Camp ground!”
I look up. Camp ground.
The words settle in me strangely. A church camp, perhaps.
A safe place. Or at least safer than sleeping behind an unfinished building with fear as a blanket.
Before I fully decide, my body rises. I climb into the bus with people who know where they are going, though I do not.
That has become the story of my life. Following movement because standing still hurts too much.

The church campground lies on the outskirts of town. By the time we arrive, the place is alive with movement. Large banners hang across the entrance, announcing a week-long camp meeting. Loudspeakers tied to poles crackle with instructions. Ushers direct people towards registration points. Mothers carry bags on their heads and babies on their backs. Young men unload mattresses from buses. Children run everywhere, free and noisy, as though the entire camp has been built for their amusement.
Nobody asks too many questions. That is the mercy of large religious gatherings. A crowd can hide a man better than darkness.

I blend in with the congregants. Members move towards registration tables. Visitors loiter uncertainly. I sit near the edge of the main auditorium and watch. The auditorium is a vast open structure roofed with corrugated sheets, supported by steel columns, and lined with rows of wooden benches. The concrete floor is cold beneath my feet. Electric bulbs hang from long wires overhead. At the far end, a raised platform holds a pulpit, musical instruments, plastic flowers, and a banner bearing the theme of the programme.
I wait for night.

When darkness settles, many of those not accommodated in the hostels begin arranging benches into makeshift beds. I pull two benches together and lie down among strangers. A man nearby snores almost immediately. Somewhere, a baby cries. A woman whispers prayers in Yoruba. The loudspeakers hum softly even when no one is speaking, like the campground itself is breathing.
I sleep badly.
At dawn, the speakers wake us with worship music, prayer points, and instructions for the day’s programme.

People rise slowly from benches and mats, folding wrappers, shaking dust from clothes, gathering buckets, soap cases, towels, and toothbrushes. I remain still, watching them. I need to know where the bathrooms are, where water is fetched, where strangers can move without questions.
After a while, I follow a group of young men towards the general bathroom area. The queue is long.

Congregants stand with buckets in hand, waiting patiently. Some talk. Some sing. Some complain. Children run between adults with the sacred freedom of those who do not yet understand shame. Mothers bathe toddlers in the open field, pouring water over small heads while scolding them for wriggling. Fathers carry sleepy children on their shoulders. Families spread clothes over shrubs, ropes, and parked vehicles.
I watch them.
A child crying because soap enters his eyes. A mother laughing while wrapping a towel around her daughter. A father adjusting his son’s oversized shirt. A girl refusing to leave her mother’s side.
The sight opens a door inside me.
And suddenly, I am no longer in Ogbomosho. I am a child again. My foster mother wakes me before dawn whenever we attend camp meeting. Her voice is soft, but final.
“Ebuka, wake up, darling. We must get to the bathroom before the queue becomes impossible.”

She is British by birth, though she has lived in Nigeria long enough to understand our impatience with queues, our love for loud greetings, and our habit of carrying more luggage than a journey requires. Her skin is pale, her hair is always packed neatly beneath a scarf during camp, and her eyes carry a kindness I do not understand when I am young.

My foster father is taller, quieter, and more deliberate. He speaks slowly, as though every word requires moral responsibility before leaving his mouth. He loves order. Lists. Labels. Timetables. He packs our things as if preparing for an expedition: blankets folded tightly, toiletries arranged in transparent bags, medicines in a separate pouch, snacks rationed, torchlights checked, Bible marked, hymn book secured.
They were not my real parents. Everybody knew. That is the wound. Not that I did not have parents in the house. But that the world never allowed me to forget they are not mine by blood.
At camp meetings, children stared. Some boldly. Some with the cruelty of curiosity.
“Why your mummy white?”
“Na your real father be that?”
“Dem buy you?”
“Are you adopted?”
“Where your real mama dey?”

The questions came like stones thrown by small hands. Most of the children did not understand the injury. They were only repeating the wonder of adults, the whispers they had heard behind doors, the half-jokes made in kitchens and church corners.
But I understand. Or rather, I felt it before I understand it.

I looked at my foster mother’s pale hand holding my dark one and felt both protected and exposed. Her love covered me, but her difference announced my history to strangers. I couldn’t disappear into the ordinary crowd of children who resembled their mothers, spoke like their fathers, and carried names that explained them.
I was always the boy with the white parents. The adopted boy. The fortunate boy. The pitied boy. The questioned boy. My foster mother handled the questions with grace.
“He is our son,” she says simply.
Not adopted son. Not foster son. Our son.

She says it with such firmness that most adults become embarrassed by their own curiosity. My foster father places his hand on my shoulder, not possessively, but reassuringly, as if to say, You belong here, no matter what they think.
I believed them sometimes. Other times, I did not. That is the problem with an orphaned heart.
Love can stand right in front of it, calling its name, feeding it, clothing it, defending it, and still the heart asks, but what if they change their mind?

I had privileges many children would envy. Good schools. Clean clothes. Books. Birthdays remembered. Medical attention when I am sick. A bed that belongs to me. Parents who attend visiting days. A mother who prays with me. A father who insists I look people in the eye when I speak because, according to him, “A child of God should not live apologetically.”
But insecurity has a wicked appetite. It eats evidence.

No matter how much they loved me, a part of me waited for rejection. I watched their faces for signs of tiredness. I listened to their correction as though discipline meant regret. I stored every disagreement in a secret chamber and labeled it proof that I did not truly belong.
Then the allegation came.
Even now, thinking of it makes my chest tighten.
A girl who was seeking an opportunity accused me of what I did not do. I remember the room. The voices. The disbelief. The shame.

Not because my foster parents immediately condemned me. They did not. My foster mother kept saying, “Let him speak. Ebuka must speak.” My foster father’s face went pale with distress, but he did not raise his voice. He asked questions. He wanted to understand.
But I was no longer hearing them. I heard every childhood question again. Where is your real mother? Are they your real parents? Dem buy you?

Inside me, the old fear stood up and says, this is it. This is when they finally return you. Maybe I mistook their confusion for rejection. Their pain for abandonment. I mistook the seriousness of the accusation for proof that love has expired. So I ran before anyone can send me away.
That is my habit. Leave first. Reject before being rejected. Strike before being struck. Disappear before being dismissed.
I tell myself I am angry. But beneath the anger is terror. I wish now that I had stayed. I wish I spoke for myself. I wish I trusted the love they spent years proving.
Instead, I threw away a beautiful life because the orphan inside me believes abandonment more easily than affection.
The queue moves forward. Someone behind me taps my shoulder and tells me to move.
I return to the campground. To Ogbomosho. To the cracked concrete floor beneath my feet. To the bucket in my hand. To the noise of children and the smell of soap and damp towels.
For a moment, I cannot move.
I watch a little boy cling to his mother’s wrapper while she pours water over his head. He cries dramatically, though nothing terrible is happening. She laughs and wipes his face.

The tenderness nearly breaks me. I once had that. Not exactly like this. But I had care. Protection. A home. And I lost it. Not all at once. Not because nobody loved me. But because I did not know how to receive love without suspecting it.

Now here I am, a grown man hiding in a church camp, with a travel bag, little money, and a past that may still catch up with me. I do not know how long I can stay here. I do not know whether the GODs will find me. I do not know whether Otunba’s money will send men after me. I do not know whether Lizzy will forgive my silence. I do not know whether my foster parents ever stopped hoping I will come home.
That last thought hurts most.
Because if so, then I have not only been abandoned. I have also abandoned hope.

When I finish bathing, I return to the auditorium and sit alone at the far end. The morning session has not started. Workers are arranging chairs near the platform. A choir member tests a microphone. Children chase each other between the benches until an usher scolds them.
I open the Bible Lizzy gave me. My eyes fall on no particular verse. I simply hold it. Like a man holding the last proof that another life may still be possible.
For the first time in many years, I allow myself to remember my foster mother’s voice without anger. “Ebuka, darling, wake up. We must get to the bathroom before the queue becomes impossible.”
A small smile touches my lips and disappears. I had a mother once. Not by blood. But perhaps love never needs blood to be real.
I bow my head. No eloquent prayer comes. Only a whisper.
“God, if You have not forgotten me… teach me how to come home to myself.”
The loudspeakers crackle. The camp begins to wake fully.
And somewhere deep inside the ruins of my orphaned heart, a door I think has been locked for years opens slightly.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-SIX
content: By the second night at the campground, I learn that benches are not made for mercy. They are made for sitting.

A man may pretend otherwise for a few hours, but by dawn his back will testify against him. The wooden bench beneath me is hard, narrow, and indifferent. I sleep on it with one arm folded under my head, my small bag tucked between my legs, and one eye half-open against the fear that someone may decide to steal the little I still own.

Around me, other men and women are also sleeping on church benches. Some are elderly. Some are young. Some are mothers with children curled against their laps. Some are students. Some look like workers who have travelled from far places and miscalculated the size of the crowd.

The annual convention draws more people than the church can comfortably hold. The hostel rooms are filled before many of us arrive. Those who can afford accommodation cannot find space. Those who cannot afford it, like me, do not bother asking twice. The auditorium becomes our dormitory, our waiting room, and for some of us, our refuge from the open night.
By morning, people wake in layers. First, the prayer warriors. Then the women with children. Then the ushers. Then the men whose bodies refuse to forgive the benches.

I sit up slowly, my back aching as if someone has packed stones inside my spine. A man beside me groans and stretches his legs. Another man rolls from one bench to another and almost falls. Someone laughs softly. A baby cries.
One woman whispers, “Thank You, Jesus.” It’s not because the night was comfortable, but because it has ended.
I rub my face.
I have survived another night. These days, survival is no longer dramatic. It is simply waking up.

After the morning devotion, I remain at the edge of the auditorium, watching people move toward the food vendors outside the campground. Some buy pap and akara. Some buy bread and tea. Some sit under trees, eating from plastic plates. I have very little money left, so I buy only a small loaf of bread and a sachet of water.
I eat slowly, stretching each bite as if patience can increase quantity. That is when I first notice him.

He is sitting under a tree not too far from me, wearing a faded blue shirt, dark trousers, and safety boots that look too serious for a church camp. A small duffel bag rests beside him. He is holding a disposable cup of tea and looking at the crowd with the tired amusement of a man who has suffered the same bench-warfare as the rest of us.
Our eyes meet. He smiles. Not the careless smile of someone trying to start unnecessary conversation. A simple, human smile.
I nod.
He lifts his cup slightly as if to greet me.
I look away. I do not want friendship. Friendship asks questions. Questions require stories. Stories open doors. And I am tired of doors.
A few minutes later, he walks toward me anyway.
“Brother, good morning.” His voice is warm and easy, with a Yoruba lilt that makes the words sound rounder.
“Good morning,” I reply.
He points at the bench beside me. “May I?”
I shrug.
He sits.
For a moment, neither of us speaks. That makes me trust him a little. People who are too eager to fill silence often want something.
He sips his tea and shakes his head. “This campground has defeated my bones.”
Despite myself, I smile.
“You slept in the auditorium?”
“Where else?” he says. “I tried to book accommodation. They told me everything is taken. Even the rooms that do not exist are taken.”
I almost laugh.
He sees the smile and continues. “Yesterday night, I slept like a folded umbrella. By morning, my waist is writing petition.”
This time, I laugh softly.
He grins. “My name is Kolawole.”
“Sammy.”
“Sammy,” he repeats, as though testing the weight of the name. “You are not from here.”
“Where are you from?”
I hesitate.
“Benin.” It is the nativity I adopted since I left my foster paremts, because I do not know where I am from. I cannot identify myself with a native hometown.
He nods.
“What brings you to Ogbomosho?”
I look toward the crowd.
“Life.”
He studies me for a moment. Then he nods again, this time more slowly.
“Life brings many people to many places.”
That answer spares me. I appreciate him for it.
He does not press.
Instead, he points toward the food vendors.
“You have eaten?”
He looks at the remaining piece of bread in my hand and knows I am lying by portion.
But he does not embarrass me. “I want to buy beans,” he says. “Come with me.”
“I’m fine.”
“I did not ask if you are fine. I said come with me.”
There is no force in his voice. Only invitation disguised as instruction.
I should refuse. Instead, I stand.

We walk to a woman selling beans, bread, boiled eggs, and tea from a wooden table. Kolawole orders two plates and pays before I can protest. He carries his plate to a plastic table under a tree and gestures for me to sit.
I sit.
The beans is hot, oily, and peppery.
For a few minutes, I forget shame. Hunger is a powerful silencer.
Kolawole eats with focus, tearing bread with his fingers and scooping beans as if the meal deserves respect. When he speaks again, his tone is casual.
“So, Sammy from Benin, what do you do?”
I wipe my fingers.
“I am looking for work.”
“What kind of work?”
“Any kind.”
He looks at me. “Any kind is not a job description.”
“It is when a man is desperate.” The words come out before I can restrain them.
His face softens, but not with pity. I hate pity.
Kolawole does not offer it. He only nods like a man receiving information.
“What can you do?”
“I was in school.”
“University?”
“What did you study?”
I hesitate again.
“Political science.”
His eyebrows rise.
“Ah. So you are a politician.”
“Good. Nigeria does not need more politicians. It needs more honest people who accidentally enter politics and survive.”
I almost smile.
“I left school,” I say.
“Money.” It is the easiest lie because it is partly true.
He nods, chewing slowly.
“Money is a stubborn gatekeeper.”
I say nothing.
“I work at a construction site,” he says. “Not far from here. I supervise the mixer and assist the foreman sometimes. The company is small, but work is work.”
I look up. “Are they hiring?”
He studies me.
“You have done site work before?”
“Can you carry blocks?”
“Can you push wheelbarrow?”
“Can you mix concrete?”
“I can learn.”
“Can you take insult from foreman without replying like university student?”
That one slows me.
He smiles.
“I am serious. Construction site is not lecture hall. Nobody cares about your grammar when cement is setting.”
“I understand.”
“No, you don’t. But you will.”
He leans back.

“The work is hard. Pay is daily. If rain falls, no work. If cement does not arrive, no work. If contractor disappears, no pay until he remembers poor people exist. But if you are serious, you can survive.”
Survive. The word enters me like permission.
“I am serious,” I say.
Kolawole looks into my face for a long moment. Perhaps he sees the hunger. Perhaps the exhaustion. Perhaps the part of me that is still running even while seated.
“Okay,” he says. “Come with me after the morning session. I will speak to the foreman.”
Just like that. No testimony. No investigation. No recommendation letter. No family background. No guarantor. Only one stranger deciding to risk a word for another stranger.
I lower my head. “Thank you.”
“Don’t thank me yet,” he says. “You have not met the foreman.”

The construction site sits beyond a dusty road, behind a half-broken fence and an iron gate that hangs slightly out of alignment. From outside, I hear the place before I understand it. Hammering. Men shouting. Wheelbarrows groaning. Iron rods clanging. A concrete mixer coughing itself into motion.

The site is a two-storey structure rising from the ground, unfinished and muscular. Columns stand exposed. Wooden formwork holds parts of the upper slab. Stacks of blocks line one side of the compound. Bags of cement are arranged under a tarpaulin near the store. Gravel and sharp sand sit in separate heaps like small hills waiting to be broken down into labour.
Men move everywhere. Some carrying head pans, some bending over iron rods, some pushing wheelbarrows, some standing around pretending to be useful until a supervisor shouts their names.
Kolawole walks through the gate as if he belongs there. I follow as someone who does not belong anywhere.
“Kola!” a man calls from beside the mixer. “You come late today.”
“I went to find new recruit,” Kolawole replies.
The man looks at me and laughs.
“This one? He looks like student.”
“He is student.”
“Then he will run before twelve.”
“I will not run,” I reply.
The man laughs harder.
Kolawole grins. “Good. Keep that sentence. You will need it.”

He leads me toward a stocky man wearing a faded hard hat, a sleeveless shirt, and trousers stained with cement. The man is holding a tape measure in one hand and shouting at two labourers who have stacked blocks too close to the fresh concrete.
“That thing will fall! Are you people blind?”
The labourers rush to correct it.
Kolawole waits until the man turns.
“Baba Fatai.”
The foreman looks at him. “What?”
“I bring somebody. He needs work.”
Baba Fatai looks me over from head to toe.
His eyes pause on my hands. Soft enough to betray school. Strong enough to be tested.
“You don work for site before?”
“No, sir.”
“At least you are not lying.”
He spits to one side.
“What is your name?”
“Sammy.”
“Where are you from?”
“Benin.”
“Can you work?”
“Yes, sir.”
“Everybody says yes until sun touches their head.”
“I can work.”
He steps closer. His breath smells faintly of kola nut and tobacco.

“This is not office. If I say carry, you carry. If I say move, you move. If I shout, you don’t squeeze face. If you break shovel, they deduct. If you spoil cement, they deduct. If you fight, you go. If you steal, police.”
“I understand.”
“You will start as labourer. Daily pay. No advance.”
I nod.
“How much?”
He names the amount.
It is small. Too small for dignity. But enough for food. Enough for another day.
“I accept,” I say.
Kolawole looks at me, as though checking if I understand what I am accepting.
I do. Maybe I do not.
But hunger is a cruel interpreter.
Baba Fatai points toward a pile of blocks. “Start there. Move those blocks to the back. Follow Sule.”
A thin labourer with a towel around his neck raises his hand.
“Come,” he says.
I follow him.
By the time I lift the first block, reality enters my body. The block is heavier than it looks. The rough edges bite into my palm. Dust rises into my nose. The sun leans hard on my head.
I carry one. Then another. Then another. At first, I try to count. After thirty, counting becomes foolishness.
By noon, my arms tremble. My back burns. My palms sting. My throat tastes of dust.
Sule laughs at me. “University boy, you never start.”
I force a smile. “I am starting.”
“Better.”
He lifts two blocks at once.
I lift one.

No one cares that I once understand books, arguments, strategy, scripture, fear, and death. Here, a man is measured by how long his body obeys work before complaint. And perhaps I need that. Perhaps I need a place where my past does not speak first.
At break time, Kolawole brings me sachet water. “You are bearing up?”
“I am alive.”
“That is not answer.”
“It is the honest version.”
He smiles.
“Good. Drink.”
I drink too quickly and cough.
He laughs.
“Slow down. Construction site is not running away.”
I look at the unfinished building.
“It looks like it is growing.”
“It is,” he says. “Every day a little. That is construction. It teaches patience by force.”
I turn to him.
“You studied engineering?”
“Mechanical engineering.”
“Then why are you here?”
The question escapes before courtesy can stop it. He does not take offence.
“Because Nigeria has many graduates and few doors,” he says. “I apply. I wait. I follow up. I pray. While waiting, I work.”
He looks toward the mixer. “Work is not shame. Idleness is more dangerous.”

The words stay with me. Work is not shame. Idleness is more dangerous. I know idleness. Idleness gives memory too much room. It allows fear to rehearse. It allows darkness to arrange chairs and sit comfortably in the mind.
After break, I return to the blocks. By evening, my body no longer belongs to me. It belongs to labour.

Every muscle protests. My palms have reddened. Cement dust cakes my trousers. Sweat dries and returns. The other workers begin to wash their hands near a drum of water. Some joke. Some quarrel over wages. Some collect their money and leave quickly, as if poverty is waiting at home with a list.
Baba Fatai pays me last. He counts the notes into my hand without looking at my face.
“You try small,” he says.
From him, I understand it is praise.
“Thank you, sir.”
“Come early tomorrow.”
“I will.”
Kolawole claps me lightly on the back.
“You survive day one.”
“Barely.”
“Barely is still survive.”

The workers begin to leave the site in groups. I follow them out. Not because I have somewhere to go. Because leaving with them makes me look like I do. This is one of the first lessons I learn in exile: appearances can protect a man from questions.
We walk down the road together. At the junction, the workers scatter into different streets, different rooms, different families, different small burdens.
Kolawole turns to me. “Where do you stay?”
“At the campground.”
“Still?”
“For now.”
He frowns.
“That place is not for staying long.”
“I know.”
“Do you have money for room?”
“Not yet.”
He looks at me closely. Again, that look. The one that notices too much.
“I can ask around.”
“No, I will manage.”
“Sammy.”
“I will manage.”
He studies me for another second, then nods. “Okay. Tomorrow, come early.”
“I will.”
He walks away.
I stand at the junction until he disappears. Then I begin walking without direction.

Ogbomosho evening receives me with its dusty tenderness. The town softens under the falling light. Women fry akara by the roadside. Children chase one another around parked motorcycles. Radios play Fuji music from small shops. Somewhere, a church bell rings. Somewhere else, a muezzin’s call rises into the sky. Ogbomosho carries many prayers at once, and I wonder which one God hears first.
I wander until darkness begins to gather. Then I return to the construction site. The gate is partly open.

The security man sits near the entrance of the compound, eating from a stainless steel plate. He is an older man with grey in his beard and eyes that look as if they have seen many people fall from one status to another. A small radio murmurs beside him.
He looks up. “You forget something?”
I hesitate.
“Then?”
I clear my throat.
“I need a place to sleep tonight.”
He stares at me.
The shame rises hot inside me, but I stand there. There is no dignity in pretending to have a bed when one does not.
“Campground no good?” he asks.
“It is far. And crowded.”
He continues eating for a moment.
Then he points with his spoon toward a one-room space by the gate.
“I stay there.”
“I can sleep outside.”
He shakes his head. “Mosquito will finish you. Come.”

The room is small, barely large enough for his mat, a kerosene stove, a bucket, a hanging shirt, and a few personal things arranged with poor man’s order. The smell of old clothes, kerosene, dried sweat, and mosquito coil sits heavily in the air.
“I no get bed for you,” he says.
“I don’t need bed.”
He looks at me with quiet understanding. “No man says that unless life has beaten him.”
I do not answer.
He gestures toward a corner.
“There are empty cement papers there. Arrange them. Sleep small. Tomorrow you find better place.”
“Thank you.”
He shrugs.
“Thank God. Me, I only open door.”

I gather empty cement packets from near the store and spread them on the floor in layers, patting them flat until they become something like a mattress. Dust rises from them and settles on my hands. I use my bag as a pillow.
It is not comfortable. But it is inside a locked compound. For tonight, that is mercy.

The security man switches off his radio after a while. Outside, the site grows quiet. The building stands unfinished in the dark, columns rising like silent witnesses. The tools rest. The mixer sleeps. The wheelbarrows lean against the wall like tired animals.
I lie on cement packets and stare into the dark.

My body aches. My hands burn. My stomach is not full. My future is still uncertain. But today, I meet Kolawole. Today, I work. Today, I earn. Today, someone opens a door. It is not a big door. It is not the kind of door a man boasts about. But it is enough to keep me from the street.
I close my eyes.
The ground is hard beneath me. The air smells of cement. A mosquito whines near my ear. Still, for the first time since I entered Ogbomosho, I feel something almost like gratitude.
Not joy. Not peace. Not yet. But a small, stubborn gratitude. The kind that survives in poor places. The kind that says, I am still here. And tonight, still here is enough.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-SEVEN
content: Our break-time lunch is not in our usual place. Kolawole takes us to a small restaurant near the main road, the kind of place where labourers, drivers, artisans, and junior civil servants gather because the food is affordable and the portions are honest. The zinc roof traps the afternoon heat. Ceiling fans rotate lazily above us, pushing the smell of hot amala, pepper stew, fried meat and dust from one table to another.
We are still in our site uniforms. Our boots leave dry cement marks on the floor.

Kola sits opposite me, laughing with three other workers under his supervision. He invited them to join us, and he is paying for everybody’s meal. That alone warns me. Men like Kolawole do not spend money publicly without a reason. He is generous, yes, but he is also deliberate.
I eat quietly. The food is warm and heavy in my stomach, but my mind is elsewhere. Lately, I depend on the rhythm of the site more than I admit.
