Wake early. Wash quickly. Report before the foreman starts shouting. Carry blocks. Mix concrete. Push wheelbarrows. Eat with Kola. Listen to his stories. Return tired enough to sleep before despair begins its sermon.
Routine has become my medicine. Kolawole has become part of that medicine. He does not know how much.

After the plates are cleared and one of the boys still licks stew from his fingers, Kola wipes his mouth with the back of his hand, sits straighter, and says, “Gentlemen, I have something to tell you.”
The table quiets.
He smiles, but there is tenderness beneath the smile. “I got the job.”
One of the workers frowns. “Which job?”
“The meteorological company in Lagos.”
For a second, nobody speaks. Then the others explode into congratulations.
“Kola!”
“Engineer!”
“Lagos man!”
Someone slaps the table. Another worker reaches across to shake his hand. Kola laughs, embarrassed and pleased at the same time.
“It is not like that,” he says. “But yes, it is a good place. Very good place. The one I have been praying for.”
I smile. I even clap. I say the words a friend should say. “Congratulations.”
And I mean it.
That is what makes the pain confusing. I am happy for him. Truly.

Kolawole deserves it. He is too intelligent to remain forever beneath the sun supervising casual labourers, arguing with suppliers, and begging contractors for wages they should pay without being chased. He has carried his own delay with dignity. If Lagos opens a door for him, he should walk through it.

But why now? The question rises inside me before I can stop it. Why now, when I have only just begun to breathe? Why now, when one familiar voice in this unfamiliar town is beginning to sound like shelter? Why now, when I am still learning how not to collapse?

I keep smiling, but something inside me begins to withdraw. This is how it happens. People appear. They help you stand. Then life removes them before your legs are strong. My foster parents. Bitty. Lizzy. The school I fled from. Now Kolawole.
Every time I begin to imagine support, something reaches in and takes it away. What is this pattern of loneliness following me? What curse makes companionship temporary in my hands?
What kind of life keeps teaching a man not to need anyone, then punishes him for being alone?
Kola notices. Of course he notices. He always notices what people try to hide.
The others are still celebrating when his eyes settle on me. His smile softens. I look away, pretending to search for a toothpick on the table.
“When are you resuming?” one of the workers asks.
“Immediately,” Kola replies. “I leave tomorrow.”
The word enters me like bad news. Tomorrow. Not next week. Not month end. Tomorrow.
The restaurant suddenly becomes too loud.

Someone laughs. Plates clash near the kitchen. A woman shouts an order. A bottle falls and rolls beneath a table. Outside, a motorcycle revs hard before speeding away. All of it comes to me as if from underwater.
Kolawole is leaving tomorrow. I lower my eyes. I do not want him to see what his good news does to me. But he sees it.
After the others finish their drinks and rise noisily, Kola stays behind a moment.
He places a hand on my shoulder. “Sammy.”
“I am fine.”
He does not remove his hand. “You are not.”
I force a smile. “I said I am happy for you.”
“I know.”
“So what else do you want me to say?”
He sits back down.
The others wait near the entrance, still joking about Lagos life.
Kola leans closer. “Listen to me. Just hang in here, okay?”
I stare at the table.
“Once I am settled in, I will help you get a job there. I will come back and take you.”

The promise should comfort me. Instead, it frightens me. Not because I think he is lying. Because promises are fragile things when held against poverty, distance, and the hunger of survival. I have heard enough promises in my life to know that many die without anyone murdering them. They simply expire under pressure.
“I will be fine,” I say.
He squeezes my shoulder once and stands.
I rise too.
But I am the least excited among us as we begin walking back to the site.
The others keep talking about Lagos. Traffic. Money. Big offices. Women. Weather instruments they know nothing about. Kola laughs with them occasionally, but he keeps glancing at me.
I walk in silence. The sun is harsh. The road shimmers. My uniform sticks to my body. Dust rises with each step. Inside me, something heavier than heat begins to settle.
By the time we approach the construction site, we hear shouting.

At first, it sounds like ordinary site trouble. Workers quarrel every day. Labourers shout. Foremen threaten. Suppliers argue over measurements, cement count, iron rods, wages, sand quality, and everything else that can produce money or blame.
But this shouting is different. Sharper. More frightened.
We quicken our steps. Then we see the vehicles. Three of them are parked outside the gate. One official-looking SUV. One pickup. One police van.
My stomach tightens.

A crowd of workers has gathered by the entrance. Some are holding their tools. Others are standing in cement-stained clothes with confused faces. Two policemen are pushing men away from the gate. One worker protests too loudly and is shoved backward. Another tries to retrieve his bag from inside the compound, but a policeman blocks him with a baton.
“What is happening?” Kola asks, already moving ahead.
Nobody answers immediately.

The site that only hours ago is noisy with hammers, wheelbarrows, mixers, and men shouting instructions now looks violated. Work has stopped. The concrete mixer is silent. Head pans lie abandoned. A shovel rests in wet mortar. Blocks are stacked halfway along an unfinished wall, as if the building itself has been interrupted mid-sentence.
A man in a neatly ironed shirt stands near the gate holding a file. He speaks with the authority of a person who does not build anything but has the power to stop everything.
“Government directive,” he announces to whoever will listen. “This site is sealed until further notice.”
“Sealed?” one of the masons shouts. “For what?”
“Irregularity in the property filing.”
“What irregularity?”
“You can direct your questions to the State Development Agency.”
Another worker curses under his breath.
A policeman turns sharply.
“You, come here.”
The worker steps back immediately.
Fear restores discipline.
Kola pushes through the crowd.
“Officer, please, what exactly is going on?”
The official looks him over.
“Who are you?”
“Site supervisor.”
“Then tell your people to leave peacefully.”
“We have materials inside. Tools. Personal things.”
“You will apply officially to retrieve them.”
“Apply?” Kola repeats, disbelief breaking through his voice.
The official’s face does not change.
“This property is under enforcement action. Work stops today.”
Today. The word finds me where tomorrow has already wounded me. Kola leaves tomorrow. The site closes today.
For a moment, I cannot hear anything else.

The crowd continues shouting. Workers argue. Police bark orders. Someone says the governor signed the permit. Someone else insists the owner offended the wrong people. Another says the agency has been looking for an excuse to take over the land. A carpenter complains that his wages have not been paid. A labourer asks who will pay him for the week.
Nobody answers. That is when the full meaning reaches me. I have lost my job. Not gradually. Not with warning. Not even through my own failure. Just like that.
One file. One signature. One government seal. One statement from a man whose shoes have never stepped into wet cement. And the only structure holding my days together collapses.
I stand outside the gate, looking at the unfinished building. Only this morning, it seems like a place of hardship. Now it looks like mercy being taken away. My chest tightens.
I try to breathe slowly. Do not panic. Do not show it. Do not let the old darkness see you shaking. But inside me, I am breaking.
How does a man keep standing when every support is removed one after another? How does he continue believing in mercy when mercy keeps arriving late or leaving early?
I think of Sunday, the little boy with the tyre. When you rest finish, you go stand up. But what if I am tired of standing? What if standing itself has become the punishment?
Kolawole returns to my side. His face has changed. He is no longer the laughing man from the restaurant. He is calculating now. Thinking. Searching for options. “Sammy.”
I do not answer.
“Sammy, look at me.”
I force myself to look at him.
“We will sort something out.”

The words are kind. Too kind. I almost hate him for saying them. Because he is leaving. He has a job. He has Lagos. He has a future calling him by name. I have a sealed gate. A travel bag. Little money. And a town that does not know me.
“You said we will sort something out,” I say quietly. “But you are leaving tomorrow.”
Pain crosses his face. “I know.”
“You will be gone.”
“I know.”
The crowd noise swells around us.
I laugh once. Not because anything is funny. Because if I do not laugh, I may fall apart. “Of course.”
“Sammy…”
“Of course this happens today.”
He says nothing.
“What else should I expect?” My voice remains low, but bitterness has entered it. “The day you tell me you are leaving, the site closes. Perfect arrangement.”
“This is not your fault.”
“I know.”
But knowing does not help. That is the cruelty of some suffering. It does not require guilt to feel personal.
Kola steps closer. “Listen. You are not alone.”
The words hit me too hard. I turn away. “Don’t say that.”
“Sammy…”
“Please.” My voice cracks.
I hate that it cracks. I hate that he hears it. I stare at the sealed gate because I cannot look at him.
“You don’t know what that means to someone like me.”
He becomes quiet.
The policemen continue pushing workers away. The official pastes a notice near the gate. A red seal appears across the entrance like a wound.
I feel something inside me sink. Not anger. Not even fear. Something worse. Resignation. The kind of tiredness that does not ask what next because it no longer trusts the answer.

Kolawole places his hand on my shoulder again. This time, I do not pull away. But I do not lean into it either. I cannot afford to. Leaning is how falling begins when the person holding you has somewhere else to go.
We stand there until the crowd begins to thin.
Workers leave in small groups, carrying what little they are allowed to take. Some curse the government. Some curse the contractor. Some curse the country. A few say nothing at all.
Silence is the language of men calculating hunger.
When the gate is finally locked, the sound of the padlock closing enters me like a final word.
Kola exhales. “I will speak to someone before I leave,” he says. “There may be another site.”
I nod. Not because I believe it. Because he needs me to.
The sun is beginning to go down.
Its light falls on the unfinished building, on the silent mixer, on the abandoned blocks, on the notice pasted at the gate, on the dust-covered faces of men who come to work and leave unemployed.
I realize then that the building and I are the same. Interrupted. Unfinished. Sealed off by forces neither of us can argue with.
And as Kolawole walks beside me away from the site, I understand that Ogbomosho has not become my hiding place. It has become my wilderness.
And the wilderness is not merely a place where a man is alone. It is a place where every false support is stripped away until he finds out whether anything inside him still wants to live.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-EIGHT
content: The room in Ogbomosho becomes smaller than it is when I first rented it. Not physically. The bed is still against the wall. The reading table still leans slightly to one side. The old chair still creaks under my weight. The curtain still hangs from a crooked rod, holding back very little of the harmattan dust that slips in through the window.

But tonight, the room feels smaller. As if the walls are moving closer while I am not looking. As if the ceiling is lowering itself above my head. As if the air itself is tired of carrying me. I sit on the edge of the bed and stare at nothing.

There is no work tomorrow. No construction site. No Kolawole. No routine to drag me out of myself. No voice to ask whether I have eaten. No hand on my shoulder. No promise I can trust without fearing it will leave like the others.
Everything that holds me upright in this town has been taken away quietly, one support after another, until my life becomes a building without scaffolding.
I laugh once. A dry, broken sound. Even my laughter sounds like someone else’s.
“You saw me,” I say into the room. My voice is low at first.
Then it rises, not in anger alone, but in the desperate honesty of a man who has run out of respectable prayers. “You saw me at the grave.”
The silence does not answer.

“I intentionally laid the cross upright at the grave of Your son. Your servant. I did it as an expression of my faith in You. I did it because I wanted to stand with the Man who was crucified on the cross. I did it because I believed You had forgiven me.”
My throat tightens. “But it appears You did not take notice.”
The words frighten me as soon as I say them. Yet I continue.
“Or is my sin too great for Your forgiveness?”
The question hangs in the room.

I look at my hands. Hands that have held weapons. Hands that have signed allegiance. Hands that have pushed boys toward darkness. Hands that have also held Legion’s hand while he died asking for prayer.
Now, it’s holding a rope I have carefully knotted.
“Why is my life marked with misery?” I ask. “Why does every path I take end in misfortune?”

The room remains still. I think of my foster parents. Their house. Their dining table. My foster mother’s voice calling me darling. My foster father’s hand on my shoulder. The false allegation. The shame. The day I ran before I can be sent away.
“Once, I was accused of a crime I did not commit,” I whisper. “Something I never thought about. Something that never crossed my mind. Where was Your justice?”
My eyes burn.
“I was driven away from people I called my parents. People I thought loved and trusted me like a father would trust and love a son.”
I press my palms against my eyes. But the memories keep coming.
“I have no father. No mother. No one.”
The words collapse inside me.

“I am sorry for joining the fraternity. I am sorry. But they were family to me when I needed one. They called me brother when I had forgotten what family felt like. They gave me a name when I was ashamed of the one life gave me.”
My voice breaks.
“And now that name is hunting me.”
Outside, somewhere in the compound, a door shuts. Someone coughs. A child laughs briefly, then is silenced by an adult voice. Life continues around me with cruel normalcy.
“It is only a matter of time before the GODs find me,” I continue. “No matter where I run, they will find me. The dreams terrify me. The memory of that night at the mountain terrifies me.”
The fear rises again. Not like a thought. Like a presence. The mountain. The darkness. The old covenant. The terrifying certainty that some powers do not forget names easily.
“I am tired,” I say.
This time there is no anger in it. Only defeat.
“I am tired of running. Tired of waking up afraid. Tired of beginning again. Tired of losing everyone.”

The room blurs. I do not know when I stand. I only know that something inside me crosses from thought into action, and that frightens me less than it should. That is how far I have fallen. Not into courage. Into exhaustion.

There are moments when despair does not shout. It organizes. It makes the room quiet. It makes the hands steady. It makes the mind speak with dangerous calm, as though all arguments have ended and only one terrible conclusion remains.
I move slowly through the room. The chair scrapes faintly against the floor. The sound is ordinary. Too ordinary until I place it precisely where I had imagined.
That almost breaks me. How can a moment so final be surrounded by such common sounds?
A neighbour’s radio murmurs through the wall. A pot lid clatters somewhere outside.
The child screams in another apartment. “No mummy, please, mummy please!” Gabriel. His voice pierces through the room.
I freeze.
The cry comes again, sharper this time. “Ahhh! Mummy, it’s okay! I will not do it again!”
I close my eyes. Not now. Please, not now. I tell myself to ignore him.

Gabriel is always fighting with his mother. The boy is seven, mischievous, stubborn, streetwise, and strangely gifted at finding trouble. His mother complains frequently that he refuses to do chores, refuses to do homework, refuses to sit still, refuses to behave like children in storybooks.
I have often saved him from beatings. Too often, perhaps.
Each time, I settle their war with a candy bar. Gabriel has learned the arrangement. His mother too, I suspect. Maybe both of them have been exploiting me in their own small domestic conspiracy.
Usually, the thought amuses me. Tonight, it hurts.
Because even this small routine, this childish exchange, this little rescue performed with sweets, is one of the few things that makes me feel useful in Ogbomosho.
The cane lashes again. Gabriel screams. Something in me gives way. I step out of my room.
His mother stands near their doorway, anger and tiredness mixed on her face. Gabriel pulls away from her the moment he sees me and runs straight into my arms.
He crashes against me and clings tightly, panting, sobbing, his small body trembling with outrage and relief.
“Uncle Sam!”
I hold him.
For a moment, I cannot speak. He smells of dust, sweat, and childhood. His fingers grip my shirt as if I am a place of safety. Me. Sammy. A man who cannot save himself.
“Okay,” I whisper. “Okay. Come.”
I take his small hand and lead him back to my room.
His crying reduces to soft hiccups as we enter. I leave him near the chair and go into the bedroom to get his prize.
The last candy bar.

I stand before the small drawer and stare at it. He will not know this is the last candy he will be getting from me. He will not know that by tomorrow he might hear people whispering about Uncle Sam in frightened tones. He will not understand why adults gather outside a door. He will not understand why his mother covers his ears. Or perhaps he will not remember at all.
Children forget easily, people say. But do they?

Will he remember the man who bribes him with sweets when his mother’s anger becomes too much? Will he remember my voice? My hand holding his? My foolish attempt to stand between him and pain? Or will I vanish from his mind like everyone else has vanished from mine?
The thought opens something inside me. A wound beneath the wound.
I pick up the candy bar and return.
Gabriel is no longer seated where I left him. He is standing in the middle of the room, staring at what I have tried to keep hidden from my own conscience.
His face is bright with curiosity.
“Uncle Sam,” he asks innocently, “what do you want to do?” He asks holding the rope I had made into a knot in his tiny hands.
The world stops. My breath catches.
I move quickly and take him away from that corner of the room, more sharply than I intend.
“Leave that place,” I say, my voice harsh and low.
He startles.
Then he looks from me to the rope with the terrible logic of children who have seen too much and understand too little.
“Uncle, are you going away?”
The words strike me harder than any blow I have ever received.
He says it without horror. Without comprehension.
Without knowing that he has just walked into the deepest darkness of my life carrying the innocence of a child asking a simple question.
Tears rise before I can stop them.
Gabriel unwraps the candy bar. “Should I stay with you?”
I cannot breathe.

The candy wrapper crackles in his small hands. His eyes remain bright, curious, trusting. He has no idea what he is saying. No idea that his innocence has become a mirror. No idea that God has used his small mouth to drag death into the light and make it look foolish, ugly, and unbearable.
“No,” I whisper. My voice is broken.
He frowns.
“Go home,” I say. “Go to your house.”
He shrugs, already distracted by the candy.
Then he skips out of the room, chewing happily, the crisis forgotten before he reaches the door.
I stand still until his footsteps fade. Then my knees give way. I sink to the floor. Not gracefully. Not prayerfully. I collapse to the floor.
Strength leaves my body as though someone has opened a hidden wound and all the darkness is draining out at once.
I begin to weep.
Not the controlled tears of grief. Not the dignified sorrow of a man still trying to appear strong.
I weep like a child.

Like the child who runs from his foster parents. Like the boy who wants a family badly enough to join a fraternity. Like the man who places a cross on a grave and expects heaven to answer before despair comes knocking.
“God,” I sob, “I don’t even know if You still see me.” My voice breaks into pieces. “But if You do…” I bend forward until my forehead touches the floor. “I am tired.”
The words come from somewhere deeper than speech. “Please help me. I have nowhere else to go.”
I weep until my chest hurts. Until the room loses its shape. Until the night outside grows quiet.
And for the first time in many days, I do not ask God to explain my life. I only ask Him to keep me alive.

chapters.title: Chapter TWENTY-NINE
content: The only job available to me is the one that asks for nothing except a loud voice, strong legs, and the willingness to be insulted from morning till night. Bus conductor.
No certificate required. No recommendation letter. No question about school.

Just shout the route, collect fares, hang at the door, slap the body of the bus when passengers are complete, quarrel over change, inhale dust, swallow exhaust fumes, and pretend that every insult from drivers, passengers, touts, and area boys does not enter the soul.
It is honest work. That is what I keep telling myself. But honesty does not make it easy.

By the end of each day, my throat burns from shouting destinations across Ogbomosho roads. My palms smell of dirty notes and coins. My shirt clings to my body with sweat, dust, and the sour odour of transport work. Mosquito bites and heat rashes cover my arms and neck. I scratch until the skin breaks. At night, I return to a room that has no comfort, only a mattress, stale air, and thoughts waiting patiently for me.
The job does not merely exhaust me. It strips me.
Every day, I feel another layer of myself fall away. Student. Cardinal. Lethal Weapon. Disciple. Fugitive. Labourer. Conductor.
Soon, I fear, nothing will remain except a tired body carrying a name nobody calls.
I began drinking, not because I longed for alcohol again, but because it quietens the noise for a while. It does not remove the fear. It only spreads a cloth over it and tells me not to look.
But fear is patient. It waits beneath every bottle. It waits beneath every laugh. It waits beneath every foolish attempt to forget.
One morning, after a night I can barely remember, I wake with my head pounding and my mouth bitter. Something inside me says, Call Lizzy.
I resist it.
Then I hear it again. Not a voice. More like the last sane thought in a collapsing mind. Call Lizzy.

So I do. I do not know what I expect from her. Perhaps anger. Perhaps questions. Perhaps silence. Perhaps I only want to hear one voice from the life I fled before darkness finishes convincing me that I have no one.
When she answers, I almost end the call.
“Sammy?”
Her voice breaks something in me.
“I need to see you,” I say.
There is a pause.
“Where are you?”
I tell her.
Then I wait.
For hours, I regret it.

By the time she finds me, I am not where she left me. Not in a church. Not in a lecture hall. Not standing beside a grave with new faith burning inside my chest. Not in the heroic posture in which people prefer to meet those they once admire.

She finds me outside a beer parlour, sitting near a stained wall, smelling of cheap alcohol and shame. My beard is untrimmed. My eyes are swollen. My shirt carries the dust of the motor park. My slippers are worn. My arms are covered with marks from bites and scratches.
For a moment, I wish the earth would open and cover me. But the earth has seen worse men and refuses to perform that miracle.
Lizzy stops when she sees me. She does not speak immediately.
That silence hurts more than accusation.

Her eyes move over me slowly, not with disgust, but with shock. The kind of shock that grieves before it understands. Her lips part slightly. She looks as if she wants to say my name but cannot find the version of me that used to answer.
“Sammy,” she murmurs at last. Her voice is soft.
But it does not pamper my disgrace.
“What happened to you?”

I expect the question. Still, I do not know how to answer it. How does a man explain a fall that happens one step at a time? How does he describe the distance between a graveyard confession and a beer parlour chair?
I look away.
Lizzy’s eyes fill with tears. “What happened to you, Sammy?” she asks again. This time her voice trembles. “Where have you been living?”
I cannot speak.
Her tears finally fall.

She turns her face slightly, wipes them quickly, then notices where we are. The beer parlour is quiet because it is still early in the day. A few wooden tables stand outside beneath a torn shade. Two men sit far away, already drinking in silence. A radio inside plays old highlife music with poor reception.
Lizzy glances around, suddenly aware of the eyes that may be watching.
“Let’s go outside,” she says.
“We are outside.”
“Farther.”
I obey.
We sit at one of the wooden tables near the edge of the compound, where the dust from the road settles gently on everything.
For a while, neither of us speaks.
Then she folds her hands on the table. “You asked me to come,” she says. “What do you want?”
Her tone is controlled. Not cold. Controlled.
That frightens me more than tears.
The words fail.
“I just want to see you before...”
She watches me.
“Before what?”
I laugh without joy. “Before I disappear completely.”
Her face changes instantly. “Who told you you are disappearing?”
“I am the Key.” The sentence escapes with the tired certainty of a man who has repeated his fear too often to know it is fear. “I carry a sentence.”
I scratch my arm hard.
Lizzy’s eyes follow the movement. She reaches gently across the table and takes my wrist before I can continue. “What is this?”
“Nothing.”
“Sammy.”
“Insect bites.”
She studies my arm, then my neck, then my face. The grief returns to her eyes, but this time something else comes with it. Determination.
“You are not that thing,” she says.
“What thing?”
“The Key. Lethal Weapon. Whatever name they put on you.” Her grip tightens slightly. “You are Samuel Briggs.”
The name enters me strangely. Samuel Briggs. Not Cardinal. Not Lethal Weapon. Not fugitive. Not debtor. Not target. Samuel Briggs.
I almost do not recognize him.
“What does it change?” I whisper. “I still carry a sentence.”
“Nobody wants me.”
“That is not true.”
“My parents rejected me. The fraternity is after my life. The GODs will find me. Otunba’s money is hanging over me. I don’t have anyone.”
“You have me,” she says quickly.
I look at her.
“You have Bitty.”
I turn away.
“You ran away, Sammy. We did not abandon you.”
The words strike me because they are true. Truth sometimes hurts more when it is spoken gently.
“You cannot help me, Lizzy.”
“I can try.”
“You cannot stop this.”
“So why did you call me?”
I do not have an answer. Perhaps that is the answer. Some part of me still wants to be stopped from disappearing. Some stubborn remnant of life inside me still knows where to cry for help.
“What do you think is keeping me alive?” I ask. “It is the cross. They do not want to destroy me yet because they want me to reverse it.”
“Then let the Cross keep you alive until your faith grows stronger.”
“God doesn’t care about me.”
“He does.”
“He doesn’t.”
“He loves you.”
“Then why does He keep leaving me like this?”
Lizzy says nothing.
The question hangs between us, raw and ugly.

“Why didn’t He save Legion?” I continue. “Why didn’t He stop the accusation years ago? Why didn’t He give me a family that stays? Why does He keep watching me start again only to lose everything?” My voice breaks.
“Legion was right. I do not belong here.”
Lizzy reaches into her bag and brings out her phone.
“What are you doing?”
“Calling Bitty.”
“Lizzy, don’t.”
She ignores me.
I become suddenly conscious of myself. My shirt. My smell. My beard. My trembling hands. My itching skin. My disgrace sitting openly at a wooden table outside a beer parlour.
She turns away slightly as the call connects.
“Bitty?” Her voice is steady for only one second. Then it breaks.
“I found him.”
She listens.
“No, he is not well.”
I look away.
“He has become… he is drinking. He is not himself. He is talking like he expects everything to end.”
My throat tightens.
“I don’t know what to do,” she says.
A pause.
Then her eyes meet mine.
She does not hide the fear in them.
“I am afraid of leaving him.”
I turn my face away.
There are few humiliations deeper than hearing someone discuss your brokenness because they are trying to save you.
Lizzy holds the phone out to me. “Here. Bitty wants to talk to you.”
I hesitate. Then I take it.
“Hello, Bitty.”
“How are you, Sammy?”
“I’m fine.”
“He’s not fine,” Lizzy says sharply. “He’s having a death wish.”
Our eyes meet.
She grimaces at me as if daring me to lie again.
Bitty’s voice returns, firmer now.
“I told you to hold on to the Cross, Sammy. You failed.”
My head drops.
The words hurt. Because they come from Bitty. Because they are not entirely false.
“You don’t understand,” I whisper.
There is a pause.
Then his voice changes. “You are not speaking with Lizzy now. It is me. Bitty.”
I close my eyes.
“Did you say I don’t understand?”
“I’m sorry.”
“You think I don’t understand fear? You think I don’t understand guilt? You think I don’t know what it means to run from the fraternity and still hear its footsteps behind you?”
I say nothing.
“You are not alone in this fight.”
The sentence enters me slowly. Not as comfort at first. As disbelief.
“We are going to fix this together,” he says. “But I need your cooperation.”
I swallow.
“Are you listening?”
“You will do what Lizzy tells you.”
I glance at her.
She is watching me with tears still on her face.
“Do we have a deal?”
For a moment, I cannot answer. A deal means tomorrow. A deal means I must remain long enough for help to reach me. A deal means I must trust people again.
That may be the hardest part.
“Sammy?”
I breathe in.
“Deal.”
“Good. Give Lizzy the phone.”
I hand it back.
Lizzy walks a short distance away, listening carefully. She nods several times. She wipes her face. Then, gradually, something changes in her expression. Hope.
I see it before she speaks.
When she ends the call, she returns to the table with a smile that looks almost out of place in that beer parlour.
“Bitty has asked me to get you an apartment.”
I stare at her.
“He is sending money to pay for it and furnish it.”
I blink.
“And clothes,” she adds. “Proper clothes. Toiletries. Food. Everything you need to live in this place and start again.”
I look at her, but the words do not immediately reach the part of me that knows how to receive good news. My heart has become calloused by hardship. I do not know whether to be excited.

Perhaps adversity can become so familiar that good things begin to feel suspicious. Perhaps a man can suffer so long that kindness frightens him because it gives hope, and hope creates something new to lose.
“Thank you,” I mutter.
Lizzy sits back down.
I look at her.
“Don’t thank me like a stranger.” Her eyes soften. “You are not a burden, Sammy.”
The words nearly undo me. I look away quickly.
She lets the silence rest for a moment before speaking again. “God uses our adversities and difficulties as instruments to mold our character,” she says. “You need patience.”
I almost laugh.
Patience. How gentle the word sounds in the mouth of someone not being crushed by waiting. But I do not mock her. Because beneath the admonition, I hear love.
“You have to give God time to work on you,” she continues.
I stare at my hands. “What if He gets tired?”
“He doesn’t.”
“What if I do?”
“Then we will help you stand until you can stand again.”
We. The word is small. But it opens something inside me. For months, my life has been I. I fled. I hid. I suffered. I drank. I feared. I survive.
Now Lizzy places we on the table like bread before a starving man.
“We will help you stand.” She rises. “So,” she says, wiping the last trace of tears from her face, “let’s get to work finding you a place.”
I look around the beer parlour. The stained wall. The rough table. The empty bottles. The dust. The smell of alcohol and defeat. Then I stand. Slowly. But standing.

Lizzy leads the way toward the road, already asking a passerby about available apartments. Her steps are brisk, purposeful, almost excited. She looks more hopeful than I feel, and perhaps that is what support does. It lends a man hope before his own returns.
I follow her.

Behind us, the beer parlour remains open. The bottles remain. The dust remains. The shame remains. But I am leaving with someone who comes when I call, weeps when she finds me, refuses to abandon me, and brings another friend into my darkness.
For the first time in a long while, I feel a flash of something warm move through my chest. Not happiness. Not peace. Something smaller. But alive.
Maybe hope does not always arrive like sunrise. Sometimes it arrives as a phone call. A friend’s voice. A promise of rent. A woman rising from a wooden table and saying, let’s find you a place.
I walk beside Lizzy into Ogbomosho town, uncertain of tomorrow, still afraid of the GODs, still ashamed of what I have become. But no longer completely alone. And for today, that is enough.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY
content: My apartment is in the hinter part of Ogbomosho, where the town begins to loosen into farms, footpaths, old compounds, and fields that smell of dry grass, cassava stems, firewood smoke, and red earth. I chose this place because it does not invite attention.

No one looking for a fugitive and former disciple of the Inverted Cross will naturally come here first. The streets are not fashionable. The buildings do not make promises. Most of the people here are farmers, petty traders, labourers, apprentices, and families who have learned to stretch small income across long months. The place is ordinary enough to hide a man.
And for now, ordinary is safety. There is a knock at the door. I already know it is Lizzy.

She has a way of knocking that is both gentle and stubborn, as if she is asking permission while making it clear she will not be ignored. I open the door and find her standing there with two shopping bags in her hand.

We had gone out earlier to buy a few things for the room – plastic plates, a bucket, a small kettle, bedsheet, detergent, a mosquito coil holder, and other humble items that make poverty look organized. I thought that is all she has brought.
But when she enters, I see another bag. She places it on the table.
“These are for you,” she says.
I look at her, then at the bag.
“For me?”
I open it.
Inside is a Holy Bible and a book. The book has a dark cover and the face of the author on it. In Pursuit of Purpose, By Myles Munroe.
I hold it for a moment without speaking.

I am not particularly given to reading. Books remind me of school, and school reminds me of everything I have lost. Besides, my mind is still too noisy for pages. Thoughts run through me like frightened animals. Regret. Fear. Memory. Shame. Sometimes I cannot even finish a prayer without another image interrupting it.
Lizzy watches me.
“Please,” she says softly, “I’d like you to read the book.”
“I’ll try.”
“No,” she says at once. “Not try. Tell me you will.”
I glance at her.
Her face is serious. Not harsh. Serious.
The kind of seriousness that has carried me through the darkest weeks of my life and refused to let me disappear into myself.
I look down at the book again.
“Okay,” I say. “I will.”
Her face brightens. “Thank you.”
I sit on the edge of the bed and place the Bible beside the book.
The two of them look strangely powerful on my poor table. A Bible. A book on purpose. Two quiet witnesses against the emptiness of the room.
“Lizzy.”
“I want to ask you a favour.”
She turns to me.
“Please don’t come to check on me when you return to school.”
Her face falls. The pain arrives before she can hide it.
“It is in our interest.”
“What does that mean?”
“They may find me through you.”
The room becomes still.
I look away from her eyes because her eyes are difficult to lie to.

“Otunba is ruthless,” I continue. “By now, he probably believes I have run away with his money. He may have given my description to the police. Or to worse people. And the Order…” I pause. “The Order does not forget.”
Lizzy says nothing.

“I have gotten myself into very deep entanglement with the Dukes. By God’s grace, I am beginning to overcome the thoughts that come from that covenant. But I do not want to expose myself to that fear again. And I do not want to expose you.”
“Are you driving me away?” she asks.
The question hurts.
“Then what are you doing?”
“Trying to keep you alive.”
She looks at me for a long moment. I cannot tell whether she is angry, wounded, or afraid. Maybe all three.
“I am grateful for everything you have done for me,” I say. “More grateful than I know how to say. But I don’t want anything that will endanger your life.”
She lowers herself slowly into the chair. The chair creaks beneath her. For a moment, neither of us speaks.
I pick up the book again, partly because I do not know what else to do with my hands. “So,” I say, forcing a small change in the air, “what is the book about?”
She looks at me. I can tell she knows I am changing the subject. But she allows it.
“It is self-explanatory.”
I turn the book over, studying the cover like a man trying to read the future from a title.
“You think my life still has meaning to God?”
Her eyes sharpen.
“I think you should stop asking that question as if God is the one confused about you.”
I look up.
She leans forward.
“You are not rubbish because you fell apart.”
The words strike quietly. Not loudly. Quietly. That makes them enter deeper.

“Every human being born into the earth receives the breath of life,” she says. “But breath is not all God gives. He forms each life with intention. Like clay in the hands of a potter, He shapes us with gifts, burdens, abilities, sensitivities, and desires that are meant to serve His purpose.”
I listen.
Outside, a goat bleats somewhere near the back of the compound. A child runs past the window. A woman calls someone’s name in Yoruba. Life continues in small ordinary sounds.

Lizzy continues. “None of us comes into life as an accident to God. Even our thumbprints are unique. That alone should tell you something. God does not mass-produce people. He designs. He assigns. He equips.”
“But I have failed Him many times, Liz.”
“Yes,” she says.
The honesty surprises me. She does not rush to comfort me falsely.

“Yes, you have failed Him. But failure does not have the power to erase what God put in you. Sin corrupts purpose. It does not create it. The deceiver takes what God designed and bends it toward darkness.”
I look at her. “What do you mean?”

“I mean your leadership did not come from the fraternity. Your courage did not come from the fraternity. Your ability to influence young people did not come from the fraternity. Those things were already in you. The fraternity only hijacked them.”
The sentence unsettles me. I have never thought of it that way.
“They gave you a wrong altar,” she says. “A wrong family. A wrong kingdom. But they did not create the gift. God did.”
I look down at my hands. Hands that once carried violence. Hands that now tremble over a book.
“So what happens now?”
“You return to Jesus,” she says. “You stay in the light. You let Him teach you what those gifts are really for.”
“And this book will help me discover that?”
“It can help you start asking the right questions.”
I run my fingers over the cover. Purpose.
The word feels too large for me. Too clean. Too far from cement dust, rented rooms, fear, police, cult oaths, and nightmares. Yet something in me wants to believe it.
“So,” I say quietly, “this book explains how a man can discover why he is here?”
Lizzy smiles. “Yes.”
I nod slowly. “I will read it.”
Her smile widens as if she has won a trophy. “Good.” Then she stands. “I should go. I still have to return to school.”
The room changes at once. The leaving returns. Everyone always leaves. But this time, I do not let bitterness speak first.
“Can I give you a hug?” I ask.
She studies me. Not suspiciously, exactly. Carefully. As if she is reading my motive, measuring the boundary, deciding whether tenderness can remain clean in a world that has made many things unsafe.

Then she steps closer. Her body language says it is okay. I rise. She gives me a brief, gentle hug. A platonic hug. No confusion. No demand. No hidden bargain. Just warmth. Just a human being telling another human being, I am still with you, even if I cannot stay.
My eyes close for a second. “Thank you, Lizzy,” I whisper. “Thanks for saving my life.”
She pulls back and looks at me. “Thank Jesus for giving you a new life,” she says. “And remain faithful to Him, Sammy.”
I nod. “I will try.”
Her eyes narrow slightly.
I correct myself.
“I will.”
She smiles again. Then she takes her bag and walks to the door. When she leaves, the room feels quiet in a different way. Not empty. Charged.

I sit back on the bed and look at the Bible and the book on the table. The room is still poor. The future is still uncertain. The Order is still out there. Otunba still believes I owe him. Fear still knows my name.
But on my table sits a Bible and a book about purpose. Two small objects. Two quiet beginnings. I pick up the book, In Pursuit of Purpose and open the first page.
For the first time in many days, I am not merely trying to survive the night. I am trying to understand why I am still alive.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-ONE
content: I started reading the book, In Pursuit of Purpose that night as soon as Lizzy left me. At first, I read it the way a sick man tastes medicine he does not trust.

A page today. Two pages tomorrow. Sometimes I read a paragraph and close the book because the words enter places I am not ready to expose. It is not the difficulty of the language that slows me down. It is the difficulty of recognition.
The book keeps asking questions I have spent years avoiding. Who am I? Why am I here? What did God place inside me? What has life corrupted that purpose can still redeem?

I have always thought of purpose as something reserved for men with clean histories, men who grow up in stable homes, men whose names are not whispered by enemies, men whose hands have not touched darkness. Purpose sounds too noble for people like me.

But the more I read, the more I begin to understand something that unsettles me. Purpose is not cancelled because a man has fallen. It can be buried. Corrupted. Misused. Redirected. But not erased. That thought stays with me.
For days.
Perhaps because it accuses me. Perhaps because it comforts me.

I began reading the Bible Lizzy gave me with a different hunger. Not the desperate reading of a man looking for a verse to survive the night, but the searching of a man trying to understand why he survives at all.
One evening, while reading the opening words of Jeremiah, something takes hold of me. Before I formed you in the womb, I knew you. Before you were born, I set you apart.
I read the passage again. Then again.
For a long time, I sit with the Bible open on my lap, unable to move. Before shame gave me a name, God knew me.
I do not fully understand it, but for the first time in many years, the thought that my life may exist in God’s mind before it became damaged in men’s hands gives me a strange, trembling hope.
If God knew me before darkness named me, then perhaps darkness does not have the final right to define me.
I pace the small room, the book in one hand and the Bible on the table, trying to identify what remains of me that can still be useful.
I have no certificate worth presenting. No completed degree. No professional training. No clean résumé. No father to introduce me. No uncle to call. No powerful man to recommend me.
But I have experience.
Not the kind anyone would put proudly on an application form. Still, experience. I have led young men.

I have watched boys tremble during initiation and learned how to steady them. I have been assigned as Guardian Angel to new disciples, not merely to protect them, but to shape them. To teach them the codes. To explain hierarchy. To discipline them. To monitor their fears. To read their weaknesses. To know when a boy needs pressure, praise, correction, or warning.

In the fraternity, I used those skills to draw young men deeper into darkness. That truth hurts. But another truth stands beside it. The skill itself is not evil. Leadership is not evil. Influence is not evil. Understanding young people is not evil. The corruption is in the direction.
A knife in the hand of a murderer kills. A knife in the hand of a surgeon can save.

Perhaps my life is not empty of gifts. Perhaps my gifts, in Lizzy’s words, have simply served the wrong altar. I think of the boys I have known in the fraternity. Legion. Restless, proud, hungry for recognition. Steel. Angry, wounded, desperate to possess what he loves. Ugo. Brilliant, dangerous, broken by ambition. Even Bitty. Gifted, guilty, divided between escape and responsibility. And myself. We are all young once. Before the nicknames. Before the oaths. Before violence convinces us that fear is respect.
What if someone had reached us earlier? What if someone had seen the hunger beneath the arrogance? What if someone had interrupted the road before we become useful to darkness?
The answer comes quietly.
Teach. At first, I dismiss it.
Me? A teacher?
The idea almost makes me laugh.
I once mocked teachers. I once thought classroom work belongs to men who cannot survive elsewhere. But Lizzy teaches me without ever standing before a blackboard.
Perhaps teaching is not merely a profession. Perhaps it is rescue in slow motion.
The next morning, I write my first application letter.

It is poorly arranged. Too emotional. Too honest in some places and too vague in others. I write that I am willing to teach Biology, Basic Science, or any subject within my ability. I write that I have passion for mentoring young people. I write that I can volunteer first if they are not ready to employ me.
Then I copy it several times.

I walk through the area submitting applications to schools. Private secondary schools. Tutorial centres. Lesson houses. Small post-secondary institutes preparing students for WAEC, JAMB, and remedial examinations.
Some proprietors dismiss me immediately. “Where is your certificate?”
“I have not completed my degree.”
“Teaching experience?”
“I have worked with young people.”
“What does that mean?”
I hesitate.
Because how does a man explain Guardian Angel work in a fraternity without condemning himself?
Others collect the letter politely and tell me they will get back to me, which I quickly learn is a professional way of saying goodbye without cruelty.
Still, I continue. Every rejection humbles me. But it does not stop the stirring inside me.

While returning from another unpromising school visit, I sight a banner tied to the fence of a community hall. DEREKH YOUTH DEVELOPMENT PROGRAMME… Discover Your Potential. Understand Your Path. Build Your Future.
Something about the word path holds my attention. Derekh. It sounds ancient. Directional. Like road. Like way.
I enter the hall out of curiosity.

About twenty young people sit inside, mostly secondary school leavers and undergraduates on break. A woman in her thirties stands before them, speaking with controlled enthusiasm. She’s dressed in a simple jacket over a patterned skirt and moves with the confidence of someone used to holding the attention of restless minds.
On the board behind her are written three questions:
What can you do naturally?
What problem troubles you deeply?
Who benefits when you become better?
I stand at the back. I intend to leave after a few minutes. I do not.

The session is about identifying potential. The anchor explains that many young people chase courses, jobs, or lifestyles without first understanding their natural abilities, emotional burdens, personality patterns, and spiritual convictions. She speaks about gifts, temperament, interests, discipline, values, and service.
It sounds familiar. Not because I have heard it in church. Because I have seen the opposite in the fraternity.
Young men with leadership potential become gang leaders. Bold boys become violent. Intelligent boys become manipulators. Loyal boys become instruments. Creative boys become forgers of wicked plans.
Everything she says is what should have happened to us before the Order found us. At the end of the session, she distributes assessment sheets.
I almost walk away. Then she sees me.
“You at the back,” she calls. “You can take one too.”
Some of the students turn. I feel exposed.
“I’m only observing,” I say.
“Observation is the first stage of discovery.”
A few people laugh.
I accept the sheet.
The questions are simple, but they disturb me.

What activities make you feel most alive? When do people naturally seek your help? What kind of injustice angers you most? What age group do you connect with easily? What problem do you feel called to solve?
I stare at the last question for a long time. What problem do you feel called to solve?
Young people being captured by systems that should have protected them. I do not write it immediately. The answer frightens me.
Because once a man names a burden, he becomes responsible to it. After the programme, I wait behind. The anchor is packing her materials when I approach.
She looks up.
“My name is Samuel.”
She smiles.
“I noticed you.”
I feel uneasy. “That is not always good.”
She laughs. “In this case, it is.”
I hold the assessment sheet awkwardly.
“I want to work with young people.”
“Are you a teacher?”
“Not officially.”
“What are you officially?”
The question corners me. I look away. “I am trying to become useful.”
Her expression changes. Not pity. Attention.
“Sit down, Samuel.”
I sit.

For some reason, I tell her more than I intended. Not everything. Not the names. Not the fraternity details. But enough. I tell her I have worked with young men in a structured group, that I understand peer pressure, fear, identity crisis, and the need for guidance. I tell her many young people are not evil; they are merely unguided, angry, vulnerable, and available to anyone who gives them belonging.
She listens without interrupting.
When I finish, she asks, “Why do you want to teach?”
I have rehearsed many answers.
None comes. So, I tell the truth. “Because I know what happens when nobody reaches boys early enough.”
Her eyes soften.
That answer seems to satisfy her more than a certificate would have.
“What subject can you teach?”
“Biology. Basic Science. Some general studies. But more than that, I can mentor.”
“Mentorship is not a small thing.”
“I know.”
She studies me. “You sound like someone running from something.”
I look down.
“Maybe.”
“And also running toward something.”
That makes me look up.
She smiles.
“There is a post-secondary tutorial institute not far from here. They prepare students for WAEC, NECO, JAMB, and remedial exams. They are always looking for people who can handle difficult students.”
I almost laugh.
Difficult students. Perhaps that is the only qualification life has given me in abundance.
“I can write a recommendation,” she continues. “Not an employment guarantee. Just a recommendation. You will still have to prove yourself.”
“I understand.”
She pulls a sheet of letterhead from her folder.
As she writes, I feel something unfamiliar rising inside me. Not excitement. Not yet. Something steadier. Possibility.

For months, my life has been about escape. Escape from the Order. Escape from Otunba. Escape from hunger. Escape from shame. Escape from myself. But now, for the first time, I sense a movement that is not escape. Assignment.
The word frightens me. But it also gives shape to the air around me.
When she finishes, she folds the letter and hands it to me.
“Samuel,” she says, “young people do not only need people who have never fallen. Sometimes they need people who know where the pits are.”
I take the letter carefully. As though it might break.
“Thank you, ma.”
“One more thing.”
“Do not use your past to impress them.”
I frown.
“Use it to understand them.”
That sentence follows me out of the hall.

The sun is lowering over Ogbomosho, casting long shadows across the road. Motorcycles pass. Traders call to customers. Schoolchildren move in noisy groups, dragging their bags and laughing as though the world has not yet begun asking them hard questions.

I hold the recommendation letter in my hand. A small paper. Nothing extraordinary. Yet it feels heavier than many weapons I have carried. Because this one does not ask me to destroy. It asks me to serve.
As I walk toward the institute, I remember the title of the book Lizzy gives me, In Pursuit of Purpose. For the first time, the phrase does not sound like a slogan. It sounds like a road.
And perhaps, after all my wandering, I have finally found the beginning of mine.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-TWO
content: Destiny Educational Institute is housed in a tired one-storey building at the edge of a busy road in Ogbomosho. From outside, it does not look like the kind of place where futures are rebuilt. The paint on the walls has faded under years of sun and rain. The signboard near the gate leans slightly to one side, announcing in bold letters: WAEC, NECO, GCE & UME PREPARATORY CLASSES.
Beneath the inscription are promises every struggling student wants to believe. Excellent Tutors. Guaranteed Success. Moral Discipline. Academic Excellence.
I stand before the building on my first proper teaching day and stare at those words longer than necessary. The phrases sound noble.

But inside the compound, a boy is chasing another boy with a broken ruler. Two girls are arguing near the staircase. Someone upstairs is beating a desk like a drum. From one classroom comes a burst of laughter so loud that even the receptionist shakes her head without looking up.
“That is your class,” she says.
I follow the noise.
The walls are marked with old posters advertising past examination registration deadlines. Some have begun peeling away, curling at the edges like tired tongues.
As I approach the classroom, the noise becomes clearer. Laughter. Clapping. Desk-thumping. Then a boy’s voice rises dramatically above the rest.
“Please, where can I do my balancing and alignment?”
The class erupts again.
Another student, pretending to be a mechanic, answers, “Where is your car, sir?”
The first voice replies, “It is this my Legsus foot-o-four!”
More laughter.
I pause at the door. Legsus. It takes me a second. Then I understand… my shoes.
I look down at them.

The leather is cracked. The soles have begun to lean outward. The right shoe opens slightly near the side when I walk. Lizzy had bought me clothes, but I had insisted on managing the shoes for a while. Apparently, the shoes have become public property.
I step quietly into the classroom. Nobody notices at first.

The class is a room of about twenty students, though it behaves like sixty. Desks are arranged in uneven rows. Some students sit on the desks instead of the chairs. A few lean against the wall. Two boys at the back are sharing groundnuts from a folded piece of newspaper. One girl is filing her nails with the seriousness of a surgeon.

At the blackboard, Tunde stands with chalk in hand. He is tall, lean, restless-eyed, and dangerously confident. He has drawn a cartoon version of me on the board: long legs, oversized head, one hand holding a Biology textbook, and exaggerated shoes pointing in opposite directions like the front tyres of a damaged vehicle.
Under the drawing he has written: MR. LEGSUS 404 — NEEDS ALIGNMENT
The class is enjoying itself. A girl near the window sees me first. Her mouth opens.
“Tunde,” she whispers.
He continues performing.
“Customer, if we do this alignment, e go cost you extra because this one no be ordinary leg. Na imported problem.”
“Tunde!” another student hisses louder. “Teacher!”
The laughter dies in layers. First the front row. Then the middle. Then the back. Finally, Tunde turns.
His face freezes.
For a moment, his hand remains lifted in the air, still holding the chalk like a weapon caught at the crime scene.
I walk slowly toward the board.
The class becomes so quiet that I hear the ceiling fan squeak above us.
Tunde quickly starts wiping the drawing with his palm.
“No,” I say.
He stops.
“Leave it.”
He turns slowly.
“Sir, good afternoon.”
“Good afternoon, artist.”
A few students cover their mouths. Tunde lowers his head.
I stand beside the drawing and study it. “The forehead is too big,” I say.
The class stays silent, unsure whether laughter is permitted.
“And the shoes…”
I look down at my own feet. Then back at the board.
“The shoes are accurate.”
The first laugh escapes from somewhere in the back. Then another. Soon the whole class relaxes into laughter, but this time it is different. Less cruel. More surprised.
I turn to them.
“You know, on campus when students do this kind of thing, the lecturer will impose a levy on the whole class.”
A boy at the back groans. “Ah, sir!”
“So that the lecturer can not only align his Legsus…”
The students begin laughing again.
“But possibly buy a Toyota Land Cruiser.”
This time the class explodes. Even Tunde laughs, though carefully.
I wait until the laughter fades.
“But I will not impose a levy on you.”
A chorus of relief follows.
“Thank you, sir!”
“God bless you, sir!”
“Sir, you are a correct man!”
I raise my hand.
“However…”
The class quiets immediately.
“I will impose a sanction.”
Tunde’s face drops. I walk closer to him.

“Since you have demonstrated extraordinary artistic ability and a dangerous attachment to the blackboard, your punishment is that you will clean this board properly every morning before class for one week.”
The class reacts loudly.
“Good for you!”
“Tunde, blackboard prefect!”
Tunde lifts both hands. “Sir, please, one week is too much. I was only using my talent.”
“Talent without discipline becomes public nuisance,” I reply.
The class murmurs. Some laugh. Some nod.
Tunde looks at me, surprised.
The sentence has reached him more deeply than the punishment.
I turn away from him and face the students fully.
“And since this class has proven that the blackboard is in danger of becoming a television screen for comedy, we need order.”
A boy from the back calls out, “Sir, we are orderly!”
Immediately, someone throws a piece of paper at him.
I stare at him.
He grins.
“Mostly orderly, sir.”
Another student says, “Sir, this class is peaceful. Only Tunde is the problem.”
Tunde turns sharply. “Shut up, Dele. You that copied my joke yesterday.”
“Your joke no get copyright!”
The class begins laughing again.
I tap the chalk on the desk.
“Enough.”
To my surprise, they quiet down. Not fully. But enough.
I begin walking slowly between the rows.
“You think I am here to teach Biology only.”
A girl in the second row says, “That is what they employed you for, sir.”
“What is your name?”
“Bisi.”
“Bisi, you are correct. But you are not complete.”
The class reacts.
“Ah, sir!”
“She is correct but not complete!”
I point at the board.
“Biology teaches life. If I teach life without helping you understand how to live, then I am only preparing you to pass exams and fail yourselves.”
The room changes slightly. Not dramatically.
But I feel attention gather.

“You are here for WAEC, NECO, GCE, UME, and whatever other examination life has arranged for young people in this country. Some of you are here because you failed once. Some because your parents forced you. Some because university admission has delayed. Some because you are not sure what else to do. Some because this is the last chance your family can afford.”
A few faces lose their amusement. Good. Truth has entered the room.
“But whether you like it or not, this place is not a prison yard. It is not an asylum. It is not a comedy club.”
I glance at Tunde.
“It is a place of learning.”
“Yes, sir,” a few students mutter.
“And learning requires structure.”
Groans begin immediately.
“Sir, please!”
“Sir, structure is stress!”
“Sir, we are young!”
“Young people die too,” I say.
The class goes still. I did not plan to say it. But the words come out of my history before I can soften them. I look around the room.

“I have seen young people waste gifts because nobody corrected them early. I have seen young people become useful to people who did not love them. I have seen brilliant boys become dangerous men because discipline arrived too late.”
Nobody laughs now. Tunde looks at me differently. So does the girl filing her nails.
I return to the front.
“So, to that effect, I am appointing a class prefect.”
The class groans again.
“Sir, we don pass secondary school!”
“Sir, this is tutorial centre!”
“Sir, we are adults!”
“You cannot be adults only when freedom is shared,” I say. “You must also be adults when responsibility is distributed.”
That silences a few of them. I look across the room. My eyes settle on a girl seated near the window.

She is not the most serious-looking student. In fact, she has spent most of the period staring outside as if the trees are teaching a more interesting subject. Her uniform is not properly arranged. Her notebook is closed. Her expression suggests she expects nothing good from school or from me. Exactly.
“Miss Eno Okon.”
She startles.
Some students turn to her.
Her eyes widen.
“Please stand up.”
She remains seated.
“Yes, you.”
The class begins murmuring.
“Ah, sir!”
“Wrong choice!”
Eno slowly stands, suspicion written all over her face.
I look at her.
“She will be your class prefect.”
Chaos erupts.
“No, sir!”
“We no go gree!”
“She is a truant!”
“Sir, she comes late every day!”
“Sir, she no dey even bring pen!”
“Sir, appoint Bisi!”
“Appoint me, sir!”
“Anybody but Eno!”
Tunde raises his hand.
“Sir, with due respect, if Eno is prefect, this class has entered voicemail.”
The room bursts into laughter. Eno’s face hardens. She sits down abruptly.
“I don’t want it,” she says.

The class roars louder. I raise my hand, but nobody responds. So I do not shout. I stand still. Silence is sometimes louder than anger. Gradually, they notice. One by one, the voices drop. When the class becomes quiet enough, I look at Eno.
“Stand up again.”
She hesitates. Then stands.
“Why don’t you want it?”
She folds her arms. “Because they will insult me.”
“They are already insulting you.”
A few students laugh.
“Why else?”
She looks away. “I am not serious.”
The honesty surprises the class. It surprises me too.
“Who told you that?”
She shrugs. “Everybody knows.”
“Everybody can be wrong.”
She looks at me sharply. The room becomes quiet.
I continue. “A prefect is not chosen because she is perfect.”
Tunde whispers loudly, “That is why it is called prefect, not perfect.”
The class laughs again.
I turn to him. “Tunde, do you want two weeks of board cleaning?”
He seals his mouth immediately.
I face Eno again.
“A prefect is chosen because responsibility can awaken what laziness has buried.”
She says nothing. “I am not appointing you because you are the best student.”
Bisi murmurs, “Thank God sir knows.”
The class snickers. I ignore it.
“I am appointing you because I want to know what will happen when someone finally expects better from you.”
Eno’s face changes. Only slightly. But I see it.
There are sentences young people hear so rarely that when they arrive, they do not know where to keep them. Expectation can be frightening to someone accustomed to disappointment.
“You will help maintain order before teachers arrive. You will keep attendance. You will make sure this board is not used for unauthorized artistic attacks on staff footwear.”
The class laughs softly.
“You will also report students who disturb serious learning.”
A boy at the back raises his hand.
“Sir, what if she is the one disturbing?”
“Then she will report herself.”
The class laughs again. Eno fights a smile.
I point at Tunde.
“Tunde.”
“You will assist her.”
His eyes widen.
“Sir, as assistant prefect?”
“As assistant blackboard offender.”
The class erupts.
“That title is too long!” Tunde bows dramatically. “I accept my calling.”
I shake my head despite myself.
“You are intelligent,” I tell him.
The laughter fades.
He looks up.
“But if you continue using intelligence only to avoid seriousness, you will become entertainment for people who are moving forward.”
The words land. His smile weakens.
“Yes, sir.”
I turn to the whole class.

“All of you listen to me carefully. This place may not look like much. These desks may be old. The fan may sound like it is about to confess its sins. Some of you may think you are here because life delayed you.”
A few students smile.
“But delay is not denial unless you turn it into waste.”
I pick up a piece of chalk.
“From today, I will teach you Biology. But I will also disturb your ignorance, your laziness, your self-pity, and your excuses.”
A student mutters, “This teacher go stress us.”
“I heard that.”
The class laughs.
“Yes,” I continue. “I will stress you. But not to destroy you. To stretch you.”
I write on the board: BIOLOGY — THE STUDY OF LIFE. Then beneath it, I write: WHAT KIND OF LIFE ARE YOU PREPARING FOR?
The room becomes unusually quiet.
I turn to them. “That is your first note.”
A girl raises her hand. “Sir, is it Biology note or life note?”
I smile.
“Both.”
For the first time, nobody laughs.
They open their books. Slowly. Reluctantly. But they open them.

As chalk dust gathers on my fingers, I look at the faces before me – Tunde with his restless intelligence, Eno with her hidden uncertainty, Bisi with her sharp tongue, Dele pretending not to care, and many others whose names I do not yet know.

Unlike my old school days, many of these children do not seem to understand why they are here. Some have been bruised by failure. Some are hiding behind humour. Some behind rebellion. Some behind indifference. They may not yet appreciate the inestimable value of education.

But as I stand before them, I begin to understand the enormous responsibility placed in my hands. Not merely to help them pass examinations. But to help them recover hope. To teach them that discipline is not punishment. That intelligence is not mockery. That delay is not uselessness. That their lives can still become something beautiful if they learn to take themselves seriously before the world teaches them regret.

I look at the blackboard once more. The ghost of Tunde’s cartoon has not fully disappeared. I can still see faint chalk lines where my shoes had been exaggerated. For some reason, I am grateful for it.

Mockery brought me into their world faster than authority would have. And perhaps that is where teaching begins. Not where students respect you immediately. But where you decide to love them before they know how.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-ONE
content: The classroom is already noisy when I arrive. Not rebellious today. Just alive. That is one thing I am beginning to understand about young people. Noise is not always disrespect. Sometimes it is unused energy looking for direction. Sometimes it is fear wearing laughter. Sometimes it is intelligence trying to escape boredom.

The room is filled mostly with boys between seventeen and nineteen, with a few girls seated together near the windows. The girls are usually more organized, or at least better at pretending to be. Genevieve sits with her notebook open before the lesson begins. Oluchi has two pens tucked into her hair. Bisi leans sideways, whispering something to Eno, who has her arms folded across her stomach, her face quieter than usual.

At the back, Tunde is already performing for his audience. Dele and Chike are laughing at something he has said. Ephraim sits near the middle row, serious-faced, the kind of student who always wants to answer but rarely says enough. Nosa drums softly on the desk until I look at him.
He stops. Then smiles.
“Good afternoon, sir.”
The class rises lazily.
“Good afternoon, sir.”
“Sit down.”
They fall back into their seats with the usual dragging of chairs and scraping of benches.
I write on the board: ASEXUAL REPRODUCTION. Then I turn to face them. “Our topic today is asexual reproduction.”
A few boys exchange glances.
I raise my hand before they begin. “Before somebody decides to manufacture comedy from the word, let us first define it properly.”
The class laughs.
Tunde raises both hands in innocence. “Sir, I did not say anything.”
More laughter.
I lean against the table and look around the class.
“Now, who can explain the term asexual reproduction?”
Ephraim raises his hand immediately.
“Yes, Ephraim.”
He stands, clears his throat, and adjusts his shirt like a student addressing a panel. “Asexual reproduction is the opposite of sexual reproduction.”
The class chuckles.
I smile and shake my head.
“Ephraim, that is not wrong, but it is also not useful. If I ask you what light is, and you tell me it is the opposite of darkness, you have only escaped the question beautifully.”
The students laugh louder.
Ephraim smiles, embarrassed but not wounded.
“Try again.”
He thinks for a moment.
“It is reproduction without sex?”
“Better. Sit down.”
Genevieve raises her hand.
“Yes, Genevieve.”
She stands calmly.
“Asexual reproduction is a type of reproduction that does not involve the fusion of male and female gametes. It usually involves one parent producing offspring that are genetically similar to it.”
I pause.
The boys look unimpressed by the confidence of her answer, which tells me they are impressed.
“That is excellent,” I say. “Very excellent. Please sit.”
Genevieve sits, hiding a smile.
“It seems the girls are doing better in this subject.”
A voice from the back says, “Before nko? No be dem sabi pass?”
The class erupts.
I look toward the back.
Chike bends his head quickly, pretending to search for a pen.
I let the laughter pass.
There are some battles a teacher must not dignify too early.
“Before we go further,” I continue, “I want to be sure we understood what we studied last week on sexual reproduction.”
Tunde raises his hand.
I stare at him.
He smiles. That smile has warning written all over it.
“Yes, Tunde.”
He stands. “Sir, I have one question.”
“Is there going to be practical?”
The room explodes.
Dele bangs the desk.
Nosa shouts, “Scientific practical!”
Oluchi covers her face. Genevieve rolls her eyes. I do not smile.
“Tunde.”
“Yes, sir.”
“Practical on what?”
He pretends to be innocent. “On the topic we did last week, sir.”
“Which topic?”
The class begins laughing again.
He finally says it. “Sexual reproduction, sir.”
This time the whole room breaks loose.
Even some of the girls laugh despite themselves.
I wait.

In my former life, I would have humiliated him instantly. I would have cut him down with words sharp enough to make others afraid of trying me again. The old Sammy believed discipline meant domination. If a boy stepped out of line, you crushed him quickly and publicly so that others would learn fear. But I am no longer that man. Or I am trying not to be.
“Tunde,” I say quietly.
The laughter dies faster than shouting would have achieved.
He straightens.
“I have not asked you to sit down.”
He remains standing.
“Have you ever heard the phrase age of consent?”
His mischievous smile weakens.
“Yes, sir.”
“Tell the class what it means.”
He glances around. His audience is suddenly less useful.
“It is the age at which someone is considered by law old enough to agree to a sexual relationship.”
“Good.”
I walk slowly between the rows.
“Have you attained that age?”
He hesitates. “I think so, sir.”
“Yes, you have.”
The boys begin to relax, thinking he has won. “But that is not what worries me.”
The room quiets.
“What worries me is that many young people think being old enough to consent means being mature enough to carry consequences.”
Tunde looks down.

I continue, but my voice remains calm. “You may be old enough for the law to consider your consent. But are you ready for pregnancy? Fatherhood? Emotional responsibility? Financial responsibility? Disease? Shame? Confusion? A child who did not ask to be born into irresponsibility?”
The class is silent now.
I look at Tunde.
“You asked a joke. I am giving you the serious answer behind the joke.”
His face changes.
“Yes, sir.”
“You may sit.”
He sits quietly.
I return to the front.
“Any sincere question?”
Genevieve raises her hand.
“Yes, Genevieve.”
She stands.
“Sir, last week you explained that during human reproduction, the sperm fertilizes the egg and an embryo begins to form.”
“That’s correct.”
“My question is, can a man and woman have intercourse and fertilization will not take place?”
Before I can answer, Chike blurts out from the back.
“Use condom!”
Some students laugh reflexively.
But the laughter dies when they see my face.
I turn slowly toward Chike.
He tries to smile.
It does not last.
“Chike, stand up.”
He stands, still wearing half a grin.
“Do you understand Genevieve’s question?”
He shrugs.
“Yes, sir.”
“No, you don’t. If you understood it, you would know she asked a serious biological question.”
His grin disappears.
“The problem is not that you know about condoms. At your age, you will hear many things. The problem is that you do not yet know how to respect a serious conversation.”
The classroom becomes very still. I look around.

“Listen to me, all of you. Biology is not vulgarity. Reproduction is not a dirty topic. Your body is not a joke. A classroom is one of the few places where you can ask questions and receive truth without shame. But if you turn every serious matter into mockery, you will remain ignorant in areas where ignorance can damage your life.”
Chike lowers his eyes.
In my former life, I would have thrown him out and enjoyed the fear it produced. But I see something in his face. Not rebellion alone. Embarrassment.
A boy trying to look bold because he does not want anyone to know he is still unsure of himself.
“Chike.”
“Yes, sir.”
“You will apologize to Genevieve.”
He looks up sharply. “Sir?”
“You interrupted her question disrespectfully. Apologize.”
The boys murmur.
“Sir, I was just joking.”
“I know. Apologize.”
For a moment I think he will refuse.
Then he turns reluctantly toward Genevieve. “Sorry.”
“Properly,” I say.
He exhales.
“I’m sorry, Genevieve.”
Genevieve nods without looking at him.
“Sit down.”
He sits. I let the silence breathe.
“Good. Now we can learn.”
I turn back to Genevieve.

“Your question is important. Fertilization does not always occur simply because intercourse has taken place. In women who have reached puberty, the body goes through what is called the menstrual cycle. It is usually about twenty-eight days, though it can vary from person to person.”
I draw a simple diagram on the board.

“An egg is released from the ovary. It travels through the oviduct. If sperm is present and the egg is mature, fertilization may occur. If fertilization does not occur, the unfertilized egg and the thickened lining of the womb break down and are discharged from the body during menstruation.”
The class listens. Really listens. Perhaps because the laughter has been disciplined, not destroyed.
I continue. “This is why understanding your body matters. It helps you make wise decisions. It helps you respect yourself. It helps you respect others.”
I glance at the boys.
“Especially you boys. Many boys joke about things they do not understand because society allows them to be careless. But biology will not excuse carelessness because you are male.”
Some of the girls murmur approval. Tunde raises his hand slowly.
I look at him.
“Sir, so you mean responsibility is part of Biology?”
A few students laugh softly. But the question is not foolish.
“Yes,” I say. “Because Biology is the study of life. And life without responsibility becomes suffering.”
He nods thoughtfully. I am about to continue when I notice Eno shifting in her seat.

At first I think she is simply uncomfortable with the topic. Her arms are pressed around her stomach. Sweat gathers on her forehead. Oluchi, seated beside her, leans closer and whispers something. Eno shakes her head.
Then her face twists. “Aaagh…”
The sound is low at first. I stop writing.
She grips the edge of the desk. “Aaagh… mmm…”
Oluchi catches her arm.
Eno bends forward, her body folding into pain.
The class rises in confusion.
“What happened?”
“Give her space!”
“Water! Bring water!”
“Don’t pour water on her,” I say sharply. “Everybody sit down except Oluchi and Genevieve.”
Nobody sits. Fear has entered them.
I move quickly to Eno’s desk. The old Sammy knew how to respond to danger with force. This moment requires something harder. Tenderness.
I kneel beside her. “Eno, can you hear me?”
She nods weakly, breathing fast.
“Where is the pain?”
She points to her abdomen, unable to speak clearly.
Oluchi’s eyes are wide with panic.
“Sir, she was complaining since morning, but she said it would go.”
I look at Genevieve.
“Go to the office. Tell them we need transport to the hospital now.”
Genevieve runs.
I turn to the class.
“Tunde.”
He stands immediately. “Yes, sir.”
“Clear the way. Keep everyone calm.”
For once, he does not joke.
“Yes, sir.”
“Chike.”
He rises slowly. “Sir?”
“Go and get her bag. Move quickly.”
He grabs Eno’s bag.
I look back at her. “You are going to be alright. Just breathe slowly.”
She winces. “I can’t…”
“You can. Look at me.”
Her eyes meet mine.
For one brief moment, I see not a truant, not a difficult student, not the girl the class mocked as unfit for leadership. I see a frightened young person in pain.
My voice softens. “You are not alone, Eno. We are here.”
Her fingers grip my sleeve.

And something inside me breaks open quietly. This is why I am here. Not to dominate. Not to frighten. Not to command young people into obedience as I once did in darkness. But to stand close when fear enters their bodies and tell them they are not alone.
The hospital building is painted pale green, though dust has softened the colour into something tired.

We arrive with Eno supported by Oluchi and Genevieve, while Tunde and Chike follow behind carrying her bag and my books. A receptionist asks questions too slowly for my patience, but I force my voice to remain calm. Anger will not help Eno. Fear will not help her either.
Soon she is taken inside. The rest of us wait in the lounge.

A woman with a baby sits near the door. An elderly man coughs into a handkerchief. Somewhere beyond the corridor, a nurse calls a patient’s name. Genevieve sits with her hands folded beneath her chin. Oluchi keeps shaking one leg anxiously.
Tunde stands near the wall, unusually quiet. Chike sits apart from the others, staring at the floor. I look at them.
For all their noise in class, they are still children in many ways. Young people pretending to be older than fear, until fear brings them to a hospital waiting room.
Tunde finally speaks. “Sir.”
“Will she be okay?”
“I hope so.”
He nods. Then after a pause, he says, “She always acts like she doesn’t care.”
I look at him. “Maybe it is easier than showing she does.”
No one replies.
Chike clears his throat. “Sir.”
I turn to him.
“I’m sorry for what I said in class.”
The apology surprises me. “You already apologized to Genevieve.”
“I know. But I mean… generally.” His eyes remain on the floor. “I didn’t know the class would turn like this.”
“You were not responsible for Eno’s pain.”
