“I know.” He hesitates. “But sometimes we joke too much.”
There is something sincere in his voice. I sit beside him.
“Humour is not the problem, Chike. Humour can help people survive. But when laughter becomes a way to avoid maturity, it begins to harm you.”
He nods slowly. “I understand, sir.”
The door opens before I can respond. Doctor Oladeji steps out with a nurse behind him.
He is a lean man with tired eyes, the kind of doctor whose face has learned to hide conclusions until he is ready to speak them.
“Who is responsible for the girl?”
I stand immediately. “I am.”
He looks at me. “You are her brother?”
“No. Her teacher.”
Something flickers across his face. Surprise perhaps. Or approval.
“Please, come with me.”
I follow him down the corridor. Behind me, the students remain silent. For once, no jokes follow me. No laughter. No noise. Only concern.
And as I walk behind the doctor, I realize that a classroom can change in one afternoon. Not because a teacher shouted. But because compassion entered and stayed.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-FOUR
content: Doctor Oladeji’s office is smaller than I expected. His desk occupies most of the room. On it are stacked patient files, a blood pressure monitor, a stethoscope, two pens in a cracked plastic holder, a half-empty bottle of hand sanitizer, and a desktop calendar turned to the wrong month.

Behind him, a metal cabinet leans slightly against the wall, its glass doors revealing folders arranged with more urgency than order. A framed certificate hangs above it. Beside the window, an examination couch is covered with a faded blue sheet. The room is functional, not comfortable; the kind of office where too many serious conversations have taken place under fluorescent light.
The doctor sits briskly and points to the chair before his desk. “Please, sit down.”
I do.
He leans forward and offers his hand. “I am Dr. Oladeji. Lloyd Oladeji.”
“Sammy Briggs,” I reply, taking his hand. “Nice to meet you, sir.”
“Delighted to meet you, Mr. Briggs.”
He pauses, studying me briefly.
“May I call you Briggs?”
“I actually prefer Sammy.”
He nods. “Alright, Sammy.” He opens the file before him. “How are you related to the girl?”
“She is one of my students at Destiny Institute of Preliminary Studies.”
“I see.” He writes something.
“And are you taking responsibility?”
“The institute will, doctor.”
He looks up from the file. “No, Sammy. I mean personally.”
I frown.
“Personally?”
His expression becomes more direct. “Are you responsible for the pregnancy?”
The word strikes me so suddenly that for a moment I only stare at him.
“Pregnancy?” My voice sounds foolish in my own ears. “She is pregnant?”
Doctor Oladeji watches my reaction carefully.

I realize then that his question was not accusation alone. It was assessment. He has probably seen enough frightened men deny responsibility with rehearsed outrage. But my shock is not rehearsed. It is genuine.
He leans back slightly. “I am not surprised you do not know. She is about four months gone. From what we can tell, she has been concealing it for some time.”
I feel the room tilt quietly. Eno. Pregnant.
The girl who sat near the window pretending not to care about anything. The truant. The newly appointed class prefect. The child the class had mocked that morning. Four months.
I struggle to place the information beside the Eno I know, but it refuses to fit.
Doctor Oladeji pulls out a sheet and hands it to me.

I take it, pretending to understand what I am reading. The words blur. My mind has already left the paper. It is back in the classroom. Eno folding her arms over her stomach. Eno shifting uncomfortably. Oluchi whispering beside her. Her sudden cry. Her fingers gripping my sleeve. You are not alone, Eno. We are here.
The doctor’s voice brings me back. “We have run preliminary tests. There are complications.”
I look up. “What complications?”
His face tightens. “It appears she has taken substances in an attempt to terminate the pregnancy. From her symptoms and what she reluctantly admitted to the nurse, she has used them more than once.”
The paper trembles slightly in my hand. I lower it to my lap.
“Doctor…” I swallow. “Is she going to be alright?”
Doctor Oladeji does not answer quickly.
That frightens me.
“Her condition is critical,” he says at last. “But she came in early enough for us to try. We will do everything medically necessary to stabilize her. But we need cooperation.”
“Whatever you need,” I say quickly. “I just want her alive.”
He nods, but his tone remains professional.

“Emergency care has already started. We will not abandon a patient in distress. But because she is young, pregnant, and may require further intervention, we need a responsible guardian or relative. We also need financial undertaking. If the institute is willing to take responsibility, I need that in writing from an authorized person.”
“I can get that.”
“Good.”
He folds his hands.
“But I still need to know who her parents or guardians are.”
I hesitate.
“I don’t know all the details.”
The doctor’s eyes sharpen. “Then you must find out.”
“She is an orphan,” I say. The words come out more sharply than I intend.
Doctor Oladeji stops. Something in his expression changes.
“An orphan?”
I nod.

“At least that is what I know. She lives with foster parents or guardians. I don’t know how they treat her. I don’t know what she has told them. But if she has been hiding this for four months and taking dangerous substances, then she is afraid of someone.”
The room becomes quiet.
The doctor leans back, his right hand resting against his chin.
I hear myself continue, though my voice has softened.

“Please understand, doctor. I am not asking you to ignore procedure. I know there must be rules. But this is an unexpected pregnancy. She is young. She is scared. And if the people she lives with reject her, she may do worse to herself next time.” The words catch in my throat.

Because I know what it means to flee a house before you are thrown out. I know what fear can make a young person do when shame stands at the door wearing the face of family. For a moment, I am no longer in Doctor Oladeji’s office. I am back in my foster parents’ house. The accusation. The voices. The confusion. My foster mother saying, “Let him speak.” My foster father’s face pale with pain. And me, hearing none of their love because shame had become louder than truth.
I ran. Not because they had stopped loving me. Because I believed rejection before love could defend itself.
I look at the doctor. “I made a terrible mistake once because I felt alone,” I say quietly. “I don’t want her to make hers alone.”
Doctor Oladeji studies me for several seconds. Then he exhales.
“Sammy, I appreciate your concern. But you must understand that compassion also needs structure. If we do this wrongly, we may create more problems for everyone.”
“I understand.”
“You cannot simply sign everything as her guardian unless she confirms that she wants you involved and unless the hospital has a supporting representative from the institution.”
“I will get someone from the institute.”
“And we will ask her, when she is stable enough, who she wants us to contact.”
“That is fair.”
He nods. “For now, you may stand as the contact person who brought her in, pending proper documentation. But I need another official from the institute here as soon as possible.”
“I will arrange it.”
“And Sammy…”
“Yes, doctor?”
“This is delicate. Do not announce her condition to the other students.”
“I won’t.”
“No moral speeches. No panic. No blame.”
His eyes hold mine.
“She needs care first. Questions can come later.”

The instruction humbles me. Because part of me had already begun asking questions. Who was responsible? Who knew? Who supplied the pills? Why did no one notice? But the doctor is right. Care first. Questions later.
“Thank you, doctor,” I say.
He extends his hand. I shake it.
“You are welcome. Go and speak with the institute. I will update you after further review.”
I rise and leave the office.
The corridor feels longer on the way back.
Genevieve and Oluchi stand as soon as they see me. Tunde and Chike are also there, quieter than I have ever seen them. Their faces carry fear, curiosity, and helplessness.
“What did the doctor say?” Genevieve asks.
“She will be alright,” I say.
The lie is too quick. Not entirely a lie. Not entirely truth. A protective sentence.
Oluchi narrows her eyes. “What is wrong with her?”
“They still need to run further tests before treatment continues.”
Genevieve studies me. She is too intelligent to accept the answer completely.
Oluchi’s eyes fill with something like anger. “It’s a lie.”
The words stop me.
“What?”
She steps closer, lowering her voice.
“Eno is pregnant.”
Genevieve turns sharply.
“Oluchi!”
“I knew it,” Oluchi says, her voice trembling. “I knew something was wrong. She has been tying her stomach. She stopped changing with us. She vomits sometimes and says it is ulcer.”
Tunde looks stunned. “Pregnant?”
“Keep your voice down,” I say firmly.
He immediately lowers his head.
Chike stares at the floor.
Genevieve’s face has gone pale. “Is she going to die?”
The question comes out small. Not dramatic. Small. That makes it worse.
I look at all of them.
In the classroom, they were noisy, careless, joking about reproduction as though life were a game. Now life has entered the room without asking permission, and they are children again.
“She is in serious condition,” I say carefully. “But the doctor is attending to her.”
Oluchi begins to cry silently. “I told her to talk to somebody,” she whispers. “I told her. She said nobody would understand.”
I feel a heavy ache in my chest. “Did she tell you who is responsible?”
Oluchi shakes her head quickly. “No. She refused.”
“Did she tell anyone else?”
“I don’t know.”
Genevieve folds her arms around herself.
“She was afraid.”
I nod.
Tunde speaks from the wall. “Sir, what do we do?”
The question surprises me. Not because he asks it. Because he asks without mischief.
“We protect her dignity,” I say.
They look at me.
“No gossip. No jokes. No whispering in class. No using her pain as gist. If anyone asks, she is receiving treatment. That is all.”
Chike nods slowly. “Yes, sir.”
I look at him.
“And if I hear that any one of you made this a story for entertainment, I will personally make sure you understand the difference between correction and discipline.”
Tunde looks at me.
For a moment, I sense the old fear I once knew how to create.
I soften my voice. “But I am not saying this to frighten you. I am saying it because Eno needs people around her who can be better than the world she is afraid of.”
No one speaks. Oluchi wipes her tears. “Can we see her?”
“Not yet. Let the doctors stabilize her first.”
Genevieve nods. “What should we tell the class?”
“That she is receiving treatment and that we should pray for her.”
Chike looks up. “Sir?”
“I’m sorry for joking earlier.”
“You already apologized.”
“I know. But now…” He struggles. “Now it feels somehow.”
“It should,” I say gently. “That feeling is called growing up.”
His eyes remain lowered. I check my watch.
“You all need to go home.”
“No, sir,” Genevieve says immediately. “I want to wait.”
“Me too,” Oluchi adds.
I shake my head.
“You cannot all remain here indefinitely. I will stay. I will call the institute. I will make sure someone responsible comes.”
Tunde steps forward. “Sir, let me stay with you.”
I look at him. “Why?”
He shrugs, embarrassed by his own sincerity. “In case you need to send somebody.”
For the first time that day, I almost smile. The boy who mocked my shoes now wants to be useful.
“No,” I say. “Go home. But thank you.”
He nods.
They begin to move reluctantly toward the exit.
Oluchi stops and turns back. “Sir?”
“Please don’t let them throw her away.”
The words enter me deeply.

Because I know exactly what she means. Not discharge. Not school suspension. Throw away. That is what shame does to young people in frightened households and moralistic communities. It does not correct them. It discards them.
I look at Oluchi. “I will do my best.”
She nods, then follows the others out.
I remain in the waiting room after they leave. Patients come and go. The hospital continues its tired work of receiving pain. I sit with my elbows on my knees and my hands clasped together.

This morning, I entered class to teach reproduction. By afternoon, reproduction had become a frightened girl on a hospital bed, a secret in her body, a life inside her, and a room full of students learning that jokes become foolish when consequences arrive.

I think again of my former self. The man who was mean. The man who knew how to make younger boys tremble. The Guardian Angel who used fear to shape obedience. If that man had met Eno, he would have judged her. Mocked her weakness. Called her careless. Used her mistake as warning to others.

But sitting in that hospital, I feel no desire to condemn her. Only a strange, aching compassion. Perhaps this is what grace does first. It does not make a man holy all at once. It teaches him to see wounded people without reaching for stones.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-FIVE
content: Tonight, I cannot sleep. The room is quiet, but my mind is not. Lantern burns on my reading table, throwing a tired yellow light over the heap of students’ notebooks waiting to be marked.

Outside my window, Ogbomosho settles into its familiar night sounds – a dog barking somewhere behind the compound, the faint murmur of a radio from another room. But inside me, the day refuses to end.

Eno’s cry still lives in my ears. The way she folds into pain. The panic in Oluchi’s voice. The fear on Tunde’s face when he realizes the class joke has become a hospital visit. The doctor’s words. Pregnant. Four months. Unsafe pills. Complications.
I hold my head in both hands and stare at the notebooks.

Teaching, I am discovering, does not end when one leaves the classroom. A teacher carries the faces home. The serious ones. The rebellious ones. The wounded ones pretending to be stubborn. The noisy ones hiding fear behind laughter.
My hand reaches for the notebook on top of the pile. The name on the cover stops me. ENO OKON.

The cover is worn, the edges curled, the pages bent from careless handling. Her handwriting begins neatly in the first few pages, then deteriorates gradually into rushed lines, unfinished notes, missed topics, and empty spaces. Her scores are poor, one after another, except for the last test.

Nine over ten. I stare at that score for a long time. There it is. Proof. Not that she has become perfect. Not that everything inside her life has suddenly arranged itself. But proof that something in her can still rise when given the chance.

I open my drawer and bring out cardboard paper, scissors, gum, cello tape, and a spare notebook cover. I work slowly, carefully, as though repairing the notebook might somehow repair the child. I remove the damaged back cover, trim the cardboard, fix a clean covering around the book, and press the edges until they hold.
Then I take a marker and write on the back cover: ENO, THE MIRACLE.
I sit back and look at it. It is only a notebook. But sometimes a small thing becomes a message when placed in the right hands.

Daylight enters through the half-open window, softened by pale curtains that have seen better years. Eno lies on the bed, one arm stretched beside her where the intravenous line is taped gently to her skin. The drip stand stands near the head of the bed. A plastic chair rests beside her. On the small bedside drawer are a stainless cup, a folded towel, and a packet of medication.

She looks younger asleep. Without the stubbornness on her face, without the careless posture she wears in class, without the armour of indifference, she looks exactly like what she is. A frightened girl.
Her eyes open when she hears me placing the provisions on the drawer. She turns her head slowly.
“Good day, teacher,” she says faintly.
“Good day, Eno.” I smile.
“You look much better.”
She tries to smile back, but it trembles at the edges.
“I brought some things for you.”
I unpack the bag: milk, beverages, biscuits, bottled water, tissue, soap, and a few other items Lizzy once teaches me are necessary when visiting someone in hospital.
“Thank you, sir,” she whispers.
“You are welcome.”
I bend and arrange some of the provisions in the drawer. For a moment, neither of us speaks.
Then she says, “Thank you for everything, teacher. I don’t know what I would have done without you.”
I straighten and look at her.
Her eyes fill immediately. “I owe you my life.”
“No, You do not owe me anything.” I say gently. “If there is anything you can do for me, it is to be strong.”
She looks away. “I will try.”
“Not try.”
She glances at me.
“Be strong.”
A faint smile touches her lips.
I pull the chair closer and sit beside the bed. “The doctor says you may be here for some time.”
“Two weeks,” she says. “Maybe more.”
“That is not the problem.”
“What is the problem?”
“How you use the time.”
She looks puzzled.
“You can lie here and let shame preach to you, or you can lie here and decide that your life has not ended.”
Her lips begin to tremble. “Sir…”
I wait.
She turns her face toward the window. “I heard about the cartoon.”
My chest tightens.
So the gossip has reached her already.

Tunde has drawn another cartoon after the incident, wickedly suggesting that I am responsible for Eno’s condition. By the time I enter the class, he is already wiping the board. I pretended not to see it though.

I am beginning to understand that Tunde’s mischief is not only rebellion. It is also a gift without discipline. He can draw. Very well. His humour is cruel sometimes, but the talent beneath it is real. I have been thinking about how to help him develop it before it becomes another wasted thing.
Still, Eno’s pain matters more than Tunde’s talent in this moment.
“Eno, don’t worry about that now.”
“How can I not worry?” she cries. “How can they say something like that?”
“Because people often speak before they understand pain.”
She covers her face. “I will never go back there.”
“Do not say that.”
“I can’t face them.”
“You can.”
“No, sir. I can’t.”
She begins to sob. “They will laugh at me. They will call me names. They will point. Everybody will know.”
I lean forward.
“Listen to me.”
She shakes her head.
“Eno.” My voice is firm but soft.
She slowly uncovers her face.
“We all make mistakes. Some mistakes are public. Some are hidden. Some people are only fortunate that their own shame has not yet found a stage.”
She stares at me.
“No matter how long a person has failed, life still offers opportunities to stand again.”
“It doesn’t feel like that.”
“I know.”
“How do you know?”
The question enters me deeply. Because I do know. Not in theory. In scars.

“I know what it means to feel as if one mistake has become your whole name,” I say quietly. “I know what it means to fear that the people who should protect you will reject you. I know what it means to want to disappear before shame finishes its work.”
She stops crying for a moment. “What happened to you?”
“Not today.”
She nods slowly.
I reach into the bag and bring out the notebook. “Let me show you something.”
I hand it to her.
She takes it carefully. “What is this?”
“Open it.”
She turns it over and sees the back cover. Her eyes move across the words. ENO, THE MIRACLE.
For the first time since I enter the room, she smiles. A real smile. Small. Fragile. But real.
She runs her fingers across the letters. Then she opens the notebook. The smile fades. Her eyes move across the untidy pages, the poor scores, the incomplete notes.
“The cover is beautiful,” she says quietly. “But inside is still the same record of my failed marks.” She looks up at me. “Why didn’t you remove those pages?”
“Because it is your notebook.”
She frowns.
“And because our failures are part of our story.”
She looks down again.
“Not the whole story,” I add. “But part of it.”
She turns another page.
“Sometimes we want God to make the outside beautiful while we hide the pages inside. But healing is not pretending the bad pages were never written. Healing is letting God continue the story.”
Her hand tightens around the notebook.
“If I tear those pages out, the book will look cleaner. But it will not be honest.”
She is quiet.
“Keep turning.”
She obeys. Then stops. Her eyes widen. “Nine over ten.”
I smile. Expecting her to turn to me.
“Because you did not give up on yourself.”
Her lips part slightly.
“That one good mark does not erase the poor ones,” I say. “But it proves the poor ones are not the end.”

She closes the notebook and holds it against her chest. Her eyes shut. For several seconds, she does not speak. When she opens them again, tears run quietly down her face. “Do you mind if I ask you a personal question?”
“If it will help you heal, ask.”
She looks at me directly. “Have you ever been in love?”
The question surprises me. I smile faintly.
“Many years ago, I could swear I was.”
She waits.
“As I grow older, I realize love is deeper than what I imagine.”
She looks down.
“Did you ever cross boundaries?”
I understand what she is really asking.
She seems genuinely puzzled.
“If you truly love somebody, is it wrong to prove it?”
I watch her carefully. There is no arrogance in her question. Only confusion. A young heart trying to understand how something called love has left her wounded.
“You feel that way too, don’t you?” I ask.
Her face collapses.
“You believed he loves you.”
She begins to cry again. “It hurts so much.”
I let the tears come.
“How can love hurt like this?” she asks.
“Sometimes what hurts us is not love,” I say. “Sometimes it is our hunger to be loved.”
She looks at me through tears.
“There is a difference. Love protects. Love waits. Love takes responsibility. Love does not pressure someone into secrecy and leave them alone with consequences.”
Her lips tremble. “He said he loves me.”
“I believe he said it.”
“But he leaves.”
I say nothing. The silence answers enough.
She turns her face away. “I was deceived.”
I lean closer.
“Eno, listen carefully. There are many ways to express love. And intimacy without responsibility can become a burden too heavy for a young person to carry alone.”
Her tears fall faster. “I wish I met you before now.”

The words break my heart. Because I have heard versions of that sentence in my own life. If only someone reached me earlier. If only someone warned me differently. If only love found me before the fraternity did.
“It is okay to regret,” I say. “But do not let regret become your grave.”
She sobs quietly. “I feel dirty.”
“No, no Eno.”
“You are wounded. You are afraid. You made a mistake. But you are not rubbish.”
She looks at me.
“You are not what happened to you.”
The room becomes still. The drip continues its slow work beside her.

I continue gently. “The Bible says there are times of ignorance that God overlooks, but now He calls people to repentance. That means God does not invite you back so He can humiliate you. He invites you back so He can heal you.”
“Will He forgive me?”
“Even me?”
“Especially you.”
She closes her eyes. “Will you pray for me?”
“I will.”
I hesitate.
“But I want you to do two things.”
She opens her eyes. “What?”

“First, you must be willing to forgive the person who hurts you. Not because what he did is right. Not because he deserves access to you again. Forgiveness does not mean you excuse evil or return to danger. It means you release the poison so it does not keep ruling your heart.”
She listens, crying silently.
“Second, you must receive Jesus and allow Him to free you from guilt and the power of sin.”
Her eyes move to the ceiling. For a long moment, she says nothing. Then she whispers, “I am willing.”
I hold her hand gently. “Are you sure?”
“I am willing,” she repeats, sobbing now. “I am willing.”
As I pray with her, I remember Legion. His cold fingers tightening around mine. His voice asking for prayer at the edge of death.

The memory still pains me, but it has also opened something in me. Legion’s final moments show me a dimension of God I did not understand before – the mercy that can reach even the unlovable, even the violent, even those who mock faith until breath begins to fail.
I once thought grace is for people who deserve a second chance. Now I am beginning to see that grace is second chance. When the prayer ends, Eno’s crying softens.
She still looks wounded. Still frightened. Still uncertain. But something in her face has changed. Not joy. Not yet. Relief. A small space opens where shame has been sitting.
“Have you heard the song Amazing Grace?” I ask.
She nods. “Yes.”
“Do you know what the words mean?”
She does not answer.
Instead, she begins to sing softly. Her voice is weak at first. Almost a whisper.
I join her. Quietly.

The room does not become magical. The hospital does not vanish. The pain does not disappear. Her condition remains serious. Her future remains uncertain. The gossip outside the room still waits. The consequences of her choices are still real.
But as we sing, I understand something. Grace does not always remove the wound immediately. Sometimes it simply enters the room and sits beside the wounded until they remember they are still loved.
Eno holds the notebook against her chest as we sing.
Eno, the miracle. Not because her pages are clean. But because her story is not finished.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-SIX
content: My office at Destiny Institute is hardly an office in the dignified sense of the word. It is a narrow room at the end of the administrative corridor, furnished with an old wooden desk, two mismatched chairs, a metal filing cabinet whose lock no longer works, and a bookshelf that leans slightly to the left under the weight of abandoned textbooks.

A dusty louvre window opens toward the courtyard, allowing in sunlight, students’ voices, chalk dust, and the smell of roasted groundnuts from a hawker who has made the school gate her permanent territory.
Still, to me, the room becomes sacred. It is the first place in many years where I sit not as a fugitive, not as a broken man hiding from death, but as someone entrusted with young lives.

Besides preparing the students for WAEC, NECO, and university entrance examinations, I persuade the proprietress to let us do something more. These children do not only need examination drills. They need to discover what is buried inside them before failure, poverty, ridicule, pregnancy, peer pressure, and hopelessness convince them they are ordinary.

To my surprise, she agrees. Perhaps she sees my seriousness. Perhaps she sees the students’ restlessness. Perhaps she simply wants the institute to appear progressive. Whatever her reason, she gives permission.
So, I register the school for a Christmas concert organized by Youth Aflame Ministry in Lagos. When I announce it in class, the students react as I expected.

They laugh first. Then deny interest. Then mock the idea. Then accuse me of wanting to disgrace them before Lagos people. But beneath the noise, I see the real enemy. Fear. They are afraid of failing in public. Afraid of being seen. Afraid of discovering that the little gifts they joke about may actually demand discipline.
By the time they understand I am serious, it is too late to retreat. The proprietress has already approved the project and hired Miss Harrison, a professional creative arts coach, to train them.

Miss Harrison is not easily impressed. She is slender, sharp-eyed, and graceful in the disciplined manner of people who have spent years turning raw talent into performance. She wears her hair low, her nails clean, her shoes practical, and carries a leather folder that seems to contain not only training notes but judgment. Her voice is usually calm, but her silence can make a noisy student reconsider his entire life.

Our third weekly briefing takes place in my office on a hot afternoon. The ceiling fan turns lazily above us, clicking at intervals like a tired metronome. Outside, students are rehearsing a chorus badly. Their voices rise, collide, scatter, and return in disorder. Someone is off-key with the confidence of a prophet.
Miss Harrison sits before my desk, her folder open on her lap, disappointment written across her face before she speaks.

The preparation is not going well. The concert is close. The students are unserious. The few who can sing are either shy, proud, distracted, or unwilling. The eager ones have enthusiasm but very little musical discipline. The rehearsals have become a battlefield of laughter, mockery, excuses, late-coming, and raw adolescent self-consciousness.

She has discovered Eno’s voice. Beautiful, wounded, unexpectedly clear. But Eno is still fragile, still recovering from the hospital, still learning to lift her face without expecting shame to strike it.
Patrick has a strong baritone, deep and textured, the kind that can steady a choir if trained properly. But he comes to rehearsals more interested in discouraging others than developing himself.
Jumoke can sing. Very well. Her voice is bright, confident, almost theatrical. Unfortunately, her attitude often arrives before her talent and remains after it has left.
And then there is Tunde.
Miss Harrison mentions him last, but her tone changes when she does. Tunde is the most gifted. Not merely funny. Not merely creative. Gifted.

His drawings have life. His timing is instinctive. His eye for exaggeration, expression, and movement is unusually mature. He can turn a moment into a cartoon, a face into commentary, a joke into an image that stays in the mind. The problem is not talent. The problem is direction.

He is impatient. He wants the stage before training. Recognition before discipline. Money before mastery. Like many gifted young people, he is in love with the future but offended by the process required to reach it.
Miss Harrison reaches into her handbag and brings out an envelope. She places it on my desk. The envelope is ordinary brown paper, but something about the way she releases it makes me uneasy.
It contains an artist engagement contract Tunde has asked her to review. He didn’t want me to see it, Miss Harrison tells me. That alone tells me enough.

I open the envelope slowly and bring out the document. The letterhead looks impressive at first glance: bold logo, exaggerated promises, dramatic language about talent discovery, music-video appearances, brand visibility, and future royalties. But beneath the polish lies the familiar smell of exploitation.
Fraud often comes well-dressed. Miss Harrison has already checked the supposed record label. It is not what it claims to be.

The company has no credible structure, no verifiable roster, no transparent management, and a reputation for trapping desperate young creatives into unfair agreements. They are not offering Tunde a future. They are hunting his impatience.
The envelope suddenly feels heavier. I place it back on the table.
That boy wants to fly before learning how to walk. The thought stays with me long after Miss Harrison rises to leave.

I see her to the gate. She walks briskly, as if already calculating rehearsal adjustments in her head. The courtyard shimmers under the afternoon sun. Students move between classrooms, some carrying notebooks, others carrying nothing but noise. Somewhere upstairs, a group rehearses again, this time worse than before.
When I return to my office, I place the envelope in my drawer and sit quietly.
Tunde’s contract is important. But his heart is more urgent.

The boy’s cartoons, his jokes, his mockery, his restlessness, his hunger for recognition, all of it points to the same place. He wants to be seen. He wants proof that he is not ordinary. And if we do not help him understand the discipline of his gift, someone else will teach him the cost of selling it cheaply.
I send for him.

He knocks a few minutes later. When he enters, the old mischief is missing from his face. He looks around the office with the alert guilt of a boy expecting judgment. He sees the drawer. Sees my face. Sees the chair before the desk. Perhaps he imagines I called him because of the cartoons. Perhaps because of the contract. Perhaps because he knows that his own life is beginning to outrun his wisdom.
He sits carefully.
The office is quiet except for the ceiling fan and the faint shouting of students outside. I do not begin with the contract. I begin with his gift.

Tunde does not know how to receive praise without suspicion. When I tell him he is talented, apology rushes to his mouth before gratitude can form. That is how many young people survive correction: apologize quickly, escape consequences, and return unchanged.
But I do not want his apology. Not yet. I want his understanding.
I allow the silence to stretch.

Then I lead him, carefully, into the weight of careless judgment. I make him taste what it feels like to be accused by rumour. Not cruelly. Not to destroy him. But to hold up a mirror. The lesson strikes him harder than punishment would have.

His face changes slowly – from confusion, to fear, to embarrassment, to recognition. He begins to understand. A cartoon is not always harmless because it is funny. A joke is not innocent simply because people laugh. A gifted hand can wound as easily as it can heal.
I watch the lesson settle over him.

The old Sammy would have enjoyed his discomfort. The old Sammy knows how to make young men tremble and call that transformation. But now, sitting behind this old desk in a room filled with dust and sunlight, I feel no pleasure in his shame. Only responsibility.
Tunde is not wicked. He is unformed. That distinction matters.

I speak to him about love, mercy, and the kind of world created when people become misanthropic, merciless, unforgiving, ruthless, and uninterested in the pain of others. I speak not as a man repeating moral lessons from a textbook, but as one who has once lived in that world and knows its taste.
Pain. Sorrow. Emptiness. Restlessness. A hunger for peace that violence can never satisfy. The story of the Good Samaritan enters the room quietly. Not as Sunday school decoration.
As a mirror. A wounded man. Religious passersby. Indifference dressed as respectability. Then a stranger who stops. A stranger who spends. A stranger who returns.
Tunde listens. Really listens. For once, his face carries no performance.

When he stands, something in him has shifted. He does not make a joke. He does not ask whether the lecture is over. He does not defend himself. He simply turns toward the door with the solemnity of a boy who has found an errand for his conscience.
He wants to apologize to Eno.
At the door, he stops and looks back. The question he asks is small, but revealing.
“Sir, did the Samaritan keep his promise to return?”
I look at him for a moment. “Yes,” I say. “A true neighbour returns.”
He accepts the answer like a child holding something precious and leaves the room quietly.

When the door closes, I lean back and shut my eyes. I whisper a prayer of gratitude. Not because I have spoken perfectly. But because I have not allowed offense to speak for me. Patience, I am learning, is sometimes grace with clenched teeth.
A few minutes later, another knock comes. I open my eyes and look toward the door. The room has grown still. “Yes, come in.”
The door opens.

A bright ray of afternoon sunlight floods the office so suddenly that I lift my hand to shield my eyes. For a moment, the doorway becomes only light. The corridor behind it disappears. Dust particles float in the brightness like tiny living things.
Then she steps in and closes the door gently behind her. The light seems to follow her.

She is young – perhaps sixteen – but carries herself with a calm maturity that makes age difficult to place. Her uniform is neatly worn. Her posture is graceful without being proud. Her face holds a softness that does not look naïve, and when she smiles, the entire room seems to loosen its breath.
There is something unusual about her presence. Not dramatic. Not mystical in a way I can explain. Just clean. Quiet. Like water placed beside thirst.

She sits in the chair before my desk with a poise that contrasts sharply with the restless energy of most students who enter my office. Her hands rest neatly on her lap. Her eyes are bright, attentive, and unafraid.
I find myself speaking more gently than usual. “How can I help you?”
She smiles again. “My name is Rachel.”
Then she hesitates, as though choosing whether to step fully into whatever burden has brought her.
“I overheard you mention the Good Samaritan,” she says.
I wait.
“And I come because I need help.”
The words settle in the room.
Outside, the institute continues its ordinary noise – students laughing, chairs dragging, voices rising in rehearsal, life moving forward with all its careless urgency.
But inside my small office, with the sunlight still spread across the floor and Rachel sitting before me like a message I do not yet know how to read, I sense that another door has opened.
In this place of examinations, unfinished dreams, wounded students, raw talents, false contracts, hidden pregnancies, and fragile hope, my assignment is widening.
I think I am sent to teach Biology. Perhaps God brought me here to learn mercy.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-SEVEN
content: “There is someone I want you to see.”
“A girl.” Her voice is gentle, almost too gentle for the urgency in her eyes.
I look around instinctively. “Where is she?”
Rachel rises and heads to the door. “Come.”
That is all. No explanation. No pleading. No drama. Just come.

I should ask more questions. I should demand details. I should remind her that the day has been long, that I have scripts to mark, that the Christmas concert is two days away and Miss Harrison is already close to accusing me of overworking the students. I should also remember that I am not rich, not stable, not healed enough to become anybody’s rescuer.
But Rachel is already walking. And something in me follows.

When we get to somewhere in the town, she leads me to a narrower path behind a row of old shops. The air changes. It smells of damp earth, stale refuse, and the forgotten corners of town where people pass quickly in daylight and avoid altogether at night. A half-collapsed fence leans beside a patch of tall grass. Beyond it stands a deserted building, its windows dark, its plaster peeled away in patches, its roofline broken in places like a mouth with missing teeth.
I stop at the entrance. “Rachel, what is this place?”
“She is inside.”
“Who is inside?”
“The girl.”
I look at her.
“Does anyone know you brought me here?”
She does not answer directly. Instead, she steps through the open doorway.

The inside is darker than the evening outside. Dust rises beneath our feet. The air is cold, not with weather alone, but with abandonment. Old cartons are stacked against one wall. Torn posters cling to the plaster. A wooden door, swollen by damp, hangs slightly ajar.
Rachel walks ahead and pushes it open. “Come,” she says again.
I enter.
At first, I see nothing. Then Rachel strikes a match.
The flame trembles between her fingers before it catches the wick of a short candle placed on a broken windowsill. Yellow light spreads weakly across the room.
That is when I see her.

A girl lies in the corner on a pack of folded paper cartons. She is curled into herself, shivering under a thin cloth that barely covers her. Her face is pale beneath the dust. Her lips are dry. Her hair clings to her forehead. One hand is tucked under her cheek, the other pressed weakly against her stomach, as if she is trying to hold herself together.
“Jesus Christ,” I whisper. Compassion moves me before caution.

I cross the room quickly and kneel beside her. Her skin is hot and cold at once – the strange feverish confusion of a body fighting too many battles. I touch her forehead with the back of my hand, then her neck. Her breathing is shallow. Her eyes remain shut.
“She is going to be alright, isn’t she?” Rachel asks.
I turn to her sharply. “Have you given her anything? Food? Drugs? Water?”
“I tried to give her food. She would not take it. I don’t know what drugs to give her.”
I look back at the girl.
“She needs a hospital immediately.”
Rachel nods, as if she has known that all along.
“Does she have relatives around?”
“Where did you find her?”
“Here.”
“That is not an answer.”
Rachel looks at me with that same calm expression. “She is alone.”
I stand, anger rising because fear has no better clothing.

“Rachel, listen to me. I don’t have money on me. I am still owing the institute. Eno’s hospital bill was only just settled. I cannot keep taking people to hospital and hoping mercy will pay the bill.”
Rachel’s eyes do not change. “I heard about Eno.”
“Then you know I am not in a position to do this.”
“Isn’t there a way we can save her life?”
I look away.
She steps closer. “Are you going to leave her here and walk away?”
The question enters me quietly.
“Like the priest and the Levite?” Rachel adds.
I stare at her. The words strike a place I have been trying to protect. The priest. The Levite. The Samaritan.

I know that story. I have taught it. I have used it to explain love as something practical, not decorative. But stories are easier when the wounded man is not lying before you and the purse is not empty.
“Rachel,” I say weakly, “I…”
“The Samaritan did not have enough either,” she says.
I look at the girl again. The candlelight moves over her face.
She seems younger than I first think. Too young to be here. Too young to be nameless in a broken building.
Rachel’s voice lowers. “My father will pay back all when she is fully restored.”
I turn to her.
“Your father?”
She only smiles. That smile unsettles me more than the darkness.
I bend again and touch the girl’s neck. Her pulse is weak but present.
“What is her name?” I ask.
Rachel answers softly. “Her name is Rakiya.”
For a moment, I find it hard to stand. The name enters the room like something already written before I arrive.
Rakiya.
I do not know why, but I feel as if I have just stepped into a story that began long before this abandoned building.
Rachel is still having that calm smile on her face.

Dr. Lloyd’s Hospital is quiet when we arrive, but not empty. Hospitals are never empty, even when they appear calm. Pain has its own silence. It sits in waiting chairs, leans against walls, breathes behind curtains, and watches doors open and close.

A nurse rushes Rakiya inside when she sees her condition. Rachel follows only as far as the reception, then sits with her hands folded on her lap. I give the receptionist what little information I can. Name: Rakiya. Age: unknown. Relatives: unknown. Address: unknown. Relationship to patient: stranger.
Each unknown makes me feel more foolish.
A stranger. That is what I write. Yet my signature enters the form as the responsible person.
Dr. Lloyd calls me into his office after examining her. He removes his glasses slowly and looks at me in a way that tells me the matter is serious before he speaks.
“You seem to be associated with a lot of people with problems,” he says. “Is she one of your students too?”
“No, sir.”
He raises his brows.
“She is a stranger.”
He looks down at the form.
“A stranger.”
“Yes, sir.”
“I presume the institute is paying the bills too.”
“No, sir. They are not.”
He leans back in his chair.

“She will be in intensive care for a while,” he says. “Your patient has complications. She is severely weak, possibly malnourished, and there are signs of untreated infection. We will need to stabilize her properly.”
My head drops. The word bills stands in the room though he does not say it again immediately.
“I am concerned about how you intend to pay,” he continues plainly. “You understand this will not be ordinary treatment.”
I nod, though I do not understand anything except the size of my helplessness. Rachel said her father will pay. But I do not know Rachel’s father. I barely know Rachel.
What am I doing? Am I being compassionate, or reckless? Faithful, or foolish? Generous, or irresponsible?
Would I rather the girl dies because I am afraid of debt? The question answers itself before I finish asking it.
Dr. Lloyd watches me struggle. Then his face softens.
“Okay,” he says. “You know what we will do? We will start her treatment. I will give you some time to find anyone related to the girl or anyone who can take responsibility for her bills.”
Relief weakens me. “Oh, thank you, Dr. Lloyd. Thank you, sir.”
He adjusts his chair and studies me for a moment.

“Young man,” he says, “years ago, when I was your age, because of the difficulty of my upbringing, I could only wish the world would become a better place for all of us to live.” He pauses, shaking his head slightly. “That desire motivated me to study medicine. I felt I had a calling to give life to those who had infirmities. But no sooner had I started practice than I discovered that such a world was an illusion. Perhaps even an impossibility.”
He looks at me firmly. “I think you are overwhelmed with that desire too.”
I sit quietly.

Maybe he is right. Maybe compassion is beginning to outrun wisdom in me. Maybe I am trying to rescue every wounded person because I cannot rescue the old version of myself. Still, the words rise from my heart before I arrange them.
“So, how is the girl? What is her name again?”
“Yes. Eno.”
“She is fine, sir. Recovering.”
“I hope she is doing well.”
“She is.”
Then I remember the card. “Oh, lest I forget.” I open my bag and bring out the invitation envelope.
“My students are performing at a Christmas concert on Saturday. I would like you to honour us with your presence, sir.”
He receives it with visible surprise.
“Your students?”
He opens the envelope and reads the card carefully. The corner of his mouth lifts. “Come Let Us Adore Him,” he reads.
“It is a composition from Luke chapter two.”
“Interesting.” He puts the card back into the envelope and places it on a stack of files. “I will be there.”
“Thank you, sir.”
He stands. “I have a procedure to attend to.”
He escorts me to the reception and asks the clerk to guide me with the paperwork.
That is when I remember Rachel. I step out into the waiting area. The chair where I left her is empty. I look toward the entrance. Then the corridor. Then the reception. No Rachel.
“She probably returned to the centre,” I tell myself.
But something about her absence troubles me. It is not fear. It is mystery.
I complete the forms for Rakiya’s treatment with my signature feeling heavier each time I write it.

When I leave the hospital, Ogbomosho’s evening has deepened. The road outside is busy as usual with taxis, motorcycles, fruit sellers, and people hurrying home before darkness becomes complete. I walk back slowly, my mind carrying one question after another.
Where do I get the money? Do I ask the proprietress again?
No. How can I? Just days after Eno’s bill?
Rakiya is not even one of her students. Do I call Bitty? Do I call Lizzy to help me speak to him? No. Is that right?
Am I beginning to make my compassion everybody else’s financial burden? I stop near a roadside shop and press my fingers to my forehead. “Oh God,” I whisper, “You must help me.”
The answer does not come. Only the noise of town. Only the memory of Rachel’s calm voice. The Samaritan did not have enough either.

The building rises behind decorative lights, glass doors, and banners announcing the Christmas concert. Cars line the compound. Families arrive dressed in festive colours. Children run ahead of parents. Ushers in matching outfits direct guests toward the hall. Music spills from inside each time the doors open.
It’s Saturday evening, the event centre is glowing like another world. For a moment, I stand near the entrance and simply watch.
This is not the abandoned building. Not the hospital. Not the construction site. Not the motor park. This is beauty arranged deliberately. A hall prepared for applause.

Inside, chandeliers hang from the ceiling, scattering light across the polished floor. The stage is dressed with red, white, and gold fabric. A large nativity backdrop stands behind the microphones. Speakers flank the platform. Rows of chairs fill the hall. The air smells of perfume, new clothes, floral decorations, and warm electrical equipment.
I join Miss Harrison backstage for the last-minute preparation of the students’ performance.
Backstage is a small world of nervous energy.

Students stand in clusters, adjusting collars, smoothing skirts, fixing buttons, whispering lyrics, stepping through choreography under their breath, and pretending not to be afraid. The event centre staff move about with headsets and urgency, carrying cables, bottled water, microphones, and last-minute instructions. Somewhere behind the curtain, the audience applauds another performance, and the sound enters backstage like thunder from another room.
Miss Harrison stands before the students, speaking to them with the disciplined calm of a woman who has spent weeks turning chaos into order.

“Listen carefully,” she says. “Do not rush the stage. Do not fight for position. If you miss a step, continue. If someone beside you misses a line, do not look at the person as if judgment day has arrived. Keep smiling. Follow the music. Trust the rehearsal.”
Tunde raises his hand. “Ma, what if the Holy Spirit leads me to improvise?”
Genevieve turns sharply. “The Holy Spirit will not lead you to spoil our work.”
The students laugh.
Miss Harrison points at him. “Tunde, tonight, your own spiritual gift is obedience.”
Even I laugh quietly from where I stand near the entrance. They do not notice me at first. And I am grateful for that. It gives me time to look at them. Really look at them.

They are charming in their concert uniforms, almost unrecognizable from the noisy, restless, wounded group I first meet at the institute. Their clothes are neatly arranged. Their shoes are polished. Their faces carry powder, nervous smiles, and the fragile pride of young people who know they are expected to do something excellent.

Jumoke stands slightly apart, humming to herself, her eyes closed in concentration. Patrick keeps tapping rhythm on his thigh, pretending it is not nervousness. Genevieve holds the lyric sheet like a legal document. Oluchi adjusts Eno’s sleeve with a seriousness that makes them both look like sisters. Tunde keeps peeping through the curtain, counting the audience with the exaggerated anxiety of a man about to perform before presidents.
Eno sees me first. Her face lights up. She waves.
I wave back.
She whispers to Tunde, and he turns quickly. He waves too, then one after another the others turn, their faces brightening when they see me. They wave as if I am the one going on stage.
Look at them. They look so cute. So alive. So much more than their test scores. So much more than their mistakes. So much more than the names people have called them.
Miss Harrison comes over to me.
“How are they doing?” I ask.
“Well,” she says, exhaling, “they are ready.”
“Just ready?”
She gives me a tired look. “They are… you know, I have never had to work with such a difficult set of children before.”
I glance at them again. They all wave at me once more. Tunde salutes. Genevieve pushes his hand down.
“What do you think about them?” I ask, unable to hide my anxiety.
Miss Harrison turns and studies them for a moment. For once, she does not answer quickly. Then she says, “I think they are fantastic.”
Something loosens in my chest. “They are what you made them,” I whisper.
She looks at me. “No,” she says softly. “They are what you made me believe they are.”
For a moment, we stare at each other. The words touch me more deeply than she can know.

When I first begin teaching them, I did not know what I was doing. I only know that I cannot bear to watch young people drift toward waste. I saw arrogance, laziness, rebellion, humour, stubbornness, shame, and hidden fear. But beneath all that, I saw potential. Perhaps because I know what happens when potential is misnamed. I know what happens when no one calls a boy by the name God gave him before darkness arrives with another one.
“Thank you, Miss Harrison,” I whisper emotionally.
“You’re welcome,” she replies. “It has been a privilege.”
The current performance ends. The applause rises, falls, and rises again.
An usher peeps through the curtain and signals to Miss Harrison. “They are next.”
The students become still. That stillness frightens me more than their noise.
Miss Harrison claps once. “Positions.”
They move. Not perfectly. But beautifully.

I leave backstage and walk into the hall, my heart beating faster than it should. The event centre is lavishly decorated for Christmas. Golden lights hang from the ceiling. Red and white fabric drapes the stage. A large nativity backdrop glows behind the microphones. The air is filled with the fragrance of perfume and air freshener and the faint sweetness of fruit drinks served by the ushers. I pick up a cup from the tray.
Families fill the rows. Parents whisper. Children fidget. Guests fan themselves with programmes booklet.

The proprietress, Mrs. Rose Animashaun, sits in the front row where I reserved seats for her and Dr. Lloyd. She looks proud already, even before the students appear. Dr. Lloyd sits beside her, dressed in a simple but elegant native attire, his invitation card folded neatly inside the programme booklet.
I sit beside them.
“You look nervous,” Mrs. Animashaun says.
She smiles. “That is good. It means you care.”
Dr. Lloyd leans toward me. “Which group is yours?”
“Destiny Institute, sir.”
“The ones performing *Come Let Us Adore Him*?”
“Yes, sir. It is a composition from Luke chapter two.”
He nods. “Good title.”

Then the announcer’s voice fills the hall. “Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome the students of Destiny Educational Institute as they present a special Christmas composition titled, *Come Let Us Adore Him*.”
My breath catches. The hall applauds.
Jumoke steps onto the stage first. For a second, she looks smaller under the lights. Then she lifts her head.
The hall quietens.
The music begins softly – keyboard first, then a gentle drumbeat, then strings from the instrumental track Miss Harrison has arranged. Jumoke opens her mouth and sings the first line.
Her voice is clear. Tender. Almost trembling. But it holds.
A silence spreads across the hall, not the silence of boredom, but the silence of people realizing that something beautiful has begun.
She sings of shepherds watching by night. Of an angel appearing. Of fear giving way to good tidings.

Then Patrick steps onto the stage, his voice joining hers from the side. His tone is warmer, deeper than I expect. He carries the next verse with surprising steadiness. Some people in the audience begin nodding slowly.
Then Genevieve enters. The moment her voice rises, the hall changes. It is not merely that she sings well. She sings as if the words have found a home in her chest. “Glory to God in the highest…”

Her voice climbs, bright and strong, and the entire hall seems to lift with it. Someone stands. Then another. Then another. Within seconds, people are on their feet, clapping even before the full choir joins.

I stare at the stage, unable to breathe properly. Is this Genevieve? The same Genevieve who once hides behind seriousness so nobody sees fear? Now she stands under stage lights, her voice ringing over the hall, leading people into worship.
Then the others enter.

Tunde comes in with the male harmony, disciplined for once, his face shining with effort. Oluchi, Bisi, and the girls move into formation behind Jumoke and Genevieve. The younger students step in from both sides, carrying simple white cloths that they raise and lower in slow choreography, like wings moving under light.
The song grows. “Come, let us adore Him…” Their hands move together. Their feet step in rhythm.

They form a small circle, then open outward, representing the shepherds going to Bethlehem. Patrick kneels briefly at centre stage. Jumoke and Genevieve stand on either side, their voices weaving through the chorus. Tunde turns at exactly the right beat, and I nearly laugh with relief.
The audience is fully awake now. Clapping. Smiling. Some recording with phones. Some singing along though they do not know the arrangement.

Dr. Lloyd stands beside me, clapping with the grin of a man unexpectedly captured by joy. Wrinkles gather around the corners of his eyes. Mrs. Animashaun presses a hand to her chest, her lips moving silently, perhaps in prayer.

I stand too. Not because everyone else is standing. Because I cannot remain seated. My students are on stage. My difficult, noisy, wounded, brilliant students. And they are beautiful. For a few minutes, I forget Ogbomosho’s dust. I forget the construction site. The beer parlour. The shame. The debts. The fear of the GODs. I forget even myself. All I see is possibility.
The final chorus begins softly. The lights dim slightly. The students raise their hands. Genevieve’s voice carries the line alone: “Unto us is born a Saviour…”
Then the entire group answers, “Christ the Lord.”
The hall erupts. Applause rises like rain on a zinc roof.

People stand fully now, clapping, cheering, some shouting “Bravo!” and “Hallelujah!” at the same time. The students bow awkwardly, laughing and almost breaking formation. Miss Harrison appears briefly at the side curtain, clapping with tears in her eyes.
Then I notice Dr. Lloyd’s phone vibrating.
At first, he ignores it. Then it vibrates again. He looks at the screen. His smile fades. He steps aside to answer.

I keep clapping, still watching the students leave the stage, but my attention splits. Dr. Lloyd turns slightly away from us, pressing the phone close to his ear. His shoulders stiffen. He says very little.
Then his hand drops. He turns to me. The grin is gone. His eyelids lower. The corners of his mouth turn downward.
A cold feeling passes through me. He ends the call and comes close enough. “Samuel,” he says quietly, “please see me at the hospital after the concert.”
My throat dries. “Is it Rakiya?”
He hesitates. That is answer enough.
“Doctor?”
“After the concert,” he says. “Come immediately.”
He turns to Mrs. Animashaun, congratulates her and excuses himself. He leaves before I can ask more questions.
I watch him walk toward the exit. The applause is still going on. The hall is still bright. My students are still being celebrated. But the joy has acquired a shadow.
Mrs. Animashaun touches my arm. “Is everything alright?”
I force my face to behave. “Yes, ma.” But I know it is not.
For the rest of the show, people keep congratulating me.
“Your students were excellent.”
“That soloist! What is her name?”
“That boy with the harmony was impressive.”
“The choreography was beautiful.”
“Who trained them?”
“Destiny Institute has surprised us tonight.”
Mrs. Animashaun receives most of the commendations with gracious pride, then turns each one toward me.
“Mr. Briggs and Miss Harrison did the hard work,” she says repeatedly.
I smile. I nod. I say thank you.
But Dr. Lloyd’s face stays in my mind. After the final presentation, I return backstage.

The place has become a celebration. Students from other schools move around. Parents come to take pictures. Teachers hug performers. Someone is shouting about missing shoes. Someone else is crying because her headpiece is lost, though the programme is over.
Miss Harrison stands near the wall, receiving hugs from the students.
Before I can call them, Tunde sees me.
“Mr. Briggs!”
The whole group turns.
Then they rush toward me. I brace myself too late.

They collide with me in a wave of laughter and youthful victory. For a second, I almost lose my balance. Tunde is shouting. Patrick is laughing. Oluchi is crying. Jumoke is asking whether I hear the audience clap for her. Genevieve tries to control them, but she is crying too.
“You people want to kill me?” I say, laughing.
“No, sir!” Tunde shouts. “We want to celebrate you.”
“Celebrate yourself first.”
“We did it, sir!”
“Yes,” I say, looking at them one by one. “You did it.”
Eno stands a little away from the group, watching us slack-jawed.

She looks different tonight. Not free from shame completely. Not untouched by what has happened. But present. She has come. She has stood with the group. She has watched them perform. That alone is a victory.
Slowly, she waddles toward me. Her protruded stomach presses against my abdomen as she hugs me tightly. “Thank you for everything,” she whispers.
My throat tightens. I place one hand gently on her shoulder. “You are welcome, Eno.”
She pulls back and wipes her eyes quickly. “I didn’t think I could come.”
“But you did.”
She nods. “And did you see Genevieve?”
“I saw her.”
“She nearly carried the roof.”
I laugh. “She did.”
Tunde pushes forward.
“Sir, what of me?”
“You obeyed instructions.”
His face falls. “That’s all?”
“That is a miracle.”
The others burst into laughter.
Miss Harrison joins us, smiling.
“Mr. Briggs,” she says, “your students are officially stars.”
“Our students,” I correct.
She nods. “Our students.”
The words warm me.
Our. Again that word. It appearis again in my life like mercy trying to teach me a new language. That I am not alone.
I gather the students around. “Listen to me.”
They quieten slowly.
“I am proud of you. Very proud.”
Their faces soften.
“You worked hard. You listened. You represented the institute well. You represented yourselves well. And tonight, people saw what I have always believed about you.”
Tunde raises his hand. “That we are troublesome but talented?”
“Yes,” I say. “But more talented than troublesome.”
They laugh.
“School officially goes on recess from tonight,” I continue. “Enjoy Christmas. Rest. Help your parents at home. Do not use this holiday to scatter everything we have built.”
Some of them groan.
I point at Tunde. “That warning is especially for you.”
“Sir, why always me?”
“Because prophecy is better than regret.”
They laugh again.
“Merry Christmas,” I say.
“Merry Christmas, sir!” They chorus and crowd me again.
For a few minutes, I allow myself to receive their joy.
This is what teaching gives back. Not money. Not comfort. Not status. Something harder to name.

The knowledge that a young life can bend toward light because you stand near it long enough. But as the students begin dispersing, as parents call them, as Miss Harrison begins collecting costumes and arranging leftover materials, the shadow returns. Rakiya. Dr. Lloyd. The phone call. Come immediately.
I step aside and take a deep breath.
Mrs. Animashaun is still in the hall speaking with guests, glowing with pride. I should tell her. I should explain that I may need help again. But shame holds me by the throat.
How many times can one man bring emergencies to the same kind woman? How long before compassion begins to look like exploitation?
I look once more at the students.

They are laughing, taking pictures, teasing one another, refusing to let the night end. Then I remember the abandoned building. The candle. Rakiya shivering on cartons. Rachel’s calm smile. The Samaritan did not have enough either.
I close my eyes briefly. “Lord,” I whisper, “give me courage for whatever is waiting.”
Then I leave the event centre quietly.
Outside, the night air is cooler. Cars are still pulling out of the compound. Drivers honk impatiently. Children run beside their parents. Music continues faintly from inside the hall.
The world behind me is bright with applause. The road ahead leads to the hospital.

I begin walking. The joy of the concert remains in my chest. But beside it, fear walks with me. And I know before I reach Dr. Lloyd that Rakiya’s story is about to demand more from me than I think I have left to give.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-EIGHT
content: The corridors are quieter than usual, but not peaceful. Hospitals have a way of holding silence differently. It is never empty. It carries the weight of pain, waiting, fear, unanswered questions, whispered prayers, and footsteps that move too quickly toward rooms where families are not ready for bad news.
The fluorescent lights hum above the passage. Somewhere, a child coughs. A nurse moves past me with a tray of medication.
I sight Dr. Lloyd before he sees me.
He is standing near the nurses’ station with a file in his hand, his shoulders slightly lowered in a way that tells me the day has taken something from him.
When he turns and sees me, his face changes. Not dramatically. Doctors learn how to protect their faces. But I see enough.
“Mr. Briggs,” he says quietly.
I stop walking. Something inside me prepares for impact before the words come.
He walks toward me. “I’m sorry.”
Two words. Enough to empty a man.
“The girl didn’t make it.”
For a moment, I do not understand him.
Or perhaps I understand too quickly and my mind refuses the meaning.
The corridor seems to tilt slightly. The sound of the hospital recedes until all I can hear is my own breathing.
Rakiya!
The girl Rachel led me to.
The quiet, broken child whose eyes seem too old for her face. The one whose bills are paid by a father I have never seen. The one Dr. Lloyd says has only a fifty-fifty chance of waking. Gone.
Dr. Lloyd continues speaking, but his voice sounds far away. “You need to inform her family immediately.”
Her family. The word strikes me with helplessness.
“Where do I find them?” I mutter.
He looks at me.
“I don’t know them.”
The admission feels foolish and shameful. I stand here as helper, teacher, witness, and advocate – yet I do not know where to find the people who should be standing where I stand.
“But her bills have been paid,” he says.
That sentence enters the corridor like a whisper from another world.

Her bills have been paid. My mind goes immediately to Rachel. The girl of light. The girl who appeared in my office asking for help and then vanishes from the institute as though she has never been there. Her words return to me clearly. “My father will pay for her bills.”
I have looked for Rachel after that day. In classrooms. In corridors. During rehearsals. At the gate. Among students. I have not seen her again. Not once.
Now, standing before Dr. Lloyd with death announced over Rakiya, I feel the mystery of Rachel pressing against the grief.
“Where have you laid her?” I ask.
Dr. Lloyd pauses.
“She is still in the ward.”
“I want to see her.”
He hesitates, then nods. “Briefly.”
I do not wait for more permission.

Rakiya’s room is dim when I enter. The curtain is drawn halfway across the window, letting in the last pale light of evening. A drip stand stands beside the bed. The bedsheet is tucked neatly around her small frame. Medical equipment rests quietly nearby, no longer urgent, no longer fighting, no longer hopeful.
She lies still. Too still.

Her face has the strange calm of someone who has stepped beyond pain. Her lips are slightly parted. Her hands rest by her sides. The room smells faintly of spirit and the tired air of a place where hope has been exhausted.

I stand at the foot of the bed and cannot move. Death has a presence. Sometimes it is simply the absence of argument. The body stops insisting. The room stops expecting. The people left behind are the only ones still resisting.
I draw the chair beside the bed and sit. My hands tremble as I bring out my Bible. I do not know what I am doing. Not fully.
I am not a prophet. Not an apostle. Not a man with a ministry of miracles.
I am Sammy Briggs – a broken man still learning how to pray without bargaining, still carrying guilt, still afraid of dreams, still unsure why God preserves me.

But I have seen something once. I lost my sight on the road to violence. And God restored it. That memory becomes a small altar inside me. A place I return to whenever despair tells me heaven is silent.
I place the Bible on my lap and whisper a prayer. Not loud. Not impressive. “Lord, let her hear Your word.” Then I open to the account of Jairus’ daughter. I do not read quickly.
Each sentence feels like a stone placed carefully across a river I am afraid to cross.
Jairus pleading for his dying child.
The crowd pressing around Jesus.
The woman with the issue of blood reaching for the hem of His garment.
The delay.
The terrible message arriving: the child is dead.
Then the words of Jesus.
Do not be afraid.
Only believe.
I stop there. The words seem to enter the room and remain standing.
Do not be afraid.
Only believe.
I look at Rakiya. Her body does not move. I continue reading, my voice lower now, almost breaking.
Jesus entering the house.
The mourners weeping.
The unbelieving laughter.
The child lying still.
Then the hand of Christ reaching for a small cold hand.
I stare at Rakiya’s hand. For several seconds, I cannot touch it. Fear holds me back. Not fear of her. Fear of what will happen if nothing happens.
It is one thing to believe in miracles when they remain printed on a page. It is another thing to sit beside a lifeless child and ask the God of that page to step into the room.
I force my hand forward. Her fingers are cold. I flinch and pull back immediately. The coldness enters me like an answer I do not want.
I turn back to the Bible, blinking through tears.
“Talitha koum,” I whisper.
Little girl, get up.
A knock interrupts the room. I turn.
A nurse stands at the door, her expression gentle but professional. “Sir,” she says softly, “you have to leave now. We need to prepare her.”
Prepare her. The words almost break me.
I nod, but I do not stand immediately. I look again at the final lines of the passage.
The girl rises.
The people are overwhelmed.
And Jesus tells them to give her something to eat.
Something to eat.
Such an ordinary instruction after such an impossible miracle.
Food.
As if resurrection must be followed by care. As if the proof of life is not noise, not spectacle, not applause, but hunger. I close my eyes.
“Dear Father,” I whisper, “the One who restored my sight when I went blind…” My voice trembles. “I believe You can heal this child. I know it is not impossible with You.”
Tears roll down my face.
“In the name of Jesus, let her receive life. Let her spirit return to her body. Let her live.”
The prayer ends. Nothing happens. No wind enters the room. No light breaks through the ceiling. No angel appears. The hospital remains a hospital. The nurse still waits by the door.
Rakiya still lies silent.
And I sit there, ashamed of how much I hoped.
After a while, I gather the Bible against my chest and stand. My legs feel heavy.
The walk to the door seems longer than the corridor outside.
I reach for the handle. Then I hear it. Soft. Almost too soft to be real.
“Can you give me something to eat?”
My hand freezes on the door.
The nurse gasps.
I turn slowly.
Rakiya’s eyes are open.
Not wide. Not dramatic. Open.
Her gaze is weak, unfocused at first, then slowly settling on me.
“I’m hungry,” she whispers.
For a moment, I cannot move. The Bible slips from my hand onto the chair.
My knees weaken. All the air leaves my body in one broken cry. “Thank You, Jesus.”
The nurse rushes past me to the bed, calling for Dr. Lloyd. Footsteps erupt in the corridor. The quiet room suddenly becomes alive with movement, voices, urgency, disbelief.
But I hardly hear them. I move toward Rakiya and stop beside the bed, afraid to touch her and afraid not to.
“I’ll get you something to eat,” I say, my voice shaking. “Okay?”
She nods faintly. Tears blur my eyes.

The Scripture has not ended on the page. It has entered the room. And in that moment I understand something that will never leave me again: faith is not loud because it is certain. Sometimes faith is a trembling hand reaching toward death with nothing but a remembered miracle, a desperate prayer, and the name of Jesus.
Rakiya is alive.
And somewhere beyond my understanding, Rachel’s father indeed paid the bill.

chapters.title: Chapter THIRTY-NINE
content: Destiny Institute has become unusually quiet, stripped of the restless energy that usually fills its corridors. No students shouting across the staircase. No chairs dragging across classroom floors. No Tunde making mischief on the blackboard. No Eno moving quietly with her notebook pressed to her chest. No Jumoke arguing over rehearsal notes. No Patrick pretending not to care whether he sings well.
Only silence. The kind of silence that follows departure.

I sit in my small office checking workers’ reports, ensuring that the classrooms are locked properly, the registers submitted, and the final notes prepared for Mrs. Animashaun before the holidays begin. Outside the window, the compound looks tired but peaceful under the afternoon sun. The year is ending.
And for the first time in a long while, I am still alive to see one end. That thought unsettles me. A knock comes at the door.
The security man opens it halfway and leans in. “Sir, a dispatch rider is at the gate. He has a parcel for you.”
“For me?”
“Yes, sir.”
“Please receive it on my behalf.”

He returns a few minutes later carrying a neatly wrapped parcel. I recognize Lizzy’s handwriting before I open it. Something moves inside me. Not excitement exactly. Something softer. Something I have not allowed myself to feel for too long.
The parcel contains a Christmas card and a small box. Inside the box is a tie, carefully rolled and held in place with soft paper. I stare at it. A tie.
“Why is Lizzy sending me a tie?” The question makes me smile before I can stop myself. Then I open the card.

Her words are written with the same careful grace with which she speaks. She wishes me a merry Christmas. She tells me she has heard about the miracle God used me to witness. She thanks me for yielding to the Master’s call. She writes that although I may count my lost school years as wasted, God will restore what I have lost and establish me in His own time.
Then comes the line that makes me laugh quietly. The tie, she says, is her first gift to the man of God.
“Man of God,” I whisper, shaking my head.
The words sound too large for me. Too clean. Too far from the boy who once answers to Lethal Weapon. Yet as I hold the tie in my hand, memory opens.

I see myself in the departure hall with Bitty and Lizzy, afraid of what will become of me after he leaves. I see Bitty warning me not to let loneliness call me back to the fraternity. I see the Cross standing upright on Ikenna’s grave, wet earth around it, silence heavy over the cemetery. I see Steel’s face when he demands I reverse it. I see Legion dying with his hand in mine. I see myself in Ogbomosho, broken outside a beer parlour while Lizzy weeps because she has found me alive but almost unrecognizable.
And now this. A tie. A Christmas card. A woman far away still choosing to call me by what God may make of me rather than what darkness has already made me.
I place the card on the table and sit quietly.
I miss her.
The realization comes gently, then painfully. “I should call her,” I whisper. “Tomorrow.”
If she has not travelled for the holidays, perhaps she can come. Not only because I want to see her. Because I need help.
Rakiya has become a question I can no longer answer alone.

Since her discharge from the hospital, I have been thinking of how to manage her situation. I cannot force her back to her family. Not after hearing her story. Not after listening to the quiet terror beneath her words.

She fled from home because her family arranged for her to marry a man old enough to be her father. A family friend, they call him, as if familiarity can make bondage respectable. She pleaded to be allowed to continue school. She wants to study. She wants a life large enough to contain the strange images that fill her mind.

But they did not listen. To them, her drawings are not a gift. They are evidence. A curse. Possession. A demonic strangeness that must be silenced by marriage, maturity, obedience, and the authority of a man who will “settle” her.

I think of my own childhood then. The way people stare at me because my foster parents are white. The way difference makes a child feel guilty for existing. The way adults sometimes explain what they cannot understand by calling it evil.
Perhaps Rakiya and I are not the same. But I understand what it means to be misread by those who should protect you.
Nobody taught her to draw. That is what she keeps saying. Nobody. No art school. No formal training. No studio apprenticeship.

Her gift grows wild inside her, like a tree in abandoned ground bending toward light nobody else can see. She tells me images come to her. Objects. Faces. Symbols. Movements of light. Doors. Children. Birds. Crowns. Burning skies. Strange landscapes whose meanings she cannot explain.

She does not always know what she is painting. Only that something moves through her hand when the images come. The first time she said it, I remembered Ikenna’s memoir. The strange symbols. The Seagull. The Archer. The Key.

The matchstick image that binds me to men whose destinies I still do not understand. Back then, I thought drawings are only drawings. Now I know better. Some images are not decorations. They are messages waiting for language.

Rakiya told me she cannot remember how she came to Lagos. She does not know anyone called Rachel. When I mentioned the girl who led me to her, she only stared blankly, as though Rachel belongs to a realm her memory cannot reach.

That troubles me. Rachel appears, points me toward Rakiya, promises that her father will pay the bills, and vanishes. And the bill was paid. She entered my office in sunlight and left behind a miracle.

Since Rakiya came home with me, she paints almost without rest. On the way back from the hospital, she asked for art equipment and paints. Not food first. Not clothes. Not even sleep. Paints. Brushes. Paper. Canvas.

She asked with the urgency of someone who has returned from death carrying images that needed to escape before they burn through her. I bought what I could afford. By evening, she was already working.

At first, her drawings were dark and crowded, filled with shadows, faceless people, closed gates, children walking through mist, birds falling from the sky, women with covered mouths, and roads that seem to lead nowhere. But after we began studying Scripture together, something changes. The images became clearer. The darkness remained in some of them, but light began to appear with intention.

Not decoration. Direction. She draws a crown hovering above children. A river running through dry bones. A lamb standing before a gate of iron. A girl walking out of fire holding a broken chain. I do not understand everything. But I recognize the atmosphere. Kingdom. Redemption. Rescue. Witness.
When I told her that she died in the hospital and that Jesus raised her back to life, she became very still. Then she said something that makes my skin rise.
“He is the One.”
“What do you mean?”
“The One who shows me most of the things I draw.” After that, she wanted to know Him.

Every day, I teach her from Scripture. Not as a pastor. Not as a scholar. As a man who is himself still learning how to follow. We read slowly. Jesus healing. Jesus forgiving. Jesus calling children. Jesus touching the rejected. Jesus raising the dead. Jesus asking frightened people to believe.
Yesterday, Rakiya prayed. Not loudly. Not dramatically.

She simply bows her head, repeats the words with tears running down her face, and receives Jesus as Lord. I watched her afterward. The way peace slowly entered her expression. The way her shoulders loosen. The way she returned to her canvas and painted a door opening into dawn. That image stays with me. A door opening into dawn.
Perhaps that is what salvation feels like to a child who has known too many closed rooms. Still, the practical problem remains.

It is not right for her to continue living with me. Even though I have no immoral intentions, wisdom must protect what innocence cannot defend. She is young. Vulnerable. Recovering. Gifted. And I know the cruelty of rumour too well to pretend the world will interpret mercy correctly.
