Neighbours have already begun asking questions.
“Who is she?”
“How is she related to you?”
“When is she leaving?” I tell them she is a relative.

The lie is small, but small lies have short legs. Soon someone will discover we are not related. Then whispers will begin. They will not ask how she is rescued, or what she has survived, or why she has nowhere else to go.
They will invent the version that makes them feel wise. A young man living with a minor. A teacher and a student. A fallen girl. A hidden affair.
I have seen how quickly people turn suffering into gossip. I will not expose Rakiya to that.
Lizzy can help. She has to.
I return home in the evening with Lizzy’s card still folded in my pocket.

Rakiya does not notice when I enter. She is sitting by the small table near the window, bent over a canvas, her left hand moving with astonishing speed and delicacy. The room smells of acrylic paint, kerosene from the stove, and the faint scent of harmattan dust drifting through the shutters. Her hair is tied loosely behind her head. Paint marks her fingers, her wrist, and one side of her cheek.
The girl has disappeared into the work. I stand behind her for a while, watching. The painting is unlike the others.
A wide field. A crowd of young people. Some carrying books. Some carrying tools. Some carrying instruments. Some carrying broken chains.

Above them is a great sky, deep blue and silver, with light breaking through like music. At the centre of the painting, a path divides the field, and at the end of the path stands a throne made not of gold, but of fire and water.
I cannot make out the full story. But it stirs something in me. The remnant. The word comes before I can reason it. The remnant generation.

I have been thinking about my work at the Centre, trying to connect the strange pieces God seems to be placing before me. Eno restored from shame. Tunde’s gift needing discipline. Rakiya raised from death with visions in her hands. Rachel appearing and vanishing like a messenger. Students carrying raw talents that can either be nurtured or exploited.

It aligns with what God has begun revealing to me. A generation hidden inside broken systems. A generation gifted but vulnerable. A generation the world will either corrupt, commercialize, silence, or use. Rakiya is proof. Not theory. Proof.
“So,” I say softly, “what are you drawing?”
She startles and turns sharply. “Oh! Uncle Sam, you’re back.”
“I have been standing here for the past one hour.”
She frowns. “That is not true. I just came out from the room like ten minutes ago.”
“Okay. Twenty minutes.”
She gives me a disapproving grimace.
The expression makes her look like a child again, and for that I am grateful. I glance toward the kitchen corner.
“And I am sure you haven’t had breakfast.”

Her face falls. That is answer enough. I do not scold her. Caring for Rakiya teaches how not to command, correct, and impose. But a gentler discipline. She is not lazy. She is consumed. Her gift does not ask politely for attention; it seizes her.

So, I go to the kitchen and make us a meal. It is my responsibility, and strangely, I do not resent it. What she is doing feels like ministry, though she does not yet have language for it. God has placed her under my care for a season, and my task is to protect the vessel while He shapes the gift.
When the food is ready, I set the plates on the table. She reluctantly leaves the canvas and joins me.

She eats slowly at first, then with the appetite of someone who has forgotten she is hungry. I watch her as I would watch a daughter. The thought surprises me, then warms me, then frightens me. A daughter.
I have never imagined myself fathering anything except fear.
After the meal, she wants to return to the painting immediately. I refuse. She protests. I remind her that even prophets sleep. That makes her laugh. Eventually, she goes to bed.
I stand at the doorway until her breathing settles. Only then do I return to the sitting room.
The couch has become my bed. I lie there staring at the ceiling, Lizzy’s card on the table, the tie beside it, Rakiya’s unfinished painting leaning against the wall.

Tomorrow, Lizzy will come. I will ask her to help me decide what to do. Perhaps she can take Rakiya somewhere safer. Perhaps she can reach someone trustworthy. Perhaps together we can protect the girl before the world misunderstands the rescue.

But as I lie there, another memory opens. The university. The hostel corridors. Ugo’s ambition. Legion’s hunger for mystery. All those young lives, each one carrying a gift that has been bent toward darkness. I think of boys like Tunde, impatient to be discovered. Girls like Eno, deceived by affection dressed as love.
Children like Rakiya, misunderstood by family and hunted by systems that know how to profit from unusual gifts.

The thought chills me. Merchants of the ivory towers do not sell books. They sell access. They prey on hunger. Ambition. Loneliness. Shame. Talent. Desire. The secret ache of every young person to be seen.
Sometimes they come as sponsors. Mentors. Patrons. Recruiters, and even prophets. They study the wound before offering the promise. They name the hunger before selling the trap.

And suddenly I understand why God does not allow me to die in Ogbomosho. I am not rescued merely to survive. I am rescued to remember. To remember how fear is dressed as family and exploitation as opportunity. To remember so I can warn others before their gifts are bought, bent, and buried.
I turn my head toward Rakiya’s painting. The blue and silver sky seems to glow faintly in the dim room.
Tomorrow Lizzy will come. And perhaps, with her arrival, another path of the road will open.

chapters.title: Chapter FORTY
content: Since the miracle at Dr. Lloyd’s hospital, I have come to regard Rakiya’s presence in my life as both a mercy and a responsibility. She is accompanying me to the motor park to pick up Lizzy. I initially did not want to bring her along. Not because I am ashamed of her. A girl like Rakiya does not know how to disappear. Even when she is quiet, something about her invites notice.
Perhaps it is the way she observes things too carefully, as though every colour, shape, movement, and shadow belongs to a language only she can read. But going out with her unsettles me.

The motor park is an open yard off the major road, edged with shops built mostly from corrugated roofing sheets and uneven timber frames. Some sell soft drinks, sachet water, biscuits, and cigarettes. Others sell food in large aluminum pots darkened by years of firewood smoke. A woman stirs beans near a smoky stove. A boy carries passengers’ luggage on his head. Conductors lean against buses, calling destinations in tired, musical repetition.
“Lagos! Lagos remaining two!”
“Ibadan! One chance!”
“Ogbomosho inside town!”
Passengers argue over change, search for missing bags, shout at conductors, and greet relatives with the relief of people who have survived Nigerian roads again.

When I first arrived in Ogbomosho, the bus had dropped us near the highway side of the park. The smaller section closer to the road seemed reserved for vehicles commuting passengers into the town itself. Now I stand near that same section, scanning faces the way a hunted man scans doors.
Rakiya stands beside me, holding a small sketchpad to her chest. She has brought it without asking.

She always carries something to draw on. If there is no sketchpad, she uses receipts. If there is no paper, she traces shapes on her palm with her finger. Her left hand moves often, almost unconsciously, as if some invisible line in the air is waiting for her to follow it.
“Uncle Sam,” she says quietly, “that woman’s wrapper has river colours.”
I follow her gaze.
A woman near a kiosk is wearing a blue and silver Ankara wrapper.
“It is just cloth,” I say.
Rakiya tilts her head. “No. It is moving.”
I look again. The cloth flutters in the wind. To me, it is fabric.
To her, it is water. I say nothing.

After about twenty minutes, Lizzy’s bus finally rolls into the park, tired and dust-covered from the journey. Passengers begin adjusting themselves even before the vehicle stops. Hands reach for bags. The conductor jumps down and begins untying luggage from the back.
Lizzy alights carefully, one hand holding her bag, the other shielding her face slightly from the sun. For a moment, I forget to scan the park.

She is dressed in a cinnamon-brown sleeveless jumpsuit with loose cropped legs that move softly around her ankles. Black flat loafers give her look a simplicity that makes it more elegant. Her hair is neatly styled, her face fresh from the journey but still composed. Lizzy has a way of dressing that never calls attention to itself loudly, yet somehow gathers the eye and keeps it there.

Lizzy has the kind of face that does not reveal itself all at once. At first, one notices the calmness. Then the light in her eyes. Then the firmness around her mouth, the quiet intelligence in her expression, the delicate confidence of a woman who has been broken in places but not cheapened by pain.
She sights me and walks toward us with measured steps, her shoulders relaxed, her gaze steady, her presence clean in the midst of the confusion and chaos of the motor park.
Seeing her again pulls me back to the university in a painful flash.

The fellowship walkway. Her Bible pressed to her chest. Her calm rebuke when I spoke foolishly. Her frightened face on the night Ugo invaded Ikenna’s apartment. Her tears outside the beer parlour when she found what Ogbomosho had reduced me to.
Lizzy has witnessed too many versions of me. And still she comes.
“Hello, Lizzy,” I say when we reach her.
“Hi, Sammy.”
Rakiya steps forward.
“Good afternoon, Aunty Lizzy.”
Lizzy’s face softens immediately. “Oh, baby, no aunty. Lizzy is okay. How are you?”
“I am fine.” Rakiya studies her outfit with open admiration. “I like your clothes.”
Lizzy laughs. “Thank you. I admire your honesty.”
Rakiya smiles briefly, then becomes quiet again.
“How was your trip?” I ask.
“Very fine,” Lizzy says. “It took a while for the bus to get filled up. You know how it is. But I’m here.”
As she speaks, I scan the park.

Two men near a drink shop. A driver arguing with a passenger. A boy selling recharge cards. An elderly woman sitting on a wooden bench. Nothing unusual. Still, fear has a way of finding patterns even where none exist.

Passengers in the rear seats in the bus are delayed from leaving the bus because the conductor is struggling with luggage. Two young men at the back wait impatiently, craning their necks as bags are removed one after another.
I look away. “So, shall we?” I say. “I want us to have a meal first, maybe at an eatery along the way.”
“No,” Lizzy protests quickly. “I can’t wait to see Rakiya’s artworks. Let’s go home.”
Rakiya lowers her eyes, uncomfortable with the attention.
“In that case,” I say, “we’ll get take-away.”
“Okay, if you really want to bother. I’m not hungry.”
“You travelled from school to Ogbomosho and you are not hungry?”
She smiles. “I said I’m not hungry, not that I won’t eat.”
That sounds like Lizzy.

We stop at Aunty Anjola’s Kitchen on the way. It is a much-talked-about restaurant in Ogbomosho, a one-storey building with wide glass walls that make the interior visible from outside. I have passed it many times but have never entered. From the road, it always looks too clean for my pocket, too bright for the kind of life I have been living.

Inside, the restaurant is busy, warm, and fragrant. Food sits displayed behind glass shelves at the counter: jollof rice, fried rice, white rice, stews, fried plantain, chicken, fish, moi-moi, pastries, and small chops arranged in neat trays. Customers move in and out almost every minute. Plates clatter. A microwave beeps. Someone laughs too loudly near the far wall.
I lead Lizzy and Rakiya to the counter. Rakiya chooses a snack. Lizzy orders plantain and chicken. I turn and scan the room.

Two young men enter. One is wearing an oversized black polo shirt; the other wears a blue Chelsea jersey and a fez cap. Instead of approaching the counter, they walk to a table and sit, laughing together as though they have come only to gist. Maybe they are ordinary boys. Maybe not.
“Sammy,” Lizzy calls.
I turn too quickly. “Yes. Jollof rice, plantain and chicken.”
The waiter nods.
“You will wait for us to microwave it.”
“No,” I say. “Just dish it like that.”
Lizzy’s eyes remain on me.
She has noticed. Of course she does.

When our food is ready, we move toward the exit. I glance once more at the boys. They are still seated, still talking, still laughing. Nothing in their posture suggests interest in me. But fear does not require evidence to begin its work.

I try to relax as we board a tricycle to my apartment. When we alight near my street, I pay the rider and take a few seconds to inspect the area. The street is quiet, mostly deserted at this hour, with low buildings set back from the road, patches of dry grass, and a narrow footpath leading toward my compound. A goat noses through a pile of leaves near a fence. Two children play with bottle covers under a tree. No strangers standing around. No parked vehicle waits suspiciously.
Maybe I am only anxious. Maybe exile has made me suspicious of peace.
Rakiya’s excitement returns the moment we enter the house.

She leads Lizzy to the corner where her paintings are arranged against the wall, some on cardboard, some on cheap canvas, some on pieces of board I begged a carpenter to give me. There is no studio, no easel, no proper storage, no formal order. Yet the works possess an unsettling authority, as if the room itself has become too small for what they carry.
Lizzy stops before them. For several minutes she does not speak. People often praise too quickly when they want to be kind. True astonishment begins in silence. She picks up one canvas carefully.

It shows a tomb, but not as one might see in children’s Bible illustrations. This tomb seems to breathe. The stone has been rolled aside, and from the dark mouth of the grave comes a visible wind, almost human in movement, rising into light. The brushwork is not technically perfect, yet the emotion is precise. The lines slant with unusual energy. The darker tones curve away from the source of light as though afraid of it.
“What is this one?” Lizzy asks softly.
“Free Spirit,” Rakiya replies.
“Do you know what it means?”
Rakiya nods.
“It is the spirit of Jesus leaving the grave after He was crucified.”
Lizzy turns slowly to me.
The look on her face asks more than one question. I answer none of them.
She examines the painting again, this time closer. “Where did you learn to draw?”
“I didn’t,” Rakiya says.
The answer is immediate. Unembellished. No pride. No apology.
Lizzy looks at her. “Nobody taught you?”
Rakiya shakes her head. “I just see things.”
Her left hand moves as she speaks, unconsciously tracing a small curve against her skirt.
“I draw them before they leave.”
Lizzy notices the hand. I notice Lizzy noticing it.
That is how Lizzy is. She does not merely hear words; she collects details. A glance. A pause. A repeated movement. A silence where a full answer should have been.
“She was taught by the Holy Ghost,” I say.
Lizzy does not dispute it. She picks up another painting. Then another.
A field of children under a divided sky.
A crown above a broken chain.
A narrow path through water.
A woman with covered eyes holding a lamp.

The paintings are not polished in the academic sense. Some proportions shift. Some perspectives bend strangely. Some backgrounds remain unfinished. But none of the works feels careless. The imperfections do not weaken them. They make them alive. They feel discovered rather than taught.
“These are very strong works,” Lizzy says.
Rakiya looks down.
“They don’t look amateurish, Sammy.”
I nod.
“But they don’t look schooled either,” Lizzy adds, more to herself than to us.
I look at her.
“What do you mean?”
She runs her fingers near the edge of a canvas without touching the paint. “There’s something raw about them. Untamed. Like she didn’t learn the rules first. She found her own way into them.”
Rakiya smiles faintly, though I doubt she understands the compliment.
Lizzy turns one canvas over. “She’s not signing them.”
“She doesn’t like to.”
Rakiya’s face closes slightly. “I don’t want people to know.” The answer is simple. Too simple. Fear hides inside it.
Lizzy crouches before her. “Baby girl, you don’t have to write your full name. But you should put a mark on your work. Even if it is simply the letter R.”
“Yes. Something that says this came from you.”
Rakiya thinks about it. Then she nods slowly. Lizzy presses her palms to her cheeks and exhales.
“You are gifted.”
“She is more than that,” I respond
Lizzy looks at me. Not disagreeing. Waiting.
“Come,” I say. “Let’s go to the table. There is something I need to share with you.”
I turn to Rakiya. “Come and eat.”
“I’m not hungry now.”
“You are always not hungry when you are painting.”
She makes a small face.
Lizzy smiles. “Let her be. She will eat later.”

We move into the sitting room and sit around the centre table that serves as dining table, reading desk, ironing board, prayer desk, and everything else a table can be. I unpack the food, but Lizzy does not begin eating immediately.
“It’s because of the girl you wanted to see me,” she says.
I stop. “How did you know?”
“It’s obvious, Sammy.”
That is Lizzy again. She does not need a confession when the evidence has already arranged itself before her.
“I will help,” she says. “But I am travelling to spend Christmas with Judith. When I return, I’ll come and take her. Is that okay?”
Relief moves through me quietly. “Yes. That works.”
“How long will she stay before going back to her family?”
“She doesn’t want to go back.”
Lizzy’s expression changes.

I tell her everything I can. Not hurriedly. Not as gossip. As responsibility. I tell her about her family. About the arranged marriage to her father’s friend. About her parents interpreting her drawings as possession. About her flight. About Rachel. About the hospital. About the miracle. About the way Rakiya’s paintings have become clearer since Scripture entered her life.
Lizzy listens without interrupting. But her face moves through many emotions. Shock. Anger. Pity. Wonder. Concern.
When I finish, I say, “She is one of the elect of the remnant generation.”
Lizzy stares at me. “What does that mean?”
I look toward the corner where Rakiya is quietly arranging her paintings again.
Then I lower my voice. “Beyond arranging shelter for her, there is something I need to tell you. A secret I was forbidden to reveal.”
Lizzy’s attention sharpens. I look at her directly.
“Not even Steel knows this.”
Her eyes narrow.
“You remember the night Bitty was leaving, when I asked him about the symbols in the former Hangman’s memoir?”
“Yes. But I don’t know what you people discussed.”
“I wanted to know if he was part of a covert Program the GODs had conscripted me to execute on campus.”
Lizzy leans forward. “A Program?”
“Not the regular cult activities.”
“I’m listening.”
There is impatience in her voice. Not rude impatience. The impatience of a daring mind that hates being kept outside locked doors.
“The real reason I fled school was not just the cross on the Hangman’s grave,” I say. “It was also the election.”
I tell her about Otunba’s money. About Legion collecting it. About my refusal. About Legion’s death. About the conspiracy to hang the debt on me.
“One of us had collected…”
“One of them,” Lizzy corrects immediately. “You are no longer a cult member.”
The correction strikes me gently. I nod.

“One of the fraternity members collected the money. It was supposed to be paid to me to run the election process, but I refused. He did not return it. After he was killed, the others told Otunba I received it.”
“Legion?”
Lizzy’s face hardens. “And Steel is part of it?”
“I suspect he was.”
“He started buying things after that election,” she says quietly. “New shoes. A chain. Drinks. He was suddenly spending.”
I watch her.
“Steel is such a betrayer.”
“It is the way of the cult,” I say. “No one trusts anyone.”
Then I tell her what matters.
“They did not understand why I was handpicked for that election project. I tried to protect Legion, but I could not tell him the real reason. I was forbidden.”
Lizzy’s voice softens. “If you do not think I should know, don’t tell me.”
“No. You need to know now.”
The room seems to close around us.
Outside, Rakiya’s brush moves faintly against paper.

I continue. “Apart from running a system that makes young people fight and kill one another for no reason, there is another Program. It was designed to use the fraternity to harvest the intellect of vulnerable young people.”
Lizzy’s face goes still.

“The Program is encoded in the Fraternity’s Testament by the first Hangman, the Seagull. But he did not initiate the course before his fall. Because of the vacuum, no one was found to carry it out until I was ordained by the GODs, with Otunba, to initiate the system on campus.”
“Otunba was to occupy governance,” Lizzy says slowly.
“And I was to oversee recruitment.”
Her mouth opens slightly. “That was why they killed Hugo Boss?”
“It is a long story. But yes… partly. He was seeking something connected to it.”
The food sits untouched. The room feels warmer.

I lean back, choosing my words carefully. “The GODs have an oracle that directs the spiritual affairs of the Order. I think satan had foreknowledge of what the Lord ordained me to become and inspired the oracle to conscript me into a counterfeit system.”
Lizzy says nothing.
“Rakiya’s coming has helped me understand what God has been revealing.”
“What does Rakiya have to do with this?”

I look toward the doorway. She is seated on the floor now, sketchpad open, her left hand moving with quick, instinctive certainty. She is not listening to us. Or if she is, she gives no sign. Her face has entered that faraway concentration I have learned not to interrupt.

“I received a revelation that we experience life in three realms of reality,” I say. “At first, I thought I was hallucinating, knowing we live in a 2 dimensional realm, the spiritual and the physical. But the Spirit showed me another realm that would be created by man’s ingenuity, a virtual realm that would mimic the spiritual in form.”
Lizzy’s brows draw together. “A virtual experience?”
“Yes. So real that many people will prefer it to spiritual encounters.”
I can see she is trying to follow.

“But the Spirit also revealed that there is a remnant who will be endowed with wisdom for creative works directed by God’s word. I did not understand it until I began working with the students at the Centre. Then Rakiya came.”
I lower my voice. “She is a sign. The firstfruit of the remnant generation.”
Lizzy turns slightly and looks toward her.
Rakiya continues drawing.
The soft scratch of pencil fills the silence.

“The Program is codenamed Merchants of the Ivory Towers,” I say. “It is the counterfeit. I was ordained to replace God’s purpose with a system that lures unsuspecting youths into a platform that promises to help them fulfil their dreams.”
“But exploits them,” Lizzy completes my statement.
“Yes. Takes earnings. Controls exposure. Owns access. But it is more than that.” I swallow. “It is a harvest of souls.”
Lizzy’s expression darkens.
“Once these emerging talents are drafted in, they mortgage their consciousness for fame and riches. They enter a system that rewards visibility but captures identity.”
“And there is no way out,” she says.
“That is how it is designed.”
For a while, neither of us speaks.

The revelation sounds impossible even to me, yet every fragment of my life has begun arranging itself around it. The fraternity. The university. Politics. The students. Tunde’s fake contract. Eno’s brokenness. Rakiya’s gift. Rachel’s appearance. The miracle.
A generation of young people hungry to be seen. And merchants waiting at the gates of their hunger.
“I knew these things were true when I saw Rakiya’s works,” I say. “And the inspiration behind them.”
Lizzy looks at the painting of the tomb again. The one Rakiya calls Free Spirit.
“She doesn’t paint like someone trying to impress people,” Lizzy says quietly. “She paints like someone trying to remember what she saw.”
The sentence strikes me. “Yes.”
“And she uses her left hand?” Lizzy sounds emotional.
I nod. “She does everything with it.”
Lizzy absorbs that detail.

“I have a foreboding the GODs will soon find me,” I say. “Before that happens, I want to be sure Rakiya is safe. She is not mature enough to face the kind of contention that may come because of her gift.”
Lizzy’s gaze remains on the girl. “You want me to disciple her.”
“You are the only one I trust.”
She takes a slow breath.
“Sammy, I have been in this fight since I came to faith through Jshap’s ministry. When I return from Judith’s place, I will come and take her.”
Relief loosens something inside me.
“Thank you.”
She looks back at me.
“But you also need to be careful.”
“I know.”
“No, Sammy. You know danger. That is not the same thing as accepting help.”
I lower my eyes. She lets the words settle, then deliberately changes the tone.
“So,” she says, finally opening her food, “tell me about your work at the Study Centre.”

For the first time that afternoon, I smile. I tell her about the students. About Eno’s recovery. About Tunde’s stubborn talent. About Miss Harrison and the Christmas concert. About Jumoke’s pride, Patrick’s baritone, Genevieve’s seriousness, Oluchi’s loyalty, and the slow miracle of seeing young people begin to believe that their lives still hold meaning.
As I speak, Rakiya sits quietly near the window. Her pencil moves over paper. Left hand swift. Eyes distant. Face calm. She is not the center of our conversation. Not yet.

chapters.title: Chapter FORTY-ONE
content: The rumbling of the sky interrupts our conversation. The sound rolls slowly above the roof, deep and distant, as though the heavens have dragged a heavy stone across the sky. It is December.

Rain should not be announcing itself with such confidence. But nature has never needed human permission to break pattern. A cool wind pushes through the half-open window, lifting the edges of Rakiya’s loose sketches on the table. One sheet slides toward the floor. She catches it quickly with her left hand and places it beneath a paint-stained book.
Lizzy looks toward the window. “I should start going.”
“You haven’t eaten much,” I say.
“I know.” She closes the food pack gently. “But if the rain starts, it may be hard to get a vehicle.”
I want to ask her to stay longer. Not because it is wise. Because her presence steadies the room.
Since she entered my life, Lizzy has had a way of making dangerous things feel discussable. She does not remove fear. She simply stands near it long enough for a man to remember that fear is not God.
“Let’s pray before I go,” she says.
Rakiya comes closer immediately.
The three of us stand in my small sitting room, surrounded by take-away packs, unfinished paintings, drying brushes, Lizzy’s bag, and the heaviness of everything I have revealed.
Lizzy prays first.

She pours her heart out quietly, not with theatrical force, but with the kind of reverence that makes the room attentive. She prays for Rakiya’s protection. For my safety. For wisdom. For the will of God to prevail over the schemes of darkness. She prays that no counterfeit system will swallow the young people God has marked for His purpose.
As she mentions the remnant generation, Rakiya lifts her face slightly. She does not fully understand. But perhaps some part of her spirit does.
When Lizzy finishes, we all say amen.
For a moment, the room is still. Then Lizzy turns to Rakiya and hugs her. Not lightly. Not politely. She holds her with the tenderness of someone who has just been given custody of a fragile secret.
Rakiya stiffens at first, then slowly relaxes into the embrace.
Lizzy strokes her hair gently. “Would you like to come and live with me?”
Rakiya’s eyes brighten. She turns to me quickly, searching my face for permission.
I nod.
The smile that breaks across her face is almost childlike. “Yes,” she says. “Yes.”
Lizzy laughs softly and hugs her again. “Give me a few days, okay? I’ll return from my scheduled holiday trip, and then I’ll come and take you to my house.”
“Okay, Aunty.”
“Lizzy,” she corrects gently.
Rakiya smiles. “Okay… Lizzy.”
“And don’t stop painting. When I return, we’ll talk about school and everything else.”
“Thank you.”
Lizzy lifts her bag.
At the door, Rakiya waves. “Bye.”
“Bye, baby girl.”
The word lingers. Baby girl.
A phrase simple enough to sound ordinary, but in Rakiya’s face I see what it does. It gives her something her family had taken away. Tenderness without suspicion.
I walk Lizzy to the end of my street.

The air outside has changed. The dry harmattan smell has been softened by the promise of rain. Clouds gather low and heavy above the quiet road. The street is almost deserted, the houses withdrawn behind low fences and rusted gates. A goat noses at dry leaves near a gutter. A woman in the distance hurries to remove clothes from a line.
Lizzy walks beside me without speaking at first. She knows me well enough not to fill silence carelessly.
At the end of the street, we wait near the bend where tricycles sometimes pass on their way toward the main road. I scan the surroundings by habit. Left. Right. Across the road. Behind us.
A parked motorcycle near a kiosk. Two boys playing with bottle covers under a tree. An elderly man carrying a basin. Nothing unusual.
Still, unease moves beneath my skin. Lizzy notices.
“You don’t have to escort me to the motor park,” she says.
“I should.”
“No.” Her tone is gentle, but firm.
“You should go back.”
I look at her. “Lizzy –”
“I’ll be fine.” She studies my face. “You are more worried about being followed than about me finding my way.”
I do not answer. That is answer enough.

Lizzy has been around hard men long enough to read the small language of danger. The sudden glance over the shoulder, the pause before turning a corner, the quick scan of unknown faces, the refusal to stand with one’s back exposed. She knows I have been watching since she arrived.
A tricycle approaches, its engine rattling loudly. Lizzy waves it down.
The driver slows.
“Park?” she asks.
The driver nods.
She turns to me. “Call me tomorrow.”
“I will.”
“And Sammy…”
I look at her.
“Do not carry everything alone.”
The words strike deeper than she knows.
I nod.
She enters the tricycle.

For a moment, before the vehicle moves, she looks back at me. There is concern in her eyes, but also trust. She believes I will return to the house. She believes Rakiya is safe for the night. She believes this farewell is temporary.
I want to believe it too.
The tricycle throttles forward and rolls away down the road, its small body shaking over potholes. I stand there watching until it blends into the movement of the main road and disappears from sight.

Only then do I allow myself to breathe. A brief, foolish thought crosses my mind. What would my life have become without Lizzy? Without her, I might have returned to the fraternity. Without her, I might have died in Ogbomosho before anyone knew my name. Without her, Rakiya would still be alone. I turn to go back.
Then I see them.
Across the road, near the corner of a closed provision shop, two young men step out from behind a parked bus.

One is wearing an oversized black polo shirt. The other a blue Chelsea jersey and a face cap. My body recognizes them before my mind finishes the work. Aunty Anjola’s Kitchen. The two boys who entered after us. The ones who sat without ordering. The ones I told myself were only talking and laughing. They are no longer laughing.
They cross the road quickly, not running, but moving with the certainty of men who have been waiting for the right moment.
My heartbeat changes. Not faster. Lower. Heavier. The kind of heartbeat that comes when the body understands it has entered danger before the mind starts negotiating.
I step back. A motorcyclist passes between us, briefly blocking my view.
When he clears, they are closer. Too close. The one in the Chelsea jersey looks past me. Not at me. Behind me.
I turn.
Two policemen are coming from the opposite direction.
They are not strolling. They are not patrolling. Their eyes are fixed on me.

One holds a folded paper. The other’s hand rests near his baton. The road suddenly narrows. The houses retreat. The clouds darken. For one second, I consider running. The thought arrives instinctively, like an old reflex. But where? Toward the two boys? Toward the policemen? Back to the house where Rakiya is alone?
No. If I run, I lead them to her. If I fight, I become the criminal they have been told to expect. If I shout, the street will gather, and the story will no longer belong to truth.
So, I stand still.
The policemen reach me first. “Samuel Briggs?” one asks. His voice is official, but not uncertain.
They already know.
I look from him to the two boys now standing a few paces away. The boy in the black polo smiles faintly. Not triumphantly. Professionally. As though delivery has been made.
“Are you Samuel Briggs?” the policeman repeats.
I swallow. “Yes.”
The second officer unfolds the paper. A warrant. “You are required to come with us.”
“For what?”
The first officer steps closer. “There is a petition against you. Fraud. Conspiracy. Absconding with political funds.”
Otunba!
The name does not need to be spoken. My legs feel suddenly hollow. So, this is how the debt arrives. Not with the GODs in ceremonial darkness.
With policemen on a quiet Ogbomosho street and two boys from a restaurant confirming my identity.
“Officer, I don’t know anything about that money.”
“You will explain at the station.”
The sky rumbles again. Closer now. A wind passes through the street, lifting dust and dry leaves into a brief restless spiral. I glance toward my compound.
Rakiya is inside. Painting. Waiting for me to return with ordinary news.
Lizzy is gone.
And I understand, with a coldness that spreads through my chest, that she has led them to me unknowingly. Not by betrayal. By love. They must have followed her. Or watched the motor park.
I do not know. It does not matter. The net has closed.
The policeman reaches for my arms.
I do not resist.

The touch is firm, not brutal, but it carries the weight of an entire machinery – politics, accusation, fraternity, police, money, fear. Everything I fled has found legal clothing and placed its hand on me.
The boy in the Chelsea jersey speaks for the first time. “That’s him.”
The words are simple. Final. A light drizzle begins.

Thin drops strike the dust, darkening the road in scattered spots. I look once more toward my street. Toward the small room where Rakiya’s unsigned paintings lean against the wall. Toward the table where Lizzy had prayed. Toward the life I was just beginning to rebuild.
Then I lower my eyes. I know now. My time is up. The GODs have come for me.
They have come with Otunba’s petition, policemen in daylight, and two ordinary-looking boys who learned my face over rice and plantain. As they lead me away, the rain begins to fall harder.
And somewhere behind me, in a room full of paintings, the future I was trying to protect continues drawing without knowing that the door has just been left unguarded.

books.title: Rhapsodies of the Coming Regent
books.author: Lizzy
books.blurb: Through Rakiya’s prophetic art, Lizzy uncovers the final mystery that turns grief into revelation and darkness into hope.
books.summary:
She thought she was only keeping faith with the memory of the man she loved.

But when Sammy Briggs is arrested, Rakiya disappears, and the paintings of an untrained girl begin to speak with the language of prophecy, Elizabeth finds herself drawn into the unfinished purpose of Ikenna Obiajulu’s death. Once known on campus for charm, beauty, and controversy, Lizzy has become a witness of the Cross she once scarcely understood.

Carrying the burden of three disciples of the Inverted Cross, she traces the hidden merchants who traffic not only in bodies, art, influence, and money — but in destinies. To rescue the girl, save Sammy, and expose the system, Ifeanyi must betray the man who once sheltered him, confront the guilt of leading Ikenna into Dike’s house, and return to the father he abandoned.

And through Rakiya’s prophetic paintings, the story finally rises into its true crescendo: The Regent is coming. And the Cross that darkness tried to invert has become the way home.

chapters.title: Chapter ONE
content: My holiday trip with Judith came as a surprise and a prayer answered. She wants my company in her big family house during the hols. She complained she’s mostly lonely because her siblings are out of the country. Her invitation was an answered prayer because I had been longing for some distance and distraction from all that is happening around me.

The semester had ended with too much weight on my heart. Bitty’s departure, Sammy’s sudden disappearance from school, the rumours around Otunba’s missing election money, and then my journey to Ogbomosho, where I found Sammy living like a man whom life had stripped almost beyond recognition.

Now, I have just returned from Ogbomosho carrying more than luggage. The holiday will afford me the time and space to think through the secret Sammy shared with me about the Program hidden inside the fraternity’s Testament, a counterfeit system designed to harvest young people’s gifts and mortgage their future under the promise of fame, influence, and fulfilment.
I think of Rakiya. Her unsigned paintings leaning against a wall in that small room, each one crowded with symbols one cannot fully understand.
When Judith’s invitation came to spend part of the Christmas break with her family, I accept it quickly. “I need rest,” I tell myself.

But the truth is simpler. I want one week without mysteries. One week without cult names, prophecies, police rumours, spiritual warfare, or young men whose destinies always seem to lead toward graves.
Judith takes the holiday more seriously than I do. She drags me into a boutique near the City Square, insisting I cannot spend Christmas looking like “a missionary on assignment.”

The boutique is small but stylish, with glass shelves displaying handbags, perfumes, shoes, costume jewellery, folded denim, and brightly coloured dresses arranged under soft white lights. A mannequin near the entrance wears a green jumpsuit with a gold belt and stands with the confidence of a woman who has never worried about school fees, cultists, or police stations.
Judith moves through the racks with practised excitement.
“This one will fit you.”
“You haven’t even looked at it.”
“I can see the colour from here.”
“That is the problem with you, Lizzy. You dress like you are always preparing for a serious conversation.”
“I often am.”
She rolls her eyes. “Exactly. That is what I am trying to cure.”

I laugh, but my heart is not fully inside the laughter. Every now and then, my mind returns to Ogbomosho. To Rakiya’s left hand moving over paper and Sammy scanning the restaurant like a hunted man. I twitch at the thought that my own name, Lizzy, had become a possible trail to his hiding place. I dismissed the thought after I left.
Judith eventually convinces me to buy a modest dress, a scarf, and a pair of sandals I do not need. She buys twice as much and still complains that she has “shown restraint.”

When we step out of the boutique, the afternoon sun has softened, but the road remains busy. Cars move slowly. Hawkers pass with bottled drinks, biscuits, groundnuts and light food. A taxi stops ahead of us, then leaves before we can reach it.
Judith lifts her shopping bags with a sigh. We stand near the edge of the road waiting for another taxi. Then a car slows in front of us. I recognize the car.
David is always in Steel’s company, after Sammy left him. He sits behind the wheel. Steel occupies the front passenger seat.

He looks different in daylight. Cleaner. Better dressed. Less like the hardman of hostel corridors and more like a young man trying to resemble success. He’s brushed up with Otunba’s windfall. His eyes are the same, restless, proud, wounded, always searching for something to possess or accuse.
“Lizzy,” he calls.
I do not move.
Judith glances from him to me. “You know him?”
“Unfortunately.”
Steel smiles as if he has not heard. “Where are you going? Let me drop you.”
“No, thank you.”
“Come on. It’s Christmas period. Don’t stand under the sun forming independent woman.”
“We’re fine.”
Judith leans closer to me. “Lizzy, who is this?”
“Nobody we need.”
Steel’s smile fades slightly. David looks away, pretending sudden interest in the dashboard.
“Can I speak with you for a minute?” Steel asks.
“It’s important.”
“It usually is when you want attention.”
His jaw tightens.
“It’s about Sammy.”
The world around me seems to pause. The road noise does not stop, but it moves farther away.
“What about Sammy?”
Steel’s eyes hold mine.
“He was arrested yesterday.”
For a moment, I do not understand the words. Or maybe I understand too quickly and my heart refuses them. “Arrested?”
Steel nods. “In Ogbomosho.”
My shopping bags suddenly feel heavy.
Judith turns sharply to me. “Ogbomosho?”
I ignore her.

Steel opens the car door and steps out. The old arrogance remains, but beneath it I sense something else. Not guilt exactly. Fear, perhaps. Or the discomfort of someone who has delivered a message that may open a door he cannot control.
“You should go and see him,” he says.
“Answer me. Why was he arrested?”
He looks around, then lowers his voice. “There is a petition against him.”
“What petition?”
“Abduction. Exploitation of a minor. Maybe more.”
The words strike me like cold water. Rakiya.
My stomach tightens.
Steel watches my face carefully.
I sense he does not know everything. He knows enough to be dangerous, but not enough to understand what truly happened in Sammy’s room.
Judith grabs my arm. “Lizzy, what is going on?”
I cannot answer.
Steel continues, “He is at the station. If you care about him the way he thinks you do, you should go now.”
The sentence carries poison. But the information is real. I step closer to him. “Did you have anything to do with this?”
His face hardens. “Be careful.”
“No. You be careful.”
For once, my voice does not tremble. “You people have taken enough from him.”
His eyes flash. “You people?”
“Yes. You people.”
Judith tightens her grip on my arm.
Steel laughs once, without humour. “Lizzy, you don’t know the kind of things Sammy has done.”
“I know enough.”
“No. You know the church version.”
“And you know the version that excuses everything because it wears a black beret.”
His face darkens.
For a second, I think he will say something cruel. Instead, he steps back.
“Go and see him.”
He enters the car, shuts the door, and they drives off.
Judith remains beside me, staring as though I have become someone unfamiliar. “Lizzy,” she says slowly, “what deadly circle are you involved with?”
“I am not involved with them.”
“But you know them.”
“I knew them from school.”
“And Sammy?”
I do not answer quickly.
Judith stares at me.
“This is the same Sammy you travelled to see?”
“The same one you said was trying to rebuild his life?”
“And now he has been arrested for abduction and exploitation of a minor?”
“It is not what it sounds like.”
“How do you know?”
“Because I know him.”
Judith’s expression sharpens. “Do you?”

The question hurts. Not because it is unfair. Because it is the same question fear has begun asking me. How much do I truly know? I know Sammy’s repentance. I know his guilt. I know how desperately he wants to protect Rakiya. But I also know that parts of his past still stand behind him like dark trees.

Judith lowers her voice. “Lizzy, listen to me. You are not a police officer. You are not his lawyer. You are not his sister. You cannot keep throwing yourself into the fire because broken men tell you they are changing.”
“He did not ask me to throw myself anywhere.”
“But you are already preparing to go.”
I look at the road. A taxi approaches and passes without stopping.
Judith’s voice softens. “You were supposed to travel with me tomorrow.”
“I know.”
“My mother is expecting you.”
“I know.”
“You bought clothes.”
“I know.”
“Then call someone else. Call his family. Call whoever should handle this.”
“He has no one here.”
“That is not your fault.”
The words land heavily.
I look at her. “No. But it is my choice what I do with that knowledge.”
Judith closes her eyes briefly, frustrated.
“This friendship is dangerous.”
“And you know it?”
“Then why?”

I look down at the shopping bags in my hand. New sandals. A scarf. A dress for a holiday I suddenly cannot imagine taking. “Because if a person is drowning and you are the only one near the river, you don’t first ask whether helping will disturb your plans.”
Judith looks at me for a long time. Then she sighs.
“You Christians and your impossible conscience.”
A faint smile almost comes to my face. But it does not survive.
“Come,” she says. “Let’s get a taxi.”
“Judith…”
“I am still angry. But I am not leaving you on the road with cultists looking for you.”
That is Judith. She can scold you while saving you.
By evening, my holiday bag remains unpacked. The dress stays folded on my bed. The sandals remain in their box.
I leave for Ogbomosho the next morning with a small bag, my Bible, some cash, and a fear I cannot discuss with anyone except God.

Wooden benches line the waiting area. A faded national emblem hangs crookedly on the wall. The police station is poorly ventilated, but I am not sweating from the heat circulated by the noisy standing fan in the lobby. Other visitors are seated in different corners, some angry, some resigned, some pretending not to be afraid.
A woman argues with an officer near the counter. A boy no older than seventeen sits barefoot beside the wall, his eyes fixed on the floor.
When I give Sammy’s name, the officer behind the desk looks at me with a curiosity that tightens my skin.
“Relative?”
“Friend.”
His pen pauses.
“What kind of friend?”
“The kind that came to see him.”
He stares at me for another second, then points toward a corridor. A constable leads me to a small interview room.
Sammy is sitting on a wooden chair behind a metal table. For a moment, I stop at the door.

He looks thinner than when I left him in Ogbomosho. Not physically alone, but inwardly… as if some part of him has already begun withdrawing from the world. His shirt is rumpled. His eyes are tired but strangely calm. That calm frightens me more than panic would have.
He looks up. “Lizzy.”
My name in his mouth carries relief and sorrow together. I rush forward, but the constable’s presence restrains me.
“I’m sorry,” I say.
The words come before any greeting. “I am so sorry. I think I led them to you. Someone must have followed me that day I came.”
Sammy watches me quietly. There is no accusation in his face.
“I knew this hour would come,” he says.
“That does not make it my fault?”
“It feels like my fault.”
“The Lord prepared me for this.”
His calmness annoys me.
“Sammy, don’t speak like someone already writing his last chapter.”
He looks away.
I sit opposite him.
“What are they charging you with?”
His mouth tightens.
“Abduction. Sexual exploitation of a minor. Possibly conspiracy.”
The words turn my stomach.
“Rakiya?”
He nods.
“Where is she?”
His eyes return to mine.
“Gone.”
For a moment, I do not understand.
“What do you mean gone?”
“When they took me, she was still in the house. By the time enquiries were made, she was no longer there.”
My breath catches.
“Her parents?”
“I don’t know.”
“Did they take her?”
“I don’t know.”
“The police?”
“I don’t know.”
His voice finally cracks.
“I don’t know, Lizzy. That is the worst part.”
The room seems to close in around us.

Rakiya’s smile flashes through my mind. Her shy correction when I told her not to call me aunty. Her left hand moving over paper. Her unsigned paintings. Her excitement when I asked her if she’d come and live with me, “Yes. Yes.”
I press my hand against my mouth.
Sammy leans forward.
“You have to find her.”
“Where do I look?”
“I don’t know.”
“Sammy, I barely know how to describe her properly.”
“You saw her.”
“Yes, but only once.”
“You saw the paintings.”
His eyes sharpen.
“Find the paintings. Find anyone who has them. Find Rachel if she exists. Find the hospital record. Anything.”
“Rachel?”
“The girl who led me to her.”
“The one you said disappeared?”
I stare at him.
The case is already larger than an arrest. It is becoming a maze.
“This is a frame-up,” I whisper.
“Otunba?”
“His petition opened the door. But Rakiya’s disappearance complicates it.”
He looks toward the door, then lowers his voice. “I don’t know how much time I have.”
“Stop saying that.”
“They will kill me.”
“They will.”
“No, Sammy.”
He smiles faintly. Not with joy. With pity. As if he wishes my denial has power over the machinery closing around him.
“Lizzy, listen to me.”
“I am listening.”
“You remember what I told you in Ogbomosho? About the Program?”
I nod.
“The Program the GODs wanted you to execute with Otunba.”
“Yes. That politician is not merely a sponsor. He is an agent of the counterfeit system.”
The word moves strangely through the room.

Sammy’s voice drops further. “The world they are building is not just political. It is cultural. Spiritual. Intellectual. They want access to young people at the point where desire is strongest – fame, education, influence, escape, technology, art, entertainment, opportunity.”
I remember Rakiya’s paintings, and what Sammy told me about Tunde’s fake contract and the students at the Centre preparing for a concert as though performance can save them from obscurity.
“The Program promises fulfilment,” Sammy continues. “But it harvests identity.”
I swallow. “And Rakiya?”
“She is proof that God is doing a new thing.”

His eyes burn now, not with madness, but conviction. “The Lord is raising a remnant generation. Gifts that will not be owned by darkness. Talents that will not be sold to the merchants. Weak things of the world to confound the mighty.”
“Sammy…”
“I know how it sounds.”
“It sounds dangerous.”
“It is.”
The constable outside moves past the door. We both go quiet.
Then Sammy speaks again, softer. “I had a dream.”
I wait.
“My father was blessing me.” His face changes as he says it.
The calm breaks.
For the first time since I entered, I see the frightened orphan beneath the prophet, beneath the fugitive, beneath the former cultist.
“He laid his hand on my head,” Sammy whispers. “He prayed for me. I could not hear all the words, but I knew he was blessing me.”
“Your foster father?”
He nods. “I want to see them, Lizzy.”
I feel the impact of his request more deeply than all the revelations before it. A son wanting to go home before death finds him.
“I ran away from them,” he says. “I thought they rejected me. But I have been remembering things. I think I was wrong. I want to reconcile. I want to see my father. I want his blessing before I die.”
“Before you live,” I correct him.
He looks at me.
“Lizzy…”
“No. Before you live.”
His eyes fill.
“I don’t know if they will want to see me.”
“Then we will find out.”
“You will help me?”
“You promise?”
The word costs me. Because a promise, once given, becomes a road.
“I promise.”
He lowers his head.
For several seconds, he says nothing.
Then I reach across the table. The constable looks in through the door, but does not stop me. I place my hand over Sammy’s.
“But you must promise me something too.”
He looks up.
“What?”
“Stay alive.”
His gaze falls.
The silence that follows frightens me.
“Sammy.”
His fingers tremble beneath mine.
“I don’t know if I can control that.”
“I am not asking whether they will try to kill you. I am asking whether you will help me fight for your life.”
He closes his eyes. His breathing becomes uneven. For one terrible moment, I think he will refuse. Then he takes a short breath.
“I will stay alive,” he mutters.
“Say it like you mean it.”
He opens his eyes. They are wet.
“I will stay alive.”
I hold his hand tighter.

Outside the station, someone shouts. A phone rings. A typewriter clacks in another room. The world continues in its ordinary indifference. But inside that small interview room, my holiday ends completely. The vacation clothes folded on my bed no longer belong to the life I am living.

Sammy has been arrested. Rakiya is missing. Otunba’s shadow stretches farther than I feared. And somewhere between a police station, a lost foster family, a vanished girl, and a secret Program designed to harvest a generation, I understand that my own calling has changed.
I came to help a friend. But I leave the station with an assignment.

chapters.title: Chapter TWO
content: Following Sammy’s description, it’s easy for me to locate Destiny Education Institute. Although school was officially on vacation, it’s the proprietress, Mrs. Animashaun’s birthday and she had invited the students to come celebrate with her.
Both the teachers and students will be expecting to see him. Not that Sammy commanded me to go, but as a burden handed over through trembling eyes in a police station.
“Tell them the truth what has happened, so they don’t hear a false version,” he had said.
Sammy was quietly rebuilding young people in Ogbomosho while the rest of us still thought of him only as a man hiding from the fraternity.

Destiny Institute stands at the edge of a busy road, a modest one-storey building with faded paint, old signboards, and classrooms that seem too small for the noise I imagine they contain. But this morning, the compound is unusually quiet.
No laughter. No shouting. They have heard the news, I suppose. It does not look like they came for a birthday party, but to mourn.

A few students stand in clusters near the staircase. Some turn when I enter the gate. Others look away immediately, as though my arrival reminds them of the person who is missing. I recognize some faces from Sammy’s stories: Tunde, restless even in stillness; Genevieve, composed but troubled; Oluchi, visibly anxious; Patrick, standing with his hands in his pockets; Jumoke, unusually subdued; and Eno, seated near the veranda with a notebook pressed against her chest.
The security man directs me to the proprietress’ office.

Mrs. Animashaun receives me with the politeness of a woman trying to remain official while grief presses against her composure. She is in her late forties, perhaps early fifties, neatly dressed in a patterned blouse and skirt, her glasses resting low on her nose. Her desk is covered with files, examination timetables, a ledger, and photographs from what looks like a recent event.
“You must be Miss Lizzy,” she says.
“Yes, ma.” How did you know me? I ask myself.
“Please sit.”
I sit.
For a brief moment, neither of us speaks.
“Happy Birthday ma. He told me, it’s…”
“Have you seen him?”
Then she removes her glasses and sighs.
“How is he?”
The question is simple, but I do not know how to answer it. “He is alive,” I say finally.
Her face falls slightly, as though she understands everything I do not say.
“Alive is something,” she murmurs.
“Yes, ma.”
She looks toward the window, where students linger in the courtyard, pretending not to wait for information.
“They have not been themselves since the news came.”
“I can imagine.”
“No,” she says softly. “I don’t think you can.”
I look at her.

She turns back to me. “When Mr. Briggs first came here, I honestly did not know what to make of him. He had no strong certificate to present. No polished background. No confident professional history. But he had something I could not easily dismiss.”
“What was that?”
“Burden.”
The word surprises me.
“He looked at these children as if he could see beyond their noise. That was rare. Many teachers complain about students. He studied them.”
She picks up one photograph from the table and hands it to me. It is from the Christmas concert.

The students stand on a stage, dressed in coordinated outfits. Some are smiling broadly. Others look nervous. Tunde stands at one side holding what appears to be a painted backdrop. Eno stands near the centre, microphone in hand, her eyes lowered but her posture graceful.
I stare at the photograph.
“They performed?” I ask.
Mrs. Animashaun smiles faintly. “They did more than perform. They surprised us.”

She hands me another photograph. This one shows Sammy standing beside the students, not in front like a celebrity teacher, but slightly to the side, almost as if he wants the attention to remain on them.
My throat tightens.
“He never told me much about the concert,” I say.
“He would not. He was not a man who praised himself.”
She arranges the photographs slowly.

“These children came here disillusioned. Some had failed examinations. Some were rejected by schools. Some were waiting for admission that never came. Some had lost confidence completely. A few were simply careless. Others were angry. Many had already started believing that nothing meaningful would come out of their lives.”
She pauses.
“Mr. Briggs did not treat them like failures. He treated them like unfinished work.”
The sentence stays with me. Unfinished work. That is Sammy too.

The door opens gently and a man steps in. Mrs. Animashaun introduces him as Mr. Adewale, the principal. He is tall and lean, with a mild face and tired eyes. He greets me warmly, but sadness sits behind his smile.
“I was just telling her about Mr. Briggs,” Mrs. Animashaun says.
The principal nods.
“Then tell her the truth. He gave us trouble.”
Despite myself, I smile.
“What kind of trouble?”

“The kind good teachers give administrators,” he says. “He wanted things. Time with students. Permission for mentoring sessions. Space for rehearsals. Extra access to classrooms. Then he wanted us to look beyond exam preparation and help students identify talents.”
Mrs. Animashaun shakes her head.
“At first I thought it was too much.”
“It was too much,” the principal says. “But it worked.”
He turns to me.
“What amazed me most was how he handled misconduct. These students can be difficult. Very difficult. Tunde especially.”
As if summoned by his name, faint movement comes from outside the office window.
The principal notices and smiles sadly. “Normally, when students misbehave, teachers punish the behaviour. Sammy would ask what the behaviour was protecting.”
I frown slightly. “What do you mean?”

“He did not excuse nonsense. But he always looked deeper. A student mocked another student, and he would ask why that student needed laughter so badly. A girl stopped attending class, and he would ask what shame was holding her hostage. A boy became arrogant, and he would ask what insecurity the arrogance was covering.”
Mrs. Animashaun adds, “Sometimes I thought he was too patient.”
“And was he?” I ask.
The principal smiles. “No. He was patient in a way that made the rest of us feel shallow.”
The room becomes quiet. I look again at the concert photograph. Sammy had been hiding in Ogbomosho, yet he had not hidden from purpose. That realization humbles me.
“May I meet the students?” I ask.
Mrs. Animashaun nods. “I was hoping you would ask.”
They gather in Sammy’s classroom. Not because anyone instructs them to choose it.
They simply go there.

The room still carries his presence. The blackboard has faint chalk marks from a previous lesson. A pile of old assignment sheets sits near the teacher’s table. On one side of the board is written in fading chalk. Talent without discipline becomes public nuisance.
I almost laugh.
That sounds like him.
The students sit quietly, which makes them appear younger than they are. Without noise, their fear becomes visible.
Mrs. Animashaun introduces me briefly.
“This is Miss Lizzy. She is Mr. Briggs’ friend.”
Friend.

The word moves across the room with many meanings. Some students look at me with hope. Some with suspicion. Some with accusation. Tunde’s eyes are red, though he tries to hide it. Eno holds her notebook tighter.
I stand before them and suddenly understand why Sammy has asked me to come. This is not a courtesy visit. It is a handover of grief.
“Good morning,” I say.
A few respond weakly. “Good morning, ma.”
“Please don’t call me ma. Lizzy is fine.”
No one smiles.
So I continue. “I saw Sammy yesterday.”
The entire room sharpens. Tunde leans forward. Eno’s lips part slightly.
Genevieve asks the question first. “How is he?”
“He is alive.”
Tunde mutters, “That one is not answer.”
A few students turn to him, but I raise my hand gently. “It is not a full answer,” I say. “But it is the first truth.”
The room quiets again.
“He is in police custody. There are allegations against him. I believe those allegations are false.”
“False?” Patrick asks. “Then why did they arrest him?”
“Because false things can still wear uniforms and carry files.”
The principal glances at me, but he does not interrupt.
Tunde stands suddenly. “It is because of us.”
“No,” I say.
“It is because of Rakiya,” he insists. “Because he helped her. Because he always wants to help everybody. Now see.”
His voice cracks at the end. He sits down quickly, ashamed of his emotion.
Oluchi wipes her eyes.
Jumoke speaks next, her proud voice unusually soft. “Will he come back?”
The question hurts.
“I don’t know.”
That answer wounds them more than a comforting lie would have, but I refuse to insult their grief with false certainty.
“I don’t know when. But I know he asked about you.”
At that, the room changes slightly.
“He asked about us?” Eno whispers.
“Yes.” I look at her.
“He asked me to come here. To check on you. To make sure you are not abandoned.”
Eno lowers her head and begins to cry quietly. Genevieve places an arm around her shoulder. For several seconds, I let the room breathe.
Then Tunde raises his hand, though he is already speaking. “Did he mention me?”
A few students look at him.
His face reddens. “I’m just asking.”
I smile faintly. “He did not mention names. But I know he would want you to stop acting like your talent is a joke.”
The class murmurs.
Someone whispers, “That one na for Tunde.”
Tunde sniffs and looks away. “He told you about my drawing?”
“He told me enough.”
Tunde swallows. “I was going to show him something after the holidays.”
“What?”
“A design. For the concert group.”
“Then keep it safe.”
His face tightens.
“What if he doesn’t come back?”
“Then you keep it safe until he does.”
“And if…”
“He will need to see that you did not waste what he believed in.”
That silences him.
Eno slowly raises her face. “Miss Lizzy?”
“People are saying he ran away with money. That he was pretending to be good.”
A murmur moves through the class. I hear the fear beneath the question. Not fear that Sammy is guilty. Fear that the one adult who made them believe in themselves might become another disappointment.
I take a breath.

“People will say many things. Some from ignorance. Some from cruelty. Some because gossip makes them feel important. But you must learn something Sammy tried to teach you: do not judge a person only by rumours. Look at fruit.”
I point gently around the room. “You are part of his fruit.”
They become still.
“He met many of you discouraged. Now you are singing, drawing, studying, leading, apologizing, caring for one another. That is fruit.”
Eno wipes her face. “He saved my life,” she says.
The room goes quiet.

She stands slowly. “I don’t care what people say. He saved my life. When everybody would have laughed at me, he did not laugh. When I thought I was finished, he told me my story was not finished.” Her voice trembles, but she continues. “He called me miracle.”
She holds up her notebook.
On the back, in careful lettering, are the words, ENO, THE MIRACLE.
Several students lower their heads. Even Tunde. The principal turns away slightly, pretending to adjust his glasses.
Oluchi stands too. “He also told us not to gossip about her,” she says. “He said if the world was what she feared, we should be better than the world.”
Genevieve adds, “He made this class different.”
Patrick folds his arms. “He disturbed us too much.”
For the first time, faint laughter moves through the room.
Patrick shrugs. “But good disturbance.”
Jumoke smiles through tears. “He made us rehearse when we didn’t want to.”
Tunde mutters, “He punished me for drawing.”
“No,” Genevieve corrects. “He redirected you.”
The class laughs again, softer this time.
Tunde rolls his eyes. “I don’t like big grammar.”
“You need it,” Genevieve replies.

Watching them, I understand something. Sammy has not merely taught these students. He has entered their language. Their jokes. Their wounds. Their arrogance. Their shame. Their hunger to be seen. He has given them a small community where failure is not final and discipline does not mean rejection.
That is worth preserving. I walk to the blackboard and pick up a piece of chalk.
“What are you doing?” Tunde asks.
I write my phone number on the board. The students lean forward.
“This is my number,” I say. “Tunde, make sure everyone copies it.”
“Yes, you.”
He stands immediately, as if the responsibility itself has lifted him. “Yes, Miss Lizzy.”
“From today, if there is an emergency, if any of you is in trouble, if anyone threatens you because of Sammy, or if you hear anything about Rakiya, you call me.”
Genevieve’s eyes sharpen. “Rakiya is missing?”
The room freezes. I regret saying the name too quickly. But it is too late.
“Yes,” I say carefully. “And we need to find her.”
Tunde’s face changes. “The girl that paints?”
“You saw her?”

He nods. “Only once. She came to the Centre with Mr. Briggs. She stayed near the office and kept drawing. I remember because she held pencil like this.” He lifts his left hand and imitates a sketching motion.
My heart tightens. Left-handed.
“What did she draw?” I ask.
“I don’t know. I tried to look. She covered it.”
“Did she speak to anyone?”
Tunde looks around.
Eno shakes her head. “She smiled at me, but she didn’t talk.”
Oluchi says, “I saw her at the gate with a small book. She was drawing the signboard.”
“The institute signboard?” I ask.
Oluchi shrugs.
“Listen carefully. No one goes looking for her alone. No one confronts strangers. No one follows anyone claiming to know where she is. You call me. Understand?”
“Yes,” they answer.
“Good.”
Tunde raises his hand. “Miss Lizzy?”
“Are you going to be there for us?”
The question lands heavily. Not because it is childish. Because it is direct. Everyone looks at me.

I think of Judith’s warning. This friendship is dangerous. My mind goes to my abandoned holiday plans. The dress still folded, the sandals still boxed. The journey I longed for, to rest. Then I think of Sammy’s face in the police station. “Find her. Find my father.”
I look at the students. “I will do my best.”
Tunde shakes his head. “That is adult answer.”
Some students murmur in agreement. I almost laugh.
“What answer do you want?”
“A real one,” he says.
Eno’s voice comes softly. “Will you leave us?”
The question is not about logistics. It is about abandonment. I feel it. I move closer to the first row.
“I cannot become Sammy,” I say.
Nobody speaks.
“And I cannot promise that everything will be easy. I have to search for someone very important to him. I also have to help find Rakiya. So I may not be here every day.”
Their faces fall slightly.
“But I promise this: I will not forget you. I will stay in contact. I will speak with Mrs. Animashaun and Mr. Adewale. We will make sure what Sammy started here does not die because he is absent.”
Tunde lowers his eyes. “That one is better.”
The class gives a faint laugh. I stretch out my hand toward him.
“Come.”
He looks confused. “Come where?”
“Here.”
He steps forward cautiously. I place my hand out, palm down.
“Put your hand here.”
He stares at me. “Why?”
“Must everything have explanation?”
The class laughs. He places his hand over mine.
She comes forward and places hers on top of Tunde’s.
“Genevieve.”

Then Oluchi. Patrick. Jumoke. Bisi. Chike. One by one, the students come forward, some shy, some emotional, some trying to hide tears behind jokes. Soon a cluster of hands forms at the front of the class. I look around at them.
“From now,” I say, “we protect what God started through Sammy.”
Tunde, unable to resist himself, says, “So we are now the Sammy Briggs gang?”
The class bursts into laughter. Even I can’t hold myself. The principal covers his mouth. Mrs. Animashaun shakes her head, smiling through tears.
I look at Tunde.
“No cult names.”
“Not cult, Miss Lizzy. Positive gang.”
“Still no.”
“Sammy Briggs Team?”
“That is better.”
“Remnant Squad?” Patrick suggests.
The room becomes noisy again, but this time the noise is alive with affection rather than disorder.
I raise my hand.
“Whatever you call yourselves, remember this: Sammy did not gather you to worship him. He helped you see that your lives matter to God. So, honour him by becoming serious with your lives.”
The room quiets.

“Study. Sing. Draw. Apologize when you are wrong. Protect one another. Don’t let shame destroy anyone here. And if any of you sees another student going down a dangerous path, do not laugh. Do not gossip. Stop them.”
Eno nods slowly. Genevieve wipes her eyes. Tunde looks at the board.
“I will copy the number,” he says.
“Good.”
As they return to their seats, the atmosphere has shifted.
They are still grieving. Still afraid. Still children in many ways. But something has been preserved. A small flame Sammy lit among them has not gone out.
Mrs. Animashaun walks me back to her office afterward.
“I did not expect that,” she says.
“Neither did I.”
“You have a hold on them.”
“No,” I reply. “Sammy does.”
She considers that.
“Then perhaps you are holding the rope until he returns.”
The sentence stays with me. Holding the rope.

When I leave the institute, the afternoon sun has begun to lean westward. Students linger near the gate, copying my number from Tunde’s notebook, arguing over whether “Sammy Briggs Team” sounds too ordinary.
I should smile. But my mind has already moved ahead. To Sammy’s request. His foster father. His dream of blessing. His fear of death.

The journey before me suddenly feels larger than finding an address. I am not merely searching for a man. I am searching for the missing root of Sammy’s life. The father whose blessing he believes he needs before darkness finishes its work.

As I walk away from Destiny Institute, I understand that Sammy’s assignment has placed assignments on all of us. The students have to keep hope alive. And somehow, somewhere, I have to find the father of a broken son who is trying desperately to come home before it is too late.

chapters.title: Chapter THREE
content: The suitcase remains open on my bed long after I return from Destiny Institute. I stand before it without touching anything.

The clothes are neatly folded, arranged with the hopeful care of a woman who expected rest. A floral dress lies on top. Beneath it are two casual tops, a scarf Judith insisted I pack, a pair of sandals still wrapped in tissue paper, and a small bottle of perfume I bought because holidays, I told myself, deserve softness.
I stare at them until they begin to look like belongings from another woman’s life. That woman was supposed to be on her way to Abuja.

She was supposed to sit beside Judith in a bus, laughing at roadside drama, complaining about delayed departure, buying roasted plantain at a stopover, and arriving tired but light-hearted. She was supposed to spend Christmas in a house where nobody whispered about cultists, police stations, missing girls, secret Programs, or young men marked by forces they once served.

She was supposed to rest. I almost envy her. The woman I might have been if Sammy had never crossed my life. If Ikenna had not died. If Bitty had not carried guilt like a second shadow. If the name Jshap had remained only a testimony from fellowship, and not the doorway into a history of blood, prophecy, redemption, and unfinished assignments.
I sit on the edge of the bed. The suitcase stares back at me.
I think of Sammy behind that metal table at the police station, his eyes tired but strangely peaceful, as though he had already begun preparing himself for death while I was still arguing with life.
His final request echoes in my subconscious, “Find Rakiya. Find my father… Help me reconcile before death finds me.”

I reach for the floral dress and lift it out. For a moment, I press it against my chest. Then I hang it back in the wardrobe. One by one, I unpack. Each item returns to its place with the heaviness of surrender.
It is strange how sacrifice sometimes looks ordinary, without a drame. Just a woman taking clothes out of a suitcase because a friend is in trouble and she cannot pretend she does not know.
When the suitcase is empty, I close it and push it beneath the bed. I have a flash of illusion that I can remain uninvolved.
I leave the house before evening and go to Judith’s. She opens the door with excitement already forming on her face.
“Madam traveler!”
Then she sees me properly. Her smile fades.
“What happened?”
I step in without answering.
Her sitting room smells of air freshener. A Christmas garland hangs above the television. Two travel bags stand near the sofa, already packed. Judith has always been more organized than she admits.
“I’m not travelling again,” I say.
For several seconds, she stares at me. Then she laughs once. Not because she finds it funny. Because she thinks I cannot possibly be serious.
“Lizzy, please don’t start.”
“I’m serious.”
“You cannot be serious.”
She folds her arms. “Because of Sammy.”
I do not answer.
“That silence is your confession.”
I sit down.
Judith remains standing. “So you are cancelling your holiday because of a man who was arrested for abducting a girl?”
“It is a frame-up.”
“You keep saying that.”
“Because it is true.”
“How do you know?”
“Because I know what happened.”
“Do you?” she snaps.
The sharpness in her voice surprises me. “Lizzy, do you really know? Or do you know the part Sammy told you with tears in his eyes?”
I look away.
She comes closer. “I am not trying to be wicked. I am trying to keep you alive. Do you understand the kind of people you keep attaching yourself to?”
“I am not attached to them.”
“You are.”
“Yes, you are. First it was Jehoshaphat.”
My chest tightens. “Do not bring him into this.”

“I will bring him into it because you never left that story. Jehoshaphat died, and somehow his death became a ministry in your life. Then Bitty came with his guilt and escape. Now Sammy. Three men connected to the same dark fraternity, and you keep standing near their fire as if smoke cannot stain you.”
Her words hurt because they are not careless. They are observations sharpened by concern.
“Ikenna was different,” I say quietly.
“Was he?”
“And Bitty?”
“He needed help.”
“And Sammy?”
“He needs help too.”
Judith exhales sharply. “Everybody needs help, Lizzy. That does not mean God appointed you to carry every broken man with a cult history on your back.”
I feel tears pressing behind my eyes but refuse them. “This is not about broken men.”
“Then what is it about?”

I open my mouth. No answer comes quickly. Because the truth is not simple. It is not affection alone. IT’s not pity, stubbornness nor guilt. Something began with Jshap that I still do not fully understand.

His life, his death, his writings, his testimony, the strange way his choices continued moving people after he was gone. It had opened a door in me. I had tried to close it many times. I had told myself to return to ordinary concerns, ordinary friendships, ordinary ambitions. But something would not let go. Something in me would not give up on Jshap. Not the man alone. The meaning of him.

The proof that darkness could lose a soul it thought it owned. The proof that grace could enter a fraternity and overturn its symbols. The proof that a life surrendered to God could trouble systems built on fear even after death. And now Sammy stands at the edge of that same mystery.
Not as Jshap, not as a replacement. But as another witness that God is harvesting men from the very places the enemy thought he had secured.
“I don’t know how to explain it,” I say finally.
Judith softens slightly, but only slightly. “Try.”
I look at her.
“When Ikenna died, I thought it was the end of a tragic story. But it wasn’t. His death kept speaking. Bitty was changed by it. Sammy was changed by it. Even I was changed by it.”
Judith sits slowly.

“I don’t think this is just about helping Sammy. I think there is something God is doing through these people, through their redemption, their pain, their unfinished stories. I don’t understand it fully, but every time I try to step away, something pulls me back.”
“That sounds dangerous.”
“It is.”
“Then why are you calm?”
“I am not calm.” My voice breaks. “I am terrified.”
That silences her. The tears finally come.

“I am tired, Judith. I am tired of police stations. Tired of cult names. Tired of watching young men destroy themselves. Tired of hearing secrets that make sleep difficult. Tired of being the one people call when everything is falling apart.”
Judith’s face changes.
“I am tired of being brave,” I whisper.
She moves closer and sits beside me. For a while, neither of us speaks.
Then she says softly, “Then let someone else go.”
“I can’t.”
“Because Sammy has no one else.”
“He has God.”
“Yes. And sometimes God sends people.”
Judith wipes her face with one hand, frustrated and emotional at once.
“You always know how to make selfishness sound like wisdom if one disagrees with you.”
A faint laugh escapes me through tears. “I am not asking you to understand everything.”
“I don’t.”
“I know.”
“I don’t like this,” she confesses.
“I know.”
“I think it is dangerous.”
“It is.”
“I think you should inform someone older. A pastor. A lawyer. Someone with sense and gray hair.”
“I will.”
“You promise?”
She studies me.
“And you are still not travelling.”
She leans back and closes her eyes. “My mother will ask questions.”
“Tell her I’m sorry.”
“She will say you have started your drama again.”
“She may be right.”
Judith looks at me, and despite everything, we both smile faintly.
Then the smile fades.
“What will you do now?” she asks.
I shake my head. “I don’t know.”
That is the most honest thing I have said all day.

I do not know how to find Sammy’s foster father. I do not know where to begin looking for Rakiya. I do not know whether the fraternity is watching me. I do not know whether Steel is warning me, manipulating me, or both. I do not know whether Sammy will still be alive by the time I find the man whose blessing he wants.
I only know that I cannot travel to Abuja and pretend I have chosen peace.
Judith sees me off on my way home, and warns me again to be careful. I return home and kneel beside my bed. The room is quiet.
I open my Bible but cannot read. Words blur. My mind keeps moving between faces. Jehoshaphat. His name still hurts.
I did not choose this road. Or maybe I chose it each time I refused to walk away. My friends had warned me. Judith had warned me.

Others had hinted, scolded, questioned, and withdrawn. They wondered why my life kept circling the fraternity, why I remained emotionally tied to stories that should have frightened any sensible woman away.

Maybe they were right to worry. Maybe I am foolish. Maybe compassion can become recklessness when it refuses counsel. But there is something beneath my decisions that feels larger than pity. A thread I cannot cut. A burden I do not know how to drop.
I lower my head.
“Lord,” I whisper. The word comes out broken. “Please direct me.”
Silence.
I press my forehead to the edge of the bed. “I don’t know what to do.”
That confession opens the tears fully.
